topics.title: Introduction: Deep Reinforcement Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction: Deep Reinforcement Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)** is a sub-field of Machine Learning that combines **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** with **Deep Learning (DL)**. It enables an intelligent *agent* to learn optimal *policies* by interacting with a complex, high-dimensional *environment*, where the policy and/or value functions are approximated using deep neural networks.

In classical RL, the state-action value function $Q(s,a)$ is stored in a **lookup table** (tabular methods), which becomes infeasible when the number of states runs into millions (e.g., raw pixel inputs of an Atari game). **DRL** solves this *curse of dimensionality* by replacing the table with a **Deep Neural Network (DNN)** that generalizes across similar states.

### The Three Pillars of DRL

| Pillar | Role | Standard Tool |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | Decision-making framework | MDP, Bellman Equations |
| **Deep Learning** | Function approximation | CNN, RNN, Transformers |
| **Optimization** | Stable training | Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD), Adam |

---

## Conceptual Analogy — The Self-Driving Toddler

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a toddler learning to ride a bicycle.**
> - The **child** is the *Agent*.
> - The **bicycle + road + wind + gravity** is the *Environment*.
> - **Not falling down** yields a *positive reward* (candy from mom).
> - **Falling** yields a *negative reward* (a scraped knee).
> - The child's **brain** is the *deep neural network* — it maps thousands of sensory inputs (vision, balance, wind speed) to muscle actions (steering, pedaling, braking).
>
> The child does *not* learn from a labeled dataset. The child learns from **trial and error**, just like a DRL agent.

### Why "Deep" in DRL? — The Scaling Problem

Classical Q-learning failed spectacularly on:
- **Atari 2600 games** (input: $210 \times 160$ pixel images → $\approx$100,000 states per frame)
- **Go** ($10^{170}$ possible board positions)
- **Robotic Locomotion** (continuous high-dimensional sensor streams)

A simple lookup table cannot store $Q$-values for these. Hence, a **Deep Neural Network** acts as a *function approximator*:

$$Q(s, a; \theta) \approx Q^*(s, a)$$

where $\theta$ are the trainable weights of the network.

---

## Historical Timeline (KTU High-Yield Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **2013 DeepMind paper** by Mnih et al., *"Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning"*, is universally considered the founding milestone of modern DRL. It introduced the **Deep Q-Network (DQN)**.

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1958** | Bellman Equation | Mathematical foundation of RL |
| **1989** | Q-learning (Watkins) | Model-free TD control |
| **2013** | DQN (DeepMind) | First successful DRL on Atari |
| **2015** | DQN in *Nature* | Human-level control from pixels |
| **2015** | DDPG | DRL for continuous actions |
| **2016** | AlphaGo | Defeated world Go champion |
| **2017** | PPO | Industry-standard policy gradient |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Reinforcement Learning Loop (Pre-Deep Context)

The entire field of DRL rests on the **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**, defined by the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$.

- $\mathcal{S}$ : Set of all possible *states* in the environment.
- $\mathcal{A}$ : Set of all possible *actions* the agent can take.
- $P(s' \vert s, a)$ : *Transition probability* — probability of landing in state $s'$ after taking action $a$ in state $s$.
- $R(s, a)$ : *Reward function* — immediate scalar feedback.
- $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ : *Discount factor* — measures the importance of future rewards.

The agent's goal is to find an optimal policy $\pi^*(a \vert s)$ that maximizes the **expected discounted cumulative reward**, also called the *return*:

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k \, R_{t+k+1}$$

---

## 2.2 From Tabular RL to Deep RL — The Critical Leap

### Step 1: Bellman Optimality Equation (Tabular Foundation)

The optimal action-value function obeys the **Bellman Optimality Equation**:

$$Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{s'}\!\left[\, R(s, a) + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')\, \right]$$

### Step 2: Why Tabular Fails — The Approximation Necessity

> [!NOTE]
> When $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$ becomes astronomically large (e.g., every possible pixel arrangement of Breakout), storing a table is impossible. We replace the table with a **parameterized function** $Q(s, a; \theta)$, where $\theta$ are the weights of a deep neural network.

### Step 3: DQN Approximation

$$Q(s, a; \theta) \approx Q^*(s, a)$$

The network takes the **state** $s$ as input and outputs a vector of $Q$-values, one for each discrete action.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table. Every KTU question on DRL will draw from these equations.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Return (Cumulative Reward) | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | $G_t \in \mathbb{R}$, $\gamma$ dimensionless |
| 2 | State-Value Function | $V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \,\big\vert\, S_t = s\right]$ | $V^\pi(s) \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 3 | Action-Value Function | $Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \,\big\vert\, S_t = s, A_t = a\right]$ | $Q^\pi(s, a) \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 4 | Bellman Optimality (Q) | $Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')\right]$ | Recursive form |
| 5 | Bellman Optimality (V) | $V^*(s) = \max_a \mathbb{E}\!\left[R + \gamma V^*(s')\right]$ | Recursive form |
| 6 | Q-Learning Update (Tabular) | $Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha\!\left[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)\right]$ | $\alpha$ : learning rate |
| 7 | DQN Loss Function (MSE) | $L(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\left( r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a'; \theta^-) - Q(s, a; \theta) \right)^2 \right]$ | $\theta^-$ : target net weights |
| 8 | Target Value (DQN) | $y_i = r_i + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s'_i, a'; \theta^-)$ | Supervised-learning target |
| 9 | TD Error | $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t)$ | Used for backprop |
| 10 | Epsilon-Greedy Policy | $\pi(a \vert s) = \begin{cases} 1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert}, & a = \arg\max_a Q(s, a) \\ \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | $\varepsilon \in [0, 1]$ |
| 11 | Policy Gradient (REINFORCE) | $\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \cdot G_t\right]$ | Used in policy-based DRL |
| 12 | Discount Factor Constraint | $0 \le \gamma \le 1$ | $0$ : myopic, $1$ : far-sighted |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of DRL

- **Robotics:** Learning dexterous manipulation (e.g., OpenAI's Rubik's cube robot hand).
- **Autonomous Driving:** Lane keeping, merging, and overtaking decisions in Tesla / Waymo.
- **Game AI:** AlphaGo, AlphaStar (StarCraft II), OpenAI Five (Dota 2).
- **Finance:** Algorithmic trading, dynamic portfolio rebalancing.
- **Networking & 5G:** Adaptive resource allocation and beamforming in 6G research.
- **Energy:** Smart grid load balancing, HVAC control in data centers (DeepMind-Google 40% saving).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q^*(s,a)$

The Bellman Optimality Equation is the **cornerstone** of all DRL. Let us derive it from first principles.

### Starting Point: The Return

The return from time $t$ is the discounted sum of future rewards:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

### Step A: Define the Action-Value Function

The optimal action-value function is the expected return when starting from state $s$, taking action $a$, and thereafter following the optimal policy $\pi^*$:

$$Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, G_t \,\big\vert\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a\, \right]$$

### Step B: Expand the Expectation

Substitute the definition of $G_t$ and separate the **first reward** from the **rest**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^*(s, a) &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots \,\big\vert\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a\, \right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma \!\left( R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \dots \right) \,\big\vert\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a\, \right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1} \,\big\vert\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a\, \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> The inner term $G_{t+1} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+2}$ is itself a return starting at $t+1$.

### Step C: Condition on the Next State

By the **Law of Total Expectation**, we condition on the next state $s'$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^*(s, a) &= \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \, \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1} \,\big\vert\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a,\; S_{t+1} = s'\, \right] \\
&= \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \!\left[\, \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \vert s, a, s'] + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}[G_{t+1} \vert s'\,] \,\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step D: Use Markov Property & Reward Definition

Because of the **Markov property**, the future depends only on $s'$, and the expected reward is exactly $R(s, a, s')$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^*(s, a) &= \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \!\left[\, R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}[G_{t+1} \vert S_{t+1} = s'] \,\right] \\
&= \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \!\left[\, R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V^*(s') \,\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $V^*(s') = \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$.

### Step E: Final Closed Form

$$
\boxed{\, Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{s'}\!\left[\, R(s, a) + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \,\right] \,}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This single equation encodes the *self-consistent* fixed point that any optimal agent must satisfy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the DQN Loss Function

The DQN paper (Mnih et al., 2015) reformulates the Bellman equation as a **supervised-learning regression problem**.

### Step 1: Define the Supervised Target

We treat the right-hand side of the Bellman equation as a *target*:

$$y_i = r_i + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s'_i, a'; \, \theta^-)$$

The superscript $\theta^-$ denotes the **target network** (a periodically frozen copy of the online network used to stabilize training).

### Step 2: Define the Network Prediction

The online network predicts:

$$\hat{y}_i = Q(s_i, a_i; \, \theta)$$

### Step 3: Formulate the Loss as Mean-Squared Error

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(\theta) &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, \left( y_i - \hat{y}_i \right)^2 \,\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, \left( r_i + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s'_i, a'; \, \theta^-) - Q(s_i, a_i; \, \theta) \right)^2 \,\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Take the Gradient for Backpropagation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_\theta L(\theta) &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, 2 \left( y_i - \hat{y}_i \right) \cdot \nabla_\theta Q(s_i, a_i; \, \theta) \,\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, 2 \left( r_i + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s'_i, a'; \, \theta^-) - Q(s_i, a_i; \, \theta) \right) \nabla_\theta Q(s_i, a_i; \, \theta) \,\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Update via Gradient Descent

$$\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \, \nabla_\theta L(\theta)$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate (typically $10^{-4}$ for DQN).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a target network $\theta^-$?** If we used the *same* network for both target and prediction, the target would shift on every gradient step, causing **non-stationary targets** and divergence. Freezing $\theta^-$ for, say, $C = 10{,}000$ steps stabilizes training.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why Each DRL Innovation Was Needed

| Problem in Naive Q-Learning | Symptom | DRL Solution | Math Trick |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Correlated consecutive samples | Loss of i.i.d. assumption, instability | **Experience Replay Buffer** | Sample mini-batches uniformly at random |
| Moving target on every step | Oscillation / divergence of $Q$-values | **Target Network** $\theta^-$ | Freeze weights for $C$ steps |
| Overestimation bias (max operator) | Inflated $Q$-values | **Double DQN** | Decouple action selection & evaluation |
| Sparse rewards | Agent never learns | **Reward Shaping / Curiosity (ICM)** | Add intrinsic $r^{\text{int}}_t$ |
| Discrete actions only | Fails for robotics | **DDPG / TD3** (Actor-Critic) | Deterministic Policy Gradient |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The DRL Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Agent] -->|Action a_t| B[Environment]
    B -->|Reward r_t and New State s_{t+1}| A
    A -->|Stores transition s_t a_t r_t s_{t+1}| C[Experience Replay Buffer D]
    C -->|Random mini-batch sampling| D[Deep Q-Network Q theta]
    D -->|Predicted Q-values for all actions| A
    E[Target Network Q theta_minus] -.->|Frozen target every C steps| D
    D -->|Loss L theta computed via Bellman MSE| F[SGD Optimizer]
    F -->|Update weights theta| D
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **agent** picks actions; the **environment** returns new states and rewards. Transitions are pushed into the **replay buffer** $\mathcal{D}$ and sampled randomly to train the **online DQN**. A **target network** (dashed arrow) provides the stable training target and is refreshed every $C$ gradient steps.

---

## 4.2 DQN Architecture Block Diagram (e.g., Atari Breakout)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        I1["Preprocessed 84x84x4 Frame Stack"]
    end

    subgraph ConvBlock1[Convolutional Block 1]
        C1["Conv2D 16 filters 8x8 stride 4 ReLU"]
    end

    subgraph ConvBlock2[Convolutional Block 2]
        C2["Conv2D 32 filters 4x4 stride 2 ReLU"]
    end

    subgraph DenseBlock[Fully Connected Block]
        F1["Flatten Layer"]
        F2["Dense 256 units ReLU"]
        F3["Dense num actions units Linear"]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        O1["Q-values for each discrete action"]
    end

    I1 --> C1 --> C2 --> F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> O1
```

> **Engineering Note:** For Atari, the input is a stack of 4 grayscale frames ($84 \times 84 \times 4$) so the network can infer *velocity* and *direction* — a single frame is Markov-incomplete.

---

## 4.3 Taxonomy of DRL Algorithms (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Deep Reinforcement Learning] --> M[Model Free]
    R --> N[Model Based]

    M --> V[Value Based]
    M --> P[Policy Based]
    M --> A[Actor Critic]

    V --> V1[DQN]
    V --> V2[Double DQN]
    V --> V3[Dueling DQN]
    V --> V4[Rainbow]

    P --> P1[REINFORCE]
    P --> P2[TRPO]
    P --> P3[PPO]

    A --> A1[A3C]
    A --> A2[DDPG]
    A --> A3[TD3]
    A --> A4[SAC]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Deep Reinforcement Learning. Mention two key challenges that classical (tabular) Q-learning cannot handle but DRL solves.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) is a machine-learning paradigm in which an agent learns an optimal policy by interacting with an environment, using a **deep neural network** as a function approximator for the value function $Q(s,a)$ or the policy $\pi(a \vert s)$.

> **Two challenges solved (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Curse of Dimensionality** — Tabular Q-learning cannot store $Q(s,a)$ when $\vert\mathcal{S}\vert$ is huge (e.g., pixel inputs). A deep network generalizes across similar states.
> 2. **Partial Observability & High-Dimensional Inputs** — Deep networks (CNNs, RNNs) can extract features from raw, unstructured inputs such as images, audio, or sensor streams.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q^*(s,a)$ and explain the role of the discount factor $\gamma$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Equation (2 marks):**
> $$Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{s'}\!\left[\,R(s,a) + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a')\,\right]$$
> It states that the optimal value of taking action $a$ in state $s$ equals the expected immediate reward plus the discounted optimal value of the next state.

> **Role of $\gamma$ (1 mark):** $\gamma \in [0,1]$ controls how much the agent values future rewards. If $\gamma = 0$, the agent is **myopic** (greedy on immediate reward). If $\gamma \to 1$, the agent becomes **far-sighted**, caring equally about distant future rewards. It also ensures the infinite geometric sum converges.

---

## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Derive the Bellman Optimality Equation for the action-value function $Q^*(s,a)$ from first principles. Clearly state the role of the Markov property in your derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Deep Q-Network (DQN). Why is the **target network** required during training? Show the DQN loss function and explain each term. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of Bellman Optimality Equation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Define the return $G_t$ (1 mark):**

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

**Step 2 — Define $Q^*$ as expected return (1 mark):**

$$Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, G_t \,\big\vert\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a\, \right]$$

**Step 3 — Split the return into immediate + future (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^*(s, a) &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots \,\big\vert\, s, a\, \right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1} \,\big\vert\, s, a\, \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Law of Total Expectation over $s'$ and Markov Property (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^*(s, a) &= \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a)\!\left[\, R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}[G_{t+1} \vert s'] \,\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{s'}\!\left[\, R(s, a) + \gamma \, V^*(s') \,\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $V^*(s') = \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$, we obtain the final equation:

$$Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{s'}\!\left[\, R(s, a) + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \,\right]$$

> **Role of Markov Property:** It guarantees that the future $G_{t+1}$ depends *only* on $s'$ and not on the entire history, which is what allows the recursive decomposition to be valid. **(1 mark)**

**(b) DQN Architecture and Loss (7 marks)**

**Architecture (3 marks):** The DQN takes the preprocessed state $s_t$ (e.g., a stack of 4 Atari frames of size $84 \times 84$) as input, passes it through two convolutional layers (feature extractor), flattens the result, and feeds it through a fully-connected layer of 256 ReLU units, finally outputting a vector of $Q$-values — one for each discrete action.

*(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2.)*

**Target Network — Why? (2 marks):** In the loss function

$$L(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\left( r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a'; \theta^-) - Q(s, a; \theta) \right)^2\right]$$

both $Q(s, a; \theta)$ and $Q(s', a'; \theta^-)$ depend on $\theta$. If we used the *same* $\theta$, the **target would shift on every gradient step**, creating a non-stationary supervised-learning problem and causing the loss to diverge. The target network's weights $\theta^-$ are **frozen** for $C$ steps (typically $C = 10{,}000$) and then copied from $\theta$. This stabilizes training dramatically.

**Loss Function Terms (2 marks):**
- $r$ : observed scalar reward.
- $\gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a'; \theta^-)$ : discounted predicted optimal future value.
- $Q(s, a; \theta)$ : network's current prediction.
- The squared error is minimized via SGD.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Compare **Value-Based**, **Policy-Based**, and **Actor-Critic** approaches in Deep Reinforcement Learning. Give one example algorithm for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Experience Replay** in DQN. How does it help overcome the **correlation problem** between consecutive training samples? Show mathematically how the loss is computed over a minibatch sampled from the replay buffer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison of DRL Paradigms (7 marks)**

| Feature | Value-Based | Policy-Based | Actor-Critic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| What is learned? | $Q(s, a; \theta)$ | $\pi(a \vert s; \theta)$ | Both $Q$ and $\pi$ |
| Action selection | $\arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ | Sampled from $\pi$ | Sampled from actor |
| Action space | Discrete (originally) | Discrete or continuous | Continuous (ideal) |
| Convergence | Can oscillate | High variance, stable convergence | Lower variance, faster |
| Example algorithm | **DQN** | **REINFORCE / PPO** | **A3C / DDPG** |
| On/Off-policy | Off-policy | On-policy (usually) | Both possible |

**Mark split:** 1 mark for tabular comparison, 1 mark per correct example, 2 marks for theoretical justification of trade-offs.

**(b) Experience Replay & Correlation (7 marks)**

**Concept (2 marks):** Experience Replay, introduced by Lin (1992) and popularized by Mnih et al. (2015), stores every transition $(s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1})$ in a buffer $\mathcal{D}$ of fixed capacity (e.g., $10^6$ transitions). During training, instead of using the *most recent* transition, the network is trained on a **random mini-batch** sampled uniformly from $\mathcal{D}$.

**Correlation Problem (2 marks):** Consecutive game frames are highly correlated. Training a neural network on correlated samples violates the **i.i.d. assumption** of stochastic gradient descent, causing the network to overfit to recent trajectories and "forget" older experiences (catastrophic forgetting). Random sampling decorrelates updates and stabilizes learning.

**Minibatch Loss (3 marks):** Given a minibatch of $N$ transitions $\{(s_i, a_i, r_i, s'_i)\}_{i=1}^N$ sampled from $\mathcal{D}$:

$$L(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( r_i + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s'_i, a'; \theta^-) - Q(s_i, a_i; \theta) \right)^2$$

> The gradient $\nabla_\theta L(\theta)$ is backpropagated, and weights are updated via SGD or Adam.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Forgetting the discount factor** $\gamma$ in the target — leads to **–1 to –2 marks** deduction in derivations.
> 2. **Confusing $\theta$ and $\theta^-$** — the online and target networks are *separate*; many students write the same $\theta$ for both and lose **2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up Bellman equations** for $V^*$ and $Q^*$ — examiners will deduct **1 mark** if the wrong recursion is written.
> 4. **Omitting the Markov property assumption** in derivations — this is a **mandatory** justification step worth 1 mark.
> 5. **Writing "DQN uses supervised learning"** — DQN is a *regression toward a moving target derived from RL*. Writing that it is "pure supervised learning" costs **1 mark** because it is conceptually incorrect.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Deep Reinforcement Learning**

- **DRL = Deep Learning + Reinforcement Learning.** Deep NN replaces the $Q$-table.
- **Foundational Paper:** Mnih et al. (2015, *Nature*) — DQN on Atari.
- **MDP Tuple:** $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$.
- **Goal:** Maximize $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.
- **Bellman Optimality (Q):** $Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')\right]$.
- **DQN Loss:** $L(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\left( r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a'; \theta^-) - Q(s, a; \theta) \right)^2 \right]$.
- **Two Pillars of DQN Stability:**
  1. **Experience Replay Buffer** $\mathcal{D}$ → decorrelates samples.
  2. **Target Network** $\theta^-$ → freezes moving target every $C$ steps.
- **Epsilon-greedy exploration:** with probability $\varepsilon$ pick random action, else $\arg\max_a Q(s,a)$; $\varepsilon$ decays from $1.0$ to $0.1$.
- **Three DRL Families:** Value-Based (DQN), Policy-Based (REINFORCE, PPO), Actor-Critic (A3C, DDPG, SAC).
- **On-policy** = learns from the policy it executes (PPO); **Off-policy** = learns from any policy's data (DQN).
- **KTU favorite exam hooks:** *derive Bellman equation*, *explain target network*, *write DQN loss with all terms*, *compare value-based vs policy-based*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suitability of Reinforcement Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Suitability of Reinforcement Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is a machine learning paradigm concerned with how an **intelligent agent** ought to take **sequential actions** in an **environment** so as to **maximize the notion of cumulative reward**, learned through **trial-and-error interaction** rather than from explicit labelled supervision.

**Suitability of Reinforcement Learning** refers to the set of problem characteristics, environmental constraints, and learning objectives that determine whether an RL formulation is the **most appropriate and tractable** approach for solving a given engineering or decision-making problem.

A problem is said to be *suitable for RL* if it can be formally modeled as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** satisfying the Markov property, where:

$$
P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \dots) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The first module establishes the *situational awareness* required before applying RL. Students must identify *when to use RL* and distinguish it clearly from **Supervised Learning (SL)** and **Unsupervised Learning (UL)** paradigms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine teaching a **toddler to walk**. You don't give them a labelled dataset of "correct muscle movements." You don't cluster their falls into groups. Instead:

- The toddler **tries** an action (taking a step).
- The environment responds with a **new state** (still standing, or fallen).
- The toddler receives a **reward signal** (applause for staying upright, a thud for falling).
- Over thousands of trials, the toddler **learns a policy** (a strategy) of muscle coordination that maximizes long-term balance.

This is precisely what RL does — **learning optimal behavior from consequences**, not from explicit instructions.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Summary:** Use RL when the problem is about *learning what to do* (sequential decision-making), not just *learning what something is* (classification/regression) or *learning the structure of data* (clustering).

### 1.3 Core Components That Define RL Suitability

A problem becomes suitable for RL when the following **five canonical elements** are identifiable:

1. **Agent** — the decision-maker (e.g., a robot, a trading algorithm, a game AI).
2. **Environment** — everything external to the agent that it interacts with.
3. **State ($s_t$)** — a complete description of the environment at time $t$.
4. **Action ($a_t$)** — the set of moves available to the agent.
5. **Reward ($r_t$)** — the scalar feedback signal the environment provides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Reward Hypothesis (Sutton & Barto, 2018):** *All goals can be described by the maximization of the expected cumulative scalar reward signal.* If your problem cannot be expressed in terms of a scalar reward, RL may not be the natural fit.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

While RL is largely computational, certain standard metrics are used to quantify suitability:

- **Discount Factor ($\gamma$)** — typically $\gamma \in [0, 1]$, with **$\gamma = 0.99$** being a common default in engineering applications.
- **Exploration Probability ($\epsilon$)** — for $\epsilon$-greedy policies, typical range is **$\epsilon \in [0.01, 0.1]$**.
- **Learning Rate ($\alpha$)** — for tabular Q-learning, often **$\alpha = 0.1$** as a default.

These are **hyperparameters**, not physical constants, but they are the standard engineering metrics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind RL Suitability

A problem is **suitable for RL** when **all** of the following conditions are simultaneously met:

- **Sequentiality:** Decisions made at time $t$ affect future states and rewards at $t+1, t+2, \dots$ The problem is not a one-shot decision.
- **Incomplete Information / Trial-and-Error Necessity:** The agent does not possess a complete model of the environment's transition dynamics. Learning must occur by *doing*.
- **Well-defined Scalar Reward:** The objective can be quantified as a scalar reward signal (even if delayed).
- **Markov Property Approximation:** The current state contains sufficient information to make an optimal decision — the past is conditionally independent of the future given the present.
- **Repeated Interaction:** The agent can interact with the environment (or a high-fidelity simulator) many thousands or millions of times.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Exploration-Exploitation Trade-off** is the *central challenge* of RL. The agent must balance:
> - **Exploration:** Trying new actions to discover their rewards.
> - **Exploitation:** Using known actions that yield high reward.
>
> This trade-off has **no counterpart in supervised or unsupervised learning**, and its presence is a strong indicator of RL suitability.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Equation / Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Boundary / Default** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Markov Property | $P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, \dots) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$ | Future depends only on present | Holds for MDPs |
| Cumulative Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}$ | Total discounted future reward | $\gamma \in [0,1]$ |
| Bellman Optimality | $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) V^{*}(s') \right]$ | Optimal state-value function | Recursive, converges for $\gamma < 1$ |
| Q-Value Function | $Q^{*}(s,a) = R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s',a')$ | Optimal action-value | Tabular or approximated |
| Policy | $\pi(a \mid s) = P(a_t = a \mid s_t = s)$ | Agent's behavior strategy | Stochastic or deterministic |
| Exploration Rate | $\epsilon$ in $\epsilon$-greedy | Probability of random action | Decays from **1.0 to 0.01** |
| Discounted Objective | $\max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^{t} r_t \right]$ | RL optimization target | Infinite horizon if $T \to \infty$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Markdown Tables:** The vertical bar symbol `$\vert$` in mathematical notation has been replaced with absolute clarity to avoid breaking the markdown table rendering. All conditioning bars use a clean horizontal rendering.

### 2.3 The Three Paradigms of Machine Learning — A Decision Lens

| **Paradigm** | **Feedback Type** | **Data Form** | **Decision Nature** | **Example Task** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Supervised Learning** | Labelled (input–output pairs) | Static dataset | One-shot prediction | Image classification |
| **Unsupervised Learning** | No feedback | Static dataset | Pattern discovery | Customer clustering |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | Scalar reward (delayed) | Sequential, interactive | Sequential decision-making | Game playing, robotics |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse RL with supervised learning because both use feedback. The fundamental difference is: **SL uses ground-truth labels provided upfront**; **RL uses reward signals that depend on the agent's own actions** and are often *delayed*.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Identifying RL Suitability

Properly identifying RL suitability is critical in:

- **Robotics and Autonomous Systems:** Path planning, manipulation, locomotion — all sequential, all reward-driven.
- **Industrial Process Control:** Where actions now affect future system dynamics.
- **Resource Management:** Data center cooling (Google's DeepMind case), smart grids, traffic signal optimization.
- **Finance:** Portfolio rebalancing under uncertainty.
- **Healthcare Treatment Policies:** Sequential intervention planning.
- **Game AI and Strategy:** Chess, Go, Atari, StarCraft.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Insight:** If a problem can be solved by *reacting to a label*, use SL. If it can be solved by *finding structure in data*, use UL. **Only when the problem is about *acting optimally over time* under uncertainty should you reach for RL.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix — Is Your Problem Suitable for RL?

A rigorous, multi-criteria evaluation is required to determine RL suitability. The following derivation establishes the **mathematical threshold** for each criterion.

#### 3.1.1 The Markov Property Verification

A problem is *Markovian* if for **all** states $s, s'$ and actions $a$:

$$
P(s_{t+1} = s' \mid s_t = s, a_t = a, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \dots) = P(s_{t+1} = s' \mid s_t = s, a_t = a)
$$

**Derivation Logic:**

- *Step 1:* Assume history $H_t = (s_0, a_0, s_1, a_1, \dots, s_t, a_t)$ contains the full interaction history.
- *Step 2:* The conditional transition probability is $P(s_{t+1} \mid H_t)$.
- *Step 3:* The Markov property claims $P(s_{t+1} \mid H_t) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$.
- *Step 4:* This means **$s_t$ is a sufficient statistic** of $H_t$ for predicting the next state.
- *Step 5:* If the property fails, the state representation is **incomplete** — additional features must be engineered into the state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** If the natural state of the problem does not satisfy the Markov property, you can *force* it to by augmenting the state with the necessary history. This is called **state augmentation** or **frame stacking**.

#### 3.1.2 The Cumulative Return and Its Mathematical Justification

The objective of an RL agent is to maximize the **expected discounted return**:

$$
G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} r_{t+3} + \dots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}
$$

**Why discount by $\gamma$?**

- **Step 1 (Mathematical Necessity):** If rewards are bounded ($\vert r_t \vert \leq R_{\max}$) and $\gamma < 1$, the infinite geometric series converges:
$$
G_t \leq \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{\max} = \frac{R_{\max}}{1 - \gamma}
$$
- **Step 2 (Economic Rationale):** A reward received now is worth more than the same reward received later (time value of utility).
- **Step 3 (Uncertainty Rationale):** Future rewards are uncertain; discounting models this uncertainty.
- **Step 4 (Boundary Cases):**
  - If $\gamma = 0$: The agent is **myopic** (greedy), caring only about immediate reward.
  - If $\gamma = 1$: The agent is **far-sighted**; all future rewards weighted equally (only converges if the task is episodic with finite horizon).

#### 3.1.3 The Bellman Optimality Equation — Derivation

The state-value function under policy $\pi$ is defined as:

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_t \mid s_t = s \right]
$$

Expanding the expectation one time-step:

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid s_t = s \right]
$$

Separating the immediate reward and the future return:

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_{t+1} \mid s_{t+1} = s' \right] \right]
$$

Recognizing the inner expectation as $V^{\pi}(s')$:

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') \right]
$$

For the **optimal** policy $\pi^{*}$, we take the maximum over actions:

$$
V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{*}(s') \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence Guarantee:** The Bellman operator $T$ defined by the right-hand side is a **$\gamma$-contraction mapping** in the supremum norm. By the **Banach Fixed-Point Theorem**, repeated application of $T$ converges to a unique $V^{*}$, provided $\gamma < 1$. This is the theoretical foundation of **Value Iteration** algorithms.

#### 3.1.4 Suitability Score — A Composite Metric

Engineers often use a composite suitability score to *quantify* how well RL fits a problem. Define:

$$
S_{\text{RL}} = w_1 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{sequential}} + w_2 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{Markov}} + w_3 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{reward}} + w_4 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{interactive}} - w_5 \cdot C_{\text{sample}}
$$

Where:

- $\mathbb{1}_{\text{condition}}$ is an indicator (1 if the condition holds, 0 otherwise).
- $w_1, \dots, w_5$ are non-negative weights summing to 1 (e.g., $w_i = 0.2$).
- $C_{\text{sample}} \in [0,1]$ is the **relative sample complexity cost**.

**Interpretation:**

- If $S_{\text{RL}} \geq 0.6$: **Strong RL suitability.**
- If $0.3 \leq S_{\text{RL}} < 0.6$: **RL is feasible but may need engineering effort.**
- If $S_{\text{RL}} < 0.3$: **Consider supervised, unsupervised, or classical control methods first.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** This composite score is a *pedagogical construct* for KTU examination purposes. It is not a universally standardized metric in RL literature, but it is a rigorous, defensible engineering heuristic derived from the canonical RL problem definition.

### 3.2 When RL is *NOT* Suitable — The Exclusion Conditions

| **Exclusion Condition** | **Reason** | **Alternative Paradigm** |
|---|---|---|
| Large labelled dataset exists; one-shot prediction needed | RL wastes sequential interaction | Supervised Learning |
| Goal is to discover hidden structure, no decision involved | No agent, no reward | Unsupervised Learning |
| Reward signal is undefined or non-scalar | Reward hypothesis violated | SL with custom loss, or inverse RL |
| Environment is non-stationary and unbounded | MDP assumption breaks | Meta-learning, online learning |
| Sample interaction is extremely costly or dangerous | Trial-and-error infeasible | Simulators (if available) or imitation learning |
| Full transition model $P(s' \mid s,a)$ is known exactly | No need to learn; use planning | Dynamic Programming / MPC |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The RL Agent-Environment Interaction Loop

This diagram illustrates the canonical **RL interaction cycle**, which is the *defining structural feature* of any RL-suitable problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    agentA[AGENT] -->|"ACTION a_t"| envB[ENVIRONMENT]
    envB -->|"REWARD r_{t+1}"| agentA
    envB -->|"NEW STATE s_{t+1}"| agentA
    agentA -->|"UPDATES POLICY PI"| policyC[POLICY PI]
    policyC -->|"ACTION a_t"| agentA
    style agentA fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style envB fill:#A8D5BA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style policyC fill:#F4A6A6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **Agent** takes an **action** $a_t$ based on its current **policy** $\pi$.
- The **Environment** receives the action and transitions to a **new state** $s_{t+1}$.
- The Environment emits a **reward** $r_{t+1}$ back to the Agent.
- The Agent uses $(s_{t+1}, r_{t+1})$ to **update its policy**.
- The cycle repeats at $t = t+1$.

### 4.2 Decision Block Diagram — Is RL the Right Choice?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[PROBLEM IDENTIFIED] --> checkB{IS THE PROBLEM SEQUENTIAL?}
    checkB -->|NO| exitC[USE SUPERVISED OR UNSUPERVISED LEARNING]
    checkB -->|YES| checkD{CAN THE GOAL BE EXPRESSED AS A SCALAR REWARD?}
    checkD -->|NO| exitE[USE INVERSE RL OR REWARD SHAPING]
    checkD -->|YES| checkF{IS THE ENVIRONMENT INTERACTIVE OR SIMULABLE?}
    checkF -->|NO| exitG[USE IMITATION LEARNING OR OFFLINE RL]
    checkF -->|YES| checkH{APPROXIMATELY SATISFIES MARKOV PROPERTY?}
    checkH -->|NO| exitI[AUGMENT STATE OR USE POMDP]
    checkH -->|YES| acceptJ[REINFORCEMENT LEARNING IS SUITABLE]
    style startA fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333
    style exitC fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#333
    style exitE fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#333
    style exitG fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#333
    style exitI fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#333
    style acceptJ fill:#B3FFB3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

**Interpretation:** This is a **top-down decision flowchart** for determining RL suitability. The green block at the end is the affirmative terminal — RL is the recommended paradigm. Each red block represents a *failure mode* where RL is unsuitable and an alternative must be chosen.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RL System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSOR_LAYER[PERCEPTION LAYER]
        obs1[SENSORS AND OBSERVATIONS]
    end
    subgraph STATE_REP[STATE REPRESENTATION MODULE]
        rep1[STATE ENCODER F S T]
    end
    subgraph POLICY_ENGINE[POLICY AND VALUE ENGINE]
        pol1[POLICY NETWORK PI A S]
        val1[VALUE FUNCTION V S OR Q S A]
    end
    subgraph LEARNING_CORE[LEARNING CORE]
        alg1[REINFORCEMENT ALGORITHM]
        buf1[EXPERIENCE REPLAY BUFFER]
    end
    subgraph ACTUATION[ACTUATION LAYER]
        act1[ACTION EXECUTOR]
    end
    obs1 --> rep1
    rep1 --> pol1
    rep1 --> val1
    pol1 --> act1
    act1 --> obs1
    alg1 --> pol1
    alg1 --> val1
    buf1 --> alg1
    style SENSOR_LAYER fill:#FFF4CC,stroke:#333
    style STATE_REP fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#333
    style POLICY_ENGINE fill:#E0CCFF,stroke:#333
    style LEARNING_CORE fill:#FFD9CC,stroke:#333
    style ACTUATION fill:#CCFFCC,stroke:#333
```

**Architectural Notes:**

- The **Perception Layer** collects raw observations from the environment.
- The **State Representation Module** encodes observations into a Markovian state.
- The **Policy and Value Engine** maintains the agent's decision-making logic.
- The **Learning Core** updates the policy and value estimates from experience.
- The **Actuation Layer** converts the agent's chosen action into real-world effect.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Q1. Define Reinforcement Learning and list any two characteristics of problems suitable for RL.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Reinforcement Learning is a machine learning paradigm in which an **agent learns to make sequential decisions** by interacting with an **environment** to **maximize a cumulative scalar reward signal**, learning through **trial-and-error** rather than from explicit supervision.

**Two characteristics of RL-suitable problems:**

1. **Sequential Decision-Making:** The current action affects future states and rewards. The problem cannot be reduced to a one-shot prediction.
2. **Trial-and-Error Learning:** The agent must explore unknown actions to discover their consequences, as the transition and reward models are not known a priori.

*(Acceptable additional characteristics: Markov property, delayed reward, exploration-exploitation trade-off, interaction with an environment/simulator.)*

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between Supervised Learning and Reinforcement Learning in terms of feedback signal and decision nature.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Supervised Learning** | **Reinforcement Learning** |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback Signal** | Explicit, ground-truth labels provided upfront for each input | Scalar reward signal emitted by the environment, often *delayed* in time |
| **Decision Nature** | One-shot prediction (input $\to$ output) | Sequential decision-making (state $\to$ action $\to$ next state $\to$ ...) |
| **Data Source** | Static, pre-collected labelled dataset | Generated by the agent's own interaction with the environment |
| **Goal** | Minimize prediction error on the dataset | Maximize expected cumulative reward over a trajectory |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme, ESE Module 1]**

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the fundamental components of a Reinforcement Learning system. Discuss how the **exploration-exploitation trade-off** makes RL distinct from supervised and unsupervised learning. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A robotic arm is tasked with picking objects from a conveyor belt and placing them in bins based on color. Discuss whether this problem is **suitable for RL** by evaluating it against the canonical RL components. *(7 Marks)*

---

**(a) Model Solution — RL Components and Exploration-Exploitation:**

The five canonical components of an RL system are:

1. **Agent:** The learner and decision-maker (e.g., the algorithm controlling the robotic arm).
2. **Environment:** Everything external to the agent that responds to its actions (e.g., the conveyor belt, the bins, the physical world).
3. **State ($s_t$):** A representation of the environment at time $t$ (e.g., object position, color sensor reading).
4. **Action ($a_t$):** The set of moves the agent can take (e.g., gripper open/close, joint angles, arm trajectory).
5. **Reward ($r_t$):** A scalar feedback signal (e.g., +1 for correct bin, -1 for incorrect, 0 otherwise).

**Exploration vs. Exploitation:**

- **Exploitation** uses the agent's current knowledge to select the action believed to yield the highest reward (greedy choice based on current Q-values).
- **Exploration** selects seemingly suboptimal actions to gather information about their rewards, potentially discovering better long-term strategies.

This trade-off is **unique to RL** because:
- In **supervised learning**, the optimal mapping is given by the labels — there is nothing to "explore."
- In **unsupervised learning**, there is no notion of action or consequence.
- Only in RL does the agent's choice of action **change the data it receives**, creating a closed loop between policy and experience.

A common strategy to balance this is the **$\epsilon$-greedy policy**:

$$
\pi(a \mid s) = \begin{cases} \arg\max_{a} Q(s,a) & \text{with probability } 1 - \epsilon \\ \text{random action} & \text{with probability } \epsilon \end{cases}
$$

Typically, $\epsilon$ is **annealed** from 1.0 (full exploration) down to **0.01** (near-full exploitation) over training.

---

**(b) Model Solution — Suitability Analysis for the Robotic Arm:**

| **RL Suitability Criterion** | **Present in this Problem?** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| **Agent** | Yes | The robotic arm controller is the agent. |
| **Environment** | Yes | The conveyor belt, sensors, and bins constitute the environment. |
| **Sequential Decision-Making** | Yes | The arm must decide sequence of joint movements; one bad move affects the next. |
| **Scalar Reward** | Yes (engineerable) | Define $r = +1$ for correct bin, $r = -1$ for wrong bin, $r = 0$ otherwise. |
| **Markov Property (approx.)** | Yes (with care) | State = (object color, object position, arm pose) — sufficient for next-state prediction. |
| **Interactive or Simulated** | Yes | Real robot + physical environment, or a high-fidelity simulator (e.g., PyBullet, MuJoCo). |
| **Trial-and-Error Feasibility** | Yes | Mistakes are non-destructive and inexpensive in simulation. |

**Verdict:** The problem is **highly suitable for RL**, specifically a formulation as a **finite-state, finite-action MDP** solvable by **Q-learning** or **Deep Q-Networks (DQN)** if the state space is large.

**Sample Complexity Note:** A real robot may need 10,000–1,000,000 episodes of training. To avoid wear, training is recommended in simulation with **domain randomization**, followed by **sim-to-real transfer**.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** Derive the **Bellman Optimality Equation** starting from the definition of the state-value function $V^{\pi}(s)$. State the conditions under which the equation has a unique solution. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A medical diagnosis system must recommend a sequence of tests for a patient based on symptoms, and the cost of each test must be minimized while diagnostic accuracy is maximized. Discuss whether **RL is suitable** or whether a **supervised learning** approach would be preferable. Justify your answer with reference to the **reward hypothesis** and **sequentiality**. *(7 Marks)*

---

**(a) Model Solution — Bellman Optimality Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
The state-value function under policy $\pi$ is the expected return from state $s$ following $\pi$:

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_t \mid s_t = s \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the return:**

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} r_{t+3} + \dots \mid s_t = s \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Group immediate and future reward:**

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid s_t = s \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Expand expectation over actions and next states:**
Let $\pi(a \mid s)$ be the policy and $P(s' \mid s, a)$ be the transition probability:

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s,a,s') + \gamma \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_{t+1} \mid s_{t+1} = s' \right] \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the inner expectation as $V^{\pi}(s')$:**

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Apply optimality (max over actions):**
For the optimal policy $\pi^{*}$, the agent always picks the action maximizing value:

$$
V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^{*}(s') \right]
$$

**Conditions for unique solution:**

1. The state space $\mathcal{S}$ and action space $\mathcal{A}$ are **finite** (or the function approximator is bounded).
2. The discount factor satisfies $\gamma < 1$ (ensures the Bellman operator is a $\gamma$-contraction in the supremum norm).
3. The reward function $R(s, a, s')$ is **bounded**.

By the **Banach Fixed-Point Theorem**, repeated application of the Bellman optimality operator converges to a unique $V^{*}$.

---

**(b) Model Solution — Medical Test Recommendation: RL vs. SL:**

**Sequentiality Analysis:**

- The choice of test $k$ depends on the **outcome of test $k-1$** (e.g., if blood test is positive, order imaging next; if negative, skip it).
- The decision is therefore **inherently sequential**, not a one-shot prediction.
- Each test incurs a cost and provides information that affects the next decision.
- This satisfies the sequentiality criterion for RL.

**Reward Hypothesis Analysis:**

- A **scalar reward** can be engineered:
$$
r_t = \mathbb{1}_{\text{correct diagnosis}} \cdot W_{\text{acc}} - c_t \cdot W_{\text{cost}}
$$
where $W_{\text{acc}}, W_{\text{cost}}$ are trade-off weights, and $c_t$ is the cost of the $t$-th test.
- Maximizing cumulative $\sum r_t$ balances **accuracy and cost** over the test sequence — exactly the RL objective.

**Why RL is preferable here:**

- **SL limitation:** A supervised model would predict a *single* test recommendation given symptoms, but cannot natively model the *long-horizon trade-off* between current test cost and future diagnostic value.
- **RL advantage:** RL explicitly optimizes the **expected cumulative reward**, naturally balancing short-term cost vs. long-term diagnostic accuracy through the discount factor $\gamma$.

**Verdict:** **RL is suitable** — specifically as an MDP with state = (current symptoms, test history), actions = (which test to order), and reward = (accuracy - cost) trade-off.

**Caveat:** If a large dataset of *expert test sequences* exists, **Imitation Learning (Behavioral Cloning)** could bootstrap the RL agent, addressing the cold-start problem.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on "Suitability of RL" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating RL with Supervised Learning (Major Pitfall):** Many students incorrectly state that "RL uses labelled data." RL uses *reward signals*, not labels. **Marks lost: up to 2 marks per occurrence.**
> 2. **Forgetting the Markov Property:** When asked "is this problem suitable for RL?", students often skip the verification that the state representation is Markovian. **Marks lost: 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Ignoring Sample Complexity:** Real-world RL needs thousands to millions of interactions. Students who say "just use RL" without acknowledging simulation needs or sample cost lose marks. **Marks lost: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Omitting the Exploration-Exploitation Trade-off:** A core *defining* feature of RL. Any answer that omits this on a 7-mark sub-question loses **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **Not Defining the Reward Function Explicitly:** A vague "RL will learn the optimal policy" answer is incomplete. You **must** state the form of $r_t$. **Marks lost: 1–2 marks.**
> 6. **Confusing $V(s)$ with $Q(s,a)$:** $V$ is the value of a *state*; $Q$ is the value of a *state-action pair*. Mixing these in derivations costs marks. **Marks lost: 1 mark per misuse.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** RL is a *sequential decision-making* paradigm where an **agent** learns an **optimal policy** by interacting with an **environment** to **maximize cumulative scalar reward**.
- **Five Canonical Components:** Agent, Environment, State, Action, Reward. If a problem has all five, it is a strong RL candidate.
- **The Markov Property** is a prerequisite: $P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, H_t) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$.
- **The Exploration-Exploitation Trade-off** is the *hallmark* of RL and has no counterpart in SL or UL.
- **The Reward Hypothesis** (Sutton & Barto): all RL goals reduce to maximizing expected cumulative reward. If you cannot define a scalar reward, RL is the wrong tool.
- **Discount Factor ($\gamma < 1$):** Ensures mathematical convergence and models preference for immediate over delayed rewards.
- **Bellman Optimality Equation:** Recursive definition of $V^{*}(s)$ that admits a unique solution by the Banach Fixed-Point Theorem when $\gamma < 1$.
- **RL is unsuitable** when: (i) one-shot prediction suffices, (ii) no reward can be defined, (iii) interaction is impossible and no simulator exists, (iv) full transition model is known (use Dynamic Programming / MPC instead).
- **Composite Suitability Score $S_{\text{RL}}$:** A weighted sum of binary indicators over the canonical RL criteria; a practical engineering heuristic.
- **Real-world RL is sample-hungry:** Prefer simulation + domain randomization + sim-to-real transfer for physical systems.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** This topic is foundational — every later module (Q-learning, Policy Gradient, etc.) assumes you can identify *when* and *why* RL is the right paradigm.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of Reinforcement Learning - Agent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Components of Reinforcement Learning - Agent ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the formal framework of **Reinforcement Learning (RL)**, an **Agent** is defined as the **decision-making and learning entity** that interacts with an **environment** by perceiving its **state**, selecting **actions** through a **policy**, and receiving **rewards** that it uses to optimize its long-term cumulative objective. The agent is the *actor* in the Markov Decision Process (MDP) tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle$. Mathematically, the agent can be characterized as a function or mapping that, at each discrete time step $t$, observes a state $S_t \in \mathcal{S}$, selects an action $A_t \in \mathcal{A}$ according to a policy $\pi$, and receives a reward $R_{t+1} \in \mathbb{R}$ along with the next state $S_{t+1}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > The agent is one of the **four primary components** of an RL system, alongside the **Environment**, the **Reward Signal**, and the **Policy/Value Function**. Mastering the agent's internal structure (policy, value function, and optional model) is essential for the ESE and continuous assessment. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Dog and the Treat" Imagine a **puppy (the agent)** placed inside a house (the **environment**). The puppy does not know the layout. It can sit, stand, walk, or bark (**actions**). The owner watches the puppy and gives a **biscuit (reward)** when it performs a desired action, like fetching a ball, and says "No" (negative reward) when it chews the shoe. The puppy gradually learns: - **What works** (more biscuits) → repeat it - **What fails** (no reward or scolding) → avoid it The puppy is the **agent** — it perceives the world (state), decides (policy), acts, and learns from feedback (reward). Over time, the puppy develops an internal *strategy* to maximize biscuits — this strategy is its **policy**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** Unlike Supervised Learning where a teacher provides correct answers, the agent in RL must *discover* which actions yield the most reward through **trial-and-error interaction**. ### 1.3 Core Components Internal to the Agent | Sub-Component | Symbol | Role | |---------------|--------|------| | Policy | $\pi(a \mid s)$ | The agent's behavior function — maps states to actions | | Value Function | $V_\pi(s)$ or $Q_\pi(s,a)$ | Estimates long-term expected return from a state | | Model (Optional) | $\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}$ | Agent's internal representation of environment dynamics | ### 1.4 Classification of Agents (KTU High-Yield) Agents are broadly classified based on what they learn or use: 1. **Value-Based Agent** — Learns value functions (e.g., Q-Learning, SARSA, DQN). 2. **Policy-Based Agent** — Learns the policy directly (e.g., REINFORCE, Policy Gradient). 3. **Actor-Critic Agent** — Combines both policy (actor) and value (critic) (e.g., A2C, PPO). 4. **Model-Based Agent** — Uses an internal model of the environment (e.g., AlphaZero, Dyna). 5. **Model-Free Agent** — Learns purely from interaction without modeling dynamics. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Agent-Environment Interaction Loop At every discrete time step $t$, the following sequence occurs: 1. The **environment** is in state $S_t \in \mathcal{S}$. 2. The **agent** observes $S_t$ (possibly a partial observation $O_t$). 3. The **agent** selects an action $A_t \in \mathcal{A}(S_t)$ based on its policy $\pi$. 4. The **environment** transitions to a new state $S_{t+1}$ according to transition probability $\mathcal{P}(S_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t)$. 5. The **environment** emits a reward $R_{t+1} = \mathcal{R}(S_t, A_t, S_{t+1})$. 6. The **agent** updates its internal estimates (policy/value) using this tuple $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$. ### 2.2 Types of Policies Inside the Agent > [!NOTE] > **Deterministic Policy:** $\pi(s) = a$ — directly maps a state to one specific action. > **Stochastic Policy:** $\pi(a \mid s) = \mathbb{P}(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$ — outputs a probability distribution over actions. The stochastic policy is more general and is required when the environment is non-deterministic or partially observable. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Description | |---|---------|---------|-------------| | 1 | Deterministic Policy | $\pi : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ | Direct state-to-action mapping | | 2 | Stochastic Policy | $\pi(a \mid s) = \mathbb{P}(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$ | Probability of action $a$ in state $s$ | | 3 | State-Value Function | $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]$ | Expected return starting from $s$ under $\pi$ | | 4 | Action-Value Function | $Q_\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$ | Expected return from taking $a$ in $s$ | | 5 | Return (Discounted) | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Cumulative discounted future reward | | 6 | Bellman Optimality (V) | $V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a) \left[ r + \gamma V^*(s') \right]$ | Optimal value equation | | 7 | Bellman Optimality (Q) | $Q^*(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a) \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a') \right]$ | Optimal action-value equation | | 8 | Bellman Expectation (V) | $V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$ | Value under policy $\pi$ | | 9 | Bellman Expectation (Q) | $Q_\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a) \left[ r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q_\pi(s',a') \right]$ | Q under policy $\pi$ | | 10 | Discount Factor Range | $0 \leq \gamma \leq 1$ | $\gamma = 0$: myopic; $\gamma \to 1$: far-sighted | | 11 | Epsilon-Greedy | $\pi(a \mid s) = 1 - \epsilon + \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$ for $a^*$; $\frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$ otherwise | Exploration-exploitation balance | | 12 | Softmax (Boltzmann) | $\pi(a \mid s) = \frac{\exp(Q(s,a)/\tau)}{\sum_{a'} \exp(Q(s,a')/\tau)}$ | Probabilistic action selection | | 13 | Advantage Function | $A_\pi(s,a) = Q_\pi(s,a) - V_\pi(s)$ | Measures how good $a$ is vs. average | | 14 | Policy Gradient Objective | $J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta} \left[ \sum_t \gamma^t R_t \right]$ | Maximize expected return w.r.t. $\theta$ | | 15 | REINFORCE Update | $\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \, G_t \, \nabla_\theta \ln \pi_\theta(A_t \mid S_t)$ | Monte-Carlo policy gradient | | 16 | Temporal-Difference Error | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ | TD learning error signal | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Agent-based RL is deployed in: - **Robotics** — manipulator control, autonomous navigation (Boston Dynamics, DeepMind locomotion). - **Autonomous Vehicles** — lane keeping, decision-making in traffic. - **Game AI** — AlphaGo, AlphaZero, OpenAI Five (Dota 2). - **Recommender Systems** — Netflix, YouTube content personalization. - **Industrial Control** — HVAC optimization, data center cooling (Google DeepMind reduced cooling energy by ~40%). - **Finance** — portfolio allocation, algorithmic trading. - **Healthcare** — dynamic treatment regimes. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Bellman Equation to Agent's Update Rule **Step 1 — Define the Return.** The cumulative discounted return from time $t$ is: $$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$ **Step 2 — Define the State-Value Function.** The agent's expected return starting from state $s$ under policy $\pi$: $$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]$$ **Step 3 — Expand Using the Law of Iterated Expectations.** $$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$ **Step 4 — Separate Immediate and Future Reward.** $$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right] + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$ **Step 5 — Condition on Action and Next State.** Using the law of total probability over actions $a$ and next states $s'$: $$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s' \right] \right]$$ **Step 6 — Recognize Inner Expectation as $V_\pi(s')$.** $$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$$ **Step 7 — This is the Bellman Expectation Equation for $V_\pi$.** $$\boxed{V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]}$$ This recursive relationship is the **foundation of all value-based agents** and is the *key equation* expected in KTU exam answers. ### 3.2 Derivation: The Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q^*(s,a)$ Starting from $Q_\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$: $$Q_\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q_\pi(s', a') \right]$$ The optimal policy $\pi^*$ picks the action that **maximizes** $Q$: $$\pi^*(a' \mid s') = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } a' = \arg\max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ Substituting into the Bellman expectation gives the **Bellman Optimality Equation**: $$\boxed{Q^*(s,a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \right]}$$ This is the equation solved (approximately) by **Q-Learning** agents, which is a model-free value-based agent. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Agent Types | Property | Value-Based Agent | Policy-Based Agent | Actor-Critic Agent | Model-Based Agent | |----------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------|-------------------| | Learns | $V$ or $Q$ | $\pi_\theta$ directly | Both $\pi_\theta$ and $V_\phi$ | $\pi$, $V$, and model $\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}$ | | Action Space | Discrete (typically) | Continuous + Discrete | Continuous + Discrete | Both | | Sample Efficiency | Low (model-free) | Low | Medium | High | | Convergence | Stable for tabular | High variance | Balanced | Requires model accuracy | | Example Algorithm | Q-Learning, SARSA, DQN | REINFORCE | A2C, PPO, DDPG | AlphaZero, Dyna, MBPO | | Pros | Simple, well-studied | Handles continuous actions | Reduces variance | Fast learning | | Cons | Fails for high-dim/continuous | Slow, noisy | More hyperparameters | Model bias | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Agent-Environment Interaction Loop (Mermaid Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Environment
State S_t] nodeB[Agent
Observes S_t] nodeC{Policy pi} nodeD[Action A_t] nodeE[Reward R_t+1] nodeF[New State S_t+1] nodeG[Value Function V or Q] nodeH[Update Policy/Value] nodeA -->|Observation| nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC -->|Selects| nodeD nodeD -->|Apply| nodeA nodeA -->|Emit| nodeE nodeA -->|Transition| nodeF nodeF -->|Feedback| nodeB nodeB --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeC ``` ### 4.2 Internal Architecture of a Reinforcement Learning Agent ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[Input: State S_t] nodeB[Policy Network pi_a_given_s] nodeC[Value Network V_s or Q_sa] nodeD[Memory Replay Buffer] nodeE[Model P_sprime_a and R] nodeF[Output: Action A_t] nodeG[Learning Module: Update Weights] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeB --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeG nodeC --> nodeG nodeE --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeB nodeG --> nodeC ``` ### 4.3 Classification Hierarchy of Agents ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[RL Agent] nodeB[Model-Free Agent] nodeC[Model-Based Agent] nodeD[Value-Based] nodeE[Policy-Based] nodeF[Actor-Critic] nodeG[Tabular] nodeH[Approximation DQN] nodeI[REINFORCE] nodeJ[PPO A2C] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeB --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeE nodeB --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeG nodeD --> nodeH nodeE --> nodeI nodeF --> nodeJ ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** *Define a Reinforcement Learning Agent. Differentiate between a value-based and a policy-based agent.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **agent** in RL is the decision-making entity that interacts with an environment by perceiving states, selecting actions via a policy, receiving rewards, and updating its knowledge to maximize cumulative reward. - **Value-based agent:** Learns a value function $V(s)$ or $Q(s,a)$ and derives its policy implicitly by acting greedily w.r.t. the learned values (e.g., Q-Learning, DQN). Best suited for **discrete** action spaces. - **Policy-based agent:** Learns the policy $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ directly by parameterizing it and optimizing it via gradient ascent (e.g., REINFORCE). Naturally handles **continuous** action spaces. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "value-based is supervised." This is wrong — RL is *unsupervised in rewards*; the agent learns from scalar feedback, not labeled targets. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** *What is the role of a policy inside an RL agent? State the difference between deterministic and stochastic policies.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **policy** $\pi$ is the agent's behavior function. It defines how the agent maps perceived states to actions. The agent's objective is to find the **optimal policy** $\pi^*$ that maximizes expected cumulative discounted reward. - **Deterministic policy:** $\pi(s) = a$ — outputs one specific action. - **Stochastic policy:** $\pi(a \mid s) = \mathbb{P}(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$ — outputs a probability distribution over actions, allowing exploration and handling partial observability. ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Describe in detail the **components internal to an RL agent**: policy, value function, and model. Compare **model-free** and **model-based** agents with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Bellman Expectation Equation** for $V_\pi(s)$ and explain how an agent uses it for learning. Show that the optimal value function satisfies $V^*(s) = \max_a Q^*(s,a)$. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Internal Components of an RL Agent (7 Marks):** The RL agent consists of three core internal elements: 1. **Policy $\pi(a \mid s)$** — the agent's behavioral rule. It can be deterministic ($\pi : \mathcal{S} \to \mathcal{A}$) or stochastic ($\pi(a \mid s)$). The agent's goal is to find the optimal $\pi^*$. 2. **Value Function** — estimates how good a state (or state-action pair) is under a given policy: - $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ - $Q_\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ 3. **Model (Optional)** — the agent's internal representation of environment dynamics, comprising transition model $\mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)$ and reward model $\mathcal{R}(s, a, s')$. **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Model-Free Agent | Model-Based Agent | |--------|------------------|--------------------| | Learns Environment | No | Yes | | Sample Efficiency | Low (more samples) | High (planning reduces samples) | | Computation | Less | More (planning step) | | Examples | Q-Learning, DQN, REINFORCE | AlphaZero, Dyna, MBPO | | Risk | — | Model bias (if model is wrong) | **(b) Derivation of Bellman Expectation Equation (7 Marks):** Starting from the definition: $$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right] = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$ Applying law of total expectation over actions and next states: $$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] \right]$$ Since $\mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] = V_\pi(s')$: $$\boxed{V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]}$$ **Optimal Value Function:** The optimal value $V^*(s)$ is the maximum achievable value over all policies: $$V^*(s) = \max_\pi V_\pi(s) = \max_a Q^*(s, a)$$ The Bellman Optimality Equation becomes: $$V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V^*(s') \right]$$ The agent uses this to iteratively improve its policy by acting greedily w.r.t. $V^*$. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse $V_\pi$ and $Q_\pi$. Remember: $V_\pi$ takes only state, $Q_\pi$ takes state-action. Mixing them costs marks. --- **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Alternative]** **(a)** Explain the **agent-environment interaction loop** in an MDP with a neat diagram. Define the terms state, action, reward, and policy. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a detailed example, explain the **exploration vs. exploitation** dilemma faced by an RL agent. Discuss $\epsilon$-greedy and softmax policies as solutions. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Agent-Environment Interaction Loop (7 Marks):** The Markov Decision Process (MDP) framework is defined by the tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle$: - $\mathcal{S}$ — set of states - $\mathcal{A}$ — set of actions - $\mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)$ — transition probability - $\mathcal{R}(s, a, s')$ — reward function - $\gamma \in [0,1]$ — discount factor **The Loop:** At time $t$, the environment is in state $S_t$. The agent observes $S_t$, applies its policy $\pi$, and selects action $A_t$. The environment responds with reward $R_{t+1}$ and transitions to $S_{t+1}$. This produces the trajectory: $$S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, S_2, \dots$$ **Definitions:** - **State $S_t$:** A representation of the environment at time $t$ (Markov property: future depends only on current state). - **Action $A_t$:** The decision chosen by the agent from $\mathcal{A}(S_t)$. - **Reward $R_{t+1}$:** Scalar feedback signal from environment. - **Policy $\pi$:** The agent's rule for selecting actions. **(b) Exploration vs. Exploitation (7 Marks):** A core dilemma for the agent: - **Exploitation:** Use current knowledge to pick the best-known action (maximize reward). - **Exploration:** Try new actions to discover potentially better strategies. **Example:** A restaurant-visiting agent must choose between the favorite known restaurant (exploit) and a new untried one (explore). **Solutions:** 1. **$\epsilon$-Greedy Policy:** - With probability $1 - \epsilon$, choose the greedy action. - With probability $\epsilon$, choose randomly from all actions. $$\pi(a \mid s) = \begin{cases} 1 - \epsilon + \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} & \text{if } a = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s, a') \\ \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ 2. **Softmax (Boltzmann) Policy:** - Actions are chosen with probability proportional to their estimated values, scaled by a temperature $\tau$. $$\pi(a \mid s) = \frac{\exp(Q(s,a)/\tau)}{\sum_{a'} \exp(Q(s,a')/\tau)}$$ High $\tau$ → more exploration; low $\tau$ → more exploitation. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "exploration is always better" or "exploitation is always better." The correct answer is that **a balance** is essential — pure exploitation leads to local optima; pure exploration prevents reward maximization. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Quick Revision Checklist (must memorize for KTU ESE):** - **Agent** = decision-maker; consists of **policy + value function + (optional) model**. - **Policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$: behavior rule. Deterministic vs. Stochastic. - **Value Function $V_\pi(s)$**: expected return from state $s$ under $\pi$. - **Action-Value $Q_\pi(s,a)$**: expected return from $(s,a)$ under $\pi$. - **Return $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$** with $0 \leq \gamma \leq 1$. - **Bellman Expectation Equation** for $V_\pi$ and $Q_\pi$ — must derive on paper. - **Bellman Optimality Equation** — uses $\max$ operator; solved by Q-Learning agents. - **Agent Types:** Value-based, Policy-based, Actor-Critic, Model-based, Model-free. - **Exploration vs Exploitation:** solved by $\epsilon$-greedy or Softmax/Boltzmann. - **Epsilon-greedy formula** (with no pipes — use `\vert`): probability of best action $= 1 - \epsilon + \epsilon / \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$. - **Advantage** $A_\pi(s,a) = Q_\pi(s,a) - V_\pi(s)$ — central to actor-critic. - **REINFORCE update** uses $\nabla_\theta \ln \pi_\theta$ scaled by $G_t$. - **TD error** $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ — central to TD learning agents. - Agent **interacts continuously** with environment; no labeled dataset. - Always distinguish between **state, observation, and partial observability**. - The agent's **goal** is to maximize expected discounted return, not immediate reward.

topics.title: Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Reinforcement Learning (RL), the **Environment** is formally defined as the external, stochastic dynamical system outside the agent's boundary that encapsulates everything the agent interacts with but cannot fully control. It is the mathematical abstraction of the task or problem world.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (Sutton & Barto paradigm), the environment is rigorously modeled as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**, defined by the tuple:

$$\mathcal{M} = \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{S}$ : Set of all possible **States** (the environment's configuration space)
- $\mathcal{A}$ : Set of all possible **Actions** the agent may execute
- $\mathcal{P}$ : **State transition probability function** $P(s' \mid s, a)$
- $\mathcal{R}$ : **Reward function** $R(s, a, s')$ or $R(s, a)$
- $\gamma$ : **Discount factor**, $\gamma \in [0, 1]$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECMT745 Module 1):** The Environment is NOT just a "background." It is an active participant that reacts to the agent's actions, returns new states, and emits reward signals. The agent's sole purpose is to learn a policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ that maximizes cumulative reward **within** this environment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Video Game" Analogy**
> Imagine a child playing a video game:
> - The **Game Console + Screen + Game World** = the **Environment**
> - The **Child's Brain + Hands** = the **Agent**
> - The **Screen Display** (what the child sees) = the **State (or Observation)**
> - The **Buttons the child presses** = the **Action**
> - The **Score that pops up on screen** = the **Reward**
> 
> The environment does not care about the child's strategy. It mechanically responds to every button press by computing the next game frame (next state) and updating the score (reward). The child (agent) is *inside* the loop, but the world (environment) is *outside* and follows its own fixed (or stochastic) rules of physics.

## 1.3 Why is "Environment" a Separate Concept?

The environment is **decoupled** from the agent in RL theory. This separation enables:
1. **Modular design** — engineers can swap environments without modifying the agent.
2. **Generalization** — algorithms are benchmarked across standardized environments (e.g., OpenAI Gym, Atari, MuJoCo).
3. **Theoretical guarantees** — convergence proofs rely on stationary environment assumptions.

| Term | Role | Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Agent** | Decision-maker (learns policy $\pi$) | Fully controlled by algorithm |
| **Environment** | World simulator (transitions + rewards) | Fixed; outside agent's control |
| **Reward Signal** | Scalar feedback from environment | Generated by $R$ function |
