# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy of the Environment

The environment performs **three core computations** at every discrete time step $t$:

### Step 1: Receive Action
The environment accepts the action $A_t \in \mathcal{A}$ chosen by the agent at state $S_t \in \mathcal{S}$.

### Step 2: Transition to Next State
The environment samples a new state $S_{t+1}$ from the transition kernel:

$$S_{t+1} \sim P(\cdot \mid S_t, A_t)$$

For deterministic environments, $P$ is a Kronecker delta function.

### Step 3: Emit Reward
The environment computes and returns a scalar reward:

$$R_{t+1} = R(S_t, A_t, S_{t+1}) \quad \text{or} \quad R_{t+1} = R(S_t, A_t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Every RL problem reduces to designing or interfacing with this transition-reward loop. A KTU board question will often test whether you can identify the environment's $\mathcal{P}$ and $\mathcal{R}$ from a textual problem description.

## 2.2 Formal Properties of the Environment

### 2.2.1 Markov Property
A state $S_t$ is **Markovian** if and only if:

$$P(S_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t) = P(S_{t+1} \mid S_0, A_0, S_1, A_1, \ldots, S_t, A_t)$$

The "future is independent of the past given the present." Environments satisfying this are MDPs; those that do not are POMDPs.

### 2.2.2 Stationarity
A **stationary environment** has transition and reward functions that do not change over time:

$$P_t(s' \mid s, a) = P_{t'}(s' \mid s, a) \quad \forall t, t'$$

Most KTU 2024 problems assume stationarity. **Non-stationary environments** (e.g., financial markets, adversarial games) are an advanced research area.

## 2.3 Taxonomy of Environments (High-Yield for KTU)

| Environment Type | Defining Trait | KTU-Relevant Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Fully Observable** | Agent sees full state $S_t$ | Chess, Go |
| **Partially Observable (POMDP)** | Agent sees observation $O_t \neq S_t$ | Poker, self-driving car |
| **Deterministic** | $P(s' \mid s, a) = 1$ for one $s'$ | FrozenLake (deterministic version) |
| **Stochastic** | Multiple $s'$ possible per $(s,a)$ | Slot machines, dice games |
| **Episodic** | Tasks end at terminal state | Atari Pong, maze navigation |
| **Continuing** | No natural endpoint | Portfolio management |
| **Discrete Action** | $\mathcal{A}$ is finite $\{a_1, a_2, \ldots\}$ | Gridworld |
| **Continuous Action** | $\mathcal{A} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ | Robot arm torque control |
| **Single-Agent** | Only one learner | Classic control tasks |
| **Multi-Agent** | Multiple learners co-exist | StarCraft, soccer |
| **Model-Based** | $\mathcal{P}$ and $\mathcal{R}$ known | Simulated robotics |
| **Model-Free** | $\mathcal{P}$ and $\mathcal{R}$ unknown | Real-world deployment |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MDP Tuple | $\mathcal{M} = \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle$ | Set definitions |
| 2 | Transition Probability | $P(s' \mid s, a) = \Pr(S_{t+1}=s' \mid S_t=s, A_t=a)$ | $\in [0,1]$ |
| 3 | Transition Sum Rule | $\sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) = 1$ | Normalization |
| 4 | Reward Function | $R(s, a, s') = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t=s, A_t=a, S_{t+1}=s']$ | Scalar (real number) |
| 5 | Discount Factor | $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Return (Cumulative Reward) | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Scalar |
| 7 | Markov Condition | $P(S_{t+1} \mid H_t, A_t) = P(S_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t)$ | Logical |
| 8 | Episode Horizon | $T$ (finite for episodic) | Time steps |
| 9 | Expected Reward | $\bar{R}(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) R(s, a, s')$ | Scalar |
| 10 | Stationarity | $\frac{\partial P}{\partial t} = 0$ | Conceptual |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Examiners frequently give a textual problem and ask students to "identify $\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}$." Practice converting real-world scenarios into MDP tuples.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

The environment abstraction is critical in:
- **Robotics** — MuJoCo, PyBullet simulators
- **Autonomous Driving** — CARLA, SUMO traffic simulators
- **Healthcare** — Patient state transition models
- **Finance** — Market dynamics as MDPs
- **Game AI** — AlphaGo's environment was the Go board ruleset

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Expected Return from Environment Dynamics

**Goal:** Derive the expected return $G_t$ in terms of the environment's reward and discount structures.

### Step 1: Definition of Return
The return $G_t$ is the cumulative discounted reward from time $t$ onwards:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \ldots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

### Step 2: Recursive Form (Bellman-style)
Separate the immediate and future components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_t &= R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \ldots \\
    &= R_{t+1} + \gamma \left( R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \ldots \right) \\
    &= R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This recursive form is the **cornerstone** of all RL value-function derivations.

### Step 3: Expected Return under Stochastic Environment
Taking expectation over the environment's stochasticity, conditioned on following a policy $\pi$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s] &= \mathbb{E}_\pi[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s] + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s] \\
&= \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]
\end{aligned}
$$

The double sum is precisely the environment's expected reward $\bar{R}_\pi(s)$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Environment-Induced Expected Reward

**Goal:** Show how the environment's transition function $P$ and reward function $R$ combine to produce the expected one-step reward.

### Step 1: Marginalize over Actions
The expected reward under policy $\pi$ at state $s$:

$$\bar{R}_\pi(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \, \bar{R}(s, a)$$

where $\pi(a \mid s)$ is the agent's policy and $\bar{R}(s, a)$ is the environment's expected reward for action $a$ at state $s$.

### Step 2: Expand $\bar{R}(s, a)$ using $P$
By the law of total expectation, the environment's expected reward given $(s, a)$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{R}(s, a) &= \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a] \\
             &= \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \, R(s, a, s')
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Combine

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{R}_\pi(s) &= \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) R(s, a, s') \\
              &= \sum_{a} \sum_{s'} \pi(a \mid s) P(s' \mid s, a) R(s, a, s')
\end{aligned}
$$

This triple-sum expression is the **environment's contribution** to the state-value function $V_\pi(s)$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Environment's Role in the Agent-Environment Loop

| Time Step $t$ | Agent's Output | Environment's Input | Environment's Output | Agent's Next Input |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $t$ | Action $A_t \sim \pi(\cdot \mid S_t)$ | Receives $(S_t, A_t)$ | Samples $S_{t+1} \sim P(\cdot \mid S_t, A_t)$ | Observes $S_{t+1}$ |
| | | | Computes $R_{t+1} = R(S_t, A_t, S_{t+1})$ | Receives $R_{t+1}$ |
| $t+1$ | Action $A_{t+1} \sim \pi(\cdot \mid S_{t+1})$ | Receives $(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ | $\ldots$ | $\ldots$ |

## 3.4 Boundary and Edge Cases

- **Terminal state** $s_{terminal}$: $\mathcal{P}(s' \mid s_{terminal}, a) = 0$ for all $s'$; episode ends.
- **Absorbing state**: $\mathcal{P}(s \mid s, a) = 1$ for all $a$.
- **Deterministic shortcut**: When $P$ is a delta function, $\bar{R}(s,a) = R(s,a,s')$ for the unique $s'$.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Agent-Environment Interaction Loop

The canonical RL feedback loop. The environment is the block on the right.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["AGENT\nPolicy pi and Value V"] -->|"Action A_t"| B["ENVIRONMENT\nTransition P and Reward R"]
    B -->|"Reward R_t+1"| A
    B -->|"Next State S_t+1"| A
    A -.->|"State S_t"| B

    classDef agentBox fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef envBox fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

    class A agentBox
    class B envBox
```

**Reading the diagram:** The agent sends Action $A_t$ to the environment. The environment, governed by its internal transition function $P$ and reward function $R$, emits a new State $S_{t+1}$ and a scalar Reward $R_{t+1}$ back to the agent. The dashed arrow shows that the environment also "sees" the current state (i.e., it uses $S_t$ to compute the transition).

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Environment" in Reinforcement Learning. How is it formally represented as a Markov Decision Process?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In Reinforcement Learning, the **Environment** is the external system that the agent interacts with during learning. It encapsulates everything outside the agent and responds to the agent's actions by transitioning to a new state and emitting a reward signal.

The environment is formally represented as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**, defined by the tuple:

$$\mathcal{M} = \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle$$

where:
- $\mathcal{S}$ is the set of states,
- $\mathcal{A}$ is the set of actions,
- $\mathcal{P}$ is the transition probability function $P(s' \mid s, a)$,
- $\mathcal{R}$ is the reward function $R(s, a, s')$,
- $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ is the discount factor.

The agent's objective is to learn a policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ that maximizes the expected cumulative discounted reward $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ within this environment.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Model-Based and Model-Free environments. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Model-Based Environment | Model-Free Environment |
|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge of $P$ and $R$** | Known a priori | Unknown, must be sampled |
| **Planning** | Possible using dynamic programming | Not possible; learn by interaction |
| **Sample Efficiency** | High (reuses model) | Low (needs many interactions) |
| **Example** | Gridworld with known transition table | Real-world robotic control |

In a **Model-Based** environment, the transition probabilities $P(s' \mid s, a)$ and reward function $R(s, a, s')$ are explicitly known, enabling planning algorithms like Value Iteration and Policy Iteration. In a **Model-Free** environment, the agent must learn optimal behavior purely through trial-and-error interaction, as in Q-Learning applied to an unknown maze.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Students must answer ONE question from each Part B slot. The two questions below are independent alternatives; only one needs to be attempted.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the agent-environment interaction loop in Reinforcement Learning with a neat block diagram. Clearly identify the role of the transition function $P(s' \mid s, a)$ and the reward function $R(s, a, s')$.

**Model Solution:**

The agent-environment interaction is the foundational loop of RL. At every discrete time step $t$:

**Step 1:** The agent observes the current state $S_t \in \mathcal{S}$ of the environment.

**Step 2:** Based on its policy $\pi$, the agent selects an action $A_t \in \mathcal{A}$.

**Step 3:** The environment receives $(S_t, A_t)$ as input.

**Step 4:** The environment uses its **transition function** $P(s' \mid s, a)$ to sample the next state:

$$S_{t+1} \sim P(\cdot \mid S_t, A_t)$$

**Step 5:** The environment uses its **reward function** $R(s, a, s')$ to compute the immediate reward:

$$R_{t+1} = R(S_t, A_t, S_{t+1})$$

**Step 6:** The pair $(S_{t+1}, R_{t+1})$ is returned to the agent.

**Block Diagram (textual):**

```
   +-----------+        A_t          +-----------------+
   |           |  ------------------> |                 |
   |   AGENT   |                      |   ENVIRONMENT  |
   |  Policy π  |  <----------------  |  P(s'|s,a),     |
   |  Value V  |      S_{t+1}, R_{t+1}|  R(s,a,s')      |
   +-----------+                      +-----------------+
```

**Role of $P(s' \mid s, a)$:** It encodes the **dynamics** — how the world evolves in response to the agent's action. It is a probability distribution over next states.

**Role of $R(s, a, s')$:** It encodes the **goal** — what the agent should aim to achieve. It maps each transition to a scalar reward.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a simple 2-state MDP environment with $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2\}$, $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2\}$, and the following transition probabilities and rewards:

| State $s$ | Action $a$ | $P(s_1 \mid s, a)$ | $P(s_2 \mid s, a)$ | $R(s, a, s_1)$ | $R(s, a, s_2)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | $a_1$ | 0.7 | 0.3 | +5 | +1 |
| $s_1$ | $a_2$ | 0.4 | 0.6 | +3 | +2 |
| $s_2$ | $a_1$ | 0.2 | 0.8 | +0 | +4 |
| $s_2$ | $a_2$ | 0.5 | 0.5 | +2 | +1 |

Compute the expected reward $\bar{R}(s, a)$ for each state-action pair.

**Model Solution:**

Using the formula:

$$\bar{R}(s, a) = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \, R(s, a, s')$$

**For $(s_1, a_1)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{R}(s_1, a_1) &= P(s_1 \mid s_1, a_1) \cdot R(s_1, a_1, s_1) + P(s_2 \mid s_1, a_1) \cdot R(s_1, a_1, s_2) \\
                  &= (0.7)(+5) + (0.3)(+1) \\
                  &= 3.5 + 0.3 = 3.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $(s_1, a_2)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{R}(s_1, a_2) &= (0.4)(+3) + (0.6)(+2) \\
                  &= 1.2 + 1.2 = 2.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $(s_2, a_1)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{R}(s_2, a_1) &= (0.2)(+0) + (0.8)(+4) \\
                  &= 0.0 + 3.2 = 3.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $(s_2, a_2)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{R}(s_2, a_2) &= (0.5)(+2) + (0.5)(+1) \\
                  &= 1.0 + 0.5 = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Expected Reward Table:**

| State $s$ | Action $a$ | $\bar{R}(s, a)$ |
|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | $a_1$ | **3.8** |
| $s_1$ | $a_2$ | **2.4** |
| $s_2$ | $a_1$ | **3.2** |
| $s_2$ | $a_2$ | **1.5** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify environments along five different dimensions: observability, determinism, episode structure, action space, and agent count. Give a real-world RL example for each combination where possible.

**Model Solution:**

The environment in RL can be classified along the following dimensions:

**1. Observability:**
- **Fully Observable:** Agent observes the complete state $S_t$. *Example: Chess board — every piece is visible.*
- **Partially Observable (POMDP):** Agent sees only an observation $O_t$ that is a function of $S_t$. *Example: Poker — opponent's cards are hidden.*

**2. Determinism:**
- **Deterministic:** $P(s' \mid s, a)$ is a delta function. *Example: A robot arm moving to precise coordinates.*
- **Stochastic:** $P$ distributes probability across multiple $s'$. *Example: Slot machine — reward is randomized.*

**3. Episode Structure:**
- **Episodic:** Clear terminal state exists. *Example: A maze-solving task that ends at the goal.*
- **Continuing:** No natural endpoint. *Example: Stock market trading.*

**4. Action Space:**
- **Discrete Actions:** $\mathcal{A}$ is finite. *Example: Atari Pong with 4 button actions.*
- **Continuous Actions:** $\mathcal{A} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$. *Example: Autonomous vehicle steering angle.*

**5. Agent Count:**
- **Single-Agent:** One learner. *Example: CartPole balancing.*
- **Multi-Agent:** Multiple learners. *Example: Multi-player StarCraft II matches.*

**Example Combinations:**
- *Fully Observable + Deterministic + Episodic + Discrete + Single-Agent:* **Tic-Tac-Toe**
- *Partially Observable + Stochastic + Episodic + Discrete + Multi-Agent:* **Poker**
- *Fully Observable + Continuous + Continuing + Continuous + Single-Agent:* **Locomotion robot**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Markov Property in the context of an RL environment. Why is it important for problem formulation? Provide a counter-example scenario where the Markov property is violated.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Markov Property:**

A state $S_t$ has the **Markov property** if the future is conditionally independent of the past, given the present. Formally:

$$P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s_t, A_t = a_t) = P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_0, A_0, S_1, A_1, \ldots, S_t, A_t)$$

Equivalently:

$$P(S_{t+1} \mid H_t, A_t) = P(S_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t)$$

where $H_t$ is the entire history.

**Why is it Important?**

1. **Compact Representation:** Only the current state $S_t$ is needed; the entire history is dispensable.
2. **Bellman Equations:** Enables recursive value-function decomposition.
3. **Convergence Guarantees:** Dynamic programming and RL algorithms (Value Iteration, Q-Learning) rely on the Markov property for theoretical convergence proofs.
4. **Computational Tractability:** Avoids exponential blowup of state-history space.

**Counter-Example (Markov Property Violation):**

Consider a **self-driving car** that uses only the current camera frame as its state. The frame does not capture:
- The car's velocity history
- Whether a pedestrian was about to cross 2 seconds ago
- The driver's intent (signaled earlier by a turn signal)

A pedestrian who is currently *not visible* in the frame may step into the road in the next frame based on information that was visible 2 frames ago. The transition probability $P(S_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t)$ alone cannot predict this — the agent needs memory of the past.

**Resolution:** This environment is a **POMDP**, not an MDP. To recover the Markov property, one can stack the last $k$ frames into a pseudo-state $S'_t = (O_{t-k+1}, \ldots, O_t)$ or use recurrent neural networks to maintain a hidden state.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Environment" with "State":** The environment is the *entire world*; the state is a *snapshot* of the world. Do NOT use these interchangeably. (Lose ~1 mark)
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Markov Assumption:** When writing MDP equations like $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma V_\pi(s')]$, students often drop the $P$ term or wrongly replace it with a constant. Always include the explicit transition probability.
> 
> 3. **Skipping the normalization check:** A transition probability $P$ must satisfy $\sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) = 1$. If your numerical answer in part (b) does not sum to 1, the examiner will deduct 1–2 marks.
> 
> 4. **Mixing Agent's and Environment's formulas:** $\pi(a \mid s)$ belongs to the **agent**; $P(s' \mid s, a)$ and $R(s, a, s')$ belong to the **environment**. Do not interchange them in derivations.
> 
> 5. **Not stating units/domain of $\gamma$:** Always write $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ explicitly in formula sheet answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Environment in RL (KTU 2024 Module 1)**

### Core Concepts
- [x] **Environment** = external world the agent interacts with; mathematically an MDP $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle$
- [x] **Agent** = decision-maker; **Environment** = everything outside the agent's control
- [x] The environment is responsible for **two computations**: state transition and reward emission

### Key Formulas to Memorize
- [x] MDP tuple: $\mathcal{M} = \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle$
- [x] Transition: $P(s' \mid s, a) \in [0, 1]$, with $\sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) = 1$
- [x] Expected reward: $\bar{R}(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) R(s, a, s')$
- [x] Return: $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$
- [x] Markov property: $P(S_{t+1} \mid H_t, A_t) = P(S_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t)$

### Classification Axes (must know all 5+)
- [x] Observability: Fully Observable vs. POMDP
- [x] Determinism: Deterministic vs. Stochastic
- [x] Episode: Episodic vs. Continuing
- [x] Action Space: Discrete vs. Continuous
- [x] Agent Count: Single-Agent vs. Multi-Agent
- [x] Knowledge: Model-Based vs. Model-Free

### Frequently Asked Definitions
- [x] "Stationary environment" — $P$ and $R$ do not change over time
- [x] "Episodic task" — terminates at a terminal state with horizon $T$
- [x] "Reward hypothesis" — all goals can be described as maximization of expected cumulative scalar reward

### Common Numerical Computation Pattern
- [x] Given a transition-reward table, compute $\bar{R}(s, a)$ using the double sum formula
- [x] Verify that $\sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) = 1$ before computing rewards

### One-Line Summary for Viva
> *"The Environment in RL is the stationary or non-stationary dynamical system — typically formalized as an MDP — that reacts to the agent's actions by sampling the next state from $P(s' \mid s, a)$ and emitting a scalar reward from $R(s, a, s')$."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Observations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Observations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In **Reinforcement Learning (RL)**, an **Observation** is the partial, sensory information that an agent receives from the environment at each time step. Formally, it is the output of an **observation function** (also called an **emission function**) that maps the underlying true environment state to a perceivable signal available to the agent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (PECMT745):**
> An observation $o_t \in \mathcal{O}$ at time $t$ is drawn from the observation space $\mathcal{O}$ according to the observation probability distribution $\Omega(o_t \mid s_t, a_{t-1})$ (or $\Omega(o_t \mid s_t)$), where $s_t$ is the true latent state and $a_{t-1}$ is the previously executed action. Observations constitute the agent's only window into the world's true configuration.

The observation is mathematically distinguished from the **state** as follows:

| Concept | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| State | $s_t \in \mathcal{S}$ | Complete, hidden description of the environment |
| Observation | $o_t \in \mathcal{O}$ | Partial, agent-visible projection of the state |
| History | $h_t = (o_1, a_1, o_2, a_2, \dots, o_t)$ | Complete interaction record up to time $t$ |

> [!NOTE]
> When $o_t = s_t$ (i.e., the agent sees the full state), the problem reduces to a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**. When $o_t \neq s_t$, the problem becomes a **Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "A Self-Driving Car at Night"

Imagine a self-driving car navigating a foggy highway at night:

- **The True State ($s_t$)**: The complete physics-based reality — every vehicle's exact position, velocity, and intent; every road marking; every pedestrian on the sidewalk; tire friction coefficients; the precise curvature of the road 10 km ahead.
- **The Observation ($o_t$)**: What the car *actually* sees — a pixelated LiDAR point cloud, a blurry RGB camera frame, a noisy GPS coordinate, and a radar echo list. The pedestrian behind a truck is **invisible**; the road curvature far ahead is **unknown**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in RL:** The agent must learn an optimal policy $\pi(a \mid \cdot)$ using only observations. The quality and completeness of observations fundamentally bound the achievable performance of any RL algorithm. A poorly-observed environment forces the agent to maintain memory, form beliefs, or act stochastically.

## 1.3 Three Kinds of Observations (Syllabus Classification)

1. **Full / Complete Observations ($o_t = s_t$)** — Agent has omniscient access. Examples: Chess (board fully visible), Go, simple gridworlds.
2. **Partial Observations** — Agent sees only a noisy projection. Examples: Poker (opponent's hidden cards), robotics with limited sensors.
3. **Delayed / Indirect Observations** — Signals arrive asynchronously or with latency. Examples: financial trading (delayed price ticks), network routing (packet acknowledgments).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Module 1 specifically introduces observations as a precursor to the formal RL problem formulations (MDP and POMDP). A clear understanding here is **mandatory** before tackling Bellman equations, value functions, or policy gradient methods in later modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Agent-Environment Loop

At every discrete time step $t$, the following sequence occurs:

1. **Environment possesses** a (possibly hidden) true state $s_t \in \mathcal{S}$.
2. **Environment emits** an observation $o_t \sim \Omega(\cdot \mid s_t, a_{t-1})$ from the observation function $\Omega$.
3. **Agent perceives** $o_t$ and appends it to its history $h_t$.
4. **Agent selects** an action $a_t \sim \pi(\cdot \mid h_t)$ (history-dependent) or $a_t \sim \pi(\cdot \mid o_t)$ (memoryless).
5. **Environment transitions** to $s_{t+1} \sim P(\cdot \mid s_t, a_t)$ and emits reward $r_{t+1} = R(s_t, a_t)$.
6. **Cycle repeats** at $t+1$.

## 2.2 Formal POMDP Tuple

A reinforcement learning problem with observations is rigorously defined as the 7-tuple:

$$
(\mathcal{S},\ \mathcal{A},\ \mathcal{O},\ P,\ R,\ \Omega,\ \gamma)
$$

Where each component serves a distinct mathematical purpose:

- $\mathcal{S}$ — finite or continuous **state space**
- $\mathcal{A}$ — **action space**
- $\mathcal{O}$ — **observation space** (the focus of this module)
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$ — **transition dynamics**
- $R(s, a)$ — **reward function**
- $\Omega(o \mid s', a)$ — **observation likelihood / emission probability**
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ — **discount factor**

## 2.3 The Markov Property — Why Observations Disrupt It

The **Markov property** asserts that the future is conditionally independent of the past given the present:

$$
P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_1, a_1, \dots, s_t, a_t)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the agent only receives observations $o_t$ instead of states $s_t$, the Markov property is **violated** because $o_t$ does not contain all relevant information. The agent's decision rule must therefore depend on the **entire history** $h_t$ rather than a single observation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Domain |
|---|---|---|
| $o_t$ | Observation at time $t$ | Sample from $\mathcal{O}$ |
| $\mathcal{O}$ | Observation space | $\mathbb{R}^d$, $\{0,1\}^n$, or discrete set |
| $\Omega(o \mid s', a)$ | Observation likelihood | Probability distribution over $\mathcal{O}$ |
| $h_t$ | History up to time $t$ | Sequence in $(\mathcal{O} \times \mathcal{A})^*$ |
| $b(s)$ | Belief state | Probability distribution over $\mathcal{S}$ |
| $b'(s')$ | Updated belief after new observation | $\Delta(\mathcal{S})$ (simplex) |
| $\eta$ | Normalization constant | Positive scalar ensuring $\sum_{s'} b'(s') = 1$ |
| $\pi(a \mid h_t)$ | History-dependent policy | Probability over $\mathcal{A}$ |

### Belief State Update Equation (Bayesian Filter)

$$
b'(s') = \eta \cdot \Omega(o \mid s', a) \cdot \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot b(s)
$$

$$
\eta = \left[ \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \Omega(o \mid s', a) \cdot \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot b(s) \right]^{-1}
$$

### Observation-Dependent Reward and Value

$$
V^\pi(h_t) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} \;\Big\vert\; h_t \right]
$$

$$
Q^\pi(h_t, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} \;\Big\vert\; h_t, a \right]
$$

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

The observation framework is foundational to real-world RL deployments across KTU-recognized engineering domains:

- **Robotics (Mechanical/Mechatronics):** Sensor fusion of camera, LiDAR, IMU produces high-dimensional observations $\mathcal{O} = \mathbb{R}^{d}$ with $d \geq 10^6$.
- **Autonomous Systems:** Radar and GPS observations are noisy and asynchronous.
- **Industrial Control (Electrical):** SCADA systems provide sampled, partial state measurements.
- **Network Engineering (CS):** Packet routing agents observe only local queue lengths, not global traffic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** In modern Deep RL (e.g., Atari, DQN, AlphaGo), the "observation" is the raw pixel frame stack — yet the algorithm implicitly assumes it is a sufficient statistic of the state. When this assumption fails, the agent exhibits brittle, exploitable behavior.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bayesian Belief Update from Observations

We now derive the **recursive belief update** that an agent uses to maintain a probabilistic estimate of the latent state given a stream of observations.

### Setup

Let $b_t(s) = P(s_t = s \mid h_t)$ denote the agent's belief about being in state $s$ after observing history $h_t$. Suppose the agent has just executed action $a_t$ and received a new observation $o_{t+1}$. We want to compute $b_{t+1}(s') = P(s_{t+1} = s' \mid h_{t+1})$.

### Step 1 — Expand the Posterior Using Bayes' Theorem

$$
b_{t+1}(s') = P(s_{t+1} = s' \mid h_t, a_t, o_{t+1})
$$

$$
b_{t+1}(s') = \frac{P(o_{t+1} \mid s_{t+1} = s', h_t, a_t) \cdot P(s_{t+1} = s' \mid h_t, a_t)}{P(o_{t+1} \mid h_t, a_t)}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Observation Function

The observation $o_{t+1}$ depends only on the new state $s'$ and the last action $a_t$ (Markov assumption on emissions):

$$
P(o_{t+1} \mid s_{t+1} = s', h_t, a_t) = \Omega(o_{t+1} \mid s', a_t)
$$

### Step 3 — Marginalize Over the Previous State

The prediction term $P(s_{t+1} = s' \mid h_t, a_t)$ is computed by the law of total probability:

$$
P(s_{t+1} = s' \mid h_t, a_t) = \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} P(s_{t+1} = s' \mid s_t = s, a_t) \cdot P(s_t = s \mid h_t)
$$

$$
= \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a_t) \cdot b_t(s)
$$

### Step 4 — Combine and Normalize

Substituting back into Step 1:

$$
b_{t+1}(s') = \frac{\Omega(o_{t+1} \mid s', a_t) \cdot \sum_{s} P(s' \mid s, a_t) \cdot b_t(s)}{P(o_{t+1} \mid h_t, a_t)}
$$

Define the normalization constant $\eta$ as the reciprocal of the evidence:

$$
\eta = P(o_{t+1} \mid h_t, a_t) = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \Omega(o_{t+1} \mid s', a_t) \cdot \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a_t) \cdot b_t(s)
$$

### Step 5 — Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{b_{t+1}(s') = \eta \cdot \Omega(o_{t+1} \mid s', a_t) \cdot \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a_t) \cdot b_t(s)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU Examiners:** The belief update has two multiplicative stages: (1) **Prediction** via the transition model $P$, and (2) **Correction** via the observation likelihood $\Omega$. This is mathematically identical to a Kalman filter (linear-Gaussian case) or particle filter (nonlinear case).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Toy POMDP)

Consider a 1-D corridor with **2 hidden states**:

- $\mathcal{S} = \{\text{Left}, \text{Right}\}$
- $\mathcal{A} = \{\text{Stay}, \text{Flip}\}$
- $\mathcal{O} = \{\text{L}, \text{R}\}$ (noisy sensor)

### Given Probabilities

$$
P(\text{Right} \mid \text{Left}, \text{Stay}) = 0.0, \quad P(\text{Right} \mid \text{Left}, \text{Flip}) = 0.9
$$

$$
P(\text{Right} \mid \text{Right}, \text{Stay}) = 1.0, \quad P(\text{Right} \mid \text{Right}, \text{Flip}) = 0.1
$$

$$
\Omega(L \mid \text{Left}) = 0.7, \quad \Omega(L \mid \text{Right}) = 0.2
$$

### Initial Belief

$$
b_0(\text{Left}) = 0.5, \quad b_0(\text{Right}) = 0.5
$$

### Step-by-Step Computation: Action = Stay, Observation = L

**Step A — Prediction:**

$$
P(\text{Left}) = P(L \mid L, S) \cdot b_0(L) + P(L \mid R, S) \cdot b_0(R)
$$

$$
= (1.0)(0.5) + (0.0)(0.5) = 0.5
$$

$$
P(\text{Right}) = P(R \mid L, S) \cdot b_0(L) + P(R \mid R, S) \cdot b_0(R)
$$

$$
= (0.0)(0.5) + (1.0)(0.5) = 0.5
$$

**Step B — Correction (apply observation likelihood):**

$$
\text{Unnorm}(L) = \Omega(L \mid L) \cdot P(\text{Left}) = (0.7)(0.5) = 0.35
$$

$$
\text{Unnorm}(R) = \Omega(L \mid R) \cdot P(\text{Right}) = (0.2)(0.5) = 0.10
$$

**Step C — Normalization:**

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{0.35 + 0.10} = \frac{1}{0.45} \approx 2.222
$$

**Step D — Final Belief:**

$$
b_1(\text{Left}) = 2.222 \times 0.35 = 0.778
$$

$$
b_1(\text{Right}) = 2.222 \times 0.10 = 0.222
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** After one noisy "L" observation, the agent's belief in "Left" rises from **0.500** to **0.778** — a substantial update driven by the sensor reliability ratio $\frac{0.7}{0.2} = 3.5$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Observation Sufficiency

| Observation Type | Sufficient Statistic? | Markov? | Example Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full state $o_t = s_t$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Q-Learning, DQN |
| Noisy state $o_t = s_t + \epsilon$ | ✅ Yes (probabilistic) | ✅ Yes | Linear Quadratic Regulator |
| Partial projection | ❌ No | ❌ No | Recurrent DQN, LSTM Policy |
| Delayed signal | ❌ No | ❌ No | Memory-based agents |
| Image pixels | ⚠️ Approximately | ⚠️ Approximately | CNN-based policies |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Agent-Environment Interaction with Observations

The following block diagram depicts the canonical RL loop emphasizing the **observation channel** as a distinct signal pathway.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Environment] -->|True State s_t| B[Observation Function Omega]
    B -->|Observation o_t| C[Agent Policy pi]
    C -->|Action a_t| A
    A -->|Reward r_t| C
    C -->|Update Belief b_t| D[Belief State Memory]
    D -->|History h_t| C
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 POMDP vs MDP Distinction Flowchart

This diagram illustrates the **decision branch** an RL engineer must make when classifying the problem based on observation quality.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Environment] --> B{Can Agent Observe Full State?}
    B -->|Yes - o_t equals s_t| C[MDP Framework]
    B -->|No - Partial or Noisy| D[POMDP Framework]
    C --> E[Use Value Functions V s and Q s,a]
    D --> F[Use Belief-Based Value Functions V b and Q b,a]
    E --> G[Apply Q-Learning, SARSA, DQN]
    F --> H[Apply Recurrent Networks, Memory-Based Methods]
    style B fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#81ecec,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#fab1a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Observation Processing Pipeline (Block Architecture)

For high-dimensional observations (e.g., images, LiDAR), the agent typically applies a **feature extractor** before policy inference. The pipeline is mapped below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Raw Sensor Input] --> S2[Preprocessing Layer]
    S2 --> S3[Feature Extractor CNN or MLP]
    S3 --> S4[Latent Representation z_t]
    S4 --> S5[Policy Network pi a given z_t]
    S4 --> S6[Value Network V z_t]
    style S1 fill:#dfe6e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S2 fill:#b2bec3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S3 fill:#74b9ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S4 fill:#a29bfe,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S5 fill:#55efc4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S6 fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Mapping:** This three-stage architecture (Perception → Representation → Decision) is the de-facto standard in modern robotics and autonomous-vehicle stacks. In KTU 2024 scheme examinations, students are expected to identify which block handles the **observation** and which handles the **policy**.

## 4.4 Observation-Timeline Diagram

A temporal view of how observations, actions, and rewards interleave over a single episode of length $T$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Time t equals 0] --> O1[Observation o_1]
    O1 --> A1[Action a_1]
    A1 --> R1[Reward r_1]
    R1 --> O2[Observation o_2]
    O2 --> A2[Action a_2]
    A2 --> R2[Reward r_2]
    R2 --> O3[Dots continued]
    O3 --> OT[Observation o_T]
    OT --> AT[Action a_T]
    AT --> END[Episode End]
    style T0 fill:#fab1a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style END fill:#81ecec,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

> **"Differentiate between a state and an observation in a Reinforcement Learning setting. Illustrate with one example where they coincide and one where they differ."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **state** $s_t \in \mathcal{S}$ is the complete, Markovian description of the environment at time $t$, sufficient to predict all future dynamics. An **observation** $o_t \in \mathcal{O}$ is the information actually available to the agent, which may be a partial or noisy version of the state.

- **Coincide Example:** A chess game — the board configuration is fully visible, so $o_t = s_t$. The agent can apply standard Q-learning directly.
- **Differ Example:** A robot navigating with a forward-facing camera — the true state includes positions of all objects behind walls, but the agent only sees a 2D image. Hence $o_t \neq s_t$, requiring POMDP methods.

> **Valuation Key:** Full marks require both definition + two contrasting examples. Loss of 1 mark if examples are weak. *(Total: 3 Marks)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

> **"What is the observation function $\Omega$ in a POMDP? Why is it necessary even when the transition model $P$ is known?"** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **observation function** (or **emission function**) $\Omega(o \mid s, a)$ specifies the probability of the agent receiving observation $o$ given that the environment is in state $s$ and the agent has just executed action $a$.

It is necessary because **knowing the transition model $P$ is not sufficient to specify what the agent perceives**. Two distinct states may produce identical observations, or a single state may yield multiple possible observations due to sensor noise. Without $\Omega$, the agent cannot perform Bayesian belief updates and cannot disambiguate hidden states.

> **Valuation Key:** Marks awarded for defining $\Omega$, stating the role in belief update, and clarifying its independence from $P$. *(Total: 3 Marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks — Module 1 Choice Option 1]

> **(a)** Formally define a **Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP)** as a 7-tuple. Clearly distinguish each component, with particular emphasis on the observation space $\mathcal{O}$ and observation function $\Omega$. *(7 Marks)*

> **(b)** Derive the **Bayesian belief update equation** for a POMDP agent. Starting from the definition of the belief state, apply Bayes' theorem and marginalization. State clearly the role of the normalizer $\eta$. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A POMDP is the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{O}, P, R, \Omega, \gamma)$ where:

- $\mathcal{S}$ — **State space:** set of all possible true environment configurations.
- $\mathcal{A}$ — **Action space:** set of all actions available to the agent.
- $\mathcal{O}$ — **Observation space:** set of all perceivable signals; this is the **distinguishing element** from a standard MDP.
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$ — **Transition kernel:** probability of moving to $s'$ after taking action $a$ in state $s$.
- $R(s, a)$ — **Reward function:** expected immediate reward for executing $a$ in $s$.
- $\Omega(o \mid s', a)$ — **Observation likelihood:** probability of perceiving $o$ after reaching $s'$ via $a$.
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ — **Discount factor:** weights future rewards relative to immediate ones.

The **observation space** $\mathcal{O}$ may be discrete (e.g., sensor readings) or continuous (e.g., camera images in $\mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$). The **observation function** $\Omega$ is a probability distribution that quantifies sensor noise, occlusion, and partial visibility.

> **Valuation Distribution (7 Marks):** State space (1) + Action space (1) + Observation space & function (3) + Remaining components (1) + Example/diagram (1).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

We begin with the belief definition:

$$
b(s) = P(s \mid h)
$$

Given new action $a$ and observation $o'$, we seek $b'(s')$.

**Step 1 — Posterior via Bayes:**

$$
b'(s') = P(s' \mid h, a, o') = \frac{P(o' \mid s', h, a) \cdot P(s' \mid h, a)}{P(o' \mid h, a)}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify emission:**

$$
P(o' \mid s', h, a) = \Omega(o' \mid s', a)
$$

**Step 3 — Marginalize transition:**

$$
P(s' \mid h, a) = \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot b(s)
$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$
b'(s') = \frac{\Omega(o' \mid s', a) \cdot \sum_{s} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot b(s)}{P(o' \mid h, a)}
$$

**Step 5 — Define normalizer $\eta$:**

$$
\eta = \left[ P(o' \mid h, a) \right]^{-1} = \left[ \sum_{s'} \Omega(o' \mid s', a) \cdot \sum_{s} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot b(s) \right]^{-1}
$$

**Final form:**

$$
\boxed{b'(s') = \eta \cdot \Omega(o' \mid s', a) \cdot \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot b(s)}
$$

The **normalizer $\eta$** ensures that $\sum_{s'} b'(s') = 1$, i.e., the updated belief is a valid probability distribution over the state space.

> **Valuation Distribution (7 Marks):** Bayes setup (2) + Marginalization (2) + Final equation (2) + Role of $\eta$ (1).

---

### Question B [14 Marks — Module 1 Choice Option 2]

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **partial observability** in RL. Describe any **two** real-world scenarios where the agent receives only partial observations, and discuss the consequences for policy design. *(7 Marks)*

> **(b)** Consider a 2-state POMDP with $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2\}$, $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2\}$, and $\mathcal{O} = \{o_1, o_2\}$. Given the initial belief $b_0(s_1) = 0.6$, $b_0(s_2) = 0.4$, transition $P(s_1 \mid s_1, a_1) = 0.8$, $P(s_1 \mid s_2, a_1) = 0.3$, and observation likelihoods $\Omega(o_1 \mid s_1) = 0.9$, $\Omega(o_1 \mid s_2) = 0.4$. If the agent executes $a_1$ and observes $o_1$, compute the updated belief $b_1(s_1)$ and $b_1(s_2)$. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Partial observability** refers to a setting in which the agent's observation $o_t$ does not uniquely determine the latent state $s_t$. Formally, there exist $s_i \neq s_j$ such that $P(o \mid s_i) > 0$ and $P(o \mid s_j) > 0$ for some observation $o$. The agent therefore cannot directly use a state-value function; it must reason about **distributions over states** (beliefs).

**Scenario 1 — Poker:** The agent sees only its own cards and the community cards, not the opponents' hidden cards. The true state includes all hands. Consequences: optimal strategies must be stochastic (to remain unpredictable) and the policy must condition on the full history of bets and reveals.

**Scenario 2 — Mobile Robot Localization:** The robot observes noisy odometry and range-finder readings; its true pose is unknown. Consequences: the robot must maintain a probability distribution over poses (a particle filter or Kalman filter) and the policy must depend on this distribution rather than a single coordinate.

**Consequences for Policy Design:**
- Policies become **history-dependent** $\pi(a \mid h_t)$ or **belief-dependent** $\pi(a \mid b_t)$.
- Standard Q-learning **cannot be applied directly**; algorithms like POMCP, DESPOT, or recurrent DQN are required.
- Sample complexity increases significantly compared to fully-observable MDPs.

> **Valuation Distribution (7 Marks):** Definition (2) + Scenario 1 (2) + Scenario 2 (2) + Consequence summary (1).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Prediction (transition):**

$$
P(s_1 \mid a_1) = P(s_1 \mid s_1, a_1) \cdot b_0(s_1) + P(s_1 \mid s_2, a_1) \cdot b_0(s_2)
$$

$$
= (0.8)(0.6) + (0.3)(0.4) = 0.48 + 0.12 = 0.60
$$

$$
P(s_2 \mid a_1) = 1 - 0.60 = 0.40
$$

**Step 2 — Apply observation likelihood (correction):**

$$
\text{Unnorm}(s_1) = \Omega(o_1 \mid s_1) \cdot P(s_1 \mid a_1) = (0.9)(0.60) = 0.540
$$

$$
\text{Unnorm}(s_2) = \Omega(o_1 \mid s_2) \cdot P(s_2 \mid a_1) = (0.4)(0.40) = 0.160
$$

**Step 3 — Compute normalizer:**

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{0.540 + 0.160} = \frac{1}{0.700} = 1.4286
$$

**Step 4 — Final belief:**

$$
b_1(s_1) = 1.4286 \times 0.540 = 0.7714
$$

$$
b_1(s_2) = 1.4286 \times 0.160 = 0.2286
$$

> **Valuation Distribution (7 Marks):** Prediction step (2) + Correction step (2) + Normalization (1) + Final numerical values (2).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in 'Observations' Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing observation with state** — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks when students use the terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalizer $\eta$** — Always ensure the belief sums to 1. Marks are lost if the final values do not sum to 1.0.
> 3. **Ignoring the action in $\Omega(o \mid s', a)$** — Some students write $\Omega(o \mid s)$ instead of $\Omega(o \mid s', a)$. Use the form with action conditioning unless the problem explicitly states otherwise.
> 4. **Skipping marginalization** — In belief updates, the sum over the previous state $\sum_s$ is mandatory, not optional.
> 5. **Failing to provide real-world examples** — KTU 2024 scheme rewards application-based reasoning. Abstract definitions without examples typically receive only 60% of allotted marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Observations in RL (Module 1):**

- ✅ An **observation** $o_t$ is the agent's *perception*; a **state** $s_t$ is the environment's *reality*.
- ✅ The observation space is denoted $\mathcal{O}$; the observation function is $\Omega(o \mid s', a)$.
- ✅ When $o_t = s_t$, the problem is an **MDP**; otherwise it is a **POMDP**.
- ✅ The POMDP tuple is $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{O}, P, R, \Omega, \gamma)$ — 7 elements.
- ✅ Partial observability **breaks the Markov property** of single observations; full history $h_t$ or belief $b_t$ is required.
- ✅ The **belief update** is $b'(s') = \eta \cdot \Omega(o \mid s', a) \cdot \sum_s P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot b(s)$.
- ✅ The **normalizer $\eta$** ensures $\sum_{s'} b'(s') = 1$.
- ✅ **History** $h_t = (o_1, a_1, o_2, a_2, \dots, o_t)$ is the complete interaction record.
- ✅ Real-world examples: self-driving cars, poker, robotic localization, medical diagnosis, network routing.
- ✅ Policies in POMDPs are **history-dependent** $\pi(a \mid h_t)$ or **belief-dependent** $\pi(a \mid b_t)$.
- ✅ The Bayesian belief update is mathematically equivalent to a **Kalman filter** (linear-Gaussian) or **particle filter** (nonlinear).
- ✅ Always **state the role of $\eta$ explicitly** in KTU 2024 exam answers for full credit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actions. Examples - The Bandit Walk Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Actions. Examples - The Bandit Walk Environment # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of an Action in Reinforcement Learning In the formal framework of Reinforcement Learning (RL), an **Action** $A_t$ is the mechanism by which an **agent** intentionally influences its environment at a discrete time step $t$. The set of all valid actions available to the agent is termed the **Action Space**, denoted as $\mathcal{A}(s)$ (state-dependent) or simply $\mathcal{A}$ (state-independent). Formally, at every time step $t$, the agent observes the current state $S_t \in \mathcal{S}$ and selects an action $A_t \in \mathcal{A}(S_t)$, which causes the environment to transition to a new state $S_{t+1}$ and emit a scalar **reward** $R_{t+1} \in \mathbb{R}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > An action is a decision made by the agent from a predefined set of choices. The mapping from states to actions (or to action probabilities) constitutes the agent's **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Steering Wheel Imagine you are driving a car down a long road. - The **environment** is the road, traffic, and weather. - The **state** is your current speed, position, and surroundings. - The **action** is your hands turning the steering wheel **left**, **right**, or keeping it **straight**. - The **reward** is reaching your destination safely (positive) or having an accident (negative). Just as a driver cannot levitate the car (the action set is physically restricted), an RL agent is restricted to the actions defined by the environment's design. Choosing *which* action to take at each moment is the central problem that RL algorithms solve. > [!NOTE] > **Action Space Terminology (KTU High-Yield):** > - **Discrete Action Space** $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_k\}$ — e.g., {Left, Right, Up, Down}. > - **Continuous Action Space** $\mathcal{A} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ — e.g., steering angles from $-30^\circ$ to $+30^\circ$. --- ## 1.3 The Bandit Walk Environment — Overview The **Bandit Walk Environment** is a pedagogical **1-Dimensional Markov Decision Process (MDP)** used universally in introductory RL literature (notably Lapan, *Deep Reinforcement Learning Hands-On*, Ch. 2) to illustrate the interplay of states, actions, and rewards *before* introducing the complexity of stochastic multi-armed bandits. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Bandit" Walk?** > Despite its name, the Bandit Walk is **not** a multi-armed bandit. It borrows the name because it is the *simplest possible environment* (the "baby bandit" of MDPs) used to build intuition before tackling true $k$-armed bandits. ### Environment Specification | Parameter | Value / Description | |---|---| | **State Space** $\mathcal{S}$ | $\{S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4, S_5, S_6\}$ — a linear chain of **7 states** | | **Start State** | $S_3$ (the middle state) | | **Action Space** $\mathcal{A}$ | $\{0, 1\}$ — discrete binary actions | | **Action 0** | Move one step **LEFT** ($S_t \rightarrow S_{t-1}$) | | **Action 1** | Move one step **RIGHT** ($S_t \rightarrow S_{t+1}$) | | **Terminal States** | $S_0$ (LEFT exit) and $S_6$ (RIGHT exit) | | **Reward at $S_0$** | $R = -1$ (penalty — "pit of despair") | | **Reward at $S_6$** | $R = +1$ (reward — "goal of gold") | | **Reward elsewhere** | $R = 0$ | | **Episode Length** | Finite; ends upon reaching a terminal state | ### The Core Insight A perfectly rational agent will discover that choosing Action **1 (Right)** at every step guarantees a reward of $+1$ in exactly **3 steps**. Choosing Action **0 (Left)** leads to a reward of $-1$ in 3 steps. The Bandit Walk teaches that **actions have consequences that propagate through state transitions**, and the agent must learn a *sequence* of actions — not just an immediate choice. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Actions and the MDP Tuple The Bandit Walk is formally defined as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** tuple: $$\mathcal{M} = \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$$ where: - $\mathcal{S}$ — finite **state space** - $\mathcal{A}$ — finite **action space** - $P(s' \mid s, a)$ — **transition probability function** - $R(s, a, s')$ — **reward function** - $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ — **discount factor** ### Transition Dynamics in the Bandit Walk For the Bandit Walk, the transition function is **deterministic** (probability = 1): $$P(s_{t+1} = s - 1 \mid s_t = s, a_t = 0) = 1 \quad \text{(Move Left)}$$ $$P(s_{t+1} = s + 1 \mid s_t = s, a_t = 1) = 1 \quad \text{(Move Right)}$$ with **boundary protection** at the terminals: actions that would move the agent past $S_0$ or $S_6$ simply terminate the episode (no wrap-around). ### Reward Function $$R(s, a, s') = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } s' = S_6 \\ -1 & \text{if } s' = S_0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ --- ## 2.2 Why "Actions" Matter: The Agent-Environment Loop Every RL interaction follows a strict loop: 1. **Observe** the current state $S_t$. 2. **Select** an action $A_t$ using the policy $\pi(A_t \mid S_t)$. 3. **Execute** the action in the environment. 4. **Receive** the reward $R_{t+1}$ and the new state $S_{t+1}$. 5. **Update** the policy/value estimates. 6. **Repeat** from Step 1 until a terminal state is reached (one **episode**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Critical Concept:** > The agent's choice of action is the **only** mechanism by which it influences the world. The quality of an action is *not* judged by its immediate reward, but by the **cumulative reward** (the **return**) it enables over the long run. ### Return Definition The **return** $G_t$ is the cumulative discounted reward from time $t$ onward: $$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$$ For the Bandit Walk (finite horizon, no intermediate rewards), this simplifies to: $$G_t = R_{t+1}^{\text{terminal}}$$ because all intermediate rewards are zero and the episode terminates. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Action** | $A_t \in \mathcal{A}(S_t)$ | Discrete index or continuous vector | | 2 | **Action Space (Discrete)** | $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_k\}$ | Finite set of $k$ actions | | 3 | **Action Space (Continuous)** | $\mathcal{A} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ | $d$-dimensional real vector space | | 4 | **Deterministic Policy** | $a = \pi(s)$ | Maps state directly to action | | 5 | **Stochastic Policy** | $\pi(a \mid s) = P(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$ | Probability distribution over actions | | 6 | **State Transition** | $P(s' \mid s, a) = P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a)$ | Must sum to **1** over $s'$ | | 7 | **Reward Function** | $R(s, a, s') = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t+1} = s']$ | Scalar $\in \mathbb{R}$ | | 8 | **Return** | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | Discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ | | 9 | **Episode / Trial** | One full pass from $S_0$ to a terminal state | Used in episodic tasks | | 10 | **Optimal Policy (Bandit Walk)** | $\pi^{\star}(s) = 1$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$ | Always choose Action 1 (Right) | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Understanding the Bandit Walk is not academic busywork — it is the **conceptual backbone** of: - **Robotic locomotion**: A robot learning to walk uses similar 1-D action primitives ("step left foot", "step right foot"). - **Autonomous lane-keeping**: A vehicle must choose discrete actions (lane change left/right, maintain) given its state (position, velocity). - **Game AI**: A chess engine's "move action" follows the same MDP formalism. - **Industrial process control**: Discrete actuator choices (valve open / closed) at each time step mirror Bandit Walk's binary action space. > [!NOTE] > The complexity of real-world RL (Atari games, autonomous driving) is built by **composing** many Bandit-Walk-like decisions across millions of state-action pairs. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Value of States in the Bandit Walk To demonstrate *why* the Bandit Walk is solvable, we will compute the **state-value function** $v_\pi(s)$ under both the **optimal policy** and a **random policy**. ### Definition Recap The state-value function under policy $\pi$ is: $$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right] = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, S_t = s \right]$$ For the Bandit Walk, since intermediate rewards are 0 and the episode terminates upon reaching $S_0$ or $S_6$, we get: $$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{\text{terminal}} \mid S_t = s \right]$$ --- ### Derivation 1: Value Under the **Optimal Policy** $\pi^{\star}(s) = 1$ (Always Move Right) Starting from any non-terminal state $S_i$ where $0 < i \leq 6$: **Step 1 — Count the steps needed to reach $S_6$:** $$\text{Steps required} = 6 - i$$ For example, from $S_3$ we need $6 - 3 = 3$ steps. From $S_5$ we need $6 - 5 = 1$ step. **Step 2 — Identify the terminal reward received:** $$R_{\text{terminal}} = +1 \quad \text{(because we always go right)}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the undiscounted return:** Since the only reward is at the terminal step, and $\gamma$ is applied only to that single reward: $$G_t = \gamma^{(6 - i) - 1} \cdot (+1) = \gamma^{5 - i}$$ Wait — we need to be careful about the **indexing** of $\gamma$. In the return definition, $R_{t+k+1}$ is the reward received $k$ steps after $t$. If the terminal is reached at $t + (6-i)$, the reward occurs at $k = 6 - i - 1$ relative to the formula index, so: $$G_t = \gamma^{(6-i)-1} \cdot R_{\text{terminal}} = \gamma^{5-i} \cdot (+1) = \gamma^{5-i}$$ **Step 4 — Tabulate the optimal state values:** | State $S_i$ | Steps to Goal | $v_{\pi^{\star}}(S_i)$ (with $\gamma$) | $v_{\pi^{\star}}(S_i)$ (with $\gamma = 1$) | |---|---|---|---| | $S_6$ | 0 (terminal) | undefined / $+1$ | undefined / $+1$ | | $S_5$ | 1 | $\gamma^{0} = 1$ | $+1$ | | $S_4$ | 2 | $\gamma^{1}$ | $+1$ | | $S_3$ | 3 | $\gamma^{2}$ | $+1$ | | $S_2$ | 4 | $\gamma^{3}$ | $+1$ | | $S_1$ | 5 | $\gamma^{4}$ | $+1$ | | $S_0$ | 0 (terminal) | undefined / $-1$ | undefined / $-1$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insight:** With $\gamma = 1$ (no discounting), **every** non-terminal state has value $+1$ under the optimal policy. This is correct — because the agent deterministically reaches the goal, the expected return is always $+1$. --- ### Derivation 2: Value Under a **Random Policy** $\pi_{\text{rand}}(a \mid s) = 0.5$ Under a random policy, the agent chooses "left" or "right" with equal probability **0.5** at every step. **Step 1 — Setup the absorbing random walk:** We have 5 non-terminal states: $\{S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4, S_5\}$. Boundaries: $S_0$ (reward $-1$) and $S_6$ (reward $+1$). This is a classic **gambler's ruin** problem with $p = q = 0.5$ and target capital $N = 6$. **Step 2 — Apply the gambler's ruin probability of reaching $S_6$:** For a fair random walk starting at $S_i$, the probability of hitting $S_6$ *before* $S_0$ is: $$P(\text{reach } S_6 \mid \text{start at } S_i) = \frac{i}{6}$$ This is the standard result from Markov chain theory (the harmonic solution to the gambler's ruin problem). **Step 3 — Derive this result (exhaustive):** The function $f(i) = P(\text{reach } S_6 \mid S_i)$ satisfies the **Bellman equation** for the random walk: $$f(i) = 0.5 \cdot f(i-1) + 0.5 \cdot f(i+1) \quad \text{for } i \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$$ with boundary conditions $f(0) = 0$ and $f(6) = 1$. The recurrence $f(i) = 0.5[f(i-1) + f(i+1)]$ implies $f(i+1) - f(i) = f(i) - f(i-1)$, meaning the **first differences are constant**. Hence $f$ is linear: $$f(i) = f(0) + i \cdot [f(6) - f(0)] / 6 = 0 + i \cdot (1 - 0)/6 = \frac{i}{6}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the expected return at each state:** $$v_{\pi_{\text{rand}}}(S_i) = (+1) \cdot P(\text{reach } S_6) + (-1) \cdot P(\text{reach } S_0)$$ $$v_{\pi_{\text{rand}}}(S_i) = (+1) \cdot \frac{i}{6} + (-1) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{i}{6}\right)$$ $$v_{\pi_{\text{rand}}}(S_i) = \frac{i}{6} - 1 + \frac{i}{6}$$ $$v_{\pi_{\text{rand}}}(S_i) = \frac{2i}{6} - 1 = \frac{i}{3} - 1$$ **Step 5 — Tabulate the random-policy state values:** | State $S_i$ | $P(\text{reach } S_6) = i/6$ | $P(\text{reach } S_0) = 1 - i/6$ | $v_{\pi_{\text{rand}}}(S_i)$ | |---|---|---|---| | $S_1$ | $1/6 \approx 0.167$ | $5/6 \approx 0.833$ | $1/3 - 1 = -2/3$ | | $S_2$ | $2/6 = 1/3$ | $4/6 = 2/3$ | $2/3 - 1 = -1/3$ | | $S_3$ | $3/6 = 1/2$ | $3/6 = 1/2$ | $1 - 1 = 0$ | | $S_4$ | $4/6 = 2/3$ | $2/6 = 1/3$ | $4/3 - 1 = 1/3$ | | $S_5$ | $5/6$ | $1/6$ | $5/3 - 1 = 2/3$ | > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** The state values are *not* uniformly good. From $S_1$, a random agent loses money on average. The optimal agent gets $+1$ from every state. This is precisely what an RL algorithm must learn — that the *quality* of a state depends on the policy, not just the state itself. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Aspect | Optimal Policy $\pi^{\star}$ | Random Policy $\pi_{\text{rand}}$ | |---|---|---| | **Action Choice** | Always $A = 1$ (Right) | $A = 0$ or $1$ with prob 0.5 each | | **Value $v(S_3)$** | $+1$ | $0$ | | **Value $v(S_1)$** | $+1$ | $-2/3$ | | **Value $v(S_5)$** | $+1$ | $+2/3$ | | **Expected Episode Length** | 3 (from $S_3$) | Random, infinite variance possible | | **Total Expected Return** | $+1$ | $0$ | | **Learning Difficulty** | Trivial | Requires exploration–exploitation balance | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Agent–Environment Loop in the Bandit Walk ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Agent Policy pi] -->|Action At in 0,1| B(Bandit Walk Env) B -->|Reward R_t+1| C{Reward Signal} C -->|+1 if reach S6| D[Positive Reinforcement] C -->|-1 if reach S0| E[Negative Reinforcement] C -->|0 otherwise| F[Neutral Step] B -->|New State S_t+1| A D --> G[Update Value Estimates] E --> G F --> G G --> A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The agent sends an action; the environment returns both a new state and a reward. The reward is categorized (positive, negative, or neutral) and feeds back into the policy update loop. --- ## 4.2 State Transition Graph of the Bandit Walk ```mermaid flowchart LR S0((S0
Terminal
R=-1)) ---|Action 0| S1((S1)) S1((S1)) ---|Action 1| S0 S1((S1)) ---|Action 0| S2((S2)) S2((S2)) ---|Action 1| S1 S2((S2)) ---|Action 0| S3((S3
START)) S3((S3
START)) ---|Action 1| S2 S3((S3)) ---|Action 0| S4((S4)) S4((S4)) ---|Action 1| S3 S4((S4)) ---|Action 0| S5((S5)) S5((S5)) ---|Action 1| S4 S5((S5)) ---|Action 0| S6((S6
Terminal
R=+1)) S6((S6)) ---|Action 1| S5 ``` **Interpretation:** - The agent starts at $S_3$ (highlighted as START). - Moving **right** (Action 1) traverses $S_3 \to S_4 \to S_5 \to S_6$ and earns $+1$. - Moving **left** (Action 0) traverses $S_3 \to S_2 \to S_1 \to S_0$ and earns $-1$. - The edges are bidirectional, but the *agent* chooses the direction. --- ## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Action Selection & Learning Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Env[Environment: Bandit Walk] E1[Current State S_t] E2[Apply Action A_t] E3[Compute S_t+1 and R_t+1] end subgraph Agent[Agent Module] P1[Policy Network / Lookup Table] P2[Value Function Estimator] P3[Policy Updater] end E1 --> P1 P1 -->|Choose best A_t| E2 E2 --> E3 E3 -->|Tuple S_t, A_t, R_t+1, S_t+1| P2 P2 -->|TD Error / Update Signal| P3 P3 -->|Improved pi| P1 ``` **Explanation of the architecture:** 1. **Current State** $S_t$ is read from the environment. 2. The **Policy Network** (or table, in this simple case) maps $S_t \to A_t$. 3. The environment applies the action, producing $S_{t+1}$ and $R_{t+1}$. 4. The **Value Function Estimator** uses the tuple to compute a learning signal (e.g., Temporal-Difference error). 5. The **Policy Updater** adjusts the policy to favour better actions. 6. The loop continues until convergence to the optimal policy $\pi^{\star}(s) = 1$ for all $s$. --- ## 4.4 Decision Tree: What Does the Agent "See"? ```mermaid flowchart TD Q{Where am I?} Q -->|S0| R0[Terminal. Received R=-1. Stop.] Q -->|S1| R1{Action?} R1 -->|0: Left| T1a[Go to S0, R=-1] R1 -->|1: Right| T1b[Go to S2, R=0] Q -->|S2| R2{Action?} R2 -->|0: Left| T2a[Go to S1, R=0] R2 -->|1: Right| T2b[Go to S3, R=0] Q -->|S3| R3{Action?} R3 -->|0: Left| T3a[Go to S2, R=0] R3 -->|1: Right| T3b[Go to S4, R=0] Q -->|S4| R4{Action?} R4 -->|0: Left| T4a[Go to S3, R=0] R4 -->|1: Right| T4b[Go to S5, R=0] Q -->|S5| R5{Action?} R5 -->|0: Left| T5a[Go to S4, R=0] R5 -->|1: Right| T5b[Go to S6, R=+1] Q -->|S6| R6[Terminal. Received R=+1. Stop.] ``` > [!NOTE] > This decision tree is the **complete behavioral specification** of the Bandit Walk environment. It defines the action space and the deterministic outcomes — exactly what an RL algorithm needs to learn through interaction. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]` **Q: Define an "action" in Reinforcement Learning. Differentiate between discrete and continuous action spaces with one example each.** **Model Answer:** An **action** $A_t$ is the decision taken by an agent at time step $t$ that influences the environment's state transition. It is selected from the action space $\mathcal{A}(S_t)$ based on the current state $S_t$. - **Discrete Action Space:** A finite set of distinct actions. Example: In the **Bandit Walk Environment**, the action set is $\mathcal{A} = \{0, 1\}$, corresponding to "Move Left" and "Move Right". - **Continuous Action Space:** An interval or region in $\mathbb{R}^d$. Example: A robot arm choosing a joint angle $\theta \in [0^\circ, 180^\circ]$ for elbow rotation. The two differ in the algorithmic tools required: discrete actions permit enumeration and $\arg\max$ operations, while continuous actions require gradient-based optimization. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]` **Q: Describe the Bandit Walk Environment. What is the optimal action sequence from the start state?** **Model Answer:** The **Bandit Walk** is a 1-D Markov Decision Process with **7 states** $\{S_0, S_1, \dots, S_6\}$ arranged linearly. The agent starts at $S_3$ and can take two actions: **0 (Left)** or **1 (Right)**. The terminal state $S_0$ yields a reward of $-1$, while $S_6$ yields a reward of $+1$. All other states yield $R = 0$. **Optimal Action Sequence from $S_3$:** Choose Action 1 three times consecutively: $$S_3 \xrightarrow{A=1} S_4 \xrightarrow{A=1} S_5 \xrightarrow{A=1} S_6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = +1$$ This is optimal because it yields the maximum possible undiscounted return of $+1$ in the shortest path. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** Module-internal choice means you will be offered **either** Question A **or** Question B from this module. Both are provided below for thorough preparation. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** Explain the role of **actions** in the agent–environment interaction loop. Discuss the difference between deterministic and stochastic policies with reference to the Bandit Walk. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** For the Bandit Walk Environment with states $\{S_0, \dots, S_6\}$, start state $S_3$, and rewards $R(S_0) = -1$, $R(S_6) = +1$, $R(\text{others}) = 0$, compute the state-value function $v_{\pi_{\text{rand}}}(s)$ for all non-terminal states under a **uniform random policy** $\pi(a \mid s) = 0.5$ for both actions. Use the gambler's ruin formulation. **[7 Marks]** #### **Model Solution for (a):** **1. Role of Actions (3 marks):** - Actions are the agent's only means of influencing the environment. - The agent observes state $S_t$, selects $A_t \in \mathcal{A}(S_t)$, and receives $R_{t+1}$ and $S_{t+1}$. - The cumulative effect of actions over an episode determines the **return** $G_t$. **2. Deterministic Policy (2 marks):** - A deterministic policy $\pi: \mathcal{S} \to \mathcal{A}$ assigns exactly one action per state. - Example: In the Bandit Walk, $\pi^{\star}(s) = 1$ for all $s$ — always move right. **3. Stochastic Policy (2 marks):** - A stochastic policy $\pi(a \mid s) = P(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$ assigns a probability distribution over actions. - Example: $\pi_{\text{rand}}(a \mid s) = 0.5$ for both $a = 0$ and $a = 1$. - Stochastic policies are essential for **exploration** in RL. #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1:** Identify the boundary values. Since the episode ends upon reaching $S_0$ or $S_6$: $$v(S_0) = -1, \quad v(S_6) = +1$$ **Step 2:** Write the Bellman equation for the random policy at any non-terminal state $S_i$, $i \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$: $$v(S_i) = 0.5 \cdot v(S_{i-1}) + 0.5 \cdot v(S_{i+1})$$ **Step 3:** Solve the linear system. The recurrence $v(S_i) = \frac{v(S_{i-1}) + v(S_{i+1})}{2}$ implies $v$ is **linear** in $i$. Using the boundary values $v(S_0) = -1$ and $v(S_6) = +1$: $$v(S_i) = -1 + \frac{i}{6} \cdot [v(S_6) - v(S_0)] = -1 + \frac{i}{6} \cdot 2 = \frac{i}{3} - 1$$ **Step 4:** Tabulate the results: | State $S_i$ | $v_{\pi_{\text{rand}}}(S_i)$ | |---|---| | $S_1$ | $-2/3$ | | $S_2$ | $-1/3$ | | $S_3$ | $0$ | | $S_4$ | $+1/3$ | | $S_5$ | $+2/3$ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing reward with value** — Many students write $R(S_0) = -1$ everywhere, forgetting that the value is the *expected* cumulative reward under the *policy*. > 2. **Forgetting the boundary conditions** — The recurrence $v(S_i) = 0.5 v(S_{i-1}) + 0.5 v(S_{i+1})$ alone is insufficient; you MUST state $v(S_0) = -1$ and $v(S_6) = +1$ to solve it. > 3. **Mixing up the random policy and optimal policy** — The optimal policy yields $v(S_i) = +1$ for all $i \in \{1, \dots, 5\}$; the random policy yields a linear gradient. Examiners will check. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** With the aid of a clearly labeled **state-transition diagram**, describe the Bandit Walk Environment. Specify the state space, action space, start state, terminal states, and reward function. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Suppose an agent uses an $\varepsilon$-greedy policy with $\varepsilon = 0.2$ in the Bandit Walk. Starting from $S_3$, simulate the next **3 episodes** if the Q-values are initialized to $Q(s, a) = 0$ for all $s, a$, and the rewards are as defined. Show how the Q-values get updated using the rule $Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha [R - Q(s, a)]$ with $\alpha = 0.5$. **[7 Marks]** #### **Model Solution for (a):** **1. State Space (1 mark):** $\mathcal{S} = \{S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4, S_5, S_6\}$ — 7 states on a 1-D line. **2. Action Space (1 mark):** $\mathcal{A} = \{0, 1\}$ — "Left" and "Right" respectively. **3. Start State (1 mark):** $S_3$ — the central state. **4. Terminal States and Rewards (2 marks):** - $S_0$ is terminal with $R = -1$. - $S_6$ is terminal with $R = +1$. - All non-terminal intermediate states yield $R = 0$. **5. Transition Diagram (2 marks):** A linear chain connecting consecutive states, with bidirectional arrows labeled by the action that triggers each transition. (See Section 4.2 for the full diagram.) #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Setup:** - $\varepsilon$-greedy means: with probability $0.8$, choose $\arg\max_a Q(s, a)$; with probability $0.2$, choose randomly. - Q-values start at $Q(s, a) = 0$ for all entries. - Learning rule: $Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha [R - Q(s, a)] = 0.5 \cdot R$ (since $Q = 0$ initially). **Episode 1:** - At $S_3$: $\arg\max Q(s, a) = 0$ for both, so tie-break chooses $A = 0$ (left). Suppose the random $0.2$ path is taken: $A = 1$ (right). Then $S_3 \to S_4$. - At $S_4$: similarly, suppose $A = 1$. $S_4 \to S_5$. - At $S_5$: $A = 1$. $S_5 \to S_6$. **Terminal! $R = +1$.** - Q-updates (in reverse, with $\alpha = 0.5$): - $Q(S_5, 1) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \cdot (+1 - 0) = +0.5$ - $Q(S_4, 1) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \cdot (0 - 0) = 0$ (intermediate reward is 0) **Episode 2:** - At $S_3$: greedy argmax still gives 0. Random action: $A = 0$. $S_3 \to S_2$. - At $S_2$: Random $A = 0$. $S_2 \to S_1$. - At $S_1$: Random $A = 0$. $S_1 \to S_0$. **Terminal! $R = -1$.** - Q-updates: - $Q(S_1, 0) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \cdot (-1 - 0) = -0.5$ - $Q(S_2, 0) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \cdot (0 - 0) = 0$ **Episode 3:** - At $S_3$: Suppose greedy path now strongly favours $A = 0$ from past updates. With $\varepsilon = 0.2$, we explore: $A = 1$. $S_3 \to S_4$. - At $S_4$: $\arg\max Q$ — both are 0. Greedy chooses $A = 0$ (tie-break). $S_4 \to S_3$. - At $S_3$: Greedy $A = 0$. $S_3 \to S_2$. - ... (continues until termination or maximum steps). **Final Q-table (representative):** | State | $Q(s, 0)$ | $Q(s, 1)$ | |---|---|---| | $S_1$ | $-0.5$ | $0$ | | $S_2$ | $0$ | $0$ | | $S_3$ | $0$ | $0$ | | $S_4$ | $0$ | $0$ | | $S_5$ | $0$ | $+0.5$ | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall — Q-Learning Update Rule:** > 1. Students frequently confuse the **sample reward** $R$ with the **estimated return**. In the Bandit Walk, intermediate rewards are 0, so the update is $Q \leftarrow 0.5 \cdot 0 = 0$ for non-terminal steps. Do NOT write $Q \leftarrow 0.5 \cdot 1$ at every step. > 2. The $\varepsilon$-greedy action selection is **separate** from the update — the update uses the *actually executed* action, not the greedy one. > 3. In KTU exams, **always specify the random tie-breaking rule** when both actions have equal Q-values. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Actions & Bandit Walk** - [x] **Action** is the agent's choice at time $t$, drawn from $\mathcal{A}(S_t)$. - [x] **Action Space** can be **discrete** (finite set) or **continuous** (subset of $\mathbb{R}^d$). - [x] **Deterministic policy** $\pi(s)$ outputs one action; **stochastic policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ outputs a distribution. - [x] The **Bandit Walk** is a **1-D MDP** with 7 states, 2 actions, start at $S_3$. - [x] **Terminal rewards:** $R(S_0) = -1$, $R(S_6) = +1$, $R(\text{others}) = 0$. - [x] **Optimal policy:** always Action 1 (Right); yields $v^{\star}(S_i) = +1$ for all $i \in \{1, \dots, 5\}$. - [x] **Random policy:** yields $v_{\text{rand}}(S_i) = \frac{i}{3} - 1$ — a linear gradient from $-2/3$ to $+2/3$. - [x] The Bandit Walk illustrates the **exploration–exploitation trade-off**: greedy always goes one way, $\varepsilon$-greedy samples both. - [x] **Boundary protection:** $S_0$ and $S_6$ are absorbing — no wrap-around. - [x] **Return** in this environment is the terminal reward, because all intermediate rewards are 0. - [x] The **gambler's ruin probability** $\frac{i}{6}$ is the closed-form probability of reaching the right terminal from state $S_i$ under uniform random actions. - [x] **Action selection** is the *only* causal mechanism the agent has — no "teleportation" or hidden controls. - [x] For KTU exams: always state **boundary values** and **policy assumptions** explicitly before solving MDP problems.

topics.title: Agent-Environment Interaction Cycle. Markov Decision Process (MDP): The Engine of the Environment - States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Agent-Environment Interaction Cycle. Markov Decision Process (MDP): The Engine of the Environment - States ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Agent–Environment Interaction Cycle The **Agent–Environment Interaction Cycle** is the foundational loop of Reinforcement Learning (RL) that formalizes sequential decision-making. At each discrete time step $t$, the **agent** observes the **state** of the **environment**, selects an **action** according to a **policy**, executes it, and receives a numerical **reward** signal along with the next state. Formally, the cycle is expressed as the tuple: $$ \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle $$ where $\mathcal{S}$ is the state space, $\mathcal{A}$ is the action space, $\mathcal{P}$ is the state transition probability function, $\mathcal{R}$ is the reward function, and $\gamma \in [0,1]$ is the discount factor. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The interaction cycle is the *operational definition* of RL. Without this loop, there is no learning — only static optimization. #### Conceptual Analogy — "Training a Robotic Vacuum Cleaner" Imagine teaching a robotic vacuum to clean a room. The **robot (agent)** looks at its current position, dirt sensors, and battery level (**state**). It decides whether to move forward, turn, or dock (**action**). After moving, it gets feedback: a clean spot adds a small positive **reward**, hitting a wall gives a negative reward, and its sensors update with the new position (**next state**). Over thousands of such cycles, the robot *learns* a strategy to maximize total clean floor area before the battery dies. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The agent does *not* receive instructions on *how* to act. It must discover an optimal behavior purely through trial-and-error interaction with the environment. --- ### 1.2 Markov Decision Process (MDP) — The Engine of the Environment A **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** is the mathematical engine that drives the environment's response to the agent's actions. It is a discrete-time stochastic control process used for modeling decision-making in situations where outcomes are partly random and partly under the control of the decision-maker. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Sutton & Barto, 2018 — verified):** An MDP is a tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle$ where $\mathcal{S}$ is a finite set of states, $\mathcal{A}$ is a finite set of actions, $\mathcal{P}$ is the state transition probability function, $\mathcal{R}$ is the reward function, and $\gamma$ is the discount factor. --- ### 1.3 States — The Snapshot of the World A **state** $s_t \in \mathcal{S}$ is a complete description of the environment at time $t$. It is the *information packet* the agent uses to decide its next action. > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** A state $s_t$ is said to possess the **Markov Property** if and only if it captures *all* relevant information from the history of interactions up to time $t$. Mathematically: > $$\mathbb{P}[s_{t+1} \mid s_t] = \mathbb{P}[s_{t+1} \mid s_1, s_2, \dots, s_t]$$ In simple words: **the future is conditionally independent of the past, given the present.** #### Real-World Analogy — "Chess Without Memory" Consider a chess game. If you are shown only the *current arrangement* of pieces on the board (and not how the game got there), you have enough information to decide your next move. The current state is **Markov** because it contains all the history needed for future predictions. In contrast, if you were shown only the positions of *your* pieces, the state would **not** be Markov — the opponent's hidden arrangement matters. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** If your state representation requires memory of past events to predict the future, your state is **not** fully Markov. Always engineer state features (e.g., velocity in addition to position) to achieve Markovianity. --- ### 1.4 Types of State Spaces | State Type | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Discrete State Space** | Finite, countable set of states | Chess board positions ($64$ squares, finite configurations) | | **Continuous State Space** | Infinite, uncountable set of states (vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$) | Cart-pole with $\theta, \dot{\theta}, x, \dot{x}$ as continuous variables | | **Fully Observable** | Agent sees the true state $s_t$ | Chess — board is fully visible | | **Partially Observable** | Agent sees observation $o_t \neq s_t$ (POMDP) | Poker — opponent's hidden cards | > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Confusing **observation** with **state**. In fully observable MDPs, $o_t = s_t$. In Partially Observable MDPs (POMDPs), $o_t \neq s_t$, and the agent must maintain a belief state. ## 2.
