Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of the Interaction Cycle — Step by Step The agent–environment interaction proceeds as follows at each time step $t$: 1. **Perceive:** The agent observes the current state $S_t = s \in \mathcal{S}$. 2. **Decide:** Using its policy $\pi(a \mid s)$, the agent selects an action $A_t = a \in \mathcal{A}$. 3. **Act:** The environment receives action $a$ at time $t$. 4. **Transition:** The environment transitions to a new state $S_{t+1} = s'$ sampled from the transition dynamics $\mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)$. 5. **Reward:** The environment emits a scalar reward $R_{t+1} = r \in \mathbb{R}$ from the reward function $\mathcal{R}(s, a, s')$. 6. **Learn:** The agent updates its policy/value estimates using the tuple $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$. 7. **Repeat:** Set $t \leftarrow t+1$ and goto step 1. > [!NOTE] > **Why this order?** The reward $R_{t+1}$ is indexed by $t+1$ because it is a *consequence* of the action taken at time $t$, observed at time $t+1$. --- ### 2.2 The Markov Property — Formal Decomposition The Markov Property can be decomposed into two conditional independence statements: $$ \mathbb{P}(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t-1}, A_{t-1}, \dots) = \mathbb{P}(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a) $$ $$ \mathbb{P}(R_{t+1} = r \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t+1} = s', \dots) = \mathbb{P}(R_{t+1} = r \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t+1} = s') $$ Both equations say the same thing: **history is irrelevant once you condition on the present state and action.** --- ### 2.3 State Transition Probability and Reward Function The **state transition probability** $\mathcal{P}$ defines the dynamics of the environment: $$ \mathcal{P}_{ss'}^{a} = \mathbb{P}[S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a] $$ This must satisfy the **probability axiom**: $$ \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \mathcal{P}_{ss'}^{a} = 1, \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}, \; \forall a \in \mathcal{A} $$ The **reward function** $\mathcal{R}$ specifies the immediate feedback: $$ \mathcal{R}_{s}^{a} = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a] $$ Alternatively, the reward can depend on the next state: $$ \mathcal{R}_{ss'}^{a} = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t+1} = s'] $$ --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the KTU 2024 high-yield formula sheet for this topic. Memorize these for ESE.** | Symbol | Name | Formula / Definition | Domain / Constraint | |---|---|---|---| | $\mathcal{S}$ | State Space | Set of all possible states $s$ | Finite or infinite | | $\mathcal{A}$ | Action Space | Set of all possible actions $a$ | Finite or infinite | | $\mathcal{P}_{ss'}^{a}$ | Transition Probability | $\mathbb{P}[S_{t+1}=s' \mid S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | $\in [0,1]$, $\sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}_{ss'}^{a} = 1$ | | $\mathcal{R}_{s}^{a}$ | Expected Reward | $\mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | $\in \mathbb{R}$ | | $\gamma$ | Discount Factor | Weight on future rewards | $\gamma \in [0,1]$ | | $G_t$ | Return (cumulative reward) | $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | Finite if $\gamma < 1$ | | $G_t^{(h)}$ | Finite-horizon Return | $\sum_{k=0}^{h} R_{t+k+1}$ | Episodic tasks | | $\pi(a \mid s)$ | Stochastic Policy | $\mathbb{P}[A_t=a \mid S_t=s]$ | $\sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) = 1$ | | Markov Property | Memoryless property | $\mathbb{P}[S_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t] = \mathbb{P}[S_{t+1} \mid S_1, \dots, S_t, A_t]$ | Holds iff state is complete | | Episode | Task with terminal state | Sequence of $T$ steps ending in $S_{terminal}$ | $T$ may be fixed or random | | Continuing Task | Task with no terminal state | Infinite horizon | Requires $\gamma < 1$ for convergence | --- ### 2.5 The Return — Bridging States to Long-Term Value The agent's goal is to maximize the **expected return** $G_t$ starting from time $t$: $$ G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \dots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} $$ This can be written recursively (a key relationship for bootstrapping algorithms like TD-learning): $$ G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** This recursive form is the mathematical backbone of nearly all RL algorithms. Bellman equations, TD($\lambda$), Q-learning — all exploit this self-similar structure. --- ### 2.6 Episodic vs. Continuing Tasks | Aspect | Episodic Task | Continuing Task | |---|---|---| | **Termination** | Has a terminal state $S_{terminal}$ | No terminal state — runs forever | | **Return** | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | | **Convergence** | Always finite | Finite only if $\gamma < 1$ | | **Example** | A chess game, a maze run | A standing thermostat, lifelong trading bot | --- ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications > [!IMPORTANT] > MDPs are not just academic toys — they power: > - **Robotics:** Locomotion, manipulation, autonomous driving (state = sensor fusion output) > - **Healthcare:** Treatment policies for chronic diseases (state = patient vitals) > - **Finance:** Portfolio management (state = market indicators) > - **Operations Research:** Inventory control, supply chain optimization > - **Game AI:** AlphaGo, OpenAI Five (state = board/frame observations) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Markov Property from First Principles **Goal:** Show that a state with the Markov Property makes history redundant for predicting the future. **Step 1 — Write the chain rule of probability** for the joint distribution of state-action-reward history. By the chain rule of probability, the probability of an entire trajectory $(S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, \dots, S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$ is: $$ \mathbb{P}(S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, \dots, S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}) = \prod_{k=0}^{t} \mathbb{P}(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k) $$ This is valid because each next state and reward depends only on the current state and action — this is the *definition* of the transition dynamics. **Step 2 — Identify what the agent needs to predict** the next state distribution. The agent needs $\mathbb{P}(S_{t+1} \mid \text{history})$. Using conditional probability: $$ \mathbb{P}(S_{t+1} \mid S_0, A_0, R_1, \dots, S_t, A_t) = \frac{\mathbb{P}(S_{t+1}, R_{t+1}, S_t, A_t, \dots)}{\mathbb{P}(S_t, A_t, \dots)} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the Markov assumption** to simplify the numerator. The Markov assumption states that the next state and reward depend *only* on the immediate previous state and action: $$ \mathbb{P}(S_{t+1}, R_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t) = \mathbb{P}(S_{t+1}, R_{t+1} \mid S_0, A_0, \dots, S_t, A_t) $$ **Step 4 — Conclude** the simplification. Substituting back: $$ \mathbb{P}(S_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t) = \mathbb{P}(S_{t+1} \mid S_0, A_0, \dots, S_t, A_t) $$ This is the formal statement of the **Markov Property**. The future $(S_{t+1})$ is conditionally independent of the entire history given the present $(S_t, A_t)$. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Recursive Form of the Return **Goal:** Show that $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$. **Step 1 — Write the definition of $G_t$ explicitly.** $$ G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \dots $$ **Step 2 — Factor out the immediate reward and group the remaining terms.** $$ G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \left( R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+4} + \dots \right) $$ **Step 3 — Recognize the bracketed expression as $G_{t+1}$.** By definition, the return starting at $t+1$ is: $$ G_{t+1} = R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+4} + \dots $$ **Step 4 — Conclude the recursion.** $$ G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \quad \blacksquare $$ This is the **Bellman-style recursion** at the heart of nearly all RL algorithms. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Example:** A simple 3-state MDP with the following dynamics. The agent navigates states $s_1, s_2, s_3$, with $s_3$ being terminal. | State $s$ | Action $a$ | Next State $s'$ | Probability $\mathcal{P}_{ss'}^{a}$ | Reward $\mathcal{R}_{ss'}^{a}$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | $s_1$ | $a_1$ | $s_2$ | $0.7$ | $+5$ | | $s_1$ | $a_1$ | $s_1$ | $0.3$ | $-1$ | | $s_1$ | $a_2$ | $s_3$ | $1.0$ | $+10$ | | $s_2$ | $a_1$ | $s_1$ | $0.5$ | $0$ | | $s_2$ | $a_1$ | $s_3$ | $0.5$ | $-2$ | | $s_2$ | $a_2$ | $s_2$ | $1.0$ | $+1$ | | $s_3$ | — | $s_3$ | $1.0$ | $0$ (terminal) | **Step 1 — Verify the probability axiom** for state $s_1$, action $a_1$: $$ \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}_{s_1 s'}^{a_1} = 0.7 + 0.3 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 2 — Compute the expected reward** for state $s_1$, action $a_1$: $$ \mathcal{R}_{s_1}^{a_1} = \mathbb{E}[R \mid s_1, a_1] = \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}_{s_1 s'}^{a_1} \cdot \mathcal{R}_{s_1 s'}^{a_1} $$ $$ \mathcal{R}_{s_1}^{a_1} = (0.7)(+5) + (0.3)(-1) = 3.5 - 0.3 = 3.2 $$ **Step 3 — Compute the expected reward** for state $s_1$, action $a_2$: $$ \mathcal{R}_{s_1}^{a_2} = (1.0)(+10) = 10.0 $$ **Step 4 — Decision insight:** At state $s_1$, the agent should *prefer* $a_2$ if it cares only about immediate reward ($10 > 3.2$). However, taking $a_2$ ends the episode (terminal), while $a_1$ allows further accumulation. The optimal choice depends on the *long-term return*, not just the immediate reward — this is precisely why we need value functions and Bellman equations. **Step 5 — Compute $G_0$ for one sample trajectory** $[s_1, a_2, s_3, \text{terminal}]$ with $\gamma = 0.9$: $$ G_0 = R_1 + \gamma R_2 + \gamma^2 R_3 + \dots = 10 + 0.9(0) + 0 = 10 $$ **Step 6 — Compute $G_0$ for another trajectory** $[s_1, a_1, s_2, a_2, s_2, a_1, s_1, a_1, s_2, \dots]$ (continuing indefinitely) with $\gamma = 0.9$: Since the return involves an infinite sum weighted by $\gamma^k$, the geometric series guarantees: $$ G_0 \le \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k \cdot R_{\max} = \frac{R_{\max}}{1 - \gamma} $$ For $R_{\max} = 5$ and $\gamma = 0.9$: $$ G_0 \le \frac{5}{1 - 0.9} = 50 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This bound (called the **effective horizon**) tells us that the agent effectively plans only about $1/(1-\gamma)$ steps into the future, even for infinite-horizon problems. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Agent–Environment Interaction Cycle The following block diagram captures the canonical RL loop with state, action, reward, and observation signals. ```mermaid flowchart LR agentNode[AGENT
Policy pi and Value Function] envNode[ENVIRONMENT
MDP Dynamics] stateNode[STATE S_t
s in scriptS] actNode[ACTION A_t
a in scriptA] rewNode[REWARD R_t+1
scalar real number] nextNode[NEXT STATE S_t+1
s prime in scriptS] obsNode[OBSERVATION O_t
equals S_t in MDP] timeNode[TIME t increments] stateNode -->|perceived by| agentNode agentNode -->|selects via policy pi| actNode actNode -->|executed in| envNode envNode -->|transitions to| nextNode envNode -->|emits scalar| rewNode nextNode -->|becomes| stateNode rewNode -->|feeds back to| agentNode timeNode -->|drives| envNode stateNode -.->|fully observable| obsNode obsNode -.->|fed into| agentNode ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: MDP State Transition Schematic This diagram visualizes how a single transition $\langle s, a, r, s' \rangle$ unfolds in the MDP engine. ```mermaid flowchart TD curState[CURRENT STATE S_t equals s] policyNode[POLICY pi of a given s] chosenAct[ACTION A_t equals a] transFn[TRANSITION FUNCTION
scriptP s to s prime given a] rewardFn[REWARD FUNCTION
scriptR s a s prime] nextState[NEXT STATE S_t+1 equals s prime] rewardOut[REWARD R_t+1 equals r] transProb[scriptP subscript s s prime superscript a] curState -->|input| policyNode policyNode -->|stochastic output| chosenAct chosenAct -->|input| transFn chosenAct -->|input| rewardFn transFn -->|produces| nextState rewardFn -->|produces| rewardOut transProb -.->|governs| transFn nextState -->|feeds next cycle| curState rewardOut -->|updates agent| policyNode ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Taxonomy of RL Task Types ```mermaid flowchart TD rootTask[RL TASK CLASSIFICATION] epTask[EPISODIC TASKS
finite T, terminal state exists] contTask[CONTINUING TASKS
infinite horizon, no terminal] discState[DISCRETE STATE SPACE
finite cardinality] contState[CONTINUOUS STATE SPACE
subset of real n] fullObs[FULLY OBSERVABLE
O_t equals S_t] partObs[PARTIALLY OBSERVABLE
O_t differs from S_t, POMDP] mdpCore[STANDARD MDP
fully observable] pomdpCore[POMDP
requires belief state] rootTask --> epTask rootTask --> contTask rootTask --> discState rootTask --> contState rootTask --> fullObs rootTask --> partObs fullObs --> mdpCore partObs --> pomdpCore ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Arrows indicate the *direction of information flow* (not causality). Dotted arrows (`.->`) indicate derived/secondary relationships. Each block represents a functional component of the RL system, not a physical object. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Dec 2024]** > **Define the Markov Property with respect to a state in a Reinforcement Learning environment. Why is it considered fundamental to MDP formulation?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Markov Property** states that the future evolution of a system depends only on the present state, not on the sequence of past states that led to it. Formally: $$ \mathbb{P}[S_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t] = \mathbb{P}[S_{t+1} \mid S_1, S_2, \dots, S_t, A_t] $$ **Significance:** (1 mark for statement, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for significance) - It enables **memoryless** decision-making, simplifying the learning problem. - It allows the use of **Bellman equations** and dynamic programming. - It guarantees that the state is a **sufficient statistic** for the future — the agent need not track history. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** > **Differentiate between an Episodic task and a Continuing task in Reinforcement Learning. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Episodic Task | Continuing Task | |---|---|---| | **Horizon** | Finite — ends at a terminal state | Infinite — never terminates | | **Return** | Sum over a finite number of steps | Sum over infinite steps (requires $\gamma < 1$) | | **Example** | A game of chess (one match = one episode) | A thermostat continuously regulating room temperature | (2 marks for differentiation table, 1 mark for examples) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern) > **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, June 2025]** **(a)** Explain the Agent–Environment Interaction Cycle in Reinforcement Learning with a neat block diagram. Clearly describe the role of policy, reward signal, and value function. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider a 3-state MDP with states $S = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, actions $A = \{a_1, a_2\}$, and the following transition and reward specifications: | From $s$ | Action $a$ | To $s'$ | Probability | Reward | |---|---|---|---|---| | $s_1$ | $a_1$ | $s_1$ | $0.4$ | $+2$ | | $s_1$ | $a_1$ | $s_2$ | $0.6$ | $+1$ | | $s_1$ | $a_2$ | $s_2$ | $0.5$ | $+3$ | | $s_1$ | $a_2$ | $s_3$ | $0.5$ | $+4$ | | $s_2$ | $a_1$ | $s_3$ | $1.0$ | $+5$ | | $s_2$ | $a_2$ | $s_1$ | $1.0$ | $-1$ | | $s_3$ | $a_1$ | $s_3$ | $1.0$ | $0$ | | $s_3$ | $a_2$ | $s_3$ | $1.0$ | $0$ | Compute the expected reward for taking each action from state $s_1$. Which action would a *myopic* (immediate-reward) agent prefer? Justify. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — Agent–Environment Interaction Cycle (7 marks):** The RL cycle proceeds as follows at each discrete time step $t$: **Step 1 (1 mark):** The agent **observes** the current state $S_t = s$ of the environment. The state encodes all relevant information needed for decision-making (Markov property). **Step 2 (2 marks):** The agent uses its **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ — a mapping from states to action probabilities — to select an action $A_t = a$. A policy can be: - **Deterministic:** $a = \pi(s)$ — always choose the same action in a given state. - **Stochastic:** $\pi(a \mid s) = \mathbb{P}[A_t = a \mid S_t = s]$ — actions are sampled from a distribution. **Step 3 (1 mark):** The environment **executes** the action. Internally, the environment's dynamics $\mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)$ determine the next state, and the reward function $\mathcal{R}(s, a, s')$ determines the immediate feedback. **Step 4 (1 mark):** The agent receives a **reward** $R_{t+1}$ (a scalar real number) — positive signals indicate progress toward the goal, negative signals indicate failure or cost. **Step 5 (1 mark):** The agent uses the **value function** $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ to evaluate *how good* being in state $s$ is under the current policy. The cycle repeats, and the policy is updated to maximize long-term expected return. **Step 6 (1 mark):** The cycle generates a trajectory: $S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, S_2, \dots$ --- **Part (b) — Expected Reward Calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1 (2 marks):** Expected reward for $(s_1, a_1)$: $$ \mathcal{R}_{s_1}^{a_1} = \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}_{s_1 s'}^{a_1} \cdot \mathcal{R}_{s_1 s'}^{a_1} $$ $$ \mathcal{R}_{s_1}^{a_1} = (0.4)(+2) + (0.6)(+1) = 0.8 + 0.6 = 1.4 $$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Expected reward for $(s_1, a_2)$: $$ \mathcal{R}_{s_1}^{a_2} = (0.5)(+3) + (0.5)(+4) = 1.5 + 2.0 = 3.5 $$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Myopic comparison: A myopic agent maximizes **only the immediate expected reward**: $$ \mathcal{R}_{s_1}^{a_2} = 3.5 > \mathcal{R}_{s_1}^{a_1} = 1.4 $$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Conclusion: The myopic agent would prefer action $a_2$ (expected reward 3.5 vs. 1.4). **Caveat (1 mark reserved in step 4):** This is *not necessarily* the optimal long-term choice. For example, $a_1$ from $s_1$ leads to $s_2$ with probability 0.6, and from $s_2$ action $a_1$ deterministically yields reward $+5$. The total expected return over multiple steps may favor $a_1$ — a non-myopic analysis using Bellman equations would be required to find the truly optimal policy. --- > **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Alternate]** **(a)** Define a Markov Decision Process (MDP) formally. Write and explain the Markov Property. Why is the Markov Property critical for tractable learning? **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a continuing task, derive the recursive form of the return $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$. If $\gamma = 0.95$ and the agent receives a constant reward of $+1$ at every step, compute the expected return from $t=0$. What does this tell you about the effective planning horizon? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — MDP Formal Definition (7 marks):** **Step 1 (2 marks):** Formal definition of MDP: An MDP is a tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle$ where: - $\mathcal{S}$ — finite (or infinite) set of states. - $\mathcal{A}$ — finite (or infinite) set of actions. - $\mathcal{P}: \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \times \mathcal{S} \to [0,1]$ — state transition probability function. - $\mathcal{R}: \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \times \mathcal{S} \to \mathbb{R}$ — reward function. - $\gamma \in [0,1]$ — discount factor. **Step 2 (2 marks):** The Markov Property states: $$ \mathbb{P}(S_{t+1} \mid S_t, A_t) = \mathbb{P}(S_{t+1} \mid S_1, A_1, S_2, A_2, \dots, S_t, A_t) $$ In words: **the future is conditionally independent of the past given the present.** **Step 3 (2 marks):** Why it is critical: - It **eliminates the need to store history** — the current state is a sufficient statistic. - It enables the use of **dynamic programming** and the **Bellman equation**. - It makes the problem **tractable** — without it, the state space would grow exponentially with the episode length. **Step 4 (1 mark):** Example: In chess, the board configuration is Markovian. A partial state (e.g., "I have a queen") is not — the opponent's pieces matter. --- **Part (b) — Return Recursion and Effective Horizon (7 marks):** **Step 1 (3 marks):** Derive the recursion: $$ G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \dots $$ Factor out the first reward and group: $$ G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma (R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+4} + \dots) $$ The bracketed term is, by definition, $G_{t+1}$: $$ G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \quad \blacksquare $$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the expected return for $R_{t+k+1} = 1$ for all $k \geq 0$ and $\gamma = 0.95$: $$ G_0 = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} \cdot 1 = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (0.95)^{k} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.95} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20 $$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Effective planning horizon: The "effective horizon" is $1/(1-\gamma) = 1/0.05 = 20$ steps. After about 20 steps, the contribution of future rewards becomes negligible (e.g., $\gamma^{20} \approx 0.36$, $\gamma^{40} \approx 0.13$, $\gamma^{100} \approx 0.006$). This means the agent behaves as if it plans only ~20 steps into the future, even though the task is technically infinite-horizon. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Reward indexing confusion (–1 mark typical):** Many students write $R_t$ instead of $R_{t+1}$ for the reward received *after* taking action $A_t$. The reward is always *one step delayed* — remember the timeline: $S_t \to A_t \to R_{t+1} \to S_{t+1}$. > 2. **Forgetting the probability axiom (–1 to –2 marks):** In calculation questions, students often forget to *verify* $\sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}_{ss'}^{a} = 1$. Examiners often give a "trick" transition table that violates this — always check and state the axiom explicitly. > 3. **Confusing state with observation (–1 mark):** In POMDP-related questions, students conflate $o_t$ with $s_t$. In a fully observable MDP, $o_t = s_t$; in a POMDP, $o_t \neq s_t$. State the observability assumption explicitly. > 4. **Skipping the justification (–1 to –2 marks):** A "myopic" answer that *only* states a numerical answer without justifying the comparison loses credit. Always pair the calculation with a sentence explaining the reasoning. > 5. **Geometric series mistakes in return calculations (–2 marks):** For continuing tasks, the return is an infinite geometric series. The closed form $\frac{1}{1-\gamma}$ applies only when the reward is constant. For varying rewards, sum term-by-term or use the recursive relation. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam** - ✅ The **Agent–Environment Interaction Cycle** has three core signals: **state**, **action**, and **reward**, with the policy $\pi$ governing action selection. - ✅ An **MDP** is the tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle$ — memorize this exactly. - ✅ The **Markov Property** means the current state is a **sufficient statistic** for the future. Verify it before applying Bellman equations. - ✅ The **reward index** is $R_{t+1}$ — it is a *consequence* of the action at time $t$. - ✅ **Transition probabilities** must sum to 1 over the next state: $\sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}_{ss'}^{a} = 1$. - ✅ The **return** is $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$, and the **recursive form** $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ is the cornerstone of bootstrapping. - ✅ **Episodic tasks** have a terminal state and a finite horizon; **continuing tasks** require $\gamma < 1$ to have a finite return. - ✅ The **effective horizon** is approximately $1/(1-\gamma)$ steps. - ✅ A **fully observable** MDP has $o_t = s_t$; a **POMDP** has $o_t \neq s_t$ and requires a belief state. - ✅ **State space** can be **discrete** (finite) or **continuous** (subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$). - ✅ The **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ is a probability distribution over actions given a state. - ✅ The **value function** $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ measures the long-term desirability of a state under policy $\pi$. - ✅ The agent's **goal** is to find a policy $\pi^*$ that maximizes the expected return from every state. - ✅ **Bellman equations** (covered in the next module) are the natural extension of the return recursion — make sure you understand the recursion *before* moving on.

topics.title: Actions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Actions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Reinforcement Learning (RL)**, an **Action** (denoted by the symbol $a$ or $A_t$ for an action taken at time step $t$) is a formal, mathematically defined decision or control input that an **agent** selects from a pre-specified set of allowable choices called the **Action Space**, in order to transition from its current **State** $S_t$ in the **Environment** to a new state $S_{t+1}$.

The complete collection of all possible actions available to an agent is formally expressed as:

$$\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_n\}$$

where $n = \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ is the cardinality (total count) of the action space, which may be **finite (discrete)** or **infinite (continuous)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An action is the mechanism by which an agent exerts influence on its environment. It is the only channel through which the agent's **Policy** $\pi$ expresses itself to the external world. Without actions, no state transition, no reward, and consequently, no learning can occur.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cafeteria Line" Intuition

Imagine a hungry student standing in a **KTU campus cafeteria line** at lunch. The student is the **agent**, the cafeteria (with all its food stalls, tables, and other people) is the **environment**, and the current situation (e.g., "seeing the menu, holding a tray, having ₹100 in the wallet") is the **state**.

The **action** is the specific choice the student makes at that moment:
- "Pick a masala dosa" 🥞
- "Pick a chai and a samosa" ☕
- "Walk out without eating" 🚶

Each of these is one **action** drawn from the **action set** $\mathcal{A}$. After the action, the environment responds with a new state (e.g., "now holding food, wallet has ₹60 left") and a reward (e.g., a satisfying lunch = **+1**, an empty stomach = **-1**). Over many such decisions, the student learns a **policy** — essentially a habit — for picking actions that lead to higher cumulative rewards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The action is the *bridge* between the agent's brain (policy) and the outside world. The quality of actions chosen determines the quality of learning. In RL, we don't tell the agent *which* action is correct — it discovers this through trial and error guided by rewards.

## 1.3 Action vs. Decision vs. Control Signal — A Critical Distinction

| Term | Domain | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| **Action ($a$)** | Reinforcement Learning | A discrete or continuous decision variable selected by the agent's policy. |
| **Decision** | General AI / Planning | A higher-level commitment that may decompose into many actions. |
| **Control Input ($u$)** | Classical Control Theory | A continuous signal governed by a controller, often without an explicit reward signal. |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU examinations, students often confuse *action* with *reward*. Remember: the **action is chosen by the agent**; the **reward is given by the environment**. This distinction is a frequent 2-mark question in ESE.

## 1.4 Classification of Actions

**Based on the nature of the action space:**

- **Discrete Actions:** The agent selects from a finite, countable set. Example: $\mathcal{A} = \{\text{left}, \text{right}, \text{up}, \text{down}\}$ in a grid world. Often used in board games, Atari games.
- **Continuous Actions:** The action is a real-valued vector. Example: $\mathcal{A} = [-1, 1]^{2}$ representing a torque applied to a robotic arm's joint.
- **Mixed/Hybrid Actions:** A combination of discrete high-level choices and continuous low-level parameters. Example: choosing a driving mode (discrete) and the steering angle (continuous).

**Based on the agent's selection rule:**

- **Deterministic Action:** A specific state maps to exactly one action. $a = \pi(s)$.
- **Stochastic Action:** A state maps to a probability distribution over actions. $a \sim \pi(a \vert s)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Do not equate "discrete actions" with "deterministic policy." A stochastic policy can still act over a discrete action space (e.g., $\pi(\text{left} \vert s) = 0.7, \pi(\text{right} \vert s) = 0.3$).

## 1.5 Physical/Default Constants and Notation

The following are **standard KTU-recommended symbols** for Module 1:

| Symbol | Meaning | Type |
|---|---|---|
| $a$ or $A_t$ | Action (at time $t$) | Variable |
| $\mathcal{A}$ | Action Space (set) | Set |
| $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ | Cardinality of action space | Integer / $\infty$ |
| $\pi(a \vert s)$ | Probability of taking action $a$ in state $s$ | Probability |
| $\mathcal{R}$ | Reward function (returns reward for an action) | Function |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU question papers, the symbol $A$ (uppercase, italic) almost always denotes a **random variable**, while $a$ (lowercase) denotes a **specific realized value**. Always read the question carefully to determine which form is being asked.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Role of an Action in the RL Loop

The action sits at the **interactive boundary** between the agent and the environment. The complete **agent–environment interaction loop** is:

$$\underbrace{S_t}_{\text{state}} \xrightarrow{\text{policy } \pi} \underbrace{A_t}_{\text{action}} \xrightarrow{\text{environment}} \underbrace{S_{t+1}, R_{t+1}}_{\text{next state, reward}}$$

Mathematically, the environment's response is governed by two functions:

$$p(s' \vert s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{P}\!\left[S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, \, A_t = a\right] \quad \text{— Transition Dynamics}$$

$$r(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, \, A_t = a\right] \quad \text{— Expected Reward}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The action $a$ is the *only* input the environment receives from the agent. The entire learning problem reduces to: *Given the feedback (state, reward), how should the agent update its action-selection rule to maximize long-term reward?*

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

Let us dissect the action mechanism into the following atomic operations, exactly as required by the KTU Module 1 syllabus:

- **Step 1 — State Observation:** The agent reads the current state $S_t$ from the environment.
- **Step 2 — Policy Consultation:** The policy $\pi$ maps $S_t$ to either a single action (deterministic) or a probability distribution over actions (stochastic).
- **Step 3 — Action Sampling:** A specific action $A_t$ is drawn — either chosen outright or sampled from the distribution.
- **Step 4 — Execution:** The action is transmitted to the environment, which executes the underlying physical/logical process.
- **Step 5 — Feedback:** The environment emits the new state $S_{t+1}$ and a scalar reward $R_{t+1}$.
- **Step 6 — Learning Update:** The agent uses the tuple $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$ to improve its policy.

## 2.3 Types of Action Spaces — Detailed Engineering Classification

### A. Discrete Action Space

The action space $\mathcal{A}$ is a **finite, countable** set. Common examples include:

- **Card games:** $\mathcal{A} = \{$hit, stand, double-down, split$\}$ in Blackjack.
- **Grid navigation:** $\mathcal{A} = \{\text{North}, \text{South}, \text{East}, \text{West}\}$.
- **Atari games:** $\mathcal{A} = \{1, 2, \ldots, 9\}$ representing 9 joystick positions.

The **size** $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ is bounded and known. Algorithms like Q-Learning and SARSA operate natively on discrete action spaces.

### B. Continuous Action Space

The action space is a subset of $\mathbb{R}^d$, typically a $d$-dimensional interval or hypercube. Examples:

- **Robotic arm control:** $a \in [-1, 1]^{7}$ (7 joint torques, each bounded).
- **Autonomous driving:** $a = (v, \phi)$ where $v$ is velocity and $\phi$ is steering angle.
- **Portfolio allocation:** $a \in \mathbb{R}^n$ with $\sum a_i = 1$ (weights in an $n$-asset portfolio).

For continuous action spaces, tabular Q-learning fails. We require **policy gradient methods** (e.g., REINFORCE, DDPG, PPO).

### C. Mixed/Hybrid Action Space

Used in hierarchical RL. Example: a warehouse robot first chooses a *high-level task* (pick / place / charge — discrete) and then chooses a *low-level joint trajectory* (continuous).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Module 1 problems often ask students to identify whether a given problem has a discrete or continuous action space and justify the choice of algorithm accordingly. Memorize the examples above.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following is the **complete, board-exam-grade formula sheet** for the topic "Actions" in Module 1:

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n\}$ | Definition of action set | Identifying the action space of a problem |
| 2 | $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert = n$ | Cardinality of action space | Determining problem size |
| 3 | $a = \pi(s)$ | Deterministic policy mapping | Direct action selection |
| 4 | $\pi(a \vert s) = \mathbb{P}[A_t = a \mid S_t = s]$ | Stochastic policy | Probability of choosing an action |
| 5 | $\sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \vert s) = 1, \; \forall s$ | Policy validity constraint | Sanity check on stochastic policies |
| 6 | $p(s' \vert s, a)$ | Transition probability | Environment dynamics given an action |
| 7 | $r(s, a) = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ | Expected reward function | Immediate feedback for an action |
| 8 | $Q_\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a\right]$ | Action-value function | Long-term value of taking action $a$ in state $s$ |
| 9 | $A_\pi(s, a) = Q_\pi(s, a) - V_\pi(s)$ | **Advantage Function** | How much *better* is action $a$ vs. average action |
| 10 | $a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} Q(s, a)$ | Greedy action selection | Exploitation in action choice |
| 11 | $\pi(a \vert s) = \frac{\exp(Q(s, a) / \tau)}{\sum_{a'} \exp(Q(s, a') / \tau)}$ | **Boltzmann / Softmax policy** | Probabilistic exploration |
| 12 | $\pi(a \vert s) = 1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}, \; a = a^*; \quad \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}, \; a \neq a^*$ | **$\varepsilon$-Greedy Policy** | Most common exploration strategy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parameter definitions used in the table:**
> - $\gamma \in [0, 1)$: **discount factor**, controlling the present value of future rewards.
> - $\tau > 0$: **temperature parameter** in Boltzmann policy — high $\tau$ = more random, low $\tau$ = more greedy.
> - $\varepsilon \in [0, 1]$: **exploration probability** in $\varepsilon$-greedy.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

The concept of an "action" is foundational across multiple KTU-relevant engineering domains:

- **Robotics (Mechanical + CSE intersection):** Each joint torque applied to a robotic manipulator is a continuous action. RL is used to teach robots to walk, grasp, and balance.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Steering angle, acceleration, and brake force are continuous actions. RL agents learn lane-keeping, merging, and overtaking policies.
- **Industrial Process Control:** Valve openings, pump speeds, and heater settings are continuous actions optimized via RL for energy efficiency.
- **Game AI & Game Development:** Discrete actions (move, attack, defend) chosen by RL agents that surpass human-level play (e.g., AlphaGo, AlphaZero).
- **Recommendation Systems:** The action is the item (or ad) shown to the user; the reward is the click, watch-time, or purchase.
- **Smart Grid & Energy Management:** Actions include dispatching power from various sources; rewards reflect cost, demand fulfillment, and emissions.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme project evaluations, students who can articulate *which kind* of action their problem requires (discrete / continuous / hybrid) and *why* score significantly higher in the design-viva component.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Greedy Action Selection Rule

The **greedy action** is defined as the action that maximizes the action-value function $Q_\pi(s, a)$ for a given state $s$. Formally, we wish to find:

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} Q_\pi(s, a)$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

We begin with the definition of the action-value function under a fixed policy $\pi$:

$$
Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a\right]
$$

where $G_t$ is the **return** starting at time $t$:

$$
G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}
$$

To find the **best action** $a^*$ in state $s$, we differentiate $Q_\pi(s, a)$ with respect to $a$ and set the derivative to zero, then verify it is a maximum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Note:** In a discrete action space, differentiation is replaced by an exhaustive comparison: compute $Q_\pi(s, a)$ for every $a \in \mathcal{A}$ and pick the maximum.

#### Case 1: Discrete Action Space

For a finite set $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n\}$, the greedy action is:

$$
a^* \;=\; \underset{a \in \mathcal{A}}{\arg\max} \; Q_\pi(s, a)
$$

The corresponding greedy policy is:

$$
\pi^*(a \vert s) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } a = a^* \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

This is called a **deterministic greedy policy**.

#### Case 2: Continuous Action Space

For $a \in \mathbb{R}^d$, the greedy action solves:

$$
\nabla_a Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; 0
$$

provided $Q_\pi$ is differentiable in $a$. The optimal action is the **stationary point**:

$$
a^* \;=\; \underset{a \in \mathbb{R}^d}{\arg\max} \; Q_\pi(s, a)
$$

A common simplification assumes a **linear action-value function**:

$$
Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; \theta^\top \phi(s, a)
$$

where $\phi(s, a)$ is a feature vector and $\theta$ is a parameter vector. The gradient is:

$$
\nabla_a Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; \theta^\top \frac{\partial \phi(s, a)}{\partial a}
$$

Setting this to zero and solving gives the closed-form greedy action. This is the basis of algorithms like **Least-Squares Policy Iteration (LSPI)**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the $\varepsilon$-Greedy Policy

The $\varepsilon$-greedy policy balances **exploration** (trying new actions) and **exploitation** (using the best-known action). It is defined as:

$$
\pi(a \vert s) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
1 - \varepsilon + \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}, & \text{if } a = a^* = \underset{a'}{\arg\max} Q(s, a') \\[10pt]
\dfrac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}, & \text{if } a \neq a^*
\end{cases}
$$

### Verification that this is a valid probability distribution

We must check that $\sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \vert s) = 1$.

**Step 1:** Split the sum into the greedy action and all other actions.

$$
\sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \vert s) \;=\; \pi(a^* \vert s) + \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}, a \neq a^*} \pi(a \vert s)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the definitions.

$$
= \left(1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}\right) + \sum_{a \neq a^*} \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the second sum — there are exactly $(\vert \mathcal{A} \vert - 1)$ non-greedy actions.

$$
= \left(1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}\right) + (\vert \mathcal{A} \vert - 1) \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}
$$

**Step 4:** Expand and simplify.

$$
= 1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} + \frac{(\vert \mathcal{A} \vert - 1)\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}
$$

**Step 5:** Combine the two fraction terms over the common denominator $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$.

$$
= 1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon + (\vert \mathcal{A} \vert - 1)\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \;=\; 1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert \cdot \varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \;=\; 1 - \varepsilon + \varepsilon \;=\; 1 \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Insight:** This verification (that $\pi$ sums to 1) is a **favorite 7-mark question** in KTU Module 1 exams. Students are expected to derive or verify it on paper. Memorize the four-step structure above.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Boltzmann (Softmax) Policy

The Boltzmann policy converts action-values into action-probabilities using a **Gibbs / softmax distribution**:

$$
\pi(a \vert s) \;=\; \frac{\exp\!\left(Q(s, a) / \tau\right)}{\sum_{a' \in \mathcal{A}} \exp\!\left(Q(s, a') / \tau\right)}
$$

### Derivation of limiting behavior

**Step 1:** As $\tau \to 0^+$ (temperature → 0), the exponential with the largest $Q$ dominates. By **Laplace's method**, the denominator behaves as:

$$
\sum_{a'} \exp\!\left(\frac{Q(s, a')}{\tau}\right) \;\approx\; \exp\!\left(\frac{Q(s, a^*)}{\tau}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Therefore, the policy collapses to:

$$
\pi(a \vert s) \;\approx\; \frac{\exp(Q(s, a)/\tau)}{\exp(Q(s, a^*)/\tau)} \;=\; \exp\!\left(\frac{Q(s, a) - Q(s, a^*)}{\tau}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** In the limit:

$$
\lim_{\tau \to 0^+} \pi(a \vert s) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
1, & a = a^* \\
0, & a \neq a^*
\end{cases}
$$

This is the **greedy policy** — the Boltzmann policy reduces to greedy action selection in the zero-temperature limit.

**Step 4:** Conversely, as $\tau \to \infty$:

$$
\exp\!\left(\frac{Q(s, a)}{\tau}\right) \;\to\; \exp(0) \;=\; 1, \quad \forall a
$$

So all actions are equiprobable:

$$
\pi(a \vert s) \;\to\; \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}
$$

This is the **uniform random policy** — maximum exploration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The temperature $\tau$ is therefore a **smooth knob** that interpolates between pure exploration (large $\tau$) and pure exploitation (small $\tau$). It is the continuous analog of $\varepsilon$ in the $\varepsilon$-greedy policy.

## 3.4 The Exploration–Exploitation Tradeoff in Action Selection

Every action-selection mechanism must address the **exploration–exploitation dilemma**:

- **Exploitation:** Choose the action with the highest estimated value, $a^* = \arg\max_a Q(s, a)$, to maximize short-term reward.
- **Exploration:** Choose a non-greedy action to gather information about its true value, with the hope of discovering better long-term strategies.

| Strategy | Exploration Mechanism | Exploitation Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| $\varepsilon$-Greedy | Random action with probability $\varepsilon$ | Greedy action with probability $1 - \varepsilon$ |
| Boltzmann / Softmax | Higher $\tau$ flattens distribution | Lower $\tau$ sharpens distribution |
| UCB (Upper Confidence Bound) | Bonus for under-visited actions | Choose action with highest $Q + c\sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}$ |
| Thompson Sampling | Sample from posterior over $Q$ | Action with highest sampled value |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **UCB action-selection rule** is particularly board-relevant and has the form:
>
> $$A_t = \underset{a}{\arg\max}\!\left[Q_t(a) + c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}\right]$$
>
> where $N_t(a)$ is the number of times action $a$ has been selected up to time $t$, and $c > 0$ controls the exploration bonus.

## 3.5 Summary of Action-Selection Mechanisms

| Mechanism | When Best Used | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **Greedy** | When the Q-values are already accurate | Low (rarely optimal alone) |
| **$\varepsilon$-Greedy** | Generic, simple problems, introductory exam questions | **High** ⭐ |
| **Boltzmann / Softmax** | When action preferences are graded, not binary | **High** ⭐ |
| **UCB** | Multi-armed bandit problems, regret minimization | **Medium** |
| **Thompson Sampling** | Bayesian RL settings, when priors are available | **Medium** |
| **Policy Gradient** | Continuous action spaces | Covered in later modules |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Agent–Environment Interaction Loop with Actions Highlighted

The following Mermaid block depicts the **canonical RL loop** with the **action** as the central connecting element. The agent consults its policy, emits an action, and the environment responds with a new state and reward.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV["ENVIRONMENT"]
        S_curr["Current State S_t"]
        S_next["Next State S_{t+1}"]
        R_emit["Reward R_{t+1}"]
        TRANS["Transition Dynamics p(s prime | s, a)"]
    end

    subgraph AGENT["AGENT"]
        POL["Policy pi"]
        QFN["Q-Value Estimator Q(s, a)"]
        ASEL["Action Selector"]
    end

    S_curr -- "Observation" --> POL
    POL -- "Maps state to distribution" --> ASEL
    QFN -- "Provides value estimates" --> ASEL
    ASEL -- "Action A_t" --> TRANS
    TRANS -- "Updates" --> S_next
    TRANS -- "Emits" --> R_emit
    S_next -- "New observation" --> POL
    R_emit -- "Feedback signal" --> QFN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Trace the cycle clockwise starting from `S_curr`. The state flows into the policy, the policy plus Q-values determine the action, the action drives the transition dynamics, which produce the new state and reward. The reward closes the loop by updating the Q-value estimator.

## 4.2 Hierarchical Decomposition of an Action Space

The following block diagram shows how action spaces are classified at each level — from the most abstract to the most concrete. This is the **block-level functional architecture** the KTU protocol recommends for conceptual topics.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP["Action Space A"]
    DISC["Discrete Action Space"]
    CONT["Continuous Action Space"]
    HYB["Mixed / Hybrid Action Space"]

    D1["Finite countable set A equals a1 a2 ... an"]
    D2["Tabular Q-Learning applicable"]
    D3["Examples: Atari Grid World Card games"]
    C1["Subset of R to the d"]
    C2["Policy gradient methods required"]
    C3["Examples: Robotics Autonomous driving"]
    H1["Combination of discrete and continuous"]
    H2["Hierarchical RL required"]
    H3["Examples: Warehouse robots Drone control"]

    TOP --> DISC
    TOP --> CONT
    TOP --> HYB
    DISC --> D1
    DISC --> D2
    DISC --> D3
    CONT --> C1
    CONT --> C2
    CONT --> C3
    HYB --> H1
    HYB --> H2
    HYB --> H3
```

## 4.3 Action-Selection Decision Tree

The following flowchart shows the **decision process** an agent follows to pick an action at each time step. This is one of the most frequently drawn diagrams in KTU exam answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START["At time t, observe state S_t"]
    CHECK["Random number r from Uniform 0 1"]
    QCHECK{"r less than epsilon?"}
    GREEDY["Pick greedy action a equals argmax Q(s, a)"]
    EXPLORE["Pick random action a from A"]
    BOLTZ["Boltzmann: sample a from softmax of Q over tau"]
    EXEC["Execute A_t in environment"]
    FEED["Receive S_{t+1} and R_{t+1}"]
    UP["Update Q-values or policy parameters"]

    START --> CHECK
    CHECK -- Yes --> EXPLORE
    CHECK -- No --> GREEDY
    GREEDY --> EXEC
    EXPLORE --> EXEC
    START -. "Alternative:" .-> BOLTZ
    BOLTZ --> EXEC
    EXEC --> FEED
    FEED --> UP
    UP -. "Next time step" .-> START
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Drawing this exact decision tree (with your own neat styling) typically fetches full marks on the 7-mark question: *"Explain with a flowchart how an agent selects an action in $\varepsilon$-greedy."*

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Lifecycle of an Action in One RL Step

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ObserveState
    ObserveState --> ConsultPolicy: S_t received
    ConsultPolicy --> SelectAction: pi evaluated
    SelectAction --> ExecuteAction: A_t chosen
    ExecuteAction --> ReceiveFeedback: environment responds
    ReceiveFeedback --> UpdateQ: R_{t+1} and S_{t+1} arrived
    UpdateQ --> ObserveState: time t becomes t+1
    UpdateQ --> [*]: episode terminates
```

> [!NOTE]
> The state diagram compresses the entire per-step lifecycle of an action into 6 nodes, mirroring the formal agent-environment loop. This is the cleanest diagram to reproduce in a 14-mark question's introduction paragraph.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1, Q1a]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**"Define an action and an action space in Reinforcement Learning. Differentiate between discrete and continuous action spaces with one example each."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Board-Expected Structure)

- **Definition of Action (1 mark):** An action $A_t$ is a decision or control input chosen by the agent at time $t$ from a pre-defined set of allowable choices, in order to influence the environment and transition to a new state.

- **Definition of Action Space (1 mark):** The action space $\mathcal{A}$ is the set of all valid actions available to the agent. Its cardinality is denoted $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$.

- **Difference with examples (1 mark):**
  - *Discrete action space:* A finite, countable set. Example: $\mathcal{A} = \{\text{left}, \text{right}, \text{up}, \text{down}\}$ in a grid world.
  - *Continuous action space:* A real-valued vector in $\mathbb{R}^d$. Example: $a = (\text{velocity}, \text{steering angle})$ for autonomous driving.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 1, Q1b]**

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**"Explain the role of the policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ in action selection. Why is the constraint $\sum_a \pi(a \vert s) = 1$ important?"**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Board-Expected Structure)

- **Role of Policy (1.5 marks):** The policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ is the agent's action-selection rule. It specifies the probability of choosing each action $a$ in state $s$. It is the agent's "brain" — the only mechanism by which the agent converts its current knowledge into a concrete action.

- **Importance of the Sum-to-One Constraint (1.5 marks):** This constraint ensures that $\pi$ is a **valid probability distribution** over actions for every state. It guarantees that exactly one action (in a probabilistic sense) is selected at every step, preventing ambiguous or undefined behavior. Without this constraint, the agent's action selection would not be mathematically well-defined.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part A:**
> - Do NOT write only the formula $\pi(a \vert s)$ without explaining its **meaning**. Board examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for definitions that are "formula-only."
> - Avoid writing vague statements like "policy chooses the best action." It chooses based on a *probability distribution*, not always the best one. Precision is rewarded.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1, Q2 (Choice-Based)]**

---

### Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **$\varepsilon$-greedy action-selection policy**. Derive the probabilities of selecting the greedy and non-greedy actions. Show that the policy forms a valid probability distribution. (Module 1, RL Foundations)

**(b) [7 Marks]** A robot in a 2×2 grid world has the action space $\mathcal{A} = \{\text{North}, \text{South}, \text{East}, \text{West}\}$. In a particular state, the estimated Q-values are:
$Q(s, N) = 5, \quad Q(s, S) = 2, \quad Q(s, E) = 7, \quad Q(s, W) = 4$.

If the agent uses the **$\varepsilon$-greedy** policy with $\varepsilon = 0.2$, calculate the probability of selecting **each action**. Which action is the greedy action? Justify your answer.

---

#### Model Answer to B1(a) [7 Marks]

**1. Definition of $\varepsilon$-greedy policy (2 marks):**
The $\varepsilon$-greedy policy is a simple action-selection strategy that with probability $1 - \varepsilon$ chooses the action having the highest estimated action-value (the greedy action), and with probability $\varepsilon$ chooses uniformly at random from all actions (including possibly the greedy one). It is one of the most widely used exploration mechanisms in RL.

**2. Probability of selecting the greedy action $a^*$ (1.5 marks):**
The greedy action is $a^* = \arg\max_{a} Q(s, a)$. Under the $\varepsilon$-greedy policy:

$$
\pi(a^* \vert s) \;=\; 1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}
$$

**3. Probability of selecting a non-greedy action $a \neq a^*$ (1.5 marks):**
For any non-greedy action $a$:

$$
\pi(a \vert s) \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}
$$

**4. Verification that the policy is a valid probability distribution (2 marks):**
As shown in Section 3.2, the sum of probabilities over all actions equals 1, satisfying the axiom of total probability. The four-step derivation (split the sum, substitute, evaluate, simplify) should be reproduced on the answer sheet.

---

#### Model Answer to B1(b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify the greedy action (1 mark):**

$$
a^* \;=\; \underset{a \in \mathcal{A}}{\arg\max} \; Q(s, a) \;=\; \underset{a}{\arg\max} \{5, 2, 7, 4\} \;=\; \text{East}
$$

The greedy action is **East**, with $Q(s, E) = 7$.

**Step 2 — Compute the cardinality of the action space (1 mark):**

$$
\vert \mathcal{A} \vert \;=\; 4
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the probability of the greedy action (1.5 marks):**

$$
\pi(\text{East} \vert s) \;=\; 1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \;=\; 1 - 0.2 + \frac{0.2}{4} \;=\; 0.8 + 0.05 \;=\; 0.85
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the probability of each non-greedy action (1.5 marks):**
For North, South, and West (each non-greedy):

$$
\pi(\text{North} \vert s) \;=\; \pi(\text{South} \vert s) \;=\; \pi(\text{West} \vert s) \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \;=\; \frac{0.2}{4} \;=\; 0.05
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the sum (1 mark):**

$$
\pi(\text{North}) + \pi(\text{South}) + \pi(\text{East}) + \pi(\text{West}) \;=\; 0.05 + 0.05 + 0.85 + 0.05 \;=\; 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Answer Box:**

$$
\boxed{\pi(\text{North}) = 0.05, \;\; \pi(\text{South}) = 0.05, \;\; \pi(\text{East}) = 0.85, \;\; \pi(\text{West}) = 0.05}
$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 1, Q3 (Choice-Based)]**

### Question B2 — Choice B (14 Marks) — *Alternative to B1*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **Boltzmann (Softmax) action-selection policy**. Explain how the **temperature parameter** $\tau$ controls the exploration–exploitation tradeoff. What happens in the limits $\tau \to 0$ and $\tau \to \infty$? (Module 1, Exploration Strategies)

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a state $s$ with three available actions and the following Q-values:
$Q(s, a_1) = 2.0, \quad Q(s, a_2) = 1.0, \quad Q(s, a_3) = 0.0$.

Using the **Boltzmann policy** with $\tau = 1.0$, compute the probability of selecting each action. **Repeat the computation with $\tau = 0.1$ and $\tau = 10.0$**. Comment on the effect of $\tau$ on the action-selection distribution.

---

#### Model Answer to B2(a) [7 Marks]

**1. Definition of Boltzmann policy (2 marks):**
The Boltzmann policy converts Q-values into a probability distribution over actions using the softmax function:

$$
\pi(a \vert s) \;=\; \frac{\exp\!\left(Q(s, a) / \tau\right)}{\sum_{a' \in \mathcal{A}} \exp\!\left(Q(s, a') / \tau\right)}
$$

The temperature $\tau > 0$ is a positive scalar that controls how "peaked" or "flat" the distribution is.

**2. Role of temperature — qualitative explanation (2 marks):**
A **small $\tau$** amplifies the differences between Q-values, making the best action have probability close to 1 (more exploitation, less exploration). A **large $\tau$** dampens the differences, making all actions nearly equiprobable (more exploration, less exploitation). The temperature is therefore a **smooth dial** for the exploration–exploitation tradeoff.

**3. Limit $\tau \to 0^+$ — Greedy policy (1.5 marks):**

$$
\lim_{\tau \to 0^+} \pi(a \vert s) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
1, & a = a^* \\
0, & a \neq a^*
\end{cases}
$$

The Boltzmann policy reduces to a deterministic greedy policy.

**4. Limit $\tau \to \infty$ — Uniform random policy (1.5 marks):**

$$
\lim_{\tau \to \infty} \pi(a \vert s) \;=\; \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}
$$

All actions are equally likely, regardless of their Q-values.

---

#### Model Answer to B2(b) [7 Marks]

The Boltzmann formula with $\tau$ is:

$$
\pi(a_i \vert s) \;=\; \frac{\exp(Q(s, a_i) / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{3} \exp(Q(s, a_j) / \tau)}
$$

Given Q-values: $Q(a_1) = 2.0, \quad Q(a_2) = 1.0, \quad Q(a_3) = 0.0$.

---

**Case 1: $\tau = 1.0$ (2 marks)**

Compute the exponentials:

$$
\exp(2.0 / 1.0) \;=\; e^2 \;\approx\; 7.389
$$

$$
\exp(1.0 / 1.0) \;=\; e^1 \;\approx\; 2.718
$$

$$
\exp(0.0 / 1.0) \;=\; e^0 \;=\; 1.000
$$

Sum:

$$
Z \;=\; 7.389 + 2.718 + 1.000 \;=\; 11.107
$$

Probabilities:

$$
\pi(a_1) = \frac{7.389}{11.107} \approx 0.665, \quad \pi(a_2) = \frac{2.718}{11.107} \approx 0.245, \quad \pi(a_3) = \frac{1.000}{11.107} \approx 0.090
$$

---

**Case 2: $\tau = 0.1$ (2 marks)**

Compute the exponentials:

$$
\exp(2.0 / 0.1) \;=\; e^{20} \;\approx\; 4.85 \times 10^8
$$

$$
\exp(1.0 / 0.1) \;=\; e^{10} \;\approx\; 2.20 \times 10^4
$$

$$
\exp(0.0 / 0.1) \;=\; e^0 \;=\; 1.000
$$

Sum:

$$
Z \;\approx\; 4.85 \times 10^8 + 2.20 \times 10^4 + 1.000 \;\approx\; 4.85 \times 10^8
$$

Probabilities:

$$
\pi(a_1) \approx \frac{4.85 \times 10^8}{4.85 \times 10^8} = 0.99995, \quad \pi(a_2) \approx \frac{2.20 \times 10^4}{4.85 \times 10^8} \approx 4.5 \times 10^{-5}, \quad \pi(a_3) \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-9}
$$

The policy is **sharply peaked** on $a_1$ — essentially greedy.

---

**Case 3: $\tau = 10.0$ (2 marks)**

Compute the exponentials:

$$
\exp(2.0 / 10.0) \;=\; e^{0.2} \;\approx\; 1.221
$$

$$
\exp(1.0 / 10.0) \;=\; e^{0.1} \;\approx\; 1.105
$$

$$
\exp(0.0 / 10.0) \;=\; e^0 \;=\; 1.000
$$

Sum:

$$
Z \;\approx\; 1.221 + 1.105 + 1.000 \;=\; 3.326
$$

Probabilities:

$$
\pi(a_1) \approx \frac{1.221}{3.326} \approx 0.367, \quad \pi(a_2) \approx \frac{1.105}{3.326} \approx 0.332, \quad \pi(a_3) \approx \frac{1.000}{3.326} \approx 0.301
$$

The policy is **nearly uniform** — actions are almost equiprobable.

---

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
As $\tau$ increases from 0.1 to 10.0, the action distribution **flattens** — moving from near-greedy ($\pi(a_1) \approx 1$) to near-uniform ($\pi(a_i) \approx 0.33$). This empirically confirms the theoretical limit analysis in part (a).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B:**
> - **Numerical Rounding:** Do not write $\pi(a_1) = 0.66$ for $\tau = 1.0$; use at least **three decimal places** (0.665). Truncation costs 0.5 marks.
> - **Forgetting the denominator:** Many students compute only the numerators and skip the normalization. This forfeits 2–3 marks.
> - **Not commenting on the trend:** Part (b)'s final mark is awarded for the qualitative comment about $\tau$'s effect. Writing only numbers without interpretation costs the last mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Actions**

- ⭐ An **action** $A_t$ is the decision variable chosen by the agent at time $t$ from the **action space** $\mathcal{A}$.
- ⭐ The **policy** $\pi(a \vert s)$ is the action-selection rule — it can be deterministic or stochastic.
- ⭐ For any stochastic policy, $\sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \vert s) = 1$ **must** hold.
- ⭐ **Discrete action space:** $\mathcal{A}$ is finite and countable — use Q-Learning / SARSA.
- ⭐ **Continuous action space:** $\mathcal{A} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ — use policy gradient methods.
- ⭐ The **$\varepsilon$-greedy** policy gives the greedy action a probability of $1 - \varepsilon + \varepsilon / \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ and every other action a probability of $\varepsilon / \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$.
- ⭐ The **Boltzmann (Softmax) policy** uses the temperature $\tau$ to smoothly interpolate between greedy ($\tau \to 0$) and uniform ($\tau \to \infty$).
- ⭐ **Exploration–Exploitation tradeoff:** Greedy action exploitation maximizes immediate reward; non-greedy exploration gathers information for long-term gain.
- ⭐ The **action-value function** $Q_\pi(s, a)$ quantifies the *long-term value* of taking action $a$ in state $s$ under policy $\pi$.
- ⭐ The **advantage function** $A_\pi(s, a) = Q_\pi(s, a) - V_\pi(s)$ measures how much *better or worse* a specific action is compared to the average action in that state.
- ⭐ In KTU board exams, always **state the formula, show the substitution, perform the arithmetic**, and **verify the result** (e.g., probability sum = 1) wherever possible.
- ⭐ **Action ≠ Reward.** Action is chosen by the agent; reward is delivered by the environment. This distinction is tested every year.
- ⭐ **Diagram-based questions** (e.g., drawing the action-selection flowchart) carry dedicated 2–3 marks — always include a labeled diagram in your 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transition Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transition Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the formal framework of a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**, an agent's interaction with its environment is mathematically captured by the tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle$. Among these elements, the **Transition Function** (also called the **Transition Probability Function**, **Dynamics Function**, or **Model of the World**) is the component that describes how the environment evolves over time in response to the agent's actions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Transition Function**, denoted $\mathcal{P}$, is a probability distribution that specifies the likelihood of transitioning from a given state $s \in \mathcal{S}$ to a successor state $s' \in \mathcal{S}$ upon executing a given action $a \in \mathcal{A}$. Formally:
> $$\mathcal{P}: \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \times \mathcal{S} \rightarrow [0, 1]$$
> $$\mathcal{P}(s, a, s') = \Pr(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a)$$

The transition function embodies the **Markov Property**: the conditional probability distribution over the next state depends *only* on the current state and action, and is *independent* of the entire history of prior states and actions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are playing a game of **snakes and ladders** on a physical board. Every square on the board is a "state," and rolling the dice to move forward is your "action." Now, suppose the board has a few **trick tiles** — when you land on tile 23, there is a 70% chance you stay there, a 20% chance a gust of wind slides you forward to tile 25, and a 10% chance you slip backward to tile 21.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Transition Function IS the Rulebook of the Board.**
> The board itself doesn't have preferences, but its physical layout (slippery spots, springs, hidden doors) determines *probabilistically* where you will end up. That probability map is exactly the transition function. The agent (you, the player) does not control the dice outcome or the slippery floor — it can only choose *which move to attempt*, while the environment (the board + physics) dictates the *consequence*.

In short:
- **State** = "Where you are standing right now."
- **Action** = "Which move you decide to make."
- **Transition Function** = "The world's physical rulebook telling you where you will *probably* end up."

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Notation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Probability Range | **$[0, 1]$** | Bounded probability measure |
| Normalization Constraint | **$\sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \mathcal{P}(s, a, s') = 1$** | Ensures valid probability mass |
| Time Index | **$t \in \mathbb{N}$** (discrete) or **$t \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$** (continuous) | Step in the decision horizon |
| Discount Factor | **$\gamma \in [0, 1)$** | Long-horizon value discounting |
| Stationarity | **$\mathcal{P}$ is time-invariant** | Markov property assumption |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition of the Transition Function

The transition function can be deconstructed into the following logical strata:

- **Step 1 — State Observation**: At time $t$, the agent occupies a specific state $s \in \mathcal{S}$, fully observable by the agent. This is the *conditioning context* for the next state distribution.

- **Step 2 — Action Selection**: Using its current policy $\pi(a \mid s)$, the agent samples an action $a \in \mathcal{A}(s)$ from the set of admissible actions in state $s$.

- **Step 3 — Stochastic Environment Response**: The environment, governed by the *true underlying* transition function $\mathcal{P}$, samples a successor state $s' \in \mathcal{S}$ from the conditional distribution $\mathcal{P}(\cdot \mid s, a)$.

- **Step 4 — Reward Ejection**: A scalar reward $r = \mathcal{R}(s, a, s')$ is emitted by the environment, forming the so-called **SARSA tuple**: $\langle s, a, r, s' \rangle$ (or $\langle s, a, r, s' \rangle$ for Q-learning targets).

- **Step 5 — Why This Matters**: The transition function is the *only* source of stochasticity in the agent–environment loop. It encapsulates everything the agent cannot directly control — wind, opponent moves, hardware faults, sensor noise, market volatility.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "How" — Implementation Pathways:**
> 1. **Tabular Form**: For discrete MDPs with $\lvert \mathcal{S} \rvert = N$ and $\lvert \mathcal{A} \rvert = M$, the transition function is stored as an $M \times N \times N$ tensor, where each "slice" $T^{(a)}$ is a stochastic row-matrix.
> 2. **Matrix Exponential Form**: For linear dynamics $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$, the discrete-time equivalent is $\mathcal{P}(s, a, s') = \delta(s' - (As + Ba))$.
> 3. **Sample-Based Model**: In model-free RL, the transition function is *implicitly* estimated through experience tuples without ever being stored explicitly.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — TRANSITION FUNCTION (Module 1)**

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **LaTeX Expression** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transition Probability | $\mathcal{P}(s, a, s') = \Pr(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a)$ | Core definition |
| 2 | Normalization (Row-Stochastic) | $\sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \mathcal{P}(s, a, s') = 1$ | Probability mass conservation |
| 3 | State Distribution Evolution | $\mathcal{S}_{t+1}(s') = \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathcal{S}_t(s) \pi(a \mid s) \mathcal{P}(s, a, s')$ | Forward dynamics of occupancy |
| 4 | Expected Reward | $\mathcal{R}(s, a) = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \mathcal{P}(s, a, s') \mathcal{R}(s, a, s')$ | Marginalized reward |
| 5 | Action-Space Marginalization | $\mathcal{P}(s, s') = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \mathcal{P}(s, a, s')$ | Transition under policy |
| 6 | Stationary Distribution | $\mu(s') = \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \mu(s) \mathcal{P}(s, s')$ | Long-run occupancy under $\pi$ |
| 7 | Deterministic Special Case | $\mathcal{P}(s, a, s') = \delta(s' - f(s, a))$ | Dirac delta at next state |
| 8 | Continuous-State Form | $p(s' \mid s, a)$ as probability density | Replaces discrete probability |
| 9 | Bellman Expectation Equation | $V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s, a, s')[R + \gamma V^\pi(s')]$ | Foundation of DP/RL |
| 10 | Empirical Estimator (MLE) | $\hat{\mathcal{P}}(s, a, s') = \frac{N(s, a, s')}{N(s, a)}$ | Maximum-likelihood from samples |
| 11 | Bayesian (Dirichlet) Posterior | $\mathcal{P}(s, a, \cdot) \sim \text{Dirichlet}(\alpha_1 + N_1, \dots, \alpha_N + N_N)$ | Uncertainty-aware estimation |
| 12 | Model-Error Bound | $\lVert \hat{\mathcal{P}} - \mathcal{P}^\star \rVert_1 \leq \sqrt{\frac{2 \ln(2 / \delta)}{N(s, a)}}$ | PAC-style confidence |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **Role of Transition Function** |
|---|---|
| **Robotic Locomotion** | Models joint actuation noise, surface friction, and contact dynamics |
| **Autonomous Driving** | Captures stochasticity of other drivers' behavior and weather effects |
| **Algorithmic Trading** | Encodes market microstructure and liquidity shocks |
| **Industrial Process Control** | Represents batch-to-batch variability in chemical/pharma manufacturing |
| **Smart Grid Management** | Models stochastic renewable generation and demand fluctuations |
| **Healthcare Decision Systems** | Captures patient-response uncertainty in treatment recommendation |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Joint to Conditional Probability

We begin from the formal definition and derive the normalization constraint.

**Starting Point:**

$$
\mathcal{P}(s, a, s') = \Pr(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a)
$$

**Step 1:** By the **axiom of total probability**, summing over all possible successor states $s' \in \mathcal{S}$ must yield unity because the environment *must* land in some state in $\mathcal{S}$:

$$
\sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \Pr(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a) = 1
$$

**Step 2:** Substituting the definition of the transition probability into the sum:

$$
\sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \mathcal{P}(s, a, s') = 1
$$

**Conclusion:** Every row of the transition tensor — indexed by the pair $(s, a)$ — forms a valid probability distribution over $\mathcal{S}$. This is the **row-stochasticity property**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Forward Evolution of the State Occupancy Distribution

Given a starting state distribution $\mathcal{S}_0(s)$ and a fixed policy $\pi$, derive $\mathcal{S}_{t+1}(s')$.

**Step 1:** By the **law of total probability**, the probability of being in state $s'$ at time $t+1$ is the sum over all possible predecessor states and chosen actions:

$$
\mathcal{S}_{t+1}(s') = \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \Pr(S_{t+1} = s', S_t = s, A_t = a)
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **chain rule of probability** to factor the joint into a sequence of conditionals:

$$
\Pr(S_{t+1} = s', S_t = s, A_t = a) = \mathcal{S}_t(s) \cdot \pi(a \mid s) \cdot \mathcal{P}(s, a, s')
$$

Here:
- $\mathcal{S}_t(s) = \Pr(S_t = s)$ is the marginal probability of being in state $s$ at time $t$.
- $\pi(a \mid s)$ is the policy's probability of choosing action $a$ in state $s$.
- $\mathcal{P}(s, a, s')$ is the environment's transition probability to $s'$.

**Step 3:** Substitute and rearrange to obtain the **forward dynamics equation**:

$$
\mathcal{S}_{t+1}(s') = \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathcal{S}_t(s)\, \pi(a \mid s)\, \mathcal{P}(s, a, s')
$$

**Interpretation:** This is the discrete-time analog of the **master equation** in stochastic processes, and it enables **trajectory rollouts** for policy evaluation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Marginalizing the Transition Function over Actions (Policy-Induced Transition)

When the policy $\pi$ is fixed, we can collapse the action dimension and obtain a *simpler* transition function over states only.

**Step 1:** The probability of moving from $s$ to $s'$ under policy $\pi$ is the sum over all actions weighted by the probability that the policy selects them:

$$
\mathcal{P}_\pi(s, s') = \Pr(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, \pi)
$$

**Step 2:** Use the **total probability theorem** conditioning on the intermediate action $a$:

$$
\mathcal{P}_\pi(s, s') = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \Pr(A_t = a \mid S_t = s, \pi) \cdot \Pr(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a)
$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the first factor as the policy and the second as the environment transition function:

$$
\mathcal{P}_\pi(s, s') = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s)\, \mathcal{P}(s, a, s')
$$

**Use Case:** This collapsed form is the foundation of **Markov Chain** analysis under a fixed policy and is essential for proving convergence of policy iteration.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Empirical Maximum-Likelihood Estimator of the Transition Function

In model-based RL, we collect samples $(s, a, s')$ from environment interaction and estimate $\mathcal{P}$ empirically.

**Step 1:** Define the count random variable $N(s, a, s')$ = number of times the tuple $(s, a, s')$ was observed, and $N(s, a) = \sum_{s'} N(s, a, s')$.

**Step 2:** Assume the samples are i.i.d. and that $\mathcal{P}(s, a, \cdot)$ is a categorical distribution parameterized by the vector $\mathbf{p} = (p_1, p_2, \dots, p_N)$ where $p_i = \mathcal{P}(s, a, s'_i)$.

**Step 3:** The likelihood of observing the empirical counts is the **multinomial likelihood**:

$$
L(\mathbf{p}) = \prod_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} p_{s'}^{N(s, a, s')}
$$

**Step 4:** Take the **log-likelihood** to convert products into sums:

$$
\ell(\mathbf{p}) = \log L(\mathbf{p}) = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} N(s, a, s') \log p_{s'}
$$

**Step 5:** Maximize subject to the constraint $\sum_{s'} p_{s'} = 1$ using a **Lagrange multiplier** $\lambda$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{p}, \lambda) = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} N(s, a, s') \log p_{s'} - \lambda \left( \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} p_{s'} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 6:** Take the partial derivative with respect to $p_{s'}$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial p_{s'}} = \frac{N(s, a, s')}{p_{s'}} - \lambda = 0
$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $p_{s'}$:

$$
p_{s'} = \frac{N(s, a, s')}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 8:** Apply the normalization constraint:

$$
\sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} p_{s'} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} N(s, a, s') = 1
$$

$$
\lambda = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} N(s, a, s') = N(s, a)
$$

**Step 9:** Substitute back to obtain the **MLE estimator**:

$$
\hat{\mathcal{P}}(s, a, s') = \frac{N(s, a, s')}{N(s, a)}
$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum-likelihood estimate of the transition probability is simply the **relative frequency** of observing the successor state $s'$ given that the pair $(s, a)$ was visited.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Stationary Distribution under a Fixed Policy

In infinite-horizon discounted or average-reward settings, we often seek the long-run distribution of states.

**Step 1:** Start with the policy-induced transition function $\mathcal{P}_\pi(s, s') = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \mathcal{P}(s, a, s')$.

**Step 2:** Define the **stationary distribution** $\mu(s)$ as a fixed point of the forward dynamics operator:

$$
\mu(s') = \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \mu(s)\, \mathcal{P}_\pi(s, s')
$$

**Step 3:** Express this in **matrix–vector form** by letting $\mathbf{P}_\pi$ be the $\lvert \mathcal{S} \rvert \times \lvert \mathcal{S} \rvert$ row-stochastic matrix of $\mathcal{P}_\pi$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ be the row vector of state probabilities:

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \boldsymbol{\mu} \mathbf{P}_\pi
$$

**Step 4:** This is a left-eigenvector equation with eigenvalue **1**. Existence is guaranteed by the **Perron–Frobenius theorem** for irreducible, aperiodic Markov chains, and uniqueness is guaranteed up to a multiplicative constant that is then fixed by $\sum_s \mu(s) = 1$.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Position of the Transition Function in the Agent–Environment Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Agent] -->|Action a_t| node2[Environment]
    node2 -->|Reward r_t| node1
    node2 -->|Next State s_{t+1}| node1
    node3[Transition Function P] -. governs .-> node2
    node4[Reward Function R] -. governs .-> node2
    node5[Policy pi] -. governs .-> node1

    classDef primary fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a4a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef secondary fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#5a7ba8,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f4e79
    classDef function fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px,color:#7f6000

    class node1,node2 primary
    class node3,node4,node5 function
```

**Reading Guide:** The solid arrows represent the observable data flow $(s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1})$ at every timestep. The dashed arrows expose the *latent generative mechanisms* — the transition function $\mathcal{P}$ and reward function $\mathcal{R}$ are *not* directly observable by the agent; they are the hidden rules that produce the data stream.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Tabular Representation of the Transition Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[State s] -->|combine| nodeB[Action a]
    nodeB -->|lookup| nodeC[Tensor T shape M x N x N]
    nodeC -->|slice T_a| nodeD[Stochastic Matrix N x N]
    nodeD -->|row s| nodeE[Distribution over next states]
    nodeE -->|sample| nodeF[Next State s prime]

    classDef state fill:#c6e0b4,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px,color:#1e4d1e
    classDef tensor fill:#f8cbad,stroke:#c65911,stroke-width:2px,color:#5a2d0c
    classDef output fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#2e75b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0b2a4a

    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeF state
    class nodeC,nodeD,nodeE tensor
    class nodeF output
```

**Reading Guide:** This is the **functional data structure** of the transition function. For every action $a$, we extract a slice $T^{(a)}$ — an $N \times N$ stochastic matrix where $T^{(a)}_{ij} = \mathcal{P}(s_i, a, s_j)$. To sample a next state, we locate row $s$ in slice $T^{(a)}$ and draw from that probability vector.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Example State Transition Graph for a 4-State MDP

```mermaid
graph LR
    s1((S1)) -->|a1: 0.7| s2((S2))
    s1 -->|a1: 0.3| s3((S3))
    s2 -->|a2: 0.5| s3((S3))
    s2 -->|a2: 0.5| s4((S4))
    s3 -->|a1: 1.0| s3((S3))
    s4 -->|a1: 1.0| s1((S1))

    classDef terminal fill:#ffe699,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:3px,color:#7f6000
    classDef selfloop fill:#f4b084,stroke:#c65911,stroke-width:3px,color:#5a2d0c

    class s3 selfloop
```

**Reading Guide:** Each directed edge $(s_i \xrightarrow{a: p} s_j)$ represents a single transition probability $\mathcal{P}(s_i, a, s_j) = p$. Notice that **all outgoing edges from any state under any action must sum to 1** — for example, from $S_1$ under $a_1$: $0.7 + 0.3 = 1.0$. The self-loop on $S_3$ under $a_1$ indicates an **absorbing-like** behavior where the system stays in place with probability 1.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Block-Level Architecture of Model-Based vs Model-Free RL

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MB[Model-Based RL]
        MB1[Environment Interaction] --> MB2[(s,a,r,s prime) Buffer]
        MB2 --> MB3[Estimated Transition Function P hat]
        MB2 --> MB4[Estimated Reward Function R hat]
        MB3 --> MB5[Planning Module e.g. Value Iteration]
        MB4 --> MB5
        MB5 --> MB6[Policy pi]
    end

    subgraph MF[Model-Free RL]
        MF1[Environment Interaction] --> MF2[(s,a,r,s prime) Buffer]
        MF2 --> MF3[Direct Value or Policy Update]
        MF3 --> MF4[Policy pi or Q function]
    end

    classDef buffer fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#5a7ba8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f4e79
    classDef model fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px,color:#7f6000
    classDef policy fill:#c6e0b4,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px,color:#1e4d1e

    class MB2,MF2 buffer
    class MB3,MB4,MB5 model
    class MB6,MF3,MF4 policy
```

**Reading Guide:** This contrast crystallizes the central role of the transition function. In **Model-Based RL** (e.g., Dyna, AlphaZero), $\mathcal{P}$ is *explicitly estimated* from samples and then used for planning. In **Model-Free RL** (e.g., Q-learning, PPO), $\mathcal{P}$ is *never stored*; the agent implicitly learns values or policies that "bake in" the dynamics.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

### Question A1

**Q:** Define the transition function of a Markov Decision Process. What is the Markov property it embodies?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
