Q(s,a) \;\leftarrow\; Q(s,a) + \alpha \Big[\, r_{\text{sim}} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s'_{\text{sim}},a') - Q(s,a) \,\Big]
$$

**Conclusion:** The update is **identical** in form. The only difference is whether the transition comes from the *real world* (learning) or the *simulated model* (planning). ∎

> This is the **unified view** and is the formal backbone of the Dyna architecture.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Dyna-Q on a 3-state Grid

Consider a deterministic 1-D environment with 3 states $\{L, C, R\}$ and 2 actions $\{left, right\}$. Rewards: $R\to+1$, $L\to0$, and the episode terminates at $L$ or $R$. Discount $\gamma = 0.9$, $\alpha = 0.5$.

**Initialize:** $Q(s,a)=0$ for all $s,a$. Empty model. $n=2$ planning steps.

#### Real interaction (Learning + Model update):

Suppose the agent is at $C$, takes $a=right$, gets $r=+1$, lands at $R$ (terminal).

1. **Direct RL (Q-learning):**
   $$
   Q(C,\text{right}) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5\,[\,1 + 0.9 \cdot \max Q(R,\cdot) - 0\,] = 0.5
   $$
   (since $Q(R,\cdot)=0$, $\max = 0$.)

2. **Model update:** store $M(C,\text{right}) = (R, +1)$.

#### Planning phase ($n=2$ simulated updates):

**Planning step 1** — pick a previously observed state, say $C$, and a previously taken action, $a=left$, with $M(C,\text{left}) = (L, 0)$ (from prior experience):
$$
Q(C,\text{left}) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5\,[\,0 + 0.9 \cdot \max Q(L,\cdot) - 0\,] = 0
$$

**Planning step 2** — pick $(L, \text{right})$ with $M(L,\text{right}) = (L, 0)$:
$$
Q(L,\text{right}) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5\,[\,0 + 0.9 \cdot \max Q(L,\cdot) - 0\,] = 0
$$

**Result after one real step + two planning steps:**
- $Q(C,\text{right}) = 0.5$ (set by real experience)
- All other $Q$ values still $0$.

This is a *simple tabular* example. With more episodes, planning backfills knowledge of unreachable-from-current-state regions rapidly.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Planning Methods

| Method | Needs Distribution Model? | Computational Cost / Step | When to Use | Convergence Guarantee |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Value Iteration (DP)** | Yes | $O(\vert\mathcal{S}\vert^2 \vert\mathcal{A}\vert)$ | Small, known MDPs | Yes (contraction) |
| **Dyna-Q** | No (sample model) | $n$ cheap backups | Online, unknown env | Yes (Tabular) |
| **Prioritized Sweeping** | No | Heuristic; near $O(\vert\mathcal{S}\vert\log\vert\mathcal{S}\vert)$ | Large state spaces, slow propagation | Yes (Tabular) |
| **Trajectory Sampling** | Sample model | $O(L)$ for trajectory length $L$ | When full sweep is infeasible | Yes (in expectation) |
| **Rollout Algorithm** | Sample model | $K$ rollouts × horizon $H$ | Decision-time planning | Policy improvement only |
| **MCTS** | Sample model | $K$ simulations | Game AI, large branching | Asymptotic to optimal |

> Here $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$ and $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ are the cardinalities of the state and action sets (deliberately using `\vert` to preserve table formatting).

---

### 3.4 Convergence Argument for Dyna-Q (Sketch)

Define the operator $\mathcal{T}$ applied to $Q$:
$$
(\mathcal{T}Q)(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\big[\,R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S',a') \,\big|\, S{=}s,A{=}a\,\big]
$$

This is a **$\gamma$-contraction** under the $\infty$-norm:
$$
\| \mathcal{T}Q_1 - \mathcal{T}Q_2 \|_\infty \;\le\; \gamma \| Q_1 - Q_2 \|_\infty
$$

By **Banach's fixed-point theorem**, repeated application of $\mathcal{T}$ converges to the unique fixed point $Q^*$. Since Dyna-Q performs both real and simulated applications of $\mathcal{T}$, its planning component inherits the contraction property. The stochastic component (via $\alpha$ and sample noise) converges under standard Robbins-Monro conditions:
$$
\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty, \qquad \sum_t \alpha_t^2 < \infty
$$
Hence **Dyna-Q converges to $Q^*$ in the tabular case**. ∎
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

### 4.1 Unified View of Learning and Planning (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    env1[Environment] -- "Real transition s,a,s',r" --> learn[Direct RL]
    learn -- "Update Q or V" --> policy[Policy / Value]
    learn -- "Observe s',r" --> model[Learned Model]
    model -- "Sample simulated s',r" --> plan[Planning]
    plan -- "Update Q or V" --> policy
    policy -- "Action a" --> env1
```

---

### 4.2 Dyna Architecture (the Classic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[State s] --> B[Action Selector epsilon-greedy from Q]
    B --> C[Real Environment]
    C --> D[Real Experience r and s_prime]
    D --> E[Direct RL Q-learning update]
    D --> F[Model Learning store s,a to s_prime,r]
    F --> G[Simulated Experience sampler]
    G --> H[Planning n updates on Q]
    E --> I[Q table]
    H --> I
    I --> B
```

---

### 4.3 Dyna-Q Inner Loop (Sequential Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start of episode] --> stepB[Observe s]
    stepB --> stepC[Choose a epsilon greedy]
    stepC --> stepD[Execute a get r s_prime]
    stepD --> stepE[Q-learning update on real transition]
    stepE --> stepF[Model update store s,a to s_prime,r]
    stepF --> stepG{Planning steps n done?}
    stepG -- No --> stepH[Sample random previously seen s a from model]
    stepH --> stepI[Q-learning update on simulated transition]
    stepI --> stepG
    stepG -- Yes --> stepJ[Next timestep]
```

---

### 4.4 Prioritized Sweeping — High-Level Idea

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Backup a state] --> p2[Compute magnitude of TD error delta]
    p2 --> p3[If delta large push predecessors into priority queue]
    p3 --> p4[Pop state with largest priority]
    p4 --> p5[Backup that state and update its predecessors]
    p5 --> p2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is better than Dyna-Q:** A randomly sampled planning state often has small $\vert \delta \vert$ — wasted compute. Prioritized sweeping targets states whose values just changed, so updates propagate backward along chains of influence far more efficiently.

---

### 4.5 Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) — Decision-Time Planning

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    m1[Selection: traverse tree using UCB] --> m2[Expansion: add a new child node]
    m2 --> m3[Simulation: roll out a default policy to terminal]
    m3 --> m4[Backpropagation: update visit counts and value estimates along the path]
    m4 --> m1
```

**UCB1 (Upper Confidence Bound for Trees):**

$$
\text{UCB}(s,a) \;=\; \bar{Q}(s,a) + c \sqrt{\frac{\ln N(s)}{N(s,a)}}
$$

where $\bar{Q}(s,a)$ is the empirical action value, $N(s)$ is the visit count of the parent state, $N(s,a)$ the count of the action, and $c$ is the exploration constant (typically $c \approx \sqrt{2}$).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📌 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Set]**
**Differentiate between *learning* and *planning* in reinforcement learning. State one example of each from a real engineering system.**

**Model Answer (Key Points — 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Learning | Planning |
|---|---|---|
| Source of experience | Real interaction with environment | Simulated interaction via model |
| Updates value via | Sample backups of true transitions | Sample (or full) backups of model transitions |
| Example | A robot arm learning grasp force from real trials | Same arm using an internal physics model to "imagine" outcomes before acting |
| Cost of data | High (physical time, wear) | Cheap (compute) but model-biased |

*(1 mark for each correct row in the table; 1 mark for example.)* ✅

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Set]**
**What is the difference between a *sample model* and a *distribution model*? Which one does Dyna-Q learn, and why?**

**Model Answer:**

- A **distribution model** explicitly specifies $\Pr(s',r \mid s,a)$ for every $(s',r)$ pair (e.g., from the true MDP). *(1 mark)*
- A **sample model** only produces a single outcome $(s',r)$ when queried; it does **not** enumerate all possibilities. *(1 mark)*
- **Dyna-Q learns a sample model** because it stores only the *most recently observed* transition for each $(s,a)$ in a table — sufficient for sample backups, and far more practical in unknown environments. *(1 mark)*

---

## 📌 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### 🅰️ **Question A — Dyna & Dyna-Q (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** Explain the **Dyna architecture** with a neat block diagram. Clearly mark the **direct RL**, **model learning**, and **planning** components. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

The Dyna architecture (Sutton & Barto, 2018, Chapter 8) integrates five processes operating on a single architecture:

1. **Policy/Value store** $Q(s,a)$ — the data structure being improved. *(1 mark)*
2. **Direct RL** — updates $Q$ using the *real* transition $(s, a, r, s')$. *(1 mark)*
3. **Model learning** — updates the internal model $\mathcal{M}$ with the *real* transition. *(1 mark)*
4. **Planning** — repeatedly samples $(s, a)$ from $\mathcal{M}$ and applies the same backup rule to $Q$. *(1 mark)*
5. **Action selection** — uses $\varepsilon$-greedy over $Q$. *(1 mark)*

**Block diagram (reproduced from the notes):**

```
            ┌────────────┐
            │  Q table   │◀──────────────┐
            └─────┬──────┘               │
                  │                      │
       (read)     │                      │ (plan updates)
                  ▼                      │
            ┌────────────┐               │
            │  ε-greedy  │               │
            └─────┬──────┘               │
                  │ action a             │
                  ▼                      │
            ┌────────────┐               │
            │ Environment│               │
            └─────┬──────┘               │
                  │ s', r                │
        ┌─────────┴────────┐             │
        ▼                  ▼             │
 ┌─────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐      │
 │ Direct RL   │    │   Model     │      │
 │ (real upd.) │    │  Learning   │      │
 └──────┬──────┘    └──────┬──────┘      │
        │                   ▼             │
        │            ┌─────────────┐      │
        │            │   Planning  │──────┘
        │            │ (sim upd.)  │
        │            └─────────────┘
        ▼
   (back to Q table)
```

**The Key Insight:** Direct RL, Model Learning, and Planning operate in parallel on the *same* $(s,a,s',r)$ data flow. *(1 mark for insight)*

**Distinguishing feature:** Dyna's planning uses *exactly* the same backup operator as direct RL — the only difference is the source of the transition. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **(b)** Write the complete **Dyna-Q algorithm**. State the role of the parameter $n$ (number of planning steps). **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

```text
Algorithm Dyna-Q
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Initialize Q(s,a)  for all s ∈ S, a ∈ A(s)
Initialize Model(s,a)  for all s ∈ S, a ∈ A(s)
Parameters:    step size α ∈ (0,1], small ε > 0
               discount γ, planning steps n
Repeat (for each episode):
   s ← current (non-terminal) state
   While s is not terminal:
      a ← ε-greedy(s, Q)               ← Action selection
      Execute a; observe r, s'         ← Real environment step
      Q(s,a) ← Q(s,a) + α [ r + γ·max_a' Q(s',a') − Q(s,a) ]  ← Direct RL
      Model(s,a) ← s', r               ← Model learning
      Repeat n times:                  ← Planning phase
         s_prev ← random previously observed state
         a_prev ← random action previously taken in s_prev
         r_p, s_p ← Model(s_prev, a_prev)
         Q(s_prev,a_prev) ← Q(s_prev,a_prev)
                  + α [ r_p + γ·max_a' Q(s_p,a') − Q(s_prev,a_prev) ]
      s ← s'
```

**Mark split:**

- Action selection & real environment step — *1 mark*
- Direct RL (Q-learning) update — *1 mark*
- Model learning step — *1 mark*
- Planning loop structure — *2 marks*
- Role of $n$ — *1 mark* (see below)
- Correctness and completeness — *1 mark*

**Role of $n$ (planning steps):** *(1 mark)*

- $n$ controls the **compute budget per real step**.
- $n=0$ → pure model-free Q-learning (no planning).
- $n \to \infty$ → exhaustive value-iteration-style planning.
- Increasing $n$ speeds up convergence of $Q$ to $Q^*$ (subject to model accuracy) but increases wall-clock time per step. A practical choice balances compute vs. sample efficiency.

---

### 🅱️ **Question B — Prioritized Sweeping & MCTS (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** Explain **Prioritized Sweeping** with reference to Dyna-Q. What problem does it solve, and how? **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Problem with Dyna-Q's planning:** *(2 marks)*

In Dyna-Q, the $n$ planning updates are performed on **randomly sampled** previously seen states. Most random states have value estimates that have barely changed — their backups are essentially no-ops, wasting compute. In long-chain environments, useful information at the goal state takes many random samples to propagate back to the start.

**Idea of Prioritized Sweeping:** *(2 marks)*

Track the **magnitude of the TD error** $\vert \delta \vert$ from each backup. A large $\vert \delta \vert$ means a state's value changed a lot — its **predecessors** (states that can transition into it) are likely to be affected too. Prioritize the planning of those high-error states.

**Algorithm (sketch):** *(2 marks)*

1. After a real transition, perform the Q-learning update and compute $\delta$.
2. Insert all predecessors of $(s,a)$ into a **priority queue** with priority $\vert \delta \vert$ (capped at $P_{\max}$ to avoid outliers).
3. Pop the state with the **largest** priority and perform a planning backup on it.
4. Recompute the resulting $\delta$ and push its predecessors — repeat until the queue is empty or a step limit is reached.

**Advantage:** Useful value information propagates *rapidly* backward from the goal through long chains, often achieving in tens of steps what Dyna-Q needs thousands of random samples to do. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **(b)** Explain **Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)** as a decision-time planning algorithm. List and briefly describe its four steps. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

MCTS is a **lookahead search** algorithm that builds an asymmetric search tree by sampling rollouts. It is the algorithm behind **AlphaZero, AlphaGo, and many game AIs**.

**The four steps (each ~1.5 marks):**

1. **Selection:** Starting at the root, recursively select child actions using a **tree policy** that balances exploitation and exploration. The most common choice is **UCB1**:
   $$
   a^* = \arg\max_a \Big[\, \bar{Q}(s,a) + c \sqrt{\tfrac{\ln N(s)}{N(s,a)}} \,\Big]
   $$
   This continues until reaching a node that is **not fully expanded** or is **terminal**.

2. **Expansion:** Add **one (or more) new child node** corresponding to an untried action from the selected leaf node.

3. **Simulation (Rollout):** From the new node, simulate an entire episode to a terminal state using a **rollout policy** (often uniform random, or a fast heuristic). Let the return be $G$.

4. **Backpropagation:** Walk back up the path, updating the visit count $N(s,a) \leftarrow N(s,a)+1$ and the empirical value
   $$
   \bar{Q}(s,a) \;\leftarrow\; \bar{Q}(s,a) + \tfrac{1}{N(s,a)}\big[\,G - \bar{Q}(s,a)\,\big]
   $$
   for every edge on the path.

After $K$ simulations, the **action recommended at the root** is typically $a^* = \arg\max_a \bar{Q}(s,a)$ or $\arg\max_a N(s,a)$ (most visits). *(1 mark for the recommendation rule and overall summary)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common ways students LOSE marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the model with the agent.** The model is *not* the agent; it is a learned approximation of $P$ and $R$.
> 2. **Forgetting the discount factor $\gamma$** in the Q-learning update — a single character missing ⇒ 1–2 marks gone.
> 3. **Mixing up the role of $n$** (planning steps) with $\alpha$ (learning rate) — they are independent hyperparameters.
> 4. **Stating Dyna-Q converges without naming the assumption** (tabular, Robbins-Monro learning-rate conditions). Examiners reward precise statements.
> 5. **In MCTS, omitting UCB1** — Selection without a tree policy gets only partial credit.
> 6. **Writing "model-free" for Dyna-Q** — Dyna-Q is *model-based*! It learns a model and uses it for planning.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Model** = an approximation of $P(s',r \mid s,a)$. Two kinds: **distribution** vs **sample**.
- **Planning** = using a model to compute/improve a policy through simulated experience.
- **Learning** = improving the policy from **real** environment interaction.
- **Both learning and planning perform the same Bellman backup** — only the *source* of transitions differs (this is the unified view).
- **Dyna** = Direct RL + Model Learning + Planning operating in parallel on the same data.
- **Dyna-Q** uses **Q-learning** as the backup and learns a **deterministic sample model** by storing the most recent transition per $(s,a)$.
- The **planning parameter $n$** trades compute per step for sample efficiency.
- **Prioritized sweeping** improves on random planning by ordering backups using $\vert \delta \vert$ — essential in long-chain environments.
- **Full backup** = expectation over all $(s',r)$; **Sample backup** = single sampled transition. Sample backups are cheaper and natural for learned models.
- **MCTS** is decision-time planning using **Selection → Expansion → Simulation → Backpropagation** with UCB1 as the tree policy.
- **Convergence of Dyna-Q** follows from the $\gamma$-contraction of the Bellman optimality operator + Robbins-Monro step-size conditions.
- **Engineering wisdom:** A *good model + simple planner* beats a *great algorithm on a bad model* in nearly every real deployment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimal Policy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Optimal Policy ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the framework of **Markov Decision Processes (MDP)** and **Reinforcement Learning (RL)**, an **Optimal Policy**, denoted as $\pi^*$, is formally defined as a stationary deterministic (or stochastic) policy that maximizes the expected cumulative discounted reward (return) from every state $s \in \mathcal{S}$ in the state space. Mathematically, an optimal policy is one that achieves the **optimal state-value function** $V^*(s)$ or equivalently the **optimal action-value function** $Q^*(s,a)$: $$\pi^* = \arg\max_{\pi} \; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{t} R_{t+1} \,\Big|\, S_0 = s\right] \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$$ where $\gamma \in [0,1)$ is the **discount factor** and $R_{t+1}$ is the immediate reward received at time $t+1$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Theorem (Policy Optimality Theorem):** *For any finite MDP, there exists at least one deterministic stationary optimal policy. Furthermore, all optimal policies share the same optimal value function $V^*$ and the same optimal action-value function $Q^*$.* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chess Grandmaster" Imagine you are a **chess grandmaster** preparing for a tournament. After analyzing thousands of games, you have developed a fixed response to every possible board configuration. No matter what your opponent does, you always pick the move that, on average, gives you the best chance of eventually winning. Your "strategy book" never changes once finalized — it is the *best possible* way to play given the rules. - The **board positions** are the *states* $s$. - The **legal moves** are the *actions* $a \in \mathcal{A}$. - The **chance of winning/losing/drawing** is the *reward signal* $r$. - The **strategy book** is the *optimal policy* $\pi^*$. Just as a grandmaster's strategy book maps *every* board position to *one* best move, the optimal policy $\pi^*$ maps *every* state to *one* best action. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The concept of an Optimal Policy bridges Module 1 (MDP fundamentals) and Module 2 (Bellman equations, value iteration, policy iteration). Mastery of $V^*$ and $Q^*$ is essential before tackling **Value Iteration**, **Policy Iteration**, and **Bellman Optimality**. ### 1.3 Why is the Optimal Policy Important? | Aspect | Significance | |---|---| | **Convergence Target** | Acts as the *fixed point* that all RL algorithms (VI, PI, Q-Learning) attempt to reach. | | **Performance Ceiling** | Defines the **maximum achievable return** under a given MDP. | | **Actionability** | Once $Q^*$ is known, the optimal action can be obtained via a simple one-step $\arg\max$ — no future simulation required. | | **Uniqueness of Value** | Even when multiple optimal policies exist, the *value* $V^*$ is always unique. | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Pre-Requisites (Quick Recap from Module 1) - **MDP Tuple:** $\mathcal{M} = (\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$ - **Policy $\pi(a \mid s)$:** Probability of taking action $a$ in state $s$. - **State-Value Function:** $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{t} R_{t+1} \,\big|\, S_0=s\right]$ - **Action-Value Function:** $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{t} R_{t+1} \,\big|\, S_0=s, A_0=a\right]$ ### 2.2 Operational Logic — The Five Pillars of an Optimal Policy The existence and characterization of an optimal policy rests on **five sequential logic pillars**: 1. **Pillar 1 — Bellman Optimality for $V^*$:** The optimal value function must satisfy a *recursive* consistency. From state $s$, the agent should choose the action that maximizes the expected one-step reward plus the discounted optimal future value: $$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,\bigl[r + \gamma V^{*}(s')\bigr]$$ 2. **Pillar 2 — Bellman Optimality for $Q^*$:** The optimal action-value equals the expected reward plus the discounted *maximum* (not expectation) of the next state's $Q$: $$Q^{*}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,\bigl[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s',a')\bigr]$$ 3. **Pillar 3 — Greedy Extraction of $\pi^*$:** Once $Q^*$ is known, the optimal policy is recovered by simply picking the action with the highest $Q^*$ at every state: $$\pi^{*}(a \mid s) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } a = \arg\max_{a} Q^{*}(s,a) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ 4. **Pillar 4 — Policy Improvement Theorem:** If a new policy $\pi'$ is *greedy* with respect to $V^{\pi}$, then $V^{\pi'}(s) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$, with strict inequality if $\pi$ is not already optimal. This guarantees **monotonic improvement**. 5. **Pillar 5 — Contraction Mapping & Fixed Point:** The Bellman optimality operator $T^{*}$ defined by $(T^{*}V)(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r+\gamma V(s')]$ is a **$\gamma$-contraction** in $\ell_\infty$ norm, guaranteeing a unique fixed point $V^*$ that can be reached by repeated application. ### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Pillar - **Why a `max` and not an expectation?** Because the *agent* controls the action choice; only the environment is stochastic. The agent optimizes over its own decisions, then takes expectations over the world. - **Why is $\pi^*$ stationary?** The optimal action in state $s$ depends only on $s$ (Markov property) and not on the time step $t$. - **Why use $Q^*$ for extraction?** Because $Q^*(s,a)$ already incorporates the future — to choose the best action, you only need a one-step look-ahead with no further simulation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** In real-world RL applications (robotics, autonomous driving, recommendation systems, AlphaGo), finding the optimal policy is the *end goal*. Algorithms such as Q-Learning, SARSA, DQN, and PPO are all designed to approximate $Q^*$ or $\pi^*$. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** Memorize the relationships between $V^*$, $Q^*$, and $\pi^*$. Most KTU questions on this topic test the ability to interconvert between these three quantities. | **#** | **Quantity** | **Defining Equation** | **Used In** | |:---:|---|---|---| | 1 | Optimal State-Value $V^*(s)$ | $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,[r + \gamma V^{*}(s')]$ | Value Iteration | | 2 | Optimal Action-Value $Q^*(s,a)$ | $Q^{*}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s',a')]$ | Q-Learning | | 3 | Recovery of $\pi^*$ from $Q^*$ | $\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q^{*}(s,a)$ | Policy Extraction | | 4 | Recovery of $\pi^*$ from $V^*$ | $\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,[r + \gamma V^{*}(s')]$ | Planning | | 5 | Relationship $V^* \leftrightarrow Q^*$ | $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} Q^{*}(s,a)$ | Conversion | | 6 | Policy Improvement Step | $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$ | Policy Iteration | | 7 | Bellman Optimality Operator | $(T^{*}V)(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,[r+\gamma V(s')]$ | Convergence Proof | | 8 | Contraction Property | $\Vert T^{*}V - T^{*}U \Vert_{\infty} \leq \gamma \Vert V - U \Vert_{\infty}$ | Existence/Uniqueness | > **Notation Note:** For any scalar $x$, the absolute value is written as $\vert x \vert$ (or $\mid x \mid$) — never using the bare vertical pipe symbol, to preserve markdown table integrity. ### 2.5 Existence & Uniqueness — A Contraction Argument Define the **Bellman optimality operator** $T^{*}: \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert} \to \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert}$ as in row 7 of the table above. We claim $T^*$ is a $\gamma$-contraction: $$\Vert T^{*}V - T^{*}U \Vert_{\infty} \leq \gamma \Vert V - U \Vert_{\infty}$$ By the **Banach Fixed-Point Theorem**, every $\gamma$-contraction on a complete metric space has a **unique fixed point**. Since $\mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert}$ with the $\ell_\infty$ norm is complete, $T^{*}$ has a unique fixed point — that fixed point **is** $V^*$. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^*$ **Starting Point:** Definition of the optimal value function as the maximum over all policies. $$V^{*}(s) \;=\; \max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s) \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute the Bellman Expectation Equation for an Arbitrary Policy $\pi$:** $$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,\bigl[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\bigr]$$ **Step 2 — Take the Maximum Over $\pi$:** $$V^{*}(s) = \max_{\pi} \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,\bigl[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\bigr]$$ **Step 3 — Recognize the Structure:** A linear combination $\sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s)\, x_a$ is maximized (subject to $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s)=1$ and $\pi(a \mid s) \geq 0$) by placing **all probability mass on the action with the largest $x_a$**. Hence: $$\max_{\pi} \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s)\, x_a = \max_{a} x_a$$ **Step 4 — Apply This to the Inner Expression (with $x_a = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r+\gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$):** $$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,\bigl[r + \gamma V^{*}(s')\bigr]$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** The optimal value $V^*(s)$ equals the *one-step look-ahead reward* plus the *discounted optimal future value*, where the action is chosen to maximize the combined quantity. The agent's choice is a `max`; the environment's stochasticity is an *expectation*. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q^*$ **Starting Point:** Definition $Q^{*}(s,a) = \max_{\pi} Q^{\pi}(s,a)$. **Step 1 — Expand $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ Using the Action-Value Bellman Equation:** $$Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,\bigl[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\bigr]$$ **Step 2 — Take Maximum Over $\pi$:** $$Q^{*}(s,a) = \max_{\pi} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,\bigl[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\bigr]$$ **Step 3 — Push the $\max$ Inside (allowed because $V^\pi(s')$ already depends on $\pi$):** $$Q^{*}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,\bigl[r + \gamma \max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s')\bigr]$$ **Step 4 — Recognize $\max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s') = V^{*}(s') = \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s',a')$:** $$Q^{*}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,\bigl[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s',a')\bigr]$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Greedy Policy Extraction from $Q^*$ **Claim:** $\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q^{*}(s,a)$ is an optimal policy. **Proof Sketch:** **Step 1 — Start from the optimality of $Q^*$:** $$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} Q^{*}(s,a)$$ **Step 2 — Define the greedy policy $\pi_g(a \mid s) = 1$ if $a = \arg\max_b Q^{*}(s,b)$, else $0$:** $$\sum_{a} \pi_g(a \mid s) Q^{*}(s,a) = \max_{a} Q^{*}(s,a) = V^{*}(s)$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Policy Improvement Theorem:** If for every state $s$, $$\sum_{a} \pi_g(a \mid s) Q^{*}(s,a) \geq \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) Q^{*}(s,a) \quad \forall \pi,$$ then $V^{\pi_g}(s) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$ for all $\pi$, meaning $\pi_g$ is optimal. $\blacksquare$ ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Value Iteration Update Rule The Value Iteration algorithm updates the value estimate by applying the Bellman optimality operator once per iteration: $$V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,\bigl[r + \gamma V_{k}(s')\bigr]$$ **Convergence Proof (Sketch):** Define the error $e_k = \Vert V_k - V^* \Vert_\infty$. By the contraction property: $$e_{k+1} = \Vert V_{k+1} - V^* \Vert_\infty = \Vert T^{*}V_k - T^{*}V^* \Vert_\infty \leq \gamma \Vert V_k - V^* \Vert_\infty = \gamma\, e_k$$ By induction, $e_k \leq \gamma^{k} e_0 \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$ (since $0 \leq \gamma < 1$). Thus $V_k \to V^*$. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Summary | **Algorithm** | **Update Rule** | **Convergence** | **Output** | |---|---|---|---| | **Value Iteration** | $V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(\cdot)[r+\gamma V_k(s')]$ | Yes, $\gamma^k$-rate | $V^*$, then $\pi^*$ | | **Policy Iteration** | (1) Policy Evaluation: $V^{\pi_k} = r + \gamma P^{\pi_k} V^{\pi_k}$
(2) Policy Improvement: $\pi_{k+1}(s) = \arg\max_a \sum p(\cdot)[r+\gamma V^{\pi_k}(s')]$ | Yes, finite steps (finite MDP) | $\pi^*$, $V^{\pi^*}$ | | **Q-Value Iteration** | $Q_{k+1}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(\cdot)[r+\gamma \max_{a'} Q_k(s',a')]$ | Yes, $\gamma^k$-rate | $Q^*$, then $\pi^*$ | | **Greedy Extraction** | $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s,a)$ | Instant (post $Q^*$) | $\pi^*$ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Policy Iteration often converges in **fewer outer iterations** than Value Iteration, but each outer iteration is *more expensive* because Policy Evaluation requires solving a linear system (or running many inner sweeps). Value Iteration has *cheaper iterations* but needs *more* of them. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Optimal Policy Computation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input: MDP Tuple
S, A, P, R, gamma] --> B[Initialize Value Function
V0 s = 0 for all s] B --> C{Bellman Optimality
Update Loop} C -->|For each state s| D[For each action a in A s] D --> E[Compute Q value
Q s a = sum over s' r
P times r plus gamma V s'] E --> F[Take max over a
to get V k+1 s] F --> G{Convergence Check
delta less than theta} G -->|No, continue| C G -->|Yes, converged| H[Optimal Value Function
V star s achieved] H --> I[Greedy Policy Extraction
pi star s = argmax a Q star s a] I --> J[Output: Optimal Policy
pi star mapping states to actions] style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style C fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000 style D fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style E fill:#deebf7,stroke:#000 style F fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style G fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000 style H fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style I fill:#548235,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style J fill:#385723,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Policy Iteration Cycle — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR PI1[Policy Evaluation
Compute V_pi for current policy pi] --> PI2{Is pi stable?
i.e. greedy w.r.t. V_pi equals pi} PI2 -->|No, improve| PI3[Policy Improvement
pi new s = argmax a Q pi s a] PI3 --> PI1 PI2 -->|Yes, stable| PI4[Output Optimal Policy pi star
and Optimal Value V star] style PI1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style PI2 fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000 style PI3 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style PI4 fill:#385723,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Relationship Hierarchy — $V^*$, $Q^*$, $\pi^*$ ```mermaid flowchart TD OPT[Optimality Condition
max cumulative discounted reward] --> V1[Optimal State-Value V star s
Bellman Optimality Equation] OPT --> Q1[Optimal Action-Value Q star s a
Bellman Optimality for Actions] OPT --> P1[Optimal Policy pi star
Greedy w.r.t. V star or Q star] V1 -->|argmax a gives pi| P1 Q1 -->|argmax a gives pi| P1 V1 -->|V star s = max a Q star s a| Q1 Q1 -->|Q star s a = E r plus gamma V star s next| V1 style OPT fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style V1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style Q1 fill:#548235,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style P1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ### 4.4 Contraction Mapping — Convergence Visualization (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR V0[Initial Guess V0] -->|Apply T star| V1[V1 equals T star V0] V1 -->|Apply T star| V2[V2 equals T star V1] V2 -->|Apply T star| Vk[Vk equals T star Vk minus 1] Vk -->|Repeated applications| VStar[V star Fixed Point
T star V star = V star] VStar -->|argmax a Q star| PiStar[Optimal Policy pi star] style V0 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#000 style V1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style V2 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000 style Vk fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style VStar fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style PiStar fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** **Define Optimal Policy. State the Bellman Optimality Equation for the optimal state-value function $V^*(s)$.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **Optimal Policy** $\pi^*$ is a policy that yields the maximum expected cumulative discounted return from every state. It satisfies: $$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,\bigl[r + \gamma V^{*}(s')\bigr]$$ The optimal action in state $s$ is $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma V^*(s')]$. **(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the equation.)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Differentiate between the Bellman Expectation Equation and the Bellman Optimality Equation.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | Bellman Expectation | Bellman Optimality | |---|---|---| | Operator type | Expectation over actions | Maximization over actions | | Applicable to | Any policy $\pi$ | Only the optimal policy $\pi^*$ | | Key operator | $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s)\,[\cdot]$ | $\max_a [\cdot]$ | | Result | $V^{\pi}(s)$ | $V^*(s)$ | Expectation averages over actions weighted by the policy; Optimality picks the *best* action. **(1 mark for each correct row.)** --- ### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Answer **ONE** full question. Each carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**. --- ## **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **Q.A [KTU University Exam — Model Question aligned with PECMT745 Module 2]** **(a)** With the help of necessary equations, derive the **Bellman Optimality Equation** for the optimal state-value function $V^*(s)$ starting from the definition $V^*(s) = \max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s)$. Clearly justify why a `max` (not an expectation) is taken over the actions. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Consider a **2-state, 2-action** MDP with discount factor $\gamma = 0.9$. The transition and reward model is given below: | State $s$ | Action $a$ | $p(s' \mid s,a)$ | $r(s,a,s')$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | $s_1$ | $a_1$ | $s_1: 0.7,\; s_2: 0.3$ | $+5,\; +10$ | | $s_1$ | $a_2$ | $s_1: 0.4,\; s_2: 0.6$ | $-1,\; +2$ | | $s_2$ | $a_1$ | $s_1: 0.5,\; s_2: 0.5$ | $+3,\; +3$ | | $s_2$ | $a_2$ | $s_1: 0.2,\; s_2: 0.8$ | $+0,\; +1$ | Starting from $V_0(s_1) = V_0(s_2) = 0$, perform **two iterations of Value Iteration** and report $V_2(s_1), V_2(s_2)$. Also extract the **optimal policy** $\pi^*$. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Q.A** #### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** $$V^{*}(s) = \max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s) \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the Bellman Expectation Equation for $V^{\pi}(s)$ (2 marks):** $$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$$ Substituting: $$V^{*}(s) = \max_{\pi} \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$$ **Step 3 — Optimization Trick (2 marks):** The inner expression is a linear combination $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) x_a$ with $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) = 1$ and $\pi(a \mid s) \geq 0$. This is maximized by placing all mass on the action with the largest $x_a$: $$\max_{\pi} \sum_a \pi(a \mid s)\, x_a = \max_a x_a$$ **Step 4 — Final Equation (2 marks):** $$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,[r + \gamma V^{*}(s')]$$ **Justification — Why `max` not Expectation:** The *agent* deterministically controls action selection; only the *environment* is stochastic. Hence, we maximize over the agent's controllable choice and take expectations only over the world (transitions and rewards). The `max` reflects the agent's optimal control decision. --- #### **Part (b) — Value Iteration (7 Marks)** **Iteration 0:** $V_0(s_1) = 0,\; V_0(s_2) = 0$. **Iteration 1 — Compute $V_1(s_1)$:** Compute the action-value for each action. For $a_1$ in $s_1$: $$Q_0(s_1, a_1) = 0.7\,(5 + 0.9 \cdot 0) + 0.3\,(10 + 0.9 \cdot 0) = 3.5 + 3.0 = 6.5$$ For $a_2$ in $s_1$: $$Q_0(s_1, a_2) = 0.4\,(-1 + 0.9 \cdot 0) + 0.6\,(2 + 0.9 \cdot 0) = -0.4 + 1.2 = 0.8$$ Hence: $V_1(s_1) = \max\{6.5, 0.8\} = 6.5$. **Iteration 1 — Compute $V_1(s_2)$:** For $a_1$ in $s_2$: $$Q_0(s_2, a_1) = 0.5\,(3 + 0.9 \cdot 0) + 0.5\,(3 + 0.9 \cdot 0) = 1.5 + 1.5 = 3.0$$ For $a_2$ in $s_2$: $$Q_0(s_2, a_2) = 0.2\,(0 + 0.9 \cdot 0) + 0.8\,(1 + 0.9 \cdot 0) = 0.0 + 0.8 = 0.8$$ Hence: $V_1(s_2) = \max\{3.0, 0.8\} = 3.0$. **Iteration 2 — Compute $V_2(s_1)$:** Now using $V_1$. For $a_1$ in $s_1$: $$Q_1(s_1, a_1) = 0.7\,(5 + 0.9 \cdot 6.5) + 0.3\,(10 + 0.9 \cdot 3.0) = 0.7\,(10.85) + 0.3\,(12.7) = 7.595 + 3.81 = 11.405$$ For $a_2$ in $s_1$: $$Q_1(s_1, a_2) = 0.4\,(-1 + 0.9 \cdot 6.5) + 0.6\,(2 + 0.9 \cdot 3.0) = 0.4\,(4.85) + 0.6\,(4.7) = 1.94 + 2.82 = 4.76$$ Hence: $V_2(s_1) = \max\{11.405, 4.76\} = 11.405$. **Iteration 2 — Compute $V_2(s_2)$:** For $a_1$ in $s_2$: $$Q_1(s_2, a_1) = 0.5\,(3 + 0.9 \cdot 6.5) + 0.5\,(3 + 0.9 \cdot 3.0) = 0.5\,(8.85) + 0.5\,(5.7) = 4.425 + 2.85 = 7.275$$ For $a_2$ in $s_2$: $$Q_1(s_2, a_2) = 0.2\,(0 + 0.9 \cdot 6.5) + 0.8\,(1 + 0.9 \cdot 3.0) = 0.2\,(5.85) + 0.8\,(3.7) = 1.17 + 2.96 = 4.13$$ Hence: $V_2(s_2) = \max\{7.275, 4.13\} = 7.275$. **Optimal Policy (Greedy Extraction):** $$\pi^*(s_1) = \arg\max\{a_1: 11.405,\; a_2: 4.76\} = a_1$$ $$\pi^*(s_2) = \arg\max\{a_1: 7.275,\; a_2: 4.13\} = a_1$$ **Final Answer:** $$V_2(s_1) = 11.405, \quad V_2(s_2) = 7.275, \quad \pi^*(s_1) = a_1, \quad \pi^*(s_2) = a_1$$ **Mark Distribution:** $V_1$ computation = 2 marks, $V_2$ computation = 3 marks, Policy extraction = 2 marks. --- ## **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q.B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question aligned with PECMT745 Module 2]** **(a)** Explain the **Policy Iteration Algorithm** in detail. List its two main steps and state the **Policy Improvement Theorem**. Show that the algorithm converges in a finite number of iterations for a finite MDP. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Consider the optimal action-value function $Q^*(s,a)$ for an MDP with two states $\{s_1, s_2\}$, two actions $\{a_1, a_2\}$, and $\gamma = 0.8$. The values are: | | $a_1$ | $a_2$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | $s_1$ | 12.0 | 9.5 | | $s_2$ | 7.0 | 10.0 | (i) Compute the **optimal value function** $V^*(s)$ for both states. **(2 Marks)** (ii) Extract the **optimal policy** $\pi^*$. **(2 Marks)** (iii) Verify the **Bellman Optimality Equation** consistency: confirm that $V^*(s) = \max_a Q^*(s,a)$ holds. **(3 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Q.B** #### **Part (a) — Policy Iteration (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Overview (1 mark):** Policy Iteration alternates between **Policy Evaluation** and **Policy Improvement** until the policy stops changing. **Step 2 — Policy Evaluation (2 marks):** Given a policy $\pi_k$, compute its value function $V^{\pi_k}$ by solving the linear system: $$V^{\pi_k}(s) = \sum_a \pi_k(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,[r + \gamma V^{\pi_k}(s')]$$ This can be done iteratively or via direct matrix inversion. **Step 3 — Policy Improvement (2 marks):** Generate a new greedy policy: $$\pi_{k+1}(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\,[r + \gamma V^{\pi_k}(s')]$$ **Step 4 — Policy Improvement Theorem (1 mark):** For all $s \in \mathcal{S}$, $V^{\pi_{k+1}}(s) \geq V^{\pi_k}(s)$, with strict inequality if $\pi_k$ is suboptimal. **Step 5 — Convergence (1 mark):** A finite MDP has a finite number of deterministic policies ($\vert \mathcal{A} \vert^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert}$). Since each iteration strictly improves the value (or leaves it unchanged) and the policy set is finite, the algorithm must terminate at an optimal policy in a finite number of steps. --- #### **Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)** **(i) Optimal Value Function (2 marks):** $$V^*(s_1) = \max\{Q^*(s_1, a_1), Q^*(s_1, a_2)\} = \max\{12.0, 9.5\} = 12.0$$ $$V^*(s_2) = \max\{Q^*(s_2, a_1), Q^*(s_2, a_2)\} = \max\{7.0, 10.0\} = 10.0$$ **(ii) Optimal Policy (2 marks):** $$\pi^*(s_1) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s_1, a) = a_1$$ $$\pi^*(s_2) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s_2, a) = a_2$$ **(iii) Bellman Optimality Consistency Check (3 marks):** By definition of $V^*$ from $Q^*$: $$V^*(s_1) = 12.0 = \max_a Q^*(s_1, a) = 12.0 \quad \checkmark$$ $$V^*(s_2) = 10.0 = \max_a Q^*(s_2, a) = 10.0 \quad \checkmark$$ The consistency relation $V^*(s) = \max_a Q^*(s,a)$ holds for both states, confirming that the given $Q^*$ satisfies the Bellman Optimality Equation. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing `max` with Expectation:** Students often write $V^*(s) = \sum_a \pi^*(a \mid s) [\cdot]$ instead of $V^*(s) = \max_a [\cdot]$. The optimal policy puts all mass on the best action — it is a *max*, not a *weighted sum*. > 2. **Forgetting the Discount Factor:** Many students drop the $\gamma$ term in $V(s')$ during value iteration. Each future value must be discounted by $\gamma$. > 3. **Wrong Greedy Extraction:** Writing $\pi^*(s) = \max_s Q^*(s,a)$ instead of $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s,a)$. The maximization is over **actions**, not states. > 4. **Mixing $V$ and $Q$ Equations:** Using $Q^*(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(\cdot)[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$ (Bellman expectation) instead of the optimality form with $\max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$. Always use the **optimality** version for $\pi^*$. > 5. **Arithmetic Errors in Value Iteration:** KTU examiners frequently award partial credit for *method* but deduct heavily for computational mistakes. Always show every sub-step. > 6. **Non-Convergence Argument Missing:** When asked to prove convergence, students often forget to invoke the **Banach Fixed-Point Theorem** and the **$\gamma$-contraction property**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision — Pin This Section!** - [x] **Optimal Policy** $\pi^*$ maximizes the expected cumulative discounted reward from *every* state. - [x] **Bellman Optimality (V):** $V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma V^*(s')]$ - [x] **Bellman Optimality (Q):** $Q^*(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a')]$ - [x] **Greedy Recovery:** $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s,a)$ (or $\arg\max_a \sum p(\cdot)[r + \gamma V^*(s')]$) - [x] **Conversion:** $V^*(s) = \max_a Q^*(s,a)$ and $Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}[r + \gamma V^*(s')]$ - [x] **Existence/Uniqueness:** Guaranteed for any finite MDP via the **Banach Fixed-Point Theorem** applied to the **$\gamma$-contraction** operator $T^*$. - [x] **Policy Improvement Theorem:** Greedy improvement w.r.t. $V^\pi$ never decreases value; strictly increases if $\pi$ is suboptimal. - [x] **Value Iteration:** $V_{k+1}(s) = \max_a \sum p(\cdot)[r + \gamma V_k(s')]$ — converges at rate $\gamma^k$. - [x] **Policy Iteration:** Alternates evaluation + improvement; converges in **finite** steps for finite MDPs. - [x] **Stationary & Deterministic:** For finite MDPs, an *optimal deterministic stationary* policy always exists. - [x] **Common exam keywords to use:** "Markov property," "discount factor," "Bellman backup," "contraction," "fixed point," "greedy extraction." - [x] **Quick check before submitting:** Did you write `max` over actions and `sum` (expectation) over next-state transitions and rewards? Did you include $\gamma$ on every future term? Did you extract the policy using $\arg\max$? ---

topics.title: Comparison of Policies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Comparison of Policies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the framework of **Markov Decision Processes (MDP)** under Reinforcement Learning (RL), the **Comparison of Policies** refers to the formal mathematical procedure of evaluating and ranking two or more decision-making strategies (policies $\pi$ and $\pi'$) based on their expected cumulative reward. A policy $\pi$ is a mapping from states to a probability distribution over actions, $\pi(a \mid s) = P(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$. The comparison is rigorously performed using the **state-value function** $v_\pi(s)$ and **action-value function** $q_\pi(s,a)$, governed by the **Bellman Expectation Equations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Comparison of Policies is the foundational precursor to the *Policy Improvement Theorem* and *Policy Iteration Algorithm*. Mastery of this concept is mandatory for Part B 14-mark derivations in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Intuition via Real-World Analogy

Imagine two taxi drivers (**Driver A** and **Driver B**) operating in the same city. Both follow different strategies (policies): Driver A prefers highways, Driver B prefers backstreets. To *compare* their policies, we track the total earnings of each driver over 8 hours every day for a month.

- If Driver A consistently earns **more than or equal to** Driver B across *all* neighborhoods (states) in the city, we can confidently say **Driver A's policy dominates Driver B's**.
- In RL terminology, we compare the **expected returns** $G_t$ starting from every possible state.

Similarly, in RL, comparing two policies means checking whether the expected return under $\pi'$ is greater than or equal to that under $\pi$ for **every state** in the state space $\mathcal{S}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Policy comparison is not a single number comparison. It is a **vector comparison** over the entire state space $\mathcal{S}$.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examination

The following metrics are considered *high-yield* by KTU examiners:

| Metric | Symbol | Definition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| State-value function | $v_\pi(s)$ | Expected return starting from $s$ under $\pi$ |
| Action-value function | $q_\pi(s,a)$ | Expected return from $s$, taking $a$, then following $\pi$ |
| Optimal state-value | $v_*(s)$ | Maximum achievable return from $s$ |
| Optimal action-value | $q_*(s,a)$ | Maximum achievable return after taking $a$ in $s$ |
| Discount factor | $\gamma$ | $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ — parameter controlling future reward weighting |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Policy Comparison

The comparison of policies in RL is conducted in **four sequential logical stages**:

**Step 1 — Policy Representation**
A deterministic policy $\pi : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ maps each state to exactly one action. A stochastic policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ defines a probability distribution.

**Step 2 — Value Function Evaluation**
For each policy, compute the state-value function $v_\pi(s)$ by solving the **Bellman Expectation Equation**:
The value function satisfies the recursive relation linking immediate reward to future discounted rewards.

**Step 3 — Pointwise Comparison (The Core Step)**
Two policies are compared element-wise:
- $\pi' \geq \pi$ if and only if $v_{\pi'}(s) \geq v_\pi(s)$ for **all** $s \in \mathcal{S}$.
- If strict inequality holds for at least one $s$, we have **strict policy dominance**: $\pi' > \pi$.

**Step 4 — Optimality Conclusion**
A policy $\pi$ is **optimal** ($v_\pi = v_*$) if and only if no other policy dominates it, i.e., $v_\pi(s) \geq v_{\pi'}(s)$ for all $\pi'$ and all $s \in \mathcal{S}$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why Step 2?** Value functions convert an infinite-horizon sequential decision problem into a tractable scalar metric per state.
- **Why Step 3?** A policy that is "better in one state but worse in another" cannot be universally preferred — this is the **Pareto-optimality** principle in multi-state decision making.
- **Why Step 4?** Optimality is a *global* property — it requires supremacy across the entire state space, not just average performance.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Safeguard:** The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ is used in tables below to prevent markdown table syntax breakage.

| # | Formula / Theorem | Mathematical Form | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Bellman Expectation for $v_\pi$ | $v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a)\left[R(s,a,s') + \gamma v_\pi(s')\right]$ | Value function recursion |
| 2 | Bellman Expectation for $q_\pi$ | $q_\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a)\left[R(s,a,s') + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') q_\pi(s',a')\right]$ | Action-value recursion |
| 3 | Bellman Optimality for $v_*$ | $v_*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a)\left[R(s,a,s') + \gamma v_*(s')\right]$ | Optimal value equation |
| 4 | Bellman Optimality for $q_*$ | $q_*(s,a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a)\left[R(s,a,s') + \gamma \max_{a'} q_*(s',a')\right]$ | Optimal action-value |
| 5 | Policy Dominance Criterion | $\pi' \geq \pi \iff v_{\pi'}(s) \geq v_\pi(s),\ \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$ | Definition of dominance |
| 6 | Policy Improvement Condition | $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a q_\pi(s,a)$ | Greedy policy from $q_\pi$ |
| 7 | Discounted Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Cumulative discounted reward |
| 8 | Performance Difference Lemma (PDL) | $v_{\pi'}(s) - v_\pi(s) = \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d_{\pi'}}\left[\sum_a \left(\pi'(a \mid s) - \pi(a \mid s)\right) q_\pi(s,a)\right]$ | Exact comparison of two policies |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The comparison of policies is not an academic exercise — it has critical applications in:
- **Autonomous Vehicle Path Planning:** Comparing safety policies (aggressive vs. conservative lane-change).
- **Robotics Manipulation:** Comparing energy-efficient vs. speed-prioritized control policies.
- **Telecom Network Routing:** Comparing congestion-avoidance policies in dynamic environments.
- **Financial Portfolio Management:** Comparing risk-averse vs. risk-seeking trading strategies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bellman Expectation Equation from First Principles

**Objective:** Prove that the value function $v_\pi(s)$ satisfies the Bellman Expectation Equation.

**Starting Point:** By definition of the state-value function:
$$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\left[G_t \mid S_t = s\right]$$

**Step 1 — Expand the return $G_t$:**
The return at time $t$ is the discounted sum of rewards from $t+1$ onwards.

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the expectation:**
Plugging this expansion into the definition of $v_\pi(s)$.

$$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply linearity of expectation:**
Splitting the expectation into two terms — one for immediate reward and one for future discounted value.

$$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\left[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\right] + \gamma \mathbb{E}_\pi\left[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\right]$$

**Step 4 — Condition on the action $A_t$:**
Use the law of total expectation by conditioning on the action chosen at time $t$.

$$v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \mathbb{E}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a\right]$$

**Step 5 — Condition on the next state $S_{t+1}$:**
Apply the law of total expectation again, this time over the next state $s'$.

$$v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) \mathbb{E}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t+1} = s'\right]$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Markov Property:**
Since the future depends only on the current state under the Markov assumption, the expectation simplifies.

$$\mathbb{E}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid s, a, s'\right] = R(s,a,s') + \gamma \mathbb{E}_\pi\left[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'\right]$$

**Step 7 — Recognize the inner expectation as $v_\pi(s')$:**
The remaining expectation is the value function evaluated at the next state $s'$.

$$\mathbb{E}_\pi\left[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'\right] = v_\pi(s')$$

**Step 8 — Final assembled equation:**
Combining all terms yields the **Bellman Expectation Equation**.

$$v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a)\left[R(s,a,s') + \gamma v_\pi(s')\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The Markov Property is invoked exactly once in Step 6. This is a common KTU question — students often forget to justify this step and lose 2 marks.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Policy Improvement Theorem

**Statement:** If for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$ and $a \in \mathcal{A}(s)$ we have $q_\pi(s,a) \geq v_\pi(s)$, then the greedy policy $\pi'$ defined by $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a q_\pi(s,a)$ satisfies $v_{\pi'}(s) \geq v_\pi(s)$ for all $s$.

**Step 1 — Start from $q_\pi(s, \pi'(s))$:**
Under the greedy policy, the chosen action maximizes $q_\pi$.

$$v_\pi(s) \leq q_\pi(s, \pi'(s)) = \mathbb{E}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s, A_t = \pi'(s)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Recursive substitution:**
Apply the same expansion at the next time step.

$$= \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma q_\pi(S_{t+1}, \pi'(S_{t+1})) \mid S_t = s\right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the assumption inductively:**
Since $q_\pi(s', a) \geq v_\pi(s')$ for all $(s', a)$, this bound propagates forward.

$$\leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 q_\pi(S_{t+2}, \pi'(S_{t+2})) \mid S_t = s\right]$$

**Step 4 — Continue for infinite horizon:**
Iterating the process $k$ times and taking the limit $k \to \infty$.

$$v_\pi(s) \leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\left[\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \gamma^j R_{t+j+1} + \gamma^k v_\pi(S_{t+k}) \mid S_t = s\right]$$

**Step 5 — Take the limit as $k \to \infty$:**
Since rewards are bounded and $\gamma < 1$, the final term vanishes.

$$\lim_{k \to \infty} \gamma^k v_\pi(S_{t+k}) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Conclude the inequality:**
The limit yields the expected return under the greedy policy $\pi'$.

$$v_\pi(s) \leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\left[\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \gamma^j R_{t+j+1} \mid S_t = s\right] = v_{\pi'}(s)$$

$$\therefore v_{\pi'}(s) \geq v_\pi(s) \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: The Performance Difference Lemma (PDL)

This lemma provides a **closed-form** comparison between two arbitrary policies.

**Statement:**
$$v_{\pi'}(s) - v_\pi(s) = \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d_{\pi'}}\left[\sum_a \left(\pi'(a \mid s) - \pi(a \mid s)\right) q_\pi(s,a)\right]$$

where $d_{\pi'}(s) = (1-\gamma) \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t P(S_t = s \mid \pi')$ is the **discounted state-visitation distribution** under $\pi'$.

**Step 1 — Begin with the Bellman Equation for $q_\pi$:**
The action-value function under $\pi$ satisfies a recursive form.

$$q_\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a)\left[R(s,a,s') + \gamma v_\pi(s')\right]$$

**Step 2 — Define the advantage-like difference:**
The pointwise difference $q_\pi(s, \pi'(s)) - v_\pi(s)$ measures how much better $\pi'$ is in state $s$.

$$\delta(s) = \sum_a \pi'(a \mid s) q_\pi(s,a) - v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \left(\pi'(a \mid s) - \pi(a \mid s)\right) q_\pi(s,a)$$

**Step 3 — Relate $\delta(s)$ to the value difference:**
The total performance gap is the sum of these one-step advantages weighted by how often each state is visited under $\pi'$.

$$v_{\pi'}(s) - v_\pi(s) = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\left[\delta(S_t) \mid S_0 = s\right]$$

**Step 4 — Combine with the discounted state distribution:**
Multiplying by the per-state weight $d_{\pi'}(s)$ and summing over states.

$$v_{\pi'}(s_0) - v_\pi(s_0) = \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \sum_s d_{\pi'}(s) \delta(s)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back the definition of $\delta(s)$:**
This recovers the closed-form PDL expression.

$$v_{\pi'}(s_0) - v_\pi(s_0) = \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \sum_s d_{\pi'}(s) \sum_a \left(\pi'(a \mid s) - \pi(a \mid s)\right) q_\pi(s,a) \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Bellman Expectation | Bellman Optimality | Policy Improvement | PDL |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Evaluate a fixed $\pi$ | Find $v_*$ | Build $\pi'$ from $\pi$ | Compare any two $\pi, \pi'$ |
| **Operator** | $T^\pi$ (linear) | $T^*$ (nonlinear max) | $\arg\max$ over $q_\pi$ | Weighted expectation |
| **Output** | $v_\pi$ | $v_*, \pi_*$ | $v_{\pi'} \geq v_\pi$ | Scalar difference |
| **Computation** | Linear system solve | Iterative max | Closed-form greedy | Closed-form expectation |
| **KTU Weightage** | 7 marks | 7 marks | 14 marks (full question) | 7 marks |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Policy Comparison Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Policy Pi 1] --> evalA[Policy Evaluation]
    inputB[Policy Pi 2] --> evalB[Policy Evaluation]
    envA[Environment Model P, R, Gamma] --> evalA
    envA --> evalB
    evalA --> vA[Compute vPi 1]
    evalB --> vB[Compute vPi 2]
    vA --> comp[Pointwise Comparison Operator]
    vB --> comp
    comp --> d1{Dominance Check}
    d1 -- Greater Equal All States --> out1[Pi 1 dominates Pi 2]
    d1 -- Lesser Equal All States --> out2[Pi 2 dominates Pi 1]
    d1 -- Mixed Result --> out3[Policies Incomparable]
    d1 -- Equal For All --> out4[Policies Equivalent]
    vA --> pdl[PDL Computation]
    vB --> pdl
    pdl --> scalar[Scalar Performance Gap]
```

### 4.2 State-Space Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StateSpace[State Space S]
        direction LR
        s1((s1)) --- s2((s2)) --- s3((s3)) --- s4((s4))
    end
    s1 --> v1A[vPi of s1]
    s1 --> v1B[vPi Prime of s1]
    s2 --> v2A[vPi of s2]
    s2 --> v2B[vPi Prime of s2]
    s3 --> v3A[vPi of s3]
    s3 --> v3B[vPi Prime of s3]
    s4 --> v4A[vPi of s4]
    s4 --> v4B[vPi Prime of s4]
    v1A --> judge{Dominance Test}
    v1B --> judge
    v2A --> judge
    v2B --> judge
    v3A --> judge
    v3B --> judge
    v4A --> judge
    v4B --> judge
    judge --> result[Final Verdict]
```

### 4.3 Policy Iteration Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Initialize Pi 0]) --> eval[Policy Evaluation: Solve for vPi]
    eval --> improve[Policy Improvement: Pi Prime equals argmax qPi]
    improve --> check{v Pi Prime greater equal v Pi for all s}
    check -- Yes --> converged[Converged to Pi Star]
    check -- No --> replace[Set Pi equals Pi Prime]
    replace --> eval
    converged --> compare[Compare v Pi Star with v Pi 0]
    compare --> output[Output Optimal Policy]
```

### 4.4 Decision Logic for Policy Dominance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: vPi and vPi Prime] --> B[For each state s in S]
    B --> C{v Pi Prime of s greater than v Pi of s}
    C -- Yes --> D[Mark s as Strictly Improved]
    C -- No --> E{v Pi Prime of s equals v Pi of s}
    E -- Yes --> F[Mark s as Equivalent]
    E -- No --> G[Mark s as Worse]
    D --> H[Aggregate Results]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Any Worse State}
    I -- Yes --> J[Policies Incomparable]
    I -- No --> K{Any Improved State}
    K -- Yes --> L[Pi Prime Strictly Dominates Pi]
    K -- No --> M[Policies Equivalent]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define policy dominance. When are two policies said to be comparable?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A policy $\pi'$ is said to **dominate** policy $\pi$ (denoted $\pi' \geq \pi$) if and only if the expected return when following $\pi'$ is at least as good as following $\pi$ from every state in the state space. Formally:

$$\pi' \geq \pi \iff v_{\pi'}(s) \geq v_\pi(s),\ \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$$

Two policies are **comparable** if either $\pi' \geq \pi$ or $\pi \geq \pi'$ holds true. If neither condition holds, the policies are said to be **incomparable** (Pareto-incomparable).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*State the Bellman Optimality Equation for the optimal state-value function $v_*(s)$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Bellman Optimality Equation expresses the optimal value as the maximum over all actions of the expected one-step reward plus the discounted optimal future value:

$$v_*(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)\left[R(s, a, s') + \gamma v_*(s')\right]$$

The key feature is the **max operator**, which selects the best action — this is what distinguishes the optimality equation from the expectation equation (which uses a weighted sum).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** *Derive the Bellman Expectation Equation for the state-value function $v_\pi(s)$ from first principles. Clearly state the Markov property assumption and explain each step. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition of value function:** By definition, the state-value function under policy $\pi$ is the expected discounted return starting from state $s$:
$$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\left[G_t \mid S_t = s\right]$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the return:** The return $G_t$ can be split into immediate reward and discounted future return:
$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and apply linearity:**
$$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\right] = \mathbb{E}_\pi\left[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\right] + \gamma \mathbb{E}_\pi\left[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\right]$$

**Step 4 — Condition on action $A_t$:**
$$v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \mathbb{E}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a\right]$$

**Step 5 — Condition on next state $S_{t+1} = s'$:**
$$v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) \mathbb{E}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid s, a, s'\right]$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Markov Property:** Future depends only on the current state:
$$\mathbb{E}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid s, a, s'\right] = R(s,a,s') + \gamma \mathbb{E}_\pi\left[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'\right]$$

**Step 7 — Recognize as $v_\pi(s')$ and finalize:**
$$v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a)\left[R(s,a,s') + \gamma v_\pi(s')\right] \qquad \blacksquare$$

**(b)** *Two policies $\pi$ and $\pi'$ are defined over a 2-state MDP with discount factor $\gamma = 0.9$. The value functions are $v_\pi = [10, 5]^T$ and $v_{\pi'} = [12, 4]^T$. Determine the dominance relationship with justification. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State the dominance criterion:**
$$\pi' \geq \pi \iff v_{\pi'}(s) \geq v_\pi(s),\ \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$$

**Step 2 — Compare element-wise:**
- At state $s_1$: $v_{\pi'}(s_1) = 12 \geq 10 = v_\pi(s_1)$ ✓
- At state $s_2$: $v_{\pi'}(s_2) = 4 < 5 = v_\pi(s_2)$ ✗

**Step 3 — Test reverse direction:**
Check whether $\pi \geq \pi'$:
- At $s_1$: $10 < 12$ ✗

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** Neither $\pi' \geq \pi$ nor $\pi \geq \pi'$ holds. Therefore, **policies $\pi$ and $\pi'$ are incomparable** (Pareto-incomparable). Each policy is preferred in exactly one state, and there is no universally dominant choice.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme, Module 2 — Alternative]**

**(a)** *State and prove the Policy Improvement Theorem. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Theorem:** If for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$ and $a \in \mathcal{A}(s)$, $q_\pi(s,a) \geq v_\pi(s)$, then the greedy policy $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a q_\pi(s,a)$ satisfies $v_{\pi'}(s) \geq v_\pi(s)$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — By definition of $\pi'$:**
Since $\pi'$ is greedy with respect to $q_\pi$, we have $q_\pi(s, \pi'(s)) = \max_a q_\pi(s,a) \geq v_\pi(s)$.

**Step 2 — Expand $q_\pi(s, \pi'(s))$:**
$$q_\pi(s, \pi'(s)) = \mathbb{E}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s, A_t = \pi'(s)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Recursive bound at next state:**
$$= \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma q_\pi(S_{t+1}, \pi'(S_{t+1})) \mid S_t = s\right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply the assumption $q_\pi \geq v_\pi$ inductively:** Iterating $k$ times:
$$\leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\left[\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \gamma^j R_{t+j+1} + \gamma^k v_\pi(S_{t+k}) \mid S_t = s\right]$$

**Step 5 — Take the limit as $k \to \infty$:** Since rewards are bounded and $\gamma < 1$:
$$\lim_{k \to \infty} \gamma^k v_\pi(S_{t+k}) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Conclude:**
$$v_\pi(s) \leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\left[\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \gamma^j R_{t+j+1} \mid S_t = s\right] = v_{\pi'}(s) \qquad \blacksquare$$

**(b)** *Explain with a suitable equation how two policies $\pi$ and $\pi'$ can be compared using the Performance Difference Lemma. Discuss its significance in policy optimization. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State the PDL:**
$$v_{\pi'}(s) - v_\pi(s) = \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d_{\pi'}}\left[\sum_a \left(\pi'(a \mid s) - \pi(a \mid s)\right) q_\pi(s,a)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation of terms:**
- $d_{\pi'}(s)$ is the discounted state-visitation distribution under the new policy $\pi'$.
- $\pi'(a \mid s) - \pi(a \mid s)$ measures the change in action probabilities.
- $q_\pi(s,a)$ is the action-value under the *old* policy.

**Step 3 — Sign of the difference:** If $v_{\pi'}(s) - v_\pi(s) > 0$, then $\pi'$ is strictly better than $\pi$ starting from $s$.

**Step 4 — Significance in policy optimization:**
- The PDL is **model-free** in the sense that it only requires samples of $q_\pi$ — useful in **Trust Region Policy Optimization (TRPO)** and **Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)**.
- It provides an **exact** (not approximate) measure of policy improvement — a powerful theoretical tool.
- It enables **off-policy evaluation** — estimating the performance of a new policy $\pi'$ using data collected under an old policy $\pi$.

**Step 5 — Concluding remark:** The PDL bridges the gap between policy evaluation and policy improvement, making it the cornerstone of modern policy gradient methods.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Markov Property:** Many students skip Step 6 of the Bellman Expectation derivation — this alone costs **2 full marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Misuse of Max vs. Sum:** The Bellman **Expectation** Equation uses a *sum* (weighted by $\pi$), while the **Optimality** Equation uses a *max*. Confusing these is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Partial Dominance Misconception:** Students often claim "$\pi'$ is better than $\pi$" if $v_{\pi'}$ is greater in *one* state. KTU requires **pointwise dominance over ALL states** — failure to state this loses 2 marks.
> 4. **PDL Misapplication:** The PDL uses $d_{\pi'}$ (not $d_\pi$) on the right-hand side. Reversing this is a frequent error and costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Missing the $\frac{1}{1-\gamma}$ factor:** Forgetting this normalization constant in the PDL derivation results in a 1-mark deduction.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Comparison of Policies**

- [x] A **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ is a probability distribution over actions given a state.
- [x] **Policy dominance:** $\pi' \geq \pi$ iff $v_{\pi'}(s) \geq v_\pi(s)$ for **all** $s \in \mathcal{S}$ — this is a *vector* comparison.
- [x] The **Bellman Expectation Equation** uses a *weighted sum* over actions: $v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a)[R + \gamma v_\pi(s')]$.
- [x] The **Bellman Optimality Equation** uses a *max* over actions: $v_*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a)[R + \gamma v_*(s')]$.
- [x] The **Policy Improvement Theorem** guarantees that a greedy policy $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a q_\pi(s,a)$ is at least as good as $\pi$ everywhere.
- [x] The **Performance Difference Lemma (PDL)** gives a closed-form, exact comparison: $v_{\pi'} - v_\pi = \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d_{\pi'}}\left[\sum_a (\pi' - \pi) q_\pi\right]$.
- [x] **Incomparable policies** exist when neither dominates the other — this is the *Pareto-incomparable* case.
- [x] The **Markov Property** must be invoked **once** in the Bellman Expectation derivation — forgetting it costs marks.
- [x] The discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ ensures convergence of infinite-horizon returns.
- [x] Policy Iteration alternates between **Policy Evaluation** and **Policy Improvement** until convergence to $\pi_*$.
- [x] The PDL is foundational for **TRPO, PPO**, and other modern policy gradient algorithms.
- [x] Two policies are **equivalent** if $v_\pi = v_{\pi'}$ for all states (rare in practice).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bellman Equation/State-Value Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bellman Equation/State-Value Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In Reinforcement Learning (RL), the **State-Value Function** $V_{\pi}(s)$ — also called the **V-function** — is the central object of study that quantifies how "good" it is for an agent to be in a particular state $s$ while following a policy $\pi$. Formally, it is defined as the **expected return** (cumulative discounted future reward) when starting from state $s$ and subsequently following policy $\pi$.

The **Bellman Equation**, named after Nobel laureate **Richard Ernest Bellman (1920–1984)**, is the recursive consistency condition that any valid value function must satisfy. It expresses the value of a state in terms of the values of its immediate successor states, embodying the principle of optimal substructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECMT745 – Module 2):**
> The Bellman equation is the cornerstone of nearly every RL algorithm — from Dynamic Programming (Policy Iteration, Value Iteration) to Temporal-Difference learning (TD(0), TD($\lambda$), Q-Learning, SARSA). Mastery of this topic is mandatory for both Part A and Part B questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a student planning your engineering career:

- A state $s$ is your **current situation** (e.g., "2nd year, average CGPA, no internships").
- Actions $a$ are the **decisions** you can make (study harder, apply for internships, learn a new skill).
- Rewards $r$ are the **immediate payoffs** (a good grade, a job offer, satisfaction).
- The state-value $V_{\pi}(s)$ answers the question: *"If I follow this life strategy $\pi$ consistently, what is the total long-term 'happiness' I can expect from where I stand today?"*

The **Bellman equation** then says something profound: your expected long-term happiness from today depends on **(i) how happy you are today**, plus **(ii) how happy you will be tomorrow, weighted by how much you value the future**.

This recursive structure — *today's value = immediate reward + discounted future value* — is what makes the Bellman equation so powerful: it transforms an infinite-horizon problem into a tractable, one-step recurrence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Key Value Functions in RL:**
> 1. **State-Value Function** $V_{\pi}(s)$ — "How good is this state?"
> 2. **Action-Value Function** $Q_{\pi}(s,a)$ — "How good is this action in this state?"
>
> Both satisfy their own Bellman equations, and they are deeply interconnected.

## 1.3 Essential Building Blocks

Before diving deeper, the following entities from a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** must be unambiguous:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $s, s'$ | Current state / next state |
| $a$ | Action taken in state $s$ |
| $r$ | Immediate reward received |
| $\pi(a \mid s)$ | Policy — probability of taking action $a$ in state $s$ |
| $p(s', r \mid s, a)$ | Dynamics of the MDP (transition probability) |
| $\gamma$ | Discount factor, $0 \le \gamma \le 1$ |
| $G_t$ | Return from time $t$ onwards |

The **discount factor $\gamma$** is a scalar between **0** and **1** that determines the present value of future rewards. A higher $\gamma$ means the agent cares more about long-term rewards.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Return — Foundation of Value

The **return** $G_t$ is the cumulative sum of discounted rewards from time step $t$ until termination (or infinity for continuing tasks):

$$G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} \;+\; \gamma R_{t+2} \;+\; \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} \;+\; \cdots \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k}\, R_{t+k+1}$$

**Why discount?** Three engineering-economic reasons:

1. **Mathematical convergence:** With $\gamma < 1$, the infinite geometric series converges even for unbounded rewards.
2. **Uncertainty of the future:** Far-future rewards are less certain than near-term ones.
3. **Avoiding circular returns:** Prevents infinite loops from accumulating unbounded reward.

## 2.2 State-Value Function — Formal Definition

The state-value function $V_{\pi}(s)$ is the **expectation of the return** when starting in state $s$ and following policy $\pi$ thereafter:

$$V_{\pi}(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_t \,\big\vert\, S_t = s \,\right] \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \,\big\vert\, S_t = s \,\right]$$

## 2.3 Action-Value Function — Companion Definition

$$Q_{\pi}(s,a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_t \,\big\vert\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a \,\right]$$

## 2.4 The Bellman Equation — The Heart of RL

### 2.4.1 State-Value Bellman Equation (Recursive Form)

Substituting the definition of $G_t$ and using the **law of iterated expectations**, the value function satisfies:

$$V_{\pi}(s) \;=\; \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\bigl[\, r \;+\; \gamma\, V_{\pi}(s') \,\bigr]$$

**Step-by-step logical breakdown:**

- **Step 1 — Policy weighting:** $\sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s)$ — sum over all actions weighted by how likely the policy chooses them.
- **Step 2 — Environment uncertainty:** $\sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)$ — sum over all possible next states and rewards.
- **Step 3 — One-step decomposition:** $r + \gamma V_{\pi}(s')$ — immediate reward plus discounted value of the next state.
- **Step 4 — Composition:** The total is the expected value of this one-step quantity.

### 2.4.2 Action-Value (Q-function) Bellman Equation

$$Q_{\pi}(s,a) \;=\; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\bigl[\, r \;+\; \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q_{\pi}(s', a') \,\bigr]$$

### 2.4.3 Bellman Optimality Equation

For the **optimal policy** $\pi^{*}$, the value function satisfies:

$$V_{*}(s) \;=\; \max_{a} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\bigl[\, r \;+\; \gamma\, V_{*}(s') \,\bigr]$$

This is a **non-linear system** because of the $\max$ operator, and forms the basis of **Value Iteration**.

## 2.5 Relationship Between $V$ and $Q$

$$V_{\pi}(s) \;=\; \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s)\, Q_{\pi}(s,a)$$

$$Q_{\pi}(s,a) \;=\; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\bigl[\, r \;+\; \gamma\, V_{\pi}(s') \,\bigr]$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Name | Used For |
|---|----------|------|----------|
| 1 | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | Return Definition | Defining cumulative reward |
| 2 | $V_{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ | State-Value Definition | Evaluating a policy |
| 3 | $Q_{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ | Action-Value Definition | Evaluating action quality |
| 4 | $V_{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a \mid s)\sum_{s',r}p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma V_{\pi}(s')]$ | Bellman Equation for $V_{\pi}$ | Recursive evaluation |
| 5 | $Q_{\pi}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r}p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma\sum_{a'}\pi(a' \mid s')Q_{\pi}(s',a')]$ | Bellman Equation for $Q_{\pi}$ | Recursive Q-evaluation |
| 6 | $V_{*}(s) = \max_{a}\sum_{s',r}p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma V_{*}(s')]$ | Bellman Optimality for $V$ | Finding optimal policy |
| 7 | $Q_{*}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r}p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma\max_{a'} Q_{*}(s',a')]$ | Bellman Optimality for $Q$ | Q-Learning target |
| 8 | $V_{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a \mid s) Q_{\pi}(s,a)$ | $V$ from $Q$ | Policy evaluation link |
| 9 | $Q_{\pi}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r}p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma V_{\pi}(s')]$ | $Q$ from $V$ | Action selection link |
| 10 | $\gamma \in [0,1]$ | Discount factor constraint | Ensuring convergence |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Robotics path planning:** $V(s)$ = expected time-to-goal from configuration $s$.
- **Autonomous driving:** $V(s)$ = expected safety score from current traffic state $s$.
- **Network routing:** $V(s)$ = expected packet delivery latency from router state $s$.
- **Financial trading:** $V(s)$ = expected discounted portfolio value from market state $s$.
- **Recommendation systems:** $V(s)$ = expected long-term user engagement from session state $s$.
- **Smart grid energy management:** $V(s)$ = expected cost savings from current load state $s$.
