<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 2: ACTION-VALUE FUNCTION (Q-FUNCTION)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic is a foundational building block of Module 2 (Finite Markov Decision Processes / Bellman Equations) of the Elective course *PECMT745 – Reinforcement Learning*. It is a **direct high-weightage area** for Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions, as it bridges state-value estimation to action-value estimation — the core idea behind **Q-Learning** and **SARSA**.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (Sutton & Barto / KTU Terminology)

The **Action-Value Function** for policy $\pi$, denoted $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$, is defined as the **expected return** $G_t$ when the agent starts in state $s$, takes a specific action $a$, and thereafter follows policy $\pi$:

$$Q^{\pi}(s, a) \;\triangleq\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\,G_t \;\middle|\; S_t = s,\; A_t = a \,\right]$$

where the return $G_t$ is the cumulative discounted reward:

$$G_t \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} \, R_{t+k+1}, \quad 0 \le \gamma \le 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Q"?** The letter **Q** stands for **"Quality"** — i.e., *how good is it to take action $a$ in state $s$*? The function is therefore a measure of the **quality** of an action.

### 1.2 Relationship with the State-Value Function

The state-value function $V^{\pi}(s)$ and the action-value function $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ are mathematically linked. The state value is the expected action value when actions are sampled from the policy $\pi$:

$$V^{\pi}(s) \;=\; \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s)\, Q^{\pi}(s, a)$$

Conversely, the action value can be decomposed using the environment dynamics $p(s', r \mid s, a)$:

$$Q^{\pi}(s,a) \;=\; \sum_{s', r}\, p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\Bigl[\,r \;+\; \gamma\, V^{\pi}(s')\,\Bigr]$$

### 1.3 The Optimal Action-Value Function

The **Optimal Action-Value Function** $Q^{*}(s,a)$ is defined as the maximum action-value over all possible policies:

$$Q^{*}(s, a) \;\triangleq\; \max_{\pi}\, Q^{\pi}(s, a)$$

It satisfies the **Bellman Optimality Equation**:

$$Q^{*}(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s', r}\, p(s', r \mid s, a)\,\Bigl[\,r \;+\; \gamma\, \max_{a'}\, Q^{*}(s', a')\,\Bigr]$$

Once $Q^{*}$ is known, the **optimal policy** is trivially obtained by greedy action selection:

$$\pi^{*}(a \mid s) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } a = \arg\max_{a'} Q^{*}(s, a') \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Guide" 🍽️

Imagine you are in a **new city** (state $s$) and you must choose a restaurant (action $a$) for dinner. You do not know how good each restaurant is beforehand.

- The **State-Value Function $V^{\pi}(s)$** answers: *"If I am in this city and follow my usual dining habits (policy $\pi$), how satisfying will my dinner be on average?"*
- The **Action-Value Function $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$** answers: *"What if I specifically pick the rooftop Italian place tonight — how satisfying will it be, given that I will revert to my usual habits afterward?"*

The **Optimal Q-function $Q^{*}(s,a)$** is the **Michelin Guide** — it tells you, for *every* restaurant in *every* city, the absolute best total dining experience you can expect if you pick that place and then continue optimally.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Memory Hook:** $V$ = "How good is this **state**?" | $Q$ = "How good is this **action in this state**?"

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Breaking Down the Q-Function

The $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ function can be understood as a **recursive expectation**. Here is its operational decomposition:

1. **Sample the immediate reward $r$** received for taking action $a$ in state $s$, and observe the next state $s' \sim p(\cdot \mid s,a)$.
2. **From the next state $s'$**, the agent now follows policy $\pi$ to pick the next action $a' \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s')$.
3. The expected future return from $s'$ onwards is exactly $V^{\pi}(s')$.
4. Combine steps 1–3 with discount $\gamma$ to obtain $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$.

This recursive structure is why we obtain the **Bellman Equation for $Q$**:

$$Q^{\pi}(s,a) \;=\; \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q^{\pi}(s',a') \,\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Variant:** Deriving the Bellman equation for $Q$ in terms of itself (not $V$) is a **14-mark favourite** in Part B. Memorize the structure above.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Q-Function

| Question | Why We Care | Engineering Implication |
|----------|-------------|------------------------|
| Why introduce $Q$ when $V$ already exists? | $V^{\pi}(s)$ requires the **one-step look-ahead model** $p(s',r \mid s,a)$ to be known to act greedily. | $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ lets the agent act greedily **without knowing the transition model** — pure model-free. |
| Why is $Q^{*}$ important? | $Q^{*}$ encodes the optimal policy in a single function lookup. | Foundation of Q-Learning, DQN, and virtually all modern deep RL. |
| How does $Q$ help exploration? | We can rank actions in any state by simply sorting $Q(s, \cdot)$. | Enables $\epsilon$-greedy and Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) strategies cleanly. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | Definition of action-value | 3-mark definitional Q |
| 2 | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ | $V$ is the $\pi$-weighted average of $Q$ | Conversion problems |
| 3 | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$ | $Q$ in terms of $V$ and dynamics | Derivation problems |
| 4 | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q^{\pi}(s',a') \,\right]$ | Bellman Expectation Eq. for $Q$ | 14-mark derivation |
| 5 | $Q^{*}(s,a) = \max_{\pi} Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ | Optimal action-value | Definition Q |
| 6 | $Q^{*}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s',a') \,\right]$ | Bellman **Optimality** Eq. for $Q$ | 14-mark derivation |
| 7 | $\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q^{*}(s,a)$ | Greedy policy extraction | Algorithm Q |
| 8 | $A^{\pi}(s,a) = Q^{\pi}(s,a) - V^{\pi}(s)$ | **Advantage function** (advanced) | Bonus / elective depth |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** In all formulas above, the term $\max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')$ uses $\max$ (an operator), **not the absolute-value pipe symbol**, so it does not break markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Q-Function

- **Robotics:** $Q(s,a)$ estimates the cumulative "task-success" reward if a specific motor torque is applied in the current joint configuration.
- **Autonomous Driving:** State = sensor fusion snapshot; action = {turn left, turn right, brake, accelerate}; $Q$ ranks them for safety-aware planning.
- **Recommender Systems (e.g., YouTube, Netflix):** $Q$(user-context, recommended-video) estimates the long-term watch-time and engagement.
- **Industrial Process Control:** $Q$(system-state, valve-setting) guides optimal set-point selection without needing a full process simulator.
- **Game AI (AlphaGo, AlphaZero):** Deep neural networks approximate $Q^{*}(s,a)$ for board positions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Definition to Bellman Equation for $Q^{\pi}$

**Goal:** Starting from the definition
$$Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t=s, A_t=a]$$
derive the recursive Bellman expectation equation
$$Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q^{\pi}(s',a') \,\right].$$

### Step 1 — Expand the Return
The return is $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$. By the linearity of expectation, condition on the next-step outcomes $(S_{t+1}=s', R_{t+1}=r)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{\pi}(s,a) 
&= \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \,\middle|\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a \,\right] \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{s', r}\, p(s', r \mid s, a)\, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \,\middle|\, S_t=s,\; A_t=a,\; S_{t+1}=s',\; R_{t+1}=r \,\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Separate the Immediate Reward from the Continuation Value
Because $R_{t+1}=r$ is already fixed by the conditioning, we can pull it out of the expectation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{s', r}\, p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_{t+1} \,\middle|\, S_{t+1}=s' \,\right] \,\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Recognize the Inner Expectation as $V^{\pi}(s')$
By the definition of the state-value function, the expected future return from $s'$ under policy $\pi$ is precisely $V^{\pi}(s')$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{s', r}\, p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma\, V^{\pi}(s') \,\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Expand $V^{\pi}(s')$ in Terms of $Q^{\pi}(s', a')$
Using the relation $V^{\pi}(s') = \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q^{\pi}(s', a')$ from Section 1.2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{\pi}(s,a) 
&= \sum_{s', r}\, p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma \sum_{a' \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q^{\pi}(s', a') \,\right] \\[4pt]
&\quad\blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: From $Q^{\pi}$ to the Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q^{*}$

**Goal:** Show that
$$Q^{*}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s',a') \,\right].$$

### Step 1 — Start from the Bellman Equation for $Q^{\pi}$
Take the result of Section 3.1, but now with an **optimal** policy $\pi^{*}$. By definition of the optimum, the policy must select the *best* $a'$ at $s'$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi^{*}(a' \mid s') &= \mathbf{1}\!\left[\, a' = \arg\max_{a} Q^{*}(s', a) \,\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Deterministic Greedy Policy
The expectation $\sum_{a'} \pi^{*}(a' \mid s') Q^{*}(s', a')$ collapses to a single **max** operation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{*}(s,a) 
&= \sum_{s', r}\, p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi^{*}(a' \mid s')\, Q^{*}(s', a') \,\right] \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{s', r}\, p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma\, \max_{a'}\, Q^{*}(s', a') \,\right] \\[4pt]
&\quad\blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is a "fixed point":** The right-hand side expresses $Q^{*}$ in terms of $Q^{*}$ evaluated at the *next* state. This non-linear system can be solved by **Value Iteration**, **Policy Iteration**, or approximated by **Q-Learning** when the model $p$ is unknown.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Properties of $Q^{\pi}$ and $Q^{*}$

| Property | Statement | Engineering Significance |
|----------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **Recursivity** | $Q^{\pi}$ satisfies a Bellman fixed-point equation | Enables dynamic programming |
| **Greedy extraction** | $\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max_a Q^{*}(s,a)$ | No need for $\pi$ representation at decision time |
| **Model-free** | Q-Learning estimates $Q^{*}$ using samples | Works in unknown environments |
| **Sample-based updates** | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\!\left[\,r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)\,\right]$ | Stochastic approximation |
| **Convergence** | $Q \to Q^{*}$ as all $(s,a)$ pairs are visited infinitely often (Robbins-Monro conditions) | Theoretical guarantee from stochastic approximation |
| **Off-policy capability** | Q-Learning learns $Q^{*}$ while following an exploratory behaviour policy | Re-use of historical data (replay buffers in DQN) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Information Flow of the Q-Function

The diagram below shows how a single transition $(s, a, r, s')$ is used to update $Q(s,a)$ and propagate value to neighbouring state-action pairs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State S_t equals s] --> B[Agent selects action a]
    B --> C[Environment returns reward r and next state s prime]
    C --> D[Compute TD target r plus gamma times max over a prime of Q of s prime and a prime]
    D --> E[Compute TD error delta equals target minus Q of s and a]
    E --> F[Update Q of s and a with learning rate alpha]
    F --> G[New action selection in s prime via epsilon greedy over Q of s prime and a prime]
    G --> H[Loop until convergence to Q star]
    A --> A
    G --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: V vs. Q — Conceptual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph StateLevel[State Level]
        Vpi[V pi of s]
    end
    subgraph ActionLevel[Action Level]
        Qpi1[Q pi of s and a1]
        Qpi2[Q pi of s and a2]
        Qpi3[Q pi of s and a3]
        QpiN[Q pi of s and aN]
    end
    Vpi -->|weighted by policy pi| Qpi1
    Vpi -->|weighted by policy pi| Qpi2
    Vpi -->|weighted by policy pi| Qpi3
    Vpi -->|weighted by policy pi| QpiN
    Qpi1 -->|max over a| Qstar[Q star of s and a]
    Qpi2 --> Qstar
    Qpi3 --> Qstar
    QpiN --> Qstar
    Qstar -->|arg max| PiStar[Optimal Policy pi star of a given s]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of Q-Learning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Agent[RL Agent]
        Pol[Policy Module e.g. epsilon greedy] -->|select a| Env
        QTab[Q Table or Q Network approximator] -->|Q values| Pol
        Upd[Q Update Rule] -->|new Q values| QTab
    end
    subgraph Env[Environment]
        EnvDyn[Transition Dynamics p of s prime r given s a] -->|reward r and next state s prime| Agent
    end
    Upd -->|reads s a r s prime| EnvDyn
    Pol -->|action a| EnvDyn
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** Examiners reward diagrams that show *direction of information flow*. Always use arrows that distinguish **observation** (agent ← env) from **action** (agent → env).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, 3 Marks]`
**Define the action-value function $Q^{\pi}(s, a)$ and explain its significance in reinforcement learning.**

**Model Answer:**

The action-value function $Q^{\pi}(s, a)$ is the expected cumulative discounted return $G_t$ when the agent starts in state $s$, takes action $a$, and then follows policy $\pi$ thereafter:

$$Q^{\pi}(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_t \,\middle|\, S_t = s,\; A_t = a \,\right]$$

**Significance:**
- It quantifies the **quality** of taking a specific action in a specific state, removing the need to reason about future actions.
- It enables **model-free** control: once $Q^{*}$ is learned, the optimal action is simply $\arg\max_{a} Q^{*}(s, a)$.
- It forms the theoretical foundation of model-free algorithms such as **Q-Learning** and **SARSA**.

(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for significance)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, 3 Marks]`
**Distinguish between the state-value function $V^{\pi}(s)$ and the action-value function $Q^{\pi}(s, a)$.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | $V^{\pi}(s)$ | $Q^{\pi}(s, a)$ |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|
| Definition | Expected return starting from $s$ and following $\pi$ | Expected return starting from $s$, taking $a$, then following $\pi$ |
| Arguments | Only the state $s$ | Both state $s$ and action $a$ |
| Action choice | Implicit (averaged over $\pi$) | Explicit (conditioned on $a$) |
| Use | Policy evaluation | Action selection / model-free control |
| Relation | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$ |

(3 marks — 1 mark definition each + 1 mark relation)

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### ✦ Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** State and derive the Bellman optimality equation for the optimal action-value function $Q^{*}(s, a)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the deterministic grid-world shown below, where the agent starts at $S_1$ and must reach the goal $S_5$ (reward +10), with all other transitions yielding a reward of $-1$ per step, and $\gamma = 0.9$. The action set is $\{ \text{up, down, left, right} \}$. Assuming the optimal action-value function is initialized to zero, compute $Q^{*}(S_1, \text{right})$ and $Q^{*}(S_1, \text{down})$ using **one** application of the Bellman optimality update. **(7 marks)**

```
+--------+--------+
|  S1    |  S2    |
|  (start)        |
+--------+--------+
|  S3    |  S4    |
|        |        |
+--------+--------+
|  S5    |  S6    |
| (goal, +10)     |
+--------+--------+
```

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Definition.**
$$Q^{*}(s, a) \;\triangleq\; \max_{\pi}\, Q^{\pi}(s, a)$$

**Step 2: Use the Bellman equation for $Q^{\pi}$ with an optimal policy $\pi^{*}$.**
$$Q^{*}(s, a) = \sum_{s', r}\, p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma \sum_{a'}\, \pi^{*}(a' \mid s')\, Q^{*}(s', a') \,\right]$$

**Step 3: Since $\pi^{*}$ is deterministic-greedy w.r.t. $Q^{*}$, the inner sum collapses to a max.**
$$Q^{*}(s, a) = \sum_{s', r}\, p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma \max_{a'}\, Q^{*}(s', a') \,\right]$$

This is the **Bellman Optimality Equation** for $Q^{*}$. *(1 mark for definition, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for stating final form and noting it is a fixed-point equation.)*

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

We use the one-step Bellman optimality update assuming $Q^{*}(\cdot, \cdot) = 0$ for all non-current pairs:

**Right action from $S_1$ → reaches $S_2$:**
$$Q^{*}(S_1, \text{right}) = r(S_1, \text{right}, S_2) + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(S_2, a') = -1 + 0.9 \times 0 = -1$$

**Down action from $S_1$ → reaches $S_3$:**
$$Q^{*}(S_1, \text{down}) = r(S_1, \text{down}, S_3) + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(S_3, a') = -1 + 0.9 \times 0 = -1$$

**Conclusion:** After one update, both actions have $Q = -1$ because the goal $S_5$ is more than one step away. Further iterations (or **value iteration** until convergence) would increase $Q^{*}(S_1, \text{down})$ since the path $S_1 \to S_3 \to \dots \to S_5$ leads to the goal.

*(2 marks for correct setup, 3 marks for correct computation, 2 marks for explanation.)*

---

### ✦ Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Derive the Bellman expectation equation for the action-value function $Q^{\pi}(s, a)$ starting from first principles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Q-Learning** update rule:
$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \!\left[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t) \,\right]$$
and discuss why it is an **off-policy** algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

Already shown in full detail in **Section 3.1** above. Key milestones for marks:
- 1 mark: Write the definition of $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$.
- 2 marks: Expand $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ and condition on $(S_{t+1}, R_{t+1})$.
- 2 marks: Identify the inner expectation as $V^{\pi}(s')$ and substitute.
- 2 marks: Expand $V^{\pi}(s')$ in terms of $Q^{\pi}$ to obtain the final recursive form.

### Part (b) — Q-Learning Explanation (7 marks)

**Step 1: TD Target.**
The bracketed term $R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a)$ is the **TD target** — an estimate of the true return $G_t$ using the current $Q$-table and the observed transition.

**Step 2: TD Error.**
$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t)$ is the **temporal-difference (TD) error**. The update moves $Q(S_t, A_t)$ toward the TD target by a fraction $\alpha$ (the **learning rate**).

**Step 3: Why Off-Policy.**
- The **behaviour policy** (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy) is what the agent uses to *explore* and generate experience.
- The **target policy** is the *greedy* policy implicit in the $\max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a)$ term.
- Because the update uses the maximum over actions (greedy target) but the agent may be acting $\epsilon$-greedily, Q-Learning is learning about the optimal policy *while* following a different (exploratory) policy — hence **off-policy**.

**Step 4: Convergence.** Under standard stochastic approximation conditions (Robbins-Monro: $\sum \alpha_t = \infty,\; \sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$) and all $(s,a)$ pairs visited infinitely often, $Q \to Q^{*}$.

*(2 marks TD target/error, 2 marks for off-policy argument, 1 mark for $\epsilon$-greedy distinction, 2 marks for convergence statement.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Q-Function Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the discount factor $\gamma$** in the Bellman equation — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the summation over $s'$ with the summation over $a'$**. Remember: $p(s',r \mid s,a)$ is the *environment* dynamics, while $\pi(a' \mid s')$ is the *policy*. Mixing these up is the #1 mistake.
> 3. **Treating $\max$ as linear**: Examiners love to test whether students understand that the **Bellman Optimality** equation has a `max` operator (non-linear), unlike the Bellman *Expectation* equation which is linear.
> 4. **Off-policy confusion**: Students often claim Q-Learning is on-policy because the agent "sees" its own actions. The correct reason it is off-policy is the *greedy* $\max$ inside the target.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency**: In numerical questions, ensure $\gamma$ is dimensionless (a scalar) and rewards $r$ are summed directly without dimension conversion.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **action-value function** $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ = expected return from $(s,a)$ under $\pi$.
- ✅ $Q$ is to **actions** what $V$ is to **states**.
- ✅ $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s)\, Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ — averaging over policy.
- ✅ $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[\, r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') \,\right]$ — using dynamics.
- ✅ The **Bellman Expectation Equation** for $Q$ is linear in $Q$.
- ✅ The **Bellman Optimality Equation** for $Q^{*}$ is non-linear due to the $\max$ operator.
- ✅ $Q^{*}$ encodes the **optimal policy** via $\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max_a Q^{*}(s,a)$.
- ✅ **Q-Learning** is the sample-based, off-policy algorithm for estimating $Q^{*}$.
- ✅ The **TD target** for Q-Learning is $R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')$.
- ✅ The **TD error** drives the update; learning rate $\alpha$ controls step size.
- ✅ Converges to $Q^{*}$ under Robbins-Monro conditions and sufficient exploration.
- ✅ Practical uses: robotics, recommender systems, autonomous driving, game AI, process control.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** Always carry the *two coupled equations* ($V$-from-$Q$ and $Q$-from-$V$) into the exam hall in your memory. They are the *Rosetta Stone* of all RL theory questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Action-Advantage Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Action-Advantage Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Action-Advantage Function**, commonly denoted as $A(s, a)$, is a fundamental quantity in Reinforcement Learning (RL) that quantifies the relative desirability of taking a specific action $a$ in a given state $s$, compared to the average (expected) performance achievable from that state when following a given policy $\pi$. 

Formally, the advantage function is defined as the difference between the **Action-Value Function** $Q_\pi(s, a)$ and the **State-Value Function** $V_\pi(s)$:

$$A_\pi(s, a) = Q_\pi(s, a) - V_\pi(s)$$

This function was prominently introduced in the **Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C)** and **Asynchronous Advantage Actor-Critic (A3C)** architectures, and it forms the mathematical backbone of modern policy gradient algorithms like **PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization)** and **TRPO (Trust Region Policy Optimization)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The advantage function is a *core derivation* expected in Module 2 of PECMT745. Students must be able to derive it from the Bellman equations and apply it in policy gradient theorems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a student preparing for the KTU semester exam:

- **State ($s$):** You have studied 5 chapters out of 10 the night before the exam.
- **Average performance ($V(s)$):** The expected score if you follow a "typical" strategy — for example, attempting all questions with balanced effort.
- **Action ($a$):** Now, consider a *specific* action — say, focusing extra time on the high-weightage module questions first.
- **$Q(s, a)$:** The expected score if you specifically follow this focused strategy.
- **$A(s, a)$:** The *extra marks* you would gain (or lose) by adopting this focused strategy instead of the average one.

If $A(s, a) > 0$, the action is **better than average** — a "good" move.
If $A(s, a) < 0$, the action is **worse than average** — a "bad" move.
If $A(s, a) = 0$, the action is exactly as good as the average policy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** The advantage function strips away the "baseline" value of being in a state, leaving only the *incremental benefit* of choosing a particular action. This makes it a powerful, low-variance signal for learning.

## 1.3 Why is it Called "Advantage"?

The term "advantage" is borrowed from game theory and betting terminology. It represents the **relative edge** an action provides. In RL, this edge is measured against the *expected return* under the current policy, making it a **centered, baseline-subtracted estimator** — a key property that reduces variance in policy gradient updates.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| $A(s, a)$ | Advantage value | $\mathbb{R}$ (any real number) |
| $\gamma$ | Discount factor | $[0, 1)$ |
| $\lambda$ | GAE parameter | $[0, 1]$ |
| $\delta_t$ | TD-error | $\mathbb{R}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation: From Bellman to Advantage

The advantage function is not defined in isolation. It is a direct consequence of the **Bellman Optimality Equations** studied earlier in the KTU syllabus. Let us trace the logical chain:

### Step 1: State-Value Function (Recap)

$$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, S_t = s \right]$$

This is the expected return starting from state $s$ and following policy $\pi$ thereafter.

### Step 2: Action-Value Function (Recap)

$$Q_\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$

This is the expected return when taking action $a$ in state $s$ and following $\pi$ thereafter.

### Step 3: The Connection

If we take the expectation of $Q_\pi(s, a)$ over actions sampled from the policy $\pi$ in state $s$, we recover $V_\pi(s)$:

$$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \, Q_\pi(s, a)$$

This identity is the **theoretical bridge** that allows us to define the advantage as a deviation from a meaningful baseline.

### Step 4: Definition of the Advantage Function

$$\boxed{A_\pi(s, a) = Q_\pi(s, a) - V_\pi(s)}$$

The interpretation: $A_\pi(s, a)$ is the *extra reward* an agent can expect by selecting action $a$ instead of following the policy's default action distribution in state $s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Property:** The expectation of the advantage function over actions is always **zero**:
> $$\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi} \left[ A_\pi(s, a) \right] = 0$$
> This makes the advantage an *unbiased baseline* — its expected contribution to any policy update vanishes, which is the mathematical reason for its variance-reduction property.

## 2.2 Why Use the Advantage Function? (The "Why")

### 2.2.1 Variance Reduction in Policy Gradients

The classic REINFORCE policy gradient is:

$$\nabla J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \, G_t \right]$$

where $G_t$ is the full return. The problem: $G_t$ has **high variance** because it includes stochastic future rewards.

By subtracting a baseline $b(s)$ (which is $V_\pi(s)$ in our case):

$$\nabla J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \, (G_t - b(s)) \right]$$

The variance is reduced **without introducing bias**, because $\mathbb{E}[\nabla \log \pi \cdot b(s)] = 0$ when $b$ does not depend on $a$.

### 2.2.2 Intuitive Decision-Making

The advantage tells the agent **how much better or worse** an action is compared to the average, enabling more nuanced policy updates than raw returns.

### 2.2.3 Compatibility with Actor-Critic Methods

In the **Actor-Critic** framework:
- The **Critic** estimates $V_\pi(s)$ (the baseline).
- The **Advantage** is computed as $A = R + \gamma V(s') - V(s)$, an estimate of the TD-error $\delta_t$.
- The **Actor** updates the policy using this advantage signal.

## 2.3 Practical Computation: Temporal-Difference (TD) Approximation

In practice, we rarely have access to the true $Q_\pi$ and $V_\pi$. We use a one-step TD estimate:

$$\hat{A}_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$$

This is called the **1-step TD advantage estimate** or **TD-error**. The actor-critic algorithm uses exactly this.

## 2.4 Generalized Advantage Estimation (GAE)

Introduced by Schulman et al. (2015), GAE is the **state-of-the-art** method for computing advantages in KTU-referenced algorithms. It blends multiple TD steps using a parameter $\lambda \in [0, 1]$:

$$A_t^{GAE(\gamma, \lambda)} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (\gamma \lambda)^k \, \delta_{t+k}$$

where the TD-error at each step is:

$$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$$

- When $\lambda = 0$: only 1-step TD error is used (low variance, high bias).
- When $\lambda = 1$: full Monte Carlo return is used (high variance, low bias).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Used In |
|---|---------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | $A_\pi(s, a) = Q_\pi(s, a) - V_\pi(s)$ | Basic definition | All advantage methods |
| 2 | $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) Q_\pi(s, a)$ | State-value from action-values | Baseline derivation |
| 3 | $\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi}[A_\pi(s,a)] = 0$ | Zero-mean property | Variance reduction |
| 4 | $\hat{A}_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$ | 1-step TD advantage | Actor-Critic, A2C |
| 5 | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$ | TD-error | GAE |
| 6 | $A_t^{GAE} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (\gamma\lambda)^k \delta_{t+k}$ | GAE formula | PPO, A3C |
| 7 | $\nabla J(\theta) \approx \mathbb{E}[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \, A_\pi(s, a)]$ | Policy gradient with advantage | A2C, PPO |
| 8 | $A^*_\pi(s, a) = Q^*_\pi(s, a) - \max_{a'} Q^*_\pi(s, a')$ | Optimal advantage | Optimal policy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The advantage function is used in:
> - **Robotics:** Teaching robot arms to grasp objects by comparing successful vs. average grasps.
> - **Autonomous Driving:** Evaluating whether a lane-change maneuver outperforms cruise behavior.
> - **Game AI (e.g., AlphaGo, AlphaStar):** Reducing the search space by ranking moves.
> - **Recommendation Systems:** Quantifying whether showing a particular ad outperforms the average CTR.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Advantage Function from Bellman Equations

We begin from the Bellman equation for $Q_\pi(s, a)$:

$$Q_\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V_\pi(S_{t+1}) \,\Big|\, S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$

### Step 1: Expand the Expectation

$$Q_\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \vert s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$$

**Explanation:** The dynamics function $p(s', r \vert s, a)$ gives the probability of transitioning to state $s'$ and receiving reward $r$ when taking action $a$ in state $s$.

### Step 2: Split the Sum

$$Q_\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \vert s, a) \, r + \gamma \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \vert s, a) \, V_\pi(s')$$

**Explanation:** We use linearity of expectation to separate the immediate reward from the discounted future value.

### Step 3: Substitute the Definition of $V_\pi(s)$

Recall that the state-value is:

$$V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) Q_\pi(s, a) = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \vert s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$$

### Step 4: Form the Advantage

Subtracting $V_\pi(s)$ from $Q_\pi(s, a)$:

$$A_\pi(s, a) = Q_\pi(s, a) - V_\pi(s)$$

### Step 5: Verify the Zero-Mean Property

Take the expectation of $A_\pi(s, a)$ over actions $a \sim \pi(\cdot \vert s)$:

$$\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi}\left[A_\pi(s, a)\right] = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) \left[ Q_\pi(s, a) - V_\pi(s) \right]$$

$$\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi}\left[A_\pi(s, a)\right] = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) Q_\pi(s, a) - V_\pi(s) \sum_a \pi(a \vert s)$$

$$\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi}\left[A_\pi(s, a)\right] = V_\pi(s) - V_\pi(s) \cdot 1 = 0$$

**Conclusion:** The expected advantage over the policy's own action distribution is always zero. This is the key reason the advantage function works as a variance-reducing baseline.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: TD-Error as an Unbiased Estimator of the Advantage

We want to show that the one-step TD-error $\delta_t$ is an **unbiased estimator** of the advantage function.

### Step 1: Define the TD-Error

$$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V_\pi(S_{t+1}) - V_\pi(S_t)$$

### Step 2: Compute Its Expectation

Take the expectation conditional on $S_t = s, A_t = a$:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[ \delta_t \,\Big|\, S_t = s, A_t = a \right] = \mathbb{E}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V_\pi(S_{t+1}) \,\Big|\, S_t = s, A_t = a \right] - V_\pi(s)$$

### Step 3: Recognize the First Term as $Q_\pi(s, a)$

By definition:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V_\pi(S_{t+1}) \,\Big|\, S_t = s, A_t = a \right] = Q_\pi(s, a)$$

### Step 4: Conclude

$$\mathbb{E}\left[ \delta_t \,\Big|\, S_t = s, A_t = a \right] = Q_\pi(s, a) - V_\pi(s) = A_\pi(s, a)$$

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}\left[ \delta_t \,\Big|\, S_t = s, A_t = a \right] = A_\pi(s, a)}$$

**Conclusion:** The TD-error is an **unbiased, one-step sample estimate** of the advantage function. This is the cornerstone of all practical Actor-Critic algorithms.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Policy Gradient with Advantage Function

### Step 1: Recall the Policy Gradient Theorem

The objective is to maximize expected return $J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}[G_0]$.

The gradient is:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi, a \sim \pi_\theta} \left[ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \, Q_{\pi_\theta}(s, a) \right]$$

### Step 2: Introduce the Baseline

Subtract $V_{\pi_\theta}(s)$ (which doesn't depend on $a$):

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi, a \sim \pi_\theta} \left[ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \left( Q_{\pi_\theta}(s, a) - V_{\pi_\theta}(s) \right) \right]$$

**Why this is valid:**

$$\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi_\theta} \left[ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \, V_{\pi_\theta}(s) \right] = V_{\pi_\theta}(s) \nabla_\theta \sum_a \pi_\theta(a \vert s) = V_{\pi_\theta}(s) \nabla_\theta (1) = 0$$

### Step 3: Final Form (Advantage Actor-Critic)

$$\boxed{\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{s, a} \left[ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \, A_{\pi_\theta}(s, a) \right]}$$

This is the **Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C)** policy gradient.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | REINFORCE ($G_t$) | Actor-Critic ($A_t$) |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Estimator Used | Monte Carlo return | TD-error / GAE |
| Bias | Unbiased | Slight bias (with function approximation) |
| Variance | **High** | **Low** |
| Online Learning | No (needs full episode) | Yes (works step-by-step) |
| Convergence Speed | Slow | Fast |
| Algorithm Examples | Vanilla PG, REINFORCE | A2C, A3C, PPO |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Advantage Function

The following block diagram shows how the advantage function is computed and used inside an Actor-Critic architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Environment State St] --> B[State Encoder]
    B --> C[Critic Network Vphi]
    B --> D[Actor Network Pitheta]
    C --> E[Value Estimate V St]
    C --> F[Next State Value V St+1]
    G[Reward Rt+1 from Environment] --> H[Advantage Calculator]
    F --> H
    E --> I[Advantage At]
    H --> I
    D --> J[Policy Action At]
    J --> K[Log Prob log pi At St]
    I --> L[Policy Gradient Loss]
    K --> L
    E --> M[Value Loss]
    L --> N[Total Loss Function]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Backpropagation]
    O --> C
    O --> D
```

## 4.2 Relationship Between V, Q, and A

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State s] --> B[V pi s baseline]
    A --> C[Action a]
    C --> D[Q pi s a action value]
    D --> E[A pi s a advantage]
    B --> E
    E --> F{Decision}
    F -- A greater than 0 --> G[Encourage Action a]
    F -- A less than 0 --> H[Discourage Action a]
    F -- A equals 0 --> I[Neutral no update bias]
```

## 4.3 Workflow of Generalized Advantage Estimation (GAE)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trajectory s0 a0 r1 s1 a1 r2 s2] --> B[Compute TD errors delta t]
    B --> C[delta 0 equals r1 plus gamma V s1 minus V s0]
    B --> D[delta 1 equals r2 plus gamma V s2 minus V s1]
    B --> E[delta 2 equals r3 plus gamma V s3 minus V s2]
    C --> F[Weighted Sum with lambda]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[A t GAE equals sum of gamma lambda k delta t plus k]
    G --> H[Use A t GAE in PPO or A2C update]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Block: REINFORCE vs. Advantage Actor-Critic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Policy Gradient Methods] --> B[REINFORCE]
    A --> C[Actor Critic]
    B --> D[Uses full return Gt]
    C --> E[Uses advantage At equals Q minus V]
    D --> F[High Variance Slow Convergence]
    E --> G[Low Variance Fast Convergence]
    E --> H[A2C and A3C]
    E --> I[PPO and TRPO]
    H --> J[On Policy]
    I --> J
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]`
**Q: Define the Action-Advantage Function $A_\pi(s, a)$ in Reinforcement Learning. Why is it preferred over the raw action-value $Q_\pi(s, a)$ in policy gradient methods?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Action-Advantage Function is defined as:

$$A_\pi(s, a) = Q_\pi(s, a) - V_\pi(s)$$

where $Q_\pi(s, a)$ is the expected return when taking action $a$ in state $s$ and following policy $\pi$ thereafter, and $V_\pi(s)$ is the expected return from state $s$ under policy $\pi$.

It is preferred over raw $Q_\pi(s, a)$ because:
1. **Variance Reduction:** The state-value $V_\pi(s)$ acts as a baseline. Since $\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi}[A_\pi(s, a)] = 0$, subtracting the baseline reduces variance without introducing bias.
2. **Relative Measure:** It measures *how much better* an action is than the average, providing a centered, interpretable learning signal.
3. **Compatibility with Actor-Critic:** The TD-error $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$ is an unbiased estimator of $A_\pi(s, a)$, enabling online learning.

**(1 Mark for definition, 2 Marks for advantages.)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]`
**Q: Show that the expected value of the advantage function $A_\pi(s, a)$ over actions sampled from policy $\pi$ is zero.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Given $A_\pi(s, a) = Q_\pi(s, a) - V_\pi(s)$, we take the expectation over $a \sim \pi(\cdot \vert s)$:

$$\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi}\left[A_\pi(s, a)\right] = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) \left[ Q_\pi(s, a) - V_\pi(s) \right]$$

$$= \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) Q_\pi(s, a) - V_\pi(s) \sum_a \pi(a \vert s)$$

By the definition of $V_\pi(s)$: $\sum_a \pi(a \vert s) Q_\pi(s, a) = V_\pi(s)$, and $\sum_a \pi(a \vert s) = 1$:

$$= V_\pi(s) - V_\pi(s) \cdot 1 = 0$$

**Hence, $\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi}[A_\pi(s, a)] = 0$. QED.**

**(1 Mark for setup, 1 Mark for substitution, 1 Mark for conclusion.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the **Temporal-Difference (TD) advantage estimate** used in Actor-Critic methods. Show that the TD-error $\delta_t$ is an unbiased estimator of the true advantage function $A_\pi(s, a)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Generalized Advantage Estimation (GAE)** method with the bias-variance tradeoff. Show the GAE formula and discuss the effect of the parameter $\lambda$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Define the TD-Error (2 Marks)**

The one-step TD-error at time $t$ is defined as:

$$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V_\pi(S_{t+1}) - V_\pi(S_t)$$

**Step 2: Compute the Conditional Expectation (2 Marks)**

Taking expectation conditional on $S_t = s$ and $A_t = a$:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\delta_t \,\Big|\, S_t = s, A_t = a\right] = \mathbb{E}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma V_\pi(S_{t+1}) \,\Big|\, S_t = s, A_t = a\right] - V_\pi(s)$$

**Step 3: Recognize the First Term (2 Marks)**

By the definition of the action-value function:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma V_\pi(S_{t+1}) \,\Big|\, S_t = s, A_t = a\right] = Q_\pi(s, a)$$

**Step 4: Conclude (1 Mark)**

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\delta_t \,\Big|\, S_t = s, A_t = a\right] = Q_\pi(s, a) - V_\pi(s) = A_\pi(s, a)$$

Hence, the TD-error is an **unbiased estimator** of the advantage function. QED.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Definition of GAE (3 Marks)**

Generalized Advantage Estimation, proposed by Schulman et al. (2015), combines multiple n-step TD-advantages using an exponentially weighted average controlled by parameter $\lambda \in [0, 1]$:

$$A_t^{GAE(\gamma, \lambda)} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (\gamma \lambda)^k \, \delta_{t+k}$$

where the TD-errors are:

$$\delta_{t+k} = R_{t+k+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+k+1}) - V(S_{t+k})$$

**Step 2: Bias-Variance Tradeoff (2 Marks)**

| $\lambda$ Value | Behavior |
|-----------------|----------|
| $\lambda = 0$ | Uses only 1-step TD-error. **Low variance, high bias** (relies on inaccurate value function). |
| $\lambda = 1$ | Reduces to full Monte Carlo return. **High variance, low bias** (no bootstrapping). |
| $\lambda \in (0,1)$ | Blends both extremes — empirically $\lambda \in [0.9, 0.99]$ is standard. |

**Step 3: Practical Significance (2 Marks)**

GAE is the **de facto standard** in modern policy gradient algorithms (PPO, A2C) because:
- It allows smooth control over the bias-variance tradeoff via a single hyperparameter.
- It can be computed recursively in $O(n)$ time per trajectory.
- It provides a stable, low-variance gradient direction for the actor.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper — Alternative Choice]`

**Q: (a)** With suitable derivations, prove that introducing the advantage function $A_\pi(s, a)$ in the policy gradient theorem does not introduce bias but reduces variance compared to using the raw action-value $Q_\pi(s, a)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **REINFORCE algorithm** and the **Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C)** algorithm in terms of their gradient estimators, variance, bias, and convergence properties. Provide the gradient update equations for both. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: The Policy Gradient Theorem (2 Marks)**

From the KTU syllabus, the policy gradient is:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi, \, a \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \, Q_{\pi_\theta}(s, a)\right]$$

**Step 2: Subtract the Baseline $V_\pi(s)$ (2 Marks)**

$$= \mathbb{E}_{s, a}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \left(Q_{\pi_\theta}(s, a) - V_{\pi_\theta}(s)\right)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{s, a}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \, V_{\pi_\theta}(s)\right]$$

**Step 3: Show the Second Term Vanishes (2 Marks)**

$$\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \, V_{\pi_\theta}(s)\right] = V_{\pi_\theta}(s) \sum_a \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s)$$

$$= V_{\pi_\theta}(s) \nabla_\theta \sum_a \pi_\theta(a \vert s) = V_{\pi_\theta}(s) \nabla_\theta(1) = 0$$

**Step 4: Final Policy Gradient (1 Mark)**

$$\boxed{\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{s, a}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \, A_{\pi_\theta}(s, a)\right]}$$

This is **unbiased** because the subtracted baseline $V_\pi(s)$ is independent of $a$ and has zero expected contribution. The **variance** is reduced because the advantage is *centered* around zero, leading to lower-magnitude updates.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: REINFORCE Gradient (2 Marks)**

REINFORCE uses the full Monte Carlo return $G_t$:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \, G_t\right]$$

Update rule: $\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \, G_t \, \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s)$

**Step 2: A2C Gradient (2 Marks)**

A2C uses the TD-error as an advantage estimate $\hat{A}_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \, \hat{A}_t\right]$$

Update rule: $\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \, \hat{A}_t \, \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s)$

**Step 3: Comparison Table (3 Marks)**

| Property | REINFORCE | A2C |
|----------|-----------|-----|
| Estimator | $G_t$ (full return) | $\hat{A}_t$ (TD-error) |
| Bias | Unbiased | Slight bias (bootstrapping) |
| Variance | **High** | **Low** |
| Update Frequency | End of episode | Every step |
| Needs Model of Environment | No | No |
| Requires Critic | No | Yes (learns $V(s)$) |
| Sample Efficiency | Poor | Good |
| Convergence Speed | Slow | Fast |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Advantage-Function Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the zero-mean property:** Many students fail to verify $\mathbb{E}[A_\pi(s, a)] = 0$, which is the **core reason** the advantage reduces variance. **Loss: 2 Marks.**
> 2. **Confusing $A(s, a)$ with $Q(s, a)$:** The advantage is a *difference*, not an absolute value. Writing only $Q(s, a)$ as the answer is **incorrect**.
> 3. **Wrong TD-error formula:** The 1-step TD-error is $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$, **not** $R_t + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$. Off-by-one reward index is a frequent error.
> 4. **Mixing up GAE parameters:** $\gamma$ is the **discount factor**, while $\lambda$ is the **GAE trace-decay parameter**. Do not interchange them.
> 5. **Missing the unbiased-estimator proof:** When asked to "show $\delta_t$ is unbiased," students often only write the formula without computing the conditional expectation. **Loss: 3-4 Marks.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the Action-Advantage Function:**

- **Definition:** $A_\pi(s, a) = Q_\pi(s, a) - V_\pi(s)$ — measure of *relative* action quality.
- **Zero-Mean Property:** $\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi}[A_\pi(s, a)] = 0$ — this is the foundation of variance reduction.
- **Unbiased TD Estimator:** $\mathbb{E}[\delta_t \vert s, a] = A_\pi(s, a)$ where $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$.
- **Policy Gradient Form:** $\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \, A_\pi(s, a)]$.
- **GAE Formula:** $A_t^{GAE} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (\gamma \lambda)^k \, \delta_{t+k}$ — adjustable bias-variance tradeoff.
- **$\lambda = 0$:** Low variance, high bias (1-step TD).
- **$\lambda = 1$:** High variance, low bias (Monte Carlo).
- **Baseline Subtraction:** $V_\pi(s)$ is independent of $a$, so it does not bias the gradient.
- **Actor-Critic Mapping:** Critic estimates $V(s)$; Actor uses the advantage for policy updates.
- **Algorithms Using Advantage:** A2C, A3C, PPO, TRPO, GAE.
- **KTU 2024 Weightage:** The advantage function is a **high-yield** Module 2 topic — expect 7-Mark derivation sub-parts in ESE.
- **Negative Advantage:** Means the action is **worse** than the policy's average — penalize it.
- **Positive Advantage:** Means the action is **better** than the policy's average — reinforce it.
- **State-Value Bridge:** $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) Q_\pi(s, a)$ is the key identity.
- **Sample Efficiency:** Advantage-based methods are **far more sample-efficient** than vanilla REINFORCE.
- **Convergence:** Advantage Actor-Critic converges to a **local optimum** of the policy objective under standard assumptions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimality. Exploitation and Exploration of Reinforcement Learning: Bandits - Single State Decision Problem (Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) Problem)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Optimality. Exploitation and Exploration of Reinforcement Learning: Bandits - Single State Decision Problem (Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) Problem)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) Problem — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) Problem (Sutton & Barto Framework):** A *k*-armed bandit problem is a simplified, single-state reinforcement learning task in which, at each of *T* discrete time steps *t* ∈ {1, 2, ..., T}, an agent (the *decision-maker* or *learner*) must select exactly one action *A_t* from a fixed set of *k* available actions {a₁, a₂, ..., a_k}. Each action *a* delivers a reward drawn from an unknown stationary probability distribution *R(a)* with an unknown expected (true) value *q*(a) = 𝔼[R_t | A_t = a]. The objective of the agent is to maximize the **expected cumulative reward** over the time horizon, which is equivalent to maximizing the *expected average reward per time step* and, by extension, minimizing the **total regret** incurred relative to the optimal action a*.

$$
q_*(a) \;\triangleq\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[R_t \,\big|\, A_t = a\right], \quad a \in \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_k\}
$$

$$
a_* \;=\; \underset{a}{\arg\max}\; q_*(a)
$$

The agent does **not** know the true value *q*\*(*a*) of any action in advance. It must learn a *policy* π that maps situations to actions by *interacting with the environment* and *observing rewards* — a hallmark characteristic of the **exploration–exploitation dilemma**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Casino Slot Machine

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The "One-Armed Bandit"):** A traditional slot machine in a casino is called a *one-armed bandit* because it has a single lever (arm) and reliably drains the gambler's money. Now, imagine a gambler standing in front of a row of *k* such slot machines, each with its **own hidden payout probability distribution** that never changes during the game. The gambler can pull the lever of *exactly one* machine per round. After each pull, the machine emits a numerical reward (a coin payout). The gambler's strategic dilemma becomes:
> - **Exploitation** → "I have a hunch that machine #3 pays well. Should I keep pulling it to lock in my winnings?"
> - **Exploration** → "What if machine #7 pays even better? I should try it at least a few times to be sure."
>
> Pulling the lever of the *best machine* repeatedly = **exploitation**. Trying unfamiliar machines = **exploration**. The optimal strategy balances both.

## 1.3 Exploration vs. Exploitation — Core Trade-Off

| Concept | Definition | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Exploitation** | Use current knowledge to select the action believed to yield the highest estimated reward (greedy action). | Misses better actions whose value has not yet been discovered → *insufficient learning*. |
| **Exploration** | Select a non-greedy action to gather more information about its true reward distribution. | Sacrifices short-term reward in the hope of making better long-term decisions → *wasted pulls*. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The fundamental challenge in reinforcement learning is the *trade-off between exploration and exploitation*. To obtain a large reward, a learning agent must prefer actions that it has tried in the past and found to be effective in producing reward (exploit), but to discover such actions it has to try actions it has not selected before (explore). Neither purely greedy nor purely random strategies are optimal in general.

## 1.4 K-Armed Bandit as a Special Case of MDP

The MAB problem is mathematically a **Markov Decision Process (MDP) with a single non-terminal state**, no transitions between states, and stationary reward distributions. This simplification isolates the exploration–exploitation dilemma and is the canonical entry point for analyzing sequential decision-making under uncertainty.

$$
\mathcal{M} = (\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R), \quad \text{where } \vert \mathcal{S} \vert = 1
$$

This reduction is the reason MABs serve as the *conceptual building block* for the full RL theory developed in Modules 3 and 4 of the PECMT745 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Estimating Action Values

Since the true action value *q*\*(*a*) is unknown, the agent must **estimate** it. A natural estimator is the *sample-average* (Monte Carlo) method:

$$
Q_t(a) \;\triangleq\; \frac{\text{sum of rewards when } a \text{ taken prior to } t}{\text{number of times } a \text{ taken prior to } t}
 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} R_i \cdot \mathbf{1}_{A_i = a}}{\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} \mathbf{1}_{A_i = a}}
$$

By the **Law of Large Numbers**, as N(a) → ∞, the estimate Q_t(a) converges to the true value q\*(a).

## 2.2 Action Selection Policies

### 2.2.1 The Greedy Action Selection Rule

$$
A_t \;=\; \underset{a}{\arg\max}\; Q_t(a)
$$

- Always exploits current knowledge.
- Performs **zero exploration** → can become permanently stuck in a suboptimal action if the initial estimates are misleading.

### 2.2.2 The ε-Greedy Policy (ε ∈ (0, 1])

- With probability **1 − ε**: select the greedy action (exploit).
- With probability **ε**: select an action uniformly at random from the *k* actions (explore).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ε-greedy is asymptotically optimal:** As the number of steps t → ∞, every action will be sampled infinitely often, so Q_t(a) → q\*(a) for all *a*, and the probability of selecting the optimal action converges to **1 − ε + ε/k > 0**. A small constant ε (e.g., 0.1) balances exploration and exploitation efficiently.

### 2.2.3 Optimistic Initial Values

Initialize Q₁(a) to a value *much higher* than the largest conceivable reward, e.g., Q₁(a) = +5 when typical rewards are in [0, 1].

- The greedy action is then *whichever has the highest initial estimate* — initially all ties, forcing systematic exploration.
- After every real reward, the estimate drops. By the time all actions are tried, the agent automatically performs exploitation.
- **Limitation:** Optimism is *not* permanent; it fades as evidence accumulates.

### 2.2.4 Upper-Confidence-Bound (UCB) Action Selection

UCB addresses the *non-stationary uncertainty* of the estimates by adding an *exploration bonus* proportional to the *number of times* each action has been tried:

$$
A_t \;=\; \underset{a}{\arg\max}\!\left[\,Q_t(a) + c \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}\,\right]
$$

- *N_t(a)* = number of times action *a* has been selected prior to time *t*.
- *c* > 0 = confidence-level parameter controlling the *degree of exploration*.
- The square-root term implements the **principle of optimism in the face of uncertainty**: actions that have not been tried frequently receive inflated estimates.

### 2.2.5 Gradient Bandit Algorithm

Uses a *softmax* distribution over preferences H_t(a) ∈ ℝ, initialized uniformly:

$$
\Pr\{A_t = a\} \;=\; \pi_t(a) \;\triangleq\; \frac{e^{H_t(a)}}{\sum_{b=1}^{k} e^{H_t(b)}} \qquad \text{(Boltzmann / softmax)}
$$

Preference update rule with learning rate α > 0:

$$
H_{t+1}(A_t) \;\leftarrow\; H_t(A_t) + \alpha\!\left(R_t - \bar{R}_t\right)\!\left(1 - \pi_t(A_t)\right)
$$

$$
H_{t+1}(a) \;\leftarrow\; H_t(a) - \alpha\!\left(R_t - \bar{R}_t\right)\pi_t(a) \quad \text{for } a \neq A_t
$$

where $\bar{R}_t \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **running average of all rewards received up to time *t***, used as a *baseline* to reduce variance.

## 2.3 The Concept of Optimality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimality in RL (PECMT745 Module 2 definition):** A policy π\* is *optimal* in the bandit setting iff it maximizes the expected reward per time step. Equivalently, it minimizes the **expected regret** (also called *Bayesian regret* or *pseudo-regret*) over a horizon of *T* steps:
>
> $$\rho_T \;=\; T \cdot q_*(a_*) - \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathbb{E}\!\left[q_*(A_t)\right] = \sum_{a}\;\mathbb{E}[N_T(a)]\cdot\Delta_a$$
>
> where the *regret gap* of action *a* is defined as Δ_a = q\*(a\*) − q\*(a) ≥ 0. The goal is to design algorithms for which ρ_T grows *sublinearly* in *T* (e.g., ρ_T = O(√T) for UCB).

## 2.4 The Sample-Average (Incremental) Update Rule

Directly storing all past rewards is memory-inefficient. An equivalent *incremental* form, derived from a simple algebraic rearrangement, is:

$$
Q_{n+1}(a) \;=\; Q_n(a) + \frac{1}{n}\!\left(R_n - Q_n(a)\right)
$$

In a unified *constant-step-size* form with step-size parameter α_n(a) ∈ (0, 1]:

$$
Q_{n+1}(a) \;=\; Q_n(a) + \alpha_n(a)\!\left(R_n - Q_n(a)\right), \quad \text{with } \alpha_n(a) = \frac{1}{n} \text{ for sample average.}
$$

A *constant* α ∈ (0, 1) yields an *exponential recency-weighted average*:

$$
Q_n(a) \;=\; (1-\alpha)^{n} Q_1(a) + \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{n}(1-\alpha)^{n-i} R_i
$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2 / MAB Topic**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | True action value | $q_*(a) = \mathbb{E}[R_t \mid A_t = a]$ | Unitless expectation; reward units arbitrary. |
| 2 | Sample-average estimate | $Q_t(a) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} R_i \cdot \mathbf{1}\{A_i = a\}}{N_t(a)}$ | $\mathbf{1}$ = indicator function. |
| 3 | Greedy selection | $A_t = \arg\max_a Q_t(a)$ | No exploration. |
| 4 | ε-greedy selection | Choose greedy w.p. 1−ε; random w.p. ε | Typical ε ∈ {0.01, 0.05, 0.1}. |
| 5 | Incremental update | $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha(R_n - Q_n)$ | α = 1/n (sample avg.) or const. (exponential). |
| 6 | UCB selection | $A_t = \arg\max_a\!\left[Q_t(a) + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}\right]$ | c controls exploration. |
| 7 | Softmax policy | $\Pr(A_t=a) = \frac{e^{H_t(a)}}{\sum_b e^{H_t(b)}}$ | Numerical stability via log-sum-exp. |
| 8 | Gradient preference update (chosen) | $H_{t+1}(A_t) = H_t(A_t) + \alpha(R_t - \bar{R}_t)(1 - \pi_t(A_t))$ | $\bar{R}_t$ is reward baseline. |
| 9 | Gradient preference update (other) | $H_{t+1}(a) = H_t(a) - \alpha(R_t - \bar{R}_t)\pi_t(a)$ | $a \neq A_t$. |
| 10 | Pseudo-regret | $\rho_T = \sum_a \mathbb{E}[N_T(a)]\cdot \Delta_a$ | Sublinear ρ_T = O(√T) is optimal up to constants. |
| 11 | Regret gap | $\Delta_a = q_*(a_*) - q_*(a) \geq 0$ | Zero only for optimal action. |
| 12 | Exponentially weighted average | $Q_n = (1-\alpha)^n Q_1 + \alpha\sum_{i=1}^n (1-\alpha)^{n-i} R_i$ | Gives recent rewards more weight. |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The MAB framework powers a wide range of industrial decision systems:

- **A/B/n Testing in Tech Companies** (e.g., website layout optimization, recommendation systems).
- **Clinical Trial Design** — adaptively allocating more patients to the most promising treatments.
- **Adaptive Routing in Networks** — choosing routes that minimize latency under unknown traffic loads.
- **Financial Portfolio Rebalancing** — sequentially allocating capital across assets.
- **Hyperparameter Tuning in ML** — replacing exhaustive grid search with bandit-based optimizers (e.g., Hyperband, BOHB).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sample-Average Estimator

**Starting definition.** The sample-average estimator of *q*\*(*a*) after *n* observations of action *a* is:

$$
Q_n(a) \;\triangleq\; \frac{R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_n}{n}
$$

**Step 1 — Algebraic rearrangement.** Add and subtract *Q_{n−1}* inside the numerator:

$$
Q_n(a) \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i \;+\; R_n\right) = \frac{1}{n}\!\left((n-1) \cdot \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i \;+\; R_n\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the definition of Q_{n−1}.** Recognising that $\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i = Q_{n-1}$:

$$
Q_n(a) \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\!\left((n-1)\,Q_{n-1}(a) + R_n\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Distribute 1/n and split the terms.**

$$
Q_n(a) \;=\; \frac{n-1}{n}\,Q_{n-1}(a) + \frac{1}{n}\,R_n
$$

**Step 4 — Use the identity (n−1)/n = 1 − 1/n.**

$$
Q_n(a) \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right) Q_{n-1}(a) + \frac{1}{n}\,R_n
$$

**Step 5 — Distribute Q_{n−1} and isolate the correction term.**

$$
Q_n(a) \;=\; Q_{n-1}(a) - \frac{1}{n}Q_{n-1}(a) + \frac{1}{n}R_n
$$

**Step 6 — Factor 1/n to obtain the canonical incremental form.**

$$
\boxed{\,Q_n(a) \;=\; Q_{n-1}(a) + \frac{1}{n}\!\left(R_n - Q_{n-1}(a)\right)\,}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The new estimate equals the *old estimate plus a step-size-weighted error*. The "error" $R_n - Q_{n-1}(a)$ is the *prediction residual*; the "step size" 1/n shrinks as we accumulate more samples, ensuring convergence.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Exponentially Weighted (Constant-Step-Size) Estimate

**Step 1 — Generalise.** Replace 1/n in the incremental rule by a *constant* α ∈ (0, 1):

$$
Q_{n+1}(a) = Q_n(a) + \alpha\!\left(R_n - Q_n(a)\right) = (1-\alpha)Q_n(a) + \alpha R_n
$$

**Step 2 — Unroll the recursion.**

$$
Q_{n+1}(a) = (1-\alpha)\!\left[(1-\alpha)Q_{n-1}(a) + \alpha R_n\right] + \alpha R_n
$$

**Step 3 — Iterate the expansion.**

$$
Q_{n+1}(a) = (1-\alpha)^2 Q_{n-1}(a) + \alpha(1-\alpha)R_n + \alpha R_n
$$

**Step 4 — Continue to the base case Q₁.**

$$
Q_{n+1}(a) = (1-\alpha)^n Q_1(a) + \alpha\sum_{i=1}^{n}(1-\alpha)^{n-i} R_i
$$

**Step 5 — Verify weights sum to 1.** The geometric series yields:

$$
\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (1-\alpha)^i = \frac{1 - (1-\alpha)^n}{1 - (1-\alpha)} = \frac{1 - (1-\alpha)^n}{\alpha}
$$

Multiplying by α in the unrolled expression above confirms the weights sum to 1 asymptotically.

> **Interpretation:** A reward received *i* steps ago is weighted by (1−α)^{n−i}. For α = 0.1, a reward 10 steps old has weight 0.9¹⁰ ≈ 0.349; a reward 100 steps old has weight 0.9¹⁰⁰ ≈ 2.7 × 10⁻⁵. Hence constant step-sizes emphasise *recent* rewards — ideal for **non-stationary** bandits.

## 3.3 UCB Regret-Bound Intuition (Sketch)

The regret after T steps decomposes as:

$$
\rho_T = \sum_{a: \Delta_a > 0} \Delta_a \cdot \mathbb{E}[N_T(a)]
$$

UCB ensures that suboptimal actions are pulled at most $O\!\left(\frac{\ln T}{\Delta_a^2}\right)$ times in expectation. Substituting this into the regret sum:

$$
\rho_T^{\text{UCB}} = \sum_{a:\Delta_a>0} \Delta_a \cdot O\!\left(\frac{\ln T}{\Delta_a^2}\right) = O(\sqrt{kT\ln T})
$$

This $O(\sqrt{T \ln T})$ growth rate is *sublinear* in *T*, which means the **average regret per step** $\rho_T/T \to 0$ as $T \to \infty$. Hence UCB is *asymptotically no-regret* — a key notion of *optimality* in the bandit sense.

## 3.4 Gradient Bandit Algorithm — Complete Update Derivation

**Objective:** Maximise the expected reward $\mathbb{E}[R_t] = \sum_a \pi_t(a)\,q_*(a)$ via stochastic gradient ascent on the preferences H_t(a).

**Step 1 — Compute the partial derivative of expected reward with respect to H(a).** Using the chain rule and the softmax derivative:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbb{E}[R_t]}{\partial H_t(a)} = \sum_b q_*(b)\,\frac{\partial \pi_t(b)}{\partial H_t(a)} = q_*(a)\,\pi_t(a) - \pi_t(a)\sum_b q_*(b)\,\pi_t(b) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(q_*(A_t) - \mathbb{E}[R_t]\big)\cdot \mathbf{1}\{A_t = a\}\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Replace the unknown true values q\*(A_t) by the observed reward R_t** (a stochastic unbiased estimate), and replace the unknown $\mathbb{E}[R_t]$ by the empirical running average $\bar{R}_t$:

$$
H_{t+1}(a) \leftarrow H_t(a) + \alpha\!\left(R_t - \bar{R}_t\right)\!\left(\mathbf{1}\{A_t = a\} - \pi_t(a)\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Split into the two cases (chosen action vs. unchosen).**
- If $a = A_t$: indicator = 1, so update is $+\alpha(R_t - \bar{R}_t)(1 - \pi_t(a))$.
- If $a \neq A_t$: indicator = 0, so update is $-\alpha(R_t - \bar{R}_t)\pi_t(a)$.

This yields the formulas in the Section 2.2.5 cheat sheet, completing the derivation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Multi-Armed Bandit Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Begin Episode]) --> init[Initialize Q sub a for all a in 1 to k]
    init --> loop{For each step t}
    loop -->|t less than T| policy[Choose action A sub t using policy pi]
    policy -->|epsilon greedy| choice{Uniform random less than epsilon}
    choice -->|Yes| random[Select random action from set A]
    choice -->|No| greedy[Select A sub t equals argmax Q sub t of a]
    random --> env
    greedy --> env[Environment returns reward R sub t]
    env --> update[Update estimate Q sub t of A sub t]
    update --> step[Increment t]
    step --> loop
    loop -->|t equals T| end([Return cumulative reward])
```

```mermaid
    D --> E
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Mapping Table

| MAB Component | Engineering Realisation | Mathematical Object |
|---|---|---|
| Arms / Actions | Treatment options, ad variants, network routes | Set $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, \ldots, a_k\}$ |
| Reward distribution | Patient recovery rate, click-through probability, latency | $\mathbb{P}(R = r \mid A = a)$ |
| Pull / Trial | Administering a treatment, serving an ad, sending a packet | $t \in \{1, 2, \dots, T\}$ |
| Action value | Expected effectiveness | $q_*(a) = \mathbb{E}[R \mid A = a]$ |
| Cumulative reward | Total patient outcomes, total ad revenue | $\sum_t R_t$ |
| Regret | Opportunity cost of suboptimal pulls | $\rho_T = Tq_*(a_*) - \sum_t q_*(A_t)$ |
