# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

### **Q1.** Define the *k*-armed bandit problem. Explain the trade-off between exploration and exploitation in reinforcement learning with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 marks — concise):**

A *k*-armed bandit is a sequential decision-making problem in which an agent repeatedly chooses one of *k* actions, each delivering a stochastic reward drawn from an unknown stationary distribution. The agent's goal is to maximise expected cumulative reward. The **exploration–exploitation trade-off** arises because the agent must decide whether to (i) **exploit** — choose the action with the highest *estimated* value to secure immediate reward, or (ii) **explore** — try other actions to refine their value estimates and potentially discover a better one. *Example:* A website choosing between four ad layouts must balance showing the currently best-performing ad (exploitation) with occasionally testing alternatives to discover whether a new layout yields higher click-through rates (exploration). *(3 marks: definition 1 + trade-off 1 + example 1.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

### **Q2.** Compare the greedy and ε-greedy action-selection strategies for a 10-armed bandit. Under what conditions does ε-greedy outperform greedy?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **greedy** strategy always picks the action with the largest current estimate Q_t(a); it performs zero exploration. The **ε-greedy** strategy behaves greedily with probability 1 − ε and picks a random action with probability ε. *Conditions where ε-greedy outperforms greedy:* (i) when the initial value estimates are *inaccurate or biased* — ε-greedy continues to sample all arms, allowing correction of false maximisers, whereas greedy may lock onto a suboptimal arm forever; (ii) when rewards are *stationary* and the horizon is *long* — guaranteed continuous exploration ensures asymptotic convergence to the optimal action. In short, ε-greedy dominates greedy whenever the cost of limited exploration (in finite horizons) exceeds the cost of occasional random pulls. *(3 marks: comparison 1 + ε-greedy description 1 + conditions 1.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 | Internal Choice Pattern: Q11 OR Q12]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — ε-Greedy and Incremental Estimation**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **incremental update rule** for action-value estimation in a *k*-armed bandit, starting from the sample-average definition. Show that the update can be written as $Q_{n+1}(A_n) = Q_n(A_n) + \alpha_n (R_n - Q_n(A_n))$. Discuss what happens to the algorithm's behaviour when (i) α_n = 1/n, and (ii) α_n is a constant α.

**(b) [7 marks]** Consider a 5-armed bandit with true action values $q_*(a_1) = 0.4, q_*(a_2) = 0.6, q_*(a_3) = 0.3, q_*(a_4) = 0.5, q_*(a_5) = 0.45$. After 100 pulls using ε-greedy with ε = 0.1, suppose the empirical estimates become $Q(a_1) = 0.38, Q(a_2) = 0.62, Q(a_3) = 0.31, Q(a_4) = 0.48, Q(a_5) = 0.46$. (i) Identify the *greedy action* under the current estimates. (ii) Calculate the **pseudo-regret** $\rho_{100}$ assuming each arm was pulled equally (20 times each on average). (iii) Suggest a modification to reduce regret in the long run.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

- **Step 1 [1 mark]**: State the sample-average estimator:

$$
Q_n(a) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i
$$

- **Step 2 [1 mark]**: Introduce the incremental form by writing Q_n in terms of Q_{n−1}:

$$
Q_n(a) = \frac{1}{n}\!\left((n-1)Q_{n-1}(a) + R_n\right)
$$

- **Step 3 [2 marks]**: Simplify:

$$
Q_n(a) = Q_{n-1}(a) + \frac{1}{n}\!\left(R_n - Q_{n-1}(a)\right)
$$

- **Step 4 [1 mark]**: Generalise using a step-size α_n:

$$
Q_{n+1}(A_n) = Q_n(A_n) + \alpha_n \big(R_n - Q_n(A_n)\big)
$$

- **Step 5 [2 marks]** — Behaviour discussion:
  - **(i) α_n = 1/n (sample average):** Each past reward is given equal weight regardless of recency; the estimator converges to the true value *q*\*(*a*) as n → ∞ (Law of Large Numbers). It is *stationary-aware* but *non-stationary-blind* — older rewards influence the estimate as much as recent ones.
  - **(ii) α_n = constant α ∈ (0, 1) (exponential recency-weighted average):** The estimate gives more weight to recent rewards, making the algorithm *adaptive* to non-stationary reward distributions. The trade-off is that the estimator never fully converges; it tracks a *weighted average* of past rewards with effective window $\sim 1/\alpha$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

- **(i) [2 marks]** Greedy action = $\arg\max_a Q(a)$ = **a₂** (estimate 0.62). Note that this *coincides* with the true optimum a₂ (= 0.6) in this dataset.
- **(ii) [3 marks]** Each arm pulled ≈ 20 times (100/5). Pseudo-regret:

$$
\rho_{100} = \sum_{a}\mathbb{E}[N_{100}(a)]\cdot \Delta_a
$$

Compute Δ_a = q\*(a₂) − q\*(a) for each arm:
- a₁: 0.6 − 0.4 = 0.2
- a₂: 0.6 − 0.6 = 0
- a₃: 0.6 − 0.3 = 0.3
- a₄: 0.6 − 0.5 = 0.1
- a₅: 0.6 − 0.45 = 0.15

$$
\rho_{100} = 20(0.2) + 20(0) + 20(0.3) + 20(0.1) + 20(0.15) = 4 + 0 + 6 + 2 + 3 = \boxed{15}
$$

- **(iii) [2 marks]** A more regret-efficient strategy would be **UCB (Upper Confidence Bound) action selection**, which preferentially explores arms with high uncertainty (low N_t(a)), concentrating exploration where it is most informative and reducing pulls on confidently suboptimal arms. Alternatively, **decaying ε** (e.g., ε_t ∝ 1/t) asymptotically reduces exploration while ensuring all arms are sampled.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — UCB and Gradient Bandit Algorithms**

**(a) [7 marks]** State and explain the **Upper-Confidence-Bound (UCB) action-selection rule**. Show mathematically why this rule implements "optimism in the face of uncertainty." Outline a step-by-step pseudocode-free algorithm description for applying UCB to a 10-armed bandit with confidence parameter c = 2.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **Gradient Bandit Algorithm** with a baseline. Given the preference update rule for action A_t:

$$
H_{t+1}(A_t) = H_t(A_t) + \alpha (R_t - \bar{R}_t)(1 - \pi_t(A_t))
$$

explain (i) the role of the **baseline** $\bar{R}_t$, (ii) why subtracting the baseline reduces variance of the stochastic gradient estimate, and (iii) prove that the stationary distribution of arm selection under this algorithm favours arms with higher expected reward.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) UCB Rule (7 marks):**

- **Statement [2 marks]**:

$$
A_t = \underset{a}{\arg\max}\!\left[Q_t(a) + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}\right]
$$

where $N_t(a)$ is the number of times action *a* was selected prior to step *t*, and *c* > 0 is a confidence parameter.

- **Optimism in the face of uncertainty [2 marks]**: The second term $c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}$ is a **confidence interval** (in the frequentist sense, by Hoeffding's inequality) on the uncertainty in the estimate Q_t(a). When an arm has been pulled few times, N_t(a) is small, the term is large, and the arm's *upper confidence bound* is inflated — making it more likely to be selected. This is "optimism" because the algorithm acts as if the true value of an under-sampled arm is as high as plausibly possible.

- **Step-by-step algorithm for 10-armed bandit with c = 2 [3 marks]**:
  1. Initialise Q(a) = 0, N(a) = 0 for all 10 arms.
  2. For each step t = 1, 2, ..., T:
     - For each arm a, compute the UCB score $Q(a) + 2\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N(a) + \epsilon}}$ (using +ε to avoid division by zero initially).
     - Select $A_t = \arg\max_a \text{UCB}_t(a)$.
     - Observe reward R_t.
     - Increment $N(A_t) \mathrel{+}= 1$.
     - Update estimate $Q(A_t) \mathrel{+}= \frac{1}{N(A_t)}(R_t - Q(A_t))$.
  3. Repeat until horizon T.

**(b) Gradient Bandit with Baseline (7 marks):**

- **(i) Role of the baseline [2 marks]**: $\bar{R}_t$ is the running *average of all rewards observed up to time t*. It serves as a **reference point** so that rewards above the average *reinforce* the chosen action, while rewards below the average *discourage* it. Without the baseline, the algorithm would shift preferences uniformly based on reward magnitude, leading to instability.

- **(ii) Variance reduction [2 marks]**: The gradient of the expected reward contains $\mathbb{E}[q_*(A_t) - \mathbb{E}[R_t]]$, an *unbiased but high-variance* estimator when we substitute R_t. The baseline $\bar{R}_t$ is correlated with the reward but **uncorrelated with the action indicator**, so subtracting it does not bias the gradient but does reduce variance. Formally:

$$
\text{Var}\big[\big(R_t - \bar{R}_t\big)\mathbf{1}\{A_t = a\}\big] < \text{Var}\big[R_t \mathbf{1}\{A_t = a\}\big]
$$

- **(iii) Stationary distribution proof [3 marks]**: At convergence, $\mathbb{E}[\Delta H_t(a)] = 0$ for all *a*, i.e.:

$$
0 = \alpha\mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(R_t - \bar{R}_t\big)\big(\mathbf{1}\{A_t = a\} - \pi_t(a)\big)\right] \quad\Rightarrow\quad \pi(a)\big(q_*(a) - \bar{q}\big) = \pi(a)\sum_b\pi(b)\big(q_*(a) - q_*(b)\big)
$$

Using the *softmax* parametrisation $\pi(a) \propto e^{H(a)}$ and the property that $\bar{q} = \sum_b \pi(b) q_*(b)$, one can show the stationary distribution satisfies $\pi(a) \propto e^{H^*( a )}$ with $H^*( a )$ monotonically increasing in $q_*(a)$. Hence arms with higher expected reward receive exponentially larger selection probability — completing the proof.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in MAB Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the indicator function** $\mathbf{1}\{A_i = a\}$ when writing the sample-average estimator. Always include it; the value is summed *only* when action *a* was selected.
> 2. **Confusing pseudo-regret with cumulative reward**. Pseudo-regret is a *loss relative to the optimum*, not a reward.
> 3. **In UCB problems, students often forget the +ε trick** to avoid division by zero when N_t(a) = 0. Always add a small positive constant to the denominator in initial steps.
> 4. **Confusing the optimal action a\* with the greedy action at time t**. The first is fixed but unknown; the second depends on the current estimates and changes over time.
> 5. **In gradient bandit questions, students frequently drop the baseline term** $\bar{R}_t$. The baseline is *essential*; without it, the algorithm is biased and unstable.
> 6. **Mixing up sample-average (1/n) with constant step-size (α)** in incremental update derivations — these have *very different convergence and stationarity* properties.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the MAB Sub-Topic (Module 2)**

- The *k*-armed bandit is a **single-state MDP** — the simplest setting in which to study exploration vs exploitation.
- **True value**: $q_*(a) = \mathbb{E}[R_t \mid A_t = a]$.
- **Estimate**: $Q_t(a)$ is the empirical average of rewards received after pulling *a*.
- **Greedy**: always pick $\arg\max_a Q_t(a)$ — no exploration.
- **ε-greedy**: with probability ε, explore randomly; with probability 1 − ε, exploit. Asymptotically optimal for stationary bandits.
- **Optimistic initial values**: bias initial estimates upward to *force* exploration early.
- **UCB**: $A_t = \arg\max_a\!\left[Q_t(a) + c\sqrt{\ln t \,/\, N_t(a)}\right]$; guarantees $O(\sqrt{T\ln T})$ regret.
- **Gradient bandit**: uses a softmax distribution with stochastic gradient ascent on preferences, employing a **reward baseline** $\bar{R}_t$ to reduce variance.
- **Incremental update**: $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha_n(R_n - Q_n)$; α = 1/n gives sample average; constant α gives exponential recency-weighted average.
- **Pseudo-regret**: $\rho_T = \sum_a \mathbb{E}[N_T(a)]\cdot \Delta_a$; a *good* algorithm has $\rho_T = o(T)$ (sublinear).
- **Regret gap**: $\Delta_a = q_*(a_*) - q_*(a)$; only the optimal arm has $\Delta_a = 0$.
- **Law of Large Numbers** guarantees that sample averages converge to true means as the number of samples per arm → ∞.
- **Stochastic gradient ascent** on preferences yields a stationary selection distribution biased toward higher-reward arms.
- The MAB problem is the **conceptual bridge** between classical *multi-hypothesis testing* (statistics) and full Markov Decision Processes (Module 3 of PECMT745).
- **Practical tip**: When asked for an algorithm choice in exam, justify with the *horizon* (finite vs infinite) and the *stationarity* of the reward distributions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Cost of Exploration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Cost of Exploration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cost of Exploration** in Reinforcement Learning (RL) quantifies the *performance penalty* or *opportunity loss* incurred by an agent when it chooses to gather new information about the environment (exploration) instead of utilizing its current best-known knowledge to maximize immediate reward (exploitation). Formally, it is measured through the **Regret** function $R(T)$, defined as the cumulative difference between the reward obtained by an optimal (oracle) policy and the reward obtained by the learning agent over a time horizon $T$.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this concept is central to the **Exploration–Exploitation Dilemma**, one of the foundational problems in sequential decision-making under uncertainty. The *cost* of exploration is not merely the immediate action reward, but the *expected cumulative loss* over the agent's lifetime.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Choice Problem:**
> Imagine you are new to a city with 10 restaurants, and you must eat dinner there for the next **30 nights**.
> - **Exploitation:** You keep going to the restaurant that gave you the *best dinner last night*.
> - **Exploration:** You try a new restaurant to discover potentially better options.
>
> The **Cost of Exploration** is the *bad meals you eat* on nights when you tried a new restaurant that turned out to be worse than your current favourite. Even if you eventually find the best restaurant, the *total bad meals* across the 30 nights is your cumulative cost of exploring.

This same principle governs an RL agent learning in an unknown Markov Decision Process (MDP) — every exploratory action taken in place of the true optimal action contributes to the **cumulative regret**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Optimal Reward | $r^*$ | Expected reward per step under the optimal policy $\pi^*$ |
| Agent's Reward | $r_t$ | Actual reward obtained at time step $t$ |
| Instantaneous Regret | $r_t$ | Difference between optimal and actual reward at step $t$ |
| Cumulative Regret | $R(T)$ | Total regret accumulated over horizon $T$ |
| Time Horizon | $T$ | Total number of decision steps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme, students are expected to (a) define regret formally, (b) distinguish between **uniform** and **weighted** cost of exploration, and (c) compute asymptotic regret bounds for bandit algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Exploration–Exploitation Tradeoff — Structured Logic

The cost of exploration arises because of **structural uncertainty** in the environment:

- **Step 1 — The Agent's Dilemma:** Before acting, the agent has *estimates* $\hat{Q}(a)$ of the value of each action $a$, but does not know the true values $Q^*(a)$.
- **Step 2 — The Exploitation Choice:** Picking the action with the highest estimate yields the *best-known* immediate reward but prevents discovery of a better action.
- **Step 3 — The Exploration Choice:** Picking a non-greedy action may yield a worse immediate reward but *reduces uncertainty*, potentially improving future rewards.
- **Step 4 — Quantifying the Tradeoff:** The *cost* of choosing a sub-optimal action $a$ when the optimal is $a^*$ is the **gap**:
$$\Delta_a = Q^*(a^*) - Q^*(a)$$
- **Step 5 — Aggregating the Cost:** Summing these gaps over the time horizon gives the **total cost of exploration**, also called *cumulative regret*.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Cost

The cost of exploration is mathematically meaningful because:

1. **It is unavoidable in stochastic environments** — by the Law of Large Numbers, identifying the best action requires sampling non-greedy actions infinitely often (or at least logarithmically many times).
2. **It is bounded by information theory** — no algorithm can achieve sub-logarithmic regret in the general stochastic bandit setting.
3. **It decays with learning** — early exploration is expensive, but as the agent gains information, the marginal cost of further exploration drops.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Instantaneous Regret | $\ell_t = V^* - V_{A_t}$ | Loss at step $t$ from picking action $A_t$ |
| 2 | Cumulative Regret | $R(T) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \ell_t = T \cdot \mu^* - \sum_{t=1}^{T} r_t$ | Total cost of exploration over $T$ steps |
| 3 | Pseudo-Regret | $\bar{R}(T) = T \cdot \mu^* - \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mu_{A_t}$ | Expected cumulative regret |
| 4 | Regret Gap | $\Delta_a = \mu^* - \mu_a$ | Difference between best and $a$-th arm |
| 5 | UCB1 Bound | $R(T) \le 8 \sum_{a:\Delta_a>0} \frac{\ln T}{\Delta_a} + \text{const}$ | Upper bound on UCB1 algorithm's regret |
| 6 | ε-greedy Regret | $R(T) = \mathcal{O}(T^{2/3})$ | Linear in worst case without decay |
| 7 | Decaying ε-greedy | $R(T) = \mathcal{O}(\log T)$ | Achieved by setting $\varepsilon_t = 1/t$ |
| 8 | Lower Bound (Lai-Robbins) | $\liminf_{T\to\infty} \frac{R(T)}{\ln T} \ge \sum_{a:\Delta_a>0} \frac{2 \Delta_a}{\text{KL}(P_a \,\|\, P^*)}$ | No algorithm can beat logarithmic regret |
| 9 | Greedy Algorithm | $R(T) = \mathcal{O}(T)$ | May never stop exploring sub-optimally |
| 10 | Optimal Asymptotic | $R(T) = \mathcal{O}(\log T)$ | Achieved by UCB, Thompson Sampling |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Safety Rule:** In the table above, all vertical bars representing absolute value, conditioning, or norms are written using `\vert` or `\,\|\,` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. This is a KTU 2024 Scheme requirement for clean digital submissions.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of cost of exploration is foundational in:

- **Recommender Systems (Netflix, YouTube):** Balancing showing new content (exploration) vs. showing the user's preferred genre (exploitation).
- **Clinical Trials:** Testing new treatments (exploration) vs. continuing proven treatment (exploitation) — directly tied to **regret minimization** in bandit algorithms.
- **Adaptive Routing in Networks:** Discovering faster routes (exploration) vs. using the currently known fastest route.
- **Robotics:** A robot must explore unknown terrain to map it, but incurs energy/time costs.
- **Online Advertising:** Choosing which ad to display — explore new ads to learn CTR vs. show the best-performing ad.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Cumulative Regret from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No logical transitions are skipped. This is the KTU Board Examiner's expected standard for full-mark answers.

---

#### **Derivation 1: From Instantaneous to Cumulative Regret**

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of the optimal expected reward:**

Let $\mu^* = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mu_a$, where $\mu_a$ is the expected reward of arm $a$.

**Step 2 — Define instantaneous regret at time $t$:**

If the agent picks arm $A_t$ at time $t$ and receives reward $r_t$, the instantaneous regret is:

$$\ell_t = \mu^* - r_t$$

**Step 3 — Note that $r_t$ is a sample from the reward distribution of arm $A_t$:**

The expected value of $\ell_t$, conditioned on the agent's history $\mathcal{H}_{t-1}$, is:

$$\mathbb{E}[\ell_t \mid \mathcal{H}_{t-1}] = \mu^* - \mu_{A_t} = \Delta_{A_t}$$

**Step 4 — Sum instantaneous regrets to obtain cumulative (pseudo) regret:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R(T) &= \sum_{t=1}^{T} \ell_t \\
&= \sum_{t=1}^{T} (\mu^* - r_t) \\
&= T \cdot \mu^* - \sum_{t=1}^{T} r_t
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Take the expectation to obtain pseudo-regret (the "average cost"):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{R}(T) &= \mathbb{E}[R(T)] \\
&= \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathbb{E}[\Delta_{A_t}] \\
&= \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \Delta_a \cdot \mathbb{E}[N_a(T)]
\end{aligned}$$

where $N_a(T) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathbb{1}\{A_t = a\}$ is the number of times arm $a$ was pulled.

---

#### **Derivation 2: Regret of the Pure Greedy Algorithm**

**Step 1 — Greedy algorithm always picks the arm with the highest sample mean:**

$$A_t = \arg\max_{a} \hat{\mu}_a(t)$$

**Step 2 — If the greedy algorithm locks onto a sub-optimal arm $a'$ early, it never explores again** (no randomness, no uncertainty bonus).

**Step 3 — Suppose the algorithm commits to arm $a'$ with $\Delta_{a'} > 0$ from some time $t_0$ onward:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R(T) &= \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathbb{E}[\Delta_{A_t}] \\
&= \sum_{t=1}^{t_0 - 1} \mathbb{E}[\Delta_{A_t}] + \sum_{t=t_0}^{T} \Delta_{a'} \\
&\ge (T - t_0 + 1) \cdot \Delta_{a'} \\
&= \mathcal{O}(T)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** The pure greedy algorithm incurs **linear regret** $\mathcal{O}(T)$, which is the *worst possible* asymptotic cost of exploration. It is theoretically unacceptable.

---

#### **Derivation 3: UCB1 Algorithm Regret Bound**

**Step 1 — UCB1 picks the arm maximizing the upper confidence bound:**

$$A_t = \arg\max_{a} \left( \hat{\mu}_a(t) + c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_a(t)}} \right)$$

where $c = \sqrt{2}$ for rewards in $[0,1]$.

**Step 2 — For any sub-optimal arm $a$, the upper confidence term shrinks as $N_a(t)$ grows:**

$$c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_a(t)}} \longrightarrow 0 \quad \text{as } N_a(t) \to \infty$$

**Step 3 — Bound the number of pulls of a sub-optimal arm:**

By Hoeffding's inequality, after $N_a(t)$ pulls, the probability that $\hat{\mu}_a$ exceeds $\mu^* - \varepsilon$ is bounded. The arm $a$ is chosen only when:

$$\hat{\mu}_a + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_a(t)}} \ge \mu^*$$

**Step 4 — Solving for the threshold pull count $N_a^*$:**

Setting the confidence term equal to $\Delta_a$:

$$c \sqrt{\frac{\ln T}{N_a^*}} = \Delta_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_a^* = \frac{c^2 \ln T}{\Delta_a^2}$$

**Step 5 — Plug into the regret formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{R}(T) &= \sum_{a:\Delta_a > 0} \Delta_a \cdot N_a(T) \\
&\le \sum_{a:\Delta_a > 0} \Delta_a \cdot \left( \frac{4 c^2 \ln T}{\Delta_a^2} + 1 + \frac{\pi^2}{3} \right) \\
&= \sum_{a:\Delta_a > 0} \left( \frac{4 c^2 \ln T}{\Delta_a} + \Delta_a + \frac{\pi^2 \Delta_a}{3} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Final asymptotic bound:**

$$\bar{R}(T) \le 8 \sum_{a:\Delta_a > 0} \frac{\ln T}{\Delta_a} + \mathcal{O}(1) = \mathcal{O}(\log T)$$

This is **logarithmic** in $T$, which is optimal (matches the Lai–Robbins lower bound up to constants).

---

#### **Derivation 4: Cost of Exploration for ε-greedy with Decaying ε**

**Step 1 — Fix a decaying schedule** $\varepsilon_t = \min\{1, c/t\}$ for some constant $c > 0$.

**Step 2 — Expected number of times a sub-optimal arm $a$ is explored:**

With probability $\varepsilon_t$ the agent explores, and there are $K-1$ sub-optimal arms (uniformly chosen), so:

$$\mathbb{E}[N_a^{\text{explore}}(T)] = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\varepsilon_t}{K-1} = \frac{1}{K-1} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{c}{t} = \frac{c \cdot H_T}{K-1}$$

where $H_T \approx \ln T$ is the harmonic number.

**Step 3 — Pseudo-regret:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{R}(T) &= \sum_{a:\Delta_a > 0} \Delta_a \cdot \mathbb{E}[N_a(T)] \\
&\le \frac{c}{K-1} \sum_{a:\Delta_a > 0} \Delta_a \cdot \ln T + \mathcal{O}(1) \\
&= \mathcal{O}(\log T)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

| Algorithm | Regret | Type |
|---|---|---|
| Pure Greedy | $\mathcal{O}(T)$ | Linear (worst) |
| ε-greedy (constant ε) | $\mathcal{O}(T)$ | Linear (worst) |
| ε-greedy (decaying) | $\mathcal{O}(\log T)$ | Logarithmic |
| UCB1 | $\mathcal{O}(\log T)$ | Logarithmic (optimal) |
| Thompson Sampling | $\mathcal{O}(\log T)$ | Logarithmic (optimal) |
| Lower Bound (Lai-Robbins) | $\Omega(\log T)$ | Cannot be beaten |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Exploration–Exploitation Decision Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `startA`, `decideB`). No reserved keywords are used as standalone IDs. Labels contain clean uppercase text only.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START: Time Step t]) --> stateB[Agent Observes Current State s sub t]
    stateB --> estimateC[Compute Estimated Values Q hat of a for all actions a]
    estimateC --> decideD{Decision: Exploit or Explore?}
    decideD -- Exploit --> exploitE[Pick Action with Max Q hat of a]
    decideD -- Explore --> exploreF[Pick Sub-Optimal or Random Action]
    exploitE --> rewardG[Receive Reward r sub t equals Q star of a with zero regret]
    exploreF --> rewardH[Receive Reward r sub t equals Q star of a with regret Delta sub a]
    rewardG --> costI[Instantaneous Regret: zero]
    rewardH --> costJ[Instantaneous Regret: Delta sub a positive]
    costI --> updateK[Update Q Estimates Q hat of a]
    costJ --> updateK
    updateK --> totalL[Accumulate Cumulative Regret R of T]
    totalL --> checkM{Is t less than T?}
    checkM -- Yes --> startA
    checkM -- No --> endN([END: Total Regret R of T equals Sum of Delta sub a times N sub a of T])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of Regret Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        arm1[Arm 1 Distribution P sub 1]
        arm2[Arm 2 Distribution P sub 2]
        armN[Arm K Distribution P sub K]
    end

    subgraph ALGO["ALGORITHM LAYER"]
        strategyA[Exploration Strategy Epsilon Greedy UCB Thompson]
    end

    subgraph METRIC["METRIC LAYER"]
        gapCalc[Compute Gaps Delta sub a equals Mu star minus Mu sub a]
        pullCount[Count Pulls N sub a of T]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        regretCompute[Cumulative Regret R of T equals Sum Delta sub a times N sub a of T]
        boundCheck[Check Asymptotic Bound O of log T or O of T]
    end

    arm1 --> strategyA
    arm2 --> strategyA
    armN --> strategyA
    strategyA --> gapCalc
    strategyA --> pullCount
    gapCalc --> regretCompute
    pullCount --> regretCompute
    regretCompute --> boundCheck
```

### 4.3 Regret Growth Trajectory Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    baseA[Total Time Horizon T on X Axis] --> curve1[Greedy: Linear Growth O of T]
    baseA --> curve2[Epsilon Greedy Constant: Linear Growth O of T]
    baseA --> curve3[Epsilon Greedy Decaying: Logarithmic Growth O of log T]
    baseA --> curve4[UCB1: Logarithmic Growth O of log T with smaller constant]
    baseA --> curve5[Lai Robbins Lower Bound: Omega of log T]
    curve1 --> verdictW[WORST: Linear Regret]
    curve2 --> verdictW
    curve3 --> verdictB[BEST: Sub Linear Regret]
    curve4 --> verdictB
    curve5 --> verdictLB[THEORETICAL LIMIT: Cannot be beaten]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The shape of the regret curve directly measures the *long-term cost* of exploration. A linear curve means the agent never stops paying for its early mistakes; a logarithmic curve means the cost of exploration eventually plateaus, which is the *gold standard* for any practical RL agent.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define the term "Regret" in the context of Reinforcement Learning. Write the mathematical expression for cumulative regret over a horizon $T$.**

> **[Model Answer — 3 Marks]**
>
> **Regret** in RL is a measure of the total reward lost by an agent due to following a sub-optimal policy instead of the optimal one.
>
> $$\bar{R}(T) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathbb{E}\left[\mu^* - \mu_{A_t}\right] = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \Delta_a \cdot \mathbb{E}[N_a(T)]$$
>
> where $\mu^*$ is the optimal mean reward, $\Delta_a$ is the gap for arm $a$, and $N_a(T)$ is the number of pulls of arm $a$ in $T$ steps. **(1 mark definition + 2 marks formula derivation)**

---

**Q2. Differentiate between exploration and exploitation. Why is the cost of exploration unavoidable in stochastic environments?**

> **[Model Answer — 3 Marks]**
>
> - **Exploitation** uses the current best-known action to maximize immediate reward. **(1 mark)**
> - **Exploration** tries a non-greedy action to gain information about its true value. **(1 mark)**
> - The cost is *unavoidable* because by the **Law of Large Numbers**, the agent can only converge to the true value of an action by sampling it. Without exploration, the agent may lock onto a sub-optimal action forever, yielding linear regret. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Derivation-Focused*

**(a)** Derive the cumulative regret expression $R(T) = T \mu^* - \sum_{t=1}^{T} r_t$ for a multi-armed bandit setting. Clearly define all symbols. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** Show that the **pure greedy algorithm** incurs linear regret $\mathcal{O}(T)$ in the worst case. **[(7 Marks)]**

> **[Model Answer]**
>
> **(a) Derivation of Cumulative Regret [7 Marks]**
>
> *Step 1 — Define the optimal expected reward:* **(1 mark)**
>
> Let $\mu^* = \max_{a \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}} \mu_a$, where $\mu_a$ is the expected reward of arm $a$ under its true distribution $P_a$.
>
> *Step 2 — Define instantaneous regret at step $t$:* **(1 mark)**
>
> $$\ell_t = \mu^* - r_t$$
>
> where $r_t$ is the realized reward when arm $A_t$ is pulled.
>
> *Step 3 — Take conditional expectation:* **(1 mark)**
>
> $$\mathbb{E}[\ell_t \mid \mathcal{H}_{t-1}] = \mu^* - \mu_{A_t} = \Delta_{A_t}$$
>
> *Step 4 — Sum over $T$ steps to get cumulative regret:* **(1 mark)**
>
> $$R(T) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \ell_t = \sum_{t=1}^{T} (\mu^* - r_t)$$
>
> *Step 5 — Expand the sum:* **(1 mark)**
>
> $$R(T) = T \mu^* - \sum_{t=1}^{T} r_t$$
>
> *Step 6 — Rewrite as a sum over arms using $N_a(T)$:* **(1 mark)**
>
> $$\mathbb{E}[R(T)] = \sum_{a=1}^{K} \Delta_a \cdot \mathbb{E}[N_a(T)]$$
>
> *Step 7 — Conclude:* **(1 mark)**
>
> This expresses the total cost of exploration in terms of how many times each sub-optimal arm was pulled and by how much it was sub-optimal.
>
> ---
>
> **(b) Greedy Algorithm Yields Linear Regret [7 Marks]**
>
> *Step 1 — Greedy rule:* **(1 mark)**
>
> $$A_t = \arg\max_{a} \hat{\mu}_a(t)$$
>
> where $\hat{\mu}_a(t)$ is the empirical mean reward of arm $a$ up to step $t$.
>
> *Step 2 — Suppose the greedy algorithm commits to a sub-optimal arm $a'$:* **(1 mark)**
>
> After some finite time $t_0$, the agent may erroneously conclude $\hat{\mu}_{a'}(t_0) > \hat{\mu}_{a^*}(t_0)$ due to noise.
>
> *Step 3 — Once committed, the greedy never explores again:* **(1 mark)**
>
> Since there is no randomness, $A_t = a'$ for all $t \ge t_0$.
>
> *Step 4 — Cumulative regret computation:* **(2 marks)**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
R(T) &= \sum_{t=1}^{t_0 - 1} \Delta_{A_t} + \sum_{t=t_0}^{T} \Delta_{a'} \\
&\ge (T - t_0 + 1) \cdot \Delta_{a'} \\
&= \mathcal{O}(T)
\end{aligned}$$
>
> *Step 5 — Conclusion:* **(2 marks)**
>
> Since $\Delta_{a'} > 0$ is a fixed positive constant, $R(T) \to \infty$ linearly as $T \to \infty$. Hence the cost of exploration (or rather, the *failure to explore*) is $\mathcal{O}(T)$ — the **worst possible** asymptotic behavior. This justifies the need for *directed* exploration strategies like UCB or Thompson Sampling.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Algorithm-Focused*

**(a)** Explain the **Upper Confidence Bound (UCB1)** algorithm for the multi-armed bandit problem. Write its action-selection rule and explain the role of the confidence bonus term. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** Derive the **asymptotic regret bound** of UCB1 and show that $R(T) = \mathcal{O}(\log T)$. State the Lai–Robbins lower bound and comment on the optimality of UCB1. **[(7 Marks)]**

> **[Model Answer]**
>
> **(a) UCB1 Algorithm [7 Marks]**
>
> *Step 1 — Initialization:* **(1 mark)**
>
> Each arm is pulled once. Set $N_a(0) = 0$ for all $a$, then play each arm once and update $\hat{\mu}_a$.
>
> *Step 2 — Action selection rule (UCB1):* **(2 marks)**
>
> $$A_t = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \left\{ \hat{\mu}_a(t) + \sqrt{\frac{2 \ln t}{N_a(t)}} \right\}$$
>
> *Step 3 — Role of the bonus term:* **(2 marks)**
>
> The term $\sqrt{2 \ln t / N_a(t)}$ is the **upper confidence bound** — it represents the *uncertainty* in the estimate $\hat{\mu}_a$. Arms that have been pulled few times have a large bonus, forcing exploration. Arms that have been pulled many times have a small bonus, allowing the agent to exploit the best one.
>
> *Step 4 — Update rule:* **(1 mark)**
>
> After observing $r_t$, update $N_{A_t} \leftarrow N_{A_t} + 1$ and refresh $\hat{\mu}_{A_t}$.
>
> *Step 5 — Intuition:* **(1 mark)**
>
> UCB1 implements the principle of *"optimism in the face of uncertainty"* — it acts as if the unknown true mean is as high as plausibly possible, which naturally trades off exploration and exploitation.
>
> ---
>
> **(b) Regret Bound and Optimality [7 Marks]**
>
> *Step 1 — For a sub-optimal arm $a$, the bonus shrinks:* **(1 mark)**
>
> After $N_a(t)$ pulls, the bonus is $\sqrt{2 \ln t / N_a(t)}$.
>
> *Step 2 — Threshold for stopping exploration:* **(1 mark)**
>
> The agent stops pulling $a$ once $\hat{\mu}_a + \sqrt{2 \ln t / N_a(t)} < \mu^*$, which happens when:
>
> $$N_a(t) \ge \frac{2 \ln t}{\Delta_a^2}$$
>
> *Step 3 — Bound on $N_a(T)$ using Hoeffding's inequality:* **(2 marks)**
>
> $$\mathbb{E}[N_a(T)] \le \frac{4 \ln T}{\Delta_a^2} + 1 + \frac{\pi^2}{3}$$
>
> *Step 4 — Sum the regrets across sub-optimal arms:* **(1 mark)**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
\bar{R}(T) &= \sum_{a:\Delta_a > 0} \Delta_a \cdot \mathbb{E}[N_a(T)] \\
&\le \sum_{a:\Delta_a > 0} \left( \frac{4 \ln T}{\Delta_a} + \Delta_a + \frac{\pi^2 \Delta_a}{3} \right) \\
&= \mathcal{O}(\log T)
\end{aligned}$$
>
> *Step 5 — Lai–Robbins lower bound:* **(1 mark)**
>
> $$\liminf_{T \to \infty} \frac{R(T)}{\ln T} \ge \sum_{a:\Delta_a > 0} \frac{2 \Delta_a}{\text{KL}(P_a \,\|\, P^*)}$$
>
> *Step 6 — Optimality comment:* **(1 mark)**
>
> UCB1 achieves the optimal $\mathcal{O}(\log T)$ growth rate (up to constant factors), making it an **order-optimal** algorithm. The cost of exploration under UCB1 grows logarithmically — meaning the *marginal* cost per step vanishes as $T \to \infty$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Regret with Reward:** Many students write $R(T) = \sum r_t$ instead of $R(T) = T \mu^* - \sum r_t$. **Always subtract from the optimal.**
> 2. **Forgetting the expectation:** Cumulative regret is a *random variable*; the **pseudo-regret** $\bar{R}(T) = \mathbb{E}[R(T)]$ is what we bound. Mixing them loses **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Missing the constant in $\mathcal{O}(\log T)$:** Examiners expect you to mention the **gap-dependent constant** $4/\Delta_a$ and compare with **Lai–Robbins** for full marks on optimality questions.
> 4. **Treating $\varepsilon$-greedy as optimal:** Constant-$\varepsilon$ greedy gives **linear** regret, not logarithmic. State the *decaying* version explicitly.
> 5. **Vertical bar misuse in tables/expressions:** Use `\vert` or `\,\|\,` (NOT bare `|`) in markdown/LaTeX to prevent table-breaking in KTU digital answer sheets.
> 6. **Skipping the Hoeffding step:** The UCB1 derivation MUST invoke Hoeffding's inequality at least once for full marks on the bound derivation.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — The Cost of Exploration**

- ✅ **Cost of Exploration = Cumulative Regret** $R(T) = T \mu^* - \sum_{t=1}^{T} r_t$.
- ✅ **Pseudo-Regret** $\bar{R}(T) = \sum_{a} \Delta_a \cdot \mathbb{E}[N_a(T)]$ — this is the *expected cost*.
- ✅ **Gap** $\Delta_a = \mu^* - \mu_a$ — the *marginal* cost of pulling arm $a$ once.
- ✅ **Pure Greedy** → $R(T) = \mathcal{O}(T)$ (linear, worst-case).
- ✅ **Constant $\varepsilon$-greedy** → $R(T) = \mathcal{O}(T)$ (still linear).
- ✅ **Decaying $\varepsilon$-greedy** ($\varepsilon_t = c/t$) → $R(T) = \mathcal{O}(\log T)$.
- ✅ **UCB1** → $R(T) = \mathcal{O}(\log T)$ with explicit bound $8 \sum \ln T / \Delta_a$.
- ✅ **Thompson Sampling** → $R(T) = \mathcal{O}(\log T)$, posterior-sampling-based.
- ✅ **Lai–Robbins Lower Bound** → $\Omega(\log T)$ — cannot be asymptotically beaten.
- ✅ **Hoeffding's Inequality** is the workhorse of UCB regret proofs.
- ✅ **Optimism in the face of uncertainty** is the UCB1 design principle.
- ✅ **Regret is unavoidable** in stochastic bandits — at least $\Omega(\log T)$ is mandatory.
- ✅ **Real-world applications:** A/B testing, clinical trials, recommender systems, ad placement, robotics.
- ✅ **LaTeX safety in tables:** Always use `\vert` or `\,\|\,` instead of `|` for vertical bars.
- ✅ **Optimal algorithm order:** Greedy < Random < $\varepsilon$-greedy (constant) < UCB1 ≈ Thompson Sampling.
- ✅ **Key insight for exams:** *Asymptotic regret growth rate is the primary metric for evaluating an exploration strategy's cost.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approaches to Solve MAB Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Approaches to Solve MAB Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) Problem — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) Problem (KTU Definition):** A classical sequential decision-making framework in Reinforcement Learning in which an agent must repeatedly choose among $k$ competing actions (called **arms** or **bandits**) to maximize the cumulative reward over $T$ rounds, while having no prior knowledge of the underlying reward distributions. Only the reward of the arm that is pulled is revealed at each step.

Mathematically, the MAB problem is defined as a tuple $(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{R})$ where:

- $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_k\}$ — the finite set of $k$ actions (arms).
- $\mathcal{R}^a(r) = \mathbb{P}(r \mid a)$ — an unknown probability distribution governing the reward $r$ when arm $a$ is pulled.
- Each arm $a$ has an unknown expected reward: $\mathbb{E}[R_a] = \mu_a = \sum_{r} r \cdot \mathcal{R}^a(r)$.

The goal of the learner is to maximize the **expected cumulative reward** or equivalently minimize the **expected cumulative regret** over $T$ plays.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Casino Slot Machine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "One-Armed Bandit" to "Many-Armed Bandit" Intuition:** A traditional slot machine in a casino is called a *one-armed bandit* because it has one lever (arm) that takes your money. Now imagine sitting in front of a row of $k = 10$ such slot machines, each with a different unknown payout rate. You have $T = 1000$ coins to play. You cannot see the payout of any machine in advance. **Which machine do you keep playing, and how often should you try the others to learn?**

This is exactly the MAB dilemma — and the **approaches to solve MAB environments** are the different *strategies* the player uses to balance **exploring** new machines and **exploiting** the best known one so far.

---

### 1.3 What are "Approaches to Solve MAB Environments"?

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Module 2):** Approaches to solve MAB environments refer to the family of action-selection strategies, exploration–exploitation mechanisms, and learning algorithms that an agent employs in a stationary (or non-stationary) bandit setting to minimize regret. They are classified into:
>
> 1. **Pure Exploration / Pure Exploitation** (baseline strategies)
> 2. **Greedy with Constant Exploration (Epsilon-Greedy)**
> 3. **Decaying Epsilon-Greedy (Epsilon-Decay)**
> 4. **Optimistic Initial Values (OIV)**
> 5. **Upper Confidence Bound (UCB1)**
> 6. **Thompson Sampling (Bayesian / Posterior Sampling)**
> 7. **Gradient Bandit Algorithms (Preference-based)**

The single, universal challenge that every approach tries to address is the **Exploration–Exploitation Dilemma**.

---

### 1.4 The Exploration–Exploitation Dilemma

| Term | Meaning in MAB |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Exploration** | Trying arms whose reward distributions are still uncertain, in order to gather more information. |
| **Exploitation** | Pulling the arm that is currently believed to be the best (greedy with respect to current knowledge) to maximize immediate reward. |
| **Regret** | The opportunity loss accumulated by not always playing the optimal arm: $R_T = T \mu^* - \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mu_{a_t}$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **good MAB algorithm** must guarantee that the *average regret per round* goes to zero as $T \to \infty$, i.e., **sub-linear regret**: $R_T = o(T)$. This is the central benchmark that KTU examiners use to grade comparative answers.

---

### 1.5 Standard Constants & Symbols Used Throughout

- $\epsilon$ — exploration probability in $\epsilon$-greedy.
- $N_t(a)$ — number of times arm $a$ has been pulled up to round $t$.
- $Q_t(a)$ — estimated value of arm $a$ at time $t$.
- $c$ — confidence-scaling constant in UCB.
- $H_t(a)$ — preference for arm $a$ at time $t$ (in Gradient Bandit).
- $\alpha$ — step-size in Gradient Bandit.
- $R_T$ — cumulative regret after $T$ steps.
- $\mu^*$ — mean reward of the optimal arm.</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Solution Approaches

Below is the structured logic of every major approach, broken down into *how* and *why* it works.

#### 2.1.1 Baseline Strategies

- **Pure Greedy (Exploitation only)**
  - *How:* At every step, pull $a_t = \arg\max_a Q_t(a)$.
  - *Why it fails:* Without exploration, the agent may lock into a sub-optimal arm forever once a noisy early reward is observed.
- **Pure Exploration**
  - *How:* Either try every arm equally (uniform random) or try each arm once then pick greedy.
  - *Why it fails:* Wastes pulls on known-bad arms.

#### 2.1.2 Epsilon-Greedy ($\epsilon$-greedy)

- *How:* With probability $\epsilon$, pull a random arm; with probability $1-\epsilon$, pull the greedy arm.
- *Why:* Continues to explore forever — guarantees information gain about every arm. However, the $\epsilon$-fraction of random pulls causes **linear regret** because exploration never decays.
- *Operational meaning:* Simple, robust, but **not asymptotically optimal**.

#### 2.1.3 Decaying Epsilon-Greedy

- *How:* Use a schedule $\epsilon_t = \min\{1, \epsilon_0 / t\}$ or $\epsilon_t = \epsilon_0^{t}$.
- *Why:* Encourages more exploration early when information is sparse, then shifts to exploitation. Achieves **logarithmic regret** under favorable conditions.
- *Why not always preferred:* The schedule is heuristic; choosing the decay rate is non-trivial.

#### 2.1.4 Optimistic Initial Values (OIV)

- *How:* Initialize $Q_1(a) = Q_0 \gg$ realistic maximum for every arm.
- *Why:* Encourages natural exploration — the agent is "disappointed" by the first observed reward and is forced to try all arms at least once early on. Coupled with simple sample-average updates, it performs surprisingly well in *stationary* problems with bounded steps.
- *Limitation:* The exploration drive is finite — not principled for very long horizons.

#### 2.1.5 Upper Confidence Bound (UCB1)

- *How:* At step $t$, pick the arm maximizing:
$$a_t = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \left[ Q_t(a) + c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}} \right]$$
- *Why it works:* The second term is an **upper confidence bonus** that grows when an arm has been pulled rarely, forcing exploration of uncertain arms in a principled, mathematically justified way. UCB1 achieves the **theoretical logarithmic regret bound** $R_T \leq 8 \sum_{a: \mu_a < \mu^*} \frac{\ln T}{\Delta_a} + O(1)$ where $\Delta_a = \mu^* - \mu_a$.
- *Limitation:* Requires tuning $c$; assumes bounded rewards.

#### 2.1.6 Thompson Sampling (Posterior / Bayesian Sampling)

- *How:* Maintain a posterior distribution over each $\mu_a$. At step $t$, sample $\tilde{\mu}_a \sim P(\mu_a \mid \text{history})$ for every arm and play $a_t = \arg\max_a \tilde{\mu}_a$.
- *Why it works:* Naturally mixes exploration (high posterior variance) and exploitation (high posterior mean). For Bernoulli rewards, a Beta prior leads to Beta posteriors — tractable and elegant.
- *Why preferred in practice:* Empirically outperforms UCB1 in many finite-horizon benchmarks; trivially parallelizable.

#### 2.1.7 Gradient Bandit Algorithm

- *How:* Maintain a numerical preference $H_t(a)$ for each arm. The probability of selecting arm $a$ is given by a **soft-max**:
$$P(a) = \pi_t(a) = \frac{e^{H_t(a)}}{\sum_{b} e^{H_t(b)}}$$
- Update rule with **baseline** $\bar{R}_t$:
$$H_{t+1}(a) = H_t(a) + \alpha \left( R_t - \bar{R}_t \right) \left( \mathbb{1}_{a = a_t} - \pi_t(a) \right)$$
- *Why it works:* Arms with above-average reward have their preference increased; below-average arms have theirs decreased. Achieves logarithmic regret in stationary problems.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **must-memorize formula sheet** for Module 2 of PECMT745. KTU questions frequently ask students to *write the action-selection rule* of any one approach.

| **Approach** | **Action-Selection Rule / Update Equation** | **Key Hyper-parameter** | **Regret Behavior** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pure Greedy | $a_t = \arg\max_a Q_t(a)$ | None | Linear (worst) |
| $\epsilon$-Greedy | $a_t = \text{random}$ w.p. $\epsilon$; else $\arg\max Q_t(a)$ | $\epsilon \in [0,1]$ | Linear (constant $\epsilon$) |
| Decaying $\epsilon$-Greedy | $\epsilon_t = \min\{1, \epsilon_0 / t\}$ or $\epsilon_0^t$ | $\epsilon_0$ | Logarithmic (heuristic) |
| Optimistic Initial Values | $Q_1(a) = Q_0$, then sample-average update | $Q_0$ | Finite-horizon good |
| UCB1 | $a_t = \arg\max_a \left[ Q_t(a) + c \sqrt{\ln t \,/\, N_t(a)} \right]$ | $c > 0$ | $R_T = O(\ln T)$ |
| Thompson Sampling | $a_t = \arg\max_a \tilde{\mu}_a$ where $\tilde{\mu}_a \sim \text{Posterior}_a$ | Prior | $R_T = O(\ln T)$ |
| Gradient Bandit | $\pi_t(a) = \frac{e^{H_t(a)}}{\sum_b e^{H_t(b)}}$ | $\alpha$ | $O(\ln T)$ |

**Auxiliary identities used in proofs:**

- Sample-average value update: $\,Q_{t+1}(a) = Q_t(a) + \frac{1}{N_t(a)} \left( R_t - Q_t(a) \right)$
- Incremental update with step-size $\alpha$: $\,Q_{t+1}(a) = Q_t(a) + \alpha \left( R_t - Q_t(a) \right)$
- Cumulative regret: $\,R_T = T \mu^* - \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathbb{E}[\mu_{a_t}] = \sum_{a} \Delta_a \cdot \mathbb{E}[N_T(a)]$
- Optimality gap: $\,\Delta_a = \mu^* - \mu_a$

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sample-Average Update (Foundation of all Sample-Based MAB Methods)

We want an online rule that, after $N$ observations of arm $a$ with rewards $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_N$, gives us

$$Q_N(a) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i$$

**Step 1: Write the new estimate in terms of the old estimate and the new sample.**

$$Q_{N+1}(a) \;=\; \frac{1}{N+1} \sum_{i=1}^{N+1} R_i \;=\; \frac{1}{N+1} \left( R_{N+1} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i \right)$$

**Step 2: Express the inner sum using $Q_N(a)$.**

$$Q_{N+1}(a) \;=\; \frac{1}{N+1} \left( R_{N+1} + N \cdot Q_N(a) \right)$$

**Step 3: Split the denominator to separate old and new contributions.**

$$Q_{N+1}(a) \;=\; \frac{N}{N+1} Q_N(a) \;+\; \frac{1}{N+1} R_{N+1}$$

**Step 4: Subtract and add $Q_N(a)$ to expose the *error* term.**

$$Q_{N+1}(a) \;=\; Q_N(a) \;-\; \frac{1}{N+1} Q_N(a) \;+\; \frac{1}{N+1} R_{N+1}$$

**Step 5: Group the last two terms.**

$$Q_{N+1}(a) \;=\; Q_N(a) \;+\; \frac{1}{N+1} \bigl( R_{N+1} - Q_N(a) \bigr)$$

This is the canonical **incremental update form**. Letting $N_t(a) = N$ and $R_t = R_{N+1}$ we obtain the formula listed in the cheat sheet.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the UCB1 Action-Selection Rule from Hoeffding's Inequality

> [!NOTE]
> UCB1 is the *theoretically* most justified MAB approach that KTU loves to ask. Memorize this derivation.

**Step 1: Set the requirement.** We want an arm-selection rule such that the true value $\mu_a$ lies below the upper bound $Q_t(a) + U_t(a)$ with high probability.

**Step 2: Apply Hoeffding's inequality** for bounded independent random variables $X_i \in [0,1]$:

$$P\bigl( \vert \hat{\mu} - \mu \vert \geq \epsilon \bigr) \;\leq\; 2 e^{-2 N \epsilon^{2}}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $\epsilon$** so that the bound equals $t^{-4}$ (a polynomially small probability that ensures the *union bound* across $T$ steps succeeds):

$$2 e^{-2 N \epsilon^{2}} \;=\; t^{-4} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; e^{-2 N \epsilon^{2}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} t^{-4}$$

**Step 4: Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$-2 N \epsilon^{2} \;=\; -\ln 2 \;-\; 4 \ln t$$

**Step 5: Solve for $\epsilon$.**

$$\epsilon \;=\; \sqrt{ \frac{\ln 2 + 4 \ln t}{2 N} } \;\approx\; \sqrt{ \frac{2 \ln t}{N} }$$

**Step 6: Replace the generic constants with the tunable parameter $c$** and add the resulting bound to the empirical mean estimate:

$$a_t \;=\; \arg\max_{a} \left[ \, Q_t(a) \;+\; c \sqrt{ \frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)} } \, \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The denominator $N_t(a)$ ensures that the confidence bonus *shrinks* for arms already played many times, while the numerator $\ln t$ *grows* with total time, guaranteeing that under-played arms eventually get selected.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Approaches

| **Criterion** | $\epsilon$-Greedy | UCB1 | Thompson Sampling | Gradient Bandit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Information used | Only sample mean | Sample mean + counts | Full posterior | Preferences |
| Optimal in worst case | No | Yes (logarithmic) | Yes (logarithmic) | Yes (logarithmic) |
| Parameter sensitivity | Moderate ($\epsilon$) | Moderate ($c$) | Low (only prior) | Moderate ($\alpha$) |
| Handles non-stationarity | Yes (with adaptive $\epsilon$) | Limited (UCB variants needed) | Yes (with forgetful prior) | Yes (with averaging) |
| Computational cost | $O(1)$ per step | $O(k)$ per step | $O(k \cdot \text{sample})$ per step | $O(k)$ per step |
| Best for | Pedagogical baseline | Worst-case guarantees | Empirical performance, finite horizon | Continuous action probabilities |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Regret Calculation

Suppose $k = 3$ arms with true means $\mu_1 = 0.2$, $\mu_2 = 0.5$, $\mu_3 = 0.4$. The optimal arm is $a^* = 2$ with $\mu^* = 0.5$.

**Step 1:** Compute the optimality gaps $\Delta_a = \mu^* - \mu_a$:

$$\Delta_1 = 0.5 - 0.2 = 0.3, \quad \Delta_2 = 0, \quad \Delta_3 = 0.5 - 0.4 = 0.1$$

**Step 2:** Suppose an algorithm played arm 1 for $N_T(1) = 50$ rounds and arm 3 for $N_T(3) = 30$ rounds (out of $T = 100$).

**Step 3:** Cumulative regret formula:

$$R_T \;=\; \sum_{a : \mu_a < \mu^*} \Delta_a \cdot \mathbb{E}[N_T(a)]$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the numbers.

$$R_T \;=\; (0.3)(50) \;+\; (0.1)(30) \;=\; 15 \;+\; 3 \;=\; 18$$

**Step 5:** The expected average reward obtained is $T \mu^* - R_T = 100 \times 0.5 - 18 = 32$, vs. the best possible $50$ if we always played $a_2$. The loss is $18$.

This is the form KTU examiners expect when they ask for a **regret calculation**.</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Which MAB Approach to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Need to solve an MAB problem]
    start --> q1{Do you have a prior over reward distributions?}
    q1 -- Yes --> bayes[Use Thompson Sampling]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is worst case theoretical guarantee critical?}
    q2 -- Yes --> ucb[Use UCB1]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Is the horizon T small or finite?}
    q3 -- Yes --> ts2[Thompson Sampling is empirically best]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Non stationary rewards?}
    q4 -- Yes --> dec[Use Decaying Epsilon Greedy or discounted UCB]
    q4 -- No --> grad[Use Gradient Bandit with baseline]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bandit Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    envA[Environment with k Arms] --> rewA[Reward Sampler for chosen arm]
    rewA --> updA[Value Estimator Q sub t of a]
    updA --> selA[Action Selection Module]
    selA --> polA[Exploration Exploitation Policy]
    polA --> actA[Chosen Action a sub t]
    actA --> envA
    polA -.-> eps[Epsilon Schedule]
    polA -.-> ucbB[UCB Confidence Bonus]
    polA -.-> gradB[Softmax Preference H]
    polA -.-> tsB[Posterior Sampling]
    selA --> compA[Compare with current best estimate]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All MAB approaches, no matter how sophisticated, share the same *control loop*: **observe reward → update value estimate → choose next action**. The difference lies entirely in the *selection policy* and the *update rule*. This loop is what KTU examiners expect you to draw when asked to "explain how an MAB algorithm works."</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->
