A 3-armed bandit has $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, a_3\}$. The agent must learn which arm has the highest mean reward.

**Step 1: Initialize** $Q_0(a) = 0$, $N_0(a) = 0$ for all three arms. Choose $\varepsilon = 0.1$. (1 mark)

**Step 2: At each timestep $t$:**
- Generate $r \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$.
- If $r < 0.1$: pick a random arm (exploration).
- Else: pick $a_t = \arg\max_{a} Q_t(a)$ (exploitation). (1 mark)

**Step 3: Incremental Update Rule.** After observing $R_{t+1}$ for action $a_t$:

$$
Q_{t+1}(a_t) \;=\; Q_t(a_t) \;+\; \alpha_t \bigl[ R_{t+1} - Q_t(a_t) \bigr]
$$

For non-selected actions, $Q_{t+1}(a) = Q_t(a)$. (1 mark)

**Step 4: Equivalence to Sample-Average Estimator.** We must prove:

$$
Q_{N}(a) \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i
$$

*Base case:* After the first selection, $Q_1(a) = R_1$ by the update rule with $N=1$, so $Q_1 = \frac{1}{1} R_1$. ✓ (1 mark)

*Inductive step:* Assume the result holds for $N$ selections. On the $(N+1)$-th observation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{N+1}(a) &\;=\; Q_N(a) + \frac{1}{N+1}[R_{N+1} - Q_N(a)] \\[4pt]
&\;=\; \frac{N}{N+1}Q_N(a) + \frac{1}{N+1}R_{N+1} \\[4pt]
&\;=\; \frac{N}{N+1} \cdot \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i + \frac{1}{N+1}R_{N+1} \\[4pt]
&\;=\; \frac{1}{N+1}\sum_{i=1}^{N+1} R_i
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark)

*Conclusion:* By induction, the recursive update equals the sample mean for all $N$. ∎ (1 mark)

#### Part (b) — Simulation [7 Marks]

Initialize: $Q_0 = (0, 0, 0)$, $N_0 = (0, 0, 0)$, $\varepsilon = 0.1$.

We assume the random draws produced the following selected actions for the 5 steps: $a_1, a_2, a_1, a_2, a_3$ (interpreting each observed reward as belonging to the chosen arm).

| Step $t$ | Action $a_t$ | Reward $R_{t+1}$ | $Q_t(a_t)$ before | New $N_t$ | $Q_t$ after update |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_1$ | 0.6 | 0 | 1 | $0 + 1 \cdot (0.6 - 0) = 0.6$ |
| 2 | $a_2$ | 1.2 | 0 | 1 | $0 + 1 \cdot (1.2 - 0) = 1.2$ |
| 3 | $a_1$ | 0.7 | 0.6 | 2 | $0.6 + 0.5 \cdot (0.7 - 0.6) = 0.65$ |
| 4 | $a_2$ | 0.9 | 1.2 | 2 | $1.2 + 0.5 \cdot (0.9 - 1.2) = 1.05$ |
| 5 | $a_3$ | 0.5 | 0 | 1 | $0 + 1 \cdot (0.5 - 0) = 0.5$ |

**Final state after $T=5$:** $Q_5 = (0.65,\ 1.05,\ 0.5)$, $N_5 = (2, 2, 1)$. (3 marks for table, 2 marks for method explanation, 2 marks for final values and interpretation)

**Interpretation:** The greedy action would now be $a_2$ with $Q = 1.05$, consistent with its true mean $q_\star(2) = 1.0$. The estimates are reasonably close after only 5 steps, illustrating fast learning of relative arm rankings.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Derive the expected regret bound for a constant-$\varepsilon$ greedy policy on a $k$-armed bandit. Identify the optimal value of $\varepsilon$ that minimizes this bound and state the resulting asymptotic regret.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Compare the $\varepsilon$-greedy strategy with the Upper-Confidence-Bound (UCB) strategy and Thompson Sampling, focusing on (i) the principle of exploration, (ii) the form of the exploration bonus, and (iii) the asymptotic regret order.*

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Regret Bound Derivation [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Define Regret.** Cumulative regret over horizon $T$:

$$
\rho_T \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{T} \bigl[ q_\star(a^\star) - q_\star(a_t) \bigr] \;=\; \sum_{a \neq a^\star} \Delta_a \cdot \mathbb{E}[N_T(a)]
$$

where $\Delta_a = q_\star(a^\star) - q_\star(a) > 0$ for $a \neq a^\star$. (1 mark)

**Step 2: Bound the Expected Pulls of Suboptimal Arms.** For any non-optimal arm $a$, the expected number of selections decomposes as:

$$
\mathbb{E}[N_T(a)] \;\leq\; \underbrace{\varepsilon T}_{\text{exploration pulls}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathbb{P}\!\left[Q_t(a) \geq Q_t(a^\star)\right]}_{\text{exploitation pulls when lucky}}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 3: Apply Hoeffding's Inequality.** Under i.i.d. bounded rewards, the probability that the sample-mean estimate $Q_t(a)$ exceeds $Q_t(a^\star)$ by an amount $\Delta_a/2$ or more, after $n$ pulls of $a$, satisfies:

$$
\mathbb{P}\!\left[Q_t(a) \geq Q_t(a^\star)\right] \;\leq\; 2 \exp\!\left(-\frac{n \Delta_a^2}{2}\right)
$$

Summing the geometric series (after algebraic simplification), one obtains:

$$
\mathbb{E}[N_T(a)] \;\leq\; \varepsilon T \;+\; \frac{C \log T}{\Delta_a^2}
$$

for some universal constant $C$. (2 marks)

**Step 4: Total Regret.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[\rho_T] &\;\leq\; \sum_{a \neq a^\star} \Delta_a \left[ \varepsilon T + \frac{C \log T}{\Delta_a^2} \right] \\[4pt]
&\;=\; \varepsilon T \cdot L \;+\; C \log T \cdot \sum_{a \neq a^\star} \frac{1}{\Delta_a}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $L = \sum_{a \neq a^\star} \Delta_a$. (1 mark)

**Step 5: Optimize $\varepsilon$.** The bound has the form $A \varepsilon + B / \varepsilon$, minimized at:

$$
\varepsilon^\star \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{B}{A}} \;=\; \mathcal{O}\!\left( \sqrt{\frac{k \log T}{T}} \right)
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 6: Final Asymptotic Regret.** Substituting:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\rho_T^\star] \;=\; \mathcal{O}\!\left( \sqrt{k T \log T} \right)
$$

This is **sublinear** in $T$, so the average per-step regret vanishes. (1 mark)

#### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis [7 Marks]

| Aspect | $\varepsilon$-Greedy | UCB | Thompson Sampling |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Exploration Principle** | Fixed-probability uniform random exploration | Optimism-in-face-of-uncertainty: select arm with highest plausible value | Posterior sampling: sample from posterior, pick best sample |
| **(ii) Exploration Bonus** | None — uniform across all non-greedy actions | $c \sqrt{\frac{\log t}{N_t(a)}}$ — bonus decays with arm visits | Implicit — uncertainty captured by posterior variance |
| **(iii) Asymptotic Regret** | $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{k T \log T})$ (with tuned $\varepsilon$) | $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{k T \log T})$ (with optimal $c$) | $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{k T \log T})$ (Bayes-optimal constant) |
| **Parameter Sensitivity** | High — bad $\varepsilon$ causes linear regret | Moderate — depends on confidence $c$ | Low — priors absorb uncertainty |
| **Handles Unknown Priors** | Yes | Yes | Requires a prior |
| **Computational Cost** | $\mathcal{O}(k)$ per step | $\mathcal{O}(k)$ per step | $\mathcal{O}(k \cdot \text{sample})$ per step |

(2 marks for principle comparison, 2 marks for bonus/parameter form, 2 marks for regret order, 1 mark for synthesis)

**Synthesis:** All three strategies achieve the **same worst-case regret order** $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kT \log T})$ for bounded rewards, but they differ fundamentally in *how* uncertainty is represented. $\varepsilon$-greedy is the **simplest to implement and explain**, UCB is the most **principled frequentist approach**, and Thompson sampling is the **most empirically efficient** when a reasonable prior exists.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "uniform" in uniform random exploration.** Many students write "explore randomly" without specifying *uniform* over the action space. Always state "uniformly at random from $\mathcal{A}$." (–1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing the update step-size.** Use $\alpha_t = 1/N_t(a)$ for sample-average *only*; for non-stationary problems, use a **constant** $\alpha$ (exponential recency weighting). (–1 mark)
> 3. **Omitting the convergence assumption.** If a question asks for the GLIE proof, students often skip the **Robbins-Monro step-size conditions**. Without these, the proof is incomplete. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Misapplying the regret bound.** Confusing **expected regret** $\mathbb{E}[\rho_T]$ with **realized regret** $\rho_T$ — the bound is on the *expectation*. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Forgetting to state the optimal $\varepsilon$.** When asked for regret minimization, students often leave $\varepsilon$ symbolic. Always plug in $\varepsilon^\star$ to get the final $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kT \log T})$ rate. (–1 mark)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- **Epsilon-Greedy** is the simplest **exploration–exploitation balancing policy**: with probability $1 - \varepsilon$ pick the greedy action $\arg\max_a Q_t(a)$; with probability $\varepsilon$ pick uniformly at random.
- The **incremental update rule** is $Q_{t+1}(a) = Q_t(a) + \alpha_t [R_{t+1} - Q_t(a)]$; with sample-average step-size $\alpha_t = 1/N_t(a)$, it equals the arithmetic mean.
- The **exploration parameter $\varepsilon$** controls the exploration rate; typical values lie in $[0.01, 0.2]$, often **decayed** over time.
- The **regret bound** for constant $\varepsilon$ is $\mathbb{E}[\rho_T] = \mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kT \log T})$ after optimizing $\varepsilon^\star \sim \sqrt{k \log T / T}$ — **sublinear**, guaranteeing asymptotic optimality.
- **GLIE + Robbins-Monro conditions** are sufficient for the almost-sure convergence of $Q_t(a) \to q_\star(a)$.
- A key **limitation** is uniform random exploration: $\varepsilon$-greedy wastes effort on clearly suboptimal arms; UCB and Thompson sampling address this.
- Practical deployments include **recommendation engines, A/B testing, robotic control, clinical trials, and adaptive network routing**.
- For KTU exams, always state: (i) the **policy equation**, (ii) the **update rule with step-size**, (iii) the **convergence guarantee**, and (iv) the **regret bound** with optimized $\varepsilon$.
- Common confusion to avoid: $\varepsilon$-greedy is **not** an optimal strategy — it is **asymptotically optimal** only with a carefully chosen (and ideally decaying) $\varepsilon$; never claim it is "best in class."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decaying Epsilon-Greedy Strategy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decaying Epsilon-Greedy Strategy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Decaying Epsilon-Greedy Strategy** (also called the **Decaying ε-Greedy Policy**) is a refined variant of the classical **ε-Greedy exploration mechanism** used in **Multi-Armed Bandits (MAB)** and **Reinforcement Learning (RL)**. In this strategy, the exploration probability **ε** is **not held constant** across time-steps; instead, it is progressively **reduced (decayed)** as the agent gathers more experience and gains confidence in its value estimates.

Formally, at each time-step **t**, the agent selects an action **A_t** according to the policy:

$$
A_t = \begin{cases} \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} Q_t(a), & \text{with probability } 1 - \varepsilon_t \\ a \sim \text{Uniform}(\mathcal{A}), & \text{with probability } \varepsilon_t \end{cases}
$$

where **ε_t** is a **monotonically non-increasing function of t** governed by a chosen **decay schedule**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The decaying ε-greedy is fundamentally built upon the **Exploration–Exploitation Dilemma**, a core Module 2 topic. The decay schedule directly addresses the theoretical requirement that an optimal policy must **explore sufficiently** in early episodes and **exploit greedily** as knowledge converges.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just moved to a new city and you are looking for the **best restaurant** nearby.

- **Day 1 (High Exploration):** You randomly try *any* restaurant (ε ≈ 1). You have zero information, so random exploration is justified.
- **Week 1 (Balanced):** You mostly go to the restaurants you rated *4 stars or higher*, but once a week you still try a *random* new place (ε ≈ 0.3).
- **Month 6 (Low Exploration):** You have a clear favourite. You visit it **90% of the time**, but occasionally (ε ≈ 0.05) try a new place just in case a better one opened.

This gradual reduction in "trying random things" is exactly what **Decaying ε-Greedy** does — it allows the agent to be **reckless and curious initially**, and **conservative and confident** later, once enough statistical evidence has been collected.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundary Conditions

The strategy relies on two essential hyperparameters:
- **Initial Exploration Rate (ε₀):** Starting value, typically in the range **0.1 ≤ ε₀ ≤ 1.0**.
- **Decay Rate (λ or α):** Controls *how fast* exploration reduces, typically **0 < λ < 1** (exponential) or **0 < α ≤ 1** (polynomial).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A *constant* ε-greedy policy suffers from **linear regret** (cumulative loss grows unboundedly). The *decaying* variant achieves **logarithmic regret** under proper schedules, which is the theoretical benchmark for "good" exploration strategies.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

The decay schedule can be classified into **three principal families**, each with distinct convergence behaviour.

### 2.1.1 Linear Decay
$$
\varepsilon_t = \max\left(\varepsilon_{\min},\ \varepsilon_0 - \lambda \cdot t\right)
$$

- **How it works:** Exploration decreases by a *fixed amount* **λ** every step until it reaches a floor value **ε_min**.
- **Why use it:** Simple to implement and intuitive. However, it can decay *too quickly* for environments requiring long-term exploration.
- **Pitfall:** Once ε_min is reached, the agent stops exploring entirely → *premature exploitation*.

### 2.1.2 Exponential Decay (Most Common)
$$
\varepsilon_t = \varepsilon_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}
$$

- **How it works:** Exploration decays *multiplicatively*. After **1/λ** time-steps, exploration has dropped to about **37%** of its initial value.
- **Why use it:** Provides smooth, gradual decay; widely used in Deep RL libraries (Keras-RL, Stable Baselines).
- **Theoretical property:** Satisfies the **GLIE (Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration)** condition if ε → 0.

### 2.1.3 Polynomial / Inverse Decay (Theoretically Optimal)
$$
\varepsilon_t = \frac{\varepsilon_0}{t^{\alpha}}, \quad 0 < \alpha \leq 1
$$

- **How it works:** Decay slows down over time but **never reaches zero** mathematically.
- **Why use it:** Proven to achieve **logarithmic regret** in bandit settings when **α = 1/2** or **α = 1**.
- **Engineering use:** Preferred in theoretical analysis (Auer, Cesa-Bianchi & Fischer 2002-style proofs).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Element | Mathematical Form | Domain / Range | Convergence Guarantee |
|---|---|---|---|
| Epsilon-greedy action rule | $A_t = \arg\max_a Q_t(a)$ w.p. $1 - \varepsilon_t$ | $\varepsilon_t \in [0,1]$ | None (if $\varepsilon$ constant) |
| Linear Decay | $\varepsilon_t = \max(\varepsilon_{\min},\ \varepsilon_0 - \lambda t)$ | $0 \le t \le \varepsilon_0 / \lambda$ | Depends on $\varepsilon_{\min}$ |
| Exponential Decay | $\varepsilon_t = \varepsilon_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\forall t \ge 0$ | GLIE if $\lambda > 0$ |
| Inverse / Polynomial Decay | $\varepsilon_t = \varepsilon_0 / t^{\alpha}$ | $t \ge 1,\ 0 < \alpha \le 1$ | Logarithmic Regret |
| Half-life formula | $t_{1/2} = \ln(2) / \lambda$ | $t_{1/2} > 0$ | — |
| Cumulative Regret (avg.) | $R_T = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathbb{E}[\Delta_a]$ | — | $\mathcal{O}(\log T)$ optimal |
| GLIE Condition 1 | $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \varepsilon_t = \infty$ | Required | Ensures infinite exploration |
| GLIE Condition 2 | $\lim_{t \to \infty} \varepsilon_t = 0$ | Required | Ensures greedy limit |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Recommendation Systems (Netflix, YouTube):** Decay exploration as user-preference data accumulates, transitioning from "discovery" mode to "personalized" mode.
- **Autonomous Driving RL:** Initial aggressive exploration of risky maneuvers gradually suppressed as the policy converges to safe behaviour.
- **A/B Testing in Industry:** Corresponds to decreasing traffic allocation to experimental arms as statistical confidence builds.
- **Robotic Control:** Early random motor babbling slowly replaced by smooth, reward-maximising policies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** A good starting configuration for most RL tasks is **ε₀ = 1.0**, **ε_min = 0.01**, and **λ ≈ 0.001 to 0.01** (for exponential), tuned via the validation reward curve.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Regret Bound for Polynomial Decay

We derive why the choice **ε_t = ε₀ / t^α** yields **logarithmic regret**, a critical KTU-level understanding.

### 3.1.1 Setup — The Bandit Setting

Consider a **k-armed bandit** with reward distributions having means **μ₁, μ₂, …, μ_k**. Define the *optimal mean* as:

$$
\mu^* = \max_{a \in \{1,\dots,k\}} \mu_a
$$

Define the *suboptimality gap* for arm **a**:

$$
\Delta_a = \mu^* - \mu_a, \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta_a > 0 \text{ for suboptimal arms.}
$$

The **expected regret** up to horizon **T** is:

$$
R(T) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathbb{E}\left[ \mu^* - \mu_{A_t} \right] = \sum_{a \neq a^*} \Delta_a \cdot \mathbb{E}[N_a(T)]
$$

where **N_a(T)** is the expected number of times suboptimal arm *a* is pulled.

### 3.1.2 Probability Bound on Playing a Suboptimal Arm

A suboptimal arm *a* is chosen during *exploitation* only if (a) it has the highest empirical mean **Q̂_t(a)** despite its true mean being lower. By the **Hoeffding inequality**:

$$
\Pr\left( \hat{Q}_t(a) \ge \mu^* \right) \le \exp\left(-2 N_a(t) \cdot \Delta_a^2 \right)
$$

During *exploration*, arm *a* is chosen uniformly with probability **1/k**, contributing additional regret of **ε_t · Δ_a**.

### 3.1.3 Combining the Two Sources of Error

Putting both together, the expected pulls of suboptimal arm *a* up to horizon *T* satisfies:

$$
\mathbb{E}[N_a(T)] \le \frac{\log T}{2 \Delta_a^2} + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\varepsilon_t}{k}
$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**
- **Term 1** comes from solving the Hoeffding bound for the smallest **N** such that the probability of misidentification is ≤ 1/t → yields **N ≈ log(T) / (2Δ_a²)**.
- **Term 2** is the cumulative exploration penalty — at each step the agent wastes a fraction **ε_t** of its pulls on suboptimal arms.

### 3.1.4 Substituting ε_t = ε₀ / t^α

Inserting the polynomial decay:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\varepsilon_t}{k} = \frac{\varepsilon_0}{k} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{1}{t^{\alpha}}
$$

This is the **Generalised Harmonic Number** **H_T^(α)**, which behaves as:

$$
H_T^{(\alpha)} = \begin{cases} \mathcal{O}(T^{1-\alpha}) & \text{if } 0 < \alpha < 1 \\[4pt] \mathcal{O}(\log T) & \text{if } \alpha = 1 \end{cases}
$$

For **α = 1/2**, this gives **O(√T)** — too slow. For **α = 1**, we obtain:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\varepsilon_t}{k} = \mathcal{O}(\log T)
$$

### 3.1.5 Final Regret Bound

Combining all contributions:

$$
R(T) \le \sum_{a \neq a^*} \Delta_a \left( \frac{\log T}{2 \Delta_a^2} + \frac{\varepsilon_0 \log T}{k} \right) = \mathcal{O}(\log T)
$$

**Interpretation:** The decaying ε-greedy with **ε_t = ε₀/t** achieves *logarithmic regret*, matching the theoretical optimum for stochastic bandits.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Strategies

| Property | Constant ε | Linear Decay | Exponential Decay | Polynomial Decay |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Regret Bound | $\mathcal{O}(T)$ | Depends on $\varepsilon_{\min}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\log T)$ if GLIE | $\mathcal{O}(\log T)$ |
| GLIE Compliance | ✗ | ✗ (hits $\varepsilon_{\min}$) | ✓ | ✓ |
| Implementation Ease | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| Hyperparameter Sensitivity | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| Practical Use | Baseline only | Simple problems | Deep RL standard | Theoretical benchmarks |
| Asymptotic Exploration | Non-zero (bad) | Zero (premature) | Zero (good) | Zero (good) |
| Recommended α / λ | — | $\lambda \approx 10^{-3}$ | $\lambda \approx 10^{-3}$ | $\alpha = 1.0$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow of Decaying Epsilon-Greedy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start of Episode t]) --> updE[Compute epsilon_t from decay schedule]
    updE --> sampU[Sample uniform random number u from 0 1]
    sampU --> cmpCheck{u is less than epsilon_t}
    cmpCheck -- YES --> randAct[Select action uniformly at random from Action Space]
    cmpCheck -- NO --> greedyAct[Select greedy action argmax Q_t a]
    randAct --> execute[Execute action and observe reward R and next state]
    greedyAct --> execute
    execute --> updQ[Update Q estimate for the chosen action]
    updQ --> incT[Increment t to t plus 1]
    incT --> loopCheck{More episodes remain}
    loopCheck -- YES --> updE
    loopCheck -- NO --> stopA([Stop training and output final policy])
```

## 4.2 Decay Schedule Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Initial epsilon zero] --> branchL{Decay Schedule}
    branchL -- LINEAR --> linBox[Linear Decay Block epsilon t equals max epsilon min, epsilon zero minus lambda t]
    branchL -- EXPONENTIAL --> expBox[Exponential Decay Block epsilon t equals epsilon zero times e to power minus lambda t]
    branchL -- POLYNOMIAL --> polyBox[Polynomial Decay Block epsilon t equals epsilon zero divided by t to power alpha]
    linBox --> outL[Output epsilon t]
    expBox --> outL
    polyBox --> outL
    outL --> policyApply[Apply epsilon t to epsilon greedy policy]
    policyApply --> exploreOrExploit{With probability epsilon t explore else exploit}
    exploreOrExploit --> finalDecision[Final action selection]
```

## 4.3 Exploration Probability vs Time-Steps (Conceptual Sketch)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axisOrigin[Origin: time t equals zero, epsilon equals one] --> region1[Zone A: High Exploration: t between zero and one hundred, epsilon between one and point five]
    region1 --> region2[Zone B: Balanced: t between one hundred and one thousand, epsilon between point five and point one]
    region2 --> region3[Zone C: Low Exploration: t between one thousand and ten thousand, epsilon between point one and point zero one]
    region3 --> region4[Zone D: Stable Exploitation: t greater than ten thousand, epsilon approximately epsilon min]
    region4 --> axisEnd[Asymptote: epsilon approaches zero as t approaches infinity]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**Differentiate between constant ε-greedy and decaying ε-greedy strategies. State one advantage of the latter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Constant ε-greedy | Decaying ε-greedy |
|---|---|---|
| Exploration probability | Fixed across all time-steps | Decreases monotonically with time |
| Asymptotic behaviour | Maintains a constant exploration rate | ε_t → 0 as t → ∞ |
| Regret growth | Linear, $\mathcal{O}(T)$ | Logarithmic, $\mathcal{O}(\log T)$ |
| Practical use | Baseline only | Standard in modern RL |

**Advantage (1 mark):** Decaying ε-greedy converges to the **optimal greedy policy** asymptotically while still ensuring sufficient early exploration — eliminating the wasteful perpetual randomness of constant ε-greedy.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is the GLIE condition? How does decaying ε-greedy satisfy it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration (GLIE)** condition requires a policy to satisfy **two properties**:

1. **Infinite Exploration:** Every state-action pair must be visited infinitely often in the limit.
$$
\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \varepsilon_t = \infty
$$

2. **Greedy Limit:** The policy must eventually become greedy.
$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} \varepsilon_t = 0
$$

For exponential decay **ε_t = ε₀ e^(−λt)**, condition 2 is trivially satisfied. For polynomial decay **ε_t = ε₀ / t^α** with **α ≤ 1**, the harmonic series diverges, satisfying condition 1. Thus both schedules are **GLIE-compliant**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Exploration–Exploitation Dilemma** in reinforcement learning. Discuss why a constant ε-greedy policy fails to address it optimally. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **expected regret** of the **polynomial decaying ε-greedy** strategy **ε_t = ε₀ / t** for a k-armed stochastic bandit. Show that the regret grows only **logarithmically** with horizon T. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

The **Exploration–Exploitation Dilemma** is the fundamental tension an RL agent faces:

- **Exploitation:** Choose the action that currently has the highest estimated value — maximising immediate reward.
- **Exploration:** Choose a seemingly suboptimal action to gather information that may yield higher long-term reward.

**Why constant ε-greedy fails:**

A constant ε-greedy policy keeps a fixed fraction of time-steps wasted on random actions, regardless of how confident the agent becomes. This produces **linear regret**:

$$
R(T) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathbb{E}\left[\mu^* - \mu_{A_t}\right] = \mathcal{O}(T)
$$

The agent continues to "throw away" reward even after it has correctly identified the optimal arm. There is no mechanism to *stop* exploring as knowledge converges.

**Decaying ε-greedy** resolves this by letting ε → 0, so the policy asymptotically behaves like the greedy policy — eliminating the wasted exploration cost. (7 marks awarded for clear explanation + contrast + mathematical expression.)

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

Starting from the Hoeffding inequality bound derived in **Section 3.1.2**, the expected number of pulls of a suboptimal arm *a* is bounded by:

$$
\mathbb{E}[N_a(T)] \le \underbrace{\frac{\log T}{2 \Delta_a^2}}_{\text{estimation error}} + \underbrace{\sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\varepsilon_t}{k}}_{\text{exploration error}}
$$

Substituting **ε_t = ε₀ / t**:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\varepsilon_t}{k} = \frac{\varepsilon_0}{k} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{1}{t} = \frac{\varepsilon_0}{k} H_T
$$

where **H_T** is the *N-th harmonic number*. Using the well-known bound:

$$
H_T \le 1 + \ln T
$$

we obtain:

$$
\mathbb{E}[N_a(T)] \le \frac{\log T}{2 \Delta_a^2} + \frac{\varepsilon_0}{k}(1 + \log T)
$$

Combining across all **k − 1** suboptimal arms and multiplying by their respective gaps **Δ_a**:

$$
R(T) \le \sum_{a \neq a^*} \Delta_a \left[ \frac{\log T}{2 \Delta_a^2} + \frac{\varepsilon_0 (\log T + 1)}{k} \right]
$$

Simplifying:

$$
R(T) = \mathcal{O}(\log T) \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Conclusion:** The polynomial decay schedule achieves **logarithmic regret**, confirming it is *asymptotically optimal* up to constant factors. (7 marks awarded for correct derivation and final bound.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With a neat **Mermaid-style flowchart**, explain the working of the **decaying ε-greedy action selection algorithm**. Identify the two main hyperparameters and describe how each affects learning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **linear, exponential, and polynomial** decay schedules in terms of:
- (i) Mathematical form
- (ii) GLIE compliance
- (iii) Regret bound
- (iv) Practical recommendation in RL training. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

The **algorithm flow** (as drawn in **Section 4.1**) is:

1. **Initialise** Q(a) = 0 for all actions, set **ε = ε₀**, **t = 1**.
2. **Compute** **ε_t** using the chosen decay schedule (e.g., exponential).
3. **Sample** uniform random number **u ∈ [0, 1]**.
4. **Decision rule:**
   - If **u < ε_t** → select random action (exploration).
   - Else → select **argmax_a Q_t(a)** (exploitation).
5. **Execute** action, observe reward **r**.
6. **Update** Q-estimate for the chosen action.
7. **Increment** t; repeat until convergence.

**Two Key Hyperparameters:**

| Hyperparameter | Role | Effect of Increase |
|---|---|---|
| **ε₀** (Initial rate) | Sets *how much* exploration happens at the start | More early randomness, slower convergence |
| **λ / α** (Decay rate) | Controls *how fast* exploration reduces | Less exploration later, risk of premature convergence |

(7 marks — diagram + hyperparameter discussion.)

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

| Schedule | (i) Math Form | (ii) GLIE | (iii) Regret | (iv) Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linear | $\max(\varepsilon_{\min},\ \varepsilon_0 - \lambda t)$ | ✗ (truncated) | Linear if $\varepsilon_{\min} > 0$ | Toy problems only |
| Exponential | $\varepsilon_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | ✓ | $\mathcal{O}(\log T)$ | Deep RL standard |
| Polynomial | $\varepsilon_0 / t^{\alpha}$ | ✓ | $\mathcal{O}(\log T)$ | Theoretical benchmarks |

**Justifications:**
- **Linear:** Hits ε_min then stops — *not GLIE* because the infinite exploration sum is finite after truncation.
- **Exponential:** Smooth decay; the half-life **t_{1/2} = ln 2 / λ** makes tuning intuitive.
- **Polynomial:** Strongest theoretical guarantees; recommended in literature but rarely used in production code due to the integer-t requirement.

(7 marks — full comparison with reasoning.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *constant* ε-greedy with *decaying* ε-greedy in regret bounds — constant gives **O(T)**, decaying gives **O(log T)**.
> - When asked for "decay schedule," always mention both the **mathematical form** *and* the **GLIE compliance** — half the marks are lost if GLIE is skipped.
> - In derivations, **state the Hoeffding bound explicitly** before using it; the examiner expects the inequality, not just the result.
> - Avoid mixing up **α** (polynomial exponent) with **λ** (exponential rate) — they are *different hyperparameters* controlling *different families* of decay.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core idea:** ε_t is *time-dependent* and *monotonically decreasing*, unlike the constant ε-greedy baseline.
- **Three canonical schedules:** Linear, Exponential, Polynomial — each with its own pros and cons.
- **GLIE conditions:** (1) $\sum \varepsilon_t = \infty$ and (2) $\varepsilon_t \to 0$ — together guarantee convergence to the optimal greedy policy.
- **Regret bound:** Polynomial decay achieves **O(log T)** regret — the theoretical best for stochastic bandits.
- **Half-life tuning:** For exponential decay, the half-life **t_{1/2} = ln 2 / λ** is the most intuitive knob.
- **Initial value ε₀:** Typically starts near **1.0** for uninformed agents and is reduced via decay.
- **Floor value ε_min:** Common in linear decay to prevent *premature convergence*; not needed for exponential/polynomial schedules.
- **Practical recommendation:** Use **exponential decay** with **ε₀ = 1.0**, **ε_min = 0.01**, **λ ∈ [10⁻⁴, 10⁻²]** for most RL experiments.
- **Connection to UCB:** Decaying ε-greedy with **ε_t = O(1/t)** is conceptually similar in performance to **Upper Confidence Bound (UCB1)** — both achieve logarithmic regret.
- **Exam tip:** When asked to "compare" strategies, always include a **regret bound + GLIE compliance + practical use** triplet — this is exactly how KTU examiners structure their marking schemes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimistic Initialization Strategy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Optimistic Initialization Strategy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimistic Initialization Strategy (Optimistic Initial Values Technique):** A heuristic method in Reinforcement Learning (RL) where the action-value function estimates, denoted as $Q(s,a)$, are initialized to an artificially high (optimistic) value — typically greater than or equal to the maximum achievable expected return $R_{\max}$ — rather than the conventional zero or small random values. This deliberate over-estimation creates an inherent drive for **systematic exploration** in stationary environments, since any untried action is expected to disappoint the agent relative to its initial belief.

The strategy is grounded in the principle of **"Optimism in the Face of Uncertainty"**, which states that an agent should prefer actions whose returns are *believed* to be high until proven otherwise through empirical interaction with the environment.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "New Restaurant in Town" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are a food blogger visiting a new city. Your colleague hands you a list of 10 unvisited restaurants and tells you, *"I've heard ALL of these are Michelin-star level amazing — absolutely 5-star experiences."* This is the **optimistic initialization** — every restaurant starts with a maximum rating of 5 stars in your mind.
>
> Now, your natural behavior changes: you are *motivated* to visit every restaurant quickly to **test the claim**. The moment you visit the first one and it turns out to be just a 3-star diner, your belief drops. This drop is what drives you to keep checking the others — *because they are still optimistically rated 5 in your mind*.
>
> Eventually, after visiting them all, your ratings converge to the truth. Restaurants never visited will remain at "5 stars" (artificially inflated). The optimism is what *forced* the exploration.

In RL terms: the **agent (you)**, the **restaurants (actions)**, the **visits (state-action interactions)**, and the **true ratings (actual Q-values)**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $Q(s,a)$ | Estimated action-value (expected return) of taking action $a$ in state $s$ |
| $Q_0$ | Initial optimistic value assigned to all state-action pairs |
| $R_{\max}$ | Maximum possible immediate reward in the environment |
| **Stationarity** | Property where the environment's transition/reward dynamics do not change over time |
| **Exploration Bonus** | Implicit drive caused by the gap between $Q_0$ and the true value $Q^*(s,a)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The Optimistic Initialization Strategy is classified by KTU under Module 2 alongside other **Exploration Strategies** like ε-greedy, Softmax, UCB, and Thompson Sampling. It is a *simple yet powerful* method particularly suited for **stationary Markov Decision Processes (MDPs)** with a **finite number of state-action pairs**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The optimistic initialization strategy works in **five logical phases**:

1. **Initialization Phase (t = 0):**
   The agent sets $Q(s,a) = Q_0$ for every state-action pair $(s,a)$ in the state-action space $\mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A}$, where $Q_0 \geq R_{\max}$ (or more conservatively, $Q_0$ is set to a value such that $Q_0 \geq \max_{s,a} Q^*(s,a)$).

2. **Greedy Action Selection (Early Phase):**
   Since all actions in a state are tied at $Q_0$, **ties are broken arbitrarily** (often uniformly at random). The agent effectively explores *randomly* among all actions in the early stages, because the argmax cannot distinguish between them.

3. **Disappointment Phase:**
   Upon taking an action $a$ in state $s$ and observing reward $R$, the Q-value is updated using the standard update rule. Since the actual reward is *less* than $Q_0$, the value of $Q(s,a)$ **drops significantly**.

4. **Comparative Update Phase:**
   Other actions that remain unexplored still hold the value $Q_0$. Therefore, on the next decision step, the agent will naturally prefer those unexplored actions (since they are still "optimistic" and not yet disproved).

5. **Convergence Phase:**
   After every state-action pair has been visited sufficiently many times, the optimistic bias is washed out, and $Q(s,a) \to Q^*(s,a)$ as $t \to \infty$.

## 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How'

- **Why does it work?**
  The over-estimated value creates a *hypothetical* expected return that reality cannot match (in most cases). Each visit to an action provides a *negative* update relative to the prior belief, signaling to the agent that *other untested actions are still potentially better*. This is mathematically equivalent to providing an **exploration bonus** without explicitly computing one.

- **How is exploration achieved?**
  - Initially, all actions are equally "best" → **forced uniform exploration** (in expectation).
  - As actions are tried, only the tried ones lose their optimistic value.
  - The greedy policy keeps choosing the *least-explored* actions, ensuring **systematic coverage**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constraints for KTU Board Exams:** Avoid using the pipe `|` character in mathematical expressions inside markdown tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q(s,a) = Q_0 \quad \forall (s,a) \in \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A}$ | Optimistic initialization step | $Q_0 \geq R_{\max}$ for guaranteed exploration |
| 2 | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \left[ R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) \right]$ | Standard Q-Learning update rule (used after optimistic init) | $\alpha \in (0,1]$ is the learning rate; $\gamma \in [0,1)$ is the discount factor |
| 3 | $\mathbb{E}[Q(s,a)] = Q_0 \cdot (1-\alpha)^N + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha(1-\alpha)^{N-i} R_i$ | Expected value of Q after $N$ updates with samples $R_i$ | Shows how optimism decays with visits |
| 4 | $\pi(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q(s,a)$ | Greedy action selection (no ε-noise needed in early phase) | Ties broken uniformly at random |
| 5 | $R_{\max} = \max_{(s,a)} \mathbb{E}[R \mid s, a]$ | Maximum achievable expected reward | Used to set the floor for $Q_0$ |
| 6 | $\text{Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) analogue: } U(s,a) = Q(s,a) + \frac{c}{\sqrt{N(s,a)}}$ | Conceptual parallel — UCB is the *bounded-rational* generalization of optimistic init | $N(s,a)$ = visit count |
| 7 | Total Exploration Trigger Condition: $Q_0 - R_{\max} > 0$ | Condition for the optimism to be non-trivial | Strict inequality required |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics (Locomotion Tasks):** Initializing Q-values optimistically in robot arm manipulation tasks encourages the controller to try novel joint configurations before settling on a locally optimal but globally suboptimal policy.
- **Recommendation Systems:** Helps a recommender system test diverse products during cold-start phases.
- **Game Playing AI (e.g., Atari, Chess):** When training a Deep Q-Network (DQN) from scratch, optimistic initialization of the final layer can encourage the network to explore diverse strategies initially.
- **Autonomous Driving Decision Modules:** Ensures the vehicle tests less-frequented maneuvers (e.g., lane changes) before committing to a conservative path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation to remember for KTU exams:** Optimistic Initialization is **NOT suitable for non-stationary environments**, because the optimism never fades and the agent continues to "explore" actions that were once optimal but are no longer. In such cases, **ε-greedy with decay** or **UCB** is preferred.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Decay of Optimistic Bias with Repeated Visits

We want to demonstrate mathematically how the optimistic value $Q_0$ gets washed out as a particular state-action pair $(s,a)$ is visited multiple times.

### Step 1 — Start with the Standard Q-Learning Update

$$
Q_{t+1}(s,a) = Q_t(s,a) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_t(s',a') - Q_t(s,a) \right]
$$

### Step 2 — Simplify by Assuming a Terminal State (γ = 0 Case)

For a terminal state (or when we set $\gamma = 0$ to focus on immediate rewards):

$$
Q_{t+1}(s,a) = Q_t(s,a) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} - Q_t(s,a) \right] = (1-\alpha) Q_t(s,a) + \alpha R_{t+1}
$$

This shows the new estimate is a *convex combination* of the old estimate and the observed reward.

### Step 3 — Expand Recursively for $N$ Visits

For visit $1$:
$$
Q_1(s,a) = (1-\alpha) Q_0 + \alpha R_1
$$

For visit $2$:
$$
Q_2(s,a) = (1-\alpha) Q_1 + \alpha R_2 = (1-\alpha)^2 Q_0 + \alpha(1-\alpha) R_1 + \alpha R_2
$$

Continuing this pattern recursively for $N$ visits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_N(s,a) &= (1-\alpha)^N Q_0 + \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{N} (1-\alpha)^{N-i} R_i \\
&= (1-\alpha)^N Q_0 + \alpha \sum_{j=0}^{N-1} (1-\alpha)^{j} R_{N-j}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Each past reward is weighted by a geometric decay $\alpha(1-\alpha)^{N-i}$, and the initial optimism is weighted by $(1-\alpha)^N$.

### Step 4 — Compute the Limit as $N \to \infty$

Since $\vert 1-\alpha \vert < 1$ for $\alpha \in (0,1]$, the first term vanishes:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} (1-\alpha)^N Q_0 = 0
$$

The sum converges to the *true expected reward* (assuming ergodicity):

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} Q_N(s,a) = \mathbb{E}[R \mid s,a] = Q^*(s,a)
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret the Result

The optimistic bias decays **exponentially** with visit count. The number of visits required to reduce the bias to less than $\epsilon$ is:

$$
N_{\epsilon} \geq \frac{\ln\left(\frac{Q_0 - Q^*}{\epsilon}\right)}{-\ln(1-\alpha)} = \frac{\ln\left(\frac{Q_0 - Q^*}{\epsilon}\right)}{\ln\left(\frac{1}{1-\alpha}\right)}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Higher learning rate $\alpha$ → faster decay. Higher initial optimism $Q_0$ → more visits required. This is the **analytical proof** that optimistic initialization is a *finite-time* exploration strategy.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Exploration Probability in the First Episode

Assume $Q(s,a) = Q_0$ for all $a \in \mathcal{A}(s)$ at time $t=0$. The greedy policy $\pi(s) = \arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ cannot uniquely identify the best action because all are tied.

### Step 1 — Ties Broken Uniformly

If the action space has cardinality $\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert = k$, the probability of choosing any specific action $a_i$ is:

$$
P(\pi(s) = a_i) = \frac{1}{k} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}
$$

### Step 2 — Expected Number of Episodes for First Visit to All Actions

In a state with $k$ actions, the expected number of episodes (greedy selections) to visit all $k$ actions at least once is the **coupon collector problem**:

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_k] = k \cdot H_k = k \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{1}{i}
$$

where $H_k$ is the $k$-th harmonic number.

### Step 3 — Asymptotic Approximation

Using $H_k \approx \ln(k) + \gamma_E$ (where $\gamma_E \approx 0.5772$ is the Euler-Mascheroni constant):

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_k] \approx k \ln(k) + \gamma_E k
$$

> **Conclusion:** The optimistic strategy ensures that *every action is tried* in approximately $O(k \log k)$ episodes in expectation, providing a worst-case guarantee on exploration.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Method | Exploration Mechanism | Stationary? | Hyperparameter Burden | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Optimistic Initialization** | Initial value bias | **Stationary only** | 1 ($Q_0$) | **High** |
| **ε-greedy** | Random action with prob ε | Both | 1 (ε) | **High** |
| **Decaying ε-greedy** | ε decays over time | Both | 2 (ε₀, decay rate) | **High** |
| **Softmax / Boltzmann** | Probability-weighted | Both | 1 (τ) | Medium |
| **UCB** | Confidence bonus $\propto 1/\sqrt{N}$ | Stationary | 1 (c) | **High** |
| **Thompson Sampling** | Posterior sampling | Both | 0 (Bayesian) | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Optimistic Initialization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Environment MDP Definition] --> B[Initialize Q-Table with Q0 greater than Rmax]
    B --> C[For each Episode e from 1 to E]
    C --> D[Observe current state s]
    D --> E{Is Q s a all equal to Q0?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Tie-Break Uniformly Random Action selection]
    E -- No --> G[Greedy Action selection argmax a Q s a]
    F --> H[Execute Action a in Environment]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Observe Reward R and Next State sprime]
    I --> J[Apply Q-Learning Update Rule]
    J --> K{Convergence Check Q s a minus Q star s a less than epsilon}
    K -- No --> D
    K -- Yes --> L[Output Optimal Policy pi star]
```

## 4.2 State-Transition Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[State s: All Q s a equal Q0] --> S1[Action a1 selected randomly]
    S1 --> S2[Reward R1 observed less than Q0]
    S2 --> S3[Q s a1 updated downward]
    S3 --> S4[Next decision: a2 still at Q0]
    S4 --> S5[Q s a2 dominant over Q s a1]
    S5 --> S6[All actions visited once]
    S6 --> S7[Q values converge to Q star]
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic Tree for Strategy Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Choose Exploration Strategy] --> B0{Is Environment Stationary?}
    B0 -- Yes --> C0{Known Reward Bound?}
    C0 -- Yes --> D0[Use Optimistic Initialization]
    C0 -- No --> E0[Use UCB or Thompson Sampling]
    B0 -- No --> F0[Use Decaying epsilon greedy or Adaptive methods]
    D0 --> G0[Set Q0 to estimated Rmax]
    E0 --> H0[Compute upper confidence bound per action]
    F0 --> I0[Decay epsilon over episodes]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Bias Decay Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X0[Visit Count N equals 0] --> X1[Bias equals Q0 minus Q star]
    X1 --> X2[Visit Count N equals 10]
    X2 --> X3[Bias equals 0 point 9 to the power of 10 times Q0 minus Q star]
    X3 --> X4[Visit Count N equals 50]
    X4 --> X5[Bias approximately 0 point 005 times Q0 minus Q star]
    X5 --> X6[Visit Count N equals 100]
    X6 --> X7[Bias approximately negligible]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers in the above diagrams are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `S0`, `B0`) to comply with the Mermaid parser. Labels use clean uppercase text without markdown formatting to prevent rendering errors.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Optimistic Initialization Strategy in Reinforcement Learning. Why is it categorized as an exploration technique? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Optimistic Initialization is a strategy where the Q-values $Q(s,a)$ for all state-action pairs are initialized to a value $Q_0$ that is *higher* than the maximum achievable reward $R_{\max}$. This creates an artificial over-estimation of the action values at the start of learning.
>
> **Exploration Justification (1 mark):** It is an exploration technique because, with all Q-values initialized identically high, the greedy policy is forced to break ties randomly among actions in the early phase. As actions are tried, their Q-values drop, while untried actions remain optimistically high — naturally pulling the agent toward unexplored actions until all have been adequately tested.

---

### Question 2: State any **two** conditions under which Optimistic Initialization **fails** to provide adequate exploration. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Condition 1 (1.5 marks):** In **non-stationary environments**, where reward functions or transition probabilities change over time, the optimistic values do not adapt. The agent continues to chase the *original* optimistic actions even when their true values have degraded.
>
> **Condition 2 (1.5 marks):** When the **state-action space is very large or continuous**, optimistic initialization becomes infeasible because (a) the memory required to store all $Q(s,a)$ becomes prohibitive, and (b) uniform tie-breaking over a huge action set does not guarantee meaningful exploration of the relevant subspace.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: Comprehensive Analysis `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **working mechanism** of the Optimistic Initialization Strategy. Use a mathematical formulation and a numerical example to demonstrate how the optimistic bias decays with multiple visits to a state-action pair.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Working Mechanism [3 marks]:**
   - **Step 1:** Initialize $Q(s,a) = Q_0$ for all $(s,a)$, where $Q_0 > R_{\max}$.
   - **Step 2:** In early episodes, all actions in a state are tied → tie-breaking forces uniform random exploration.
   - **Step 3:** After executing action $a$, observe reward $R < Q_0$. Apply the Q-learning update:
$$
Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \left[ R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) \right]
$$
   - **Step 4:** The value of $Q(s,a)$ drops. Other unexplored actions remain at $Q_0$, so they appear "better" to the greedy policy.
   - **Step 5:** Process repeats until every action has been visited and the bias has been empirically washed out.

2. **Numerical Example [4 marks]:**
   Let $Q_0 = 10$, $R_{\max} = 5$, $\alpha = 0.5$, $\gamma = 0$, and assume true reward $R = 4$ is sampled each visit.

   - **Visit 1:** $Q_1 = (1-0.5)(10) + (0.5)(4) = 5 + 2 = 7$
   - **Visit 2:** $Q_2 = (1-0.5)(7) + (0.5)(4) = 3.5 + 2 = 5.5$
   - **Visit 3:** $Q_3 = (1-0.5)(5.5) + (0.5)(4) = 2.75 + 2 = 4.75$
   - **Visit 4:** $Q_4 = (1-0.5)(4.75) + (0.5)(4) = 2.375 + 2 = 4.375$
   - **Visit 5:** $Q_5 \approx 4.1875$

   Observe the bias $Q_N - 4$ decays: $3, 1.5, 0.75, 0.375, 0.1875, \ldots$ — exponential decay by factor $0.5$ per visit, consistent with the derived formula $Q_N = (1-\alpha)^N Q_0 + \alpha \sum (1-\alpha)^{N-i} R_i$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **Optimistic Initialization**, **ε-greedy**, and **UCB** as exploration strategies. Present your answer in a structured tabular form with at least **five** comparison criteria, and discuss a real-world scenario where Optimistic Initialization would be **preferred** over the other two.

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Comparison Table [4 marks]:**

| Criterion | Optimistic Init | ε-greedy | UCB |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Initial value bias | Random action with prob ε | Bonus $\propto 1/\sqrt{N(s,a)}$ |
| Hyperparameters | $Q_0$ | ε | $c$ (confidence level) |
| Stationarity | Stationary only | Both | Stationary only |
| Computational Cost | Low | Low | Moderate (track visit counts) |
| Theoretical Guarantee | Finite-state MDP | None (asymptotic) | Logarithmic regret |
| Action Diversity | Tied initially → uniform | Random sampling | Bonus-driven |
| Tuning Sensitivity | Low (one parameter) | Medium | High ($c$ is critical) |

**Real-World Scenario [3 marks]:**
> **Grid World Robot Navigation:** A small 5×5 grid with 4 actions per state. The environment is **stationary** (no time-varying obstacles). The agent has 20 state-action pairs — a tractable table-based Q-learning setup. Here, setting $Q_0 = 100$ (well above any cumulative reward) makes optimistic initialization ideal: simple, no decay schedule to tune, and guaranteed systematic exploration of the 4 actions in each of the 25 states. ε-greedy would require tuning ε over time, and UCB would need visit-count tracking, adding unnecessary complexity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $Q_0$ with $R_{\max}$:** Many students write $Q_0 = R_{\max}$ instead of $Q_0 \geq R_{\max}$. Always use the **strict** inequality to ensure the optimism is non-trivial.
> 2. **Forgetting the discount factor:** When applying the update rule, students often drop the $\gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a')$ term. Always include the **Bellman target**.
> 3. **Claiming it works in non-stationary environments:** This is the most common error. Optimistic initialization is *strictly* for stationary MDPs.

---

### Question B: Alternative Comprehensive Question `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **closed-form expression** for the expected value of $Q(s,a)$ after $N$ visits, starting from an optimistic initialization $Q_0$. Show explicitly that the optimistic bias **decays exponentially** with the number of visits.

**Model Answer Outline (Full Derivation [7 marks]):**

Starting with the simplified Q-learning update (γ = 0 for clarity):

$$
Q_{t+1}(s,a) = (1-\alpha) Q_t(s,a) + \alpha R_{t+1}
$$

Unrolling the recursion over $N$ visits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1(s,a) &= (1-\alpha) Q_0 + \alpha R_1 \\
Q_2(s,a) &= (1-\alpha)^2 Q_0 + \alpha(1-\alpha) R_1 + \alpha R_2 \\
Q_3(s,a) &= (1-\alpha)^3 Q_0 + \alpha(1-\alpha)^2 R_1 + \alpha(1-\alpha) R_2 + \alpha R_3 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
Q_N(s,a) &= (1-\alpha)^N Q_0 + \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{N} (1-\alpha)^{N-i} R_i
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the expectation (assuming rewards are i.i.d. with $\mathbb{E}[R_i] = Q^*$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[Q_N(s,a)] &= (1-\alpha)^N Q_0 + \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{N} (1-\alpha)^{N-i} Q^* \\
&= (1-\alpha)^N Q_0 + Q^* \left[ 1 - (1-\alpha)^N \right] \\
&= Q^* + (Q_0 - Q^*)(1-\alpha)^N
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final result:** $\mathbb{E}[Q_N(s,a)] = Q^* + (Q_0 - Q^*)(1-\alpha)^N$

The bias term $(Q_0 - Q^*)(1-\alpha)^N$ decays **exponentially** with $N$. This proves the optimistic bias is **vanishing** in the limit, validating the strategy. **[7 marks — full marks awarded for correct derivation with intermediate steps shown]**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **limitations** of the Optimistic Initialization Strategy. Propose a **modified strategy** that addresses these limitations and justify your proposal mathematically.

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Limitations [3 marks]:**
1. **Non-stationarity problem:** Fixed $Q_0$ never adapts to environmental changes.
2. **No re-exploration:** Once all actions are visited and biases are washed out, the agent settles into a greedy policy. If new information arrives (e.g., a previously suboptimal action becomes optimal), there is no in-built mechanism to re-explore.
3. **Sensitive to $Q_0$ choice:** Too high → slow convergence; too low → insufficient exploration.
4. **Memory issue in large state spaces:** Table-based Q-learning becomes infeasible.

**Proposed Modification — Decaying Optimistic Initialization [4 marks]:**

Modify the initialization to incorporate a *time-decaying* optimism:

$$
Q_0(t) = R_{\max} + \frac{c}{t^{\beta}}
$$

where $t$ is the episode number, $c > 0$ is a scaling constant, and $\beta \in (0,1)$ controls the decay rate. This can be implemented as periodic **re-initialization** of rarely-visited state-action pairs:

$$
Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q_0(T_{\text{last visit}}) \quad \text{if } T_{\text{current}} - T_{\text{last visit}} > T_{\text{threshold}}
$$

**Mathematical Justification:** The decaying term $c/t^{\beta}$ ensures that (i) early episodes have strong exploration, (ii) later episodes gracefully transition to exploitation, and (iii) state-action pairs that haven't been visited for a long time regain their "optimistic" value, addressing the non-re-exploration problem.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes in Part (b):**
> 1. **Forgetting the i.i.d. assumption** when taking expectations — clarify that rewards are assumed independent and identically distributed for the bias-decay derivation.
> 2. **Confusing the discount factor γ with the learning rate α** in the final closed-form expression.
> 3. **Not justifying the modification** mathematically — proposing a fix without proof will cost you at least 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Optimistic Initialization Strategy**

- ✅ **Core idea:** Set $Q(s,a) = Q_0$ with $Q_0 \geq R_{\max}$ for all state-action pairs.
- ✅ **Why it explores:** Initial ties → uniform random tie-breaking → systematic coverage of actions.
- ✅ **Bias decay formula:** $\mathbb{E}[Q_N] = Q^* + (Q_0 - Q^*)(1-\alpha)^N$ — *exponential decay*.
- ✅ **Stationarity requirement:** Only works in stationary MDPs. **Do NOT** use in non-stationary problems.
- ✅ **Hyperparameter:** Only **one** — the optimistic value $Q_0$. Easier to tune than ε-greedy's ε.
- ✅ **Limitation 1:** No re-exploration mechanism for non-stationary environments.
- ✅ **Limitation 2:** Infeasible for large/continuous state-action spaces (table explosion).
- ✅ **Limitation 3:** Slow convergence if $Q_0$ is set too high.
- ✅ **Limitation 4:** Sensitive to choice of $R_{\max}$ — over-estimation must be meaningful but not excessive.
- ✅ **Connection to UCB:** Optimistic init is the *static* precursor to UCB's *dynamic* confidence-bonus exploration.
- ✅ **Coupon collector bound:** All actions visited in $O(k \log k)$ episodes where $k = \vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert$.
- ✅ **Update rule post-initialization:** Standard Q-learning update with Bellman target.
- ✅ **Practical tip:** In deep RL, this translates to *weight initialization* with optimistic bias in the value head of the network.
- ✅ **Comparison mnemonic:** **"O**pti**I**nit = **O**ver-**I**nflate → **E**xplore **F**irst, **E**xploit **L**ater."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strategic Exploration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strategic Exploration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Exploration** in Reinforcement Learning (RL) refers to the principled, mathematically-grounded design of an agent's behavior policy that intelligently balances the **exploration–exploitation trade-off** in order to efficiently learn the optimal action-value function $Q^*(s,a)$ and maximize long-term cumulative reward.

In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terms, strategic exploration encompasses the family of algorithms that go beyond simple heuristics (such as random $\varepsilon$-greedy action selection) and instead use **statistical reasoning**, **optimism under uncertainty**, or **Bayesian inference** to guide the agent's decisions. These methods are essential for guaranteeing **PAC (Probably Approximately Correct)** learning bounds, **$\tilde{O}(\sqrt{SAK})$ regret bounds**, and convergence to optimal policies in finite-state Markov Decision Processes (MDPs).

The fundamental mathematical object is the **regret** experienced by a learning policy $\pi$ over $T$ timesteps, defined as:

$$
R(T) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left( V^*(s_t) - Q^*(s_t, a_t) \right)
$$

A *strategic* exploration algorithm aims to drive $R(T) = o(T)$ (sub-linear regret), meaning average regret tends to zero as $T \to \infty$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Dilemma**
>
> Imagine you have just moved to a new city. You are hungry and want to discover the **best restaurant** nearby, but trying every new place costs time and money.
>
> - **Exploitation** = Going to your favorite known restaurant (safe, predictable reward).
> - **Exploration** = Trying a brand-new restaurant (risky, but might yield a much better experience).
>
> A **strategic explorer** does not flip a coin. Instead, she:
> 1. Keeps a *mental model* of how confident she is about each restaurant's quality (e.g., "I've tried Pizza Palace 5 times and it's been great — high confidence").
> 2. Identifies restaurants where her *uncertainty is high* (e.g., "I've only tried Sushi Spot once — could be amazing or terrible").
> 3. Occasionally tries the uncertain option because the **potential upside** justifies the risk.
>
> This is exactly the philosophy behind **UCB (optimism in the face of uncertainty)** and **Thompson Sampling (Bayesian optimism)** — the two pillars of strategic exploration.

## 1.3 The Exploration–Exploitation Trade-off

The core dilemma in RL is captured by the following tension:

| Aspect | Exploration | Exploitation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Goal** | Gather new information about the environment | Maximize immediate reward using known info |
| **Risk** | Short-term reward loss | Long-term suboptimality (stuck in local optima) |
| **Information value** | High — reduces uncertainty | Low — no new state-action knowledge |
| **Example** | Pulling a slot machine arm 10 times to estimate its mean | Always pulling the arm with the empirically best mean |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Naïve Random Exploration Fails:**
> Pure $\varepsilon$-greedy exploration does NOT scale efficiently. In a large MDP with $|S|$ states and $|A|$ actions, uniform random exploration requires $\mathcal{O}(\text{poly}(|S|, |A|))$ samples per state-action pair, leading to exponential horizon regret. Strategic exploration methods use **principled uncertainty quantification** to direct effort where it matters most.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Strategic Exploration

- **$\varepsilon$** (Epsilon): The exploration probability in $\varepsilon$-greedy. Decay schedule: $\varepsilon_t = \frac{1}{t}$ or $\varepsilon_t = \varepsilon_0 \cdot \alpha^t$.
- **$c$**: The UCB confidence scaling constant (typically $c = \sqrt{2}$).
- **$N_t(s,a)$**: Number of times action $a$ was taken in state $s$ up to time $t$.
- **$H$**: The horizon (episode length) — used in PAC-MDP bounds.
- **$\delta$**: Failure probability — bounds hold with probability $\ge 1 - \delta$.
- **$\varepsilon_{accuracy}$**: PAC-MDP approximation parameter.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Strategic Exploration Strategies

Strategic exploration algorithms are organized along two orthogonal axes:

1. **Action-Selection Mechanism**:
   - Optimism-based: UCB, UCB-V, R-MAX, E^3
   - Posterior-sampling / Bayesian: Thompson Sampling, PSRL
   - Probability-matching: Boltzmann/Gibbs softmax

2. **Information Source Used**:
   - Reward uncertainty only (bandit setting)
   - Reward + transition uncertainty (full MDP)

## 2.2 Multi-Armed Bandits (MAB) — The Foundation

The **Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB)** problem is the simplest setting for studying strategic exploration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal MAB Setup:**
> An agent faces $K$ arms. Each arm $k \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ has an unknown reward distribution with mean $\mu_k$. At each round $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$, the agent selects arm $a_t \in \{1, \dots, K\}$ and receives a stochastic reward $r_t \sim \mathcal{D}_k$. The objective is to minimize the **expected cumulative regret**:
> $$\mathbb{E}[R(T)] = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \Delta_k \cdot \mathbb{E}[N_T(k)]$$
> where $\Delta_k = \mu^* - \mu_k$ is the *suboptimality gap* of arm $k$ and $N_T(k)$ is the number of pulls of arm $k$ up to time $T$.

### 2.2.1 Strategy 1: $\varepsilon$-Greedy

Select the empirically best action with probability $1 - \varepsilon_t$, and a uniformly random action with probability $\varepsilon_t$.

$$
a_t = \begin{cases} \arg\max_a \hat{Q}_t(a) & \text{with probability } 1 - \varepsilon_t \\ \text{Uniform}(A) & \text{with probability } \varepsilon_t \end{cases}
$$

**Linear regret**: $\mathbb{E}[R(T)] = \mathcal{O}(T)$ in the worst case (suboptimal arms are pulled $\Theta(\varepsilon_t T)$ times). It is **NOT** strategic in the strict sense.

### 2.2.2 Strategy 2: Upper Confidence Bound (UCB1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **UCB1 Algorithm (Auer, Cesa-Bianchi, Fischer, 2002) — A foundational strategic exploration method.**

For each action $a$, maintain:
- $N_t(a)$ — number of times $a$ was selected
- $\hat{\mu}_t(a) = \frac{1}{N_t(a)} \sum_{s=1}^{t} r_s \mathbf{1}\{a_s = a\}$ — empirical mean reward

Compute the **upper confidence bound**:
$$
\text{UCB}_t(a) = \hat{\mu}_t(a) + c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}
$$

Select the action that maximizes this bound:
$$
a_t = \arg\max_{a \in A} \text{UCB}_t(a)
$$

The second term is the **exploration bonus**, which grows large for rarely-tried actions and shrinks for well-explored ones. The constant $c = \sqrt{2}$ provides **Hoeffding-style** confidence guarantees.

**Regret bound**: $\mathbb{E}[R(T)] \le \sum_{k:\Delta_k>0} \frac{8 \ln T}{\Delta_k} + \mathcal{O}\left(\frac{K}{\Delta_k^2}\right)$, which is **logarithmic in $T$** — a major improvement over $\varepsilon$-greedy.

### 2.2.3 Strategy 3: Thompson Sampling

> [!NOTE]
> **Thompson Sampling — A Bayesian strategy that samples from the posterior distribution of each action's value.**

For Bernoulli rewards, assume a Beta prior $\text{Beta}(\alpha_k, \beta_k)$ for each arm $k$. After observing $s_k$ successes and $f_k$ failures:
- Posterior: $\theta_k \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha_k + s_k, \beta_k + f_k)$

At each step, **sample** $\tilde{\theta}_k$ from each posterior, then act greedily:
$$
a_t = \arg\max_{k} \tilde{\theta}_k
$$

**Why it works**: Actions with high uncertainty (wide posteriors) are sampled from broadly, naturally encouraging exploration. Actions with high posterior mean and low variance are sampled close to their mean, encouraging exploitation.

**Regret bound**: $\mathbb{E}[R(T)] = \mathcal{O}\left(\sqrt{KT \ln T}\right)$.

## 2.3 Strategic Exploration in Full MDPs

In a finite MDP $\mathcal{M} = (S, A, P, R, \gamma)$, strategic exploration must account for **transition uncertainty** in addition to reward uncertainty.

### 2.3.1 R-MAX Algorithm (Brafman & Tennenholtz, 2002)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **R-MAX Principle**: A state-action pair $(s,a)$ that has been visited fewer than $m$ times is *optimistically* assumed to transition to a special "absorbing max-reward" state with reward $R_{\max}$. This makes the agent prefer to visit under-explored regions.

**R-MAX Algorithm Steps:**
1. Initialize $m = 1$ (or a function of $|S|, |A|, \gamma, \varepsilon, \delta$).
2. While some $(s,a)$ has $N(s,a) < m$:
   - Take action $a$ in state $s$.
   - Update the empirical model $\hat{P}, \hat{R}$.
3. Compute the optimal policy $\pi^*$ w.r.t. the current optimistic model.
4. Execute $\pi^*$ until another $(s,a)$ becomes under-explored.

**PAC-MDP Guarantee**: R-MAX is PAC-MDP — it requires only $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(\frac{SA \cdot \text{poly factors}}{\varepsilon^3 (1-\gamma)^6}\right)$ samples to find an $\varepsilon$-optimal policy with probability $1 - \delta$.

### 2.3.2 UCB Applied to MDPs (Jaksch, Ortner, Auer, 2010)

The UCB principle extends to MDPs by adding an exploration bonus to Q-values:
$$
Q_t(s,a) = \hat{Q}_t(s,a) + c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(s,a)}}
$$

where $\hat{Q}_t(s,a)$ is computed via value iteration on the empirical model. This gives **UCBVI** (Upper Confidence Bound Value Iteration) with regret $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(\sqrt{SAT}\right)$.

### 2.3.3 Posterior Sampling for RL (PSRL)

A Thompson-Sampling analog for full MDPs:
1. Maintain a posterior over MDP parameters $(P, R)$.
2. At each episode, **sample** $(\tilde{P}, \tilde{R})$ from the posterior.
3. Compute the optimal policy $\tilde{\pi}^*$ for the sampled MDP.
4. Execute $\tilde{\pi}^*$ for one episode.
5. Update the posterior with observed data.

**Bayesian regret bound**: $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(\sqrt{SAT}\right)$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet — Strategic Exploration**

| Algorithm | Action Selection Rule | Regret Bound | Key Hyperparameter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\varepsilon$-Greedy | $a_t = \arg\max_a \hat{Q}(a)$ w.p. $1-\varepsilon$ | $\mathcal{O}(T)$ (linear) | $\varepsilon_t$ decay schedule |
| UCB1 (Bandits) | $a_t = \arg\max_a \hat{\mu}(a) + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}$ | $\mathcal{O}\left(\sum_k \frac{\ln T}{\Delta_k}\right)$ | $c = \sqrt{2}$ (Hoeffding) |
| UCB-V (Variance-aware) | $a_t = \arg\max_a \hat{\mu}(a) + c\sqrt{\frac{V_a \ln t}{N_t(a)}} + c'\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}$ | $\mathcal{O}\left(\sqrt{KT}\right)$ | $c, c'$ |
| Thompson Sampling | $a_t = \arg\max_a \theta_a,\ \theta_a \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha_a,\beta_a)$ | $\mathcal{O}\left(\sqrt{KT \ln T}\right)$ | Prior $(\alpha, \beta)$ |
| R-MAX | Greedy on optimistic model ($R_{\max}$ bonus) | PAC-MDP, $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\text{poly})$ | $m$ (visit threshold) |
| UCBVI | VI with bonus $c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(s,a)}}$ | $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(\sqrt{SAT}\right)$ | $c$ |
| PSRL | VI on posterior sample | $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(\sqrt{SAT}\right)$ | Prior over $(P, R)$ |
| Boltzmann | $P(a) = \frac{e^{Q(a)/\tau}}{\sum_a e^{Q(a)/\tau}}$ | No formal bound | $\tau$ (temperature) |

**Critical Equations:**

**Hoeffding's Inequality** (basis of UCB confidence):
$$
P\left(\left\vert \hat{\mu} - \mu \right\vert \ge \epsilon\right) \le 2 \exp\left(-\frac{2 N \epsilon^2}{1}\right)
$$

**UCB Confidence Radius**:
$$
\text{radius}(N, t) = \sqrt{\frac{2 \ln t}{N}}
$$

**PAC-MDP Sample Complexity**:
$$
N_{\text{samples}} = \tilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(\frac{S A}{\varepsilon^2 (1-\gamma)^3} \ln\frac{SA}{\delta}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Recommendation systems** (Netflix, YouTube): UCB balances showing familiar content with discovering new user preferences.
> - **A/B testing in tech**: Thompson Sampling dynamically allocates traffic to better-performing variants.
> - **Autonomous driving**: Strategic exploration enables safe but informative testing of driving policies.
> - **Robotics**: R-MAX-style optimism guides the robot toward under-explored state-action regions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the UCB1 Regret Bound (Bandit Case)

> [!EXAM HINT]
> **This derivation is a classic KTU long-answer question worth 14 marks. Understand each step carefully.**

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a stochastic bandit with $K$ arms. Arm $k$ has true mean $\mu_k$ and the optimal arm has mean $\mu^* = \max_k \mu_k$. The suboptimality gap for arm $k$ is:
$$
\Delta_k = \mu^* - \mu_k
$$

**Objective**: Bound $\mathbb{E}[N_T(k)]$ — the expected number of times a suboptimal arm $k$ is pulled by UCB1.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Decompose regret by arm**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R(T) &= \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left(\mu^* - \mu_{a_t}\right) \\
&= \sum_{k=1}^{K} \Delta_k \cdot \mathbb{E}[N_T(k)]
\end{aligned}
$$

This separates the cumulative regret into a sum over each arm's contribution. The optimal arm $k^*$ contributes $\Delta_{k^*} = 0$.

**Step 2 — Upper bound on the count of suboptimal arm $k$**:

A suboptimal arm $k$ is pulled at time $t$ only if its UCB exceeds that of the optimal arm $k^*$:
$$
\hat{\mu}_t(k) + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(k)}} \ge \hat{\mu}_t(k^*) + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(k^*)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Event decomposition**: Define two "bad" events:
- **Event A**: $\hat{\mu}_t(k^*) < \mu^* - c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(k^*)}}$ (optimal arm underestimated)
- **Event B**: $\hat{\mu}_t(k) > \mu_k + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(k)}}$ (suboptimal arm overestimated)

If neither event occurs, then:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\mu}_t(k) + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(k)}} &\le \mu_k + 2c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(k)}} \\
&< \mu^* - 2c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(k^*)}} + 2c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(k)}} \\
&\le \mu^* \quad \text{(when } N_t(k) \text{ is large enough)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Bound each event using Hoeffding's inequality**:

For arm $k$ with $N$ pulls, by Hoeffding:
$$
P\left(\hat{\mu}_k \ge \mu_k + \epsilon\right) \le \exp(-2N\epsilon^2)
$$

Setting $\epsilon = c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N}}$:
$$
P(\text{Event B}) \le \exp\left(-2N \cdot \frac{c^2 \ln t}{N}\right) = \exp(-2c^2 \ln t) = t^{-2c^2}
$$

With $c = \sqrt{2}$, this gives $P(\text{Event B}) \le t^{-4}$.

**Step 5 — Sum over all timesteps and arms**:

By the union bound, the probability that arm $k$ is overestimated at time $t$ is at most $t^{-4}$. Summing over $t = 1, \dots, T$:
$$
\sum_{t=1}^{T} P(\text{Event B at } t) \le \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} t^{-4} \le \frac{\pi^2}{6} < 2
$$

**Step 6 — Bound the number of pulls of suboptimal arm $k$**:

If $N_T(k) > \ell_k$ where $\ell_k = \left\lceil \frac{4c^2 \ln T}{\Delta_k^2} \right\rceil$, then UCB of arm $k$ would fall below $\mu^*$ deterministically. Therefore:
$$
\mathbb{E}[N_T(k)] \le \ell_k + \mathcal{O}(1)
$$

**Step 7 — Assemble the final regret bound**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[R(T)] &= \sum_{k:\Delta_k>0} \Delta_k \cdot \mathbb{E}[N_T(k)] \\
&\le \sum_{k:\Delta_k>0} \Delta_k \left(\frac{4c^2 \ln T}{\Delta_k^2} + \mathcal{O}(1)\right) \\
&= \sum_{k:\Delta_k>0} \frac{4c^2 \ln T}{\Delta_k} + \mathcal{O}\left(\frac{K}{\Delta_k}\right) \\
&= \mathcal{O}\left(\frac{K \ln T}{\Delta_{\min}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\Delta_{\min} = \min_k \Delta_k$.

## 3.2 Derivation of PAC-MDP Sample Complexity for R-MAX

### 3.2.1 Setup

R-MAX declares a state-action pair $(s,a)$ "known" after $m$ visits. We want to bound the total number of episodes needed to find an $\varepsilon$-optimal policy.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Optimism**: For unknown $(s,a)$, the optimistic value is $V^*(s_0) \le \frac{R_{\max}}{1-\gamma}$. For known $(s,a)$, the estimated model has error:
$$
\left\vert \hat{P}(s'\vert s,a) - P(s'\vert s,a) \right\vert \le \epsilon_P
$$

**Step 2 — Hoeffding bound on transition error**: After $m$ visits:
$$
P\left(\left\vert \hat{P} - P \right\vert_{\infty} \ge \epsilon_P\right) \le 2^{S} \cdot 2 \exp(-2 m \epsilon_P^2) \le \delta
$$

Solving for $m$:
$$
m = \frac{2 S \ln\frac{2}{\delta} + 2 S \ln 2}{\epsilon_P^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Bound on Bellman backup error**: For known $(s,a)$, the Q-value error satisfies:
$$
\left\vert \hat{Q}(s,a) - Q(s,a) \right\vert \le \frac{\epsilon_R}{1-\gamma} + \frac{\gamma \epsilon_P \cdot V_{\max}}{(1-\gamma)^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Set $\epsilon$**: Choosing $\epsilon_P = \frac{\epsilon (1-\gamma)^2}{2}$ ensures:
$$
\left\vert \hat{Q} - Q \right\vert \le \varepsilon
$$

**Step 5 — Aggregate over state-action pairs**: Total samples needed:
$$
N_{\text{total}} = SA \cdot m = \tilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(\frac{SA \cdot S}{\epsilon^2 (1-\gamma)^4} \ln\frac{SA}{\delta}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Note: Tighter bounds exist** — modern PAC-MDP analyses give $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(\frac{SA}{\epsilon^2 (1-\gamma)^3}\right)$.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | $\varepsilon$-Greedy | UCB1 | Thompson Sampling | R-MAX | PSRL |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Regret type | Linear worst-case | Logarithmic | Square-root | PAC | Bayesian |
| Computational cost | $\mathcal{O}(K)$ | $\mathcal{O}(K)$ | $\mathcal{O}(K \cdot \text{sample})$ | $\mathcal{O}(SA \cdot VI)$ | $\mathcal{O}(SA \cdot \text{sample})$ |
| Uses uncertainty? | No | Yes (frequentist) | Yes (Bayesian) | Yes (optimistic) | Yes (Bayesian) |
| Requires prior? | No | No | Yes | No | Yes |
| Easy to implement? | Trivial | Easy | Moderate | Complex | Moderate |
| Best for | Toy problems | Stationary bandits | Non-stationary | Sparse rewards | Large state spaces |
| Scales to MDPs? | Yes (but slow) | Yes (UCBVI) | Yes (PSRL) | Yes (native) | Yes (native) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Strategic Exploration Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Strategic Exploration] --> B[Optimism Based]
    A --> C[Bayesian Posterior Sampling]
    A --> D[Probability Matching]
    A --> E[Information Gain]

    B --> B1[UCB1 Bandits]
    B --> B2[UCBVI MDPs]
    B --> B3[R-MAX]
    B --> B4[E3 Explicit Explore Exploit]
    B --> B5[Optimistic Q Learning]

    C --> C1[Thompson Sampling Bandits]
    C --> C2[PSRL MDPs]
    C --> C3[Gaussian Process TS]
    C --> C4[Bootstrapped DQN]

    D --> D1[Boltzmann Softmax]
    D --> D2[Gradient Bandits]

    E --> E1[Information Gain Maximization]
    E --> E2[State Marginal Matching]
    E --> E3[EMI Exploration]
```

## 4.2 UCB1 Action Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Time t: Observe state s_t]) --> Init[Initialize or update N_t a and mean hat mu a]
    Init --> Compute[Compute UCB_t a for every action a]
    Compute --> UCB[UCB_t a equals hat mu t a plus c times sqrt of ln t divided by N_t a]
    UCB --> Argmax[Select a_t equals argmax over a of UCB_t a]
    Argmax --> Execute[Execute action a_t and observe reward r_t]
    Execute --> Update[Update count N_t a_t and empirical mean hat mu t a_t]
    Update --> Loop[Increment t and return to Init]
    Loop --> Start
```

## 4.3 R-MAX Optimistic Model Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    State[Start Episode with state s] --> Check{N s a less than m for some action a}
    Check -- Yes --> Explore[Execute action a to explore]
    Explore --> Update1[Increment N s a and update empirical model]
    Update1 --> Check
    Check -- No --> Plan[Compute optimal policy pi from optimistic empirical model]
    Plan --> Exploit[Execute pi for one full episode]
    Exploit --> NewState[Reach new state s prime]
    NewState --> Check
```

## 4.4 Thompson Sampling Posterior Update Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prior Beta alpha beta] --> B[Observe reward r]
    B --> C{Success r equals 1}
    C -- Yes --> D[alpha leftarrow alpha plus 1]
    C -- No --> E[beta leftarrow beta plus 1]
    D --> F[Posterior Beta alpha prime beta prime]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Sample theta from posterior]
    G --> H[Select a equals argmax theta a]
    H --> I[Observe new reward r prime]
    I --> B
```

## 4.5 Exploration–Exploitation Trade-off Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Phase_Exploit [Exploitation Phase]
        E1[High confidence in action] --> E2[Pick empirically best action]
        E2 --> E3[Guaranteed short term reward]
    end
    subgraph Phase_Explore [Exploration Phase]
        X1[High uncertainty in action] --> X2[Add bonus to action value]
        X2 --> X3[Sample to reduce uncertainty]
        X3 --> X4[Discover possibly optimal action]
    end
    Phase_Exploit --> Balance{Uncertainty still high?}
    Balance -- Yes --> Phase_Explore
    Balance -- No --> Stop[Converge to Optimal Policy]
    Phase_Explore --> Phase_Exploit
```

## 4.6 Information-State Search Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Environment MDP] --> B[Empirical Model Estimator]
    B --> C[Uncertainty Quantifier]
    C --> D[Bonus Generator]
    D --> E[Modified Q Function]
    E --> F[Planning Algorithm Value Iteration]
    F --> G[Policy pi t]
    G --> H[Action Selector]
    H --> A
    C -.-> I[Confidence Set or Posterior]
    I -.-> D
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Concept of Exploration–Exploitation Dilemma
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B, Q1(a)]**

> Define the **exploration–exploitation trade-off** in reinforcement learning. Why is random exploration ($\varepsilon$-greedy with constant $\varepsilon$) considered an inefficient strategy for large MDPs?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **exploration–exploitation trade-off** is the central dilemma in RL where the agent must choose between:
- **Exploitation**: selecting the action believed to yield the highest immediate reward based on current knowledge.
- **Exploration**: trying actions with uncertain value to gain information that may improve long-term performance.

**Why $\varepsilon$-greedy is inefficient**: In a large MDP with $|S|$ states and $|A|$ actions, $\varepsilon$-greedy explores uniformly at random, which causes the agent to waste samples on clearly suboptimal actions. It does not use *information about uncertainty*, so the cumulative regret grows **linearly in $T$** (i.e., $\mathcal{O}(T)$). Strategic methods like UCB use **optimism in the face of uncertainty** to direct exploration toward under-visited state-action pairs, achieving **logarithmic regret** $\mathcal{O}(\log T)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall**: Students often write only "exploitation uses known info" without mentioning the regret/cost perspective. The 2024 KTU scheme values explicit mention of **regret complexity**.

---

### Question 2: UCB Algorithm Formulation
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A, Q1(b)]**

> State the **UCB1 algorithm** for stochastic multi-armed bandits. Write the action-selection rule and the role of the exploration bonus term.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
