The UCB1 algorithm maintains $N_t(a)$ as the number of times action $a$ has been selected up to time $t$ and $\hat{\mu}_t(a)$ as the empirical mean reward of action $a$. The action is selected by the rule:

$$
a_t = \arg\max_{a} \left[ \hat{\mu}_t(a) + c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}} \right]
$$

**Role of the exploration bonus** $c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}$: This term inflates the value of actions that have been **tried rarely** (small $N_t(a)$) and decreases as more data is gathered. It is derived from the **Hoeffding concentration inequality** and provides a probabilistic upper bound on the true mean $\mu(a)$. The constant $c$ controls the confidence level; the standard choice is $c = \sqrt{2}$, giving regret $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{KT \ln T})$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall**: Forgetting the $\ln t$ in the numerator. Without it, the bonus does not grow, and the algorithm can get stuck in early incorrect actions.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Multi-Armed Bandits and UCB

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Q2(a) – 14 marks]**

> **(a)** Consider a 3-armed bandit problem with the following reward distributions:
> - **Arm 1**: Bernoulli with mean $\mu_1 = 0.6$
> - **Arm 2**: Bernoulli with mean $\mu_2 = 0.4$
> - **Arm 3**: Bernoulli with mean $\mu_3 = 0.2$
>
> Compute the UCB values for all three arms after $t = 10$ timesteps, given the following observed data:
> - Arm 1 was pulled $N_1 = 5$ times, with total reward $S_1 = 3$.
> - Arm 2 was pulled $N_2 = 3$ times, with total reward $S_2 = 1$.
> - Arm 3 was pulled $N_3 = 2$ times, with total reward $S_3 = 0$.
>
> Use $c = \sqrt{2}$. Which arm does UCB1 select at $t = 11$? **7 marks**

> **(b)** Derive the regret bound of UCB1 for stochastic bandits. Show that the expected number of pulls of a suboptimal arm $k$ satisfies $\mathbb{E}[N_T(k)] \le \mathcal{O}\left(\frac{\ln T}{\Delta_k^2}\right)$, and hence conclude the cumulative regret is $\mathcal{O}\left(\frac{K \ln T}{\Delta_{\min}}\right)$. **7 marks**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1**: Compute empirical means.

$$
\hat{\mu}_1 = \frac{S_1}{N_1} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6
$$

$$
\hat{\mu}_2 = \frac{S_2}{N_2} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333
$$

$$
\hat{\mu}_3 = \frac{S_3}{N_3} = \frac{0}{2} = 0.0
$$

**Step 2**: Compute the UCB confidence term for each arm with $c = \sqrt{2}$ and $t = 10$.

**Arm 1**:
$$
\text{UCB}_1 = \hat{\mu}_1 + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_1}} = 0.6 + \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\ln 10}{5}}
$$

$$
= 0.6 + 1.4142 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2.3026}{5}} = 0.6 + 1.4142 \cdot \sqrt{0.4605} = 0.6 + 1.4142 \cdot 0.6786
$$

$$
= 0.6 + 0.9598 = 1.5598
$$

**Arm 2**:
$$
\text{UCB}_2 = 0.333 + \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\ln 10}{3}} = 0.333 + 1.4142 \cdot \sqrt{0.7675}
$$

$$
= 0.333 + 1.4142 \cdot 0.8761 = 0.333 + 1.2390 = 1.5720
$$

**Arm 3**:
$$
\text{UCB}_3 = 0.0 + \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\ln 10}{2}} = 0.0 + 1.4142 \cdot \sqrt{1.1513}
$$

$$
= 0.0 + 1.4142 \cdot 1.0730 = 1.5172
$$

**Step 3**: Decision at $t = 11$.

$$
a_{11} = \arg\max\{\text{UCB}_1, \text{UCB}_2, \text{UCB}_3\} = \arg\max\{1.5598, 1.5720, 1.5172\} = \text{Arm 2}
$$

**Result**: Despite Arm 1 having the highest empirical mean, UCB selects **Arm 2** because it has been tried fewer times, generating a larger exploration bonus. This demonstrates the **optimism-in-face-of-uncertainty** principle. **Arm 2 will be explored at $t=11$.**

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1**: The expected cumulative regret decomposes as:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R(T) &= \sum_{t=1}^{T} (\mu^* - \mu_{a_t}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \Delta_k \mathbb{E}[N_T(k)]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Define two bad events for a suboptimal arm $k$:
- $E_k^{t,1} = \{\hat{\mu}_t(k) \ge \mu_k + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(k)}}\}$
- $E_k^{t,2} = \{\hat{\mu}_t(k^*) \le \mu^* - c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(k^*)}}\}$

By Hoeffding's inequality with parameter $\epsilon = c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(k)}}$:
$$
P(E_k^{t,1}) \le \exp\left(-2 N_t(k) \cdot \frac{c^2 \ln t}{N_t(k)}\right) = t^{-2c^2}
$$

**Step 3**: The arm $k$ is chosen at time $t$ only if its UCB exceeds that of $k^*$. If neither $E_k^{t,1}$ nor $E_k^{t,2}$ occurs:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCB}_t(k) &= \hat{\mu}_t(k) + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(k)}} \\
&\le \mu_k + 2c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(k)}} \\
&= \mu^* - \Delta_k + 2c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(k)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For this to be $\ge \mu^*$, we need:
$$
2c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(k)}} \ge \Delta_k \Rightarrow N_t(k) \le \frac{4c^2 \ln t}{\Delta_k^2}
$$

**Step 4**: Set the threshold $\ell_k = \lceil \frac{4c^2 \ln T}{\Delta_k^2} \rceil$. Then:
$$
\mathbb{E}[N_T(k)] \le \ell_k + \sum_{t=1}^{T} P(E_k^{t,1} \cup E_k^{t,2}) \le \ell_k + 2 \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{6} \le \ell_k + \mathcal{O}(1)
$$

**Step 5**: Plug into the regret:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[R(T)] &= \sum_{k: \Delta_k > 0} \Delta_k \left(\frac{4c^2 \ln T}{\Delta_k^2} + \mathcal{O}(1)\right) \\
&= \sum_{k: \Delta_k > 0} \frac{4c^2 \ln T}{\Delta_k} + \mathcal{O}\left(\frac{K}{\Delta_k}\right) \\
&= \mathcal{O}\left(\frac{K \ln T}{\Delta_{\min}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: UCB1 achieves **logarithmic regret**, a fundamental improvement over $\varepsilon$-greedy.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Thompson Sampling and R-MAX

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Q2(b) – 14 marks]**

> **(a)** Consider a 2-armed bandit with Bernoulli rewards and Beta priors $\text{Beta}(1, 1)$ on both arms. The agent has observed the following outcomes: Arm 1 played 4 times with 3 successes; Arm 2 played 2 times with 1 success. Use Thompson Sampling with one posterior sample: simulate $\tilde{\theta}_1$ and $\tilde{\theta}_2$ from their respective posteriors and identify the chosen arm. **7 marks**

> **(b)** Explain the **R-MAX algorithm** for strategic exploration in finite MDPs. Discuss its PAC-MDP property and compute the visit threshold $m$ for $\varepsilon = 0.1$, $\gamma = 0.95$, $|S| = 10$, $|A| = 4$, $\delta = 0.05$. **7 marks**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1**: Update the posterior for each arm. For Beta prior $\text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$ with $s$ successes and $f$ failures, the posterior is $\text{Beta}(\alpha + s, \beta + f)$.

**Arm 1**: $\text{Beta}(1 + 3, 1 + 1) = \text{Beta}(4, 2)$

**Arm 2**: $\text{Beta}(1 + 1, 1 + 1) = \text{Beta}(2, 2)$

**Step 2**: Compute the posterior mean of each arm (this is the expected value of the Beta distribution $\frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta}$):

$$
\mathbb{E}[\tilde{\theta}_1] = \frac{4}{4+2} = 0.667
$$

$$
\mathbb{E}[\tilde{\theta}_2] = \frac{2}{2+2} = 0.500
$$

**Step 3**: Simulate one sample from each posterior (e.g., using inverse CDF or a sampler). A typical draw yields values around the mean:
- $\tilde{\theta}_1 \approx 0.71$ (sample from Beta(4, 2))
- $\tilde{\theta}_2 \approx 0.42$ (sample from Beta(2, 2))

**Step 4**: Decision:

$$
a_t = \arg\max\{\tilde{\theta}_1, \tilde{\theta}_2\} = \arg\max\{0.71, 0.42\} = \text{Arm 1}
$$

**Note**: Although Arm 1 is chosen here, the key insight is that the **posterior of Arm 2 is wider** (variance $= \frac{\alpha\beta}{(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)}$). In a second sample, Arm 2 could be selected if the random draw exceeds that of Arm 1 — this is Thompson Sampling's natural exploration mechanism.

**Posterior variances:**
- Var(Beta(4, 2)) = $\frac{4 \cdot 2}{6^2 \cdot 7} = \frac{8}{252} \approx 0.0317$
- Var(Beta(2, 2)) = $\frac{2 \cdot 2}{4^2 \cdot 5} = \frac{4}{80} = 0.0500$

Arm 2 has **higher variance**, so it is more likely to be sampled highly in some draws, ensuring continued exploration.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: R-MAX Algorithm Description**

R-MAX works by maintaining a count $N(s,a)$ of visits to each state-action pair. A pair is declared **"known"** once $N(s,a) \ge m$. For **unknown** pairs, R-MAX optimistically sets the reward to $R_{\max}$ and assumes self-transition to an absorbing state. The agent's value iteration solves an **optimistic MDP** where unknown state-actions are maximally rewarding, which forces the agent to visit them. After all relevant $(s,a)$ are known, R-MAX exploits the (now accurate) empirical model.

**Step 2: PAC-MDP Property**

A PAC-MDP algorithm must find an $\varepsilon$-optimal policy with probability $\ge 1 - \delta$ using a number of samples that is **polynomial** in $1/\varepsilon$, $1/\delta$, $|S|$, $|A|$, and $1/(1-\gamma)$. R-MAX satisfies this with sample complexity:
$$
N_{\text{samples}} = \tilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(\frac{SA}{\varepsilon^2(1-\gamma)^3}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Compute the visit threshold $m$**

The standard R-MAX threshold formula is:
$$
m = \frac{2 S \ln\left(\frac{2SA}{\delta}\right)}{\epsilon^2}
$$

where $\epsilon$ is the per-$(s,a)$ model accuracy:
$$
\epsilon = \frac{\varepsilon (1-\gamma)^2}{2}
$$

**Compute $\epsilon$**:
$$
\epsilon = \frac{0.1 \times (1 - 0.95)^2}{2} = \frac{0.1 \times 0.0025}{2} = \frac{0.00025}{2} = 0.000125
$$

**Compute the denominator $\epsilon^2$**:
$$
\epsilon^2 = (0.000125)^2 = 1.5625 \times 10^{-8}
$$

**Compute the numerator $2S \ln\left(\frac{2SA}{\delta}\right)$**:
- $2SA = 2 \cdot 10 \cdot 4 = 80$
- $\frac{2SA}{\delta} = \frac{80}{0.05} = 1600$
- $\ln(1600) \approx 7.378$
- Numerator: $2 \cdot 10 \cdot 7.378 = 147.56$

**Compute $m$**:
$$
m = \frac{147.56}{1.5625 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 9.44 \times 10^{9}
$$

This is a **very large threshold**, illustrating a known limitation of R-MAX: the constants can be prohibitive in practice, even though the asymptotic rate is polynomial.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Regret vs. Sample Complexity**: Students often confuse UCB's $\mathcal{O}(\log T)$ *regret* with R-MAX's PAC-MDP *sample complexity*. They are related but distinct: regret bounds are time-averaged, while PAC-MDP bounds are epoch-based.
>
> 2. **Mis-identifying the optimal arm**: A common mistake is selecting the arm with the highest *empirical mean* in UCB problems. Always use the UCB value (mean + bonus).
>
> 3. **Hoeffding constants**: Do NOT write $c = 1$ generically; the correct constant for sub-Gaussian rewards with variance proxy 1 is $c = \sqrt{2}$. Examiners check this.
>
> 4. **Beta posterior formula**: The Beta-Bernoulli conjugate update is $\text{Beta}(\alpha + s, \beta + f)$ — many students swap $s$ and $f$ in the parameters.
>
> 5. **PAC-MDP threshold**: A common error is to write $m = \frac{SA}{\varepsilon}$ without the $\ln$ and $1/(1-\gamma)$ factors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Strategic Exploration**

- [ ] **Exploration–Exploitation trade-off** is the central tension in RL; strategic methods use *uncertainty* to resolve it.
- [ ] **Regret** is defined as $R(T) = \sum_t (V^*(s_t) - Q^*(s_t, a_t))$.
- [ ] **$\varepsilon$-greedy** has linear regret $\mathcal{O}(T)$ and is *not* a strategic method.
- [ ] **UCB1 action rule**: $a_t = \arg\max_a \hat{\mu}_t(a) + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}$, with $c = \sqrt{2}$.
- [ ] **UCB1 regret bound**: $\mathcal{O}\left(\frac{K \ln T}{\Delta_{\min}}\right)$ — logarithmic in $T$.
- [ ] **Thompson Sampling** uses Bayesian posterior sampling; for Bernoulli rewards use **Beta–Bernoulli** conjugacy.
- [ ] **Thompson Sampling regret**: $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{KT \ln T})$.
- [ ] **Hoeffding's inequality** is the mathematical backbone of UCB's confidence radius.
- [ ] **R-MAX** uses *optimism under uncertainty*: unknown $(s,a)$ get reward $R_{\max}$ and self-transition.
- [ ] **R-MAX is PAC-MDP** with sample complexity $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(\frac{SA}{\varepsilon^2(1-\gamma)^3}\right)$.
- [ ] **UCBVI** extends UCB to MDPs with bonus $c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(s,a)}}$ on Q-values.
- [ ] **PSRL** = Thompson Sampling for MDPs: sample $(P, R)$ from posterior, then plan on the sample.
- [ ] **Boltzmann/Softmax** is a probability-matching strategy with no formal regret guarantees.
- [ ] **PAC-MDP** = Probably Approximately Correct in MDPs — a polynomial-sample learning guarantee.
- [ ] **Bayesian regret** = expectation under the prior over MDPs; PSRL achieves $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{SAT})$.
- [ ] **Visit threshold $m$** in R-MAX scales as $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(\frac{S^2 A \ln(SA/\delta)}{\varepsilon^2(1-\gamma)^4}\right)$.

> **Key Takeaway**: Strategic exploration is what separates *principled RL* from *heuristic trial-and-error*. Master UCB, Thompson Sampling, and R-MAX for KTU 2024 examinations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Softmax Exploration Strategy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Softmax Exploration Strategy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2 — Softmax Exploration Strategy

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Softmax Exploration Strategy** is a probabilistic action-selection mechanism in Reinforcement Learning (RL) in which the probability of choosing an action is assigned as a *monotonically increasing function* of its estimated action-value (Q-value). It uses the **Boltzmann (Gibbs) distribution** to convert greedy preferences into a smooth, differentiable probability mass function over the action space.

Mathematically, the Boltzmann-Softmax policy selects action $a$ in state $s$ with probability:

$$
P(a \mid s) \;=\; \frac{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q(s,a)}{\tau}\right)}{\sum_{b \in \mathcal{A}} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q(s,b)}{\tau}\right)}
$$

where the scalar $\tau > 0$ is the **temperature parameter** controlling the *randomness* of selection.

This is classified under **Stochastic Policy Exploration** (as opposed to deterministic methods like $\varepsilon$-greedy) and forms the backbone of **Boltzmann Exploration** in classical RL literature.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Buffet Line Decision"**
>
> Imagine you walk into a buffet with 4 dishes. You have rated them mentally: Biryani (9/10), Pasta (7/10), Salad (4/10), Soup (2/10). But you're not 100% sure about your taste buds today.
>
> * **Pure Greedy** → You always pick Biryani (no exploration).
> * **$\varepsilon$-Greedy** → 90% of the time you pick Biryani, but 10% of the time you randomly pick anything including Soup.
> * **Softmax Exploration** → You *proportionally* prefer better dishes. Biryani has the highest chance, Pasta has a moderate chance, Salad is rare, Soup is very rare — but never **zero**. The "spice level" of your decision is controlled by a knob called **temperature** $\tau$.
>
> * High $\tau$ = "I'm too confused, just pick almost randomly" (uniform).
> * Low $\tau$ = "I trust my rating, pick the best strongly" (sharp distribution).

The fundamental idea is: **do not waste exploration on actions you already know are bad.** Softmax gives *informed* exploration — better actions get explored more often.

---

### 1.3 Why Softmax Exists — The Motivation

| Problem with $\varepsilon$-Greedy | How Softmax Fixes It |
|-----------------------------------|---------------------|
| Equal probability to ALL non-greedy actions | Probability scales with action value |
| Bad actions explored as often as near-optimal | Bad actions get exponentially less chance |
| No smooth transition between explore/exploit | Temperature $\tau$ provides continuous control |
| Wastes samples on clearly inferior actions | Sampling is value-aware and efficient |

### 1.4 Key Metrics & Notations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - $Q(s,a)$ — Estimated action value in state $s$ for action $a$
> - $\tau$ — **Temperature** (positive scalar, controls randomness)
> - $k_{B}$ — Boltzmann constant (in physics, often absorbed into $\tau$ in RL)
> - $\mathcal{A}$ — Set of available actions
> - $|\mathcal{A}|$ — Number of actions (cardinality of action space)
> - $P(a \mid s)$ — Conditional probability of action $a$ given state $s$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism

The Softmax exploration algorithm executes the following sequence at every decision step:

1. **Observation Phase:** The agent is in state $s_t$ and queries its Q-table or function approximator to retrieve the action values $\{Q(s_t, a_1), Q(s_t, a_2), \ldots, Q(s_t, a_n)\}$ for all $n$ available actions.

2. **Normalization Phase:** Each Q-value is divided by the temperature $\tau$ to obtain "scaled preferences" $z_i = \dfrac{Q(s_t, a_i)}{\tau}$. This compresses or expands the value range.

3. **Exponentiation Phase:** The exponential function $\exp(\cdot)$ is applied to each scaled preference. This **amplifies the differences** between good and bad actions (or smooths them, depending on $\tau$).

4. **Softmax Normalization (Partition Function):** The exponentials are summed to form the **partition function** $Z(s_t) = \sum_{b \in \mathcal{A}} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q(s_t, b)}{\tau}\right)$. Each exponential is divided by this sum to yield a valid probability distribution summing to 1.

5. **Stochastic Sampling:** An action $a_t$ is drawn from this probability distribution (e.g., using the inverse CDF method or Gumbel-max trick).

6. **Update Phase:** The environment returns a reward, and the Q-values are updated (e.g., via the Bellman update).

7. **Temperature Schedule (Optional):** $\tau$ may be annealed (decreased) over time to shift the agent from exploration to exploitation.

---

## 2.2 The Temperature Parameter — The Heart of Softmax

The temperature $\tau$ is the **single most important hyperparameter** in Softmax exploration. Its behavior is summarized below:

| Behavior of $\tau$ | Resulting Distribution | Agent Behavior | Mathematical Limit |
|--------------------|------------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| $\tau \to 0^{+}$ | **Dirac delta** at the best action | Pure exploitation (greedy) | $P(a^*) \to 1$, others $\to 0$ |
| $\tau$ small | **Sharp** distribution | Strong exploitation, mild exploration | Exponential gap is large |
| $\tau$ moderate | **Balanced** distribution | Mixed explore/exploit | Bell-shaped around best |
| $\tau$ large | **Flat** distribution | Mostly random exploration | Probabilities approach equality |
| $\tau \to \infty$ | **Uniform** $P(a) = \dfrac{1}{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert}$ | Pure exploration | All actions equally likely |

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Temperature Mechanism

The softmax function originates from **statistical mechanics** (Ludwig Boltzmann, 1868). In a thermodynamic system, the probability of a particle being in energy state $E_i$ is:

$$
P_i \;=\; \frac{e^{-E_i / k_B T}}{Z}
$$

In RL, we simply *invert* the sign (because we want **higher Q-values to be MORE likely**):

$$
P(a) \;=\; \frac{e^{+Q(a) / \tau}}{Z}
$$

The minus sign flips to a plus sign because in RL "energy" is analogous to "negative reward." High reward = low energy = high probability.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU Board Exam — Print and Memorize**

| # | Formula | Name / Meaning | Boundary / Unit |
|---|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $P(a \mid s) = \dfrac{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q(s,a)}{\tau}\right)}{\sum_{b} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q(s,b)}{\tau}\right)}$ | **Boltzmann-Softmax Probability** | $\tau > 0$, $\sum P = 1$ |
| 2 | $Z(s) = \sum_{b \in \mathcal{A}} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q(s,b)}{\tau}\right)$ | **Partition Function** (normalization) | $Z > 0$ always |
| 3 | $\lim_{\tau \to 0^{+}} P(a^*) = 1,\; P(a \neq a^*) = 0$ | **Greedy Limit** | Pure exploitation |
| 4 | $\lim_{\tau \to \infty} P(a) = \dfrac{1}{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert}$ | **Uniform Limit** | Pure exploration |
| 5 | $\tau_t = \tau_0 \cdot \alpha^{t},\; 0 < \alpha < 1$ | **Exponential Annealing Schedule** | $\tau_0$ initial, $\alpha$ decay |
| 6 | $\tau_t = \dfrac{c}{\log(t + e)}$ | **Logarithmic Annealing** (Auer et al.) | $c$ constant |
| 7 | $P_{\text{rank}}(a) = \dfrac{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\text{rank}(a)}{\tau}\right)}{\sum_{b} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\text{rank}(b)}{\tau}\right)}$ | **Rank-Based Softmax** | Robust to Q-scale |
| 8 | $\log P(a) = \dfrac{Q(s,a)}{\tau} - \log Z(s)$ | **Log-Softmax Identity** | Numerical stability |
| 9 | $H(P) = -\sum_a P(a) \log P(a)$ | **Entropy of Policy** | High $H$ = more exploration |
| 10 | $\text{Regret}_T \leq \mathcal{O}(\sqrt{K T \log T})$ | **Softmax Regret Bound** | $K$ actions, $T$ steps |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics:** Softmax with high $\tau$ allows a robot arm to "wiggle" around to discover good manipulation policies without committing to bad ones.
- **Autonomous Driving:** Lane-change decisions are softmax-weighted over predicted safety and speed rewards.
- **Game AI (AlphaGo, Atari):** Used for stochastic self-play exploration to avoid deterministic exploits.
- **Recommendation Systems:** Softmax over user-item preference scores selects diverse but high-quality recommendations.
- **Financial Trading:** Portfolio allocation uses softmax over expected returns of stocks, with $\tau$ as a "risk appetite" knob.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deriving the Softmax Probability from First Principles

### Goal
Starting from the principle that better actions must be more likely, derive the Boltzmann-softmax distribution.

### Step 1: Set up the "monotonic preference" axiom

We want a probability $P(a)$ that is a **strictly increasing** function of $Q(a)$. So:

$$
\frac{\partial P(a)}{\partial Q(a)} \;>\; 0 \quad \text{(more reward } \Rightarrow \text{ more probability)}
$$

### Step 2: Impose the log-derivative consistency

A common and elegant choice is to make the **log-probability linear** in the Q-value (this is the *maximum entropy* principle):

$$
\log P(a) \;=\; \beta \cdot Q(a) + C
$$

where $\beta = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$ and $C$ is a normalization constant (does not depend on $a$).

### Step 3: Exponentiate both sides

$$
P(a) \;=\; e^{\beta Q(a) + C} \;=\; e^{C} \cdot e^{\beta Q(a)}
$$

### Step 4: Apply the normalization axiom

We require $\sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} P(a) = 1$. Define $Z = e^{-C}$ (a positive constant). Then:

$$
\sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} e^{C} \cdot e^{\beta Q(a)} \;=\; 1
$$

$$
e^{C} \cdot \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} e^{\beta Q(a)} \;=\; 1
$$

$$
e^{C} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sum_{a} e^{\beta Q(a)}} \;=\; \frac{1}{Z}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute back into the probability expression

$$
P(a) \;=\; \frac{e^{\beta Q(a)}}{\sum_{b \in \mathcal{A}} e^{\beta Q(b)}} \;=\; \frac{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q(a)}{\tau}\right)}{\sum_{b \in \mathcal{A}} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q(b)}{\tau}\right)}
$$

### Step 6: Conclude

We have derived the Boltzmann-Softmax distribution from only two axioms: **(i) monotonicity** and **(ii) normalization**. The exponential form is the *unique* family satisfying both.

> **Verification:** Set $Q(a) = 0$ for all $a$. Then $P(a) = \frac{1}{Z} \cdot 1 = \frac{1}{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert}$, which is the uniform distribution. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effect of Temperature on Probability Distribution

### Setup
Suppose we have 4 actions with Q-values: $Q(a_1) = 2,\; Q(a_2) = 1,\; Q(a_3) = 0,\; Q(a_4) = -1$.

### Case A: $\tau = 0.1$ (Low temperature — strong exploitation)

$$
P(a_i) \;=\; \frac{e^{Q(a_i)/0.1}}{\sum_b e^{Q(a_b)/0.1}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z(0.1) &= e^{2/0.1} + e^{1/0.1} + e^{0/0.1} + e^{-1/0.1} \\
&= e^{20} + e^{10} + e^{0} + e^{-10} \\
&\approx 4.85 \times 10^{8} + 2.20 \times 10^{4} + 1 + 4.54 \times 10^{-5} \\
&\approx 4.85 \times 10^{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

The first term dominates completely. So:

$$
P(a_1) \approx \frac{e^{20}}{e^{20}} \approx 1.000, \quad P(a_2) \approx \frac{e^{10}}{e^{20}} \approx 4.5 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Result:** Almost all probability mass on $a_1$ → greedy.

### Case B: $\tau = 1.0$ (Medium temperature — balanced)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z(1.0) &= e^{2} + e^{1} + e^{0} + e^{-1} \\
&= 7.389 + 2.718 + 1.000 + 0.368 \\
&\approx 11.475
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(a_1) &= \frac{7.389}{11.475} \approx 0.644 \\
P(a_2) &= \frac{2.718}{11.475} \approx 0.237 \\
P(a_3) &= \frac{1.000}{11.475} \approx 0.087 \\
P(a_4) &= \frac{0.368}{11.475} \approx 0.032
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Decreasing probability distribution — proper Boltzmann behavior.

### Case C: $\tau = 100$ (High temperature — uniform)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z(100) &= e^{0.02} + e^{0.01} + e^{0} + e^{-0.01} \\
&\approx 1.0202 + 1.0101 + 1.0000 + 0.9900 \\
&\approx 4.0203
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
P(a_1) \approx 0.254,\; P(a_2) \approx 0.251,\; P(a_3) \approx 0.249,\; P(a_4) \approx 0.246
$$

**Result:** Almost uniform ($1/4 = 0.25$ for each). ✓ Convergence to uniform is confirmed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Greedy and Uniform Limits (Boundary Analysis)

### Greedy Limit ($\tau \to 0^{+}$)

Assume the best action is $a^*$ with $Q(a^*) > Q(a)$ for all $a \neq a^*$. Divide numerator and denominator by $e^{Q(a^*)/\tau}$:

$$
P(a) \;=\; \frac{e^{(Q(a) - Q(a^*))/\tau}}{\sum_b e^{(Q(b) - Q(a^*))/\tau}}
$$

As $\tau \to 0^{+}$, the exponent $\dfrac{Q(a) - Q(a^*)}{\tau} \to -\infty$ for $a \neq a^*$ (since $Q(a) - Q(a^*) < 0$), and equals $0$ for $a = a^*$. So:

$$
\lim_{\tau \to 0^{+}} P(a) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } a = a^* \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### Uniform Limit ($\tau \to \infty$)

As $\tau \to \infty$, the exponent $Q(a)/\tau \to 0$ for all $a$. Using Taylor expansion $e^x \approx 1 + x$:

$$
P(a) \;=\; \frac{1 + \dfrac{Q(a)}{\tau} + \mathcal{O}(\tau^{-2})}{N + \dfrac{1}{\tau}\sum_b Q(b) + \mathcal{O}(\tau^{-2})}
$$

where $N = \vert\mathcal{A}\vert$. As $\tau \to \infty$:

$$
P(a) \;\to\; \frac{1}{N} \;=\; \frac{1}{\vert\mathcal{A}\vert}
$$

This is the uniform distribution. ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Log-Softmax Trick (Numerical Stability)

In practice, computing $\exp(Q(a)/\tau)$ can overflow for large Q-values. The **log-sum-exp trick** is used:

$$
\log Z \;=\; \log \sum_b e^{Q(b)/\tau} \;=\; M + \log \sum_b e^{Q(b)/\tau - M}
$$

where $M = \max_b Q(b)/\tau$ (the largest exponent). This keeps the largest exponent at 0 and prevents overflow.

Then:

$$
\log P(a) \;=\; \frac{Q(a)}{\tau} - \log Z
$$

And we can recover $P(a) = \exp(\log P(a))$ safely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always mention the numerical stability issue in code/implementation questions — board examiners award bonus marks for this insight.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Softmax Decision Flow (Algorithm Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Agent at state s_t]) --> Retrieve[Retrieve Q-values: Q s t a1 ... Q s t an]
    Retrieve --> Scale[Scale by temperature: z_i = Q s t a_i divided by tau]
    Scale --> Exp[Apply exponential: y_i = exp of z_i]
    Exp --> Sum[Compute partition: Z = sum of all y_i]
    Sum --> Norm[Normalize: P a_i = y_i divided by Z]
    Norm --> Sample{Sample action from distribution P}
    Sample --> Act[Execute action a_t]
    Act --> Env[Receive reward r_t and next state s_t+1]
    Env --> Update[Update Q-value using Bellman rule]
    Update --> Anneal[Optional: anneal temperature tau]
    Anneal --> Start
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Temperature Effect on Action Probability Distribution

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LowT["Low Temperature: tau = 0.1"]
        L1["P(a1) = 1.00"] --> L2["P(a2) ~ 0.00005"]
        L2 --> L3["P(a3) ~ 0.00"]
        L3 --> L4["P(a4) ~ 0.00"]
    end

    subgraph MedT["Medium Temperature: tau = 1.0"]
        M1["P(a1) = 0.64"] --> M2["P(a2) = 0.24"]
        M2 --> M3["P(a3) = 0.09"]
        M3 --> M4["P(a4) = 0.03"]
    end

    subgraph HighT["High Temperature: tau = 100"]
        H1["P(a1) = 0.25"] --> H2["P(a2) = 0.25"]
        H2 --> H3["P(a3) = 0.25"]
        H3 --> H4["P(a4) = 0.25"]
    end

    LowT --> MedT --> HighT
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Softmax vs Epsilon-Greedy (Comparison Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Eps["Epsilon-Greedy"]
        E1[Best action: prob = 1 minus epsilon] --> E2[All other actions: prob = epsilon divided by N minus 1]
    end

    subgraph Soft["Boltzmann Softmax"]
        S1[Action probabilities proportional to exp of Q by tau] --> S2[Best action has highest prob] --> S3[Worst action has lowest but nonzero prob]
    end

    Eps -->|Comparison| Soft
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Temperature Annealing Schedule (Exploration → Exploitation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0([Start: tau = tau0 large]) --> Phase1[Phase 1: High Exploration]
    Phase1 --> Phase2[Phase 2: Balanced Sampling]
    Phase2 --> Phase3[Phase 3: Strong Exploitation]
    Phase3 --> TEnd([End: tau near 0, near-greedy])

    Phase1 -.tau decay.-> Phase2
    Phase2 -.tau decay.-> Phase3
```
