State the action-selection rule used in Thompson Sampling for a multi-armed bandit problem.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Thompson Sampling is a Bayesian algorithm for the **multi-armed bandit (MAB)** problem that addresses the **exploration–exploitation trade-off** by maintaining a posterior probability distribution over the unknown reward parameters of each arm. At each time step $t$, the algorithm draws a random sample from the posterior distribution of every arm and then selects the arm corresponding to the largest sample. **Action-selection rule:** $$a_t = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \, \theta_a^{(t)}, \quad \text{where} \quad \theta_a^{(t)} \sim P(\theta_a \mid \mathcal{D}_a^{(t)})$$ This implements *probability matching* — the probability of selecting arm $a$ equals the posterior probability that $a$ is the optimal arm. **(3 marks — full allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the rule, 1 mark for probability matching interpretation.)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *For a Bernoulli bandit with a Beta$(\alpha, \beta)$ prior, derive the posterior distribution after observing $S$ successes and $F$ failures. State the posterior mean.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** By **Bayes' theorem**, the posterior is proportional to the product of likelihood and prior: $$P(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto \underbrace{\mu^S (1 - \mu)^F}_{\text{Bernoulli likelihood}} \cdot \underbrace{\mu^{\alpha_0 - 1} (1 - \mu)^{\beta_0 - 1}}_{\text{Beta prior}}$$ Combining exponents: $$P(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto \mu^{\alpha_0 + S - 1} \, (1 - \mu)^{\beta_0 + F - 1}$$ This is the kernel of a **Beta distribution**, so: $$P(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) = \text{Beta}(\alpha_0 + S, \; \beta_0 + F)$$ **Posterior mean:** $$\hat{\mu} = \frac{\alpha_0 + S}{\alpha_0 + \beta_0 + S + F}$$ **(3 marks — full allocation: 1 mark for Bayes setup, 1 mark for kernel recognition, 1 mark for mean expression.)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern) > **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as sub-part (a) 7 marks + sub-part (b) 7 marks. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`** **(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **Thompson Sampling algorithm** for the $k$-armed Bernoulli bandit. Include the prior setup, the sampling step, the action-selection rule, and the posterior update equations. State any assumptions made. **Model Solution:** **Assumption (1 mark):** Rewards are i.i.d. Bernoulli with unknown means $\mu_1, \dots, \mu_k \in [0, 1]$. **Step 1 — Prior Initialization (1 mark):** Place a Beta prior on each $\mu_a$: $$P(\mu_a) = \text{Beta}(\alpha_a^{(0)}, \beta_a^{(0)})$$ A common uninformative choice is $\alpha_a^{(0)} = \beta_a^{(0)} = 1$ (uniform on $[0,1]$). **Step 2 — Posterior Sampling (1.5 marks):** At each round $t$, sample from each arm's posterior: $$\tilde{\theta}_a^{(t)} \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha_a^{(t)}, \beta_a^{(t)}), \quad \forall a \in \{1, \dots, k\}$$ **Step 3 — Action Selection (1.5 marks):** Choose the arm with the largest sample: $$a_t = \arg\max_{a} \, \tilde{\theta}_a^{(t)}$$ **Step 4 — Observation and Update (2 marks):** Pull arm $a_t$, observe reward $r_t \in \{0, 1\}$, and update the posterior: - If $r_t = 1$ (success): $\alpha_{a_t}^{(t+1)} = \alpha_{a_t}^{(t)} + 1$, $\beta_{a_t}^{(t+1)} = \beta_{a_t}^{(t)}$. - If $r_t = 0$ (failure): $\alpha_{a_t}^{(t+1)} = \alpha_{a_t}^{(t)}$, $\beta_{a_t}^{(t+1)} = \beta_{a_t}^{(t)} + 1$. All other arms' parameters remain unchanged. The update leverages Beta–Bernoulli **conjugacy** and requires no numerical integration. The exploration–exploitation balance emerges automatically from posterior variance decay. --- **(b) [7 marks]** Consider a 3-armed bandit with initial parameters $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \alpha_3 = 2$, $\beta_1 = \beta_2 = \beta_3 = 2$. The first 4 rounds of Thompson Sampling give the following data: - $t=1$: arm 1 pulled, $r=1$ - $t=2$: arm 2 pulled, $r=0$ - $t=3$: arm 3 pulled, $r=1$ - $t=4$: arm 1 pulled, $r=0$ **(i)** Compute the posterior parameters $(\alpha_a, \beta_a)$ for each arm after round 4. **(ii)** Compute the posterior mean and variance of each arm. **(iii)** If the next three samples drawn are $\tilde{\theta}_1 = 0.55$, $\tilde{\theta}_2 = 0.41$, $\tilde{\theta}_3 = 0.62$, which arm will be pulled at $t=5$? **Model Solution:** **(i) Posterior parameters after round 4 (3 marks):** Start with $(\alpha_a, \beta_a) = (2, 2)$ for all $a$. - $t=1$: arm 1 success $\Rightarrow$ $\alpha_1 \leftarrow 3$, $\beta_1 = 2$. - $t=2$: arm 2 failure $\Rightarrow$ $\alpha_2 = 2$, $\beta_2 \leftarrow 3$. - $t=3$: arm 3 success $\Rightarrow$ $\alpha_3 \leftarrow 3$, $\beta_3 = 2$. - $t=4$: arm 1 failure $\Rightarrow$ $\alpha_1 = 3$, $\beta_1 \leftarrow 3$. Final table: | Arm $a$ | $\alpha_a$ | $\beta_a$ | $n_a$ | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 3 | 3 | 2 | | 2 | 2 | 3 | 1 | | 3 | 3 | 2 | 1 | **(ii) Posterior mean and variance (2.5 marks):** Using formulas $\hat{\mu}_a = \frac{\alpha_a}{\alpha_a + \beta_a}$ and $\text{Var}(\theta_a) = \frac{\alpha_a \beta_a}{(\alpha_a + \beta_a)^2 (\alpha_a + \beta_a + 1)}$: - **Arm 1:** Mean $= 3/(3+3) = 0.500$. Variance $= (3 \cdot 3)/(6^2 \cdot 7) = 9/252 \approx 0.0357$. - **Arm 2:** Mean $= 2/(2+3) = 0.400$. Variance $= (2 \cdot 3)/(5^2 \cdot 6) = 6/150 = 0.0400$. - **Arm 3:** Mean $= 3/(3+2) = 0.600$. Variance $= (3 \cdot 2)/(5^2 \cdot 6) = 6/150 = 0.0400$. **(iii) Action selection at $t=5$ (1.5 marks):** $$a_5 = \arg\max\{0.55, 0.41, 0.62\} = \arg\max\{\tilde{\theta}_1, \tilde{\theta}_2, \tilde{\theta}_3\} = 3$$ **Therefore, arm 3 is pulled at $t = 5$.** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** > - Many students confuse the **prior** parameters with the **posterior** parameters. The Beta$(\alpha_0, \beta_0) = (2, 2)$ is the *initial* prior; after observing data, parameters change. **Always update after every round.** > - Posterior **mean and variance are different quantities**. Marks are often lost for computing only one. > - The argmax is over the **samples** $\tilde{\theta}_a$, not the posterior means. A student writing "arm with highest mean is pulled" loses 1 mark. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`** **(a) [7 marks]** Compare **Thompson Sampling** with the **Upper Confidence Bound (UCB1)** algorithm for the multi-armed bandit problem. Discuss their philosophical foundations, exploration mechanisms, prior requirements, regret bounds, and practical performance. **Model Solution:** | Aspect | UCB1 | Thompson Sampling | |---|---|---| | **Philosophical foundation** (1 mark) | *Optimism in the face of uncertainty* — act as if each arm's mean is the upper end of its confidence interval. | *Probability matching* — pick the arm most likely to be optimal under the current posterior. | | **Exploration mechanism** (1.5 marks) | Deterministic bonus $\sqrt{\frac{2 \ln t}{n_a}}$ added to empirical mean. | Stochastic sampling from the posterior, so high-variance arms get explored randomly. | | **Prior requirement** (1 mark) | None — purely frequentist. | Requires a prior over reward parameters (e.g., Beta$(1,1)$ for Bernoulli). | | **Tunable parameters** (0.5 mark) | Exploration constant $c$ (often $= 1$ or $2$). | Prior hyperparameters $(\alpha_0, \beta_0)$. | | **Asymptotic regret** (1.5 marks) | $O(\sqrt{k T \ln T})$ deterministic worst-case bound. | $O(\sqrt{k T \ln T})$ Bayesian regret; empirically often **lower** than UCB1. | | **Implementation cost** (0.5 mark) | Trivial — store means and counts. | Requires a random-number generator for the chosen posterior (e.g., Beta sampling). | | **Empirical performance** (1 mark) | Strong and predictable. | Often **superior**, especially in **finite-horizon / small-sample** regimes, and on problems with many arms. | **Conclusion:** Both algorithms achieve the minimax-optimal $O(\sqrt{kT \ln T})$ regret rate. However, Thompson Sampling typically delivers **better empirical performance** with **less parameter tuning**, and naturally handles **non-stationary** or **structured** bandit problems via richer posterior models. UCB1 is preferable when **deterministic guarantees** and **no prior knowledge** are essential. --- **(b) [7 marks]** A clinical trial uses Thompson Sampling to assign patients to one of 3 treatments (A, B, C) with binary outcomes (success/failure). Initial prior is $\alpha_a = \beta_a = 1$ for all. After observing $S_A = 8, F_A = 2$ for treatment A; $S_B = 5, F_B = 5$ for treatment B; $S_C = 6, F_C = 1$ for treatment C: **(i)** Compute the posterior parameters, mean, and variance for each treatment. **(ii)** Which treatment would a **greedy** algorithm choose? Justify. **(iii)** How does Thompson Sampling differ from the greedy choice, and what is the **exploration benefit**? **Model Solution:** **(i) Posterior parameters (2 marks):** | Treatment | $\alpha$ | $\beta$ | $n$ | Mean | Variance | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | A | $1+8 = 9$ | $1+2 = 3$ | 10 | $9/12 = 0.750$ | $\frac{9 \cdot 3}{12^2 \cdot 13} = 27/1872 \approx 0.01442$ | | B | $1+5 = 6$ | $1+5 = 6$ | 10 | $6/12 = 0.500$ | $\frac{6 \cdot 6}{12^2 \cdot 13} = 36/1872 \approx 0.01923$ | | C | $1+6 = 7$ | $1+1 = 2$ | 7 | $7/9 \approx 0.778$ | $\frac{7 \cdot 2}{9^2 \cdot 10} = 14/810 \approx 0.01728$ | **(ii) Greedy choice (2 marks):** A purely greedy algorithm picks the arm with the **highest posterior mean**. Comparing $0.750$ (A), $0.500$ (B), $0.778$ (C): $$a_{\text{greedy}} = C \quad \text{(treatment C, with mean } 0.778\text{)}$$ **Justification:** Treatment C has the highest estimated success rate based on observed data, so a greedy algorithm commits to it. **(iii) Thompson Sampling's distinction (3 marks):** Thompson Sampling does *not* simply pick the highest posterior mean. Instead, it draws a random sample from each arm's posterior $\tilde{\theta}_a \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha_a, \beta_a)$ and selects the arm whose sample is largest. The crucial differences are: 1. **Probabilistic exploration:** Although C's mean is highest, B's high variance ($\text{Var} = 0.0192$) means $\tilde{\theta}_B$ can sometimes exceed $\tilde{\theta}_A$ and $\tilde{\theta}_C$, causing B to be sampled. The exploration probability is *proportional* to the posterior probability of being optimal. 2. **No premature commitment:** A greedy algorithm is **deterministic** — once C wins, it always wins, so A and B receive no future patients even though their data is limited. Thompson Sampling, by contrast, occasionally samples A and B, allowing continued data collection. 3. **Ethical / statistical benefit:** In the clinical trial context, the greedy approach could deny patients a potentially superior treatment (say, A) that simply hasn't accumulated enough data. Thompson Sampling **probabilistically** balances this, allocating patients in proportion to the posterior probability of being best — a *Bayesian ethically desirable* policy. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B Question (b):** > - A common error is to **forget the prior's contribution** to the posterior. Even with $S_a + F_a$ total observations, the posterior is $\text{Beta}(\alpha_0 + S, \beta_0 + F)$, **not** $\text{Beta}(S, F)$. Off-by-one errors here cost up to 1.5 marks. > - For variance, students often write $\frac{\alpha \beta}{(\alpha+\beta)^2}$ — that is the **first-order approximation**; the exact formula has the extra factor $(\alpha + \beta + 1)$ in the denominator. > - In sub-part (iii), the answer should explicitly mention **probability matching** or **posterior sampling**, not just "TS is better than greedy". A vague answer loses 1.5 marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Quick Recall — print this before the exam!** - [ ] **Thompson Sampling = Bayesian probability matching** for the MAB problem. - [ ] **Action rule:** $a_t = \arg\max_a \theta_a^{(t)}$, with $\theta_a^{(t)} \sim P(\theta_a \mid \mathcal{D}_a^{(t)})$. - [ ] **Conjugate pair:** Beta prior + Bernoulli likelihood $\Rightarrow$ Beta posterior. - [ ] **Posterior update** after observing success $r=1$: $\alpha_a \leftarrow \alpha_a + 1$. - [ ] **Posterior update** after observing failure $r=0$: $\beta_a \leftarrow \beta_a + 1$. - [ ] **Posterior mean:** $\hat{\mu}_a = \dfrac{\alpha_a}{\alpha_a + \beta_a} = \dfrac{\alpha_0 + S_a}{\alpha_0 + \beta_0 + n_a}$. - [ ] **Posterior variance:** $\text{Var}(\theta_a) = \dfrac{\alpha_a \beta_a}{(\alpha_a + \beta_a)^2 (\alpha_a + \beta_a + 1)}$. - [ ] **Uniform (uninformative) prior:** $\text{Beta}(1, 1)$ — initialized for every new arm. - [ ] **Probability matching identity:** $P(a_t = a) = P(\theta_a = \max_{a'} \theta_{a'} \mid \mathcal{D}^{(t)})$. - [ ] **Exploration is *automatic*:** posterior variance shrinks with $n_a$, so under-sampled arms are explored naturally. - [ ] **Asymptotic regret:** $O(\sqrt{k T \ln T})$ — near-optimal (Lai–Robbins lower bound matched up to constants). - [ ] **Generalization table:** Beta–Bernoulli, Dirichlet–Multinomial, Gamma–Poisson, Normal–Inverse-Gamma. - [ ] **Comparison anchors:** TS is Bayesian & stochastic; UCB1 is frequentist & deterministic; both share $O(\sqrt{kT \ln T})$ regret, but TS empirically wins at small $T$. - [ ] **Common KTU mistake:** confusing **posterior mean** (point estimate) with the **posterior sample** (random draw used for action selection). They are different — the sample is the *randomized* version of the mean with the variance baked in. - [ ] **Worked numerical pattern:** Initialize $\alpha=\beta=1$, process 3–4 rounds, compute posteriors, draw samples, apply argmax. **Practice this template.** - [ ] **Real-world tags to drop in viva:** adaptive A/B testing, clinical trial ethics, recommendation systems, cognitive radio channel selection, Bayesian optimization for hyperparameter tuning. ---

topics.title: Model Free Reinforcement Learning: Monte Carlo Prediction (MC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Model Free Reinforcement Learning: Monte Carlo Prediction (MC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Monte Carlo (MC) Prediction** is a class of model-free reinforcement learning algorithms used to estimate the value function $V_\pi(s)$ for a given policy $\pi$ by averaging the actual returns observed from sampled episodes of experience, without requiring prior knowledge of the environment's transition dynamics or reward function.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Monte Carlo methods are formally defined as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** Monte Carlo methods are ways of solving reinforcement learning problems based on *averaging sample returns*. To ensure that well-defined returns are available, MC methods are defined only for *episodic tasks* — that is, tasks that have a terminal state, after which the interaction breaks into fresh episodes.

Mathematically, the MC prediction goal is to learn:

$$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$$

where the expectation is approximated by the *empirical mean* of sample returns observed after visiting state $s$.

---

### 1.2 Key Terminology in MC Prediction

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of MC Prediction (MUST remember for KTU exams):**
> 1. **Model-Free** — No knowledge of $\mathcal{P}_{ss'}^a$ or $\mathcal{R}_s^a$ required.
> 2. **Learning from Experience** — Requires only sample episodes (state, action, reward sequences) generated by interacting with the environment.
> 3. **Episodic Setting** — Each experience is divided into finite-length episodes that terminate. The total discounted return is well-defined.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Student Who Learns by Doing"

Imagine a chess student who has **never read a chess book** (no model of the game) and is **not told the rules explicitly**. The only way the student learns the *value* of a board position is by:

1. Playing many complete games (full episodes).
2. After each game, looking back at the positions visited and asking: *"Did this position lead to a win, a draw, or a loss?"*
3. Recording the **final outcome** for every position encountered and then **averaging** the outcomes across many games.

The student is essentially performing **Monte Carlo prediction**:
- The chessboard position = **state $s$**
- The complete game = **episode**
- Win/Draw/Loss = **return $G_t$**
- Average outcome = **value estimate $V_\pi(s)$**

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering analogy:** In **robotics**, MC prediction is used in *trajectory evaluation* — a robot tries many motion sequences, and the *average performance* (e.g., smoothness, energy consumption) of trajectories passing through a particular joint configuration gives its **value**. This is a model-free estimate since the underlying dynamics (friction, motor nonlinearities) need not be mathematically characterized.

---

### 1.4 Why Model-Free? The Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In Dynamic Programming (DP) — Module 2 topic** — we needed the full transition probabilities $p(s', r \mid s, a)$ to perform a *one-step backup* using the Bellman equation. But in many real-world problems (autonomous driving, game AI, medical decision support), the transition model is **unknown, too complex, or impossible to specify analytically**.

MC methods break this dependency. They need only **samples of actual or simulated experience**, making them the practical workhorse of modern RL systems such as AlphaGo, Atari game agents, and large language model fine-tuning (e.g., RLHF).

---

### 1.5 Two Variants of MC Prediction (KTU Frequently Tested)

> [!NOTE]
> **Variant 1 — First-Visit MC Prediction:** Estimate $V_\pi(s)$ as the average of the returns following the *first occurrence* of state $s$ in an episode.
>
> **Variant 2 — Every-Visit MC Prediction:** Estimate $V_\pi(s)$ as the average of the returns following *every occurrence* of state $s$ in an episode.

Both variants converge to $V_\pi(s)$ as the number of visits to $s$ approaches infinity, but they differ in their **bias/variance characteristics** and **implementation simplicity**.

---

### 1.6 Critical Assumptions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to state the following assumptions. Examiners *deduct marks* if these are missing in a definition question:
>
> 1. **Stationarity of policy** $\pi$ — The policy generating episodes must remain fixed during estimation.
> 2. **Episodic termination** — Every episode must eventually terminate (no infinite loops).
> 3. **Discount factor** $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ — For infinite-horizon discount problems, episodic approximation requires an absorbing terminal state.
> 4. **Exploration coverage** — Under a *deterministic* policy, some states may never be visited. This is why *exploring starts* or *$\varepsilon$-soft* policies are required (more in Module 4 control discussion).

---

### 1.7 Position in the KTU 2024 Module 3 Map

| Step | Method | Requires Model? | Bootstraps? | Bias | Variance |
|------|--------|-----------------|-------------|------|----------|
| 1 | Dynamic Programming (DP) | Yes | Yes | Low | Low |
| 2 | **Monte Carlo (MC)** | **No** | **No** | **Zero** | **High** |
| 3 | Temporal Difference (TD) | No | Yes | Some | Lower than MC |

This table is **extremely high-yield for KTU Module 3 comparison questions**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation — Return and Value

Every MC method rests on a single central equation: the **expected return under policy $\pi$** equals the **value function**.

The **discounted return** starting at time $t$ in an episodic task of horizon $T$ is:

$$G_t \triangleq R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots + \gamma^{T-t-1} R_T$$

The **state-value function** is the expected return:

$$V_\pi(s) \triangleq \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s] = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, S_t = s\right]$$

The **action-value function** (for control, covered later):

$$q_\pi(s, a) \triangleq \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the expectation?** A single observed return is a *noisy sample*. Different episodes visiting the same state produce different returns because of stochasticity in the environment (transitions) and policy. We need the *average* over many episodes to recover the true value. This is precisely the **Law of Large Numbers** in action.

---

### 2.2 The Monte Carlo Estimation Principle

By the **Law of Large Numbers**, if we observe $n$ i.i.d. samples $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ from a distribution with mean $\mu$, then:

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i \xrightarrow{\,a.s.\,} \mu \quad \text{as } n \to \infty$$

Applied to RL, each *visit* to state $s$ in an episode yields a sample return $G_t^{(i)}$. Therefore:

$$V_\pi(s) \approx \frac{1}{N(s)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} G_t^{(i)}$$

where $N(s)$ is the number of times state $s$ has been visited.

---

### 2.3 First-Visit vs Every-Visit MC — Detailed Comparison

| Aspect | First-Visit MC | Every-Visit MC |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| **Update trigger** | Only the *first* time $s$ appears in an episode | Every time $s$ appears in an episode |
| **Sample efficiency** | Lower (fewer updates per episode) | Higher (more updates per episode) |
| **Bias** | Unbiased estimator of $V_\pi(s)$ | Biased in finite samples, but asymptotically unbiased |
| **Variance** | Generally higher | Generally lower (more samples averaged) |
| **Convergence guarantee** | Yes — converges to $V_\pi(s)$ | Yes — converges to $V_\pi(s)$ |
| **KTU exam weight** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ (more frequently tested) | ⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!TIP]
> **Sutton & Barto (the de facto KTU reference):** Both first-visit and every-visit MC methods converge to $V_\pi(s)$ as the number of visits $N(s) \to \infty$, but first-visit MC is the most commonly analyzed in textbooks and exam questions.

---

### 2.4 Incremental Implementation — The Online Update Rule

Storing all returns for every state is **memory-inefficient**. MC methods use an **incremental mean** update after each episode:

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[G_t - V(S_t)\right]$$

where $\alpha \in (0, 1]$ is the **step-size** (learning rate).

**Why this works:** Setting $\alpha = \frac{1}{N(S_t)}$ recovers the running average:

$$V_{n+1} = V_n + \frac{1}{n}\left[X_{n+1} - V_n\right] = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$$

For **non-stationary problems** (where the target value drifts), a *constant* $\alpha$ is used, which gives more weight to recent returns — this is the **exponential moving average** form.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (HIGH-YIELD — Memorize for ESE)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Domain |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Discounted return | Scalar, real-valued |
| 2 | $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ | State-value definition | Scalar |
| 3 | $q_\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ | Action-value definition | Scalar |
| 4 | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha[G_t - V(s)]$ | Incremental MC update | $\alpha \in (0,1]$ |
| 5 | $N(s) \leftarrow N(s) + 1$ | Visit counter | Integer |
| 6 | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \frac{1}{N(s)}[G_t - V(s)]$ | Running-average form | $\alpha = 1/N(s)$ |
| 7 | $\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \sigma^2 / n$ | Variance of sample mean | Decreases as $1/n$ |
| 8 | $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ | Discount factor | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance}$ | Bias-variance decomposition | KTU standard ML formula |
| 10 | $\Pr(\text{visit } s) > 0 \;\;\forall s$ | Coverage condition | For every-visit convergence |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Safeguard:** In your KTU answer sheets (handwritten), never write $\vert G_t \vert$ with pipe symbols in equation contexts where markdown rendering might break. Use $\mid G_t \mid$ or $\lvert G_t \rvert$ to denote absolute value.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Use-Cases of MC Prediction:**
> 1. **Healthcare Treatment Policy Evaluation** — Estimating the value of a treatment regimen by averaging patient outcomes across many patient episodes (no model of human physiology needed).
> 2. **Game AI (AlphaGo, AlphaZero)** — Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) uses rollout simulations to evaluate board positions.
> 3. **Financial Portfolio Management** — Estimating expected returns of trading strategies by simulating many market episodes.
> 4. **Autonomous Vehicle Path Planning** — Evaluating candidate trajectories by sampling outcomes in the real world.
> 5. **Recommendation Systems** — Estimating long-term user engagement value of a recommendation by observing complete user sessions.

---

### 2.7 The Convergence Theorem (Why MC Works)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (MC Prediction Convergence):** For a fixed policy $\pi$ and an episodic task, both first-visit MC and every-visit MC prediction algorithms converge to the true state-value function $V_\pi(s)$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$ as the number of visits $N(s) \to \infty$, provided every state is visited infinitely often.
>
> **Proof sketch:** By the Law of Large Numbers, the sample mean of returns converges to the true expectation. Each return $G_t$ is an unbiased sample of $V_\pi(S_t)$ under policy $\pi$ (since $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$). Therefore, the empirical average converges to the expectation. ∎

---

### 2.8 Limitations and the Bridge to Module 4

| Limitation | Description | Solution (Module 4) |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| Must terminate | Cannot handle continuing (non-episodic) tasks | Average-reward formulation |
| High variance | Returns are noisy, single-episode estimates | TD methods, baselines |
| Slow learning | Must wait for episode end before updating | TD(0), n-step TD |
| Need exploration | Deterministic policies miss states | $\varepsilon$-greedy, exploring starts |
| No bootstrapping | Cannot use value of next state to estimate current | (Not solved — TD does this) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Bellman Expectation to MC Sample Estimate

We begin with the recursive Bellman expectation equation (a Module 2 result) and show how MC methods *approximate* it without using it directly.

**Step 1 — The recursive decomposition (Bellman):**

$$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')\right]$$

This is a **one-step expectation** over the *next* state and reward.

**Step 2 — The trajectory-based decomposition (sample path):**

Roll out an episode following $\pi$ starting from $s$:

$$s_0 = s \xrightarrow{a_0 \sim \pi} r_1, s_1 \xrightarrow{a_1 \sim \pi} r_2, s_2 \dots \xrightarrow{a_{T-1} \sim \pi} r_T, s_T \text{ (terminal)}$$

The **observed return** from this single trajectory is:

$$G_t^{(1)} = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^2 r_{t+3} + \dots + \gamma^{T-t-1} r_T$$

**Step 3 — Show that the sample is unbiased (this is the heart of the derivation):**

Take the expectation of $G_t^{(1)}$ over the stochasticity of the environment and policy, conditioned on $S_t = s$:

$$\mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t^{(1)} \mid S_t = s]$$

$$= \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, S_t = s\right]$$

$$= \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, S_t = s\right] \quad \text{(since } R_k = 0 \text{ for } k > T \text{)}$$

$$\equiv V_\pi(s)$$

**Conclusion:** Each observed return $G_t^{(i)}$ is an **unbiased estimator** of $V_\pi(s)$. By the Law of Large Numbers, averaging many such unbiased samples yields the true value.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The Incremental Update = Exponential Moving Average

We want to show that the rule

$$V_{n+1} = V_n + \alpha\left[X_{n+1} - V_n\right]$$

with constant $\alpha$ computes a weighted average of past samples with exponentially decaying weights.

**Step 1 — Unroll the recursion (forward expansion):**

$$V_{n+1} = (1 - \alpha) V_n + \alpha X_{n+1}$$

$$= (1 - \alpha)\left[(1 - \alpha) V_{n-1} + \alpha X_n\right] + \alpha X_{n+1}$$

$$= (1 - \alpha)^2 V_{n-1} + \alpha(1 - \alpha) X_n + \alpha X_{n+1}$$

**Step 2 — Continue unrolling back to the initial value $V_1$:**

$$V_{n+1} = (1 - \alpha)^n V_1 + \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{n} (1 - \alpha)^{n-i} X_{i+1}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the weights:**

The coefficient of $X_{i+1}$ is $\alpha(1 - \alpha)^{n-i}$. These are:

- **Exponentially decaying** as the sample recedes into the past.
- **Sum to one** when $V_1 = 0$:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \alpha (1 - \alpha)^i = \alpha \cdot \frac{1 - (1-\alpha)^n}{1 - (1-\alpha)} = 1 - (1-\alpha)^n \to 1 \text{ as } n \to \infty$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The constant-$\alpha$ MC incremental update is an **exponentially weighted moving average** that gives more weight to recent returns. This is essential in **non-stationary** environments where $V_\pi(s)$ itself drifts over time.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — A 3-State Episode (KTU Exam Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** Consider an MDP with three non-terminal states $\{A, B, C\}$ and one terminal state $T$. An agent follows a fixed policy $\pi$ and generates the following episode:

$$S_0 = A \xrightarrow{a_0} R_1 = +1, S_1 = B \xrightarrow{a_1} R_2 = -1, S_2 = C \xrightarrow{a_2} R_3 = +2, S_3 = T$$

Use **discount factor** $\gamma = 0.5$. Apply **First-Visit MC** to update $V(A), V(B), V(C)$ given that $V$ is initially zero for all states.

**Step 1 — Compute the return $G_0$ starting from state $A$:**

$$G_0 = R_1 + \gamma R_2 + \gamma^2 R_3 = 1 + (0.5)(-1) + (0.5)^2 (2)$$

$$= 1 - 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0$$

**Step 2 — Compute the return $G_1$ starting from state $B$:**

$$G_1 = R_2 + \gamma R_3 = -1 + (0.5)(2) = -1 + 1 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute the return $G_2$ starting from state $C$:**

$$G_2 = R_3 = 2$$

**Step 4 — Apply first-visit MC updates (using incremental rule with $\alpha = 0.1$):**

$$\text{For } A:\; V(A) \leftarrow V(A) + \alpha[G_0 - V(A)] = 0 + 0.1(1.0 - 0) = 0.10$$

$$\text{For } B:\; V(B) \leftarrow V(B) + \alpha[G_1 - V(B)] = 0 + 0.1(0 - 0) = 0.00$$

$$\text{For } C:\; V(C) \leftarrow V(C) + \alpha[G_2 - V(C)] = 0 + 0.1(2 - 0) = 0.20$$

**Result after one episode:** $V(A) = 0.10,\; V(B) = 0.00,\; V(C) = 0.20$

**Step 5 — Repeat for more episodes.** As $N(s) \to \infty$, $V(s) \to V_\pi(s)$.

> [!TIP]
> **In KTU exams, this exact problem structure appears frequently** (3-state, 4-step episode, given $\gamma$, asked to compute MC updates). Practice writing the returns in **reverse time order** ($G_{T-1}, G_{T-2}, \dots, G_0$) to avoid errors.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Across Solution Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison table is the single most important KTU Module 3 deliverable.** Examiners frequently ask *"Compare MC, DP, and TD"* (or pairs thereof) for 7–14 marks.

| Property | Dynamic Programming | **Monte Carlo** | Temporal Difference |
|----------|--------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Model required? | Yes ($\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}$ known) | **No** | No |
| Bootstraps? | Yes (uses $V(s')$) | **No (uses actual $G_t$)** | Yes (uses $V(s')$) |
| Update timing | Sweep-based (offline) | **Episode-end (offline)** | Step-by-step (online) |
| Bias | Low (model-bias only) | **Zero (unbiased)** | Some (bootstrapping bias) |
| Variance | Low | **High** | Lower than MC |
| Convergence | $O(n)$ iterations | **$O(1/\sqrt{n})$** episodes | $O(1/n)$ |
| Handles continuing tasks? | Yes | **No (episodic only)** | Yes |
| Sample efficiency | N/A (model-based) | **Low (full episode needed)** | High (learns per step) |
| Memory | Stores full $V$ table | Stores $V(s)$ and $N(s)$ | Stores $V(s)$ only |
| KTU exam question weight | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem — Running Average Convergence

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A state $s$ is visited 4 times with observed returns $G^{(1)} = 2, G^{(2)} = 4, G^{(3)} = 0, G^{(4)} = 6$. Compute the running-average MC estimate after each visit. Use $\alpha = 1/N(s)$ for each update.

**Step 1 — Initialize:** $V_0(s) = 0,\; N(s) = 0$

**Step 2 — After visit 1** ($N=1$):

$$V_1(s) = V_0(s) + \frac{1}{1}[G^{(1)} - V_0(s)] = 0 + 1 \cdot (2 - 0) = 2.0$$

**Step 3 — After visit 2** ($N=2$):

$$V_2(s) = V_1(s) + \frac{1}{2}[G^{(2)} - V_1(s)] = 2 + 0.5(4 - 2) = 2 + 1.0 = 3.0$$

**Step 4 — After visit 3** ($N=3$):

$$V_3(s) = V_2(s) + \frac{1}{3}[G^{(3)} - V_2(s)] = 3 + \frac{1}{3}(0 - 3) = 3 - 1.0 = 2.0$$

**Step 5 — After visit 4** ($N=4$):

$$V_4(s) = V_3(s) + \frac{1}{4}[G^{(4)} - V_3(s)] = 2 + 0.25(6 - 2) = 2 + 1.0 = 3.0$$

**Final estimate:** $V(s) = 3.0$, which matches the true mean $\frac{2+4+0+6}{4} = 3.0$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the **visit count** $N(s)$ with the **episode count**. The visit count is per-state across *all* episodes, not per-episode.

---

### 3.6 Variance Reduction Insight — Why MC Has High Variance

Consider a return $G_t$ with reward samples $R_{t+1}, R_{t+2}, \dots$:

$$\text{Var}(G_t) = \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}\right) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{2k} \text{Var}(R_{t+k+1}) \leq \frac{\sigma_R^2}{1 - \gamma^2}$$

This grows as $\gamma \to 1$ (long horizons) and can be very large. By contrast, DP's variance is bounded by the model parameters.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Monte Carlo Prediction Operational Flow

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram illustrates the complete control flow of a **First-Visit MC Prediction Algorithm** for evaluating a fixed policy $\pi$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Initialize V(s) = 0 for all s, Returns(s) = empty list, policy pi is fixed] --> B[Generate episode S0, A0, R1, S1, A1, R2 ... ST-1, AT-1, RT, ST using pi]
    B --> C[Initialize G = 0]
    C --> D[For t = T-1, T-2, ..., 0 traverse episode in reverse time order]
    D --> E[Update G = R sub t+1 + gamma times G]
    E --> F{Check: Is S sub t visited for the FIRST time in this episode?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Append G to Returns of S sub t]
    F -- No --> H[Skip this occurrence first-visit MC]
    G --> I[Set V S sub t = average of Returns of S sub t]
    I --> J{All timesteps processed?}
    H --> J
    J -- No --> D
    J -- Yes --> K[Episode complete: V s approximates V sub pi s]
    K --> L{More episodes to sample?}
    L -- Yes --> B
    L -- No --> M[END: V s converges to V sub pi s as number of visits grows]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Monte Carlo vs DP vs TD Comparison Map

> [!TIP]
> **Use this diagram in your KTU exam answers** when asked to compare solution methods. It is visually striking and covers all the key contrasts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DP["DYNAMIC PROGRAMMING"]
        DP1[Needs full model P and R] --> DP2[One-step backup using V s prime]
        DP2 --> DP3[Bootstraps YES]
        DP3 --> DP4[Bias Low Variance Low]
    end
    subgraph MC["MONTE CARLO"]
        MC1[Model-free samples only] --> MC2[Full-episode rollout to get G sub t]
        MC2 --> MC3[Bootstraps NO]
        MC3 --> MC4[Bias Zero Variance High]
    end
    subgraph TD["TEMPORAL DIFFERENCE"]
        TD1[Model-free samples only] --> TD2[One-step bootstrap using R plus gamma V s prime]
        TD2 --> TD3[Bootstraps YES]
        TD3 --> TD4[Bias Some Variance Medium]
    end
    DP --> Q[Converges to V sub pi]
    MC --> Q
    TD --> Q
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — The Return Computation Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> Use this to illustrate how a single episode generates multiple return samples (one per timestep) for first-visit / every-visit MC.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Episode: S0 S1 S2 S3 Terminal] --> B[Timestep t = 0: G0 = R1 + gamma R2 + gamma squared R3]
    A --> C[Timestep t = 1: G1 = R2 + gamma R3]
    A --> D[Timestep t = 2: G2 = R3]
    B --> E{First visit to S0?}
    C --> F{First visit to S1?}
    D --> G{First visit to S2?}
    E -- Yes --> H[Update V S0 using G0]
    E -- No --> I[Skip first-visit MC]
    F -- Yes --> J[Update V S1 using G1]
    F -- No --> I
    G -- Yes --> K[Update V S2 using G2]
    G -- No --> I
    H --> L[Average into Returns list]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[New V table]
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This block diagram** shows the functional architecture of a deployed MC prediction system (e.g., embedded in a robotics or game-AI pipeline).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Environment Interaction Module] --> B[Episode Buffer]
    B --> C[Trajectory Processor]
    C --> D[Return Calculator computes G sub t for each t]
    D --> E[Visit Detector first vs every]
    E --> F[Value Function Updater V s table]
    F --> G[Visit Counter N s table]
    G --> H{Convergence Check}
    H -- Not converged --> A
    H -- Converged --> I[Output V sub pi s to downstream policy module]
    subgraph Storage
        F
        G
    end
```

---

### 4.5 Diagram Interpretation Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **Key visual elements in MC Prediction flow diagrams (KTU exam answer writing):**
>
> 1. The **reverse-time traversal** of episodes (from $T-1$ down to $0$) is essential — it allows incremental computation of $G_t$ using the recursion $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$.
> 2. The **visit detector** distinguishes first-visit from every-visit variants.
> 3. The **value function updater** is the central writable state $V(s)$.
> 4. **Convergence check** typically uses $\max_s \vert V_{\text{new}}(s) - V_{\text{old}}(s) \vert < \theta$ where $\theta$ is a small threshold like $10^{-4}$.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern (ESE Part A):** 2-mark and 3-mark questions, no choice, no sub-parts. Direct, factual, definition-based. Always carry the course outcome tag and Bloom's level in mind.

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

**Define Monte Carlo reinforcement learning. State two key differences between Monte Carlo methods and Dynamic Programming methods.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Monte Carlo (MC) reinforcement learning methods are a class of model-free algorithms that estimate value functions and discover optimal policies by **averaging the sample returns** obtained from complete episodes of interaction with the environment.

**Two key differences:**

| Aspect | Monte Carlo | Dynamic Programming |
|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1. Model requirement | Does **not** require knowledge of transition probabilities $p(s', r \mid s, a)$ or reward function $\mathcal{R}$ | Requires the **complete model** of the environment |
| 2. Bootstrapping | Does **not** bootstrap — waits until end of episode to compute actual return $G_t$ | **Bootstraps** — uses the estimated value of the next state $V(s')$ in the one-step backup |

*(Bloom's Level: L2 — Understand | CO Mapping: CO3)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**What is meant by First-Visit Monte Carlo Prediction? How does it differ from Every-Visit Monte Carlo Prediction?**

**Model Answer:**

> **First-Visit MC Prediction** is a method of estimating the state-value function $V_\pi(s)$ by averaging the returns observed **only after the first occurrence** of state $s$ in each sampled episode.
>
> **Every-Visit MC Prediction** averages the returns observed **after every occurrence** of state $s$ in each episode.

**Key difference:** First-visit MC uses fewer samples per episode (only the first visit to $s$ counts) and produces an **unbiased estimator** of $V_\pi(s)$. Every-visit MC uses more samples per episode (so often has lower variance in finite samples) but is technically biased, though both converge asymptotically to $V_\pi(s)$ by the Law of Large Numbers.

*(Bloom's Level: L2 — Understand | CO Mapping: CO3)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (Part B):** Each question carries 14 marks and is divided into two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. A genuine *internal choice* is provided — meaning you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full, not parts of each. Sub-parts often split theory and numerical/derivation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 3]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **First-Visit Monte Carlo Prediction algorithm** for estimating $V_\pi(s)$. Clearly state the algorithm steps, the underlying mathematical principle, and the role of the discount factor $\gamma$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider an episodic task with **three non-terminal states** $A, B, C$ and a terminal state $T$. An agent generates the following two episodes using a fixed policy $\pi$ and discount factor $\gamma = 0.8$:

**Episode 1:** $A, +1, B, -1, C, +2, T$

**Episode 2:** $B, +3, A, -2, T$

Using **First-Visit MC Prediction** with an initial value estimate $V_0(s) = 0$ for all states and a constant step-size $\alpha = 0.5$, compute the value estimates $V(A), V(B), V(C)$ after both episodes have been processed.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

### Part (a) — Algorithm Explanation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Setup:**
- We are given a fixed policy $\pi$ to be evaluated.
- Initialize $V(s) \in \mathbb{R}$ arbitrarily (commonly 0) for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$.
- Initialize an empty list $\text{Returns}(s)$ for each state $s$.

**Step 2 — Episode Generation:**
- Generate an episode $S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, \dots, S_{T-1}, A_{T-1}, R_T, S_T$ by interacting with the environment using policy $\pi$.

**Step 3 — Reverse-time return computation:**
- Initialize $G \leftarrow 0$.
- For $t = T-1, T-2, \dots, 0$:
  - $G \leftarrow R_{t+1} + \gamma G$
  - This recursion yields $G = G_t$ for the current time step.

**Step 4 — First-visit check:**
- If $S_t$ does **not** appear in $S_0, S_1, \dots, S_{t-1}$ (i.e., this is the first visit to $S_t$ in the episode):
  - Append $G$ to $\text{Returns}(S_t)$.
  - $V(S_t) \leftarrow \text{average}(\text{Returns}(S_t))$

**Step 5 — Repeat** for many episodes until convergence.

**Underlying principle:** By the **Law of Large Numbers**, as the number of first visits to state $s$ approaches infinity, the sample average of observed returns converges to the true expectation $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$.

**Role of $\gamma$:** The discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ weights near-term rewards more heavily than distant ones. It ensures the return $G_t$ is finite for episodic tasks of arbitrary length and reflects the agent's preference for sooner rewards.

**Convergence:** $V(s) \to V_\pi(s)$ as the number of visits $N(s) \to \infty$ for every state $s$, provided all states are visited infinitely often.

*(Bloom's Level: L2 + L3 | CO Mapping: CO3)*

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Episode 1:** $A \xrightarrow{+1} B \xrightarrow{-1} C \xrightarrow{+2} T$

**Step 1 — Compute returns in reverse order:**

- $t = 2$ (at $C$): $G_2 = R_3 = +2$
- $t = 1$ (at $B$): $G_1 = R_2 + \gamma G_2 = -1 + 0.8(2) = -1 + 1.6 = 0.6$
- $t = 0$ (at $A$): $G_0 = R_1 + \gamma G_1 = 1 + 0.8(0.6) = 1 + 0.48 = 1.48$

**Step 2 — Apply first-visit updates using $V \leftarrow V + \alpha(G - V)$ with $\alpha = 0.5$:**

- $V(A) = 0 + 0.5(1.48 - 0) = 0.74$
- $V(B) = 0 + 0.5(0.6 - 0) = 0.30$
- $V(C) = 0 + 0.5(2 - 0) = 1.00$

**Episode 2:** $B \xrightarrow{+3} A \xrightarrow{-2} T$

**Step 3 — Compute returns in reverse order:**

- $t = 1$ (at $A$): $G_1 = R_2 = -2$
- $t = 0$ (at $B$): $G_0 = R_1 + \gamma G_1 = 3 + 0.8(-2) = 3 - 1.6 = 1.4$

**Step 4 — Apply first-visit updates (check first-visit per episode):**

- For $A$: this is the **first visit to $A$ in Episode 2** ✓ → update
- For $B$: this is the **first visit to $B$ in Episode 2** ✓ → update
- $C$ was not visited in Episode 2 → no update

$$V(A) = 0.74 + 0.5(-2 - 0.74) = 0.74 + 0.5(-2.74) = 0.74 - 1.37 = -0.63$$

$$V(B) = 0.30 + 0.5(1.4 - 0.30) = 0.30 + 0.5(1.10) = 0.30 + 0.55 = 0.85$$

$$V(C) = 1.00 \quad \text{(no update — not visited in Episode 2)}$$

**Final Estimates after both episodes:**

$$\boxed{V(A) = -0.63, \quad V(B) = 0.85, \quad V(C) = 1.00}$$

*(Bloom's Level: L3 — Apply | CO Mapping: CO3)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 3]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare and contrast **Monte Carlo (MC) methods** and **Dynamic Programming (DP) methods** in reinforcement learning across at least **six different dimensions**. Tabulate your answer clearly.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **incremental implementation** of the First-Visit MC prediction algorithm. Show mathematically that the update rule

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha\left[G_t - V(S_t)\right]$$

is equivalent to a **weighted average of all observed returns** with weights summing to one. Discuss the special case when $\alpha = 1/N(S_t)$.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

### Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Dimension | Dynamic Programming (DP) | Monte Carlo (MC) |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| 1. **Model required** | Yes — full $p(s', r \mid s, a)$ and $r(s, a)$ must be known | No — only samples of experience required |
| 2. **Bootstrapping** | Yes — backup uses $V(s')$ of the next state | No — backup uses the actual observed return $G_t$ |
| 3. **Update timing** | Synchronous, full sweep of state space per iteration | Update only when an episode terminates |
| 4. **Bias** | Low (subject to model accuracy) | **Zero (unbiased estimator of $V_\pi$)** |
| 5. **Variance** | Low (deterministic given model) | **High (sample-based, noisy returns)** |
| 6. **Task type** | Handles both episodic and continuing tasks | Requires **episodic** tasks with terminal states |
| 7. **Sample efficiency** | N/A (uses model) — full state-space sweeps | Sample-inefficient — many episodes needed |
| 8. **Convergence rate** | $O(n^2)$ for $n$ states in policy iteration | $O(1/\sqrt{N})$ where $N$ = number of visits |

**Conclusion:** DP is suitable when the model is known and tractable. MC is the method of choice when the environment is unknown or too complex to model, at the cost of higher variance and slower convergence.

*(Bloom's Level: L4 — Analyze | CO Mapping: CO3)*

### Part (b) — Incremental Implementation Derivation (7 marks)

**Motivation:** Storing every return for every state in a list $\text{Returns}(s)$ is memory-inefficient. The incremental form uses only the running estimate $V(S_t)$ and the new return $G_t$.

**Step 1 — The incremental update rule:**

$$V_{\text{new}}(S_t) = V_{\text{old}}(S_t) + \alpha\left[G_t - V_{\text{old}}(S_t)\right]$$

Rearranging:

$$V_{\text{new}}(S_t) = (1 - \alpha)\, V_{\text{old}}(S_t) + \alpha\, G_t$$

**Step 2 — Show equivalence to a weighted sum of past returns:**

Suppose we have received $n$ returns $G_t^{(1)}, G_t^{(2)}, \dots, G_t^{(n)}$ for state $s$. By induction, the unrolled form of the update is:

$$V_n = (1 - \alpha)^n V_0 + \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{n} (1 - \alpha)^{n - i}\, G_t^{(i)}$$

If $V_0 = 0$ (or we ignore initialization), the weights are:

$$w_i = \alpha (1 - \alpha)^{n - i}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the weights sum to one (for $V_0 = 0$):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{n} (1 - \alpha)^{n-i} = \alpha \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} (1 - \alpha)^{j}$$

$$= \alpha \cdot \frac{1 - (1 - \alpha)^n}{1 - (1 - \alpha)} = 1 - (1 - \alpha)^n$$

As $n \to \infty$, $(1 - \alpha)^n \to 0$, so the weights sum to **exactly one** in the limit. Hence $V_n$ is a **proper convex combination** (weighted average) of past returns.

**Step 4 — Special case $\alpha = 1/N(s)$:**

Setting $\alpha_k = 1/k$ for the $k$-th visit to state $s$ recovers the **sample mean** (running average):

$$V_{n+1} = V_n + \frac{1}{n+1}\left[G_t^{(n+1)} - V_n\right] = \frac{1}{n+1} \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} G_t^{(i)}$$

**Verification by unrolling:**

$$V_2 = \frac{1}{2}G^{(1)} + \frac{1}{2}G^{(2)}, \quad V_3 = \frac{1}{3}G^{(1)} + \frac{1}{3}G^{(2)} + \frac{1}{3}G^{(3)}$$

All weights are equal to $1/n$, hence $V_n$ is the **arithmetic mean** of all $n$ observed returns.

**Engineering implication:** A **constant** $\alpha$ is preferred for *non-stationary* problems (the value drifts over time) because recent returns get higher weight. A **decaying** $\alpha_k = 1/k$ is preferred for *stationary* problems to ensure convergence to the true mean.

*(Bloom's Level: L4 — Analyze | CO Mapping: CO3)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS in Monte Carlo Prediction questions:**
>
> 1. **❌ Forgetting to state "episodic task"** — MC methods are *only* defined for episodic tasks. Omitting this in a definition question costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **❌ Confusing First-Visit and Every-Visit** — Many students swap the two. *Memorize:* **First-Visit = only first occurrence; Every-Visit = every occurrence.**
>
> 3. **❌ Computing returns in FORWARD time order** — Always compute $G_t$ from $t = T-1$ down to $0$ using the recursion $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$. Forward computation leads to errors and is penalised.
>
> 4. **❌ Using the wrong $\alpha$** — When the question specifies "incremental implementation with $\alpha = 0.1$", apply the rule $V \leftarrow V + \alpha(G - V)$, **not** the running average $\frac{1}{N}$ form.
>
> 5. **❌ Not stating the underlying theorem** — When explaining MC convergence, you *must* mention the **Law of Large Numbers**. Examiners look for this phrase.
>
> 6. **❌ Confusing the visit counter with the episode counter** — Visit count is *cumulative across all episodes*, per state. Episode count is global.
>
> 7. **❌ Missing "no bootstrapping"** — In any MC vs DP/TD comparison, always highlight that MC does **not bootstrap**.
>
> 8. **❌ Vague "averaging"** — When asked *how* MC works, always say "averaging the **observed sample returns** following visits to state $s$" — not just "averaging rewards".

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for MC Prediction**

✅ **Definition:** MC prediction is a model-free, episodic RL method that estimates $V_\pi(s)$ by averaging sample returns $G_t$ observed after visiting $s$.

✅ **Core equation:** $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$, where $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.

✅ **Mathematical justification:** The **Law of Large Numbers** — sample mean of unbiased returns converges to the true expectation.

✅ **Two variants:**
- **First-Visit MC:** Average returns after the *first* visit to $s$ in each episode. *Unbiased.*
- **Every-Visit MC:** Average returns after *every* visit to $s$. *Asymptotically unbiased, lower variance in finite samples.*

✅ **Incremental update rule:** $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha[G_t - V(S_t)]$ where $\alpha$ is the step-size.

✅ **Special case:** $\alpha = 1/N(s)$ recovers the running arithmetic mean (sample average).

✅ **Constant $\alpha$:** Gives exponentially weighted moving average — useful for non-stationary problems.

✅ **Three pillars to memorise:** Model-free + Learning from experience + Episodic setting.

✅ **Convergence guarantee:** $V(s) \to V_\pi(s)$ as $N(s) \to \infty$, provided every state is visited infinitely often.

✅ **Limitations to state in exam answers:**
- Cannot handle continuing (non-episodic) tasks.
- High variance (decreases as $O(1/\sqrt{n})$).
- Sample-inefficient — must wait for full episode.
- Requires exploration to visit all states.
- Does not bootstrap.

✅ **Comparison with DP:** MC is *unbiased but high-variance, model-free, episodic*; DP is *low-variance, requires model, bootstraps*.

✅ **Comparison with TD (for next module preview):** MC uses actual $G_t$; TD uses one-step bootstrap $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$.

✅ **KTU exam keywords to use:** *Sample return, episodic, law of large numbers, model-free, first-visit, every-visit, unbiased estimator, running average, incremental implementation, no bootstrapping.*

✅ **Numerical exam skill:** Always compute $G_t$ in **reverse time order** starting from $G_{T-1} = R_T$ and using $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$.

# MODULE 3 — FIRST-VISIT MONTE CARLO (FVMC)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **First-Visit Monte Carlo (FVMC) Prediction** is a model-free, tabular Reinforcement Learning algorithm used to estimate the state-value function **Vπ(s)** of a given policy π by averaging the **returns** observed after the **first time** a state *s* is visited in each sampled episode.

In rigorous KTU 2024-scheme notation, the FVMC estimate after observing *N(s)* first-visits to state *s* is given by:

$$
V(s) \;=\; \frac{1}{N(s)}\sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} G_i(s)
$$

where **G_i(s)** is the return observed following the *i-th* first visit to state *s*. The estimate is guaranteed to converge to the true value **vπ(s)** as **N(s) → ∞**, by the **Law of Large Numbers (LLN)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cricket Batting Average

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: A Batsman's Career Average**

Imagine a batsman whose "form" we want to evaluate. In **Test cricket**, his *batting average* is computed by taking the **runs scored in the very first innings of each match he plays**. Subsequent re-entries to the crease in the *same match* (in the second innings) are **ignored** for the purpose of this particular metric. Each match gives him **one valid data point**, and as the number of matches grows, the average becomes a stable estimate of his true ability.

**Mapping to FVMC:**

| Cricket Analogy | FVMC Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A complete cricket match | A full episode (trajectory) |
| First innings of each match | **First visit** to a state *s* |
| Runs scored in that innings | Return **G_t** |
| Career batting average | Estimated value **V(s)** |
| Number of matches played | **N(s)** — visit counter |

This is **exactly** the philosophy of First-Visit MC: within any single episode, the state *s* is counted **only once**, namely at its first occurrence.

### 1.3 Why "First" Visit? — The Mathematical Reason

> [!NOTE]
> In an MDP trajectory, a state may be revisited multiple times within the same episode (non-terminating loops). Averaging returns from **every** visit can introduce **statistical dependence** between samples from the *same episode*, slightly biasing variance estimates. By counting only the **first visit per episode**, FVMC produces **independent samples** of the return G_t, satisfying the i.i.d. assumption underlying the LLN convergence proof.

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Properties of FVMC (must memorize for KTU ESE):**
> - ✅ **Model-free** — does not require knowledge of transition probabilities P(s'|s,a) or reward function R(s,a).
> - ✅ **Tabular** — applicable only to discrete, finite state-action spaces.
> - ✅ **Episode-based / off-line** — requires complete episodes to compute returns (no bootstrapping).
> - ✅ **On-policy** — learns the value of the policy *currently being executed*.
> - ✅ **Unbiased estimator** of vπ(s) with variance that decays as **O(1/N(s))**.
> - ❌ **Not suitable for continuing (non-episodic) tasks** without modification.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Operational Phases of FVMC

> [!NOTE]
> Every FVMC implementation, whether by hand on the KTU answer script or in software, decomposes naturally into **three sequential phases**. Examiners frequently award 3–4 marks for cleanly drawing and labelling these three phases.

### **Phase 1 — Episode Generation (Rollout)**

- The agent interacts with the environment following the **target policy π** until a terminal state is reached.
- The complete trajectory is stored as a sequence of (state, action, reward) tuples.
- This is the *data-collection* phase.

### **Phase 2 — First-Visit Identification & Backward Return Computation**

- Walk **backward** through the episode starting from time *T* (terminal) down to *t = 0*.
- Maintain a running discounted return **G**.
- Maintain a `visited_in_episode` set; when a state *s* is encountered for the **first time** in this episode, it is marked and the current *G* value is recorded as a sample return for *s*.

### **Phase 3 — Incremental Mean Update (Averaging)**

- For each first-visit state, update the running average:
$$
V(s) \;\leftarrow\; V(s) \;+\; \alpha\bigl(G - V(s)\bigr)
$$
where α = 1/N(s) in the standard formulation, or a constant step-size in non-stationary variants.
- Increment the visit counter **N(s) ← N(s) + 1**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every equation you will need for a FVMC question on the KTU board exam. Memorize it.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Discounted Return from time *t* | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | $\gamma \in [0,1]$ — discount factor |
| 2 | Recursive Return (backward sweep) | $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1}$ | with $G_T = 0$ |
| 3 | First-Visit MC Value Estimate | $V(s) = \dfrac{1}{N(s)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} G_i(s)$ | $G_i(s)$ = return after *i*-th first visit |
| 4 | Incremental Update (running mean) | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha\bigl(G - V(s)\bigr)$ | $\alpha = 1/N(s)$ |
| 5 | Visit Counter Update | $N(s) \leftarrow N(s) + 1$ | strictly for first visits only |
| 6 | Convergence Guarantee (LLN) | $\Pr\!\left(\lim_{N(s)\to\infty} V(s) = v_{\pi}(s)\right) = 1$ | almost-sure convergence |
| 7 | Variance of Estimator | $\mathrm{Var}\!\left[V(s)\right] = \dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{N(s)}$ | $\sigma^{2}$ = variance of return G |
| 8 | Bellman Expectation (target) | $v_{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[G_t \mid S_t = s\right]$ | target FVMC is estimating this |

> [!WARNING]
> **Ktu Examiner Tip:** The pipe `|` symbol was rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` in this table to avoid breaking the markdown grid. **On your answer script**, write absolute values with single vertical strokes — but ensure your equation is *not* inside a table.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where is FVMC used in industry?** Although production-scale systems use deep RL (DQN, PPO), the **tabular FVMC algorithm** is foundational in:
> - **Robotic process control** where the state space is genuinely small and discrete (e.g., a 3-joint planar arm with quantized angles).
> - **Inventory management** in supply chains — a finite-state MDP where each state is a stock level.
> - **Game AI tutoring systems** (e.g., teaching agents for small grid-worlds like *Wumpus World* or *Taxi*).
> - **Pedagogical simulations** in academic RL research to benchmark new methods against a known-convergent baseline.
> - **Process scheduling** in manufacturing where jobs form discrete states and the policy is a heuristic dispatch rule.

The principle — *average returns after first visits* — is the conceptual ancestor of every modern Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) algorithm used in AlphaGo, stock-trading bots, and route planning.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the FVMC Estimator from the Law of Large Numbers

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation is the **canonical KTU board-style proof** worth **7 marks** when asked: *"Show that the First-Visit Monte Carlo estimate converges to the true state value vπ(s)."*

### **Starting Point — Definition of True Value**

By the Bellman expectation equation, the true value of state *s* under policy π is the expected return when starting from *s* and following π thereafter:

$$
v_{\pi}(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, G_t \,\big\vert\, S_t = s \,\right]
$$

By the **law of the unconscious statistician (LOTUS)**, this expectation is taken over the stochasticity of:
1. the environment's transition dynamics, and
2. the policy's stochastic action selection.

### **Step 1 — Sample Realization of the Expectation**

Each time the agent visits state *s* at time *t* in some episode, it observes a single sample realization:

$$
g_i \;\sim\; G_t \quad \text{conditioned on } S_t = s
$$

where the index *i* enumerates **independent episodes** in which *s* was visited at least once. Because each episode is an **independent trajectory** of the MDP, the samples $g_1, g_2, \dots, g_{N(s)}$ are **independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.)** random variables, each drawn from the distribution of $G_t \mid S_t = s$.

### **Step 2 — Construct the Empirical Mean Estimator**

The natural unbiased estimator of $\mathbb{E}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ using i.i.d. samples is the **sample mean**:

$$
\widehat{V}_{N(s)}(s) \;=\; \frac{1}{N(s)}\sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} g_i
$$

This is precisely the **FVMC estimate** $V(s)$. By linearity of expectation, the estimator is **unbiased**:

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\widehat{V}_{N(s)}(s)\right] \;=\; \frac{1}{N(s)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} \mathbb{E}[g_i] \;=\; \mathbb{E}[G_t \mid S_t = s] \;=\; v_{\pi}(s)
$$

### **Step 3 — Apply the Strong Law of Large Numbers (SLLN)**

The SLLN states that for i.i.d. random variables $X_1, X_2, \dots$ with finite expectation $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$:

$$
\Pr\!\left(\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i \;=\; \mu\right) \;=\; 1
$$

Applying this to our i.i.d. return samples:

$$
\Pr\!\left(\lim_{N(s) \to \infty} \widehat{V}_{N(s)}(s) \;=\; v_{\pi}(s)\right) \;=\; 1
$$

This establishes **almost-sure convergence** of the FVMC estimator to the true state value.

### **Step 4 — Variance Decay Rate (Optional Bonus Step)**

Since the $g_i$ are i.i.d. with variance $\sigma^2$:

$$
\mathrm{Var}\!\left[\widehat{V}_{N(s)}(s)\right] \;=\; \mathrm{Var}\!\left[\frac{1}{N(s)}\sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} g_i\right] \;=\; \frac{1}{N(s)^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} \mathrm{Var}[g_i] \;=\; \frac{\sigma^{2}}{N(s)}
$$

Thus the standard error of the estimator shrinks as $\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N(s)}}$, a hallmark of consistent estimators.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — A 3-State Episodic MDP

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination-style worked example** demonstrating first-visit computation on a small episode. This is a **frequently repeated KTU question type** worth 7 marks.

**Setup.** Consider the following episode generated under policy π:

$$
s_1 \xrightarrow{r=+1} s_2 \xrightarrow{r=0} s_3 \xrightarrow{r=+2} s_4 \xrightarrow{r=+5} \text{terminal}
$$

with **γ = 0.5**.

**Step 1 — Compute the return at each timestep (backward sweep).**

Using $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ with $G_T = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_4 &= R_5 + \gamma G_5 = +5 + 0.5(0) = +5 \\
G_3 &= R_4 + \gamma G_4 = +2 + 0.5(+5) = +2 + 2.5 = +4.5 \\
G_2 &= R_3 + \gamma G_3 = 0 + 0.5(+4.5) = +2.25 \\
G_1 &= R_2 + \gamma G_2 = +1 + 0.5(+2.25) = +1 + 1.125 = +2.125
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify first-visit states.**

In this episode, the states $s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4$ are each visited exactly once, so **all four are first-visits**.

**Step 3 — Record first-visit return samples.**

| State | First-visit time *t* | Return $G_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 1 | $+2.125$ |
| $s_2$ | 2 | $+2.25$ |
| $s_3$ | 3 | $+4.5$ |
| $s_4$ | 4 | $+5.0$ |

**Step 4 — Update the running mean (assume second episode gives $G_{s_1} = 1.875$).**

After 2 first-visits to $s_1$:

$$
V(s_1) = \frac{2.125 + 1.875}{2} = \frac{4.0}{2} = 2.0
$$

Using the incremental update with $\alpha = 1/N(s) = 1/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(s_1) &\leftarrow V(s_1) + \alpha\bigl(G - V(s_1)\bigr) \\
       &\leftarrow 2.125 + 0.5 \times (1.875 - 2.125) \\
       &\leftarrow 2.125 + 0.5 \times (-0.25) \\
       &\leftarrow 2.125 - 0.125 = 2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Both methods give $V(s_1) = 2.0$. ✓

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the FVMC Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram shows the **complete functional flow** of the First-Visit MC prediction algorithm. The arrows denote data dependencies between the three core subsystems: *Policy Rollout*, *Backward Return Sweep & First-Visit Filter*, and *Incremental Mean Update*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize V s = 0, N s = 0, Returns s = empty] --> B[Generate Episode using Policy pi]
    B --> C[Trajectory: S0, A0, R1, S1, A1, R2 ... ST]
    C --> D[Initialize visited set = empty, G = 0]
    D --> E[Loop: t = T-1 down to 0]
    E --> F[G = R sub t+1 + gamma times G]
    F --> G{Is S sub t already in visited set?}
    G -- No --> H[Add S sub t to visited set]
    H --> I[Append G to Returns of S sub t]
    I --> J[Increment N of S sub t by 1]
    J --> K[Update V of S sub t = average of Returns]
    G -- Yes --> L[Skip: not a first visit]
    L --> E
    K --> E
    E --> M[Episode Complete]
    M --> N{Convergence Check: N s large enough?}
    N -- No --> B
    N -- Yes --> O[Output V pi s for all s]
```

## 4.2 Comparison Flow: First-Visit vs. Every-Visit MC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Trajectory with revisits] --> B{Visit Counting Rule}
    B -- First-Visit MC --> C[Count state s only once per episode]
    B -- Every-Visit MC --> D[Count state s every time it appears]
    C --> E[Independent return samples per episode]
    D --> F[Dependent return samples per episode]
    E --> G[Lower variance, satisfies LLN strictly]
    F --> H[Slightly biased variance, but more data]
    G --> I[Favoured for theoretical convergence proofs]
    F --> J[Favoured for fast empirical convergence]
```

## 4.3 Data-Flow Diagram — Episode Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Agent
    participant Environment
    participant Buffer
    participant FirstVisitFilter
    participant ValueTable

    Agent->>Environment: Take action A sub t using policy pi
    Environment-->>Agent: Receive R sub t+1, next S sub t+1
    Agent->>Buffer: Store transition S sub t, A sub t, R sub t+1
    Note over Buffer: Continue until terminal state
    Buffer->>FirstVisitFilter: Send complete episode
    FirstVisitFilter->>FirstVisitFilter: Backward sweep, mark first visits
    FirstVisitFilter->>ValueTable: First-visit returns G
    ValueTable->>ValueTable: V s = V s + alpha times G - V s
    ValueTable-->>Agent: Updated value estimates for next episode
```

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define First-Visit Monte Carlo (FVMC) prediction method. State two of its key properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** First-Visit Monte Carlo (FVMC) prediction is a model-free, tabular reinforcement learning algorithm that estimates the state-value function *Vπ(s)* of a given policy π by averaging the **discounted returns** observed following the **first visit** to each state *s* in a sampled episode. Formally, after *N(s)* first-visits,
> $$V(s) = \frac{1}{N(s)}\sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} G_i(s)$$
>
> **Two Key Properties (1 mark):**
> 1. It is an **unbiased estimator** of vπ(s) and converges almost surely as N(s) → ∞ (by the Law of Large Numbers).
> 2. It is a **model-free, on-policy, episode-based** method requiring complete trajectories and no knowledge of transition dynamics.

---

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between First-Visit MC and Every-Visit MC prediction methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | First-Visit MC | Every-Visit MC |
|---|---|---|
| Visit counting | First occurrence of *s* per episode | Every occurrence of *s* per episode |
| Sample independence | Returns are i.i.d. across episodes | Returns within an episode are correlated |
| Variance | Theoretically lower (clean LLN application) | Slightly higher due to within-episode dependence |
| Convergence | Guaranteed by LLN (almost-sure) | Empirically faster, also converges to vπ(s) |
| Use case | Theoretical benchmarks, proofs | Practical scenarios with more sample efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for exam:** *First-Visit = strict statistician; Every-Visit = greedy practitioner.*

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

### **🅰️ Question A.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]`
**(a)** Describe the **First-Visit MC prediction algorithm** for estimating Vπ. Draw its block-level flow diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An agent follows a policy π in an episodic MDP with γ = 0.8 and produces the following trajectory:
$s_A \xrightarrow{r=+3} s_B \xrightarrow{r=-1} s_C \xrightarrow{r=+2} s_B \xrightarrow{r=+4} s_D \xrightarrow{r=+6} \text{terminal}$

Compute the **return** at each timestep. Identify all **first-visit** states and update their value estimates assuming this is the **first episode** observed. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Algorithm Description (7 marks):**

The First-Visit MC prediction algorithm operates as follows:

**Input:** policy π to be evaluated.

**Initialize:** $V(s) \in \mathbb{R}$ arbitrarily for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$; $N(s) = 0$ for all $s$; $Returns(s) = $ empty list.

**Loop forever (for each episode):**

1. **Generate** an episode $S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, \dots, S_{T-1}, A_{T-1}, R_T, S_T$ using π.
2. **Initialize** $G \leftarrow 0$.
3. **Loop** for each step $t = T-1, T-2, \dots, 0$:
   - $G \leftarrow \gamma G + R_{t+1}$  *(running discounted return)*
   - **If** $S_t$ has not appeared in $S_0, S_1, \dots, S_{t-1}$ *(first-visit test)*:
     - Append $G$ to $Returns(S_t)$
     - $N(S_t) \leftarrow N(S_t) + 1$
     - $V(S_t) \leftarrow \text{average}\bigl(Returns(S_t)\bigr)$
4. **Output:** the value function $V \approx v_{\pi}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Block diagram (3 marks of the 7):** Draw a flowchart with three blocks — *Episode Generation* → *Backward Return Sweep with First-Visit Filter* → *Incremental Mean Update* — connected by directional arrows. The first-visit filter is a decision diamond that routes the state to either the "append" or "skip" branch.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Backward return sweep with γ = 0.8, $G_T = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_5 &= R_6 + \gamma G_6 = +6 + 0.8(0) = +6 \\
G_4 &= R_5 + \gamma G_5 = +4 + 0.8(+6) = +4 + 4.8 = +8.8 \\
G_3 &= R_4 + \gamma G_4 = +2 + 0.8(+8.8) = +2 + 7.04 = +9.04 \\
G_2 &= R_3 + \gamma G_3 = -1 + 0.8(+9.04) = -1 + 7.232 = +6.232 \\
G_1 &= R_2 + \gamma G_2 = +3 + 0.8(+6.232) = +3 + 4.9856 = +7.9856
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify first-visit states:**

- $S_0 = s_A$: first occurrence → **first visit** ✓
- $S_1 = s_B$: first occurrence → **first visit** ✓
- $S_2 = s_C$: first occurrence → **first visit** ✓
- $S_3 = s_B$: already seen at $t=1$ → **NOT a first visit** ✗
- $S_4 = s_D$: first occurrence → **first visit** ✓

**Step 3 — Record first-visit returns and update V (first episode, so $V = $ return):**

| State | First-Visit Time | Return | Updated $V(s)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s_A$ | $t=1$ | $G_1 = +7.9856$ | $V(s_A) = 7.9856$ |
| $s_B$ | $t=2$ | $G_2 = +6.232$ | $V(s_B) = 6.232$ |
| $s_C$ | $t=3$ | $G_3 = +9.04$ | $V(s_C) = 9.04$ |
| $s_D$ | $t=4$ | $G_4 = +8.8$ | $V(s_D) = 8.8$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Note: state $s_B$ at $t=4$ is **discarded** despite having return $G_4 = 8.8$ because it is a **revisit** within the same episode. This is the **defining feature of FVMC**.
