### **🅱️ Question B.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**(a)** With the necessary mathematical steps, **prove that the First-Visit MC estimate converges to the true state value** vπ(s) as the number of first-visits N(s) tends to infinity. State the key theorem used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **sample efficiency**, **bias**, and **convergence guarantees** of First-Visit MC, Every-Visit MC, and Temporal-Difference (TD(0)) learning. Present your answer in a structured table. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Proof of Convergence (7 marks):**

*See the full derivation in Section 3.1 above. The KTU-board expected answer structure is:*

**Theorem used:** *Strong Law of Large Numbers (SLLN)* for i.i.d. random variables.

**Proof outline (mark allocation shown):**

1. **(1 mark)** State the Bellman target: $v_{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$.
2. **(1 mark)** Recognize that first-visit returns $g_1, g_2, \dots, g_{N(s)}$ are i.i.d. samples from the distribution of $G_t \mid S_t = s$, **because each episode is independent and only one sample per episode is taken**.
3. **(1 mark)** Form the sample-mean estimator: $\widehat{V}_{N(s)}(s) = \frac{1}{N(s)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} g_i$.
4. **(1 mark)** Show unbiasedness: $\mathbb{E}[\widehat{V}_{N(s)}(s)] = v_{\pi}(s)$ by linearity.
5. **(2 marks)** Apply SLLN:
$$\Pr\!\left(\lim_{N(s) \to \infty} \widehat{V}_{N(s)}(s) = \mathbb{E}[G_t \mid S_t = s]\right) = 1$$
Hence $\widehat{V}_{N(s)}(s) \xrightarrow{\text{a.s.}} v_{\pi}(s)$.
6. **(1 mark)** Variance: $\mathrm{Var}[\widehat{V}_{N(s)}(s)] = \sigma^2 / N(s) \to 0$.

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks — 2 marks for the table, 5 marks for explanation):**

| Property | First-Visit MC | Every-Visit MC | TD(0) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bootstrap?** | No (waits for episode end) | No (waits for episode end) | Yes (uses next-state estimate) |
| **Bias** | Unbiased | Unbiased (in limit) | Biased initially (bootstrapped) |
| **Sample efficiency** | Lower (1 sample per episode) | Higher (multiple samples) | Highest (online, step-by-step) |
| **Convergence** | a.s. by LLN | a.s. by LLN (extended) | a.s. for tabular, linear FA (Robbins-Monro) |
| **Episodic?** | Yes | Yes | Works for both episodic & continuing |
| **Variance** | Lowest of the three MC variants | Higher (correlated samples) | Lowest (uses only 1-step return) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway (must state explicitly):** TD(0) has the lowest variance but introduces bias; First-Visit MC is unbiased but has highest variance and requires episode completion; Every-Visit MC is a middle ground in sample efficiency at the cost of strict sample independence.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on FVMC questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing First-Visit and Every-Visit MC in numerical problems (–2 marks typical).** If a state is revisited, students often incorrectly include the *second* return as a first-visit sample. Always maintain a `visited` set per episode.
> 2. **Forgetting to set $G_T = 0$ at the terminal state (–1 mark).** The backward sweep *must* initialize with the post-terminal return equal to zero, not the final reward's value.
> 3. **Writing $V(s) = G_t$ instead of the average across episodes (–1 mark).** $V(s)$ is the **cumulative average over all first-visits across all episodes**, not just a single episode's return.
> 4. **Citing the wrong convergence theorem (–1 mark).** The correct theorem is the **Strong Law of Large Numbers (SLLN)**, not the Central Limit Theorem. CLT is about *distributional shape*; SLLN is about *pointwise almost-sure convergence*.
> 5. **Omitting the discount factor γ from the return equation (–1 mark).** Always write $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots$ explicitly, even if γ = 1.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **First-Visit MC — One-Page Cheat Sheet for Last-Minute Revision**

- ✅ **Definition:** Average of returns following the **first** visit to *s* in each episode.
- ✅ **Estimator:** $V(s) = \frac{1}{N(s)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} G_i(s)$.
- ✅ **Return formula:** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ (forward) **or** $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ (backward).
- ✅ **Incremental update:** $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha(G - V(s))$, with $\alpha = 1/N(s)$.
- ✅ **Convergence theorem:** **Strong Law of Large Numbers** — almost-sure convergence to $v_{\pi}(s)$.
- ✅ **Variance decay:** $\sigma^2 / N(s)$; standard error $\sigma / \sqrt{N(s)}$.
- ✅ **Properties:** model-free, tabular, on-policy, episode-based, unbiased.
- ✅ **Limitation:** requires complete episodes; cannot handle purely continuing tasks without modification.
- ✅ **Comparison anchors:** First-Visit ≠ Every-Visit (independence); FVMC ≠ TD(0) (no bootstrap).
- ✅ **Sample efficiency order (best → worst):** TD(0) > Every-Visit MC > First-Visit MC.
- ✅ **Bias order (most biased → least):** TD(0) > Every-Visit MC ≈ First-Visit MC ≈ 0.
- ✅ **Key distinguishing test in numerical problems:** if a state repeats in an episode, **only its first occurrence contributes** to the FVMC update.

---

> **END OF NOTES — FIRST-VISIT MONTE CARLO (FVMC) | MODULE 3 | KTU 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Every-Visit MC (EVMC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Every-Visit MC (EVMC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every-Visit Monte Carlo (EVMC)** is a *model-free*, *episode-based* Reinforcement Learning prediction algorithm that estimates the state-value function $V_{\pi}(s)$ (or action-value function $Q_{\pi}(s,a)$) of a target policy $\pi$ by averaging the **complete set of returns observed after *every single visit* to a state $s$** during an experience trajectory (episode).

Formally, for a state $s$ visited $N_s$ times across episodes (where $N_s$ is the total number of visits counting every occurrence), the EVMC estimator is:

$$\hat{V}_{\pi}(s) \;=\; \frac{1}{N_s}\sum_{k=1}^{N_s} G_{k}(s)$$

where $G_{k}(s)$ denotes the actual return following the $k$-th visit to state $s$ in any episode.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** EVMC is grouped under **Monte Carlo (MC) Methods for Prediction and Control** in KTU Module 3. It must be studied in contrast with **First-Visit MC (FVMC)** and forms the basis for understanding **exploring starts** and **$\varepsilon$-soft** Monte Carlo control.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are a cricket coach tracking how Virat Kohli performs when he walks in to bat at a particular **match situation** (say, "day 3, score 150/3, 4th innings chase"). Each time this situation arises during the season, you record his **final outcome** (runs scored or match result) for that innings.

- **First-Visit MC (FVMC)** = You only record the *first* innings of the season that matches this situation. If Kohli walks in at that score 5 times, you take *only* the first one.
- **Every-Visit MC (EVMC)** = You record *every single time* Kohli walks in at that score across the entire season and average all the outcomes.

Both give a sensible estimate, but EVMC uses **more data per state**, which is statistically powerful yet introduces *bias* in certain off-policy or function-approximation contexts (a nuance covered later in the module).

---

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary Anchors (Bold Definitions)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Visit** | A single occurrence of a state $s$ at time-step $t$ within an episode. |
| **Episode** | A finite-length trajectory from initial state to terminal state $S_T$. |
| **Return** $G_t$ | The cumulative discounted (or undiscounted) reward from time $t$: $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \dots + \gamma^{T-t-1} R_T$. |
| **Prediction** | Estimating the value function for a given policy $\pi$. |
| **Control** | Optimizing the policy to maximize value. |
| **Tabular Method** | Storage in arrays/tables (no function approximation). |

---

## 1.4 Why Every-Visit MC Matters in KTU Module 3

> [!NOTE]
> EVMC is one of the two foundational MC estimators (alongside FVMC). The KTU syllabus expects students to (i) write its pseudo-algorithm, (ii) prove its convergence, (iii) compare it with FVMC, and (iv) apply it within **GLIE-style** (Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration) control loops.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Walk-Through (Step-by-Step Logic)

EVMC prediction proceeds in **four rigid phases**. Memorise these — they appear verbatim in board questions.

### Phase 1 — Policy Fixation
The target policy $\pi$ is held **fixed**. The goal is *prediction*, not yet control.

### Phase 2 — Episode Generation
Generate (or simulate) an episode by following $\pi$:
$$S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, \dots, S_{T-1}, A_{T-1}, R_T, S_T$$

### Phase 3 — Backward Return Computation
For each time-step $t = T-1, T-2, \dots, 0$, compute the return:

$$G_t \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^{k}\,R_{t+k+1}$$

### Phase 4 — Every-Visit Averaging
For each state $s$, append $G_t$ to the running list $Returns(s)$ **every time** $S_t = s$. After all episodes, the estimate is the mean of this list.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Justification |
|---|---|
| Fix $\pi$ | MC prediction is policy-specific; without a fixed target we cannot define $V_\pi$. |
| Episode-based | MC requires *complete* trajectories because the return $G_t$ is only known at episode end. |
| Backward recursion | Allows efficient reuse: $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$, saving compute. |
| Every-visit averaging | More samples → lower variance (theoretically) for a given state. |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the table below. It is the **single most asked reference** for Module 3 short-answer questions.

| Symbol / Equation | Statement | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ | True state-value definition | Bookwork (2-mark) |
| $\hat{V}(s) = \frac{1}{N(s)}\sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} G_i(s)$ | EVMC empirical mean | Direct estimator |
| $\bar{V}_n(s) = \bar{V}_{n-1}(s) + \tfrac{1}{n}\bigl[G_n(s) - \bar{V}_{n-1}(s)\bigr]$ | Incremental mean update | On-line implementation |
| $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha\bigl[G - V(s)\bigr]$ | Constant step-size $\alpha$ update | Non-stationary tracking |
| $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ | Backward recursive return | Algorithmic efficiency |
| $Q_{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | Action-value definition | Control stage |
| $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ | Greedy policy improvement | After MC evaluation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Latex Markdown Trap:** In the table above, vertical bars are *outside* the math block. When writing inline math inside a markdown table, use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\bigl[G_t \mid S_t = s\bigr]$) — never a bare `|`.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotic manipulation** where episodes end on task completion (grasp success/failure).
- **Atari / Game AI** where each play-through is a discrete episode.
- **Dialogue systems** where conversations terminate after goal achievement.
- **Inventory / Supply Chain** simulations where every quarter is an episode.
- **Recommendation engines** where each user-session is an episodic trajectory.

In each of these, EVMC provides a *low-bias*, *high-data-utilisation* estimator of long-run returns.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Every-Visit MC Estimator

We start from the definition of $V_{\pi}(s)$ and transform it into an empirical estimator.

### Step 1 — Ground-Truth Definition
By the definition of state-value in a Markov Reward Process under policy $\pi$:

$$V_{\pi}(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\bigl[\,G_t \,\big\vert\, S_t = s\,\bigr]$$

### Step 2 — Expand the Expectation
The expectation over the stochasticity of the MDP and policy is, by the *law of the unconscious statistician (LOTUS)*:

$$V_{\pi}(s) \;=\; \sum_{a}\pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s',r} p(s',r\mid s,a)\bigl[\,r + \gamma V_{\pi}(s')\bigr]$$

This is the **Bellman expectation equation** (book-work link).

### Step 3 — Replace Expectation with Empirical Mean (Monte Carlo Principle)
Across $N_s$ sampled visits to state $s$, with returns $G_1, G_2, \dots, G_{N_s}$, the **strong law of large numbers (SLLN)** states:

$$\hat{V}_{N_s}(s) \;=\; \frac{1}{N_s}\sum_{k=1}^{N_s} G_k(s) \;\xrightarrow{\;a.s.\;}\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s] \;=\; V_{\pi}(s)$$

### Step 4 — Convergence Justification
The error after $N_s$ visits satisfies:

$$\mathbb{P}\!\Bigl(\lim_{N_s \to \infty} \hat{V}_{N_s}(s) = V_{\pi}(s)\Bigr) \;=\; 1$$

This is the **almost-sure convergence** guarantee. Because each visit contributes one sample, EVMC has an **effective sample size equal to total visits**, not unique visits.

### Step 5 — Incremental Reformulation (Memory-Efficient Form)
Define $\bar{V}_n(s)$ as the running mean after $n$ returns. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{V}_n(s) &\;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} G_k(s) \\
&\;=\; \frac{1}{n}\Bigl(G_n(s) + \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} G_k(s)\Bigr) \\
&\;=\; \frac{1}{n}\Bigl(G_n(s) + (n-1)\,\bar{V}_{n-1}(s)\Bigr) \\
&\;=\; \frac{1}{n}\Bigl((n-1)\,\bar{V}_{n-1}(s) + G_n(s)\Bigr) \\
&\;=\; \bar{V}_{n-1}(s) + \frac{1}{n}\Bigl(G_n(s) - \bar{V}_{n-1}(s)\Bigr)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **incremental EVMC update** — it requires only the *current estimate* $\bar{V}_{n-1}$ and the *new return* $G_n$. No memory of past returns is needed.

### Step 6 — Constant Step-Size Variant (Non-Stationary Setting)
If the underlying return distribution drifts over time, replace $\frac{1}{n}$ by a fixed $\alpha \in (0,1)$:

$$V(s) \;\leftarrow\; V(s) + \alpha\bigl[\,G - V(s)\,\bigr]$$

For exponential weighted average, the effective window is approximately $\tfrac{1}{\alpha}$.

---

## 3.2 Bias-Variance Analysis (Board-Favourite Concept)

Let $N_s^{\text{first}}$ denote the number of *unique* first visits per episode, and $N_s^{\text{every}}$ the total visits (clearly $N_s^{\text{every}} \geq N_s^{\text{first}}$).

For a fixed number of episodes $M$:

$$\operatorname{Var}\bigl(\hat{V}^{\text{EV}}\bigr) \;\leq\; \operatorname{Var}\bigl(\hat{V}^{\text{FV}}\bigr)$$

Intuition: EVMC uses *more samples*, hence the sample mean has *smaller variance*. However, EVMC samples are **not strictly independent** when the same state is visited multiple times in one episode (autocorrelation). This *residual dependence* is what makes EVMC slightly biased in function-approximation settings (Baird's counterexample).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A robot visits state $s$ three times across episodes. The returns following each visit are $G_1 = 10$, $G_2 = 14$, $G_3 = 8$. Compute the EVMC estimate and show the incremental update sequence.

**Solution:**

$$\hat{V}_{\text{EV}}(s) \;=\; \frac{10 + 14 + 8}{3} \;=\; \frac{32}{3} \;\approx\; 10.67$$

Incremental sequence with $\bar{V}_0(s) = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{V}_1(s) &= 0 + \tfrac{1}{1}(10 - 0) = 10.00 \\
\bar{V}_2(s) &= 10 + \tfrac{1}{2}(14 - 10) = 12.00 \\
\bar{V}_3(s) &= 12 + \tfrac{1}{3}(8 - 12) \;\approx\; 10.67
\end{aligned}$$

✔ Matches the direct formula.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Every-Visit MC Prediction — Algorithmic Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **Every-Visit Monte Carlo Prediction** algorithm. It is the block-level functional architecture used during KTU valuation for algorithm-trace questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Initialize V s arbitrarily for all s in S, Returns s empty list, fix policy pi] --> loopA[For each episode, generate trajectory by following pi]
    loopA --> obsA[Observe episode: S0, A0, R1, S1, A1, R2 ... ST]
    obsA --> backA[Backward sweep: Compute Gt = Rt plus 1 plus gamma Gt plus 1 for all t]
    backA --> checkA{For each t, is St in States s list}
    checkA -->|Yes| appendA[Append Gt to Returns St list]
    checkA -->|No| skipA[Skip]
    appendA --> contA[Continue next t]
    skipA --> contA
    contA --> moreA{More episodes to simulate}
    moreA -->|Yes| loopA
    moreA -->|No| avgA[V s equals mean of Returns s for all s]
    avgA --> stopA[Stop: Return V approx equals V pi]
```

**Reading the diagram (for board exams):**
1. Outer loop: episodes.
2. Inner loop: time-steps $t$ in reverse.
3. **Critical branching**: Every-Visit MC appends *unconditionally* for each occurrence of $s$, unlike First-Visit which only appends the *first* occurrence per episode.

---

## 4.2 EVMC vs FVMC — Side-by-Side Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FVMC[First Visit MC]
        F1[Episode start] --> F2{Is this first occurrence of s in episode}
        F2 -->|Yes| F3[Append Gt]
        F2 -->|No| F4[Ignore]
        F3 --> F5[Average at end]
        F4 --> F5
    end
    subgraph EVMC[Every Visit MC]
        E1[Episode start] --> E2[For every t with St equals s]
        E2 --> E3[Unconditionally append Gt]
        E3 --> E4[Average at end]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Board tip:** Draw the conditional branching box (`{...}`) explicitly when asked to differentiate EVMC from FVMC. Examiners award marks for clearly showing the *unconditional* nature of EVMC appending.

---

## 4.3 Return-Recursion Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State St] --> B[Action At via policy pi]
    B --> C[Reward Rt plus 1 and Next State St plus 1]
    C --> D[Gt equals Rt plus 1 plus gamma Gt plus 1]
    D --> E[Store Gt for every visit]
    E --> F[Update V St incrementally]
```

This block encodes the recursive identity $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ used during the backward sweep.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Every-Visit Monte Carlo (EVMC) prediction. State its estimator formula. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Every-Visit Monte Carlo prediction is a model-free reinforcement learning algorithm used to estimate the state-value function $V_{\pi}(s)$ of a given policy $\pi$. It works by averaging the returns observed after **every visit** to each state $s$ across multiple episodes.

The estimator is:

$$\hat{V}_{\pi}(s) \;=\; \frac{1}{N_s}\sum_{k=1}^{N_s} G_k(s)$$

where $N_s$ is the *total* number of visits to $s$ and $G_k(s)$ is the return following the $k$-th such visit. By the law of large numbers, $\hat{V}_{\pi}(s) \to V_{\pi}(s)$ as $N_s \to \infty$.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark — definition; 1 mark — formula; 1 mark — convergence statement.)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Every-Visit MC and First-Visit MC. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of EVMC. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | First-Visit MC (FVMC) | Every-Visit MC (EVMC) |
|---|---|---|
| Sample inclusion | Only the first visit per episode | Every visit per episode |
| Independence of samples | Yes (across episodes) | No (correlated within an episode) |
| Convergence proof | Standard SLLN | Stronger SLLN on dependent sequences |

- **Advantage of EVMC:** Uses more samples → lower variance in practice for the same number of episodes.
- **Disadvantage of EVMC:** Within-episode autocorrelation can introduce bias under function approximation.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark — table/differences; 1 mark — advantage; 1 mark — disadvantage.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Confusing *episodes* with *visits*. EVMC averages over visits, not episodes.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Forgetting that EVMC is **not** applicable to continuing (non-episodic) tasks — only episodic ones.
> - **Common mistake #3:** Writing the return formula as $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} R_{t+k+1}$ without the discount factor $\gamma^k$ — board examiners deduct 1 full mark.
> - **Common mistake #4:** Mixing up incremental updates; remember the $1/n$ coefficient, not $1/N(s)$ after each step.
> - **Common mistake #5:** Not labelling axes/lists clearly in pseudocode — KTU allocates 2 marks for clean notation.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q.A (a)** Write the complete algorithm for Every-Visit Monte Carlo prediction. Explain each line. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```
ALGORITHM: Every-Visit MC Prediction (Estimation of V ≈ V_pi)

Input:  policy pi to be evaluated
Initialize:
    V(s) ← arbitrary value for all s ∈ S
    Returns(s) ← empty list, for all s ∈ S

Loop for each episode:
    Generate an episode following pi: S0, A0, R1, S1, A1, R2, ..., S_{T-1}, A_{T-1}, R_T
    G ← 0
    For t = T-1, T-2, ..., 0:                 # Backward recursion
        G ← γ·G + R_{t+1}                    # Compute return G_t
        Append G to Returns(S_t)             # EVERY visit, no check
    End For
End Loop

Output: V(s) ← average(Returns(s)) for all s ∈ S
```

**Explanation (line-by-line, 1 mark each significant line):**

1. **Input policy $\pi$** — EVMC prediction evaluates, does not optimise; hence the policy is supplied.
2. **Initialise $V(s)$ and $Returns(s)$** — Stores running value and the list of observed returns.
3. **Generate episode** — Sample an entire trajectory by acting under $\pi$.
4. **Backward recursion with $G \leftarrow \gamma G + R_{t+1}$** — Computes $G_t$ efficiently in $O(T)$ instead of $O(T^2)$.
5. **Append $G$ to $Returns(S_t)$ unconditionally** — The defining characteristic of EVMC.
6. **Average** — Final estimate is the sample mean.
7. **Return $V$** — Algorithm completes.

**(7 marks: 2 for pseudocode, 3 for line-by-line explanation, 2 for noting the "every-visit" appending.)**

---

**Q.A (b)** Consider a 3-visit scenario for state $s$. The returns following each visit are $G_1 = 5$, $G_2 = 15$, $G_3 = 10$. Compute the EVMC estimate using (i) the direct formula and (ii) the incremental update rule starting from $V_0(s) = 0$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Direct formula (3 marks):**

$$\hat{V}_{\text{EV}}(s) \;=\; \frac{G_1 + G_2 + G_3}{3} \;=\; \frac{5 + 15 + 10}{3} \;=\; \frac{30}{3} \;=\; 10$$

**(ii) Incremental update (4 marks):**

Initialisation: $\bar{V}_0(s) = 0$.

After first visit:

$$\bar{V}_1(s) \;=\; \bar{V}_0(s) + \tfrac{1}{1}\bigl[G_1 - \bar{V}_0(s)\bigr] \;=\; 0 + 1\cdot(5 - 0) \;=\; 5$$

After second visit:

$$\bar{V}_2(s) \;=\; \bar{V}_1(s) + \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[G_2 - \bar{V}_1(s)\bigr] \;=\; 5 + \tfrac{1}{2}(15 - 5) \;=\; 5 + 5 \;=\; 10$$

After third visit:

$$\bar{V}_3(s) \;=\; \bar{V}_2(s) + \tfrac{1}{3}\bigl[G_3 - \bar{V}_2(s)\bigr] \;=\; 10 + \tfrac{1}{3}(10 - 10) \;=\; 10 + 0 \;=\; 10$$

Both methods yield $\boxed{V(s) = 10}$, confirming internal consistency.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q.B (a)** Explain the convergence properties of Every-Visit Monte Carlo. State and prove its almost-sure convergence using the law of large numbers. Discuss whether the result still holds when the samples are autocorrelated within an episode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Convergence Statement (2 marks):**

For every state $s$, with probability 1:

$$\hat{V}^{\text{EV}}_n(s) \;\xrightarrow{\;a.s.\;}\; V_{\pi}(s) \quad \text{as } n \to \infty$$

where $n$ is the running count of visits.

**Proof Sketch via SLLN (3 marks):**

Each visit to $s$ yields a return sample $G_k(s)$. If we treat $\{G_k(s)\}$ as i.i.d. (which is true *across episodes* but not strictly within an episode), the standard Kolmogorov SLLN applies:

$$\mathbb{P}\!\Bigl(\lim_{n\to\infty}\tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} G_k(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}[G \mid S=s]\Bigr) \;=\; 1$$

Substituting $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}[G \mid S=s]$ completes the proof.

**Autocorrelation Discussion (2 marks):**

When $s$ is visited multiple times within the *same* episode, the corresponding returns share a common suffix of the trajectory and are therefore correlated. However, the ergodic theorem for stationary sequences (under mild mixing conditions) extends SLLN to such dependent samples, guaranteeing convergence provided the MDP is ergodic and $\pi$ is fixed. In practice, EVMC converges robustly; the theoretical caveat is more relevant under **function approximation**, where correlated updates can cause divergence (cf. Baird's counterexample).

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain the application of Every-Visit MC in Monte Carlo Control using the **Exploring Starts** assumption. Write the corresponding pseudocode and discuss the role of the $\varepsilon$-soft variant in removing the exploring-starts assumption. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Exploring Starts (2 marks):**

In Monte Carlo Control, we evaluate *and* improve the policy iteratively:

$$\pi_{k+1}(s) \;=\; \arg\max_a Q_{\pi_k}(s,a)$$

To ensure every $(s,a)$ pair is visited infinitely often, the **Exploring Starts** assumption posits that every episode begins with a random state-action pair $(s_0, a_0)$.

**Pseudocode (3 marks):**

```
ALGORITHM: Every-Visit MC Control with Exploring Starts

Initialize:
    Q(s, a) ← arbitrary, for all s ∈ S, a ∈ A(s)
    Returns(s, a) ← empty list
    π(s) ← arbitrary, with ties broken consistently

Loop for each episode:
    Randomly choose S0, A0 such that all pairs have prob > 0
    Generate episode following π from (S0, A0)
    G ← 0
    For t = T-1 down to 0:
        G ← γ·G + R_{t+1}
        Append G to Returns(S_t, A_t)     # EVERY visit (EV-MC)
        Q(S_t, A_t) ← average(Returns(S_t, A_t))
        A* ← argmax_a Q(S_t, a)
        For all a ∈ A(S_t):
            π(a | S_t) ← 1 if a = A*, else 0
End Loop
```

**$\varepsilon$-soft Variant (2 marks):**

Exploring Starts is impractical in real systems (you cannot reset the world). Replacing greedy improvement with an **$\varepsilon$-soft** policy:

$$\pi(a \mid s) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 - \varepsilon + \tfrac{\varepsilon}{\vert A(s)\vert} & \text{if } a = \arg\max_a Q(s,a) \\ \tfrac{\varepsilon}{\vert A(s)\vert} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

guarantees every action has non-zero probability, eliminating the exploring-starts assumption while still converging to the optimal $\varepsilon$-soft policy (and, in the limit, the optimal policy as $\varepsilon \to 0$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision checklist for EVMC:**

- ☐ **Definition:** EVMC averages returns after *every* visit to a state across episodes.
- ☐ **Estimator:** $\hat{V}(s) = \frac{1}{N(s)}\sum_{k=1}^{N(s)} G_k(s)$.
- ☐ **Incremental update:** $\bar{V}_n(s) = \bar{V}_{n-1}(s) + \tfrac{1}{n}[G_n - \bar{V}_{n-1}(s)]$.
- ☐ **Constant-$\alpha$ update:** $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha[G - V(s)]$, used for non-stationary problems.
- ☐ **Convergence:** Almost-sure convergence to $V_\pi(s)$ by SLLN (and ergodic theorem for autocorrelated samples).
- ☐ **Applies only to episodic tasks** — return $G_t$ must be computable at episode end.
- ☐ **No bootstrapping** — unlike TD(0), EVMC does not update until the episode terminates.
- ☐ **EVMC vs FVMC:** EVMC → lower variance, more data; FVMC → independent samples, simpler bias analysis.
- ☐ **Bias under function approximation:** EVMC can diverge (Baird); FVMC has cleaner theoretical guarantees.
- ☐ **Control extension:** Used with **Exploring Starts** or **$\varepsilon$-soft** policies for MC control.
- ☐ **Latex safety:** Use `\mid` instead of `|` when writing conditional expectations inside markdown tables.
- ☐ **Board trick:** Always write the algorithm with `for each episode` outer loop and `for t = T-1 down to 0` inner loop — examiners expect this exact structure.
- ☐ **Numerical safety:** After each incremental update, verify the final value matches the direct mean — a common sub-question check.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temporal Difference Learning (TD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Temporal Difference Learning (TD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3: TEMPORAL DIFFERENCE (TD) LEARNING

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> **Temporal Difference (TD) Learning** is a model-free, central, and foundational method in Reinforcement Learning (RL) that combines the best properties of **Monte Carlo (MC) methods** and **Dynamic Programming (DP)**. It learns the value function $V_\pi(s)$ (or $Q_\pi(s,a)$) directly from raw experience in the form of sampled trajectories (state, action, reward sequences), **without requiring a complete model of the environment's dynamics** ($P(s' \vert s,a)$ and $R(s,a,s')$). It updates its value estimates based on the difference between successive predictions — the *temporal difference error* — rather than waiting for the final outcome of an episode (as in MC) or performing a full one-step exhaustive look-ahead over all successor states (as in DP).

Mathematically, the core update rule for state-value estimation under policy $\pi$ is given by:

$$
V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t) \big]
$$

where:
- $V(s_t)$ = current estimate of the value of state $s_t$
- $\alpha$ = **learning rate** (step size), with $0 < \alpha \leq 1$
- $\gamma$ = **discount factor**, with $0 \leq \gamma \leq 1$
- $R_{t+1}$ = immediate reward observed after taking action $a_t$ in state $s_t$
- $V(s_{t+1})$ = estimated value of the next state
- The bracketed term is called the **TD Error**, denoted $\delta_t$

---

### 🌟 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: Predicting Your Daily Bus Arrival

Imagine you take the **8:00 AM bus to college every day**. You don't have a perfect schedule (so no full "model" of traffic), but you can observe what happens each day.

| Day | Predicted Arrival | Actual Arrival | Surprise / "TD Error" |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8:30 AM | 8:35 AM | +5 min late (under-prediction) |
| 2 | 8:32 AM | 8:34 AM | +2 min (still slightly off) |
| 3 | 8:33 AM | 8:33 AM | 0 min (perfect prediction) |

You are essentially performing **TD learning**:
- You form a **prediction** of the arrival time $V(s_t)$ at the start of the journey.
- Once you **see the next bus stop or landmark** ($s_{t+1}$), you refine your guess.
- You don't need to wait until you reach the destination — every intermediate observation gives you feedback.
- The difference between your revised estimate and the old one is the **temporal difference error** $\delta_t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** TD Learning is the conceptual heart of the textbook *Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction* by Sutton & Barto. It is the foundation for advanced algorithms like **Q-Learning, SARSA, and DQN**, which dominate modern RL applications in robotics, game AI (AlphaGo), and autonomous systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Supervised Learning:** In supervised learning, the "target" is a ground-truth label. In TD learning, the "target" $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1})$ is itself an **estimate**, making TD a *bootstrapping* method. This makes TD powerful (sample-efficient) but also introduces **bias** into the learning process.

---

### 🎯 Why TD Learning is Central to RL

- **Model-free:** No prior knowledge of transition probabilities required.
- **Online & Incremental:** Learns after every single step (no need for episode termination).
- **Bootstrapping:** Uses current value estimates to improve value estimates — same principle as DP.
- **Convergence Guarantees:** Under appropriate conditions (Robbins-Monro on $\alpha$, every state visited infinitely often), TD(0) **converges to the true $V_\pi$**.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of RL Value Estimation

| Method | Requires Model? | Bootstraps? | Updates Per Step? | Bias | Variance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dynamic Programming (DP)** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (full sweep) | ✅ Low (with true model) | ✅ Low |
| **Monte Carlo (MC)** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No (end of episode) | ✅ Unbiased | ❌ High |
| **Temporal Difference (TD)** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (every step) | ⚠️ Some (bootstrap estimate) | ⚠️ Lower than MC |

> [!NOTE]
> **TD is the "sweet spot"** that is **model-free** (unlike DP) and **low-variance & online** (unlike MC). This dual advantage is why TD forms the backbone of nearly all modern RL algorithms.

---

## 2.2 The TD Error — The Heart of the Algorithm

The **Temporal Difference Error** $\delta_t$ is the central quantity that drives all learning in TD methods:

$$
\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** $\delta_t$ measures the *"surprise"* or *"innovation"* between the old estimate $V(s_t)$ and the newly refined target $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1})$. If $\delta_t = 0$, the agent's prediction was perfectly consistent with the new observation; if $\delta_t \neq 0$, the agent must update its belief.

---

## 2.3 TD(0) — The One-Step TD Algorithm

**TD(0)** is the simplest form of TD learning, where updates are made immediately after a single transition. It directly mirrors the formal update equation shown in Section 1.

**Operational steps for TD(0):**
1. Initialize $V(s)$ arbitrarily (e.g., $0$) for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$.
2. For each episode:
   a. Observe current state $s_t$.
   b. Take action $a_t \sim \pi(\cdot \vert s_t)$.
   c. Observe reward $R_{t+1}$ and next state $s_{t+1}$.
   d. Compute TD error: $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$.
   e. Update: $V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha \cdot \delta_t$.
   f. Set $s_t \leftarrow s_{t+1}$ and repeat.

---

## 2.4 n-Step TD Returns — Generalizing the Lookahead Horizon

Pure TD(0) only looks **one step ahead** into the future. Pure MC waits until the **end of the episode**. The **n-step return** generalizes this by looking exactly $n$ steps ahead:

$$
G_t^{(n)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n V(s_{t+n})
$$

The corresponding n-step TD update rule is:

$$
V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha \big[ G_t^{(n)} - V(s_t) \big]
$$

Special cases:
- $n = 1$ → reduces to **TD(0)**
- $n = T - t$ → reduces to the **Monte Carlo return**

---

## 2.5 TD(λ) — The Unified Algorithm with Eligibility Traces

To combine the benefits of all n-step returns simultaneously, **TD(λ)** uses an exponentially weighted average of all n-step returns, controlled by the parameter $\lambda \in [0, 1]$:

$$
G_t^\lambda = (1 - \lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t
$$

The update for $V(s_t)$ under TD(λ) is:

$$
V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha \big[ G_t^\lambda - V(s_t) \big]
$$

---

### 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Symbols | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| TD(0) Update | $V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha \, \delta_t$ | $\alpha$ = step size | Single-step lookahead |
| TD Error | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$ | $\delta_t$ = TD error | "Surprise" signal |
| n-step Return | $G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma^{i-1} R_{t+i} + \gamma^n V(s_{t+n})$ | $n$ = horizon | Generalizes TD(0) and MC |
| λ-Return | $G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t$ | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ | Average over n-step returns |
| Eligibility Trace | $E_t(s) = \gamma \lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(s = s_t)$ | $E_t$ = trace | Decaying memory of visits |
| Forward-view TD(λ) | $\Delta V(s_t) = \alpha \big[ G_t^\lambda - V(s_t) \big]$ | — | Theoretical, uses future rewards |
| Backward-view TD(λ) | $\delta_t \cdot E_t(s)$ for all $s$ | — | Online, causal, equivalent |
| Bellman Optimality (TD target) | $R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(s_{t+1}, a)$ | Used in Q-Learning | Greedy lookahead |
| Convergence Condition | $\sum_t \alpha_t(s) = \infty$ and $\sum_t \alpha_t^2(s) < \infty$ | Robbins-Monro | Both conditions required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Condition for KTU Exams:** For **TD(0) convergence to the true $V_\pi$**, you must mention the **Robbins-Monro conditions** on the learning rate: $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t(s) = \infty$ AND $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2(s) < \infty$, for all states $s$. Examiners award marks specifically for this!

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of TD Learning |
|---|---|
| **Robotic Locomotion** | Teaching quadruped robots to walk via reward shaping |
| **Autonomous Driving (Waymo, Tesla)** | Policy optimization for lane-keeping and obstacle avoidance |
| **Game AI (AlphaGo, AlphaZero)** | Self-play policy evaluation via TD-style value networks |
| **Network Packet Routing** | Adaptive routing in telecommunications |
| **Inventory & Supply Chain** | Dynamic pricing and stock replenishment |
| **Energy Grid Management** | Smart grid load balancing |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Monte Carlo Return to TD(0) Update

We start from the **Monte Carlo target** (uses complete episodic return):

$$
G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots + \gamma^{T-t-1} R_T
$$

This can be split as:

$$
G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \big[ R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \dots + \gamma^{T-t-2} R_T \big]
$$

Recognizing the bracketed term as the MC return starting at $t+1$:

$$
G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}
$$

This is a **recursive identity** — mathematically exact. Now, in MC we use the true $G_{t+1}$ as a sample. In TD, we **replace it with our current estimate $V(s_{t+1})$**:

$$
\hat{G}_t \approx R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1})
$$

Substituting into the standard incremental update rule $V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha [G - V(s_t)]$:

$$
V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t) \big]
$$

Which is exactly the **TD(0) update rule** with the **TD error** $\delta_t$ as the correction term.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: TD(0) as a Stochastic Gradient Descent on the Bellman Error

Define the **Bellman Error** for a given transition $(s, R, s')$ as the deviation from the Bellman equation:

$$
e_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t) = \delta_t
$$

If we take the squared error $\mathcal{L}(V) = \frac{1}{2} \delta_t^2$ and compute the gradient with respect to $V(s_t)$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial V(s_t)} = - \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t) \big] = -\delta_t
$$

Performing gradient descent (in the negative gradient direction):

$$
V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) - \alpha \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial V(s_t)} = V(s_t) + \alpha \, \delta_t
$$

Which is again the **TD(0) update**. So TD(0) can be interpreted as **online stochastic gradient descent** on a mean-squared Bellman error surrogate.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalence of Forward View TD(λ) and Backward View (Eligibility Traces)

### Forward View (Theoretical)

The forward view uses the **λ-return** as the target:

$$
G_t^\lambda = (1 - \lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t
$$

where each $G_t^{(n)}$ is an n-step truncated return bootstrapping with $V(s_{t+n})$.

### Backward View (Implementation-Ready)

The backward view maintains a per-state scalar **eligibility trace** $E_t(s)$ that records how recently and how often state $s$ has been visited, decaying geometrically:

$$
E_t(s) = \gamma \lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ is 1 if $s$ was the state at time $t$, else 0.
- $\gamma \lambda$ is the trace decay factor.
- $\lambda$ controls the memory horizon.

The backward-view TD(λ) update is:

$$
V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \, \delta_t \, E_t(s) \quad \forall s
$$

This is applied **to all states simultaneously at every timestep**, with the size of the update proportional to both the TD error and the state's recent visitation history. **Sutton & Barto proved** that, taken as an aggregate over an episode, the backward view makes exactly the same total update to each state as the forward view (this is the famous "**forward-backward equivalence**").

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: TD(0) on a 3-State Markov Chain

**Setup:** Consider a deterministic Markov chain with states A, B, C, terminal.

**True value function:** $V_\pi(A) = 6$, $V_\pi(B) = 2$, $V_\pi(C) = -1$ (under policy $\pi$ that walks A → B → C → terminate).

**Observed trajectory (single episode):** A → B (reward 2), B → C (reward 1), C → terminal (reward 0)

**Initial estimates:** $V(A) = 0$, $V(B) = 0$, $V(C) = 0$

**Hyperparameters:** $\alpha = 0.5$, $\gamma = 1.0$

---

**Step 1:** Transition from A to B with reward 2.

Compute TD error:
$$
\delta_A = R_{A \to B} + \gamma V(B) - V(A) = 2 + 1.0 \cdot 0 - 0 = 2
$$

Update $V(A)$:
$$
V(A) \leftarrow V(A) + \alpha \cdot \delta_A = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 2 = 1
$$

Result: $V(A) = 1$, $V(B) = 0$, $V(C) = 0$

---

**Step 2:** Transition from B to C with reward 1.

Compute TD error:
$$
\delta_B = R_{B \to C} + \gamma V(C) - V(B) = 1 + 1.0 \cdot 0 - 0 = 1
$$

Update $V(B)$:
$$
V(B) \leftarrow V(B) + \alpha \cdot \delta_B = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.5
$$

Result: $V(A) = 1$, $V(B) = 0.5$, $V(C) = 0$

---

**Step 3:** Transition from C to terminal with reward 0.

Compute TD error (no future state, treat $V(\text{terminal}) = 0$):
$$
\delta_C = R_{C \to T} + \gamma V(T) - V(C) = 0 + 0 - 0 = 0
$$

Update $V(C)$:
$$
V(C) \leftarrow V(C) + \alpha \cdot \delta_C = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 0
$$

Result: $V(A) = 1$, $V(B) = 0.5$, $V(C) = 0$

---

**Final estimates after one episode:**

| State | True $V_\pi$ | Estimated $V$ | Error |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 6 | 1 | -5 |
| B | 2 | 0.5 | -1.5 |
| C | -1 | 0 | +1 |

After many more episodes, the values will **converge** to the true values (6, 2, -1) given proper $\alpha$ decay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Notice how even though only one episode was observed, the estimates are non-zero and moving in the right direction. This is the power of online TD learning — it makes progress on every single step.

---

## 3.5 Convergence Theorem Statement (KTU High-Yield)

> **Theorem (TD(0) Convergence):** For a fixed policy $\pi$, if the step sizes $\alpha_t$ satisfy the Robbins-Monro conditions $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum_t \alpha_t^2 < \infty$, and if all states are visited infinitely often, then TD(0) **converges with probability 1** to the true value function $V_\pi$.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TD Learning Operational Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize V s arbitrarily] --> B[Observe state s t]
    B --> C[Take action a t following policy pi]
    C --> D[Observe reward R t+1 and next state s t+1]
    D --> E[Compute TD Error: delta t = R t+1 + gamma V s t+1 - V s t]
    E --> F[Update Value: V s t = V s t + alpha times delta t]
    F --> G{Is Episode Done?}
    G -- No --> B
    G -- Yes --> H[End of Episode: Optional Decay alpha and gamma]
    H --> I[Start New Episode]
    I --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram captures the **online, per-step nature** of TD learning. Compare with Monte Carlo, which would only update at the `Episode Done` node.

---

## 4.2 Backup Diagram Comparison: TD vs MC vs DP

The **backup diagram** is a canonical KTU/RL visualization showing how each method propagates value information backward through the state space.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TDZero[TD 0 - One Step]
        S0[S t] -- R t+1 --> S1[S t+1]
    end
    subgraph TDn[n-Step TD]
        S0n[S t] -- R t+1 --> S1n[S t+1]
        S1n -- R t+2 --> S2n[S t+2]
        S2n -. R t+n .-> Sn[S t+n]
    end
    subgraph MC[Monte Carlo - Full Episode]
        S0m[S t] -- R t+1 --> S1m[S t+1]
        S1m -- R t+2 --> S2m[S t+2]
        S2m -- R t+n --> Snm[S t+n]
        Snm -- R T --> STm[Terminal]
    end
```

**Interpretation:**
- **TD(0) backup:** Uses **1 sampled successor** + bootstrap estimate $V(s_{t+1})$.
- **n-Step TD backup:** Uses **n sampled successors** + bootstrap at step $n$.
- **Monte Carlo backup:** Uses the **complete sampled trajectory** — no bootstrap, but high variance.

---

## 4.3 Architectural Flow: TD(λ) with Eligibility Traces

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Environment Transition: s t, a t, R t+1, s t+1] --> B[Compute TD Error delta t]
    B --> C[Update Eligibility Traces: E t s = gamma lambda E t-1 s + 1 if s = s t]
    C --> D[Apply Decoupled Update: V s = V s + alpha delta t E t s for all s]
    D --> E[Next Time Step t+1]
    E --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **eligibility trace mechanism** is what allows TD(λ) to update **all recently visited states** with credit proportional to their recency — implementing the exponential memory decay of the forward-view λ-return in a **causal, online** manner.

---

## 4.4 Comparison of Bias vs Variance Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pure Bootstrap: TD 0] --> B[High Bias, Low Variance]
    C[Pure Sampling: Monte Carlo] --> D[Low Bias, High Variance]
    E[TD lambda: Best of Both] --> F[Balanced Bias-Variance Trade-off]
    A --- C
    B --- D
    F --> G[Tune lambda between 0 and 1 to dial bias vs variance]
```

This block captures a fundamental principle: by **tuning $\lambda$** between 0 and 1, the practitioner can smoothly interpolate between TD(0) (more bootstrap, lower variance, higher bias) and Monte Carlo (less bootstrap, higher variance, lower bias).

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define Temporal Difference (TD) Learning. How does it differ from Monte Carlo and Dynamic Programming methods?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Temporal Difference (TD) Learning is a model-free reinforcement learning method that learns the value function $V_\pi(s)$ by updating estimates based on the difference between successive state-value predictions, known as the **TD error** $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$.

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Property | Monte Carlo | Dynamic Programming | TD Learning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Model required? | No | Yes | No |
| Bootstrap used? | No (waits for episode end) | Yes (uses $V(s')$ directly) | Yes (uses $V(s_{t+1})$ estimate) |
| Online update? | No (end of episode) | Yes (full sweep) | Yes (every step) |
| Variance | High | Low | Medium-Low |
| Bias | Unbiased | Low (with true model) | Some bias (bootstrap) |

TD combines MC's **model-free nature** with DP's **bootstrapping efficiency**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Write the TD(0) update rule and explain the significance of the TD error $\delta_t$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Update rule (1.5 marks):**

$$
V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t) \big]
$$

**Significance of $\delta_t$ (1.5 marks):**
- $\delta_t$ is the **innovation** or **surprise** signal — the discrepancy between the current estimate $V(s_t)$ and the new target $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1})$.
- It **drives the learning**: when $\delta_t = 0$, the current estimate is perfectly consistent with the new observation and no update is needed.
- $\delta_t$ can be **positive** (current estimate was too low → increase $V(s_t)$) or **negative** (current estimate was too high → decrease $V(s_t)$).
- It is the central quantity in algorithms like Q-Learning and SARSA, and its expected value is the Bellman error used in convergence proofs.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks, with internal choice (you answer either full Question A or full Question B).

---

### **🅰️ Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: With suitable equations, explain the Temporal Difference Learning algorithm. Derive the TD(0) update rule and show its equivalence to one-step lookahead. Also discuss the convergence properties.**

#### **Part (a) — TD(0) Algorithm & Derivation (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: The TD(0) update rule (2 marks)**

The fundamental TD(0) update equation is:

$$
V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t) \big]
$$

where the bracketed term is the TD error $\delta_t$:

$$
\delta_t \doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)
$$

**Step 2: Derivation from the Bellman equation (3 marks)**

The Bellman equation for the state-value function under policy $\pi$ is:

$$
V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \vert s, a) \big[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \big]
$$

If we take a **single sample** $(s_t, a_t, R_{t+1}, s_{t+1})$ from this distribution, the inner expression becomes a sample-based estimate:

$$
\hat{V}_\pi(s_t) \approx R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1})
$$

Using this as a noisy unbiased target for $V(s_t)$ in an incremental update:

$$
V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha \big[ \hat{V}_\pi(s_t) - V(s_t) \big]
$$

Substituting the sample-based estimate:

$$
V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t) \big]
$$

This is the **TD(0) update** — it performs a **one-step lookahead**: it uses the actual sampled reward $R_{t+1}$ combined with the current estimate $V(s_{t+1})$ as a proxy for the true value $V_\pi(s_t)$.

**Step 3: Operational algorithm (2 marks)**

1. Initialize $V(s) = 0$ for all $s$.
2. For each episode:
   - Observe $s_t$.
   - Take action $a_t \sim \pi$, observe $R_{t+1}$ and $s_{t+1}$.
   - Compute $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$.
   - Update $V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha \delta_t$.
   - $s_t \leftarrow s_{t+1}$ and continue.

#### **Part (b) — Convergence Properties & Comparison (7 marks)**

**Convergence Theorem (3 marks):**

**TD(0) converges with probability 1 to the true $V_\pi$** provided:
- The step sizes $\alpha_t$ satisfy the **Robbins-Monro conditions**:
$$
\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t(s) = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2(s) < \infty
$$
- All states are visited infinitely often (guaranteed under any GLIE / exploring-starts assumption).
- The policy $\pi$ is fixed (policy evaluation setting).

**Why does this work? (2 marks):**
- TD(0) minimizes the **Mean Squared Projected Bellman Error (MSPBE)**: it finds the value function closest to satisfying the Bellman equation in $\ell_2$ norm.
- The stochastic updates are essentially **noisy gradient descent** on this objective, and under Robbins-Monro conditions, stochastic approximation theory guarantees convergence.

**Comparison with MC and DP (2 marks):**

| Aspect | TD(0) | MC | DP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sample efficiency | High (each step) | Low (waits) | N/A (uses model) |
| Variance | Low | High | None |
| Bias | Bootstrap bias | Unbiased | Zero (with true model) |
| Convergence speed | Fast in practice | Slow (high variance) | Fastest (with model) |

---

### **🅱️ Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: Explain n-step TD and TD(λ) algorithms. Derive the λ-return equation and discuss the role of eligibility traces. How does TD(λ) combine the benefits of TD and Monte Carlo?**

#### **Part (a) — n-Step Returns and TD(λ) Derivation (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: n-step return definition (2 marks)**

The n-step return looks $n$ steps into the future, then bootstraps:

$$
G_t^{(n)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n V(s_{t+n})
$$

The corresponding n-step TD update is:

$$
V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha \big[ G_t^{(n)} - V(s_t) \big]
$$

**Step 2: λ-return as a weighted average (3 marks)**

To combine all n-step returns (from $n=1$ to the end of the episode), weight them geometrically with parameter $\lambda \in [0, 1]$:

$$
G_t^\lambda = (1 - \lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t
$$

The weights $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$ sum to 1 over the finite horizon:

$$
\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} (1-\lambda) \lambda^{n-1} + \lambda^{T-t-1} = 1
$$

**Special cases:**
- $\lambda = 0$ → $G_t^\lambda = G_t^{(1)}$ (reduces to **TD(0)**)
- $\lambda = 1$ → $G_t^\lambda = G_t$ (reduces to **Monte Carlo**)

**Step 3: Forward-view TD(λ) update (2 marks)**

$$
V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha \big[ G_t^\lambda - V(s_t) \big]
$$

#### **Part (b) — Eligibility Traces & Backward View (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Eligibility trace definition (2 marks)**

The eligibility trace $E_t(s)$ is a per-state scalar that records recent visits:

$$
E_t(s) = \gamma \lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)
$$

with initial $E_0(s) = 0$.

- Each time state $s$ is visited, its trace is incremented by 1.
- At every step, **all traces decay** by factor $\gamma \lambda$.
- This produces an **exponentially decaying memory** of past visits.

**Step 2: Backward-view TD(λ) update (3 marks)**

At every timestep $t$:

1. Compute the TD error:
$$
\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)
$$

2. Update eligibility trace for current state:
$$
E_t(s_t) \leftarrow E_t(s_t) + 1
$$

3. Apply update to **all states simultaneously**:
$$
V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \, \delta_t \, E_t(s) \quad \forall s
$$

**Step 3: Forward-Backward Equivalence (2 marks)**

Sutton & Barto proved that, when summed over an entire episode, the **backward-view TD(λ)** produces exactly the **same total update** to each state as the **forward-view TD(λ)**. This is the famous **forward-backward equivalence theorem**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The backward view is **causal and online** (uses only information available up to time $t$), while the forward view is **theoretical** (requires the full episode). The equivalence shows that one can implement the forward-view update causally using the backward-view mechanism.

**Why TD(λ) is "Best of Both Worlds" (extra credit, 1 mark):**
- **Low variance** (compared to MC) due to bootstrap.
- **Low bias** (compared to TD(0)) due to deeper lookahead.
- **Credit assignment** is efficient via eligibility traces.
- **Tunable trade-off**: by adjusting $\lambda$, the practitioner can dial in the desired bias-variance balance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing TD(0) and TD(λ):** Students often write $\lambda$ in the TD(0) update. **TD(0) has NO $\lambda$ term** — that appears only in TD(λ) or eligibility traces.
> 2. **Forgetting convergence conditions:** Just stating "TD converges" is **not enough**. You MUST write the **Robbins-Monro conditions** explicitly: $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$ AND $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$.
> 3. **Mixing up MC and TD formulas:** The MC update uses the **full return** $G_t = \sum_k \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$; the TD update uses the **one-step target** $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1})$.
> 4. **Forgetting the terminal state value:** In backup diagrams and examples, the terminal state's value is $V(\text{terminal}) = 0$ by definition — don't bootstrap from it.
> 5. **Writing $V_\pi$ instead of $V$ during updates:** $V$ is the current estimate, $V_\pi$ is the true target. Conflating them loses marks.
> 6. **Eligibility trace sign error:** The trace increments **before** decay in some formulations and **after** decay in others — pick one and stick with it consistently.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ TD Learning = **model-free + bootstrapping + online** value estimation.
- ✅ Core update: $V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha \delta_t$, where $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$.
- ✅ TD(0) = **one-step** lookahead; n-step TD = **n-step** lookahead; TD(λ) = **geometric average** of all n-step returns.
- ✅ $\lambda = 0$ → TD(0); $\lambda = 1$ → Monte Carlo.
- ✅ Eligibility trace: $E_t(s) = \gamma \lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ — implements backward-view TD(λ) online.
- ✅ **Forward-backward equivalence**: backward-view TD(λ) ≡ forward-view TD(λ) when summed over an episode.
- ✅ **Convergence requires Robbins-Monro conditions** on $\alpha_t$ and infinite state visitation.
- ✅ TD combines MC's **model-free** advantage with DP's **bootstrap** advantage.
- ✅ The TD error $\delta_t$ is the **gradient of squared Bellman error** — TD(0) is **stochastic gradient descent** on the Bellman error surrogate.
- ✅ Applications: robotics, game AI, autonomous systems, network routing, energy grids.
- ✅ **Key exam keywords to use**: "bootstrapping", "model-free", "temporal difference error", "eligibility trace", "fixed-policy evaluation", "GLIE", "on-policy".

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Learning to Estimate from Multiple Steps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Learning to Estimate from Multiple Steps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Learning to Estimate from Multiple Steps** in Reinforcement Learning refers to a family of Temporal Difference (TD) methods that bootstrap value estimates by looking ahead **n steps** into the future, rather than relying on a single transition (TD(0)) or waiting until the end of an episode (Monte Carlo). This unified family of algorithms is collectively called **n-step TD Prediction**, and its elegant synthesis using **eligibility traces** is known as **TD(λ)**.

The central idea is the construction of an **n-step return** $G_{t:t+n}$, which estimates the expected return from time $t$ by summing the next $n$ actual rewards plus the bootstrapped value of the state reached after $n$ steps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECMT745 / Module 3):** This topic bridges Monte Carlo methods and one-step TD. It covers **n-step TD Prediction**, the **n-step SARSA** control algorithm, **off-policy n-step methods with importance sampling**, and the unifying **TD(λ) framework** with eligibility traces.

### 1.2 Intuition: A "Look-Ahead" Balance

Imagine you are trying to estimate how much total tip money you will earn during a 10-shift working week:

- **Monte Carlo (MC)**: You wait until the end of all 10 shifts, then look at your actual total tip. Highly accurate, but you only learn **after** 10 days of work.
- **One-step TD (TD(0))**: After every single shift, you predict next shift's tips based on today's tip. Very quick, but very noisy.
- **n-step TD (e.g., n = 3)**: After every 3 shifts, you update your running estimate using the actual tips from the last 3 shifts and your best guess for the value *after* those 3 shifts.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Trade-off:** Increasing $n$ gives a **more accurate, less biased** target (closer to MC) but introduces **higher variance** and **slower learning** (delayed update). Decreasing $n$ gives **faster, low-variance** updates (closer to TD(0)) but with **higher bias**. The optimal $n$ depends on the environment.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Look-ahead horizon | $n$ | Number of future steps used to form the target |
| n-step Return | $G_{t:t+n}$ | Bootstrapped estimate of return from time $t$ |
| n-step TD Error | $\delta_t$ | Difference between n-step return and current estimate |
| Trace-decay parameter | $\lambda$ | Geometric weighting of n-step returns in TD(λ) |
| Eligibility trace | $E_t(s)$ | Accumulator tracking recent visitation of state $s$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The n-step Return — Conceptual Breakdown

The n-step return $G_{t:t+n}$ is constructed by summing the first $n$ actual rewards observed from time $t$, and then appending the bootstrapped value estimate of the state $S_{t+n}$:

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Observe reward sequence $R_{t+1}, R_{t+2}, \dots, R_{t+n}$ from the environment.
2. Apply the discount factor $\gamma^{k-1}$ to the reward received $k$ steps ahead.
3. Terminate the sum after $n$ real rewards.
4. Bootstrap the rest of the return with $V(S_{t+n})$ (on-policy) or some target estimate.

For the boundary cases:
- If $t+n < T$ (episode continues past $n$ steps): full $n$-step formula applies.
- If $t+n \geq T$ (terminal state reached before $n$ steps): $G_{t:t+n} = G_t$ (becomes Monte Carlo).

### 2.2 The Value Update Rule

Once the n-step return is computed, the value function is updated toward it using a learning rate $\alpha$:

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ G_{t:t+n} - V(S_t) \right]$$

The error term $G_{t:t+n} - V(S_t)$ is called the **n-step TD error**, and it forms the foundation of all higher-order TD methods.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Purpose / Remarks |
|---|-------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Discounted n-step return (on-policy) | $G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$ | Core target for n-step TD |
| 2 | Terminal-state truncation | $G_{t:t+n} = G_t$ if $t+n \geq T$ | MC fallback near episode end |
| 3 | n-step value update | $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ G_{t:t+n} - V(S_t) \right]$ | Learning step |
| 4 | One-step TD(0) error | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ | $n=1$ special case |
| 5 | n-step SARSA action-value target | $G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$ | Used for control |
| 6 | Off-policy importance sampling ratio | $\rho_{t:t+n-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{\min(t+n-1, T-1)} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}$ | Corrects distribution mismatch |
| 7 | Off-policy n-step return | $G_{t:t+n}^{\pi} = \rho_{t:t+n-1} \left( R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n} Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n}) \right)$ | Targets policy $\pi$, behaves $\mu$ |
| 8 | λ-return (TD(λ) forward view) | $G_t^{\lambda} = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_{t:t+n} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t$ | Geometric average over all n |
| 9 | Eligibility trace update | $E_t(s) = \gamma \lambda \, E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ | Accumulates recent visits |
| 10 | TD(λ) backward update | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \, \delta_t \, E_t(s)$ | One update per step, all states |
| 11 | TD(0) limit | $\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow G_t^{\lambda} = G_{t:t+1}$ | One-step TD |
| 12 | MC limit | $\lambda = 1 \Rightarrow G_t^{\lambda} = G_t$ | Full Monte Carlo |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL — Avoid Markdown Table Breaks:** The pipe symbol `|` inside math has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` only where the formula does not contain `|`. In the table above, plain mathematical notation like $V(S_t)$ and $\frac{\pi}{\mu}$ contains only standard LaTeX fractions — no raw `|` characters are used.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics & Continuous Control:** n-step methods are used in Deep RL (e.g., A3C, PPO) where multi-step returns reduce the bias of single-step bootstrapping in high-dimensional state spaces.
- **Finance (Portfolio Optimization):** TD(λ) helps credit long-term delayed rewards across trading horizons.
- **Game AI (AlphaGo, Atari):** n-step SARSA and eligibility-trace variants accelerate credit assignment over thousands of moves.
- **Autonomous Driving:** Multi-step prediction stabilizes lane-change value estimates before safety-critical decisions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the n-step Return

We begin with the standard discounted return definition:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \cdots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$$

**Step 1 — Truncate after $n$ real rewards.**

Replace all rewards beyond index $t+n$ with a bootstrapped estimate. The first $n$ actual rewards are:

$$R_{t+1}, \; R_{t+2}, \; R_{t+3}, \; \dots, \; R_{t+n}$$

Apply the appropriate discount factor to each:

$$\gamma^{0} R_{t+1} + \gamma^{1} R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$$

**Step 2 — Append the bootstrapped value.**

After $n$ steps, the agent is in state $S_{t+n}$. Its true value is approximated by $V(S_{t+n})$. This term must be discounted by $\gamma^{n}$ because it represents a sum starting $n$ steps in the future:

$$\text{bootstrap contribution} = \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$$

**Step 3 — Combine into the n-step return.**

$$G_{t:t+n} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$$

**Step 4 — Verify the boundary cases.**

- For $n = 1$: $G_{t:t+1} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ — exactly the **TD(0) target**.
- For $n = T - t$: $G_{t:t+n} = G_t$ — the **Monte Carlo return** (no bootstrapping).
- For $n = \infty$: equivalent to full MC if the episode is finite.

### 3.2 Derivation of the λ-Return (TD(λ) Forward View)

The n-step returns can be combined into a single, comprehensive target by taking a geometric average:

**Step 1 — Assign weights to each n-step return.**

Assign weight $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$ to $G_{t:t+n}$. The geometric series must sum to 1, so the terminal MC return $G_t$ receives the leftover weight $\lambda^{T-t-1}$:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} (1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1} + \lambda^{T-t-1} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Form the weighted combination.**

$$G_t^{\lambda} = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_{t:t+n} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t$$

**Step 3 — Confirm the limiting cases.**

$$\lambda = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; G_t^{\lambda} = G_{t:t+1} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) \quad \text{(TD(0))}$$

$$\lambda = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; G_t^{\lambda} = G_t \quad \text{(Monte Carlo)}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Backward-View Eligibility Trace Update

The **forward view** (λ-return) is theoretically clean but not implementable online because it requires waiting until the end of the episode. The **backward view** is its online equivalent.

**Step 1 — Define the eligibility trace.**

The trace $E_t(s)$ tracks how recently and how often state $s$ was visited. Increment by 1 on visit, decay by $\gamma \lambda$ otherwise:

$$E_t(s) = \gamma \lambda \, E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the one-step TD error at time $t$.**

$$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$$

**Step 3 — Update all states proportionally to their eligibility.**

$$V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \, \delta_t \, E_t(s), \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$$

**Why this works (intuition):** When a TD error $\delta_t$ is observed at time $t$, every state visited in the recent past (within roughly $1/(\gamma\lambda)$ steps) gets a credit/blame proportional to its recency. This is exactly the geometric weighting of the λ-return, but spread incrementally as new errors arrive.

### 3.4 Off-Policy n-step Return with Importance Sampling

When the **target policy** $\pi$ (to be improved) differs from the **behavior policy** $\mu$ (generating data), we must correct for the probability mismatch.

**Step 1 — Form the importance sampling ratio for the trajectory segment $t$ to $t+n-1$.**

$$\rho_{t:t+n-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{\min(t+n-1, T-1)} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply the n-step return by this ratio.**

$$G_{t:t+n}^{\pi} = \rho_{t:t+n-1} \left( \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n} Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n}) \right)$$

**Step 3 — Update the action-value estimate.**

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ G_{t:t+n}^{\pi} - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variance Warning:** Off-policy n-step methods suffer from **exponentially growing variance** as $n$ increases, because the product of importance ratios becomes unstable. Tree-backup and Retrace algorithms are common alternatives that mitigate this.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Multi-Step Learning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial State St] --> B[Action Selection via Behavior Policy mu]
    B --> C[Execute Action in Environment]
    C --> D[Receive Reward Rt+1 and Next State St+1]
    D --> E{Loop n-1 More Steps}
    E -- Continue --> B
    E -- Done after n steps --> F[Compute n-step Return G_t_to_t_plus_n]
    F --> G{On-policy or Off-policy?}
    G -- On-policy --> H[Use raw n-step return as target]
    G -- Off-policy --> I[Apply Importance Sampling Ratio rho]
    H --> J[Update Value V St or Q St At]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Eligible Traces TD_lambda enabled?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Update eligibility trace E_t and propagate backward to all recent states]
    K -- No --> M[Single-state online update]
    L --> N[Next time step t+1]
    M --> N
```

### 4.2 Unification Diagram: MC ↔ TD(0) ↔ TD(λ)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Monte Carlo: n equals T-t, full return Gt, high variance zero bias] --> B[n-step TD: fixed n, intermediate bias and variance]
    B --> C[TD lambda: geometric blend over all n using lambda]
    C --> D[TD 0: lambda equals 0, one-step bootstrap, low variance high bias]
    D --> E[Choice of n or lambda depends on environment dynamics and task horizon]
```

### 4.3 Forward View vs Backward View Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Episode Time Line t to T] --> B[Forward View: Precompute lambda-return Gt lambda at time t]
    B --> C[Forward requires full future data, theoretically elegant]
    A --> D[Backward View: Maintain eligibility trace Et and update online]
    D --> E[Backward is causal and implementable in real time]
    C --> F[Mathematical Equivalence: backward TD lambda reproduces forward lambda-return in expectation]
    E --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** Nodes use only alphanumeric labels (e.g., `node1`, `stepA` patterns) and clean uppercase text inside quotes, ensuring Mermaid renders without syntax errors in the KTU 2024 scheme study material renderer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define the n-step return $G_{t:t+n}$ for an on-policy TD prediction. Show that for $n=1$ it reduces to the TD(0) target.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The n-step return from time $t$ is the sum of $n$ actual discounted rewards followed by a bootstrapped value estimate:

$$G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$$

For $n = 1$, all intermediate terms vanish, leaving only:

$$G_{t:t+1} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+n})$$

This is the one-step TD(0) target. **[3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for limit case]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of the eligibility trace $E_t(s)$ in the TD(λ) algorithm? Write its update equation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The eligibility trace $E_t(s)$ is a temporary memory variable that assigns credit to states based on **how recently and how often** they have been visited. It decays by factor $\gamma\lambda$ each step and increments on visit. The update equation is:

$$E_t(s) = \gamma \lambda \, E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$$

It enables the backward-view TD(λ) to perform an online equivalent of the forward λ-return, allowing every state update to occur incrementally each step. **[3 marks]**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Derive the n-step return $G_{t:t+n}$ starting from the standard discounted return. Clearly state the boundary case when $t+n \geq T$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the n-step SARSA algorithm for on-policy control. Write the update rule for the action-value function. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the n-step return:**

Starting from the standard discounted return:

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$$

Truncate after $n$ rewards and bootstrap with $V(S_{t+n})$:

$$G_{t:t+n} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$$

Expanding: $G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$

**Boundary case:** If $t+n \geq T$ (the terminal state is reached before $n$ steps), the n-step return collapses to the full Monte Carlo return:

$$G_{t:t+n} = G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$$

This is because $S_T$ is the terminal state, and $V(S_T) = 0$. **[7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for derivation, 1 for boundary, 1 for explanation]**

**(b) n-step SARSA:**

n-step SARSA extends one-step SARSA to use $n$ actual transitions and bootstrap from the action-value $Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$ of the state-action pair actually taken $n$ steps later.

**Step 1 — n-step return (action-value version):**

$$G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$$

**Step 2 — Action-value update rule:**

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ G_{t:t+n} - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Policy improvement:** After the update, the policy is improved greedily (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy) with respect to the new $Q$:

$$\pi(a \mid s) = \begin{cases} 1 - \epsilon + \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert} & \text{if } a = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s, a') \\ \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> The "SARSA" name comes from the quintuple $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ in the one-step version; n-step SARSA generalizes this to longer quintuple sequences. **[7 marks: 2 for n-step return, 2 for update rule, 2 for policy improvement, 1 for naming explanation]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Derive the λ-return $G_t^{\lambda}$. Show that $\lambda = 0$ recovers TD(0) and $\lambda = 1$ recovers Monte Carlo. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the off-policy n-step learning method using importance sampling. Why does variance increase with $n$? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the λ-return:**

**Step 1 — Define weights.** Each n-step return $G_{t:t+n}$ receives weight $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$, and the terminal MC return $G_t$ receives the leftover weight $\lambda^{T-t-1}$:

$$G_t^{\lambda} = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_{t:t+n} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the n-step return definition:**

$$G_t^{\lambda} = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n}) \right] + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t$$

**Step 3 — Limit $\lambda = 0$:** Only the $n=1$ term survives:

$$G_t^{0} = (1-0) \cdot \lambda^{0} \cdot G_{t:t+1} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) \quad \text{(TD(0))}$$

**Step 4 — Limit $\lambda = 1$:** All weights shift to the terminal MC term:

$$G_t^{1} = \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t = G_t \quad \text{(Monte Carlo)}$$

**[7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for derivation, 2 for limit cases, 1 for explanation]**

**(b) Off-policy n-step learning with importance sampling:**

When the behavior policy $\mu$ (used to generate data) differs from the target policy $\pi$ (being evaluated/improved), we must reweight the n-step return by the **importance sampling ratio**:

$$\rho_{t:t+n-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{t+n-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}$$

**Step 1 — Corrected n-step return:**

$$G_{t:t+n}^{\pi} = \rho_{t:t+n-1} \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n} Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n}) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Action-value update:**

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ G_{t:t+n}^{\pi} - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Why variance increases with $n$:** The ratio $\rho_{t:t+n-1}$ is a **product of $n$ random ratios**, each potentially very large or very small. The product has:
- **Unbounded magnitude:** a single rare action under $\pi$ that is common under $\mu$ can make $\rho \gg 1$.
- **Heavy-tailed distribution:** extreme values become more likely as $n$ grows.
- **Variance scales exponentially** with $n$ in the worst case.

This is the ** curse of horizon** in importance-sampled off-policy RL. **[7 marks: 2 for ratio, 2 for update, 3 for variance analysis]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the terminal-state boundary case** $G_{t:t+n} = G_t$ when $t+n \geq T$ costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Missing the $\gamma^n$ factor** on the bootstrapped $V(S_{t+n})$ term is a frequent error — students often write $\gamma^{n-1}$ instead of $\gamma^{n}$.
> 3. **Confusing forward and backward views:** the λ-return is the forward view; eligibility traces implement the equivalent backward view online.
> 4. **Off-policy students forget the product over $k$** in the importance sampling ratio, writing only a single-step ratio.
> 5. **Not stating $\lambda \in [0,1]$** when discussing TD(λ) loses a mark on definition questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **n-step return** $G_{t:t+n}$ uses $n$ real rewards and bootstraps from $V(S_{t+n})$ or $Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$ with discount $\gamma^n$.
- **n-step SARSA** is the on-policy control extension; it updates $Q$ using an $n$-step action-value target.
- **Off-policy correction** requires the **importance sampling ratio** $\rho_{t:t+n-1}$ as a product over $n$ steps; variance grows with $n$.
- **TD(λ)** unifies all n-step methods through the **λ-return** $G_t^{\lambda}$, a geometric average of all n-step returns.
- **Eligibility traces** $E_t(s) = \gamma \lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ are the **backward, online implementation** of the forward λ-return.
- **Limit cases:** $\lambda = 0$ → TD(0); $\lambda = 1$ → Monte Carlo; intermediate $\lambda$ → blend.
- **Bias-variance trade-off:** larger $n$ (or $\lambda \to 1$) → less bias, more variance; smaller $n$ (or $\lambda \to 0$) → more bias, less variance.
- **Backward view advantages:** causal, online, single update per step, scales to large state spaces.
- **Practical tuning:** $\lambda \in [0.8, 0.95]$ is common in deep RL implementations (A3C, IMPALA, PPO).
- **Pitfall to remember:** when $t+n \geq T$, the n-step return equals the full MC return — never apply bootstrapping at terminal states.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: N-step TD Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** N-step TD Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Definition (N-step TD Learning):** N-step Temporal Difference (TD) Learning is a family of reinforcement learning algorithms that generalize one-step TD methods by using a multi-step return — composed of **n actual rewards followed by a bootstrapped estimate of the value function** — to perform the value update. It formally unifies the spectrum between one-step TD (n = 1) and Monte Carlo (n → T), providing a tunable bias–variance trade-off controlled by the horizon parameter **n**.

Mathematically, the **n-step return** is the discounted sum of n future rewards capped with the bootstrap estimate at step $t+n$:

$$G_{t:t+n} \doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} V_{t+n-1}(S_{t+n})$$

The value update is then applied at time step $t$ (after we have observed $R_{t+n}$):

$$V_{t+n}(S_t) \doteq V_{t+n-1}(S_t) + \alpha \big[ G_{t:t+n} - V_{t+n-1}(S_t) \big]$$
