where $\alpha \in (0,1]$ is the step-size parameter and $\gamma \in [0,1]$ is the discount factor.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECMT745 – Module 3):** The student must be able to (i) derive the n-step return, (ii) explain the bias-variance trade-off, (iii) draw the n-step backup diagram, and (iv) extend n-step TD to action-value (SARSA) and off-policy settings using importance sampling.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Weather Forecasting Spectrum"

Imagine three weather forecasters trying to predict tomorrow's temperature:

- **MC Forecaster (n = T)**: Refuses to guess until the *entire season* is over. Very accurate (low bias) but extremely slow (high variance, late correction).
- **One-step TD Forecaster (n = 1)**: Uses only *today's* temperature plus a quick statistical guess about tomorrow. Fast and online, but highly biased if the statistical guess is wrong.
- **N-step TD Forecaster (n = 5, say)**: Waits five days, observes five real daily temperatures, then *combines* them with a statistical guess for day six. Best of both worlds: more data, faster than waiting forever.

> The horizon **n** is essentially a **"patience dial"**: a small n gives a fast, biased update; a large n gives an accurate, slow update.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of the Update

| Pillar | Symbol | Role in the Update |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **N-step Return** | $G_{t:t+n}$ | Target value computed using n real rewards and one bootstrap |
| **Step-Size** | $\alpha$ | Learning rate controlling update magnitude |
| **Discount Factor** | $\gamma$ | Geometric weighting of future rewards ($\mathbf{0 \le \gamma \le 1}$) |

> [!TIP]  
> **Mark this in your answer sheet:** Examiners expect you to explicitly state that n-step TD is a *truncation* of the full Monte Carlo return where the infinite tail is *replaced* by a single bootstrap estimate. This single sentence often earns the CO-1 definition marks.

---

## 1.4 Why N-step TD? — The Motivation

The n-step framework was introduced to resolve an inherent limitation of one-step TD: **TD(0) propagates information only one step per update**, which is slow on long-horizon tasks. Conversely, Monte Carlo must wait for termination. N-step TD is the **natural midpoint**, controllable via n, that

- updates online (no need to wait for episode end),
- propagates information faster than TD(0),
- retains a lower variance than MC,
- and subsumes both extremes as boundary cases (n = 1 and n = T).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bias–Variance Trade-off (The Heart of the Topic)

The choice of **n** governs the central statistical trade-off in RL:

| Update type | Horizon | Bias | Variance | Update Delay |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **TD(0)** | $n = 1$ | High (relies on $V(S_{t+1})$) | Low | 1 step |
| **N-step TD** | $1 < n < T$ | Moderate | Moderate | n steps |
| **Monte Carlo** | $n = T$ | Zero (uses actual return) | High | Until termination |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **The Bias–Variance Sweet Spot:** For most practical problems the optimal **n** lies in the range $\sqrt{T}$ to $T/2$, but this is empirically tuned. Larger $\gamma$ generally demands larger n to keep the truncation error small.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]  
> Use the LaTeX `\vert` / `\mid` delimiter in any handwritten form — never the bare `|` symbol inside a markdown table cell.

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Meaning / Condition |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | n-step TD Return (prediction) | $G_{t:t+n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma^{i-1} R_{t+i} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$ | Defined for $t+n < T$; for $t+n \ge T$, $G_{t:t+n} = G_t$ |
| 2 | n-step TD Error | $\delta_t = G_{t:t+n} - V(S_t)$ | Drives the value update |
| 3 | Value Update Rule | $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \, \delta_t$ | Increment applied at step $t+n$ |
| 4 | n-step SARSA Return | $G_{t:t+n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma^{i-1} R_{t+i} + \gamma^{n} Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$ | On-policy control variant |
| 5 | n-step Expected SARSA | $G_{t:t+n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma^{i-1} R_{t+i} + \gamma^{n} \sum_a \pi(a \mid S_{t+n}) Q(S_{t+n}, a)$ | Removes inner stochasticity |
| 6 | Importance Sampling Ratio | $\rho_{t:h} = \prod_{k=t}^{h} \dfrac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}$ | Needed for off-policy n-step |
| 7 | Off-policy n-step Return | $G_{t:t+n}^{\pi} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \rho_{t+1} \big[ R_{t+2} + \gamma \rho_{t+2} [ \cdots \gamma^{n-1} \rho_{t+n-1} Q^{\pi}(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n}) \cdots ] \big]$ | Tree-backup / off-policy form |
| 8 | Truncation (Boundary) | $G_{t:t+n} = G_t$ for $t+n \ge T$ | Ensures finite return at episode end |
| 9 | Discount-Factor Boundary | $\mathbf{0 \le \gamma \le 1}$ | When $\gamma = 0$ the agent is myopic |
| 10 | Step-Size Boundary | $\mathbf{0 < \alpha \le 1}$ | Must satisfy Robbins-Monro for convergence |

---

## 2.3 Algorithmic Logic — The N-step TD Prediction Procedure (Textual Form)

The procedure below is described in **algorithm-style text** (no programming language), as is standard in KTU 2024 Scheme algorithm questions.

> **Input:** A policy $\pi$ to be evaluated, an integer n ≥ 1, step-size α, discount γ.  
> **Initialize** $V(s)$ arbitrarily for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$; $V(terminal) = 0$.  
> **For each episode:**  
>   (1) Initialize $T \leftarrow \infty$.  
>   (2) Observe $S_0$, choose $A_0 \sim \pi(\cdot \mid S_0)$.  
>   (3) For $t = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ :  
>     a. If $t < T$, take action $A_t$, observe $R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}$.  
>     b. If $S_{t+1}$ is terminal, then $T \leftarrow t+1$; else choose $A_{t+1} \sim \pi(\cdot \mid S_{t+1})$.  
>     c. The update time is $\tau \leftarrow t - n + 1$.  
>     d. If $\tau \ge 0$ :  
>       i. Compute $\rho \leftarrow \sum_{i=\tau+1}^{\min(\tau+n,\,T)} \gamma^{i-\tau-1} R_i$ (use $V(S_{\tau+n})$ if $\tau + n < T$ else 0).  
>       ii. $G \leftarrow \rho + \gamma^{n} V(S_{\tau+n})$ (with the boundary convention above).  
>       iii. $V(S_\tau) \leftarrow V(S_\tau) + \alpha \big[ G - V(S_\tau) \big]$.  
>   e. Until $\tau = T - 1$ (all updates complete).

> [!WARNING]  
> **Valuation Trap:** Many students mistakenly apply the update at time **t** (the time of decision) instead of at time **t + n** (the time when the n rewards are available). The update is *deferred* by n steps — this is the *delayed-update* nature of the algorithm.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

N-step TD methods underpin:

- **Robotic control** with long horizons (e.g., walking, grasping) where TD(0) is too slow.
- **Game-playing agents** (e.g., Backgammon, Go) where reward is sparse and delayed.
- **Energy-grid management** where credit assignment spans minutes to hours.
- **Autonomous driving** where n-step returns stabilise long-term policy evaluation.
- **AlphaZero-style architectures** which conceptually chain n-step tree backups.

In each case, the n-step framework provides a *single uniform interface* to switch between fast-biasy learning and slow-accurate learning by merely changing the integer **n**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the N-step Return

### 3.1.1 Starting Point: The Bellman Recursion

The true value of a state under policy $\pi$ is defined as:

$$V^{\pi}(s) \doteq \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$

Applying the **Bellman equation** once yields the two-step expression:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \, V^{\pi}(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s \big]$$

### 3.1.2 Unrolling the Recursion (n times)

If we unroll the Bellman equation n times (substituting $V^{\pi}(S_{t+1})$ by its own Bellman form, and so on), we obtain:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} V^{\pi}(S_{t+n}) \big]$$

This is the **exact** n-step Bellman expectation. The expected return on the right is the *ideal* n-step return.

### 3.1.3 Replacing the Tail by an Estimate (Bootstrapping)

Because we do not have access to $V^{\pi}$ in practice, we *replace* it with our current estimate $V_{t+n-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{t:t+n} &\doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} V_{t+n-1}(S_{t+n}) \\[4pt]
          &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma^{i-1} R_{t+i} + \gamma^{n} V_{t+n-1}(S_{t+n})
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **n-step TD target** used in the update rule. It contains $n$ sample rewards and **one** bootstrap.

### 3.1.4 Convergence as $n \to T$ and $n \to 1$

- If $n = T$ (the episode length), the bootstrap term drops out, and $G_{t:T}$ reduces to the **Monte Carlo return** $G_t$.
- If $n = 1$, we get $G_{t:t+1} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V_t(S_{t+1})$, which is the **one-step TD target**.

This is why n-step TD is the *interpolating family*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the N-step SARSA Return (On-Policy Control)

### 3.2.1 Action-Value Bellman Equation

$$Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \, Q^{\pi}(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \mid S_t=s, A_t=a \big]$$

### 3.2.2 Unrolling n Times

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{\pi}(s,a) &= \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} \\
&\qquad\qquad\quad + \gamma^{n} Q^{\pi}(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n}) \big]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Estimator Definition

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{t:t+n} &\doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} Q_{t+n-1}(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n}) \\[4pt]
          &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma^{i-1} R_{t+i} + \gamma^{n} Q_{t+n-1}(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Update Rule

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \big[ G_{t:t+n} - Q(S_t, A_t) \big]$$

> The update to $Q(S_t, A_t)$ is performed at time $t + n$ once the sample trajectory is complete.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Off-Policy N-step Return with Importance Sampling

When the behaviour policy $\mu$ differs from the target policy $\pi$, we must correct the expectation using the **importance sampling ratio**:

$$\rho_{t:h} \doteq \prod_{k=t}^{h} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}$$

### 3.3.1 The Off-policy n-step Return

The off-policy corrected n-step return is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{t:t+n}^{\pi} &\doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma \rho_{t+1} \big[ R_{t+2} + \gamma \rho_{t+2} [ R_{t+3} + \cdots \\
&\qquad\qquad\quad + \gamma^{n-1} \rho_{t+n-1} Q^{\pi}(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n}) \cdots ] \big]
\end{aligned}
$$

> Each transition $k$ is weighted by the ratio $\rho_{t+k}$ of the *target* to *behaviour* policy probabilities.

### 3.3.2 The Expected (Tree-Backup) Form — Avoiding Importance Sampling

For the **n-step Tree Backup** algorithm (a model-free but off-policy-compatible alternative), we replace the sampled action $A_{t+n}$ with an expectation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{t:t+n} &\doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_a \pi(a \mid S_{t+1}) Q(S_{t+1}, a) \\
&\quad + \gamma^{2} \sum_a \pi(a \mid S_{t+2}) \big[ R_{t+2} + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid S_{t+2}) Q(S_{t+2}, a') \big] \\
&\quad + \cdots + \gamma^{n} \sum_a \pi(a \mid S_{t+n}) Q(S_{t+n}, a)
\end{aligned}
$$

This formulation requires **no importance sampling** and reduces variance dramatically.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — 4-step TD on a Markov Reward Process

Consider a deterministic MRP with states $S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ (terminal), and rewards $R_1 = 1, R_2 = 2, R_3 = 3, R_4 = 4$ (immediate reward upon entering $S_4$). Let $\gamma = 0.9$ and current estimates be $V(S_0) = 0, V(S_1) = 0.5, V(S_2) = 1.0, V(S_3) = 2.0, V(S_4) = 0$.

**Step 1: Compute the 4-step return for $t = 0$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{0:0+4} &= R_1 + \gamma R_2 + \gamma^{2} R_3 + \gamma^{3} R_4 + \gamma^{4} V(S_4) \\
          &= 1 + 0.9(2) + 0.81(3) + 0.729(4) + 0.6561(0) \\
          &= 1 + 1.8 + 2.43 + 2.916 + 0 \\
          &= 8.146
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: TD error at $t = 0$**

$$\delta_0 = G_{0:0+4} - V(S_0) = 8.146 - 0 = 8.146$$

**Step 3: Value update (take $\alpha = 0.1$)**

$$V(S_0) \leftarrow V(S_0) + \alpha \delta_0 = 0 + 0.1(8.146) = 0.8146$$

> Notice the update is applied **at time $t + n = 0 + 4$** to $V(S_0)$ — *not* at $t = 0$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How N is Chosen

| Horizon Regime | Typical n Choice | Behaviour | When to Use |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Very fast feedback | $n = 1$ | One-step TD | Continual, dense rewards |
| Moderate horizon | $3 \le n \le 10$ | Balanced bias-variance | Most engineering control |
| Long horizon | $n \ge 50$ | Near-MC | Sparse, delayed rewards |
| Episode-length | $n = T$ | Pure MC | Episodic finite tasks |
| Adaptive | $n$ varies | Forward-TD($\lambda$) | Best of all worlds |

> The Forward View of TD($\lambda$) **eligibility trace** generalises n-step by *averaging* over all $n \in [1, T]$ with geometric weights $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The N-step Backup Diagram (Mermaid)

The backup diagram is the *single most important visual* for n-step TD in KTU exams. It shows the tree of sampled trajectories with the bootstrap at the leaf.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS0["S_t"] --> nodeA0["A_t"]
    nodeA0 --> nodeR1["R_t+1"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeS1["S_t+1"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeA1["A_t+1"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeR2["R_t+2"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeS2["S_t+2"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeA2["A_t+2"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeR3["R_t+3"]
    nodeR3 --> nodeSn["S_t+n"]
    Sn1["S_t+n"] --- BootNode["Bootstrap V of S_t+n"]
    BootNode --> UpdateArrow["Update V of S_t"]
    nodeS0 -.-> UpdateArrow

    style nodeS0 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style Sn1 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style BootNode fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style UpdateArrow fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

> **Interpretation of the diagram:**
> - **Solid arrows** = sample transitions actually observed.
> - **Dotted arrow** = the value update flowing *backward* in time to the root $S_t$.
> - **The leaf $S_{t+n}$** is the bootstrap node; its estimated value caps the return.
> - For $n = 1$ the diagram collapses to a single transition; for $n = T$ the leaf is the terminal state.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of N-step TD Prediction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InitBlock["Initialize V(s) for all states, alpha, gamma, n"] --> EpisodeStart["Begin Episode, observe S_0"]
    EpisodeStart --> ActionSelect["Choose A_t from policy pi"]
    ActionSelect --> EnvStep["Take action, observe R_t+1 and S_t+1"]
    EnvStep --> BufferStore["Store transition in n-step buffer"]
    BufferStore --> CheckReady{"Have we observed n transitions?"}
    CheckReady -- "No" --> ActionSelect
    CheckReady -- "Yes" --> ComputeReturn["Compute G_t:t+n with bootstrap V of S_t+n"]
    ComputeReturn --> TDError["Compute delta_t = G_t:t+n - V of S_t"]
    TDError --> ValueUpdate["V of S_t updated by alpha times delta_t"]
    ValueUpdate --> TerminalCheck{"S_t+n terminal or episode end?"}
    TerminalCheck -- "No" --> ActionSelect
    TerminalCheck -- "Yes" --> EpisodeStart

    style InitBlock fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460
    style ComputeReturn fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style ValueUpdate fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style CheckReady fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading Guide for the Examiner:** Highlight that the **Buffer** is the conceptual memory of the last n transitions and that the *Value Update* block targets a state that is **n steps in the past** — this is what makes the algorithm "deferred."

---

## 4.3 Comparison Diagram — TD vs. n-step TD vs. MC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OneStep["One-step TD n=1"]
        O1["S_t"] --> O2["R_t+1"]
        O2 --> O3["S_t+1"]
        O3 -.-> O4["Bootstrap"]
    end

    subgraph NStep["N-step TD n=4"]
        N1["S_t"] --> N2["R_t+1"]
        N2 --> N3["R_t+2"]
        N3 --> N4["R_t+3"]
        N4 --> N5["R_t+4"]
        N5 --> N6["S_t+4"]
        N6 -.-> N7["Bootstrap V"]
    end

    subgraph MC["Monte Carlo n=T"]
        M1["S_t"] --> M2["R_t+1"]
        M2 --> M3["R_t+2"]
        M3 --> M4["..."]
        M4 --> M5["R_T"]
        M5 --> M6["Terminal S_T"]
    end

    style OneStep fill:#e7f3ff
    style NStep fill:#fff7e6
    style MC fill:#ffe6e6
```

This single diagram, if reproduced well in an exam, can fetch the **full 7 marks** for a "draw the n-step backup and compare" question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1.b]

> **Define the n-step return in temporal difference learning. Show its relationship with the one-step TD and Monte Carlo returns.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The n-step return $G_{t:t+n}$ is defined as the sum of $n$ actual discounted rewards plus a bootstrap estimate of the value at state $S_{t+n}$:

$$G_{t:t+n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma^{i-1} R_{t+i} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$$

- When **n = 1**, the formula reduces to $G_{t:t+1} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$, which is the **one-step TD return**.
- When **n = T − t**, the bootstrap term is zero (terminal state) and we recover the **Monte Carlo return** $G_t$.

> Hence, n-step TD unifies TD(0) and MC as boundary cases of a single parametric family.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1.c]

> **State the role of the importance sampling ratio in off-policy n-step TD learning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **importance sampling ratio** corrects the distributional mismatch between the behaviour policy $\mu$ (used to generate the trajectory) and the target policy $\pi$ (which we wish to evaluate or improve). It is defined as:

$$\rho_{t:h} = \prod_{k=t}^{h} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}$$

- It re-weights each observed trajectory by the likelihood ratio of the actions under the two policies.
- The off-policy n-step return is then formed by cascading these ratios through the unrolled Bellman equation, ensuring that $\mathbb{E}_{\mu}[G_{t:t+n}^{\pi}] = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t:t+n}]$.
- **Caveat:** Importance sampling suffers from high variance when $\pi$ and $\mu$ diverge significantly, motivating tree-backup methods that avoid it.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q6.a/b]

> **(a)** Derive the n-step TD return from the Bellman equation and explain the bias–variance trade-off parameterised by n. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** For a Markov Reward Process with states $\{S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3\}$ and rewards $R_1 = 1, R_2 = 0, R_3 = 2$ (terminal reward), use a 3-step TD method with $\gamma = 0.8$, $\alpha = 0.1$, and current values $V(S_0) = 0$, $V(S_1) = 0.5$, $V(S_2) = 1.5$, $V(S_3) = 0$ to compute the updated $V(S_0)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution (a) — Derivation and Trade-off (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The Bellman expectation equation for state value is:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \, V^{\pi}(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s \big]$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Unroll the recursion $n$ times:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \big[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma^{i-1} R_{t+i} + \gamma^{n} V^{\pi}(S_{t+n}) \big]$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Replace $V^{\pi}$ by current estimate $V_{t+n-1}$ to form the n-step TD target:

$$G_{t:t+n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma^{i-1} R_{t+i} + \gamma^{n} V_{t+n-1}(S_{t+n})$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Update rule:

$$V_{t+n}(S_t) = V_{t+n-1}(S_t) + \alpha \big[ G_{t:t+n} - V_{t+n-1}(S_t) \big]$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Bias–Variance Trade-off** — As **n** increases:
- *Bias decreases* (more true rewards, less reliance on the inaccurate $V$ estimate).
- *Variance increases* (more random rewards accumulate, more sensitivity to stochasticity).
- *Update delay* also increases since we must wait $n$ steps before updating $S_t$.

#### Solution (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the n-step return with $n = 3$, $t = 0$:

$$G_{0:0+3} = R_1 + \gamma R_2 + \gamma^{2} R_3 + \gamma^{3} V(S_3)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute values $R_1 = 1$, $R_2 = 0$, $R_3 = 2$, $V(S_3) = 0$, $\gamma = 0.8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{0:0+3} &= 1 + 0.8 \cdot 0 + 0.64 \cdot 2 + 0.512 \cdot 0 \\
          &= 1 + 0 + 1.28 + 0 \\
          &= 2.28
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the TD error at $t = 0$:

$$\delta_0 = G_{0:0+3} - V(S_0) = 2.28 - 0 = 2.28$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply the update with $\alpha = 0.1$:

$$V(S_0) \leftarrow V(S_0) + \alpha \delta_0 = 0 + 0.1 \cdot 2.28 = 0.228$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Important:** The update is applied to $V(S_0)$ at time step $t + n = 0 + 3 = 3$, **not** at time $0$. This *deferred update* is a defining feature of n-step TD.

> **Final Answer:** $V_{\text{new}}(S_0) = \mathbf{0.228}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q7.a/b]

> **(a)** With a clear backup diagram, explain the n-step SARSA algorithm. State the update equation. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Describe the off-policy n-step return and the role of the importance sampling ratio. What is its main limitation, and how does the n-step Tree Backup algorithm address it? **(7 marks)**

#### Solution (a) — N-step SARSA (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** **Definition:** N-step SARSA is the on-policy n-step control extension of SARSA, where the action-value $Q(S, A)$ is updated using $n$ actual rewards and a bootstrap from $Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The **n-step SARSA return**:

$$G_{t:t+n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma^{i-1} R_{t+i} + \gamma^{n} Q_{t+n-1}(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The **update equation**:

$$Q_{t+n}(S_t, A_t) = Q_{t+n-1}(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \big[ G_{t:t+n} - Q_{t+n-1}(S_t, A_t) \big]$$

**Step 4 (3 marks):** **Backup diagram (textual description to accompany the diagram):**

- The root is the state-action pair $(S_t, A_t)$ to be updated.
- A chain of $n$ real transitions follows: $(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}, R_{t+2}), (S_{t+2}, A_{t+2}, R_{t+3}), \ldots, (S_{t+n-1}, A_{t+n-1}, R_{t+n})$.
- The leaf is $(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$, whose $Q$-value bootstraps the chain.
- Unlike TD(0), more than one sampled reward contributes to the target.

#### Solution (b) — Off-policy N-step and Tree Backup (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** **Off-policy n-step return derivation:** When the behaviour policy $\mu \neq \pi$, the trajectory is reweighted:

$$G_{t:t+n}^{\pi} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \rho_{t+1} \big[ R_{t+2} + \gamma \rho_{t+2} [\cdots + \gamma^{n-1} \rho_{t+n-1} Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n}) \cdots] \big]$$

with $\rho_{t+1:t+n-1} = \prod_{k=t+1}^{t+n-1} \pi(A_k \mid S_k) / \mu(A_k \mid S_k)$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Limitation of Importance Sampling:** When $\pi$ and $\mu$ are very different, the product of ratios can have huge variance — sometimes exploding to numerical infinity. This makes the off-policy estimator unstable for long horizons.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** **N-step Tree Backup Algorithm** — eliminates importance sampling by replacing each sampled $A_k$ by an expectation under $\pi$:

$$G_{t:t+n} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_a \pi(a \mid S_{t+1}) Q(S_{t+1}, a) + \gamma^{2} \sum_a \pi(a \mid S_{t+2}) \big[ R_{t+2} + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid S_{t+2}) Q(S_{t+2}, a') \big] + \cdots + \gamma^{n} \sum_a \pi(a \mid S_{t+n}) Q(S_{t+n}, a)$$

This:
- Removes the variance from importance sampling.
- Requires no behaviour-target policy ratio.
- Forms a *tree* of expected values at every non-leaf node — hence the name "tree backup."

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**  
> 1. **Confusing the update time** — n-step TD updates at $t+n$, not at $t$. This is the single most common error.  
> 2. **Forgetting the boundary condition** $G_{t:t+n} = G_t$ for $t+n \ge T$. Without this, the algorithm crashes at episode end.  
> 3. **Writing $\mid x \mid$ in tables** — never use the bare pipe `|` in markdown tables; examiners parse formulas strictly.  
> 4. **Conflating n-step TD with TD($\lambda$)** — they are related (n-step is the *basis*, TD($\lambda$) is the *average*) but they are **not** the same algorithm. Always clarify the distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Pin to Wall ✦**

- ✦ **N-step TD** generalises one-step TD by using **n actual rewards + 1 bootstrap** in the return.
- ✦ The horizon **n** controls the **bias–variance trade-off** and the **update delay**.
- ✦ Boundary cases: $n = 1 \Rightarrow$ TD(0); $n = T \Rightarrow$ Monte Carlo.
- ✦ The **n-step return** is $G_{t:t+n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma^{i-1} R_{t+i} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$.
- ✦ The **update** $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \big[ G_{t:t+n} - V(S_t) \big]$ is applied at time $t+n$ — *deferred update*.
- ✦ **N-step SARSA** uses $Q(S_{t+n}, A_{t+n})$ as the bootstrap — on-policy control.
- ✦ **N-step Expected SARSA** uses $\sum_a \pi(a \mid S_{t+n}) Q(S_{t+n}, a)$ to remove inner stochasticity.
- ✦ **Off-policy n-step** uses the importance sampling ratio $\rho = \prod \pi/\mu$ to reweight trajectories.
- ✦ **N-step Tree Backup** avoids importance sampling by replacing sampled actions with expectations under $\pi$.
- ✦ Always state the **boundary**: $G_{t:t+n} = G_t$ for $t + n \ge T$.
- ✦ Always include the **backup diagram** in 7-mark derivation questions — examiners reward clear visuals.
- ✦ **Connection to TD($\lambda$):** TD($\lambda$) is the geometric-weighted *average* of n-step returns with weights $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$, summing to 1.
- ✦ **Convergence:** N-step TD converges for all $n \ge 1$ under standard stochastic-approximation conditions (Robbins-Monro on $\alpha$).
- ✦ **Practical tip:** In KTU 14-mark questions, always (i) define the return, (ii) write the update, (iii) draw the backup diagram, and (iv) discuss the trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forward-View TD(λ)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Forward-View TD(λ)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Forward-View TD(λ)** is a theoretical formulation of the **Temporal Difference (TD) learning algorithm** that estimates the value function $V(s)$ by averaging **n-step returns** with a geometric weighting factor determined by the trace-decay parameter **λ (lambda)**, where $0 \leq \lambda \leq 1$. It is called the "forward-view" because, at time $t$, it explicitly looks *forward* into the future trajectory to compute the target update.

The central object of study in the forward view is the **λ-return** $G_t^\lambda$, defined as the exponentially weighted average of all possible n-step returns from state $S_t$ to termination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Definition:** The forward-view TD(λ) is *theoretical* — it provides the *ideal learning target* but cannot be updated online at step $t$ because it requires information from time $t+n$ in the future. The *practical* online version is the **backward-view TD(λ)** using **eligibility traces**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a cricket coach analyzing a batsman's innings **stroke-by-stroke**:

- **1-step return (TD(0))**: You judge each ball *immediately* by the runs scored on that ball (short-sighted, ignores momentum).
- **Monte Carlo return**: You wait until the batsman is *out* and judge the entire innings (perfect, but you learn nothing until the end).
- **n-step return**: You wait $n$ balls after each stroke, then judge based on the next $n$ balls.
- **Forward-View TD(λ)**: You don't commit to a single $n$. Instead, you look *n=1 balls ahead* with weight $(1-\lambda)$, *n=2 balls ahead* with weight $(1-\lambda)\lambda$, *n=3 balls ahead* with weight $(1-\lambda)\lambda^2$, and so on. The further you look, the less weight you give (controlled by $\lambda$).

If $\lambda = 0$, you only look 1 step ahead (pure TD). If $\lambda = 1$, you weight Monte Carlo most heavily.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** $\lambda = 0.9$ is a common engineering default; the geometric weights sum to 1 over the entire trajectory.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic Steps (Bulleted Breakdown)

1. **Generate an Episode**: Starting from $S_t$, follow the policy $\pi$ and observe the sequence $S_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, R_{t+2}, S_{t+2}, \dots, S_T$.
2. **Compute n-step Returns** $G_t^{(n)}$ for every possible $n = 1, 2, \dots, T-t$:
   - Each $G_t^{(n)}$ incorporates $n$ actual rewards and then *bootstraps* using the estimated $V(S_{t+n})$.
3. **Geometric Weighting**: Apply a weight $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$ to each n-step return. These weights form a **geometric distribution**.
4. **Form the λ-Return**: Sum all weighted n-step returns to get a single scalar target $G_t^\lambda$.
5. **Update the Value Function**:
   
$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ G_t^\lambda - V(S_t) \right]$$
6. **Repeat** until convergence.

### The 'Why' Behind the Mechanism

- **Why geometric weights?** They ensure the sum of weights is exactly **1** (a valid probability distribution), and they decay smoothly — the closer the horizon $n$, the larger the weight.
- **Why use a weighted average?** No single horizon $n$ is universally best. Some environments need long-horizon credit assignment (high $\lambda$), others short (low $\lambda$).

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Boundary / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $G_t^{(n)}$ | n-step return from time $t$ | $1 \leq n \leq T-t$ |
| $G_t^\lambda$ | λ-return (weighted sum of n-step returns) | $-\infty < G_t^\lambda < \infty$ |
| $\lambda$ | Trace-decay parameter | $0 \leq \lambda \leq 1$ |
| $\alpha$ | Learning rate (step size) | $0 < \alpha \leq 1$ |
| $\gamma$ | Discount factor | $0 \leq \gamma < 1$ |
| $V(S_t)$ | Estimated value of state $S_t$ | Real number |
| $T$ | Terminal time-step | $T \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |

### Core Formulas (Exam-Critical)

**1. n-step TD Return (Recursive Component):**

$$G_t^{(n)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$$

**2. Compact n-step Return (Geometric Series):**

$$G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \gamma^{k-1} R_{t+k} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$$

**3. λ-Return (Weighted Average Definition):**

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t^{(T-t-1)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The terminal term $\lambda^{T-t-1} G_t^{(T-t-1)}$ ensures the weights **sum exactly to 1** even when the episode ends before $\lambda^{n-1}$ has decayed to zero.

**4. Simplified λ-Return (Episode-Complete Case):**

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)}$$

**5. Forward-View TD(λ) Update Rule:**

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ G_t^\lambda - V(S_t) \right]$$

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotic Control**: Credit assignment in long-horizon manipulation tasks (e.g., a robot learning to walk).
- **Game AI (AlphaGo, Game Engines)**: Evaluating board positions many moves ahead with decay.
- **Autonomous Driving**: Assessing the long-term value of a lane-change decision.
- **Recommendation Systems**: Estimating cumulative user engagement over a session.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation A: From n-step Return to the n-step TD Target

**Goal:** Express the n-step return as a sum of rewards plus a bootstrapped value.

**Step 1** — Begin with the first two reward terms.

$$G_t^{(n)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$$

**Step 2** — Recognize the pattern: the first $n$ terms form a **discounted reward sum** (the prefix), and the final term is $\gamma^n$ times the value estimate at the *lookahead state*.

**Step 3** — Write it compactly using summation notation.

$$G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \gamma^{k-1} R_{t+k} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$$

**Step 4** — This is the building block. The forward-view TD(λ) algorithm computes $G_t^{(n)}$ for every $n$ from 1 to $T-t$ and then takes a geometrically weighted sum.

---

### Derivation B: Deriving the λ-Return as a Geometric Mixture

**Goal:** Show how the λ-return is constructed as a normalized weighted sum of n-step returns.

**Step 1** — Define a discrete probability distribution over horizons $n$:

$$w_n = (1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 2** — Verify that the weights form a valid distribution (sum to 1) over an infinite horizon:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} w_n = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \lambda^{n-1}$$

**Step 3** — Apply the geometric series identity $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} x^{n-1} = \dfrac{1}{1-x}$ for $\vert x \vert < 1$:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} w_n = (1-\lambda) \cdot \frac{1}{1-\lambda} = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> This confirms the weights form a **valid geometric distribution** that sums to exactly **1**.

**Step 4** — Define the λ-return as the expectation under this distribution:

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t^{(T-t-1)}$$

**Step 5** — The final term $\lambda^{T-t-1} G_t^{(T-t-1)}$ acts as a **remainder mass** — it absorbs any leftover weight when the episode terminates at time $T$, ensuring the sum still equals **1**.

---

### Derivation C: Limiting Behavior at λ = 0 and λ = 1

**Goal:** Verify the boundary conditions of the algorithm.

**Case 1: $\lambda = 0$ (pure 1-step TD)**

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-0) \cdot (0)^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} = G_t^{(1)}$$

Compute $G_t^{(1)}$:

$$G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$$

This is the classical **TD(0) target**. ✓

**Case 2: $\lambda = 1$ (pure Monte Carlo)**

As $\lambda \to 1$, weights become flatter. In the limit, each n-step return is weighted equally, and the sum telescopes to:

$$G_t^\lambda \to G_t = \sum_{k=1}^{T-t} \gamma^{k-1} R_{t+k}$$

This is the **Monte Carlo return**. ✓

---

### Analytical Summary Matrix

| Regime | $\lambda$ value | Effective Algorithm | Update Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure TD | $\lambda = 0$ | TD(0) | Bootstrapped, 1-step |
| Mixed | $0 < \lambda < 1$ | TD(λ) | Hybrid (weighted n-step) |
| Pure MC | $\lambda \to 1$ | Monte Carlo | Sample-return, no bootstrap |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: n-step Return Backup Tree (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["State St at time t"] --> B["Reward R t+1"]
    B --> C["State St+1"]
    C --> D["Reward R t+2"]
    D --> E["State St+2"]
    E --> F["Reward R t+3"]
    F --> G["State St+3"]
    G --> H["Reward R t+n-1"]
    H --> I["State St+n-1"]
    I --> J["Reward R t+n"]
    J --> K["State St+n"]
    K --> L["Bootstrapped Value V of St+n"]
    L --> M["n-step Return Gt superscript n"]
    M --> N["Weighted Sum lambda to power n-1 times 1 minus lambda"]
    N --> O["Lambda Return Gt superscript lambda"]
    O --> P["Update V of St with alpha step"]
```

**Interpretation:** Each path from $S_t$ represents one n-step return $G_t^{(n)}$. The λ-return aggregates all such paths via geometric weights.

---

### Diagram 2: Backup Diagram (Square Symbols) Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TD0["TD(0) Backup - 1 step"]
        T0A["St"] --> T0B["R t+1"]
        T0B --> T0C["St+1 approx V"]
    end
    subgraph TDn["n-step Backup - n steps"]
        TNA["St"] --> TNB["Rewards R t+1 to R t+n"]
        TNB --> TNC["St+n approx V"]
    end
    subgraph TDlambda["TD lambda Backup - Geometric Average"]
        TLA["St"] --> TLB["Weighted n-step Returns"]
        TLB --> TLC["Geometric Decay weight 1-lambda times lambda to n-1"]
    end
```

---

### Diagram 3: Weight Distribution Over Horizons

```mermaid
graph TD
    W1["n=1: weight 0.65"] --> W2["n=2: weight 0.245"]
    W2 --> W3["n=3: weight 0.092"]
    W3 --> W4["n=4: weight 0.035"]
    W4 --> W5["n=5: weight 0.013"]
    W5 --> W6["n=6: weight 0.005"]
    W6 --> W7["n=infinity: weight approaches 0"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (λ = 0.7):** The weight at $n=1$ is $(1-0.7)(0.7)^0 = 0.30$. At $n=2$ it is $0.21$, at $n=3$ it is $0.147$, etc. The sum is exactly **1.0**.

---

### Diagram 4: Forward-View vs. Backward-View Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FV["Forward-View - Theoretical"]
        F1["Observe trajectory"] --> F2["Wait until episode end"]
        F2 --> F3["Compute all n-step returns"]
        F3 --> F4["Form lambda-return target"]
        F4 --> F5["Bulk update V of St"]
    end
    subgraph BV["Backward-View - Practical"]
        B1["Observe St, R t+1, St+1"] --> B2["Increment eligibility e of St"]
        B2 --> B3["Compute TD error delta t"]
        B3 --> B4["Update V of all states with e decay"]
        B4 --> B5["Decay eligibility by lambda gamma"]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The forward-view and backward-view TD(λ) produce **mathematically equivalent** updates *off-line* (at the end of an episode), even though their implementations differ. This is called the **forward-backward equivalence theorem**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the term "n-step return" in TD learning. Write the mathematical expression for the 2-step return $G_t^{(2)}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **n-step return** $G_t^{(n)}$ is the discounted sum of the next $n$ rewards, followed by the bootstrapped value of the state reached after $n$ steps.

**Expression for the general n-step return:**

$$G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \gamma^{k-1} R_{t+k} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$$

**Specific expression for the 2-step return:**

$$G_t^{(2)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 V(S_{t+2})$$

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: What is the λ-return in forward-view TD(λ)? How does it combine n-step returns?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **λ-return** $G_t^\lambda$ is the exponentially weighted average of all n-step returns $G_t^{(n)}$, with weights following a geometric decay controlled by $\lambda$.

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t^{(T-t-1)}$$

**How it combines:** Each n-step return is weighted by $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$. The weights sum to **1** (valid distribution). Short horizons ($n$ small) receive higher weight; long horizons receive exponentially less weight. The terminal term absorbs leftover mass when the episode ends prematurely.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for the **λ-return** starting from the definition of the n-step return. Show that the weights form a valid probability distribution summing to 1. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the λ-return for the following 4-step trajectory with $\gamma = 0.9$ and $\lambda = 0.5$. Assume $V(S_5) = 0$. Data: $R_1 = 2, R_2 = 1, R_3 = 3, R_4 = 1$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1** — Write the n-step return definition:

$$G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \gamma^{k-1} R_{t+k} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$$

**Step 2** — Define the weighting function $w_n = (1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$.

**Step 3** — Sum weights to verify normalization:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1} = (1-\lambda) \cdot \frac{1}{1-\lambda} = 1$$

**Step 4** — Form the λ-return as the weighted sum:

$$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t^{(T-t-1)}$$

**Step 5** — Conclude that because weights sum to 1, $G_t^\lambda$ is a convex combination (weighted average) of n-step returns, ensuring stable learning targets.

---

**(b) Computation (7 marks):**

Given: $T - t = 4$, $R_{t+1}=2, R_{t+2}=1, R_{t+3}=3, R_{t+4}=1$, $V(S_{t+4})=0$, $\gamma=0.9$, $\lambda=0.5$.

**Step 1** — Compute each n-step return.

**$G_t^{(1)}$:**

$$G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$$

We need $V(S_{t+1})$. Since the problem only gives rewards, we assume $V$ at intermediate states is 0 unless stated. So:

$$G_t^{(1)} = 2 + 0.9(0) = 2.0$$

**$G_t^{(2)}$:**

$$G_t^{(2)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 V(S_{t+2}) = 2 + 0.9(1) + 0.81(0) = 2.9$$

**$G_t^{(3)}$:**

$$G_t^{(3)} = 2 + 0.9(1) + 0.81(3) + 0.729(0) = 2 + 0.9 + 2.43 = 5.33$$

**$G_t^{(4)}$ (terminal):**

$$G_t^{(4)} = 2 + 0.9(1) + 0.81(3) + 0.729(1) + 0.6561(0) = 2 + 0.9 + 2.43 + 0.729 = 6.059$$

**Step 2** — Apply weights $w_n = (1-0.5)(0.5)^{n-1}$.

| $n$ | $G_t^{(n)}$ | Weight $w_n$ | Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.000 | 0.50 | 1.000 |
| 2 | 2.900 | 0.25 | 0.725 |
| 3 | 5.330 | 0.125 | 0.666 |
| 4 | 6.059 | $\lambda^{3} = 0.125$ (terminal correction) | 0.757 |

> [!NOTE]
> For $n = T-t-1 = 3$, the weight is $(1-\lambda)\lambda^2 = 0.125$. The remaining terminal mass $\lambda^{T-t-1} = 0.125$ multiplies the final return $G_t^{(4)}$.

**Step 3** — Sum the contributions.

$$G_t^\lambda = 1.000 + 0.725 + 0.666 + 0.757 = 3.148$$

**Final Answer:** $G_t^\lambda \approx 3.148$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of forward-view TD(λ) with the help of the n-step return mechanism. Discuss its boundary cases at $\lambda = 0$ and $\lambda = 1$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast forward-view TD(λ) with backward-view TD(λ). Why is the forward view considered *theoretical*? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept of Forward-View TD(λ) (7 marks):**

The forward-view TD(λ) is a value estimation algorithm that combines the strengths of TD(0) (low variance, bootstrapped) and Monte Carlo (unbiased, high variance) by averaging n-step returns over all possible horizons.

**Mechanism:**
1. From state $S_t$, generate a complete episode trajectory.
2. For each $n \in \{1, 2, \ldots, T-t\}$, compute $G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \gamma^{k-1} R_{t+k} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$.
3. Apply geometric weights $w_n = (1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$ and sum to get $G_t^\lambda$.
4. Update: $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha[G_t^\lambda - V(S_t)]$.

**Boundary Cases:**

- **$\lambda = 0$**: Only the 1-step return survives. $G_t^\lambda = G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$. This is **TD(0)** — fully bootstrapped, online, but high bias.
- **$\lambda = 1$**: All n-step returns contribute (weights flatten). $G_t^\lambda \to G_t$, the full Monte Carlo return. This is **unbiased** but requires complete episodes (high variance, no bootstrapping).
- **$0 < \lambda < 1$**: Hybrid. Compromise between bias (TD-like) and variance (MC-like).

---

**(b) Forward-View vs. Backward-View TD(λ) (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Forward-View TD(λ) | Backward-View TD(λ) |
|---|---|---|
| Update timing | At end of episode | At each time-step (online) |
| Mechanism | Weighted n-step returns | Eligibility traces + TD error |
| Knowledge requirement | Future rewards from time $t$ | Only current and past info |
| Practicality | Theoretical / off-line | Practical / real-time |
| Memory | Stores all visited states | Maintains eligibility trace $e_t(s)$ |

**Why forward-view is *theoretical*:**
- At time $t$, computing $G_t^\lambda$ requires rewards $R_{t+1}, R_{t+2}, \ldots, R_{t+n}$ which are not yet observed.
- Therefore the update $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha[G_t^\lambda - V(S_t)]$ cannot be performed online.
- The backward-view (using eligibility traces) achieves the **same off-line result** with online, real-time updates — this is the *forward-backward equivalence theorem*.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the terminal correction term** $\lambda^{T-t-1} G_t^{(T-t-1)}$ — leads to weights not summing to 1 (KTU deducts 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Mixing up λ-return with eligibility trace** — the forward view *defines the target*; the backward view *implements it online*.
> 3. **Sign errors in geometric series normalization** — recall the identity $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} x^{n-1} = \frac{1}{1-x}$, valid only for $\vert x \vert < 1$ (i.e., $\lambda < 1$).
> 4. **Confusing the n-step update with the λ-step update** — n-step uses ONE horizon $n$; λ uses ALL horizons with weighting.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the discount factor $\gamma$** in the n-step return expansion (KTU explicitly checks for this).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Forward-View TD(λ)** is a *theoretical* formulation based on the **λ-return**.
- [x] The **n-step return** $G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\gamma^{k-1}R_{t+k} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$ is the building block.
- [x] The **λ-return** is the geometric weighted sum: $G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}\lambda^{n-1}G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1}G_t^{(T-t-1)}$.
- [x] Geometric weights $w_n = (1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$ sum to **1** — verified using the infinite geometric series.
- [x] **Boundary cases**: $\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow$ TD(0); $\lambda \to 1 \Rightarrow$ Monte Carlo.
- [x] The **forward view is off-line**; the **backward view is online** — both are mathematically equivalent (forward-backward equivalence theorem).
- [x] **Update rule**: $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha[G_t^\lambda - V(S_t)]$.
- [x] **Engineering defaults**: $\lambda = 0.9$, $\gamma = 0.99$, $\alpha \in [0.01, 0.5]$.
- [x] **Real-world uses**: robotics, game AI (TD-Gammon, AlphaGo), autonomous vehicles, recommendation systems.
- [x] **KTU hot spot**: derivations of the n-step return and the λ-return carry **7–8 marks** in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backward-View TD(λ)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Backward-View TD(λ)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Backward-View TD(λ)** (also called the *mechanistic* or *online* view) is an implementation of the TD(λ) prediction algorithm that uses **eligibility traces** to update the value estimates of all previously visited states *in proportion to their temporal recency and the magnitude of the most recent TD error*. It looks **backward** in time from the current state and assigns credit to the states that led to it, rather than looking forward into future states (as in the theoretical forward-view).

Mathematically, the backward-view update rule for all states $s$ is given by:

$$
V(s) \;\leftarrow\; V(s) \;+\; \alpha \,\delta_t \, e_t(s)
$$

where $\delta_t$ is the **one-step TD error** at time $t$ and $e_t(s)$ is the **eligibility trace** of state $s$ at time $t$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — "The Echo of Recent Steps"**
> Imagine you are walking through a **pitch-dark maze** and you finally bump into a wall (the TD error $\delta_t$). Your brain doesn't just update your understanding of *the wall cell* — it slightly re-evaluates *every step you took* in the last few seconds that led you there, with the **most recent steps getting the strongest blame/praise** and older steps getting a fading share.
> 
> The **eligibility trace** $e_t(s)$ is the "strength of memory" of a state $s$ at time $t$. Each time you visit $s$, its memory is **boosted to 1**, and then it **decays geometrically** by a factor of $\gamma\lambda$ at every subsequent step. When the TD error fires, this memory is used to decide *how much* each past state should be updated.
> 
> This is exactly the **backward-view** mechanism — credit/blame propagates *backwards* like ripples spreading in reverse through the state-visitation history.

### 1.3 Why "Backward"?

| View | Direction of Reasoning | What It Does |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward-View TD(λ)** | Forward in time | Looks at *future* returns up to horizon $h = \tfrac{1}{1-\lambda}$ and updates the state at time $t$ |
| **Backward-View TD(λ)** | Backward in time | Uses *eligibility traces* to update *all past* states whenever a new TD error is observed |

The backward view is the **causally implementable** form because at time $t$ we don't know the future, but we *do* know everything that happened in the past.

### 1.4 Physical / Standard Constants Highlighted

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Hyperparameters in Backward-View TD(λ)**
> - $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ — **Learning rate** (step-size)
> - $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ — **Discount factor** (default = **1.0** for episodic tasks, **< 1** for continuing)
> - $\lambda \in [0, 1]$ — **Trace-decay rate** (controls memory horizon)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Core Components of Backward-View TD(λ)

**Step 1 — The TD Error (the "shock" signal):**

$$
\delta_t \;=\; R_{t+1} \;+\; \gamma \, V(S_{t+1}) \;-\; V(S_t)
$$

This is computed at the *current* time step and represents the **one-step prediction mismatch**.

**Step 2 — The Eligibility Trace (the "memory" mechanism):**

For the **accumulating trace** variant (most common in textbook presentations):

$$
e_t(s) \;=\; \begin{cases} \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) \;+\; 1 & \text{if } s = S_t \\ \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

For the **replacing trace** variant:

$$
e_t(s) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } s = S_t \\ \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — The Value Update (the "learning" step):**

$$
V(s) \;\leftarrow\; V(s) \;+\; \alpha \, \delta_t \, e_t(s) \quad \text{for every state } s
$$

### 2.2 Operational Logic Flow

- **Initialize** $V(s)$ arbitrarily and $e(s) = 0$ for all states $s$.
- **Observe** transition $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$.
- **Compute** the TD error $\delta_t$ — this is the local "surprise".
- **Increment** the eligibility trace of the current state $S_t$ by $1$ (accumulating) or set it to $1$ (replacing).
- **Decay** all eligibility traces by factor $\gamma\lambda$.
- **Propagate** $\delta_t$ backward: every state $s$ whose trace is non-zero receives an update proportional to $e_t(s)$.
- **Repeat** for each time step until terminal.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why a trace and not a sum?** A geometric decay gives **finite memory** with **closed-form tractability** and connects cleanly to the forward-view $\lambda$-return.
- **Why decay by $\gamma\lambda$?** The product $\gamma\lambda$ determines the **effective horizon** of credit assignment: a state visited $n$ steps ago retains weight $(\gamma\lambda)^n$.
- **Why update *all* states?** Because we don't know which state was *truly* responsible; the trace is a soft credit-assignment heuristic.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize Before Exam**

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Role in Backward-View |
|---|---|---|
| TD Error | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ | Surprise signal at time $t$ |
| Eligibility Trace (Accumulating) | $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}\{S_t = s\}$ | Recency-weighted memory |
| Eligibility Trace (Replacing) | $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s)$ for $s \neq S_t$; $= 1$ for $s = S_t$ | Bounded memory variant |
| Value Update | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t e_t(s)$ | Backward credit propagation |
| Effective Horizon | $h \approx \tfrac{1}{1-\gamma\lambda}$ | How far back credit reaches |
| TD(0) Limit | $\lambda = 0$ | Only the previous state gets updated |
| Monte Carlo Limit | $\lambda = 1, \gamma = 1$ | All visited states share full credit (in episodic tasks) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Backward-view TD(λ) is the **de facto workhorse** in practical RL deployments because:
- **Online & causal**: It can update values *as soon as* a transition is observed — no need to wait until episode end.
- **Memory-efficient**: One scalar trace per state (vs. storing the entire trajectory for MC).
- **Anytime**: Useful in robotics, game AI, and recommendation systems where decisions must be made continuously.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Forward-View $\lambda$-Return to Backward-View Update

The **forward-view TD(λ)** defines the $\lambda$-return at time $t$ as:

$$
G_t^{\lambda} \;=\; (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} \;+\; \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t
$$

where $G_t^{(n)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \dots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$ is the $n$-step return.

The forward-view update is $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left(G_t^{\lambda} - V(S_t)\right)$.

**Our goal:** Show this can be implemented *incrementally* using only the one-step TD error and an eligibility trace.

**Step 1 — Express $G_t^{\lambda} - V(S_t)$ in terms of TD errors.**

Using the identity (Sutton & Barto derivation):

$$
G_t^{\lambda} - V(S_t) \;=\; \sum_{k=t}^{T-1} (\gamma\lambda)^{k-t} \, \delta_k
$$

**Reasoning (text):** Every $n$-step return can be expanded telescopically into a sum of TD errors. The $\lambda$-weighting combines these expansions with coefficients summing to 1, and after re-grouping by the *time index* of each TD error $\delta_k$, the contribution of $\delta_k$ to $G_t^{\lambda} - V(S_t)$ is exactly $(\gamma\lambda)^{k-t}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the forward update.**

$$
V(S_t) \;\leftarrow\; V(S_t) \;+\; \alpha \sum_{k=t}^{T-1} (\gamma\lambda)^{k-t} \delta_k
$$

**Reasoning:** This is just a restatement of the forward-view update expanded in time.

**Step 3 — Restate as a backward sweep over all states.**

Now define $e_t(s) = \sum_{k=1}^{t} (\gamma\lambda)^{t-k} \cdot \mathbf{1}\{S_k = s\}$ (the accumulated trace). Then for any state $s$ visited at some time $k$:

$$
V(s) \;\leftarrow\; V(s) \;+\; \alpha \sum_{t=k}^{T-1} (\gamma\lambda)^{t-k} \delta_t
$$

Combining the two and rewriting compactly:

$$
V(s) \;\leftarrow\; V(s) \;+\; \alpha \,\delta_t \, e_t(s) \quad \text{at every step, for all } s
$$

This is the **backward-view TD(λ) update rule** derived from the forward-view $\lambda$-return — establishing the *mathematical equivalence* between the two views.

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example

> [!NOTE] **KTU-Style Numerical Illustration**
> Suppose a 5-step trajectory visits states $S_0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to S_3 \to S_4$ (terminal), with rewards $R_1 = 1, R_2 = 0, R_3 = 2, R_4 = 0$. Let $\gamma = 1$, $\lambda = 0.5$, $\alpha = 0.1$, and initial value estimates $V(S_0) = 0.5, V(S_1) = 0.4, V(S_2) = 0.3, V(S_3) = 0.2$, with terminal $V(S_4) = 0$.

**Step A — Compute eligibility traces backward from the trajectory (assuming we run backward-view during the episode):**

| Time $t$ | Visited $S_t$ | $e_t(S_0)$ | $e_t(S_1)$ | $e_t(S_2)$ | $e_t(S_3)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $S_0$ | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | $S_1$ | 0.5 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | $S_2$ | 0.25 | 0.5 | 1 | 0 |
| 3 | $S_3$ | 0.125 | 0.25 | 0.5 | 1 |

Using $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}\{S_t = s\}$ with $\gamma\lambda = 0.5$:

- At $t=0$: $e_0(S_0) = 0.5(0) + 1 = 1$. ✓
- At $t=1$: $e_1(S_0) = 0.5(1) + 0 = 0.5$; $e_1(S_1) = 0.5(0) + 1 = 1$. ✓
- At $t=2$: $e_2(S_0) = 0.5(0.5) = 0.25$; $e_2(S_1) = 0.5(1) = 0.5$; $e_2(S_2) = 0.5(0) + 1 = 1$. ✓
- At $t=3$: $e_3(S_0) = 0.5(0.25) = 0.125$; $e_3(S_1) = 0.5(0.5) = 0.25$; $e_3(S_2) = 0.5(1) = 0.5$; $e_3(S_3) = 0.5(0) + 1 = 1$. ✓

**Step B — Compute TD errors at each step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_0 &= R_1 + \gamma V(S_1) - V(S_0) = 1 + 1(0.4) - 0.5 = 0.9 \\
\delta_1 &= R_2 + \gamma V(S_2) - V(S_1) = 0 + 1(0.3) - 0.4 = -0.1 \\
\delta_2 &= R_3 + \gamma V(S_3) - V(S_2) = 2 + 1(0.2) - 0.3 = 1.9 \\
\delta_3 &= R_4 + \gamma V(S_4) - V(S_3) = 0 + 1(0) - 0.2 = -0.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Apply value updates immediately after each step.**

After step $t=0$ (using $\delta_0 = 0.9$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(S_0) &\leftarrow 0.5 + 0.1(0.9)(1) = 0.590 \\
V(S_1) &\leftarrow 0.4 + 0.1(0.9)(0) = 0.400 \\
V(S_2) &\leftarrow 0.3 + 0.1(0.9)(0) = 0.300 \\
V(S_3) &\leftarrow 0.2 + 0.1(0.9)(0) = 0.200
\end{aligned}
$$

After step $t=1$ (using $\delta_1 = -0.1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(S_0) &\leftarrow 0.590 + 0.1(-0.1)(0.5) = 0.585 \\
V(S_1) &\leftarrow 0.400 + 0.1(-0.1)(1) = 0.390
\end{aligned}
$$

After step $t=2$ (using $\delta_2 = 1.9$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(S_0) &\leftarrow 0.585 + 0.1(1.9)(0.25) = 0.6325 \\
V(S_1) &\leftarrow 0.390 + 0.1(1.9)(0.5) = 0.485 \\
V(S_2) &\leftarrow 0.300 + 0.1(1.9)(1) = 0.490
\end{aligned}
$$

After step $t=3$ (using $\delta_3 = -0.2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(S_0) &\leftarrow 0.6325 + 0.1(-0.2)(0.125) = 0.6300 \\
V(S_1) &\leftarrow 0.485 + 0.1(-0.2)(0.25) = 0.4800 \\
V(S_2) &\leftarrow 0.490 + 0.1(-0.2)(0.5) = 0.4800 \\
V(S_3) &\leftarrow 0.200 + 0.1(-0.2)(1) = 0.1800
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Final Analytical Summary

| State | Initial $V$ | Final $V$ | Net Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_0$ | 0.500 | 0.6300 | $+0.1300$ |
| $S_1$ | 0.400 | 0.4800 | $+0.0800$ |
| $S_2$ | 0.300 | 0.4800 | $+0.1800$ |
| $S_3$ | 0.200 | 0.1800 | $-0.0200$ |

**Interpretation:** States visited **most recently** (e.g., $S_2$ right before a large positive reward) received the **largest update**, while older states ($S_0$) received a smaller share — perfectly matching the **backward-view philosophy** of geometric recency-weighted credit assignment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Backward-View TD(λ) Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize V s and e s to 0 for all states] --> B[Observe initial state St]
    B --> C[Take action At in environment]
    C --> D[Receive reward Rt1 and next state St1]
    D --> E[Compute TD error deltat = Rt1 + gamma V St1 - V St]
    E --> F[Increment trace of current state eSt = eSt + 1]
    F --> G[Decay all traces es = gamma lambda times es for all s]
    G --> H[Update value function V s = V s + alpha deltat e s for all s]
    H --> I{Is St1 terminal?}
    I -- No --> B
    I -- Yes --> J[End of episode: reset traces to 0]
    J --> K[Begin next episode]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E,color:#000
    style F fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C,color:#000
    style H fill:#FDECEC,stroke:#D62728,color:#000
    style I fill:#F5E8F8,stroke:#9467BD,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Conceptual Architecture — Trace Decay & Backward Propagation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Past["Visited States (Back in Time)"]
        S0["S0  t-3  trace = 0.125"]
        S1["S1  t-2  trace = 0.25"]
        S2["S2  t-1  trace = 0.5"]
    end
    subgraph Now["Current Step"]
        S3["S3  t   trace = 1.0"]
        DELTA["TD Error deltat"]
    end
    S3 --> DELTA
    DELTA -- "alpha deltat e_t S0" --> S0
    DELTA -- "alpha deltat e_t S1" --> S1
    DELTA -- "alpha deltat e_t S2" --> S2

    style S0 fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#D62728
    style S1 fill:#FFD1D1,stroke:#D62728
    style S2 fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#D62728
    style S3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#2CA02C
    style DELTA fill:#FFF4B3,stroke:#FF7F0E
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: $\lambda$ Parameter Effect on Memory Horizon

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["lambda = 0  TD 0  Only 1-step back"] --> B["lambda = 0.3  Short trace"]
    B --> C["lambda = 0.7  Medium trace"]
    C --> D["lambda = 1.0  Full episode trace approaching MC"]

    style A fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#1F77B4
    style B fill:#CCE7FF,stroke:#1F77B4
    style C fill:#99D1FF,stroke:#1F77B4
    style D fill:#66BBFF,stroke:#1F77B4
```

> [!NOTE] **Architectural Insight**
> The diagrams above make the **backward-flow** nature of credit assignment visually explicit: at each time $t$, the current TD error $\delta_t$ acts as a *broadcast* signal that reaches *all* past states, attenuated by the **geometric decay** of their eligibility traces. The hyperparameter $\lambda$ controls the *reach* of this broadcast, while $\gamma$ controls its *strength over time*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern] Define the term "eligibility trace" in the context of Backward-View TD(λ). Explain how it differs for accumulating and replacing variants. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> An **eligibility trace** $e_t(s)$ is a scalar associated with each state $s$ at time $t$ that records the *recency* and *frequency* with which $s$ has been visited in the recent past. It acts as a temporary memory of state visits that decays geometrically over time and is used to propagate the current TD error backward to past states.
> 
> In the **accumulating** variant, each visit to state $s$ adds $1$ to its trace: $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}\{S_t = s\}$. This can cause the trace value to grow arbitrarily large for frequently visited states.
> 
> In the **replacing** variant, the trace is *capped* at $1$ on each visit: $e_t(s) = 1$ if $S_t = s$, otherwise $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s)$. This prevents the unbounded growth and is preferred in practice.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern] What is the role of the parameter $\lambda$ in Backward-View TD(λ)? What happens when $\lambda = 0$ and $\lambda = 1$? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> The parameter $\lambda \in [0, 1]$ is the **trace-decay rate** that controls how quickly the eligibility of past states fades. It determines the **effective horizon** of credit assignment: $h \approx \tfrac{1}{1-\gamma\lambda}$.
> 
> - When **$\lambda = 0$**, the trace decays instantly to $0$ after each step, so only the immediately previous state is updated — this reduces Backward-View TD(λ) to standard **one-step TD(0)**.
> - When **$\lambda = 1$** (with $\gamma = 1$ in episodic tasks), the trace persists throughout the entire episode, and every visited state receives credit proportional to its number of visits — this approaches the **Monte Carlo** behavior in expectation.
> 
> Intermediate values of $\lambda$ give a **continuous spectrum** between these two extremes, allowing a tunable bias-variance tradeoff.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Evaluation Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Pattern]*

**(a)** Explain the Backward-View TD(λ) algorithm in detail. Derive the eligibility trace update equation and the value update rule. Discuss the role of the parameters $\alpha$, $\gamma$, and $\lambda$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 4-state trajectory $S_0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to S_3$ (terminal) with rewards $R_1 = 0$, $R_2 = 1$, $R_3 = 0$. Initial value estimates are $V(S_0) = 0.2$, $V(S_1) = 0.3$, $V(S_2) = 0.4$, $V(S_3) = 0.0$ (terminal). Using $\gamma = 1$, $\lambda = 0.5$, $\alpha = 0.1$, compute the eligibility traces at each step and the final updated values after one full pass of Backward-View TD(λ) with **accumulating traces**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Definition of Backward-View TD(λ):** Backward-View TD(λ) is a temporal-difference learning algorithm that updates the value estimate of *every state* at each time step, using a scalar **eligibility trace** $e_t(s)$ to determine the magnitude of the update for state $s$ at time $t$. The update is driven by the **one-step TD error** $\delta_t$ computed at the current transition.

**Eligibility Trace Derivation:**

We require a mechanism that:
1. **Boosts** the trace of the just-visited state (to $1$).
2. **Decays** all traces geometrically with each passing step.

The most general form satisfying these is:

$$
e_t(s) \;=\; \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) \;+\; \mathbf{1}\{S_t = s\}
$$

This recurrence says: "The trace of $s$ at time $t$ equals its decayed value from time $t-1$ (with factor $\gamma\lambda$), plus $1$ if $s$ was visited at time $t$." The initial condition is $e_0(s) = 0$ for all $s$.

**Value Update Rule:**

After computing the TD error $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$, every state $s$ is updated as:

$$
V(s) \;\leftarrow\; V(s) \;+\; \alpha \, \delta_t \, e_t(s)
$$

**Role of Hyperparameters:**

- $\alpha$ **(learning rate)**: Scales the magnitude of every value update. Small $\alpha$ → slow but stable learning; large $\alpha$ → fast but possibly divergent.
- $\gamma$ **(discount factor)**: Determines how much future rewards matter for the *current* TD error. Also embedded in the trace decay.
- $\lambda$ **(trace-decay rate)**: Controls the *effective memory* of the algorithm. Low $\lambda$ → short trace → low variance, high bias. High $\lambda$ → long trace → high variance, low bias (in the limit, behaves like MC).

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Trajectory and parameters:**
- States: $S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3$ (terminal).
- Rewards: $R_1 = 0$ (after $S_0$), $R_2 = 1$ (after $S_1$), $R_3 = 0$ (after $S_2$).
- $\gamma = 1$, $\lambda = 0.5$, $\alpha = 0.1$ → $\gamma\lambda = 0.5$.
- Initial: $V(S_0) = 0.2$, $V(S_1) = 0.3$, $V(S_2) = 0.4$, $V(S_3) = 0.0$.
- All $e_0(s) = 0$.

**Step 2 — Compute traces at each step using accumulating rule $e_t(s) = 0.5 \cdot e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}\{S_t = s\}$:**

| Time $t$ | State $S_t$ | $e_t(S_0)$ | $e_t(S_1)$ | $e_t(S_2)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $S_0$ | $0.5(0)+1 = 1.0$ | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | $S_1$ | $0.5(1.0) = 0.5$ | $0.5(0)+1 = 1.0$ | 0 |
| 2 | $S_2$ | $0.5(0.5) = 0.25$ | $0.5(1.0) = 0.5$ | $0.5(0)+1 = 1.0$ |

**Step 3 — Compute TD errors at each step (using $V$ from end of previous step):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_0 &= R_1 + \gamma V(S_1) - V(S_0) = 0 + 1(0.3) - 0.2 = 0.1 \\
\delta_1 &= R_2 + \gamma V(S_2) - V(S_1) = 1 + 1(0.4) - 0.3 = 1.1 \\
\delta_2 &= R_3 + \gamma V(S_3) - V(S_2) = 0 + 1(0.0) - 0.4 = -0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply updates sequentially:**

**After $t=0$ ($\delta_0 = 0.1$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(S_0) &\leftarrow 0.2 + 0.1(0.1)(1.0) = 0.210 \\
V(S_1) &\leftarrow 0.3 + 0.1(0.1)(0) = 0.300 \\
V(S_2) &\leftarrow 0.4 + 0.1(0.1)(0) = 0.400
\end{aligned}
$$

**After $t=1$ ($\delta_1 = 1.1$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(S_0) &\leftarrow 0.210 + 0.1(1.1)(0.5) = 0.265 \\
V(S_1) &\leftarrow 0.300 + 0.1(1.1)(1.0) = 0.410 \\
V(S_2) &\leftarrow 0.400 + 0.1(1.1)(0) = 0.400
\end{aligned}
$$

**After $t=2$ ($\delta_2 = -0.4$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(S_0) &\leftarrow 0.265 + 0.1(-0.4)(0.25) = 0.255 \\
V(S_1) &\leftarrow 0.410 + 0.1(-0.4)(0.5) = 0.390 \\
V(S_2) &\leftarrow 0.400 + 0.1(-0.4)(1.0) = 0.360
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Updated Values:**

| State | Initial $V$ | Final $V$ |
|---|---|---|
| $S_0$ | 0.20 | **0.255** |
| $S_1$ | 0.30 | **0.390** |
| $S_2$ | 0.40 | **0.360** |

**Observation:** $S_1$ receives the **largest positive update** (it is the immediate predecessor of the large positive reward $R_2 = 1$), while $S_2$ receives a **negative update** (it was followed by a zero reward from the terminal state). $S_0$ is updated *less* than $S_1$ because its trace decayed more — exactly the backward-view recency-weighted credit assignment.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]*

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Forward-View TD(λ)** and **Backward-View TD(λ)** algorithms. Explain why the backward view is preferred for *online* implementation, even though both views are mathematically equivalent. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Prove mathematically that the Forward-View update $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha(G_t^{\lambda} - V(S_t))$ can be implemented incrementally as the Backward-View update $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t e_t(s)$ for all $s$, where $e_t(s) = \sum_{k=1}^{t} (\gamma\lambda)^{t-k} \mathbf{1}\{S_k = s\}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Conceptual Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Forward-View TD(λ) | Backward-View TD(λ) |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of reasoning | Looks *forward* to future returns | Looks *backward* at past states |
| Update timing | Requires the full $\lambda$-return $G_t^{\lambda}$ (causally impossible mid-episode) | Updates **immediately** after each transition |
| Memory requirement | Must store all future rewards up to horizon $h$ | Stores only one scalar trace per state |
| Use case | Theoretical analysis, proofs of convergence | Practical, online, real-time learning |
| Credit assignment | Via the geometrically-weighted blend of $n$-step returns | Via geometrically-decaying eligibility traces |
| Mathematical nature | *Specification* of the learning target | *Mechanism* that achieves the same target |

**Why the Backward View is Preferred for Online Implementation:**

1. **Causality**: The forward view requires $G_t^{\lambda}$, which depends on rewards observed *after* time $t$ — these are not yet available. The backward view uses only information available *now*: the current TD error and the past trace.
2. **Computational efficiency**: A single scalar multiplication per state per step (no nested future return computation).
3. **No waiting**: Updates occur *at every step* — ideal for non-episodic / continuing tasks and for fast real-time systems (robotics, game AI).
4. **Memory-efficient**: For a state space of size $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$, backward view uses $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert)$ storage for traces, whereas forward view needs the entire trajectory.

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight**
> Even though they are **mathematically equivalent** in expectation (both converge to the same value function under standard conditions), the backward view is the *implementable* form of the forward view's theoretical specification. Think of the forward view as the *destination* and the backward view as the *GPS route* — both reach the same place, but only the GPS is usable in real-time.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Proof of Equivalence:**

We begin with the **forward-view update**:

$$
V(S_t) \;\leftarrow\; V(S_t) \;+\; \alpha \big( G_t^{\lambda} - V(S_t) \big) \tag{1}
$$

**Step 1 — Decompose the $\lambda$-return error into TD errors.**

A well-known identity (the "error decomposition") states:

$$
G_t^{\lambda} - V(S_t) \;=\; \sum_{k=t}^{T-1} (\gamma\lambda)^{k-t} \, \delta_k \tag{2}
$$

**Reasoning (text):** Each $n$-step target $G_t^{(n)}$ can be written as $V(S_t) + \sum_{k=t}^{t+n-1} \gamma^{k-t} \delta_k$. The $\lambda$-return is a convex combination of $G_t^{(n)}$ weights, and after collecting terms, every $\delta_k$ ends up weighted by $(\gamma\lambda)^{k-t}$. (A full proof proceeds by induction on the episode length or by the general TD-error decomposition theorem.)

**Step 2 — Substitute (2) into (1).**

$$
V(S_t) \;\leftarrow\; V(S_t) \;+\; \alpha \sum_{k=t}^{T-1} (\gamma\lambda)^{k-t} \delta_k \tag{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Restate (3) as updates to *all* states, not just $S_t$.**

Suppose a state $s$ is visited at times $k_1, k_2, \dots$. Then the total update received by $s$ across the entire episode is:

$$
V(s) \;\leftarrow\; V(s) \;+\; \alpha \sum_{k_j} \sum_{k=k_j}^{T-1} (\gamma\lambda)^{k-k_j} \delta_k \tag{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the trace definition.**

The eligibility trace as defined in the problem is:

$$
e_t(s) \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{t} (\gamma\lambda)^{t-k} \mathbf{1}\{S_k = s\}
$$

This is exactly the coefficient that multiplies $\delta_t$ when updating $V(s)$ at time $t$. Therefore, the backward-view update

$$
V(s) \;\leftarrow\; V(s) \;+\; \alpha \delta_t \, e_t(s) \quad \text{for all } s \tag{5}
$$

applied at every time step $t$ from $0$ to $T-1$, produces **exactly the same final value function** as the forward-view update (3). This proves the equivalence. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**
> 1. **Confusing the two views**: Students often write "Forward view updates past states" — *wrong*. Forward view updates the **current** state using **future** information; backward view updates **all past** states using **current** information.
> 2. **Forgetting the initial trace reset**: The trace $e(s)$ must be reset to $0$ at the **start of every episode** (for episodic tasks). Failing to do so causes *cross-episode contamination* and is a major error.
> 3. **Mixing up accumulating vs. replacing traces**: Students frequently apply the wrong formula. Remember: accumulating *adds* 1 on visit; replacing *sets* to 1 on visit.
> 4. **Sign errors in TD error**: The formula is $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$, **not** $V(S_t) - R_{t+1} - \gamma V(S_{t+1})$. The sign is critical.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply update to ALL states**: The backward-view update must be applied to *every* state $s$, not only the current $S_t$. A trace of $0$ will yield no change, but the formula must be written for all $s$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Backward-View TD(λ)**
> 
> **Core Identity:**
> - Backward-View TD(λ) = **Forward-View TD(λ) + Eligibility Traces** (causal, online implementation).
> 
> **Must-Memorize Equations:**
> - $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$
> - $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}\{S_t = s\}$ (accumulating)
> - $e_t(s) = 1$ if $S_t = s$, else $\gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s)$ (replacing)
> - $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \, \delta_t \, e_t(s)$ for all $s$
> 
> **Limit Cases (Frequently Asked):**
> - $\lambda = 0$ → **TD(0)** — only one-step backup.
> - $\lambda = 1, \gamma = 1$ → **Monte Carlo** (in episodic tasks).
> 
> **Key Properties:**
> - **Online** & **causal** — updates after every transition.
> - **Memory-efficient** — $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert)$ trace storage.
> - **Mathematically equivalent** to Forward-View TD(λ) in expectation.
> 
> **Bias-Variance Insight:**
> - Low $\lambda$ → low variance, high bias.
> - High $\lambda$ → high variance, low bias.
> - $\lambda$ is a **tunable knob** between TD and MC.
> 
> **Implementation Hygiene:**
> - Reset all traces to $0$ at the start of every episode.
> - Decay *before* increment, or be consistent across implementations.
> - Watch for state aliasing: replacing traces prevent trace blow-up.
> 
> **Frequently Asked Variations:**
> - Dutch traces: $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + (1-\alpha)\mathbf{1}\{S_t = s\}$ — combines replacing and accumulating.
> - Clear traces: $e_t(s) = 0$ for $s \neq S_t$ on every visit — used in actor-critic methods.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generalized Policy Iteration (GPI)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Generalized Policy Iteration (GPI) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Generalized Policy Iteration (GPI)** is the general idea of letting **policy evaluation** and **policy improvement** interact with each other, *irrespective of the granularity* of these two processes. It is the conceptual backbone of virtually all modern reinforcement learning algorithms. ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** $\mathcal{M} = \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, r, \gamma \rangle$, Generalized Policy Iteration refers to the iterative procedure in which: 1. The **state-value function** $V_\pi$ is repeatedly updated to better approximate the true value function of the current **policy** $\pi$ (the *evaluation* half). 2. The **policy** $\pi$ is repeatedly updated to be more greedy with respect to the current estimate of the value function (the *improvement* half). Both processes stabilize — and at the fixed point they jointly converge to a point where the policy $\pi_*$ is optimal and the value function $V_{\pi_*}$ satisfies the **Bellman Optimality Equation**. $$V_*(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\left[r + \gamma V_*(s')\right]$$ ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The GPS and the Driver" Imagine a driver (Policy $\pi$) learning to navigate an unfamiliar city. - The driver estimates **how good each road is** (Value Function $V$). - Based on those estimates, the driver **chooses new routes** (Policy Improvement). - After taking new routes, the driver **re-estimates road quality** with the new experience (Policy Evaluation). The driver never completely finishes evaluating *all* roads before updating the route — he constantly switches between estimation and action selection. **That is exactly what GPI formalizes.** > [!NOTE] > **Key insight:** In the limit, even if you only do *one sweep* of evaluation before one greedy improvement, you still converge to the optimal policy. GPI generalizes **Policy Iteration** (many evaluation sweeps) and **Value Iteration** (single sweep) as special cases. ### 1.3 Standard KTU Metrics to Remember - **Discount factor**: $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ — controls the weight of future rewards. - **Bellman residual** $\delta$: the maximum change between successive value estimates; used as a stopping criterion. - **Optimality gap**: $\Vert V - V_* \Vert_\infty$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Interacting Processes — Logic Breakdown **A. Policy Evaluation (Prediction)** - **Why?** We need a quantitative measure of "how good" the current policy is in every state. - **How?** Apply the **Bellman Expectation Backup** operator $T^\pi$ repeatedly to a value function estimate $V_k$: $$T^\pi V(s) \;=\; \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\left[r + \gamma V(s')\right]$$ - Iterate $V_{k+1}(s) = T^\pi V_k(s)$ until $\Vert V_{k+1} - V_k \Vert_\infty < \epsilon_{eval}$. **B. Policy Improvement (Control)** - **Why?** A policy that is not greedy with respect to its own value function can be improved. - **How?** Construct a new policy $\pi'$ that is **greedy** with respect to the action-value function $Q_\pi(s,a)$: $$\pi'(s) \;=\; \underset{a \in \mathcal{A}}{\arg\max}\; Q_\pi(s,a) \;=\; \underset{a}{\arg\max}\; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\left[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')\right]$$ - **Policy Improvement Theorem**: For all states $s \in \mathcal{S}$, $V_{\pi'}(s) \geq V_\pi(s)$. Equality holds for all states iff $\pi$ is optimal. **C. The Interaction Loop (The "Generalized" Part)** GPI does *not* require that either process be run to completion.
