The two processes cooperate and compete: - **Competition**: They pull the value function and the policy in opposite directions. - **Cooperation**: Each process creates a moving target for the other, but the overall system *jointly converges* because both operators are **contractions**. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Name | Purpose | |---|---------|------|---------| | 1 | $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s',r}p(s',r\mid s,a)[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')]$ | Bellman Expectation Eqn. | Evaluate policy $\pi$ | | 2 | $Q_\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r\mid s,a)[r + \gamma \sum_{a'}\pi(a'\mid s')Q_\pi(s',a')]$ | Bellman Expectation (Action-Value) | Evaluate $\pi$ in action space | | 3 | $V_*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r}p(s',r\mid s,a)[r + \gamma V_*(s')]$ | Bellman Optimality Eqn. | Characterize $V_*$ | | 4 | $T^\pi V(s) = \sum_a \pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s',r}p(s',r\mid s,a)[r + \gamma V(s')]$ | Policy Evaluation Operator | One Bellman backup | | 5 | $T_* V(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r}p(s',r\mid s,a)[r + \gamma V(s')]$ | Bellman Optimality Operator | One optimality backup | | 6 | $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s',r}p(s',r\mid s,a)[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')]$ | Greedy Policy Update | Policy improvement | | 7 | $\Vert T_* V - T_* W \Vert_\infty \leq \gamma \Vert V - W \Vert_\infty$ | Contraction Property | Guarantees convergence | | 8 | $V_{k+1}(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r}p(s',r\mid s,a)[r + \gamma V_k(s')]$ | Value Iteration Update | GPI with $n_{eval}=1$ | | 9 | $\delta_k = \max_s \vert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \vert$ | Bellman Residual | Stopping criterion | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** GPI is the conceptual scaffold behind *every* model-based RL method — from classical Dynamic Programming in operations research, to AlphaGo's value/policy networks, to industrial applications like inventory control, robotics motion planning, and autonomous driving decision stacks. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Contraction Property of $T_*$ We want to prove that the Bellman optimality operator is a $\gamma$-contraction in max-norm. **Step 1 — Definition.** Let $V$ and $W$ be two arbitrary value functions, and let $T_*$ denote the optimality backup operator: $$T_* V(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a)\bigl[r + \gamma V(s')\bigr]$$ **Step 2 — Fix an arbitrary state $s$.** Define the maximizing action under $V$ and under $W$: $$a_V^* = \underset{a}{\arg\max}\; \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a)\bigl[r + \gamma V(s')\bigr]$$ $$a_W^* = \underset{a}{\arg\max}\; \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a)\bigl[r + \gamma W(s')\bigr]$$ **Step 3 — Express the difference.** Since $a_V^*$ is the maximizer for $V$, but $a_W^*$ is the maximizer for $W$, evaluating the $V$-operator at the $W$-optimizer gives a value $\leq T_* V(s)$: $$\begin{aligned} T_* V(s) - T_* W(s) &= \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a_V^*)\bigl[r + \gamma V(s')\bigr] - \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a_W^*)\bigl[r + \gamma W(s')\bigr] \\ &\leq \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a_W^*)\bigl[r + \gamma V(s')\bigr] - \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a_W^*)\bigl[r + \gamma W(s')\bigr] \\ &= \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a_W^*)\,\gamma\, \bigl[V(s') - W(s')\bigr] \\ &\leq \gamma \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a_W^*)\,\max_{s'}\bigl[V(s') - W(s')\bigr] \\ &= \gamma\, \Vert V - W \Vert_\infty \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Symmetric argument.** By symmetry, we also have $T_* W(s) - T_* V(s) \leq \gamma \Vert V - W \Vert_\infty$. Combining both: $$\vert T_* V(s) - T_* W(s) \vert \leq \gamma\, \Vert V - W \Vert_\infty \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}$$ **Step 5 — Take the max over states.** $$\Vert T_* V - T_* W \Vert_\infty \;=\; \max_s \vert T_* V(s) - T_* W(s) \vert \;\leq\; \gamma\, \Vert V - W \Vert_\infty$$ **Step 6 — Banach Fixed-Point Theorem.** Because $\gamma < 1$, $T_*$ is a strict contraction, hence there exists a **unique fixed point** $V_*$ such that $T_* V_* = V_*$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Policy Improvement Theorem **Step 1 — Setup.** Suppose we have a policy $\pi$ and its value function $V_\pi$. Define a new policy $\pi'$ via: $$\pi'(s) = \underset{a}{\arg\max}\; \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a)\bigl[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')\bigr]$$ **Step 2 — One-step lookahead inequality.** For every state $s$: $$\begin{aligned} V_\pi(s) &\leq \max_{a}\sum_{s',r}p(s',r \mid s, a)\bigl[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')\bigr] \\ &= \sum_{s',r}p(s',r \mid s, \pi'(s))\bigl[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')\bigr] \\ &= \sum_{a'} \pi'(a' \mid s)\sum_{s',r}p(s',r \mid s, a')\bigl[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')\bigr] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\bigl[r_{t+1} + \gamma V_\pi(s_{t+1}) \mid s_t = s\bigr] \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Iterating the inequality.** Apply the above inequality repeatedly: $$\begin{aligned} V_\pi(s) &\leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\bigl[r_{t+1} + \gamma V_\pi(s_{t+1}) \mid s_t = s\bigr] \\ &\leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\bigl[r_{t+1} + \gamma\, r_{t+2} + \gamma^2 V_\pi(s_{t+2}) \mid s_t = s\bigr] \\ &\;\;\vdots \\ &\leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\Bigl[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}\Bigr] \\ &= V_{\pi'}(s) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Conclusion.** Therefore $V_{\pi'}(s) \geq V_\pi(s)$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$. If $\pi' = \pi$, then for the inequality to hold with equality, $\pi$ must already be greedy, which by Bellman's optimality theorem implies $\pi$ is optimal. $$\boxed{\;\pi' \neq \pi \;\Longrightarrow\; V_{\pi'}(s) \geq V_\pi(s) \text{ for all } s \in \mathcal{S}\;}$$ --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — GPI Variants at a Glance | Algorithm | $n_{eval}$ (eval steps per iteration) | $n_{imp}$ (imp steps per iteration) | Convergence Speed | Computational Cost | |-----------|---------------------------------------|------------------------------------|--------------------|--------------------| | **Policy Iteration** | $\infty$ (run until $\epsilon_{eval} \to 0$) | 1 | Few iterations | High per iteration | | **Value Iteration** | 1 | 1 (implicit, via $\max$) | Many iterations | Low per iteration | | **Modified Policy Iteration** | $k$ (fixed finite number) | 1 | In between | Tunable | | **Asynchronous GPI** | 1 state update | 1 state improvement | State-dependent | Lowest memory | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The GPI Interaction Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Initialize arbitrary policy pi0
and value function V0] --> B[Policy Evaluation Step
Apply Bellman backup T^pi to V] B --> C{Convergence check
delta less than epsilon_eval} C -- No --> B C -- Yes --> D[Compute action-value Qpi from Vpi
Qpi s a equals expected r plus gamma Vpi s'] D --> E[Policy Improvement Step
pi' s equals argmax over a of Qpi s a] E --> F{Policy stable
pi' equals pi} F -- No --> G[Set pi equals pi' and V equals Vpi] G --> B F -- Yes --> H[Fixed Point Reached
V equals V star and pi equals pi star] H --> I[Return optimal value V star
and optimal policy pi star] style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd style H fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754 style I fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754 style C fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107 style F fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107 ``` ### 4.2 GPI as the Generalization of Policy and Value Iteration ```mermaid flowchart LR GPI[Generalized Policy Iteration
GPI] --> PI[Policy Iteration
n_eval equals infinity] GPI --> MVI[Modified Policy Iteration
n_eval equals k finite] GPI --> VI[Value Iteration
n_eval equals 1] GPI --> Async[Asynchronous Dynamic Programming
in-place sweeps] style GPI fill:#0d6efd,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0a58ca style PI fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd style MVI fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd style VI fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd style Async fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd ``` ### 4.3 Convergence Trajectory (Value Function Estimate Over Iterations) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Iter1[Iteration 1] V1[V_k approximation coarse
policy still suboptimal] end subgraph Iter2[Iteration 2] V2[V_k plus 1 closer to V star
policy improved] end subgraph IterN[Iteration N] VN[V star reached
pi star greedy w.r.t. V star] end Iter1 --> Iter2 Iter2 --> IterN style Iter1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545 style Iter2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107 style IterN fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** *Define Generalized Policy Iteration. How does it differ from classical Policy Iteration?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Generalized Policy Iteration (GPI) is the general scheme in which **policy evaluation** and **policy improvement** are interleaved iteratively without requiring either process to be carried out to completion. At each step, the current value-function estimate is pushed closer to $V_\pi$ via Bellman backups, and the policy is pushed toward being greedy with respect to that estimate. The interaction continues until both stabilize at the optimal fixed point. > The difference from classical **Policy Iteration** is the *granularity*: Policy Iteration runs evaluation to full convergence (Bellman residual $\to 0$) before each improvement step. GPI permits *partial* evaluation — even a single sweep — and still converges, because both the evaluation operator and the greedy improvement operator are contractions under $\gamma \in [0, 1)$. This makes GPI computationally cheaper per iteration and is the conceptual template behind Value Iteration and Asynchronous DP. *(Mark distribution: 1 mark definition + 1 mark evaluation/improvement process + 1 mark distinction.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** *State and explain the Bellman Optimality Equation. Why is it called the optimality equation?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The Bellman Optimality Equation expresses the optimal state-value function $V_*$ as the **maximum** over actions of the expected one-step reward plus the discounted optimal future value: $$V_*(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\bigl[r + \gamma V_*(s')\bigr]$$ > It is called the *optimality* equation because, unlike the Bellman expectation equation which is parametrized by a specific policy $\pi$, this equation is **self-referential on $V_* alone$**. The maximizing action inside implicitly encodes the optimal policy. Any value function $V$ satisfying this equation must, by the policy improvement theorem, be the unique optimal value function. Existence and uniqueness are guaranteed because $T_*$ is a $\gamma$-contraction mapping. *(Mark distribution: 1 mark equation + 1 mark explanation of $\max$ + 1 mark contraction/unique fixed-point justification.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Choice > **Internal Choice:** *Answer ONE full question from the pair.* --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]** **(a)** *With neat notation, derive the Bellman Optimality backup operator and prove that it is a $\gamma$-contraction in max-norm.* **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** Step 1: Define the optimality backup operator $T_*$ acting on an arbitrary value function $V$: $$T_* V(s) \;\triangleq\; \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s',r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\bigl[r + \gamma V(s')\bigr]$$ Step 2: Take two arbitrary bounded value functions $V$ and $W$, fix any state $s$, and let $a_V^* = \arg\max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma V(s')]$ and similarly $a_W^*$. Step 3: Bound the one-sided difference: $$\begin{aligned} T_* V(s) - T_* W(s) &= \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a_V^*)[r + \gamma V(s')] - \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a_W^*)[r + \gamma W(s')] \\ &\leq \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a_W^*)[r + \gamma V(s')] - \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a_W^*)[r + \gamma W(s')] \\ &= \gamma \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a_W^*)\bigl[V(s') - W(s')\bigr] \\ &\leq \gamma \Vert V - W \Vert_\infty \end{aligned}$$ Step 4: Symmetrically, $T_* W(s) - T_* V(s) \leq \gamma \Vert V - W \Vert_\infty$. Combining both: $$\vert T_* V(s) - T_* W(s) \vert \;\leq\; \gamma \Vert V - W \Vert_\infty \quad \forall s$$ Step 5: Taking $\max_s$ on both sides: $$\Vert T_* V - T_* W \Vert_\infty \;\leq\; \gamma \Vert V - W \Vert_\infty$$ Since $0 \leq \gamma < 1$, $T_*$ is a strict contraction in max-norm. By Banach's Fixed Point Theorem, it has a unique fixed point $V_*$, and $T_*^n V \to V_*$ for any starting $V$. **(7 marks fully awarded)** --- **(b)** *Explain Generalized Policy Iteration. Show how Value Iteration and Policy Iteration emerge as boundary cases of GPI. Use a labelled block diagram of the interaction.* **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** Step 1 (2 marks): Define GPI as the cooperative interplay between (i) **policy evaluation** (driving $V \to V_\pi$ via repeated application of $T^\pi$) and (ii) **policy improvement** (driving $\pi \to \pi' = \arg\max_a Q_\pi(s,a)$). Both processes need *not* terminate; only their joint fixed point is required. Step 2 (2 marks): **Policy Iteration** = GPI with $n_{eval} \to \infty$ (run $T^\pi$ until $\delta < 10^{-k}$) and $n_{imp} = 1$. This is the **accurate but slow** boundary. Step 3 (2 marks): **Value Iteration** = GPI with $n_{eval} = 1$ and $n_{imp} = 1$ *embedded as the $\max$* inside the backup. The update becomes $V_{k+1}(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma V_k(s')]$, which is precisely $V_{k+1} = T_* V_k$. This is the **fast but coarse** boundary. Step 4 (1 mark): The labelled diagram is reproduced below (see Section 4.1 above for the full Mermaid block). The diagram must show the loop: *Initialize $\pi_0, V_0 \Rightarrow$ Evaluate $\Rightarrow$ Improve $\Rightarrow$ Check Stability $\Rightarrow$ Loop or Conclude*. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **(a)** *Derive the Policy Improvement Theorem. State clearly the assumptions required.* **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** Step 1 (1 mark): Assume a finite MDP $\mathcal{M} = \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, r, \gamma \rangle$ with $\gamma \in [0,1)$ and deterministic stationary policies $\pi, \pi'$. Step 2 (1 mark): Define $\pi'$ greedily: $$\pi'(s) = \underset{a}{\arg\max}\sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')]$$ Step 3 (3 marks): For any state $s$, the inequality $$\begin{aligned} V_\pi(s) &\leq \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')] \\ &= \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, \pi'(s))[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\bigl[r_{t+1} + \gamma V_\pi(s_{t+1}) \mid s_t = s\bigr] \end{aligned}$$ Step 4 (2 marks): Iterate the inequality $n$ times, then take $n \to \infty$: $$V_\pi(s) \;\leq\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}\Bigl[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} \mid s_t = s\Bigr] \;=\; V_{\pi'}(s)$$ Step 5 (0 marks extra): Conclude $V_{\pi'}(s) \geq V_\pi(s)$ for all $s$. If $\pi' = \pi$, equality holds and $\pi$ is optimal. **(7 marks fully awarded)** --- **(b)** *Discuss the convergence guarantees of GPI. Why does interleaving partial sweeps still converge to the optimal policy?* **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** Step 1 (2 marks): Both the policy-evaluation operator $T^\pi$ and the optimality operator $T_*$ are **$\gamma$-contractions** in max-norm (proved in 5.2(a)). By Banach's Fixed-Point Theorem, each operator applied repeatedly converges to a unique fixed point starting from any initial $V$. Step 2 (2 marks): In GPI, although we switch back and forth between operators, **the overall process is a contraction toward the joint fixed point** $(V_*, \pi_*)$. Because each operator pulls the pair $(V, \pi)$ toward the *same* optimal limit, partial sweeps still make forward progress. Step 3 (2 marks): The system is **stable** because $T^\pi$ and $T_*$ both contract by the *same* factor $\gamma$. Even with a single evaluation sweep between improvements, the composite map $V_{k+1} = T_*^{n_e} \circ T^{\pi_k^{n_i}} V_k$ is a contraction with factor $\gamma$ (or tighter), guaranteeing geometric convergence. Step 4 (1 mark): Hence **GPI converges to $V_*$ and $\pi_*$ from any starting $(V_0, \pi_0)$**, and the rate is geometric with constant $\gamma^{n_{e} + n_{i}}$. **(7 marks fully awarded)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting to justify that $\gamma < 1$.** Without this, the contraction proof fails and full 7 marks may be cut to 2. > 2. **Confusing $T^\pi$ (expectation) with $T_*$ (optimality).** They look similar but the presence/absence of $\max$ changes everything. > 3. **Skipping the "greedy policy exists" assumption** when applying the policy improvement theorem — KTU expects explicit statement of finite $\mathcal{A}$. > 4. **Writing "GPI = Value Iteration"** — this is a frequent mis-statement. Value Iteration is a *special case* of GPI; GPI is the general umbrella. > 5. **Diagrams without labels:** KTU's 2024 scheme gives marks for labelled block diagrams. A box-and-arrow sketch with NO labels typically receives 0 of the diagram marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** - GPI = **Policy Evaluation** $\leftrightarrow$ **Policy Improvement**, interleaved arbitrarily. - **Bellman Expectation Operator** $T^\pi$: no $\max$, weighted by $\pi(a \mid s)$. - **Bellman Optimality Operator** $T_*$: contains $\max_a$, defines $V_*$. - **Policy Improvement Theorem**: $V_{\pi'}(s) \geq V_\pi(s)$ for all $s$, strict if $\pi' \neq \pi$. - **Contraction factor**: $\gamma \in [0, 1)$; the smaller $\gamma$, the faster convergence. - **Policy Iteration** = GPI with infinite evaluation sweeps; **Value Iteration** = GPI with one evaluation sweep. - **Stopping criterion**: Bellman residual $\delta = \max_s \vert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \vert < \epsilon$. - **Uniqueness** of $V_*$ and $\pi_*$ follows from Banach Fixed-Point Theorem. - GPI does **not** require synchronous sweeps — asynchronous GPI sweeps individual states in place. - GPI is the **conceptual parent** of TD(0), TD($\lambda$), Q-Learning, SARSA, and Actor-Critic methods. - For **continuous** state spaces, GPI is combined with function approximation (deep RL setting). - KTU frequently asks: *“Show $T_*$ is a contraction”* — memorize the max-norm proof step-by-step. - KTU frequently asks: *“Compare Policy Iteration vs Value Iteration”* — anchor your answer in the $n_{eval}$ parameter.

topics.title: Monte Carlo Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Monte Carlo Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Monte Carlo (MC) Control** is a class of model-free reinforcement learning algorithms that learn optimal policies and value functions by **averaging sample returns obtained from complete episodes of interaction** between an agent and its environment. Unlike Dynamic Programming (DP), MC Control does **not require prior knowledge of the environment's transition dynamics** ($P$ or $R$); it learns purely from *sampled experience*.

Mathematically, MC Control estimates the action-value function $Q_{\pi}(s,a)$ for a behavior policy $\pi$ and then performs **policy improvement** by acting greedily with respect to $Q_{\pi}(s,a)$ (with some exploration mechanism).

The two principal variants in KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **On-Policy Monte Carlo Control** — improves the policy that is used to make decisions (uses $\epsilon$-soft policies).
2. **Off-Policy Monte Carlo Control** — improves a target policy $\pi$ using experience generated by a different behavior policy $b$ (uses **importance sampling**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):** The core topics under Monte Carlo Control include MC with **Exploring Starts**, the **GLIE** (Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration) property, **on-policy $\epsilon$-soft MC control**, **off-policy prediction via importance sampling**, and **off-policy MC control**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **chess player who learns by playing thousands of complete games** (not by memorizing a rulebook of all possible moves). After every game, the player reviews which moves actually led to wins and which led to losses. Over time, the player forms an intuition (a "value estimate") for each board position-action pair.

- Each **completed game** = one **episode**.
- The **final outcome** (win/loss) = the **return $G_t$**.
- The player **averages the outcomes** of every visit to a particular position-move = the **Monte Carlo estimate** of $Q(s,a)$.
- The player gradually shifts toward better moves = **policy improvement**.

This is exactly how MC Control operates — it learns *from experience of finished episodes* without needing a model of the opponent (environment).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** Dynamic Programming uses the Bellman equation as a contraction operator over a *known* model. Monte Carlo Control uses the *law of large numbers* — the sample mean of returns converges to the expected return as the number of episodes $\to \infty$.

## 1.3 Key Symbols and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\pi$ | Target policy (being improved) |
| $b$ | Behavior policy (generates experience) |
| $Q(s,a)$ | Action-value function |
| $G_t$ | Return from time $t$ onward |
| $\epsilon$ | Exploration parameter |
| $\rho_{t:T-1}$ | Importance sampling ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard metric in MC analysis: **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** of return estimates decays as $O(1/N)$ where $N$ is the number of episodes — a direct consequence of the Central Limit Theorem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generalised Policy Iteration (GPI) Framework in MC Control

MC Control operates within the **Generalised Policy Iteration (GPI)** framework, which alternates between:

- **Policy Evaluation** — Estimate $Q \approx Q_{\pi}$ by averaging returns from sampled episodes.
- **Policy Improvement** — Update $\pi$ to be greedy (or $\epsilon$-greedy) with respect to $Q$.

The process converges to the **optimal action-value function $Q^*$** and the **optimal policy $\pi^*$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For MC Control to converge, we must guarantee **infinite exploration** of all state-action pairs. Without this, the policy may prematurely lock onto a suboptimal action.

## 2.2 Monte Carlo with Exploring Starts (MCES)

**Exploring Starts** is a theoretical assumption where every state-action pair $(s,a)$ has a non-zero probability of being selected as the initial point of an episode.

- **Policy Evaluation:** Use *first-visit* or *every-visit* MC to estimate $Q(s,a)$.
- **Policy Improvement:** Make $\pi$ **deterministically greedy** with respect to $Q$:

$$
\pi(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q(s,a)
$$

- This guarantees strict policy improvement by the **Policy Improvement Theorem**.

> [!WARNING]
> Exploring Starts is **unrealistic in practical environments** because we cannot freely reset the agent to an arbitrary state-action pair. KTU examiners may deduct marks if students present ES as a deployable algorithm.

## 2.3 On-Policy $\epsilon$-Soft MC Control

To remove the need for exploring starts, the policy is constrained to be **$\epsilon$-soft**: every action has probability $\geq \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}$ of being chosen from any state.

**Policy Improvement (for $\epsilon$-soft policies):** Any $\epsilon$-greedy policy with respect to $Q_{\pi}$ guarantees improvement:

$$
\pi'(a \vert s) =
\begin{cases}
1 - \epsilon + \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}, & a = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s,a') \\
\frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**GLIE Conditions for Convergence:**

1. **All state-action pairs visited infinitely often:** $\lim_{k \to \infty} N_k(s,a) = \infty$.
2. **Policy converges to greedy:** $\lim_{k \to \infty} \pi_k(a \vert s) = \mathbb{1}\left[a = \arg\max_{a'} Q_k(s,a')\right]$.

A common GLIE schedule: $\epsilon_k = \frac{1}{k}$, decaying as episodes accumulate.

## 2.4 Off-Policy Prediction via Importance Sampling

When the **target policy $\pi$** differs from the **behavior policy $b$**, returns generated under $b$ must be re-weighted to estimate $Q_{\pi}$.

The **importance sampling ratio** for trajectory from $t$ to $T-1$:

$$
\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \vert S_k)}{b(A_k \vert S_k)}
$$

**Two Variants:**

1. **Ordinary Importance Sampling:**

$$
V(s) = \frac{\sum_{t \in \mathcal{T}(s)} \rho_{t:T(t)-1} \, G_t}{\vert \mathcal{T}(s) \vert}
$$

2. **Weighted Importance Sampling:**

$$
V(s) = \frac{\sum_{t \in \mathcal{T}(s)} \rho_{t:T(t)-1} \, G_t}{\sum_{t \in \mathcal{T}(s)} \rho_{t:T(t)-1}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Weighted IS is preferred in practice** — it has bounded variance even when the ratio is unbounded. Ordinary IS is unbiased but suffers from very high variance.

## 2.5 Off-Policy MC Control Algorithm

The target policy $\pi$ is **deterministic and greedy**; the behavior policy $b$ is **$\epsilon$-greedy** (or otherwise exploratory). Updates use **weighted importance sampling**:

$$
Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \frac{W}{C(S_t, A_t)} \left[ G_t - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]
$$

where:

- $W = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{1}{b(A_k \vert S_k)}$ (since $\pi$ is greedy, the ratio collapses to $1/b$ for greedy actions and $0$ otherwise).
- $C(S_t, A_t)$ is the cumulative sum of weights (for weighted-average form).
- $W$ is reset to $0$ when $W = 0$ (target policy wouldn't take the behavior's action).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Usage |
|---|---|---|
| $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Discounted return | MC prediction target |
| $Q(s,a) = \frac{1}{N(s,a)} \sum_i G_i(s,a)$ | Sample-mean action-value | On-policy evaluation |
| $\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \vert S_k)}{b(A_k \vert S_k)}$ | Importance sampling ratio | Off-policy reweighting |
| $V_{\text{ordinary}}(s) = \frac{\sum \rho_i G_i}{\vert \mathcal{T}(s) \vert}$ | Ordinary IS estimate | Unbiased, high variance |
| $V_{\text{weighted}}(s) = \frac{\sum \rho_i G_i}{\sum \rho_i}$ | Weighted IS estimate | Biased (asymptotically unbiased), low variance |
| $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ | Greedy policy improvement | DP/MC-ES control |
| $\pi'(a\vert s) = (1-\epsilon) + \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$ if greedy | $\epsilon$-greedy improvement | On-policy soft control |
| $\epsilon_k = 1/k$ | GLIE exploration decay | Convergence guarantee |
| $W = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{1}{b(A_k \vert S_k)}$ | Weight for off-policy update | Greedy target policy |
| $Q \leftarrow Q + \alpha [G - Q]$ | Incremental update form | Online MC estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** MC Control is foundational in robotics (sampled trajectory rollouts), game AI (AlphaGo's rollout policy), financial trading (Monte Carlo option pricing), and clinical decision systems. Importance sampling is the **mathematical engine** of safe counterfactual reasoning in off-policy evaluation (e.g., A/B test analysis without re-running experiments).

## 2.7 Why the $\epsilon$-Soft Modification Works

The standard **Policy Improvement Theorem** only applies to deterministic greedy updates. For stochastic $\epsilon$-soft policies, the corresponding theorem states:

> If $\pi'$ is an $\epsilon$-greedy policy with respect to $Q_{\pi}$, then $Q_{\pi'}(s,a) \geq Q_{\pi}(s,a)$ for all $s,a$.

This guarantees monotonic improvement under soft policies — a key insight in MC Control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Importance Sampling Ratio

### 3.1.1 Setup

We want to estimate:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \vert S_t = s] = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \, \mathbb{E}[G_t \vert S_t=s, A_t=a]
$$

But our trajectories are sampled from $b \neq \pi$.

### 3.1.2 Trajectory Probability under a Policy

For a trajectory $\tau = (S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, \dots, S_{T-1}, A_{T-1}, R_T)$:

$$
\Pr(\tau \vert \pi) = \prod_{k=0}^{T-1} \pi(A_k \vert S_k) \cdot p(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \vert S_k, A_k)
$$

### 3.1.3 Ratio Construction

$$
\frac{\Pr(\tau \vert \pi)}{\Pr(\tau \vert b)} = \frac{\prod_{k=0}^{T-1} \pi(A_k \vert S_k) \, p(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \vert S_k, A_k)}{\prod_{k=0}^{T-1} b(A_k \vert S_k) \, p(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \vert S_k, A_k)}
$$

The dynamics $p(\cdot \vert \cdot)$ **cancel** because they are the same under both policies:

$$
\frac{\Pr(\tau \vert \pi)}{\Pr(\tau \vert b)} = \prod_{k=0}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \vert S_k)}{b(A_k \vert S_k)}
$$

This is the full-trajectory importance sampling ratio. Truncating to start at time $t$:

$$
\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \vert S_k)}{b(A_k \vert S_k)}
$$

### 3.1.4 Unbiasedness of Ordinary Importance Sampling

$$
\mathbb{E}_b[\rho_{0:T-1} G_0] = \sum_{\tau} \Pr(\tau \vert b) \cdot \frac{\Pr(\tau \vert \pi)}{\Pr(\tau \vert b)} \cdot G_0(\tau)
$$

Reordering the summation:

$$
\mathbb{E}_b[\rho_{0:T-1} G_0] = \sum_{\tau} \Pr(\tau \vert \pi) \cdot G_0(\tau) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_0]
$$

> **Logic:** The ratio $\rho$ converts the expectation under $b$ into one under $\pi$, because it equals the Radon–Nikodym derivative between the two trajectory distributions. The environment dynamics cancel, leaving only the policy probabilities.

## 3.2 Derivation: $\epsilon$-Greedy Policy Improvement

Starting from a state $s$ with action space $\mathcal{A}(s)$:

$$
Q_{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) = \sum_a \pi'(a \vert s) Q_{\pi}(s,a)
$$

Substituting the $\epsilon$-greedy distribution (with $a^* = \arg\max_a Q_{\pi}(s,a)$):

$$
Q_{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) = \left(1 - \epsilon + \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}\right) Q_{\pi}(s, a^*) + \sum_{a \neq a^*} \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert} Q_{\pi}(s,a)
$$

Splitting the greedy contribution from the rest:

$$
Q_{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) = (1-\epsilon) Q_{\pi}(s,a^*) + \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert} \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} Q_{\pi}(s,a)
$$

But the **max** satisfies:

$$
Q_{\pi}(s,a^*) = \max_{a} Q_{\pi}(s,a) \geq \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert} \sum_{a} Q_{\pi}(s,a)
$$

Therefore:

$$
Q_{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) \geq (1-\epsilon) \cdot \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert} \sum_{a} Q_{\pi}(s,a) + \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert} \sum_{a} Q_{\pi}(s,a)
$$

$$
Q_{\pi}(s, \pi'(s)) \geq \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert} \sum_{a} Q_{\pi}(s,a) = V_{\pi}(s)
$$

Thus $\pi'$ improves on $\pi$ at every state.

> **Logic:** The $\epsilon$ mass that goes to non-greedy actions is, in expectation, worth at most the greedy action value, because the greedy action is by definition the maximum. Hence the $\epsilon$-greedy policy can never be worse than the old policy.

## 3.3 Derivation: Incremental Off-Policy MC Update

The weighted-average update for $Q$ can be written incrementally. Define:

$$
C_{n+1} = C_n + W_{n+1}
$$

$$
Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \frac{W_{n+1}}{C_{n+1}}\left[ G_{n+1} - Q_n \right]
$$

Expanding $Q_{n+1}$:

$$
Q_{n+1} = \frac{C_n \cdot Q_n + W_{n+1} G_{n+1}}{C_n + W_{n+1}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i G_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} W_i}
$$

Hence the incremental form is **mathematically identical** to the weighted-average ratio form — it requires $O(1)$ memory per state-action pair instead of storing all returns.

> **Logic:** The denominator $C(s,a)$ acts as a running sum of importance weights, providing a natural adaptive step size $\alpha = W/C$. This avoids manual tuning of learning rates.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Ordinary vs Weighted IS

| Property | Ordinary IS | Weighted IS |
|---|---|---|
| Bias | Unbiased ($0$) | Biased (but $\to 0$ as $N \to \infty$) |
| Variance | Unbounded | Bounded (theoretically finite) |
| Convergence | Almost surely (by SLLN) | Almost surely |
| Practical preference | Rarely used | **Standard in production** |
| Effective sample size | None | Inherent via weights |
| Sensitivity to outlier $\rho$ | Extreme | Smoothed by denominator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 frequently tests the **bias-variance tradeoff** in importance sampling. Memorize the comparison: *Ordinary IS = unbiased but explosive variance; Weighted IS = small bias but tractable variance.*

## 3.5 Derivation: First-Visit vs Every-Visit Equivalence (Asymptotic)

For first-visit MC, let $N_{s}^{(1)}$ = number of first visits to $s$ across episodes. The estimator:

$$
V^{(1)}(s) = \frac{1}{N_{s}^{(1)}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{s}^{(1)}} G_i(s)
$$

By the **Law of Large Numbers**, since $G_i(s) \sim$ i.i.d. with mean $V_{\pi}(s)$:

$$
\lim_{N_{s}^{(1)} \to \infty} V^{(1)}(s) \xrightarrow{a.s.} V_{\pi}(s)
$$

A similar result holds for every-visit MC, but the samples within a single episode are **not independent** (correlated through shared state dynamics). Nevertheless, by the Ergodic Theorem for Markov chains, the every-visit estimator also converges:

$$
\lim_{N_{\text{ep}} \to \infty} V^{(\text{every})}(s) \xrightarrow{a.s.} V_{\pi}(s)
$$

> **Logic:** Both estimators are consistent, but every-visit has lower MSE for small sample sizes due to more data points; first-visit has a cleaner theoretical analysis.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Monte Carlo Control — GPI Loop (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Initialize Policy pi and Q s a arbitrarily"] --> B["Generate Complete Episode using pi or b"]
    B --> C["Observe Trajectory S0 A0 R1 S1 ... ST"]
    C --> D["Compute Return Gt for each timestep t"]
    D --> E["Accumulate Returns into Q S A using first visit or every visit rule"]
    E --> F["Apply Importance Sampling Weights W if off policy"]
    F --> G["Update Q S A via incremental rule Q Q plus W over C times G minus Q"]
    G --> H["Policy Improvement Step"]
    H --> I["On policy: set pi to epsilon greedy w.r.t. Q"]
    H --> J["Off policy: set pi greedy w.r.t. Q"]
    I --> K{"Convergence Check: Q stable or GLIE satisfied?"}
    J --> K
    K -->|No| B
    K -->|Yes| L["Output Optimal Q star and pi star"]
```

## 4.2 On-Policy vs Off-Policy MC Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "On Policy MC Control"
        A1["Behavior and Target are SAME: pi"]
        A2["Episode generated by pi"]
        A3["Q updated by sample average of Gt"]
        A4["pi becomes epsilon greedy"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A1
    end
    subgraph "Off Policy MC Control"
        B1["Behavior b is exploratory"]
        B2["Target pi is greedy"]
        B3["Episode generated by b"]
        B4["Compute rho = pi A S over b A S"]
        B5["Weighted update of Q using rho and Gt"]
        B6["pi becomes greedy w.r.t. Q"]
        B1 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6
        B2 --> B5
    end
```

## 4.3 Importance Sampling Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Trajectory tau sampled under behavior policy b"] --> B["At time t record action At and state St"]
    B --> C["Compute ratio rho = pi At over St divided by b At over St"]
    C --> D["Multiply return Gt by rho to get weighted return"]
    D --> E["Aggregate weighted returns per state action pair"]
    E --> F["Ordinary: divide by N visits"]
    E --> G["Weighted: divide by sum of weights"]
    F --> H["Final V s estimate"]
    G --> H
```

## 4.4 GLIE Convergence Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Episode k begins"] --> B["Exploration parameter epsilon k = 1 over k"]
    B --> C["Sample action via epsilon greedy policy"]
    C --> D["Update Q using episode return"]
    D --> E{"k greater than threshold?"}
    E -->|No| A
    E -->|Yes| F["As k tends to infinity: epsilon tends to 0"]
    F --> G["Policy pi becomes greedy and optimal"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Monte Carlo Control. Why is "exploring starts" generally considered unrealistic in practical RL problems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Monte Carlo Control is a model-free reinforcement learning approach that learns the optimal policy by **averaging sample returns from complete episodes** of agent–environment interaction, alternating between policy evaluation and policy improvement (GPI framework).

Exploring Starts assumes that every state-action pair has non-zero probability of being the initial point of an episode. This is **unrealistic** because:
- Real environments cannot be reset to arbitrary initial states on demand.
- Many applications (autonomous driving, medical robotics) prohibit arbitrary state resets.
- It is impractical for continuous or high-dimensional state spaces.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the realism limitations.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between on-policy and off-policy Monte Carlo control methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | On-Policy MC | Off-Policy MC |
|---|---|---|
| Behavior policy | Same as target $\pi$ | Different $b \neq \pi$ |
| Exploration | $\epsilon$-soft policy required | Behavior $b$ explores, $\pi$ is greedy |
| Sample efficiency | Lower (must explore via $\pi$) | Higher (reuses exploratory data) |
| Key mechanism | $\epsilon$-greedy improvement | Importance sampling ratio $\rho$ |
| Convergence | GLIE condition | Requires coverage: $\pi(a \vert s) > 0 \Rightarrow b(a \vert s) > 0$ |

*(Award 1.5 marks for each — clarity of comparison is the differentiator.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 3 Choice]`

#### (a) Derive the importance sampling ratio used in off-policy Monte Carlo prediction. Explain why the environment dynamics cancel out in the ratio. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define the trajectory probability under a policy** (2 marks)

For a trajectory $\tau = (S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, \dots, S_{T-1}, A_{T-1}, R_T)$, the probability under policy $\pi$ is:

$$
\Pr(\tau \mid \pi) = \prod_{k=0}^{T-1} \pi(A_k \mid S_k) \cdot p(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)
$$

where $p$ denotes the (unknown) environment transition probability.

**Step 2: Form the ratio of trajectory probabilities** (2 marks)

$$
\frac{\Pr(\tau \mid \pi)}{\Pr(\tau \mid b)} = \frac{\prod_{k=0}^{T-1} \pi(A_k \mid S_k) \, p(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)}{\prod_{k=0}^{T-1} b(A_k \mid S_k) \, p(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)}
$$

**Step 3: Cancel the dynamics terms** (2 marks)

Since the same environment generates trajectories under both policies, the factor $p(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)$ is **identical** in numerator and denominator. Hence it cancels:

$$
\frac{\Pr(\tau \mid \pi)}{\Pr(\tau \mid b)} = \prod_{k=0}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)}
$$

**Step 4: Truncate to time $t$** (1 mark)

$$
\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)}
$$

**Reason why dynamics cancel:** The trajectory distribution factorizes into a *policy part* and a *dynamics part*. The dynamics depend only on the environment, not on which policy is followed. Since the environment is fixed, both $\pi$ and $b$ see the **same** transition kernel $p$, so $p$ cancels exactly in the ratio.

---

#### (b) Compare ordinary and weighted importance sampling estimators in terms of bias, variance, and practical applicability. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Write both estimators** (1.5 marks)

$$
V_{\text{ord}}(s) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \rho_i G_i}{N}, \qquad
V_{\text{wt}}(s) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \rho_i G_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \rho_i}
$$

**Step 2: Bias analysis** (1.5 marks)

- **Ordinary IS:** $\mathbb{E}[V_{\text{ord}}(s)] = V_{\pi}(s)$ — exactly unbiased for any $N$.
- **Weighted IS:** $\mathbb{E}[V_{\text{wt}}(s)] = V_{\pi}(s) + O(1/N)$ — biased for finite $N$, but bias vanishes asymptotically.

**Step 3: Variance analysis** (2 marks)

- **Ordinary IS:** Variance is $\text{Var}(\rho G)/N$, which is **unbounded** because $\rho$ can take arbitrarily large values when $b$ assigns small probability to an action $\pi$ favors.
- **Weighted IS:** Variance is **bounded** in the off-policy case when $\pi$ is deterministic — the weight $W$ itself becomes the only stochastic factor, and the normalized form caps the influence of any single sample.

**Step 4: Practical applicability** (2 marks)

- **Ordinary IS** is rarely used in practice due to its **explosive variance** when $b$ and $\pi$ differ significantly. A single trajectory where $b$ samples a low-probability action under $\pi$ can dominate the average.
- **Weighted IS** is the **de facto standard** in production systems (e.g., counterfactual estimation, recommender systems, A/B test analysis) because it is **stable, robust to outlier ratios**, and the bias is negligible for large $N$.

| Criterion | Ordinary IS | Weighted IS |
|---|---|---|
| Bias | Unbiased ($0$) | Biased ($O(1/N)$) |
| Variance | Unbounded | Bounded |
| Practical use | Theoretical / academic | **Production standard** |
| Sensitivity to $\rho$ | Severe | Mild |

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 3 Choice]`

#### (a) Explain the on-policy $\epsilon$-soft Monte Carlo control algorithm. State and justify the GLIE conditions. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Why $\epsilon$-soft?** (1 mark)

Standard MC with exploring starts cannot be deployed. We replace the deterministic greedy improvement with an **$\epsilon$-soft** policy where every action has probability $\geq \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}$ — guaranteeing continual exploration.

**Step 2: Policy improvement step** (1.5 marks)

$$
\pi'(a \mid s) =
\begin{cases}
1 - \epsilon + \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}, & a = a^* = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s,a') \\
\frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}, & a \neq a^*
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3: Policy evaluation step** (1.5 marks)

For each episode, use sample averaging:

$$
Q(s,a) \leftarrow \frac{1}{N(s,a)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(s,a)} G_i(s,a)
$$

**Step 4: State GLIE conditions** (1.5 marks)

A sequence of policies $\{\pi_k\}$ is **GLIE** (Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration) iff:

1. **Infinite exploration:** every state-action pair is visited infinitely often as $k \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{k \to \infty} N_k(s,a) = \infty \quad \forall s,a
$$

2. **Greedy in the limit:** the policy converges to a greedy one:

$$
\lim_{k \to \infty} \pi_k(a \mid s) = \mathbb{1}[a = \arg\max_{a'} Q_k(s,a')]
$$

**Step 5: Justify convergence** (1.5 marks)

A simple GLIE schedule is $\epsilon_k = 1/k$. Then $\sum_k \epsilon_k = \infty$ (ensures infinite exploration) and $\epsilon_k \to 0$ (ensures greedy convergence). Under GLIE, on-policy MC control is **guaranteed to converge to the optimal action-value function $Q^*$ and optimal policy $\pi^*$** by the policy improvement theorem for $\epsilon$-soft policies.

---

#### (b) With a clear algorithmic flow, describe off-policy Monte Carlo control using weighted importance sampling with a greedy target policy. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Setup** (1 mark)

- Behavior policy $b$: $\epsilon$-soft (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy), generates experience.
- Target policy $\pi$: deterministic greedy with respect to current $Q$.

**Step 2: Episode generation** (1 mark)

Generate a complete episode $S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, \dots, S_{T-1}, A_{T-1}, R_T$ using $b$.

**Step 3: Backward pass and weight computation** (1.5 marks)

Initialize $W \leftarrow 1$, then for $t = T-1, T-2, \dots, 0$:

- $W \leftarrow W \cdot \frac{1}{b(A_t \mid S_t)}$
- (Note: since $\pi$ is greedy, $\pi(A_t \mid S_t) = 1$ if $A_t = \arg\max_a Q(S_t, a)$, else $0$. The weight is therefore $1/b$ when $A_t$ matches the greedy action, and the loop **terminates** at the first non-greedy step.)

**Step 4: Update $Q$ using weighted IS** (1.5 marks)

$$
C(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow C(S_t, A_t) + W
$$

$$
Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \frac{W}{C(S_t, A_t)} \left[ G_t - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]
$$

**Step 5: Policy improvement** (1 mark)

$$
\pi(S_t) \leftarrow \arg\max_a Q(S_t, a)
$$

**Step 6: Repeat across episodes** (1 mark)

For each new episode, regenerate trajectories and reweight using $b$. The **coverage assumption** requires $b(a \vert s) > 0$ whenever $\pi(a \vert s) > 0$.

**Convergence guarantee:** The off-policy MC control algorithm converges to $Q^*$ provided the coverage assumption holds and the number of episodes $\to \infty$.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Monte Carlo Control Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing policy types (3 marks lost).** Students often write $\pi$ and $b$ interchangeably. Always explicitly identify which is the *behavior* and which is the *target* policy.
> 2. **Forgetting to cancel dynamics in importance sampling (2 marks lost).** The cancellation of $p(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)$ is the **key insight** — failing to state it explicitly loses partial credit.
> 3. **Writing "ordinary IS is always better because it is unbiased" (full marks lost).** Always discuss the bias-variance tradeoff; unbounded variance is fatal in practice.
> 4. **Skipping the $\epsilon$ term in $\epsilon$-greedy improvement.** The full expression is $1 - \epsilon + \epsilon / \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$, **not** just $1 - \epsilon$.
> 5. **Omitting the coverage assumption** $b(a \vert s) > 0$ whenever $\pi(a \vert s) > 0$ in off-policy MC.
> 6. **Confusing first-visit vs every-visit MC** in tabular updates — be explicit which is being used.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MC Control** learns optimal policies from complete episodes without a model of the environment.
- It operates within the **GPI** framework: alternate between **policy evaluation** (sample averaging of returns) and **policy improvement** (greedy or $\epsilon$-greedy).
- **Exploring Starts** is a theoretical assumption; **$\epsilon$-soft policies** make the algorithm practical.
- **GLIE** (Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration) guarantees convergence of on-policy MC control.
- **Off-policy MC** uses **importance sampling** to correct for the distribution mismatch between behavior $b$ and target $\pi$.
- The **importance sampling ratio** is:

$$
\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)}
$$

- The **environment dynamics cancel** in the ratio because $p$ is fixed regardless of the policy.
- **Ordinary IS** is unbiased with unbounded variance; **Weighted IS** has small bias with bounded variance — Weighted IS is preferred in practice.
- For a **greedy target policy**, the importance weight simplifies to $W = \prod 1/b(A_k \mid S_k)$, terminating at the first non-greedy step.
- The **incremental off-policy update** uses $\alpha = W/C(S,A)$ as an adaptive step size.
- The **coverage assumption** $b(a \vert s) > 0 \Rightarrow \pi(a \vert s) > 0$ is essential for off-policy convergence.
- **KTU 2024 favorite questions:** importance sampling ratio derivation, ordinary vs weighted IS comparison, GLIE conditions, $\epsilon$-greedy improvement formula, off-policy MC control algorithmic flow.
- Always state the **bias-variance tradeoff** explicitly when discussing importance sampling.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SARSA: On-Policy TD Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SARSA: On-Policy TD Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**SARSA** stands for **State–Action–Reward–State–Action**, named directly after the five-tuple transition tuple  
$$(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$$  
that drives every parameter update. It is an **on-policy Temporal-Difference (TD) control algorithm** that learns the *action-value function* $Q_\pi(s,a)$ — also denoted $Q^\pi(s,a)$ — for a behavior policy $\pi$ that is simultaneously used for both **action selection** and **value estimation**.

Formally, SARSA estimates:

$$
Q(s,a) \;\approx\; Q^{\pi}(s,a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,G_t \,\middle|\, S_t=s,\; A_t=a\,\right]
$$

where the return $G_t$ is:

$$
G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \dots \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}
$$

The SARSA update rule at each step is:

$$
Q(S_t, A_t) \;\leftarrow\; Q(S_t, A_t) \;+\; \alpha \!\left[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma\, Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \;-\; Q(S_t, A_t) \,\right]
$$

The bracketed term is the **TD error**, denoted $\delta_t$:

$$
\delta_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma\, Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \;-\; Q(S_t, A_t)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** SARSA is classified as **on-policy** because the *same* policy $\pi$ that generates the action $A_{t+1}$ (used inside the bootstrap target) is also the policy being *improved*. This is its defining distinction from Q-Learning (off-policy).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Inspector"

Imagine a food critic who is **also required to eat at every restaurant they review** (this is the on-policy constraint).

1. **State $S_t$**: The restaurant currently sitting in.
2. **Action $A_t$**: The dish ordered.
3. **Reward $R_{t+1}$**: The taste score of the dish.
4. **Next State $S_{t+1}$**: The next restaurant walked into.
5. **Next Action $A_{t+1}$**: The dish the critic *plans* to order next (evaluated under the *same* personal taste policy).

The critic updates their estimate of *"how good is Dish X at Restaurant Y?"* by combining:
- the **actual reward** they just received (real experience), **and**
- a **prediction of the next dish's quality** (using their own current preference, $\pi$).

Because the prediction uses their *own* next choice (not the best possible choice), the critic's rating reflects what they will *actually* experience — that is **on-policy learning**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Contrast with Q-Learning:** A Q-Learning critic would instead optimistically assume the *best possible* next dish regardless of personal preference. That is **off-policy** learning.

---

## 1.3 On-Policy vs Off-Policy — The Core Distinction

| Property | SARSA (On-Policy) | Q-Learning (Off-Policy) |
|---|---|---|
| Behavior policy $\pi_b$ | Same as target policy $\pi$ | Different (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy vs greedy) |
| Bootstrap target action | $A_{t+1} \sim \pi(\cdot \vert S_{t+1})$ | $\arg\max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')$ |
| Convergence target | $Q^\pi$ (current policy) | $Q^*$ (optimal) |
| Behavior under risk | Safer / risk-averse | More aggressive / "Cliff-walking" issue |
| Update target | $R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ | $R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Trivia:** In Sutton & Barto's classical *Cliff Walking* gridworld, SARSA learns the longer-but-safer upper path, whereas Q-Learning learns the shorter-but-cliff-side path — because SARSA accounts for the danger of exploratory $\epsilon$-greedy falls.

---

## 1.4 ε-Greedy Exploration Policy

Since SARSA must *both explore and exploit*, the standard behavior policy is **ε-greedy**:

$$
\pi(a \mid s) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
1 - \epsilon \;+\; \dfrac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}, & a = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s, a') \quad (\text{exploit}) \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}, & a \neq \arg\max_{a'} Q(s, a') \quad (\text{explore})
\end{cases}
$$

where $\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert$ denotes the cardinality of the action set at state $s$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Learn Q Instead of V?

In Module 2 we studied TD(0) for **state values** $V(s)$. But to do *control* (selecting actions), we need to know the value of *state-action pairs*. Two reasons:

1. **Model-free action selection:** Without a model of $P(s' \vert s,a)$, $V(s)$ alone cannot tell us which action is best.
2. **Policy evaluation under stochasticity:** $Q^\pi(s,a)$ directly gives the expected return of taking $a$ in $s$ and then following $\pi$ — exactly the quantity needed to compare actions.

Hence we replace the state-value update with an **action-value** update:

$$
\text{TD(0) on } V: \quad V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha\!\left[\,R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)\,\right]
$$

$$
\text{SARSA on } Q: \quad Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha\!\left[\,R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t)\,\right]
$$

## 2.2 The SARSA Algorithm — Logical Flow

The control cycle consists of three nested loops:

- **Outer Loop (Episodes):** Initialize $S_0$, sample $A_0 \sim \pi(\cdot \vert S_0)$.
- **Inner Loop (Time steps):** For each $t = 0, 1, 2, \dots$:
  1. **Execute** $A_t$, observe $R_{t+1}$ and $S_{t+1}$.
  2. **Choose** $A_{t+1} \sim \pi(\cdot \vert S_{t+1})$ — using the *current* $Q$ table.
  3. **Update** $Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \delta_t$, where $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t)$.
  4. **Set** $S_t \leftarrow S_{t+1}$, $A_t \leftarrow A_{t+1}$.
- **End loop** when $S_t$ is terminal.

> [!NOTE]
> The crucial subtlety (and a frequent KTU board-mark trap): the action $A_{t+1}$ is **selected before the update**, and the *same* $A_{t+1}$ is **re-used as $A_t$ in the next iteration**. Hence the five-tuple $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ is fully populated *before* any $Q$-value is changed.

---

## 2.3 Why Does SARSA Converge? — The GLIE Conditions

SARSA converges to the optimal action-value function $Q^*(s,a)$ if two conditions hold simultaneously — the **GLIE** conditions (*Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration*):

1. **Infinite Exploration:** Every state-action pair is visited infinitely often:
$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} N_t(s,a) \;=\; \infty \quad \forall\, (s,a)
$$
2. **Greedy in the Limit:** The policy eventually becomes greedy:
$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} \pi_t(a \mid s) \;=\; \mathbf{1}\!\left[\,a = \arg\max_{a'} Q_t(s,a')\,\right] \quad \forall\, s
$$
3. **Robbins-Monro step-size schedule:** The learning rate $\alpha_t$ satisfies:
$$
\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t(s,a) \;=\; \infty \qquad \text{and} \qquad \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^{2}(s,a) \;<\; \infty
$$

Under GLIE + Robbins-Monro, **SARSA converges with probability 1** to $Q^*$ (Singh, Jaakkola, Littman & Szepesvári, 1994; Dayan, 1994).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SARSA TD Error | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t)$ | Five-tuple dependent |
| 2 | SARSA Update | $Q(S_t,A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t,A_t) + \alpha \delta_t$ | On-policy update |
| 3 | Discount Factor | $\gamma \in [0,1)$ | KTU default $\gamma = 0.9$ or $0.99$ |
| 4 | Learning Rate | $\alpha \in (0,1]$ | Robbins-Monro schedule for convergence |
| 5 | ε-greedy Action Prob | $\pi(a \vert s) = \tfrac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} + (1-\epsilon) \mathbf{1}[a = a^*]$ | Exploration-exploitation mix |
| 6 | ε decay (e.g., GLIE) | $\epsilon_t = \tfrac{c}{c + t}$ for $c>0$ | Decays to 0, satisfies GLIE |
| 7 | Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | Used in MC, not directly in TD |
| 8 | Bellman Optimality (target) | $Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(S_{t+1},a') \vert S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | Limit point of SARSA |
| 9 | Bellman Expectation (TD target) | $Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[R_{t+1} + \gamma Q^\pi(S_{t+1},A_{t+1}) \vert S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | SARSA's immediate target |
| 10 | Backup Diagram Depth | SARSA samples a **1-step** transition | Compare with n-step / TD($\lambda$) |
| 11 | Bias (bootstrapped) | Yes — uses $Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ as estimate | Lower variance than MC |
| 12 | Variance | Lower than Monte Carlo | Because of 1-step bootstrap |
| 13 | Convergence requirement | GLIE + Robbins-Monro $\alpha_t$ schedule | Probability-1 convergence |
| 14 | Initial Condition | $Q(s,a) = 0$ for all $(s,a)$, or arbitrary | Terminal $Q = 0$ standard |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics path planning** under safety constraints (risk-aware).
- **Autonomous vehicle lane-changing** where exploratory mistakes are costly.
- **Industrial process control** where the current control policy must be respected during learning.
- **Recommendation systems** under business-logic constraints on which actions can be served.
- **Network packet routing** with stability guarantees (vs. aggressive Q-Learning).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the SARSA Update from the Bellman Equation

We begin from the **Bellman Expectation Equation** for action-values under a fixed policy $\pi$:

$$
Q^{\pi}(s,a) \;=\; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\, r \;+\; \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, Q^{\pi}(s', a') \,\right]\,\right]
$$

Since $\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[Q^{\pi}(s', a')] = \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q^{\pi}(s', a')$, we can write:

$$
Q^{\pi}(s,a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\, R_{t+1} \;+\; \gamma\, Q^{\pi}(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \;\middle|\; S_t = s,\; A_t = a \,\right]
$$

This is the **expectation** the sample $(R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ is approximating. Define the **TD target** $Y_t$:

$$
Y_t \;\triangleq\; R_{t+1} \;+\; \gamma\, Q^{\pi}(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})
$$

We desire a stochastic approximation that converges to $Q^\pi$. The **Widrow-Hoff / LMS** rule replaces the unknown true $Q^\pi$ with the current estimate $Q_t$ inside the target:

$$
Y_t \;\approx\; R_{t+1} \;+\; \gamma\, Q_t(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})
$$

The stochastic update rule becomes:

$$
Q_{t+1}(S_t, A_t) \;\leftarrow\; Q_t(S_t, A_t) \;+\; \alpha_t \!\left[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma\, Q_t(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \;-\; Q_t(S_t, A_t) \,\right]
$$

Substituting $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q_t(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q_t(S_t, A_t)$:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{t+1}(S_t, A_t) \;\leftarrow\; Q_t(S_t, A_t) \;+\; \alpha_t\, \delta_t\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Convergence Conditions

### 3.2.1 Why $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$?

If the steps $\alpha_t$ decay *too fast*, the algorithm may stop updating before reaching the optimum. By the **Cauchy condensation-like** argument, an infinite total step length is necessary for the noise process $w_t$ to be averaged out:

$$
\mathbb{E}[Q_{t+1} - Q^\pi] \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,(1 - \alpha_t)(Q_t - Q^\pi)\,\right]
$$

Iterating,

$$
\mathbb{E}[Q_{t+1} - Q^\pi] \;=\; Q_0 - Q^\pi \prod_{k=0}^{t}(1-\alpha_k)
$$

For the product to vanish as $t \to \infty$, we need:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \alpha_k \;=\; \infty \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \prod_{k=0}^{\infty}(1 - \alpha_k) \;=\; 0
$$

### 3.2.2 Why $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$?

If steps are too large, accumulated noise diverges. The variance of $Q_t(s,a)$ is bounded by:

$$
\mathrm{Var}[Q_t(s,a)] \;\leq\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,w_t^{2}\,\right] \sum_{k=0}^{t} \alpha_k^{2}
$$

For this to remain finite (the algorithm to be **mean-square stable**), we require:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \alpha_k^{2} \;<\; \infty
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A simple schedule satisfying both conditions is $\alpha_t = \tfrac{1}{t+1}$ (or $\alpha_t = \tfrac{c}{c+t}$), which has $\sum \tfrac{1}{t+1} = \infty$ but $\sum \tfrac{1}{(t+1)^{2}} < \infty$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A 3-state MDP has transition data. Apply **one SARSA update** with $\gamma = 0.9$, $\alpha = 0.5$, and $\epsilon = 0.2$.

Given data at time $t$:
- $S_t = s_1$, $A_t = a_2$ with $Q(s_1, a_2) = 0.4$
- $R_{t+1} = +1$
- $S_{t+1} = s_2$
- Action selected by $\epsilon$-greedy at $s_2$: $A_{t+1} = a_1$ with $Q(s_2, a_1) = 0.6$, and $Q(s_2, a_2) = 0.8$, $Q(s_2, a_3) = 0.5$

**Step 1 — Identify greedy action at $s_2$:**

$$
a^{*} \;=\; \arg\max_{a' \in \{a_1, a_2, a_3\}} Q(s_2, a') \;=\; \arg\max\{0.6, 0.8, 0.5\} \;=\; a_2
$$

**Step 2 — Choose $A_{t+1}$ via $\epsilon$-greedy:**

$$
P(A_{t+1} = a_2) \;=\; 1 - \epsilon + \tfrac{\epsilon}{3} \;=\; 0.8 + 0.0667 \;=\; 0.8667
$$

$$
P(A_{t+1} = a_1) \;=\; P(A_{t+1} = a_3) \;=\; \tfrac{0.2}{3} \;=\; 0.0667
$$

Suppose the sampled action is $A_{t+1} = a_1$, so $Q(s_2, a_1) = 0.6$.

**Step 3 — Compute TD target:**

$$
Y_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma\, Q(s_2, A_{t+1}) \;=\; 1 + 0.9 \times 0.6 \;=\; 1 + 0.54 \;=\; 1.54
$$

**Step 4 — Compute TD error:**

$$
\delta_t \;=\; Y_t - Q(s_1, a_2) \;=\; 1.54 - 0.4 \;=\; 1.14
$$

**Step 5 — Apply SARSA update:**

$$
Q_{\text{new}}(s_1, a_2) \;=\; Q(s_1, a_2) + \alpha\, \delta_t \;=\; 0.4 + 0.5 \times 1.14 \;=\; 0.4 + 0.57 \;=\; 0.97
$$

**Final Updated Value:**

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{\text{new}}(s_1, a_2) \;=\; 0.97\,}
$$

---

## 3.4 Comparison with Off-Policy Q-Learning (Same Example)

If we ran **Q-Learning** with the same data:

$$
Q_{\text{new}}(s_1, a_2) \;=\; Q(s_1, a_2) + \alpha \!\left[\,R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_2, a') - Q(s_1, a_2)\,\right]
$$

Since $\max_{a'} Q(s_2, a') = Q(s_2, a_2) = 0.8$:

$$
Y_t^{\text{QL}} \;=\; 1 + 0.9 \times 0.8 \;=\; 1.72
$$

$$
\delta_t^{\text{QL}} \;=\; 1.72 - 0.4 \;=\; 1.32
$$

$$
Q_{\text{new}}^{\text{QL}}(s_1, a_2) \;=\; 0.4 + 0.5 \times 1.32 \;=\; 1.06
$$

> [!TIP]
> Q-Learning's update is **larger** (1.06 vs 0.97) because it uses the greedy (optimistic) target instead of the actually-chosen exploratory action. This optimism is what makes Q-Learning more aggressive but also more brittle near hazards.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Backup Diagrams Comparison

| Algorithm | Backup Type | Target Uses | Bias | Variance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| MC | Full-episode sample | $G_t$ (realized) | **0** | **High** |
| TD(0) on $V$ | 1-step sample | $V(S_{t+1})$ | Some | Low |
| **SARSA** | **1-step sample** | $Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ | Some | Low |
| Q-Learning | 1-step sample | $\max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')$ | Some (max-bias) | Low |
| Expected SARSA | 1-step expectation | $\sum_a \pi(a \vert S_{t+1}) Q(S_{t+1}, a)$ | Some | Lowest |
| n-step SARSA | n-step sample | truncated $G_{t:t+n}$ | Lower | Higher |
| TD($\lambda$) / Watkins $Q(\lambda)$ | Eligibility traces | $\lambda$-mix | Tunable | Tunable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SARSA Control Loop — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start Episode]) --> initS[Initialize S_t]
    initS --> selA[Select A_t using epsilon-greedy policy from Q]
    selA --> step{For each time step t}
    step --> exec[Execute action A_t in environment]
    exec --> obs[Observe reward R_t1 and next state S_t1]
    obs --> selAnext[Select A_t1 using epsilon-greedy policy from Q]
    selAnext --> comp[Compute TD error delta_t equals R_t1 plus gamma times Q of S_t1 A_t1 minus Q of S_t A_t]
    comp --> upd[Update Q of S_t A_t equals old value plus alpha times delta_t]
    upd --> assignS[Assign S_t equals S_t1 and A_t equals A_t1]
    assignS --> check{Terminal state reached}
    check -- No --> step
    check -- Yes --> endE([End Episode])
    endE --> decay[Decay epsilon and step size alpha per GLIE schedule]
    decay --> start
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SARSA as an Engineering Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Environment["Environment Module"]
        envState[(Current State S_t)]
        envTrans[Transition Function P and Reward Function R]
    end

    subgraph Agent["SARSA Agent Module"]
        policy[epsilon-greedy Policy pi]
        qtable[(Q-Table Store)]
        tdcalc[TD Error Calculator]
        updater[Q-Value Updater]
    end

    envState --> policy
    policy -->|A_t| envTrans
    envTrans -->|R_t1, S_t1| tdcalc
    qtable --> tdcalc
    tdcalc -->|delta_t| updater
    updater -->|updated Q values| qtable
    qtable --> policy
```

## 4.3 Algorithm Pseudocode Block (Reference)

```
SARSA (Tabular, On-Policy TD Control)
-----------------------------------------
Input:  alpha in (0,1],  epsilon > 0,  gamma in [0,1),  small eps_decay
Initialize:  Q(s,a) for all s,a arbitrarily;  Q(terminal,.) = 0
Loop for each episode:
    Initialize S
    Choose A from S using policy derived from Q (e.g., epsilon-greedy)
    Loop for each step of episode:
        Take action A, observe R, S_prime
        Choose A_prime from S_prime using policy derived from Q (epsilon-greedy)
        delta  <-  R  +  gamma * Q(S_prime, A_prime)  -  Q(S, A)
        Q(S,A) <-  Q(S,A)  +  alpha * delta
        S <- S_prime
        A <- A_prime
    until S is terminal
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Trap:** Many students write pseudocode where `A_prime` is chosen *after* the $Q$-update. That makes the algorithm non-SARSA (it degenerates to expected SARSA / Q-learning hybrids). Always select `A_prime` *before* the update.

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Backup Diagram (Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root((Q of S_t A_t)) --> R[Sample R_t1]
    root --> S[Sample S_t1]
    S --> Ap[Sample A_t1 via pi]
    Ap --> Qnext[(Q of S_t1 A_t1)]
    Qnext -.bootstrap.-> root
```

The **shaded terminal** (Q of S_t1 A_t1) is the bootstrap target — its value is *not* updated in this step.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**State the SARSA update rule and clearly identify what makes the algorithm on-policy.**

**Model Answer:**

The SARSA update rule at time $t$ is:

$$
Q(S_t, A_t) \;\leftarrow\; Q(S_t, A_t) \;+\; \alpha \!\left[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma\, Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \;-\; Q(S_t, A_t) \,\right]
$$

The algorithm is **on-policy** because the action $A_{t+1}$ used inside the TD target is sampled from the **same policy $\pi$** that generates $A_t$ and is being improved. There is no separate "behavior policy" and "target policy"; they are identical. Hence the algorithm estimates $Q^\pi$ for the policy actually being followed — not the optimal $Q^*$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**List and justify the GLIE conditions that guarantee SARSA converges to $Q^*$.**

**Model Answer (3 key bullets):**

1. **Infinite exploration:** Every state-action pair $(s,a)$ must be visited infinitely many times so no $Q$-estimate is permanently stuck.
2. **Greedy in the limit:** The policy must become deterministic-greedy as $t \to \infty$, ensuring the algorithm tracks the improving target.
3. **Robbins-Monro step-size schedule:** The learning rates satisfy $\sum_t \alpha_t(s,a) = \infty$ and $\sum_t \alpha_t^{2}(s,a) < \infty$, ensuring both convergence of the mean and bounded variance.

Justification: GLIE conditions combined with the contraction property of the Bellman operator on the action-value space yield convergence of $Q_t$ to $Q^*$ with probability 1 (Singh et al., 1994).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the SARSA algorithm in detail. Write its complete pseudocode and explain the role of each variable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, demonstrate **one step of the SARSA update** for a 4-action MDP. Assume $\alpha = 0.3$, $\gamma = 0.95$. Show clearly the computation of the TD error and updated $Q$-value. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution A(a) — Algorithm Explanation + Pseudocode

**Definition:** SARSA is an on-policy Temporal-Difference control algorithm that iteratively improves an $\epsilon$-greedy policy by estimating its action-value function $Q^\pi(s,a)$.

**Variable Roles:**

- $Q(s,a)$ — current estimate of action-value at state $s$ for action $a$.
- $\alpha$ — learning rate (step size).
- $\gamma$ — discount factor for future rewards.
- $\epsilon$ — exploration probability for the $\epsilon$-greedy policy.
- $R_{t+1}$ — observed reward after executing $A_t$ in $S_t$.
- $\delta_t$ — TD error signal driving the update.

**Pseudocode** (already given in §4.3).

**Key Properties:**

- One-step bootstrap using sample of $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$.
- Both behavior and target policies are the same $\epsilon$-greedy policy.
- Convergence to $Q^*$ under GLIE + Robbins-Monro.

#### Solution A(b) — Numerical Demonstration

**Setup:**
- $S_t = s_3$, $A_t = a_2$, $Q(s_3, a_2) = 0.5$
- $R_{t+1} = -0.2$
- $S_{t+1} = s_4$
- Q-table at $s_4$: $Q(s_4, a_1) = 0.3$, $Q(s_4, a_2) = 0.7$, $Q(s_4, a_3) = 0.4$, $Q(s_4, a_4) = 0.6$
- $\epsilon = 0.1$, sample $A_{t+1} = a_4$ (lucky exploration outcome)

**Step 1 — Identify greedy at $s_4$:** $a^* = a_2$ since $Q(s_4, a_2) = 0.7$ is max.

**Step 2 — Verify sampling:** $P(A_{t+1} = a_4) = \tfrac{0.1}{4} = 0.025$, but suppose stochastic draw returned $a_4$.

**Step 3 — Compute TD target:**

$$
Y_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma\, Q(s_4, a_4) \;=\; -0.2 + 0.95 \times 0.6 \;=\; -0.2 + 0.57 \;=\; 0.37
$$

**Step 4 — Compute TD error:**

$$
\delta_t \;=\; Y_t - Q(s_3, a_2) \;=\; 0.37 - 0.5 \;=\; -0.13
$$

**Step 5 — Apply update:**

$$
Q_{\text{new}}(s_3, a_2) \;=\; 0.5 + 0.3 \times (-0.13) \;=\; 0.5 - 0.039 \;=\; 0.461
$$

**Final:** $Q_{\text{new}}(s_3, a_2) = \mathbf{0.461}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Compare and contrast **SARSA** with **Q-Learning**. Include the update rules, on/off-policy nature, convergence targets, and a discussion of the Cliff-Walking experiment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **GLIE** property and show that the step-size schedule $\alpha_t = \tfrac{c}{c+t}$ for $c > 0$ satisfies the **Robbins-Monro conditions** required for SARSA convergence. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution B(a) — SARSA vs Q-Learning Comparison

**Update rules side-by-side:**

$$
\text{SARSA:}\quad Q(S_t,A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t,A_t) + \alpha\!\left[\,R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t,A_t)\,\right]
$$

$$
\text{Q-Learning:}\quad Q(S_t,A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t,A_t) + \alpha\!\left[\,R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a') - Q(S_t,A_t)\,\right]
$$

| Aspect | SARSA | Q-Learning |
|---|---|---|
| Policy class | On-policy | Off-policy |
| Bootstrap action | Sampled $A_{t+1} \sim \pi$ | Greedy $\arg\max$ |
| Convergence target | $Q^\pi$ for current $\pi$ (then $\to Q^*$) | $Q^*$ directly |
| Risk profile | Conservative | Optimistic (max-bias) |

**Cliff Walking Discussion (Sutton & Barto):** In the 4×12 gridworld, agents must travel from start to goal while avoiding a cliff. With $\epsilon$-greedy exploration:
- **SARSA** learns the *safe upper path* because it accounts for the $\epsilon$-probability of stepping off the cliff.
- **Q-Learning** learns the *shorter cliff-side path* because it optimistically assumes the next action will be the safe greedy one — but during training the exploratory $\epsilon$-steps cause many cliff falls.

This empirically demonstrates the trade-off: **SARSA is safer during learning; Q-Learning learns the optimal final policy (with no exploration).**

#### Solution B(b) — Robbins-Monro Verification

**Step-size schedule:** $\alpha_t = \dfrac{c}{c + t}$.

**Check condition 1 — $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$:**

$$
\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \frac{c}{c + t} \;=\; c \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{c + t} \;\geq\; \sum_{t=c}^{\infty} \frac{1}{t} \;=\; \infty
$$

(The harmonic series diverges.) ✓

**Check condition 2 — $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$:**

$$
\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \frac{c^{2}}{(c + t)^{2}} \;=\; c^{2} \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(c + t)^{2}} \;\leq\; c^{2} \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{t^{2}} \;=\; c^{2} \cdot \frac{\pi^{2}}{6} \;<\; \infty
$$

(Convergent $p$-series with $p = 2 > 1$.) ✓

Hence the schedule satisfies both Robbins-Monro conditions. Combined with GLIE, SARSA converges to $Q^*$ with probability 1. **Q.E.D.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Order of action selection:** Choosing $A_{t+1}$ *after* the update (instead of before) — this is not SARSA; loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $\max$ vs sampled action:** Writing $\max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')$ inside SARSA's target turns it into Q-Learning — major conceptual error, -4 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting GLIE:** Stating "SARSA converges" without enumerating infinite-exploration, greedy-in-the-limit, and Robbins-Monro conditions — loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong TD error sign:** Using $+ Q(S_t, A_t)$ instead of $- Q(S_t, A_t)$ — propagates to incorrect numerical answers.
> 5. **Mixing $\alpha$ and $\epsilon$:** Treating $\alpha$ as the exploration rate — incorrect; $\alpha$ is the learning rate, $\epsilon$ controls exploration.
> 6. **Skipping units / variable definitions:** Always define $S, A, R, \gamma, \alpha, \epsilon$ at first use.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SARSA** = **S**tate, **A**ction, **R**eward, next **S**tate, next **A**ction — memorize the five-tuple order.
- It is an **on-policy TD(0) control** algorithm — uses the same policy for action selection and bootstrap.
- Update rule: $Q(S_t,A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t,A_t) + \alpha [R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t,A_t)]$.
- TD error: $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t)$.
- Convergence requires **GLIE** (infinite exploration + greedy in limit) and **Robbins-Monro** step-sizes.
- Converges to $Q^*$ with probability 1 (Singh et al., 1994).
- Compare with Q-Learning: SARSA samples $A_{t+1}$; Q-Learning uses $\max_{a'} Q$.
- $\epsilon$-greedy exploration: $\pi(a \vert s) = \tfrac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$ for non-greedy, $1 - \epsilon + \tfrac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$ for greedy.
- Standard decay: $\epsilon_t = c/(c+t)$ satisfies GLIE.
- Choose $A_{t+1}$ **before** updating $Q$; reuse it as $A_t$ in the next step.
- Bias-variance: SARSA has **bias** (uses estimate $Q$ as target) but **lower variance** than Monte Carlo.
- Cliff-walking experiment: SARSA safer, Q-Learning more aggressive — classic KTU exam illustration.
- Initial $Q$ can be arbitrary (often 0); $Q(\text{terminal}, \cdot) = 0$ by convention.
- Extensions: **Expected SARSA** (expectation over actions), **n-step SARSA**, **Watkins' $Q(\lambda)$** with eligibility traces.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Q-learning: Off-Policy TD Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Q-learning: Off-Policy TD Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Q-Learning** is a model-free, **off-policy Temporal Difference (TD) control algorithm** introduced by Christopher Watkins (1989) that learns the optimal action-value function $Q^*(s, a)$ directly, independent of the policy being followed by the agent. The optimal action-value function satisfies the Bellman Optimality Equation, and Q-Learning iteratively approximates it by minimizing the TD error between the current estimate and the bootstrapped target that uses a *greedy* (max) operator over next-state action-values.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECMT745 – Module 3):**
> Q-Learning is a flagship off-policy TD control method. Students must be able to derive the update rule, distinguish on-policy from off-policy behaviour, and explain why the max-operator decouples the learning target from the behaviour policy.

The canonical Q-Learning update rule is:

$$
Q(S_t, A_t) \;\leftarrow\; Q(S_t, A_t) \;+\; \alpha \Big[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t)\,\Big]
$$

where every symbol carries a precise KTU-defensible meaning listed below.

| Symbol | Meaning | Domain / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $Q(S_t, A_t)$ | Current estimate of action-value | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $\alpha$ | Learning rate (step-size) | $0 < \alpha \le 1$ |
| $R_{t+1}$ | Immediate reward received | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $\gamma$ | Discount factor | $0 \le \gamma < 1$ |
| $\max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a)$ | Greedy bootstrap estimate | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $S_t, A_t$ | State and Action at time $t$ | Discrete / Continuous |

> [!NOTE]
> The bracketed quantity $R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t)$ is the **TD Error** $\delta_t$. It is the signed discrepancy that Q-Learning drives towards zero.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a food critic in a new city who is *learning* to rank restaurants.

- He can only visit a *fraction* of restaurants on any given day (this is the **behaviour policy** – e.g. ε-greedy; sometimes he explores randomly).
- However, after every meal he *mentally* asks: **"If I had eaten at the absolute best possible restaurant in the next neighbourhood, how good would it have been?"** (this is the **max operator** – the target policy).
- He then updates his *estimate* of the restaurant he *actually* visited by moving it towards that imagined best-case future estimate.
- He is therefore improving his estimate of the restaurant he visited **using a target that assumes perfect future choices**, even though his actual choices are noisy and exploratory.

That is Q-Learning in a nutshell: **the action that updates is the one actually taken, but the value used to update it is the value of the best possible next action.** Because the update is *as if* the agent were greedy thereafter, Q-Learning is off-policy — its learning target corresponds to a different (greedy) policy than the one generating the data.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Viva:** "Q-Learning = SARSA but with `max` instead of `Q(S',A')` for the next action." Just replacing the sampled next-action value with the maximum over all actions converts the on-policy SARSA into the off-policy Q-Learning.

## 1.3 Why Off-Policy? — The Core Distinction

| Aspect | On-Policy TD (SARSA) | Off-Policy TD (Q-Learning) |
|---|---|---|
| Learning target | Uses the action **actually taken** in $S_{t+1}$ | Uses the **best possible** action in $S_{t+1}$ |
| Policy followed during data collection | Same as target policy (e.g. ε-greedy) | Different from target (target is greedy) |
| Convergence target | $Q^\pi$ of the ε-soft policy | $Q^*$ (optimal) directly |
| Real-world analogy | You learn from the path you walked | You learn from the path you walked, but evaluate it as if you would have taken the *best* next step |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bellman Optimality Equation (Foundation of Q-Learning)

The optimal action-value function $Q^*(s,a)$ is defined as the expected return when starting from state $s$, taking action $a$, and thereafter following an *optimal* policy. By the Bellman Optimality Equation:

$$
Q^*(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\Big[\,R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(S_{t+1}, a') \;\bigm|\; S_t = s,\; A_t = a\,\Big]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Q-Learning is a **stochastic approximation algorithm** that samples the right-hand side of this equation, with $\alpha$ acting as a step-size for an iterative fixed-point solver.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Q-Learning

1. **Initialize** the Q-table: $Q(s, a) \gets 0$ (or arbitrary) for all $s \in \mathcal{S}, a \in \mathcal{A}$.
2. **Observe** the current state $S_t$.
3. **Choose action** $A_t$ using an *ε-greedy* (or other exploration) policy derived from current Q estimates.
4. **Execute** $A_t$ in the environment; observe reward $R_{t+1}$ and next state $S_{t+1}$.
5. **Compute the TD target** using the max operator:
   $$\text{Target} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a)$$
6. **Update** the chosen Q-value:
   $$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \big[\,\text{Target} - Q(S_t, A_t)\,\big]$$
7. **Set** $S_t \leftarrow S_{t+1}$; repeat until convergence.

## 2.3 Exploration Strategy — ε-Greedy (The Behaviour Policy)

The behaviour policy that generates data is typically ε-greedy:

$$
\pi(a \mid s) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
1 - \varepsilon + \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}, & a = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s, a') \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}, & a \neq \arg\max_{a'} Q(s, a')
\end{cases}
$$

- With probability $1 - \varepsilon$ the agent acts **greedily** (exploits).
- With probability $\varepsilon$ the agent acts **randomly** (explores).
- This is the *data-generating* policy, **not** the target policy.
