> **Conversion logic:** This shows Dyna-Q achieves the *same* asymptotic convergence guarantees as tabular Q-learning (Bertsekas & Tsitsiklis, 1996), with a finite-sample efficiency multiplied by $(1+n)$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Tabular Q-Learning | Watkins's Q(λ) | Dyna-Q |
|----------|--------------------|----------------|--------|
| Multi-step credit | No (one-step) | Yes (via traces) | No (one-step) but multiplied by $n$ |
| Off-policy safe | Yes (greedy bootstrap) | Yes (reset trace on greedy argmax) | Yes (Q-learning is off-policy) |
| Model required | No | No | **Yes** |
| Sample efficiency baseline | $1\times$ | $\frac{1}{1-\lambda}$ effective speed-up | $(1+n)\times$ real samples |
| Memory cost | $\mathcal{O}(\vert S\vert \vert A\vert)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\vert S\vert \vert A\vert)$ for $Q$ + same for $e$ | $\mathcal{O}(\vert S\vert \vert A\vert)$ for $Q$, $M$, observed-states list |
| Convergence guarantee | Robbins-Monro + GLIE | Same (with bounded traces) | Same (with accurate model) |
| Failure mode | Slow on sparse rewards | Trace parameter tuning | Model bias |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Watkins's Q(λ) — Algorithm Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1["Initialize Q s a and e s a to zero"]
    n2["Repeat for each episode"]
    n3["Initialize state S"]
    n4["Repeat for each step of episode"]
    n5["Choose A from S using epsilon greedy policy"]
    n6["Take action A observe reward R and next state S prime"]
    n7["Increment eligibility e S A by one"]
    n8["Decay all eligibilities by factor gamma lambda"]
    n9["Compute TD error delta equals R plus gamma max Q S prime a minus Q S A"]
    n10["For every s and a update Q s a equals Q s a plus alpha delta e s a"]
    n11["If A equals argmax Q S prime then reset e S A to zero"]
    n12["Set S equals S prime"]
    n13{"Is S terminal?"}
    n14{"More episodes remain?"}

    n1 --> n2
    n2 --> n3
    n3 --> n4
    n4 --> n5
    n5 --> n6
    n6 --> n7
    n7 --> n8
    n8 --> n9
    n9 --> n10
    n10 --> n11
    n11 --> n12
    n12 --> n13
    n13 -- No --> n4
    n13 -- Yes --> n14
    n14 -- Yes --> n2
    n14 -- No --> stop(["Algorithm terminates"])
```

> **Reading guide:** The forward flow (steps $n_5 \to n_6 \to n_7 \to n_8$) is the *trace bookkeeping*; steps $n_9$ and $n_{10}$ perform the *credit assignment sweep*. The diagonal reset in $n_{11}$ is what guarantees the **off-policy safety** of Watkins's Q(λ).

---

### 4.2 Dyna-Q — Integrated Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ACT["ACTING"]
        a1["Current state S"]
        a2["Epsilon greedy action selection A"]
        a3["Execute A in environment"]
        a4["Observe R and S prime"]
    end

    subgraph DR["DIRECT RL"]
        d1["Q learning update on real transition"]
    end

    subgraph ML["MODEL LEARNING"]
        m1["Update model M S A equals R S prime"]
        m2["Store S in observed states list"]
    end

    subgraph PL["PLANNING"]
        p1["Repeat n times"]
        p2["Sample random previously observed S rand"]
        p3["Sample random A rand from actions taken in S rand"]
        p4["Query model for R rand and S prime rand"]
        p5["Q learning update on simulated transition"]
    end

    a1 --> a2 --> a3 --> a4
    a4 -- real transition --> d1
    a4 -- store experience --> m1
    a4 -- bookkeeping --> m2
    m1 --> p1
    m2 --> p1
    p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> p4 --> p5
    p5 -- loop n times --> p1
    p5 --> a1
```

> **Reading guide:** All four boxes share a single $Q$-table. The single arrow `p5 --> a1` closes the outer loop, indicating that after one *real* step and $n$ *simulated* steps the agent returns to acting with the *updated* value function.

---

### 4.3 Eligibility Trace Decay Visual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0["Time t0 visit pair s0 a0"]
    t1["Time t1"]
    t2["Time t2"]
    t3["Time t3"]
    t4["Time t4"]
    decay["Decay multiplier gamma lambda"]

    t0 -- "e equals 1" --> t1
    t1 -- "gamma lambda" --> t2
    t2 -- "gamma lambda" --> t3
    t3 -- "gamma lambda" --> t4

    note["After k steps without revisit the trace equals gamma lambda raised to k"]
    t4 -. informational .-> note
```

> **Reading guide:** Each step *without re-visit* multiplies the eligibility by $\gamma \lambda$; the trace reaches $\gamma\lambda^{k}$ after $k$ idle steps — this is the **exponential memory** property.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Differentiate between replacing and accumulating eligibility traces in Watkins's Q(λ).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Accumulating Trace | Replacing Trace |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------|
| Formula | $e_t = \gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1} + \mathbf{1}$ | $e_t = \mathbf{1}$ on visit; else $\gamma\lambda \, e_{t-1}$ |
| Upper bound | Unbounded (can grow large) | Bounded above by $1$ |
| Effect on revisit | Trace **grows** additively | Trace is **reset** to $1$ |
| Recommended use | Sparse-reward problems | Most control tasks (default in Sutton & Barto) |

> **Mark split (3):** 1 mark formula, 1 mark bound comparison, 1 mark recommendation.

---

#### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the four processes integrated in the Dyna-Q architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Acting** — the agent executes actions in the real environment using a policy derived from $Q$. (0.75)
2. **Direct RL** — the real transition is used to update $Q$ via the Q-learning rule. (0.75)
3. **Model Learning** — the model $M(s,a)$ is updated to record the observed $(r, s')$. (0.75)
4. **Planning** — using $M$, $n$ simulated transitions are sampled and the Q-learning rule is applied to each. (0.75)

> **Mark split (3):** 0.75 mark per process.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** *Answer **ONE** full question. Each carries 14 marks split as Part (a) 7 marks + Part (b) 7 marks.*

---

#### **QUESTION A — On Watkins's Q(λ)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With suitable equations, explain the **Watkins's Q(λ) algorithm**. Highlight the role of the eligibility trace and the off-policy safeguard. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **TD error** used in Watkins's Q(λ) from the $n$-step truncated Q-learning target. Show how the backward-view update using $\delta_t \, e_t(s,a)$ is equivalent to the forward-view $\lambda$-return update. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

1. **Algorithm state:** Maintain two tables $Q(s,a)$ and $e(s,a)$ of size $\vert S \vert \times \vert A \vert$. **(0.5)**
2. **Episode loop:** Initialize $s$; repeat per step. **(0.5)**
3. **Action selection:** $a \leftarrow \varepsilon\text{-greedy}(Q,s)$. **(0.5)**
4. **Environment step:** observe $(r, s')$. **(0.5)**
5. **Trace increment (accumulating):** $e(s,a) \mathrel{+}= 1$. **(1.0)**
6. **Trace decay (for all pairs):** $e(s', a') \leftarrow \gamma \lambda \, e(s',a')$. **(0.5)**
7. **TD error:**
   $$\delta_t = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) \quad \textbf{(1.0)}$$
8. **$Q$-update (for all pairs):**
   $$Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \, \delta_t \, e_t(s,a) \quad \textbf{(1.0)}$$
9. **Off-policy safeguard (Watkins's lazy reset):** set $e(s,a) = 0$ if $a = \arg\max_{b} Q(s',b)$. This ensures multi-step targets terminate at a greedy bootstrap, preserving convergence. **(1.0)**
10. **Termination:** $s \leftarrow s'$; end episode if $s$ terminal. **(0.5)**

##### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

1. **Definition of $n$-step target:** State the $n$-step Q-learning return. **(1.0)**
2. **Definition of $\lambda$-return:** Geometrically weighted average of $n$-step returns. **(1.0)**
3. **Expand weights:** Show $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}=1$. **(1.0)**
4. **Eligibility trace recursion:** Derive accumulating trace recursion from the gradient accumulation:
   $$\frac{\partial G_t^{\lambda}}{\partial Q(s_k,a_k)} = \gamma^{t-k}(\gamma\lambda)^{t-k} \;\Rightarrow\; e_t(s,a) = \sum_{k: s_k=s, a_k=a} (\gamma\lambda)^{t-k} \quad \textbf{(1.5)}$$
5. **TD error decomposition:** Expand $\delta_t e_t(s,a)$:
   $$\sum_{k=1}^{t} (\gamma\lambda)^{t-k}\bigl[r_{t+1} + \gamma\max Q(s_{t+1}) - Q(s_t,a_t)\bigr]\mathbf{1}(s_k,a_k) \quad \textbf{(1.0)}$$
6. **Equivalence proof:** Show term-by-term this equals $G_t^{\lambda} - Q_{t-1}(s,a)$ scaled by $\alpha$. **(1.0)**
7. **Conclusion:** Hence the *backward-view* $\alpha \delta_t e_t(s,a)$ update is *mathematically equivalent* to the *forward-view* $\lambda$-return update. **(0.5)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> (1) Many students forget to **reset the trace** on greedy actions — without this, off-policy convergence guarantees break. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> (2) Several answers confuse **accumulating** vs **replacing** traces; the *default* in Sutton & Barto is **replacing** for tabular control. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> (3) Students often quote $\lambda \in [0,1]$ without stating that $\lambda=0$ recovers one-step Q-learning and $\lambda=1$ recovers Monte-Carlo. **(Lose 1 mark)**

---

#### **QUESTION B — On Dyna-Q** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected]`

**(a)** Explain the **Dyna-Q architecture** with a labelled block diagram. List the four integrated processes and their order of execution within one real-time step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Dyna-Q agent operates in a deterministic gridworld with $\vert S \vert = 16$, $\vert A \vert = 4$. After $50$ real steps it has visited $40$ unique state–action pairs. If $n=5$ planning steps are executed per real step, compute: (i) total Q-learning updates after 50 real steps, (ii) the *speed-up factor* relative to model-free Q-learning. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

1. **Block diagram:** Draw the four boxes *Acting*, *Direct RL*, *Model Learning*, *Planning* with arrows (as in §4.2). **(2.0)**
2. **Acting (1):** Select $a$ from $s$ via $\varepsilon$-greedy $Q$; execute; observe $(r, s')$. **(1.0)**
3. **Direct RL (2):** Apply Q-learning on the real transition $(s,a,r,s')$. **(1.0)**
4. **Model Learning (3):** Update deterministic tabular model $M(s,a) \leftarrow (r, s')$; append $s$ to observed-states list. **(1.0)**
5. **Planning (4):** Repeat $n$ times — sample $(s_p,a_p)$ uniformly at random from observed list, query $M(s_p,a_p) = (r_p, s_p')$, apply Q-learning update on simulated transition. **(1.5)**
6. **Order of execution within one real step:** Acting → Direct RL → Model Learning → Planning (loop $n$ times). **(0.5)**

##### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**(i) Total Q-learning updates after 50 real steps:** **(3 marks)**

Each real step triggers 1 direct-RL update. Each real step also triggers $n = 5$ planning updates.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total updates} \;&=\; \text{real steps} \times (1 + n) \\
&=\; 50 \times (1 + 5) \\
&=\; 50 \times 6 \\
&=\; \mathbf{300 \text{ updates}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Speed-up factor relative to model-free Q-learning:** **(4 marks)**

Model-free Q-learning would perform only 1 update per real step, i.e., 50 updates in 50 steps. The speed-up factor is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speed-up} \;&=\; \frac{\text{Dyna-Q updates}}{\text{Model-free updates}} \\
&=\; \frac{300}{50} \\
&=\; \mathbf{6\times}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Generalization:** In a deterministic environment with a perfect model, the effective sample efficiency grows linearly with $(1+n)$; doubling the planning budget halves the required real-environment experience asymptotically.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> (1) Students often forget that **planning updates** also use the **Q-learning rule** — Dyna-Q is *not* value iteration. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> (2) Confusing Dyna-Q with **Dyna-PI / Dyna-Q+** — these are *different* variants; Dyna-Q uses plain Q-learning and deterministic models. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> (3) Forgetting that the **model is learned from real experience**, so early in training the model is unreliable — the planning step's value grows over time. **(Lose 1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Watkins's Q(λ)**

- [x] Q(λ) = Q-learning **+ eligibility traces**.
- [x] **TD error** $\delta_t = r + \gamma \max_a Q(s',a) - Q(s,a)$ is the *single* quantity that drives all updates.
- [x] **Accumulating** trace: $e_t = \gamma\lambda\, e_{t-1} + \mathbf{1}$; **Replacing** trace caps at $1$.
- [x] **Update**: $Q(s,a) \mathrel{+}= \alpha \, \delta_t \, e_t(s,a)$.
- [x] **Off-policy safeguard**: $e(s,a) \leftarrow 0$ when $a$ is the greedy action in $s'$ (Watkins's lazy reset).
- [x] **Boundary cases**: $\lambda = 0$ ⇒ one-step Q-learning; $\lambda = 1$ ⇒ Monte-Carlo.
- [x] **Convergence**: guaranteed under standard stochastic approximation + GLIE + tabular setting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Dyna-Q**

- [x] Dyna-Q = **Direct RL + Model Learning + Planning**, all sharing one $Q$-table.
- [x] **Deterministic tabular model** $M(s,a) = (r,s')$.
- [x] **Planning step**: random sample from previously-observed $(s,a)$ pairs, Q-learning on hallucinated transition.
- [x] **Speed-up factor** = $(1+n)$.
- [x] **Variants**: Dyna-PI (policy iteration), Dyna-Q+ (exploration bonus + time-since-visited), prioritized sweeping.
- [x] **Limitation**: model bias — inaccurate model ⇒ planning reinforces wrong values.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid — Mnemonic "T-E-R-M" for Dyna-Q:**
> **T**able (Q) – **E**xperience (real) – **R**ecord (Model M) – **M**ultiply (planning).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trajectory Sampling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Trajectory Sampling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Trajectory Sampling** is a simulation-based estimation technique in Reinforcement Learning (RL) and dynamic programming where complete sequences of state-action-reward transitions (called *trajectories* or *rollouts*) are generated from an environment model (or the actual environment), and their returns are averaged to estimate value functions, evaluate policies, or perform planning.

A trajectory is formally defined as:

$$\tau = (S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, S_2, \dots, A_{T-1}, R_T, S_T)$$

The expected return under policy $\pi$ starting from state $s_0$ is:

$$v_\pi(s_0) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s_0 \right] = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \mid S_t = s_0 \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Trajectory Sampling bridges the gap between full-width Dynamic Programming (which requires exhaustive sweeps over the state space) and pure Temporal-Difference learning. It is the computational engine behind **Dyna architecture**, **Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)**, and **model-based planning**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Student's Exam Strategy"

Imagine a student preparing for a difficult examination. Instead of memorizing every possible question (which would be **exhaustive DP**), or just attempting one problem and correcting it (which would be **TD(0)**), the student instead **solves 50 full mock papers** end-to-end. Each mock paper is a *trajectory* — a complete sequence of questions and answers. The student then uses the average score from these mock papers to predict their real exam performance.

In RL:
- **Mock paper** = Simulated trajectory
- **Average score** = Expected return / value estimate
- **Solving more papers** = More samples → better estimate (Law of Large Numbers)

## 1.3 Why Trajectory Sampling Matters in KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, trajectory sampling is examined as the *engine* of model-based planning. The key distinction students must grasp is between:
> 1. **Full Backup (DP)**: $V(s) \leftarrow \sum_a \pi(a\mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r\mid s,a)[r + \gamma V(s')]$ — requires knowledge of all transitions.
> 2. **Sample Backup (Trajectory Sampling)**: $V(s) \leftarrow \mathbb{E}_{\text{sample}}[R + \gamma V(S')]$ — uses one simulated next state.

Trajectory sampling trades **exhaustive computation** for **statistical estimation**, enabling RL to scale to large state spaces where DP is infeasible.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Workflow of Trajectory Sampling

Trajectory Sampling operates through a structured five-stage pipeline:

- **Step 1 — Model Acquisition:** Either a learned model $\hat{p}(s',r \mid s,a)$ or the true environment provides the transition dynamics.
- **Step 2 — Policy Selection:** A behavior policy $\pi$ (could be on-policy $\pi$ or exploratory like $\epsilon$-greedy) governs action selection at every state.
- **Step 3 — Rollout Generation:** Starting from an initial state $S_0$ (often sampled from a distribution $d_0$), the agent simulates a complete trajectory of horizon $H$ or until a terminal state.
- **Step 4 — Return Accumulation:** Along the trajectory, the discounted return $G_0 = \sum_{k=0}^{H-1} \gamma^k R_{k+1}$ is computed.
- **Step 5 — Value Estimation:** Multiple trajectories are averaged to form the Monte Carlo estimate:
$$\hat{v}_\pi(s_0) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_0^{(i)}$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why simulate at all?** Real interactions are expensive, dangerous, or slow (robotics, healthcare). Sampling decouples *learning* from *acting*.
- **Why full trajectories?** They naturally handle the **temporal credit assignment** problem and avoid the need to enumerate all successor states.
- **Why average?** By the **Law of Large Numbers** and **Central Limit Theorem**, the sample mean converges to the true expectation with error $O(1/\sqrt{N})$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Trajectory $\tau$ | $(S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, \dots, S_T)$ | Sequence of random variables |
| Discounted Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | $\gamma \in [0,1]$ dimensionless |
| State Value | $v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ | Cumulative reward |
| Action Value | $q_\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ | Q-function |
| Bellman Expectation | $v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a\mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r\mid s,a)[r + \gamma v_\pi(s')]$ | Full backup |
| Bellman Optimality | $v_*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r\mid s,a)[r + \gamma v_*(s')]$ | DP recursion |
| Sample-Backup Update | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha [R + \gamma V(S') - V(s)]$ | TD(0) form |
| MC Estimate | $\hat{v}_\pi(s) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N G^{(i)}$ | Unbiased estimator |
| Sample Mean Error | $\text{Var}(\hat{v}_\pi) = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}$ | $N$ = sample count |
| Importance Sampling Ratio | $\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)}$ | Off-policy correction |
| Dyna-Q Update | $Q(S,A) \leftarrow Q(S,A) + \alpha [R + \gamma \max_a Q(S',a) - Q(S,A)]$ | From simulated model |
| Eligibility Trace | $e_t(s) = \gamma \lambda e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbb{1}(S_t = s)$ | TD($\lambda$) bridge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR KTU SCRIBES:** When writing absolute value, set notation, or conditional probability in exam scripts, **always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\lvert x \mid y \rvert$** — never bare `|x|` which can confuse the examiner. The KTU 2024 valuation key penalizes ambiguous notation.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

Trajectory sampling powers some of the most celebrated AI systems:
- **AlphaGo / AlphaZero (DeepMind):** Uses Monte Carlo Tree Search with rollouts to evaluate board positions.
- **Autonomous Driving (Waymo, Tesla):** Trajectory prediction for pedestrian and vehicle behavior.
- **Robotics Manipulation:** Sampling-based motion planning (RRT, PRM).
- **Healthcare RL:** Simulating patient treatment trajectories before deployment.
- **Financial Trading:** Backtesting strategies over historical price trajectories.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bellman Expectation to Trajectory Sample

**Starting Point — The Bellman Expectation Equation:**

$$v_\pi(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \sum_{r \in \mathcal{R}} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma v_\pi(s') \right]$$

**Step 1 — Recognize the Nested Expectation:**

The right-hand side is a composition of two expectations: one over actions chosen by the policy, and one over environment stochasticity.

$$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_{A \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)} \left[ \mathbb{E}_{S', R \sim p(\cdot \mid s, A)} \left[ R + \gamma v_\pi(S') \mid S = s, A \right] \mid S = s \right]$$

**Step 2 — Combine into a Single Expectation:**

$$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi, p} \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s \right]$$

> This shows that $v_\pi(s)$ is simply the expected value of the **one-step sample backup target** $R_{t+1} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1})$.

**Step 3 — Expand Recursively (Unroll in Time):**

Apply the same expectation repeatedly:

$$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+2} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+2}) \mid S_{t+1} \right] \mid S_t = s \right]$$

$$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 v_\pi(S_{t+2}) \mid S_t = s \right]$$

**Step 4 — Unroll Until Terminal / Horizon $H$:**

$$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{H-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} + \gamma^H v_\pi(S_{t+H}) \mid S_t = s \right]$$

$$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]$$

> This derivation is the **mathematical foundation of trajectory sampling**: the value is the expected return over trajectories.

**Step 5 — Monte Carlo Approximation via Sampling:**

Draw $N$ i.i.d. trajectories $\{\tau^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^N$ from $\pi$ and the model:

$$\hat{v}_\pi(s) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_0^{(i)} \xrightarrow{N \to \infty} v_\pi(s) \quad \text{a.s.}$$

**Step 6 — Bounding the Estimator Error:**

By the Central Limit Theorem, for large $N$:

$$\hat{v}_\pi(s) \sim \mathcal{N}\left( v_\pi(s), \frac{\sigma_G^2}{N} \right)$$

where $\sigma_G^2 = \text{Var}_\pi(G_0 \mid S_0 = s)$. The 95% confidence half-width is:

$$\epsilon = 1.96 \cdot \frac{\sigma_G}{\sqrt{N}}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Backup Types

| **Backup Type** | **Computation** | **Bias** | **Variance** | **Requires Model?** | **Depth** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Full Backup (DP) | $\sum_a \sum_{s',r}$ | Zero | Zero | Yes | Full lookahead |
| Sample Backup (1-step) | One sample of $(s',r)$ | None | $\sigma^2$ | Yes | 1-step |
| **Trajectory Backup (MC)** | Full episode | None | High ($\sigma_G^2$) | No (if real) | Episode |
| TD(0) | One real step | Yes (bootstrapped) | Low | No | 1-step |
| TD($\lambda$) | Eligibility trace | Partial | Tunable | No | $\lambda$-blend |
| Dyna Trajectory | Simulated + Real | Tunable | Tunable | Yes (learned) | Episode |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The most common 14-mark question asks to *compare* these backup types along with an example. Memorize this matrix and the bias-variance trade-off — it is a guaranteed 14-marker.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** Consider a 3-state MDP with states $\{A, B, C\}$, where $C$ is terminal. The policy $\pi$ is deterministic: $\pi(A) = B$, $\pi(B) = C$. Discount factor $\gamma = 0.9$. Generate two trajectories and estimate $v_\pi(A)$.

**Trajectory 1:** $A \xrightarrow{R=+5} B \xrightarrow{R=+2} C$

$$G_0^{(1)} = 5 + 0.9 \cdot 2 = 5 + 1.8 = 6.8$$

**Trajectory 2:** $A \xrightarrow{R=+3} B \xrightarrow{R=+4} C$

$$G_0^{(2)} = 3 + 0.9 \cdot 4 = 3 + 3.6 = 6.6$$

**Estimate:**

$$\hat{v}_\pi(A) = \frac{6.8 + 6.6}{2} = 6.7$$

**True value (if rewards are deterministic and stochastic over runs):** Assuming mean rewards $\bar{r}(A,B) = 4$, $\bar{r}(B,C) = 3$:

$$v_\pi(A) = 4 + 0.9 \cdot 3 = 4 + 2.7 = 6.7$$

> **The sample estimate matches the true value exactly** because the two sampled rewards happen to be symmetric around the means. In general, more samples reduce the error.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Trajectory Sampling Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input: Initial State S0] --> B0[Step 1: Sample Action from Policy pi]
    B0 --> C0[Step 2: Sample Next State S prime and Reward R from Model p]
    C0 --> D0{Step 3: Terminal State Reached or Horizon H}
    D0 -->|No| B0
    D0 -->|Yes| E0[Step 4: Compute Return G0 equals sum of gamma to k times R k plus 1]
    E0 --> F0[Step 5: Accumulate Return into Value Estimate]
    F0 --> G0{Step 6: Enough Samples N collected}
    G0 -->|No| H0[Step 7: Reset and Generate New Trajectory]
    H0 --> A0
    G0 -->|Yes| I0[Step 8: Output Average Return as v pi hat of S0]
```

## 4.2 Dyna Architecture — Where Trajectory Sampling Lives

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Env[Real Environment] -->|Real Experience| Agent[Direct RL Component]
    Agent -->|Update Policy and Value| ModelStore[Learned Model M]
    ModelStore -->|Store Experience| DirectRL[Direct RL Update]
    DirectRL --> Agent
    ModelStore -->|Simulated Experience| Planner[Planning Component]
    Planner -->|Sample Trajectories| ValueUpdate[Value Function Update]
    ValueUpdate --> Agent
    Agent -->|Action| Env
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Dyna Insight:** The "Planning Component" block generates trajectories by sampling from the **learned model** $M$, not the real environment. This is the *core contribution* of trajectory sampling in model-based RL: it allows the agent to "imagine" and learn from synthetic experience.

## 4.3 Bias-Variance Trade-off in Backup Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Backup Strategy Selection] --> L1{Full State Space Known?}
    L1 -->|Yes| L2[Full Backup DP: Zero Bias, Zero Variance, O of S squared A]
    L1 -->|No| L3{Model Available?}
    L3 -->|Yes| L4[Trajectory Sampling: Unbiased, Variance sigma squared over N, Scalable]
    L3 -->|No| L5[Model Free TD or MC: Online Learning, Higher Bias for TD]
    L4 --> L6[Outputs: Estimated Value Function]
    L5 --> L6
    L2 --> L6
```

## 4.4 Sampling Tree (Rollout Tree for MCTS-style Planning)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Root State S0] --> A1[Action A1]
    Root --> A2[Action A2]
    Root --> A3[Action A3]
    A1 --> S1[Next State S1]
    A1 --> S2[Next State S2]
    A2 --> S3[Next State S3]
    A2 --> S4[Next State S4]
    A3 --> S5[Next State S5]
    S1 --> T1[Trajectory 1 Leaf]
    S1 --> T2[Trajectory 2 Leaf]
    S2 --> T3[Trajectory 3 Leaf]
    S3 --> T4[Trajectory 4 Leaf]
    S4 --> T5[Trajectory 5 Leaf]
    S5 --> T6[Trajectory 6 Leaf]
    T1 --> Agg[Aggregate Returns to Estimate Q of S0 A]
    T2 --> Agg
    T3 --> Agg
    T4 --> Agg
    T5 --> Agg
    T6 --> Agg
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MCTS Connection:** Each path from the root to a leaf is one sampled trajectory. Modern MCTS algorithms (PUCT, AlphaZero) sample **thousands of trajectories** per decision and back up averaged returns.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### Q1. Define trajectory sampling in reinforcement learning. Mention its role in model-based planning. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Trajectory sampling is a simulation-based technique where complete sequences of states, actions, and rewards (trajectories) are generated by sampling from a policy and a transition model. The expected return over these sampled trajectories is used to estimate the value function.

$$\hat{v}_\pi(s) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_0^{(i)}$$

In **model-based planning** (such as the Dyna architecture), the learned model $\hat{p}$ acts as a *simulator*; trajectory sampling allows the agent to perform "simulated experience" — generating many synthetic rollouts cheaply to improve the value function and policy without real environment interaction. This accelerates convergence and decouples *learning* from *acting*.

---

### Q2. Distinguish between full backup and sample backup with suitable equations. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A **full backup** computes the value by enumerating *all* possible next states and rewards using the model:

$$V(s) \leftarrow \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V(s') \right]$$

A **sample backup** (trajectory-sampling style) draws a *single* sample of $(s', r)$ and updates using that sample:

$$V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \left[ R + \gamma V(S') - V(s) \right]$$

| Aspect | Full Backup | Sample Backup |
|---|---|---|
| Computation | $O(\lvert \mathcal{S} \rvert^2 \lvert \mathcal{A} \rvert)$ | $O(1)$ per step |
| Variance | Zero | Non-zero |
| Bias | Zero | Bootstrap-dependent |
| Scalability | Poor for large spaces | Excellent |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

### **Question A: Trajectory Sampling in Policy Evaluation [14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the algorithm for **first-visit Monte Carlo policy evaluation** using trajectory sampling. Describe how $N$ trajectories are used to estimate $v_\pi(s)$. State the convergence guarantees. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A robot operates in a 4-state grid. The policy moves it deterministically rightward with reward $R = +1$ on every step, except the rightmost terminal state has $R = +10$. Discount factor $\gamma = 0.5$. Simulate **3 trajectories** starting from the leftmost state and compute the Monte Carlo estimate of the value. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Answer for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Algorithm: First-Visit MC Policy Evaluation via Trajectory Sampling**

```
Input: Policy π, state s to evaluate, number of episodes N
Initialize: Returns(s) ← empty list
For episode = 1 to N:
    Generate trajectory τ following π from s:
        S_0 = s, then A_t ~ π(·|S_t), S_{t+1} ~ p(·|S_t, A_t), R_{t+1} observed
    Compute return G ← 0
    For t = T-1 down to 0:
        G ← γ·G + R_{t+1}
        If S_t is the FIRST visit to state s in this episode:
            Append G to Returns(s)
Estimate V(s) ← average(Returns(s))
```

**Convergence guarantees:**
- By the **Law of Large Numbers**: $V(s) \to v_\pi(s)$ as $N \to \infty$.
- The estimate is **unbiased**: $\mathbb{E}[\hat{V}(s)] = v_\pi(s)$.
- **Mean Squared Error**: $\text{MSE} = \text{Var}(G_0)/N$, so error decreases as $O(1/\sqrt{N})$.

**Justification of "first-visit":** In first-visit MC, each state $s$ contributes to its estimate *only once per episode* (its first occurrence). This avoids the bias introduced by counting repeated visits and gives a consistent estimator.

---

### **Model Answer for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Setup:** States $S_0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to S_3$ (terminal), $\gamma = 0.5$, deterministic policy moves right.

**Trajectory 1:** $S_0 \xrightarrow{R=1} S_1 \xrightarrow{R=1} S_2 \xrightarrow{R=10} S_3$

$$G_0^{(1)} = 1 + 0.5(1) + 0.5^2(10) = 1 + 0.5 + 2.5 = 4.0$$

**Trajectory 2:** $S_0 \xrightarrow{R=1} S_1 \xrightarrow{R=1} S_2 \xrightarrow{R=10} S_3$

Since policy and rewards are deterministic, $G_0^{(2)} = 4.0$.

**Trajectory 3:** Identical by determinism, $G_0^{(3)} = 4.0$.

**Monte Carlo Estimate:**

$$\hat{v}_\pi(S_0) = \frac{4.0 + 4.0 + 4.0}{3} = 4.0$$

**True analytical value (verification):**

$$v_\pi(S_0) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot v_\pi(S_1)$$
$$v_\pi(S_1) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot v_\pi(S_2)$$
$$v_\pi(S_2) = 10$$
$$\Rightarrow v_\pi(S_1) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 10 = 6$$
$$\Rightarrow v_\pi(S_0) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 6 = 4.0$$

> **The estimate matches the true value exactly** because the system is deterministic. In stochastic environments, more samples would be needed to reduce variance.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice): Dyna Architecture and Simulated Trajectories [14 Marks]**

**(a)** Describe the **Dyna architecture** for integrated learning and planning. Explain how trajectory sampling from the learned model supplements direct RL. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **bias-variance trade-off** when using trajectory sampling from an imperfect (learned) model. How does the number of planning steps $n$ affect performance? **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Answer for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks**

The **Dyna architecture** (Sutton, 1990) interleaves two parallel processes:

1. **Direct RL:** The agent interacts with the real environment, observes $(S, A, R, S')$, and updates $Q$ using e.g. Q-learning:
$$Q(S, A) \leftarrow Q(S, A) + \alpha \left[ R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S', a') - Q(S, A) \right]$$

2. **Model Learning:** The same experience is used to update a tabular or function-approximation model $\hat{p}(s', r \mid s, a)$.

3. **Planning via Trajectory Sampling:** The agent performs $n$ **planning steps** — in each step, it samples a previously-visited state-action pair $(S, A)$ from memory, then *samples* a successor $(S', R)$ from the learned model $\hat{p}$, and applies the same Q-learning update as a "simulated experience":

$$Q(S, A) \leftarrow Q(S, A) + \alpha \left[ R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S', a') - Q(S, A) \right]$$

This synthetic rollout is the **trajectory** sampled from the model. Each planning step acts as a one-step trajectory sample. With $n = 50$ planning steps, every real environment interaction yields 50 simulated updates, dramatically accelerating learning.

**Key advantage:** Decoupling real experience from planning means the agent can rehearse policies without risk — critical in domains like clinical decision support or autonomous driving.

---

### **Model Answer for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks**

When the model is **imperfect** (as all learned models are), trajectory sampling introduces both **bias** and **variance**:

| Source | Description | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Model bias** | Inaccurate $\hat{p}$ leads to systematically wrong value estimates | Use probabilistic ensembles, conservative updates |
| **Sampling variance** | Stochastic rollouts fluctuate around the true model-based value | More samples $N$ reduce by $1/N$ |
| **Compounding error** | Errors accumulate over horizon $H$: $\text{error} \propto O(H \cdot \epsilon_{\text{model}})$ | Limit rollout depth, use short rollouts |

**Effect of planning steps $n$:**

- **Small $n$:** Insufficient use of model — slow learning, low computation.
- **Large $n$:** Asymptotically faster convergence in tabular settings, but in **function approximation** with model error, large $n$ causes **policy divergence** (Sutton, 2008; van Hasselt et al., 2019). The simulated values can drift far from reality.
- **Sweet spot:** Typically $n = 5$ to $n = 50$ depending on model quality.

**Modern solutions:**
- **Trajectory Replay (Dreamer, MuZero):** Use latent dynamics models with uncertainty estimation.
- **Conservative Q-Learning:** Penalize Q-values for out-of-distribution model-generated states.
- **Short $k$-step rollouts:** Limit horizon to control compounding error.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls — Trajectory Sampling Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing trajectory sampling with full DP backup (–2 marks):** Trajectory sampling is *stochastic* and produces a *sample estimate*; do not write the full Bellman expectation sum.
> 2. **Forgetting the discount factor in the return (–1 mark):** Always write $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{H-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ explicitly. Examiners mark "discount term present" as a sub-criterion.
> 3. **Not specifying "first-visit" vs "every-visit" MC (–1 mark):** State which MC variant you are using. First-visit is the default in KTU textbooks.
> 4. **Ignoring model error in Dyna (–2 marks):** When discussing planning, mention that the model is *learned* and *imperfect*. Failing to do so marks you down on the "limitations" criterion.
> 5. **Mixing $\pi$ and $b$ in importance sampling (–3 marks):** Off-policy trajectory sampling requires the importance ratio $\rho$. Never omit it; examiners look for it explicitly.
> 6. **No convergence statement (–1 mark):** Always close with "by the Law of Large Numbers, the estimate converges to the true value."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Trajectory Sampling** = simulating full state-action-reward sequences and averaging returns to estimate values.
- The **mathematical basis** is the Bellman expectation: $v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$.
- **First-visit MC estimator:** $\hat{v}_\pi(s) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N G^{(i)}$ — unbiased, consistent, error $O(1/\sqrt{N})$.
- **Sample backup** uses one $(s', r)$ draw; **full backup** enumerates all transitions.
- **Dyna architecture** combines *direct RL* (real experience) with *planning* (simulated trajectories from a learned model) using $n$ planning steps per real step.
- **Bias-variance trade-off:** MC/trajectory sampling has *zero bias* but *high variance*; TD has *low variance* but *some bias*.
- **MCTS connection:** Each leaf in a search tree is a sampled trajectory; aggregating leaf returns estimates $Q(s, a)$.
- **Off-policy correction:** Trajectory sampling under behavior policy $b$ requires importance ratio $\rho = \prod_k \pi(A_k \mid S_k)/b(A_k \mid S_k)$ to estimate $v_\pi$.
- **Compounding model error** in Dyna/MuZero limits effective rollout length — short $k$-step rollouts (typically $k = 5$ to $20$) are preferred over long rollouts.
- **Key equations to memorize for KTU:**
  - $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$
  - $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha [R + \gamma V(S') - V(s)]$
  - $Q(S,A) \leftarrow Q(S,A) + \alpha [R + \gamma \max_a Q(S',a) - Q(S,A)]$
  - $\hat{v}_\pi(s) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N G^{(i)}$
- **Engineering applications:** AlphaGo/AlphaZero, autonomous driving, robotics, healthcare RL, financial backtesting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Value Based Reinforcement Learning: Deep Reinforcement Learning Agents with Sequential Feedback
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Value Based Reinforcement Learning: Deep Reinforcement Learning Agents with Sequential Feedback

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Value-Based Reinforcement Learning (Value-Based RL)** is a paradigm of reinforcement learning in which the agent learns an optimal action-value function (also called the Q-function) that estimates the expected cumulative future reward for taking a given action in a given state, and subsequently derives a policy by selecting actions that maximize these values.

**Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)** extends this paradigm by approximating the value function using deep neural networks, enabling the agent to operate in high-dimensional, continuous, or partially observable state spaces where traditional tabular methods fail.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *Deep Reinforcement Learning Agent with Sequential Feedback* is an agent that combines (i) a function approximator (typically a deep neural network) to estimate $Q(s,a)$, (ii) a feedback mechanism (Bellman backup / Temporal-Difference error) that propagates reward information backward through the sequence of states, and (iii) a stabilization mechanism (Experience Replay + Target Network) to break temporal correlation between consecutive training samples.

The term **Sequential Feedback** refers to the credit-assignment signal that flows backward in time from a delayed reward to the sequence of state-action pairs $(s_0,a_0,s_1,a_1,\ldots,s_t,a_t)$ that produced it, formally defined through the Bellman recursion.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a child learning to play the piano. Each keystroke produces a sound (an immediate sensory outcome), but the *reward* — the applause after a full performance — arrives only at the very end.

- The child cannot simply copy the last keystroke that "won." Instead, she must mentally ask: *Which notes in the long sequence actually mattered?*
- She stores every performance in her memory and occasionally replays past sessions to learn patterns.
- She also keeps a "shadow teacher" — a slowly-updated model of her own performance — that provides stable feedback while she practices.

This is precisely how a Deep RL agent works:

| Human Analogy | Deep RL Component |
|---------------|-------------------|
| Memory of past performances | **Experience Replay Buffer** |
| Slowly updated "shadow teacher" | **Target Network** |
| Mapping notes → applause | **Deep Q-Network (DQN)** |
| Assigning blame to past notes | **Sequential Feedback (Bellman Backup)** |

> [!NOTE]
> The defining feature of *sequential feedback* is that the **learning signal is temporally decoupled** from the actions that caused it. The Bellman equation provides the formal vehicle for propagating this signal backward across the sequence.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Glossary)

- **State ($s$)** : A complete description of the environment at time $t$.
- **Action ($a$)** : The choice made by the agent from action space $\mathcal{A}$.
- **Reward ($r$)** : Scalar feedback signal from environment.
- **Discount Factor ($\gamma$)** : $\gamma \in [0,1]$; controls the present value of future rewards.
- **Q-Function ($Q^\pi(s,a)$)** : Expected return when starting from $s$, taking $a$, and following policy $\pi$ thereafter.
- **TD Error ($\delta_t$)** : Difference between TD target and current estimate — the *core learning signal*.
- **Experience Tuple** : $(s_t, a_t, r_{t+1}, s_{t+1})$ stored in replay buffer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bellman Optimality Foundation

Value-based RL rests on the **Bellman Optimality Equation** for action values:

$$
Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{s'}\left[\, r(s,a) + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a') \,\right]
$$

This recursive form is the mathematical embodiment of *sequential feedback*: the value of acting now depends on the value of acting optimally in the next state.

## 2.2 Why Tabular Q-Learning Fails in Sequential, High-Dimensional Problems

Tabular Q-Learning maintains a discrete lookup table $Q[s,a]$. For sequential feedback problems with:

- **Large state spaces** (e.g., Atari frames $\sim 10^{68000}$),
- **Continuous states** (e.g., robotics joint angles),
- **Partial observability** (only recent history is visible),

…the table becomes infeasible. This motivates **function approximation**: $Q(s,a;\boldsymbol{\theta})$, where $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ are neural network parameters.

## 2.3 Deep Q-Network (DQN) — The Foundational DRL Agent

Mnih et al. (2015) introduced the modern DQN architecture. Its three pillars are:

1. **Deep Q-Network as function approximator** : $Q(s,a;\boldsymbol{\theta})$.
2. **Experience Replay Buffer** : A FIFO memory $\mathcal{D}$ storing tuples $(s_t, a_t, r_{t+1}, s_{t+1})$.
3. **Target Network** : A periodically-updated copy $Q(s,a;\boldsymbol{\theta}^-)$ used to compute stable TD targets.

## 2.4 The DQN Loss Function

The DQN parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ are trained to minimize:

$$
L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{(s,a,r,s')\sim \mathcal{D}} \left[\, \left( r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a';\boldsymbol{\theta}^-) - Q(s,a;\boldsymbol{\theta}) \right)^2 \,\right]
$$

Taking the gradient with respect to $\boldsymbol{\theta}$:

$$
\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}\left[\, \left( r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a';\boldsymbol{\theta}^-) - Q(s,a;\boldsymbol{\theta}) \right) \cdot \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} Q(s,a;\boldsymbol{\theta}) \,\right]
$$

The expression inside the parentheses is the **TD Error** $\delta_t$:

$$
\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1},a';\boldsymbol{\theta}^-) - Q(s_t, a_t;\boldsymbol{\theta})
$$

## 2.5 Sequential Feedback Mechanisms

Sequential feedback can be realized in three escalating ways:

| Mechanism | Description | Use Case |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **Single-step TD** | Feedback flows one step back via $\delta_t$ | Standard DQN |
| **n-step Returns** | Feedback aggregates $n$ future rewards: $R_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} + \gamma^n \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+n},a')$ | Faster credit assignment |
| **Recurrent / Transformer Memory** | Hidden state $h_t$ summarizes the entire history $(s_0,a_0,\ldots,s_t)$ | POMDPs, long-horizon tasks |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **must-memorize** formula set for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic.

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | KTU Use |
|------------------|----------------------|---------|
| Bellman Optimality | $Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a')]$ | Define optimal Q |
| Q-Learning Update | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) \right]$ | Tabular baseline |
| DQN Loss | $L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}[(y_i - Q(s_i,a_i;\boldsymbol{\theta}))^2]$ where $y_i = r_i + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s'_i,a';\boldsymbol{\theta}^-)$ | Train network |
| TD Error | $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1},a';\boldsymbol{\theta}^-) - Q(s_t,a_t;\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | Core learning signal |
| n-step Target | $R_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} + \gamma^n \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+n},a';\boldsymbol{\theta}^-)$ | Multi-step credit |
| Target Update (Hard) | $\boldsymbol{\theta}^- \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta}$ every $C$ steps | Stabilization |
| Target Update (Soft / Polyak) | $\boldsymbol{\theta}^- \leftarrow \tau \boldsymbol{\theta} + (1-\tau)\boldsymbol{\theta}^-$ with $\tau \ll 1$ | Smooth update |
| Epsilon-Greedy | $a = \arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ w.p. $1-\varepsilon$, else random | Exploration |
| $\varepsilon$ Decay | $\varepsilon_t = \varepsilon_{\min} + (\varepsilon_{\max} - \varepsilon_{\min}) e^{-\lambda t}$ | Schedule |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

Value-based DRL with sequential feedback is the workhorse behind:

- **Autonomous Game Playing** (Atari, Go via AlphaGo) — sequential score-based feedback.
- **Robotic Manipulation** — delayed reward from task completion.
- **Dialogue Systems** — feedback from user satisfaction after long exchanges.
- **Network Packet Routing** — delayed throughput/latency signals.
- **Algorithmic Trading** — delayed PnL as a sparse reward.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Bellman Recursion to TD Error

We begin from the Bellman optimality operator $\mathcal{T}$ acting on any action-value function $Q$:

$$
\mathcal{T}Q(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{s' \sim P(\cdot \vert s,a)}\left[\, r(s,a) + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') \,\right]
$$

The optimal Q-function $Q^*$ is the unique fixed point of $\mathcal{T}$, i.e., $Q^* = \mathcal{T}Q^*$.

Subtracting $Q(s,a)$ from both sides of the Bellman equation:

$$
Q^*(s,a) - Q(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\left[\, r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a') - Q(s,a) \,\right]
$$

The right-hand side is the **Bellman Residual**. For a sample transition $(s,a,r,s')$, the sample estimate of this residual is the **TD error**:

$$
\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1},a';\boldsymbol{\theta}^-) - Q(s_t, a_t;\boldsymbol{\theta})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The TD error $\delta_t$ is the engine of sequential feedback. It tells the network *by how much* its current estimate for $(s_t, a_t)$ is off, given what it knows about the *next* state $s_{t+1}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Closed-Form Loss Minimization (for Linear Case)

Suppose the Q-function is linear: $Q(s,a;\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\phi}(s,a)^\top \boldsymbol{\theta}$, where $\boldsymbol{\phi}$ is a feature vector.

The DQN loss becomes:

$$
L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}\left[\, \left( y_i - \boldsymbol{\phi}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\theta} \right)^2 \,\right]
$$

where $y_i = r_i + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s'_i, a'; \boldsymbol{\theta}^-)$ is treated as a constant target w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.

Differentiate and set to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) &= \mathbb{E}\left[\, -2 \boldsymbol{\phi}_i \left( y_i - \boldsymbol{\phi}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\theta} \right) \,\right] = 0 \\
\mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\phi}_i y_i] &= \mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\phi}_i \boldsymbol{\phi}_i^\top]\, \boldsymbol{\theta} \\
\boldsymbol{\theta}^* &= \left( \mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\phi}_i \boldsymbol{\phi}_i^\top] \right)^{-1} \mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\phi}_i y_i]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **normal equation** for the linear case, confirming the DQN update is a stochastic gradient step toward the optimal $\boldsymbol{\theta}^*$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Gradient of the DQN Loss (Non-Linear Case)

For a deep network, the loss is:

$$
L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - Q(s_i, a_i; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \right)^2
$$

Differentiate term by term. Let $e_i = y_i - Q(s_i, a_i; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ be the TD error for sample $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} \left( y_i - Q(s_i, a_i; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \right)^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 2 \left( y_i - Q(s_i, a_i; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \right) \cdot \left( - \frac{\partial Q(s_i, a_i; \boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} \right) \\
&= -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} e_i \cdot \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} Q(s_i, a_i; \boldsymbol{\theta})
\end{aligned}
$$

Using gradient descent with learning rate $\eta$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta} + \eta \cdot \frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} e_i \cdot \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} Q(s_i, a_i; \boldsymbol{\theta})
$$

The factor $e_i$ is the *sequential feedback signal* back-propagated to update parameters for action $a_i$ in state $s_i$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Convergence of Q-Learning under Sequential Feedback

For **tabular Q-Learning**, Robbins-Monro conditions on the step-size $\alpha_t$ guarantee convergence:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t = \infty, \quad \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2 < \infty
$$

The update rule:

$$
Q_{t+1}(s_t, a_t) = Q_t(s_t, a_t) + \alpha_t \left[\, r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_t(s_{t+1}, a') - Q_t(s_t, a_t) \,\right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **deep (non-linear) Q-Learning**, convergence is **not** guaranteed in general. The target network and replay buffer are *practical heuristics* that empirically stabilize training by reducing the **deadly triad**: bootstrapping + off-policy + function approximation.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Stability Mechanisms

| Source of Instability | Symptom | Mitigation Mechanism |
|----------------------|---------|----------------------|
| Correlated sequential samples | Catastrophic forgetting | **Experience Replay** (shuffle) |
| Moving TD target | Divergence / oscillation | **Target Network** (delayed update) |
| Over-estimation bias | Inflated Q-values | **Double DQN** (decoupled selection & evaluation) |
| POMDP / partial observability | Non-Markov perception | **Recurrent / Attention Memory** |
| Sparse delayed reward | Slow credit assignment | **n-step returns** |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Mathematical Justification of Sequential Feedback

| Question | Mathematical Answer |
|----------|---------------------|
| Where does the gradient signal come from? | The TD target $y_i = r_i + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s'_i, a'; \boldsymbol{\theta}^-)$ encodes future returns. |
| Why is it called *sequential*? | Because $y_i$ depends on $Q(s'_{i})$, which itself depends on $Q(s''_i)$, recursively, propagating reward through the chain. |
| Why is it called *feedback*? | Because $r$ observed at time $t+1$ is fed back to update the value of $(s_t, a_t)$. |
| What breaks the sequence? | **Experience Replay** (samples drawn i.i.d. from buffer) — by design! |
| How is correlation restored? | Through the **Bellman backup**, which links consecutive states via $Q(s', \cdot)$. |

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (Conceptual)

Consider a 2-step MDP: $s_0 \xrightarrow{a_0, r=+1} s_1 \xrightarrow{a_1, r=+2} s_2$ (terminal). Let $\gamma = 0.9$.

**Step 1 — Compute TD target at $s_1$:**

$$
y_1 = r_1 + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_2, a'; \boldsymbol{\theta}^-) = 2 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 2
$$

(since $s_2$ is terminal, $Q(s_2, \cdot) = 0$)

**Step 2 — Update $Q(s_1, a_1)$ with $\alpha = 0.5$, $Q_{\text{old}}(s_1, a_1) = 0$:**

$$
Q_{\text{new}}(s_1, a_1) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot (2 - 0) = 1.0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute TD target at $s_0$ using updated $Q$:**

$$
y_0 = r_0 + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_1, a'; \boldsymbol{\theta}^-) = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 1.0 = 1.9
$$

**Step 4 — Update $Q(s_0, a_0)$, $Q_{\text{old}} = 0$:**

$$
Q_{\text{new}}(s_0, a_0) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot (1.9 - 0) = 0.95
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The reward $r=+2$ at step 1 was eventually "credited" to $Q(s_0, a_0)$ (which moved from $0$ to $0.95$), **two steps backward in the sequence**. This is *sequential feedback* in action.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level DQN Agent with Sequential Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Env[Environment] -->|state s_t| Agent
    Agent[RL Agent] -->|action a_t| Env
    Env -->|reward r_t+1| Agent
    Agent -->|store tuple| Buffer[(Experience Replay Buffer D)]
    Buffer -->|sample minibatch| Train[Trainer]
    Online[Online Network Q theta] -->|Q s,a;theta| Train
    Target[Target Network Q theta-] -->|Q s',a';theta-| Train
    Train -->|TD error delta| Online
    Train -.->|periodically copy| Target
```

## 4.2 Sequential Feedback Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State s_t] --> B[Encoder CNN/MLP]
    B --> C[Q-Head Q s,a;theta]
    C --> D[Action Selection epsilon-greedy]
    D --> E[Environment Step]
    E --> F[Reward r_t+1]
    E --> G[Next State s_t+1]
    F --> H[(Replay Buffer)]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Minibatch Sampling]
    I --> J[Compute TD Target y]
    J --> K[Loss L theta]
    K --> L[Gradient Descent on theta]
    L --> C
    J --> M[Target Network Q theta-]
    M --> J
    L -.->|copy every C steps| M
```

## 4.3 Sequential Feedback Timeline (Bellman Backup)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Env as Environment
    participant Agt as Agent
    participant Buf as Replay Buffer
    participant Net as Online Net
    participant Tgt as Target Net
    Env->>Agt: s_t
    Agt->>Env: a_t
    Env->>Agt: r_t+1, s_t+1
    Agt->>Buf: store s_t,a_t,r,s_t+1
    Buf->>Net: sample minibatch
    Net->>Tgt: query Q s',a';theta-
    Tgt-->>Net: target values
    Net->>Net: compute L theta
    Net->>Net: gradient update theta
    Note over Tgt: every C steps theta- <- theta
```

## 4.4 Information Flow of Sequential Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Past
        S0[s0] --> A0[a0] --> S1[s1]
    end
    subgraph Present
        S1 --> A1[a1] --> S2[s2]
    end
    subgraph Future
        S2 --> A2[a2] --> S3[s3]
    end
    R2[r2] -.feedback.-> A1
    R3[r3] -.feedback.-> A2
    R3 -.feedback.-> A1
    R3 -.feedback.-> A0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The arrows labeled "feedback" represent the *backward flow* of the TD error / Bellman backup. The deeper the reward is in the future, the more state-action pairs it eventually updates — this is the *sequential* nature of the credit assignment.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Deep Q-Network (DQN) loss function and explain the role of the target network in stabilizing its training.**

**Model Answer:**

The DQN loss is:

$$
L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{(s,a,r,s')\sim \mathcal{D}}\left[\, \left( r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a';\boldsymbol{\theta}^-) - Q(s,a;\boldsymbol{\theta}) \right)^2 \,\right]
$$

The target network $Q(s,a;\boldsymbol{\theta}^-)$ provides a **stable TD target** $y = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a';\boldsymbol{\theta}^-)$. Because $\boldsymbol{\theta}^-$ is updated only every $C$ steps (hard update) or slowly (soft update), the target does not shift at every gradient step, preventing the oscillating/divergent behavior that arises when the network chases its own rapidly changing predictions — a manifestation of the *deadly triad* (bootstrapping + off-policy + function approximation).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "target network reduces overfitting." This is **incorrect**. The target network's role is to **stabilize bootstrapping**, not to regularize. Marks are deducted for conflating the two.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Experience Replay and why is it critical for value-based deep reinforcement learning?**

**Model Answer:**

**Experience Replay** is a mechanism in which the agent stores every transition tuple $(s_t, a_t, r_{t+1}, s_{t+1})$ in a finite buffer $\mathcal{D}$, and during training draws **mini-batches uniformly at random** from $\mathcal{D}$ to compute the gradient of the DQN loss.

It is critical because:

1. **Breaks temporal correlation** between consecutive samples (a requirement for SGD to converge).
2. **Improves sample efficiency** by allowing each transition to be used for multiple gradient updates.
3. **Reduces variance** of the gradient by averaging over diverse samples.
4. **Stabilizes the deadly triad** by combining off-policy data with stable targets.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often claim "replay buffer stores optimal trajectories." It does **not** — it stores *all* observed trajectories, including suboptimal ones, because the agent is still exploring. The optimization comes from the **TD backup**, not from stored optimality.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** — *Module 4, Choice A*

**(a)** [7 Marks] **Derive the Bellman optimality equation for action values. Show how it leads to the Q-Learning update rule, and explain why Q-Learning is classified as an off-policy algorithm.**

**(b)** [7 Marks] **Explain the architecture of a Deep Q-Network (DQN) agent with sequential feedback. With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the roles of (i) the online network, (ii) the target network, (iii) the experience replay buffer, and (iv) the $\varepsilon$-greedy action selector. State the loss function explicitly.**

---

### Model Solution for Q(a)

**Step 1 — Define the action-value function.** The action-value of policy $\pi$ is:

$$
Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[\, \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, s_t = s, a_t = a \,\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Unroll one step using the law of iterated expectation:**

$$
Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{s',r}\left[\, r + \gamma \mathbb{E}_{a' \sim \pi}\left[ Q^\pi(s', a') \right] \,\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Take the optimal policy $\pi^* = \arg\max_\pi Q^\pi$:**

$$
Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{s'}\left[\, r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \,\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a sample-based stochastic update (Q-Learning).** Given a sample $(s,a,r,s')$, the update is:

$$
Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \left[\, r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) \,\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Off-policy justification:** The update uses $\max_{a'} Q(s',a')$ (the *greedy* action w.r.t. current $Q$), regardless of the action actually executed by the behavior policy (e.g., $\varepsilon$-greedy). Hence the *target* policy is greedy, while the *behavior* policy is exploratory. This is the definition of **off-policy learning**.

---

### Model Solution for Q(b)

**Architecture of a DQN agent with sequential feedback:**

1. **Online Network** $Q(s,a;\boldsymbol{\theta})$:
   - Outputs Q-values for all actions given state $s$.
   - Parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ are updated at every training step by gradient descent on the DQN loss.

2. **Target Network** $Q(s,a;\boldsymbol{\theta}^-)$:
   - A copy of the online network with frozen parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}^-$.
   - Computes the TD target $y = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a';\boldsymbol{\theta}^-)$.
   - Updated via **hard copy** every $C$ steps: $\boldsymbol{\theta}^- \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta}$.

3. **Experience Replay Buffer** $\mathcal{D}$:
   - Stores transitions $(s_t, a_t, r_{t+1}, s_{t+1})$ in a cyclic buffer of capacity $N$.
   - Each training step samples a random minibatch of size $B$.

4. **$\varepsilon$-Greedy Action Selector**:
   - With probability $1-\varepsilon$, picks $a^* = \arg\max_a Q(s,a;\boldsymbol{\theta})$.
   - With probability $\varepsilon$, picks a random action.
   - $\varepsilon$ decays from a high value (e.g., $1.0$) to a low value (e.g., $0.01$).

**Loss Function:**

$$
L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{(s,a,r,s')\sim \mathcal{D}} \left[\, \left( r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a';\boldsymbol{\theta}^-) - Q(s,a;\boldsymbol{\theta}) \right)^2 \,\right]
$$

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1):**

> Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the complete information flow.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is to write the target as $r + \gamma Q(s', a^*; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ with the *online* network. This is **Double DQN**, not vanilla DQN. KTU expects the **standard DQN** with $\max_{a'} Q(s',a';\boldsymbol{\theta}^-)$.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

### **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** — *Module 4, Choice B*

**(a)** [7 Marks] **Explain the concept of sequential feedback in value-based deep reinforcement learning. How does the Bellman backup propagate the reward signal backward through a trajectory? Use a 3-step numerical example with $\gamma = 0.9$ to demonstrate.**

**(b)** [7 Marks] **Compare tabular Q-Learning with Deep Q-Learning. Discuss in detail the three components that make DQN stable: (i) experience replay, (ii) target network, (iii) reward clipping / gradient clipping. Mention any one limitation of DQN and its standard remedy.**

---

### Model Solution for Q(a)

**Concept of Sequential Feedback:**

Sequential feedback is the mechanism by which a reward observed at time $t+n$ is propagated backward to update the values of all preceding state-action pairs $(s_t, a_t), (s_{t+1}, a_{t+1}), \ldots, (s_{t+n-1}, a_{t+n-1})$ in the trajectory. The Bellman equation provides the formal recursive vehicle:

$$
Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \left[\, r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t) \,\right]
$$

The TD target $r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a')$ depends on $Q(s_{t+1}, \cdot)$, which in turn is trained using the next reward, and so on. This recursive structure is the *sequential* nature of the feedback.

**Numerical Example (3-step trajectory):**

Trajectory: $s_0 \xrightarrow{a_0, r_1=+1} s_1 \xrightarrow{a_1, r_2=+2} s_2 \xrightarrow{a_2, r_3=+3} s_3$ (terminal). $\gamma = 0.9$, $\alpha = 0.5$, all $Q(\cdot) = 0$ initially.

**Step 1 — Update $Q(s_2, a_2)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_2 &= r_3 + \gamma \cdot 0 = 3 \\
Q(s_2, a_2) &\leftarrow 0 + 0.5(3 - 0) = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Update $Q(s_1, a_1)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= r_2 + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_2, a') = 2 + 0.9 \cdot 1.5 = 3.35 \\
Q(s_1, a_1) &\leftarrow 0 + 0.5(3.35 - 0) = 1.675
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Update $Q(s_0, a_0)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_0 &= r_1 + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_1, a') = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 1.675 = 2.5075 \\
Q(s_0, a_0) &\leftarrow 0 + 0.5(2.5075 - 0) \approx 1.254
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The reward $r_3 = +3$ from the **furthest future** has propagated all the way back to influence $Q(s_0, a_0)$ — this is sequential feedback.

---

### Model Solution for Q(b)

**Tabular vs Deep Q-Learning:**

| Aspect | Tabular Q-Learning | Deep Q-Learning (DQN) |
|--------|--------------------|------------------------|
| Representation | Lookup table $Q[s,a]$ | Neural network $Q(s,a;\boldsymbol{\theta})$ |
| State Space | Discrete, small | Continuous or very large |
| Update Frequency | Every step | Mini-batch SGD |
| Stability | Guaranteed (under RM conditions) | Empirical (not guaranteed) |
| Generalization | None (one value per state) | Function approximation across states |

**Three Stability Components:**

1. **Experience Replay** — Decouples sample correlation from temporal sequence, reduces variance.
2. **Target Network** — Slow update of $\boldsymbol{\theta}^-$ prevents bootstrapping divergence.
3. **Gradient Clipping** — Clips $\|\nabla L\|$ to a maximum (e.g., $10$) to prevent exploding gradients in deep networks.
4. **Reward Clipping** (optional) — Rescales rewards to $[-1, +1]$ so the network learns a uniform scale across tasks.

**Limitation of DQN & Remedy:**

- **Limitation:** Over-estimation bias due to the $\max$ operator in the TD target.
- **Remedy:** **Double DQN (DDQN)** — decouples action *selection* from action *evaluation*:

$$
y^{\text{DDQN}} = r + \gamma Q\!\left(s', \arg\max_{a'} Q(s', a'; \boldsymbol{\theta}); \boldsymbol{\theta}^-\right)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "DQN is unstable because of the learning rate." The instability actually comes from the **deadly triad** (function approximation + bootstrapping + off-policy), not the learning rate. Marks are awarded for naming the triad explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4**

- ✅ **Value-based RL** = learns the Q-function, derives policy by $\arg\max_a Q(s,a)$.
- ✅ **Bellman Optimality:** $Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a')]$ — *the* foundation.
- ✅ **Q-Learning Update:** $Q \leftarrow Q + \alpha \delta$ with $\delta = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)$.
- ✅ **DQN** = Q-Learning + deep neural network as function approximator.
- ✅ **Three Pillars of DQN Stability:**
  1. **Experience Replay Buffer** $\mathcal{D}$ (FIFO, capacity $\sim 10^6$).
  2. **Target Network** $Q(\cdot;\boldsymbol{\theta}^-)$ (frozen, hard/soft update).
  3. **TD Target** $y = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a';\boldsymbol{\theta}^-)$.
- ✅ **Loss Function:** MSE between online Q and frozen TD target.
- ✅ **Sequential Feedback** = backward propagation of reward through Bellman backup.
- ✅ **Deadly Triad:** bootstrapping + off-policy + function approximation → solved (empirically) by replay + target net.
- ✅ **$\varepsilon$-Greedy** for exploration; $\varepsilon$ decays over training.
- ✅ **n-step Returns** propagate reward faster; $R_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} + \gamma^n \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+n},a';\boldsymbol{\theta}^-)$.
- ✅ **Double DQN** fixes over-estimation; **Dueling DQN** separates value and advantage streams.
- ✅ **Recurrent / Attention Memory** enables handling of POMDPs (partial observability).
- ✅ **Convergence:** Tabular Q-Learning converges; **deep Q-Learning does not have a convergence guarantee** — empirical stabilization only.
- ✅ **Real-world applications:** Atari (Mnih et al., 2015), autonomous control, dialogue, trading, robotics.
- ✅ **Examiner's Hot Spots:** DQN loss derivation, role of target network (not "regularization"), why replay is needed (not "to store optimal"), and identifying the deadly triad.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Value Based Reinforcement Learning: Deep RL Agents with Sequential Feedback.**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education guidelines. All formulas are mathematically standard; no fabricated references used.*
