topics.title: Evaluative Feedback
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Evaluative Feedback

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Evaluative Feedback

**Evaluative Feedback** in Reinforcement Learning (RL) refers to the fundamental learning paradigm in which an agent must learn a *policy* (mapping from situations to actions) by *trial-and-error* interaction with a dynamic environment, relying solely on a scalar **reward signal** (evaluative feedback) that evaluates how *good* or *bad* the chosen action was, but does not instruct the agent on *which* action to take.

In the strict KTU/RL-theory sense, evaluative feedback is the feedback signal that is **purely evaluative** — it tells the agent *how well* it has done, but not *what the correct action* would have been. This is the defining distinction between RL and *supervised learning* (which provides instructive feedback telling the agent exactly what to do).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):**
> Evaluative Feedback is the cornerstone of the **n-Armed Bandit Problem**, which serves as the simplest, purest mathematical abstraction of the RL dilemma — estimation vs. action, learning vs. doing.

The mathematical environment is defined as a tuple of expected (true) values:

$$q_*(a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\bigl[R_t \,\big|\, A_t = a\bigr] \;=\; \sum_{r} p(r \mid a)\, r$$

for every action $a \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$.

### 1.2 The n-Armed Bandit — The Canonical Setting

The textbook abstraction for evaluative feedback is the **n-armed bandit problem**:

* You are repeatedly faced with a choice among **n** different actions (the "arms" of n slot machines).
* After each action $A_t$, you receive a numerical reward $R_t$ drawn from a (usually stationary) probability distribution that depends on the chosen action $a$.
* The objective: maximize the **expected total reward** over a long horizon $T$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology — Stationarity Assumption:**
> In the canonical setup, the reward distributions for each action are assumed **stationary** (do not change over time). This assumption is later relaxed in *non-stationary* problems where a constant step-size is used.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Restaurant Dilemma"

Imagine you have just moved to a new city with **100 unfamiliar restaurants**. Each day at dinner time you must pick exactly one. You discover *after eating* whether the meal was great, average, or terrible. Over months, you must decide:

* **Stick with the best-known restaurant so far** (Exploitation) — guaranteed decent meal based on past evidence.
* **Try a new or rarely-visited restaurant** (Exploration) — risky, but might reveal a hidden gem.

This tension — **Exploitation vs. Exploration** — is the *soul* of evaluative feedback. You never get *prescriptive* feedback ("you *should* have gone to Restaurant 7"). You only get *evaluative* feedback ("Restaurant 23 gave you 4 stars tonight"). The brain of the agent must therefore:

1. **Estimate** the unknown reward distribution of each action from experience.
2. **Choose** the next action that strikes the best balance between exploiting the best-known action and exploring lesser-known ones.

### 1.4 Why "Evaluative" Matters — Comparison with Supervised Learning

| Aspect | Supervised Learning (Instructive) | Reinforcement Learning (Evaluative) |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback type | Correct action / label provided | Scalar reward only |
| Information content | Tells *what* to do | Tells *how good* the result was |
| Data assumption | i.i.d. samples from a labeled set | Sequential, correlated, agent-influenced |
| Agent's role | Passive learner | Active decision-maker (affects next input) |
| Example | Image classification | Playing chess, robotic control |

The *evaluative* nature is what makes RL harder in one sense (no "right answer" to copy), but also far more powerful in sequential decision-making where the agent's actions change future inputs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Estimation-Action Decomposition

The bandit problem is logically decomposed into two intertwined sub-problems that occur *simultaneously* at every time step:

**(A) Action-Value Estimation** — build a model of which actions are good.

**(B) Action Selection Policy** — use the model to pick the next action.

These two sub-problems are coupled: better estimates improve choices; better choices concentrate data on promising actions, which improves estimates.

### 2.2 Action-Value Methods (Sample-Average Estimation)

**Definition:** The *sample-average* estimate of action $a$'s value after it has been chosen $k$ times is:

$$Q_t(a) \;=\; \frac{\text{sum of rewards when }a\text{ was taken prior to }t}{\text{number of times }a\text{ was taken prior to }t} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i}{k}$$

As $k \to \infty$, the **Law of Large Numbers** guarantees $Q_t(a) \to q_*(a)$.

**Greedy action selection** is then:

$$A_t \;=\; \arg\max_{a} Q_t(a)$$

**Why does this work?** Each $Q_t(a)$ is an *unbiased estimator* of $q_*(a)$. By picking the action with the largest sample mean, we are performing **greedy optimization on noisy estimates** — a special case of the *Follow-The-Leader* algorithm.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept — Exploration Bonus in Greedy Methods:**
> Pure greedy gets stuck on the first action that looks marginally best. The whole *point* of evaluative-feedback methods like ε-greedy, UCB, and gradient bandits is to inject **systematic exploration** to escape local optima.

### 2.3 The Exploration–Exploitation Tradeoff (Formal)

* **Exploitation:** Pick the current greedy action to maximize immediate expected reward.
* **Exploration:** Pick a non-greedy action to improve its estimate (and possibly discover a better action).

Theorem (informal): A purely greedy policy is **not asymptotically optimal** on bandit problems because it ignores actions whose estimates are uncertain but whose true means might be highest. An optimal strategy must **explore infinitely often** while still exploiting most of the time.

### 2.4 The ε-Greedy Action Selection

With probability $1 - \varepsilon$ pick the greedy action; with probability $\varepsilon$ pick uniformly at random:

$$A_t = \begin{cases} \arg\max_a Q_t(a) & \text{with probability } 1 - \varepsilon \\ \text{random action} & \text{with probability } \varepsilon \end{cases}$$

* Small $\varepsilon$ (e.g., 0.01 or 0.1) ensures continual exploration without sacrificing much reward.
* Asymptotically, ε-greedy **does** converge to picking the optimal action with probability > $1 - \varepsilon$ (still sub-optimal vs. true Bayes-optimal).

### 2.5 Optimistic Initial Values

Set all initial action-value estimates to an **optimistic** constant, e.g., $Q_1(a) = +5$, even when rewards are in $[0, 1]$.

Mechanism: the agent is "disappointed" on its first try of each action, which encourages systematic exploration of *every* action before settling on the truly best one.

* **Pro:** Simple, encourages wide initial exploration.
* **Con:** Not well-suited for *non-stationary* problems (exploration drive dies out as estimates become realistic).

### 2.6 Upper-Confidence-Bound (UCB) Action Selection

A more principled exploration strategy that adds a *confidence-interval bonus* favoring actions whose value is **uncertain**:

$$A_t \;=\; \arg\max_{a} \left[\, Q_t(a) \;+\; c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}\,\right]$$

where:
* $N_t(a)$ = number of times action $a$ has been selected up to time $t$.
* $c > 0$ = degree of exploration (tunes the confidence width).
* $\ln t$ grows slowly; $N_t(a)$ in the denominator means *less-tried* actions get a bigger bonus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — UCB intuition:**
> The square-root term is a *one-sided* confidence bound on the uncertainty of $Q_t(a)$. The action with the highest plausible upper bound is chosen. As $N_t(a)$ grows, the bonus shrinks, and the algorithm naturally shifts from exploration to exploitation.

### 2.7 Gradient Bandit Algorithm

Instead of estimating action *values*, maintain a numerical **preference** $H_t(a)$ for each action, updated via stochastic gradient ascent on expected reward:

**Action probability (Softmax):**

$$\pi_t(a) \;=\; \Pr\{A_t = a\} \;=\; \frac{e^{H_t(a)}}{\sum_{b=1}^{n} e^{H_t(b)}}$$

**Preference update:**

$$H_{t+1}(a) \;=\; H_t(a) \;+\; \alpha \bigl(R_t - B_t\bigr)\bigl(\mathbf{1}_{a = A_t} - \pi_t(a)\bigr)$$

where:
* $\alpha > 0$ = step-size (learning rate).
* $B_t$ = **baseline** (e.g., running average of rewards, including possibly the time step itself as in $R_t \in [0,1]$).
* $\mathbf{1}_{a = A_t}$ = 1 if $a$ was the action taken, else 0.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a baseline $B_t$?**
> Subtracting $B_t$ converts the raw reward into a *relative* advantage signal. If $R_t > B_t$ the chosen action is reinforced; if $R_t < B_t$ the action is *de-pushed*. This dramatically reduces variance of the gradient estimate.

### 2.8 Reinforcement Comparison (RC)

A special form of gradient bandit where the action preference is updated *only* for the chosen action against a running average baseline:

$$p_{t+1} \;=\; p_t \;+\; \beta\,(R_t - \bar{r}_t)$$

$$\bar{r}_{t+1} \;=\; \bar{r}_t \;+\; \alpha\,(R_t - \bar{r}_t)$$

The probability of action $a$ is given by a soft-max rule over $p_a$:

$$\pi_t(a) \;=\; \frac{e^{p_t(a)}}{\sum_b e^{p_t(b)}}$$

### 2.9 Associative Search (Contextual Bandits — Brief)

In the *associative* (contextual) setting, the agent perceives a state $S_t$ and must learn a *policy* mapping states to actions. This is the bridge between pure evaluative bandit feedback and full RL. KTU Module 4 introduces it as a stepping-stone toward Markov Decision Processes (covered later in Module 5).

### 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Method | Core Equation | Key Symbol | Notes / Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | True value of action $a$ | $q_*(a) = \mathbb{E}[R_t \mid A_t = a]$ | $q_*(a)$ | Unobservable target |
| 2 | Sample-average estimate | $Q_t(a) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i}{k}$ | $Q_t(a)$ | Unbiased, LLN convergence |
| 3 | Greedy selection | $A_t = \arg\max_a Q_t(a)$ | $A_t$ | Pure exploitation |
| 4 | ε-greedy | $A_t = \text{random}$ w.p. $\varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon$ | Simple uniform exploration |
| 5 | Incremental update | $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \frac{1}{n}[R_n - Q_n]$ | $n$ | Memory-efficient |
| 6 | Constant step-size | $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha [R_n - Q_n]$, $\alpha \in (0,1)$ | $\alpha$ | For non-stationary problems |
| 7 | UCB selection | $A_t = \arg\max_a\!\left[Q_t(a) + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}\right]$ | $c$ | Principled exploration |
| 8 | Softmax (Boltzmann) | $\pi_t(a) = \frac{e^{H_t(a)}}{\sum_b e^{H_t(b)}}$ | $H_t(a)$ | Gradient bandit core |
| 9 | Gradient preference | $H_{t+1}(a) = H_t(a) + \alpha(R_t - B_t)(\mathbf{1}_{a=A_t} - \pi_t(a))$ | $B_t$ | Baseline reduces variance |
| 10 | Reinforcement comparison | $p_{t+1} = p_t + \beta(R_t - \bar{r}_t)$ | $\beta$ | Special case of #9 |
| 11 | Optimistic initialization | $Q_1(a) = \text{large positive constant}$ | $Q_1$ | Encourages early exploration |
| 12 | Average reward baseline | $B_t = \frac{1}{t}\sum_{i=1}^{t} R_i$ | $B_t$ | Common choice for gradient |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> * **Online advertising** (A/B/n-testing) — bandit algorithms choose which ad to show with minimal regret.
> * **Clinical trials** — adaptive allocation of patients to treatments.
> * **Hyper-parameter tuning in deep learning** — successive halving, Hyperband, Bayesian optimization.
> * **Robotic control** — evaluative feedback in the form of task-completion reward.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sample-Average Estimate ⇒ Incremental Update

**Step 1 — Start with the sample-average definition.**

Let $k$ denote the number of times action $a$ has been selected so far. The sample-average estimate of its value is:

$$Q_k \;=\; \frac{R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_{k-1} + R_k}{k}$$

**Step 2 — Split the sum into the previous estimate plus the new term.**

$$Q_k \;=\; \frac{1}{k}\Biggl[\sum_{i=1}^{k-1} R_i \;+\; R_k\Biggr]$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that $Q_{k-1} = \frac{1}{k-1}\sum_{i=1}^{k-1} R_i$, hence $\sum_{i=1}^{k-1} R_i = (k-1)\,Q_{k-1}$.**

$$Q_k \;=\; \frac{1}{k}\Big[(k-1)\,Q_{k-1} \;+\; R_k\Big]$$

**Step 4 — Expand and group terms.**

$$Q_k \;=\; \frac{k-1}{k}\,Q_{k-1} \;+\; \frac{1}{k}\,R_k$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\frac{k-1}{k} = 1 - \frac{1}{k}$.**

$$Q_k \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{1}{k}\right)Q_{k-1} \;+\; \frac{1}{k}\,R_k$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange as a "new estimate = old estimate + step × (target − old estimate)".**

$$Q_k \;=\; Q_{k-1} \;+\; \frac{1}{k}\bigl[R_k - Q_{k-1}\bigr]$$

**Step 7 — This is the canonical incremental update rule.** Renaming $k \to n+1$ and $k-1 \to n$:

$$\boxed{\,Q_{n+1} \;=\; Q_n \;+\; \frac{1}{n}\bigl[R_n - Q_n\bigr]\,}$$

**Interpretation:**
* $R_n - Q_n$ is the **error** (surprise) between the observed reward and the prior estimate.
* The step size $\frac{1}{n}$ shrinks with $n$, giving older observations less weight.
* This is the simplest instance of the general stochastic-approximation update: $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha_n\bigl[R_n - Q_n\bigr]$.

### 3.2 Derivation: From Sample-Average to Constant Step-Size (Non-Stationary)

**Step 1 — In a non-stationary problem, recent rewards are more informative than old ones.** So we *want* the step-size to be a constant $\alpha$ rather than $1/n$.

**Step 2 — Generalize the incremental update.**

$$Q_{n+1} \;=\; Q_n \;+\; \alpha\bigl[R_n - Q_n\bigr] \quad\text{with}\quad \alpha \in (0,1)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the recursion to expose the weighted history.**

$$Q_{n+1} \;=\; \alpha R_n \;+\; (1-\alpha)Q_n$$

$$= \alpha R_n + (1-\alpha)\bigl[\alpha R_{n-1} + (1-\alpha)Q_{n-1}\bigr]$$

$$= \alpha R_n + (1-\alpha)\alpha R_{n-1} + (1-\alpha)^2 Q_{n-1}$$

**Step 4 — Unroll completely.**

$$Q_{n+1} \;=\; (1-\alpha)^n Q_1 \;+\; \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{n}(1-\alpha)^{n-i}\,R_i$$

**Step 5 — Observe the exponentially decaying weights.**

The weight on $R_i$ is $\alpha(1-\alpha)^{n-i}$. This is a **geometric / exponential weighting** scheme. The half-life of an observation is $\tau_{1/2} = \log(0.5)/\log(1-\alpha) \approx 0.693/\alpha$ for small $\alpha$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A constant step-size implements *exponential recency-weighted averaging*, ideal when the reward distribution drifts over time.

### 3.3 Derivation: UCB Upper-Confidence Bound

**Step 1 — Recall Hoeffding's inequality** for bounded random variables in $[0,1]$: the probability that the sample mean deviates from the true mean by more than $\varepsilon$ after $N$ samples decays exponentially:

$$\Pr\bigl[\,|\hat\mu - \mu| > \varepsilon\,\bigr] \;\le\; 2\,e^{-2N\varepsilon^2}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\varepsilon$ given a probability threshold $p$.** Set $p = t^{-4}$ (a slowly decaying threshold) to get the *one-sided* upper bound on $|\hat\mu - \mu|$:

$$2\,e^{-2N\varepsilon^2} \;=\; t^{-4} \;\Rightarrow\; \varepsilon \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2\ln t}{N}}$$

**Step 3 — Therefore with high probability**, the true value $q_*(a)$ lies below the upper end of a one-sided confidence interval:

$$q_*(a) \;\le\; Q_t(a) \;+\; \sqrt{\frac{2\ln t}{N_t(a)}}$$

**Step 4 — The UCB action-selection rule** simply picks the action with the largest plausible upper bound:

$$\boxed{\,A_t \;=\; \arg\max_{a}\left[\,Q_t(a) + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}\,\right]\,}$$

The constant $c$ absorbs the factor $\sqrt{2}$ in (Step 3) and is treated as a tunable hyper-parameter in practice.

### 3.4 Derivation: Gradient Bandit — Why the $(1_{a=A_t} - \pi_t(a))$ Term

**Step 1 — Define the performance objective** to be maximized: the expected reward at time $t$,

$$\mathbb{E}[R_t] \;=\; \sum_a \pi_t(a)\,q_*(a)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the gradient of $\mathbb{E}[R_t]$ with respect to preference $H_t(a)$** using the chain rule and the Softmax derivative:

$$\frac{\partial \pi_t(b)}{\partial H_t(a)} \;=\; \pi_t(b)\,\bigl[\mathbf{1}_{a=b} - \pi_t(a)\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — So the gradient of the objective is:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathbb{E}[R_t]}{\partial H_t(a)} \;=\; \sum_b q_*(b)\,\pi_t(b)\,\bigl[\mathbf{1}_{a=b} - \pi_t(a)\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Note that $\sum_b \pi_t(b) = 1$**, so the second term simplifies:

$$= \sum_b \pi_t(b)\,q_*(b)\,\mathbf{1}_{a=b} \;-\; \pi_t(a)\sum_b \pi_t(b)\,q_*(b)$$

$$= \pi_t(a)\,q_*(a) \;-\; \pi_t(a)\,\mathbb{E}[R_t]$$

$$= \pi_t(a)\,\bigl[q_*(a) - \mathbb{E}[R_t]\bigr]$$

**Step 5 — Since $q_*(a)$ and $\mathbb{E}[R_t]$ are unknown**, replace them with their unbiased *sample* surrogates: $\hat q_*(A_t) = R_t$ and $\hat{\mathbb{E}}[R_t] = B_t$ (the baseline).

**Step 6 — Stochastic-gradient ascent update** (multiplied by learning rate $\alpha$):

$$\boxed{\,H_{t+1}(a) \;=\; H_t(a) + \alpha\bigl(R_t - B_t\bigr)\bigl(\mathbf{1}_{a=A_t} - \pi_t(a)\bigr)\,}$$

**Interpretation of the update:**

* If $a = A_t$ (the chosen action): update is $\alpha(R_t - B_t)(1 - \pi_t(a))$ — *push preference up* if reward beats baseline, *push down* otherwise.
* If $a \neq A_t$: update is $\alpha(R_t - B_t)(-\pi_t(a)) = -\alpha(R_t - B_t)\pi_t(a)$ — preferences of *un-chosen* actions move in the opposite direction.
* The probabilities always sum to 1, preserving the Softmax normalization invariant.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — ε-Greedy vs. UCB

Consider a 3-armed bandit with true action values $q_*(a_1) = 0.5, \;q_*(a_2) = 0.4, \;q_*(a_3) = 0.9$. Initial estimates $Q_1(a) = 0$ for all $a$. Take $c = 2$, $\varepsilon = 0.1$.

| Step $t$ | Action $A_t$ (UCB) | $N_t(a_1)$ | $N_t(a_2)$ | $N_t(a_3)$ | $Q_t(a_1)$ | $Q_t(a_2)$ | $Q_t(a_3)$ | UCB($a_1$) | UCB($a_2$) | UCB($a_3$) | Chosen |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_1$ (tie, first) | 1 | 0 | 0 | $R_1$ | — | — | $R_1+0$ | $+\infty$ | $+\infty$ | tie-break |
| 2 | $a_2$ or $a_3$ | 1 | 1 | 0 | … | $R_2$ | — | $Q+0.69$ | $Q+0.69$ | $+\infty$ | $a_3$ |
| 3 | $a_3$ if not chosen | 1 | 1 | 1 | … | … | $R_3$ | … | … | … | $a_3$ |
| 4+ | $\arg\max$ UCB | … | … | … | … | … | … | … | … | … | $a_3$ converges |

(For ε-greedy the structure is similar but the unchosen arms are picked uniformly at random with probability $\varepsilon$ — *not* infinitely often on the first try.)

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Method Comparison

| Criterion | Greedy | ε-Greedy | UCB | Gradient Bandit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Asymptotic optimality | No | No (but approaches) | Yes (under conditions) | Yes (under conditions) |
| Exploration type | None | Uniform random | Confidence-bounded | Probability-proportional |
| Needs to store counts $N_t(a)$? | No | No | Yes | No |
| Works for non-stationary? | Poor | With constant $\alpha$ | Poor (without modifications) | Yes (with $\alpha$ tuned) |
| Computation per step | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Variance of choice | Lowest | Medium | Medium | Medium-high |
| Hyper-parameter sensitivity | None | $\varepsilon$ | $c$ | $\alpha$, $B_t$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Evaluative Feedback Loop (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Agent receives state S_t and reward context] --> B[Action-Value Estimator: Q_t a]
    B --> C{Action Selection Policy}
    C -->|with prob 1-epsilon| D[Greedy: argmax Q_t a]
    C -->|with prob epsilon| E[Random action sampling]
    C -->|UCB variant| F[UCB: argmax Q + c sqrt ln t N]
    C -->|Gradient variant| G[Softmax over preferences H_t a]
    D --> H[Execute action A_t in environment]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Environment returns reward R_t and next state S_{t+1}]
    I --> J[Update Module]
    J -->|Sample-average| K[Q_n+1 = Q_n + 1 over n times R_n - Q_n]
    J -->|Constant alpha| L[Q_n+1 = Q_n + alpha R_n - Q_n]
    J -->|UCB bonus| M[Increment N_t a and recompute UCB term]
    J -->|Gradient| N[H_{t+1} = H_t + alpha R_t - B_t indicator - pi]
    K --> B
    L --> B
    M --> C
    N --> G
    I --> A
```

### 4.2 n-Armed Bandit — Per-Step Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step t: Agent chooses A_t] --> B[Environment samples R_t from p r given A_t]
    B --> C[Update estimate Q_t A_t using R_t]
    C --> D[Step t+1: Re-evaluate argmax Q_t+1 a]
    D --> A
```

### 4.3 Exploration–Exploitation Tradeoff Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q[Current estimates Q_t a with uncertainty bars] --> P{Decision rule}
    P -->|Exploit| X[Pick argmax Q_t a]
    P -->|Explore uniform| Y[Pick random arm w.p. epsilon]
    P -->|Explore principled UCB| Z[Pick arm with largest upper CI]
    X --> R[Reward R_t]
    Y --> R
    Z --> R
    R --> U[Shrink uncertainty of chosen arm]
    U --> Q
```

### 4.4 Gradient Bandit Update Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Current preference H_t a] --> B[Softmax: pi_t a = exp H_t a divided by sum exp H_t b]
    B --> C[Sample action A_t from pi_t]
    C --> D[Receive reward R_t]
    D --> E[Compute surprise: R_t - B_t]
    E --> F[Chosen arm: H_t+1 += alpha x surprise x 1 - pi]
    E --> G[Unchosen arms: H_t+1 -= alpha x surprise x pi]
    F --> A
    G --> A
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions**

#### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the n-armed bandit problem and explain why the action values $q_*(a)$ are not directly observable.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **n-armed bandit problem** is a sequential decision-making setting in which, at each of $T$ time steps, the agent must choose one of $n$ available actions and subsequently receives a stochastic reward $R_t$ drawn from a distribution specific to the chosen action. The objective is to maximize the expected cumulative reward over the horizon.

The true action value is defined as $q_*(a) = \mathbb{E}[R_t \mid A_t = a]$, the *expected* reward of arm $a$. It is **not directly observable** because:

* (1 mark) At any single step the agent observes only **one sample** $R_t$ from the distribution of the *chosen* action, never its expectation.
* (1 mark) The other $n-1$ arms are *not* exercised, so no information is gained about them at that step.
* (1 mark) Hence the agent must *estimate* $q_*(a)$ from finite samples using statistical methods (e.g., sample averages, exponential weighting) and the choice of estimation method defines the learning algorithm.

---

#### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the exploration–exploitation dilemma. How does the ε-greedy policy resolve it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **exploration–exploitation dilemma** is the fundamental conflict in evaluative feedback between:

* **Exploitation** — choosing the currently best-known action to maximize immediate reward, and
* **Exploration** — choosing a lesser-known action to gather information that may yield higher long-term reward.

**(2 marks)** The **ε-greedy** policy resolves this dilemma probabilistically: with probability $1 - \varepsilon$ it exploits by choosing $A_t = \arg\max_a Q_t(a)$ (greedy), and with probability $\varepsilon$ it explores by sampling an action uniformly at random. This guarantees that every action is sampled infinitely often in the limit $T \to \infty$ when $\varepsilon > 0$ is fixed, while the bulk of selections still exploit the best-known arm. **(1 mark)** The exploration rate $\varepsilon$ controls the trade-off: a small $\varepsilon$ favours exploitation, a larger $\varepsilon$ favours exploration. Asymptotically the greedy choice dominates and the policy converges to selecting the truly optimal arm with probability $1 - \varepsilon$.

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A(1). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **incremental update rule** for the sample-average action-value estimate. Show that the step-size $1/n$ naturally arises from the sample-average definition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 5-armed bandit with stationary rewards in $[0, 1]$ and an agent using ε-greedy with $\varepsilon = 0.1$, explain why the *first* arm chosen in step 1 is essentially arbitrary, and show by Hoeffding-style reasoning why **UCB action selection would likely explore the other 4 arms within the first 5 steps** while ε-greedy might not. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (A1):**

**(a) Incremental update derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the sample-average definition (2 marks):

$$Q_k \;=\; \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i$$

Split the sum into "previous $k-1$ samples + new sample $R_k$" (1 mark):

$$Q_k \;=\; \frac{1}{k}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{k-1}R_i + R_k\right]$$

Recognize that $Q_{k-1} = \frac{1}{k-1}\sum_{i=1}^{k-1}R_i$, hence the first term equals $(k-1)Q_{k-1}/k$ (1 mark):

$$Q_k \;=\; \frac{k-1}{k}Q_{k-1} + \frac{1}{k}R_k$$

Apply $\frac{k-1}{k} = 1 - \frac{1}{k}$ and rearrange (1 mark):

$$Q_k \;=\; Q_{k-1} + \frac{1}{k}\bigl[R_k - Q_{k-1}\bigr]$$

Conclude with the canonical form and interpret $R_k - Q_{k-1}$ as the prediction error and $1/k$ as the *implicit step-size* that decays as the count grows (1 mark). The general form $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha_n[R_n - Q_n]$ is also acceptable (1 mark).

**(b) UCB vs. ε-greedy early-step exploration (7 marks):**

*Step 1:* In step 1, all $N_t(a) = 0$ and all $Q_1(a)$ are typically equal (e.g., zero). Hence the choice is arbitrary — a tie-break, with no information content. **(1 mark)**

*Step 2:* In step 2, the UCB rule computes $A_2 = \arg\max_a[Q_1(a) + c\sqrt{\ln 2 / N_1(a)}]$. With $N_1(a) = 0$ for $a \neq A_1$, the bonus is $+\infty$ for those arms, forcing UCB to select a previously-unplayed arm. **(2 marks)**

*Step 3:* In general, at step $t$ an arm that has been played $N_t(a) = k$ times has bonus $c\sqrt{\ln t / k}$, which **decreases** as $k$ grows. Hence UCB systematically cycles through arms that have been played the *fewest* times. With $n=5$ arms and at most 5 steps, every arm will be played at least once — a guarantee ε-greedy does not provide (it picks uniformly, so it may repeat some arms while skipping others by chance). **(2 marks)**

*Step 4:* ε-greedy *guarantees* each arm is played infinitely often only as $T \to \infty$, with the *frequency* of any particular arm's play in a finite horizon being a binomial draw. The probability that ε-greedy plays all 5 distinct arms in the first 5 steps is $5!/5^5 \approx 0.038$ — only 3.8% — compared to UCB which deterministically does so. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Question B(1). [KTU University Exam — May 2023 / Model Paper 2024] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the **Gradient Bandit Algorithm** in detail. Starting from the objective $\mathbb{E}[R_t] = \sum_a \pi_t(a)q_*(a)$, derive the preference update rule and explain the role of the **baseline** $B_t$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **sample-average action-value method**, **ε-greedy**, **UCB**, and the **gradient bandit** along three dimensions: (i) asymptotic regret behaviour, (ii) memory/computation per step, and (iii) robustness to non-stationarity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (B1):**

**(a) Gradient Bandit derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):* The gradient bandit maintains a numerical preference $H_t(a)$ for each action $a$ and converts it into a probability via Softmax:

$$\pi_t(a) = \frac{e^{H_t(a)}}{\sum_{b=1}^{n} e^{H_t(b)}}$$

*Step 2 — Performance objective (1 mark):*

$$\mathbb{E}[R_t] = \sum_a \pi_t(a)\,q_*(a)$$

*Step 3 — Softmax derivative (1 mark):* Differentiate $\pi_t(b)$ with respect to $H_t(a)$:

$$\frac{\partial \pi_t(b)}{\partial H_t(a)} = \pi_t(b)\bigl[\mathbf{1}_{a=b} - \pi_t(a)\bigr]$$

*Step 4 — Gradient w.r.t. $H_t(a)$ (1 mark):*

$$\frac{\partial \mathbb{E}[R_t]}{\partial H_t(a)} = \pi_t(a)\bigl[q_*(a) - \mathbb{E}[R_t]\bigr]$$

*Step 5 — Stochastic ascent (1 mark):* Replace unknown $q_*(a)$ by sample $R_t$ and $\mathbb{E}[R_t]$ by baseline $B_t$:

$$H_{t+1}(a) = H_t(a) + \alpha(R_t - B_t)\bigl(\mathbf{1}_{a=A_t} - \pi_t(a)\bigr)$$

*Step 6 — Role of $B_t$ (2 marks):* The baseline **centers the reward signal** so that only rewards *above* (resp. below) the average reinforce (resp. inhibit) the chosen action. This greatly reduces variance of the gradient estimate and is essential for the algorithm's convergence. A common choice is the exponential moving average of past rewards $B_t = B_{t-1} + \alpha(R_{t-1} - B_{t-1})$. If $B_t = 0$, the algorithm reduces to a simpler form whose update direction is dominated by the *magnitude* of the reward rather than its *quality*.

**(b) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Dimension | Sample-Avg + Greedy | ε-Greedy | UCB | Gradient Bandit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Asymptotic regret | $O(T)$ (worst) | $O(T)$ (constant factor) | $O(\ln T)$ (best) | $O(\ln T)$ (best) |
| (ii) Memory/step | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ + counts $N_t(a)$ | $O(n)$ |
| (iii) Non-stationary | Poor (fixed $\alpha=1/n$) | Acceptable (constant $\alpha$) | Poor (designed for stationary) | Acceptable (constant $\alpha$) |

**(i) Asymptotic regret (2 marks):** Greedy and ε-greedy suffer *linear* regret because they re-visit sub-optimal arms with positive probability forever. UCB and gradient bandits achieve *logarithmic* regret under regularity conditions on the gap between best and second-best arms.

**(ii) Memory/computation (2 marks):** All methods are $O(n)$ per step, but UCB additionally tracks $N_t(a)$ for every arm, so its constant memory is slightly higher. The other methods need only the running mean $Q_t(a)$.

**(iii) Non-stationarity (2 marks):** UCB and the sample-average method both shrink their exploration bonus / step-size over time, so once the *true* best arm is found, exploration *vanishes* — they cannot track a drifting optimum. ε-greedy and gradient bandits with constant $\alpha$ continue to explore at a fixed rate and *can* re-discover a new optimum if the distribution shifts.

**Final recommendation (1 mark):** For *stationary* problems, UCB is the principled default. For *non-stationary* problems, gradient bandits with a tuned $\alpha$ and a recency-weighted baseline are the most robust choice.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $Q_t(a)$ with $q_*(a)$** — remember $Q_t(a)$ is the *estimate* (capital Q), $q_*(a)$ is the *true value* (lowercase q). Examiners specifically check this notation discipline.
> 2. **Forgetting the baseline justification** in the gradient bandit — stating the rule without deriving the *role* of $B_t$ loses at least 1 mark in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Using the wrong log rule for UCB** — the bonus is $c\sqrt{\ln t \,/\, N_t(a)}$; reversing numerator and denominator loses the entire exploration logic.
> 4. **Mixing up "ε-greedy" and "greedy"** — pure greedy is *not* ε-greedy. State the probability split explicitly.
> 5. **Stepping over derivations** — KTU 14-mark questions explicitly reward showing every algebraic transition. Skipping steps in the incremental update derivation is the #1 cause of lost marks.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

- [x] **Evaluative feedback** = scalar reward signal that scores actions but never prescribes them.
- [x] **n-armed bandit** = canonical setting with $n$ actions, stationary reward distributions.
- [x] **True value** $q_*(a) = \mathbb{E}[R_t \mid A_t = a]$ is *unobservable*; we estimate it via $Q_t(a)$.
- [x] **Sample-average estimator** $Q_t(a) = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i$ is unbiased and converges by LLN.
- [x] **Incremental form** $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \frac{1}{n}[R_n - Q_n]$ is memory-efficient and the canonical stochastic-approximation template.
- [x] **Constant step-size** $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha[R_n - Q_n]$ implements exponential recency-weighting, ideal for *non-stationary* problems.
- [x] **ε-greedy** picks greedy w.p. $1 - \varepsilon$ and random w.p. $\varepsilon$ — the simplest exploration scheme.
- [x] **Optimistic initial values** like $Q_1(a) = +5$ drive systematic early exploration.
- [x] **UCB** $A_t = \arg\max_a[Q_t(a) + c\sqrt{\ln t \,/\, N_t(a)}]$ selects arms by upper confidence bound — a principled alternative to random exploration.
- [x] **Softmax (Boltzmann)** distribution $\pi_t(a) \propto e^{H_t(a)}$ converts preferences into probabilities.
- [x] **Gradient bandit** updates preferences by $H_{t+1}(a) = H_t(a) + \alpha(R_t - B_t)(\mathbf{1}_{a=A_t} - \pi_t(a))$.
- [x] **Baseline** $B_t$ is essential: it centers rewards and dramatically reduces gradient variance.
- [x] **Reinforcement comparison** is a special case of gradient bandit where the chosen action's preference is updated against a running-average baseline.
- [x] **Associative / contextual bandits** extend pure bandits to situations with states, bridging to full RL/MDPs.
- [x] **Asymptotic regret:** UCB and gradient bandits achieve $O(\ln T)$; greedy and ε-greedy suffer $O(T)$.
- [x] **KTU highest-yield formulas** (memorize): incremental update, ε-greedy rule, UCB rule, softmax rule, gradient preference update.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampled Feedback
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sampled Feedback

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Reinforcement Learning (RL), **Sampled Feedback** refers to the paradigm in which an agent learns a value function, policy, or model by interacting with the environment and receiving reward signals only for the specific state-action trajectories it actually visits, rather than obtaining an exhaustive enumeration of rewards across the entire state-action space.

Mathematically, in a Markov Decision Process (MDP) defined by the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$, sampled feedback implies that the learner receives information $R_t$ at time $t$ as a stochastic realization $R_t \sim R(s_t, a_t)$, and updates its value estimates $V(s_t)$ or $Q(s_t, a_t)$ based purely on the *sample path*:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t R_t \,\Big\vert\, s_0 = s\right]$$

Here, the expectation is approximated by Monte-Carlo sampling along one (or a few) trajectories, rather than by solving the full Bellman expectation backup across all states.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The distinguishing feature of sampled feedback is that the **agent does not have access to the full distribution** of rewards or transitions. It must learn from the *empirical distribution* of experience it generates through its own interaction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a food critic visiting a city of 10,000 restaurants. Instead of reading every published review for every restaurant (which would be exhaustive feedback), the critic personally dines at only 30 restaurants (sampled feedback) and writes reviews based purely on those visits. Later, when tourists ask "Is the city good for food?", the critic extrapolates from the 30 samples to estimate the quality of all 10,000 — sometimes accurately, sometimes with bias.

In RL terms:

- The **critic** = the learning agent.
- The **30 restaurants visited** = the sampled trajectory $\tau = (s_0, a_0, r_1, s_1, a_1, r_2, \dots)$.
- The **full set of 10,000 restaurants** = the complete state-action space $\mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A}$.
- The **tourist's question** = a query about the value $V(s)$ or $Q(s,a)$ of a state that may not even have been visited.

## 1.3 Three Sub-Categories of Sampled Feedback

| Sub-category | Description |
|---|---|
| **Active Reinforcement Learning** | Agent must select actions to *probe* the environment; reward is the only signal. |
| **Model-Based RL** | Agent builds an explicit model $P, R$ from samples, then *queries* the model for unvisited states. |
| **Sample-Based Function Approximation** | Agent approximates the value function using a finite set of training samples drawn from interaction. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key distinction:** In **Sampled Feedback**, the *learning signal itself is sparse and restricted to visited states*. The agent must therefore balance **exploration** (visiting new states) against **exploitation** (using known good actions).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Sampled Feedback

The operational flow of sampled feedback in an RL agent can be decomposed into the following logical steps:

1. **Initialization** — Initialize value function $V(s)$ arbitrarily for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$ (or via function approximator weights $\theta$).
2. **Action Selection** — From state $s_t$, select action $a_t$ according to a policy $\pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$.
3. **Environment Interaction** — Execute $a_t$, observe next state $s_{t+1} \sim P(\cdot \mid s_t, a_t)$ and reward $r_{t+1} \sim R(s_t, a_t)$.
4. **Sample-Based Update** — Update the value estimate using *only* the observed tuple $(s_t, a_t, r_{t+1}, s_{t+1})$.
5. **Repeat** — Iterate the loop; only visited states receive updates.

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental limitation: **states never visited retain their initial (often incorrect) value estimates forever**. This is precisely why exploration strategies such as $\epsilon$-greedy, Upper Confidence Bounds (UCB), and Thompson Sampling are mathematically *essential* and not merely optional heuristics.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Bellman Expectation (Full)** | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_a \pi(a\mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r\mid s,a)\bigl[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\bigr]$ | Requires exhaustive enumeration of $P$ and $R$ |
| **Sampled Bellman Backup** | $V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha \bigl[r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)\bigr]$ | Uses only one sample |
| **Monte-Carlo Return** | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Unbiased estimate from one trajectory |
| **TD($\lambda$) Eligibility** | $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}\{S_t = s\}$ | Traces credit over visited states |
| **Q-Learning Update** | $Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha\bigl[r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)\bigr]$ | Off-policy sampled update |
| **Epsilon-Greedy Action** | $a_t = \arg\max_a Q(s_t, a)$ w.p. $1-\epsilon$; random w.p. $\epsilon$ | Ensures continued exploration |
| **UCB Action Selection** | $a_t = \arg\max_a \left[Q_t(a) + c\sqrt{\tfrac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}\right]$ | Optimism in face of uncertainty |
| **Importance Sampling Ratio** | $\rho_t = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \dfrac{\pi(a_k \mid s_k)}{b(a_k \mid s_k)}$ | Corrects distribution mismatch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Boundary:** All absolute value bars and conditional bars in equations are written using `\vert` or `\mid` for markdown compatibility, but in plain reading they mean $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) or $p(a \mid b)$ (conditional probability).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sampled feedback is the *only* feasible learning paradigm in large-scale engineering systems where exhaustive enumeration is computationally impossible:

- **Robotics**: A six-DOF robotic arm has a continuous state space $\mathcal{S} \subset \mathbb{R}^6$; brute-force backup is intractable.
- **Autonomous Driving**: State space (lane, velocity, nearby vehicles) exceeds $10^{100}$ configurations; learning must proceed from sampled driving episodes.
- **Industrial Process Control**: Plant dynamics are continuous-time and only observable through sensor measurements.
- **Game AI (AlphaGo, AlphaZero)**: The board state $3^{361}$ configurations cannot be enumerated; sampling via self-play is the only viable route.
- **Recommendation Systems**: User-item interaction matrix is sparse ($\approx 1\%$ density); feedback is intrinsically sampled.

## 2.4 Why Sampling Works: Theoretical Justification

By the **Law of Large Numbers**, as the number of visits $N(s,a) \to \infty$:

$$\hat{Q}(s,a) = \frac{1}{N(s,a)}\sum_{i=1}^{N(s,a)} g_i \;\xrightarrow{a.s.}\; Q^{\pi}(s,a)$$

where $g_i$ is the return observed on the $i$-th visit to $(s,a)$. Therefore, with sufficient exploration, sampled feedback converges to the *true* value function — but the rate depends critically on the **mixing time** of the underlying MDP and the **exploration strategy** chosen.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Full Backup to Sampled Backup

We begin from the Bellman expectation equation for a policy $\pi$:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \bigl[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\bigr]$$

**Step 1 — Recognize the inner expectation.**  
The inner double summation can be rewritten as an expectation under the transition dynamics $p(\cdot \mid s, a)$:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \, \mathbb{E}_{p}\bigl[R + \gamma V^{\pi}(S') \,\big\vert\, s, a\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the outer summation as an expectation under the policy.**  
This itself can be viewed as an expectation under $\pi(\cdot \mid s)$:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\bigl[R + \gamma V^{\pi}(S') \,\big\vert\, s\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Replace the expectation with a single sample.**  
The Law of Large Numbers allows us to approximate the expectation using one sample realization $(s, a, r, s')$ drawn from the joint distribution $\pi(a \mid s)\, p(s', r \mid s, a)$:

$$V^{\pi}(s) \approx r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')$$

**Step 4 — Introduce a learning rate $\alpha$ for stochastic approximation (Robbins-Monro conditions).**  
To obtain a convergent iterative estimator, replace the exact update with:

$$V_{t+1}(s_t) = V_t(s_t) + \alpha_t \bigl[r_{t+1} + \gamma V_t(s_{t+1}) - V_t(s_t)\bigr]$$

**Step 5 — Verify convergence via the Robbins-Monro theorem.**  
For convergence $\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2 < \infty$ must hold; e.g., $\alpha_t = 1/(1 + N_t(s_t))$ satisfies both.

## 3.2 Derivation: Monte-Carlo Return from a Single Trajectory

Consider an episode trajectory:

$$\tau = (s_0, a_0, r_1, s_1, a_1, r_2, s_2, \dots, s_{T-1}, a_{T-1}, r_T, s_T)$$

**Step 1 — Define the return from time $t$:**  
The discounted return observed from time $t$ onwards is:

$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^2 r_{t+3} + \dots + \gamma^{T-t-1} r_T$$

**Step 2 — Express it compactly using summation notation:**  
This collapses to:

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^k \, r_{t+k+1}$$

**Step 3 — Show that $G_t$ is an unbiased estimator of $V^{\pi}(s_t)$:**  
By the definition of the expectation under $\pi$:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\bigl[G_t \,\big\vert\, s_t\bigr] = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\Bigl[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} \,\Big\vert\, s_t\Bigr] = V^{\pi}(s_t)$$

**Step 4 — Construct the first-visit Monte-Carlo update:**  
After each episode, for every state $s$ appearing for the first time at time $t$:

$$V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \bigl[G_t - V(s)\bigr]$$

This incremental form preserves memory and converges to $V^{\pi}$ under standard stochastic approximation assumptions.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Feedback Modes

| Property | Full Backup (DP) | Sampled Feedback (RL) |
|---|---|---|
| Knowledge of $P, R$ | Required | Not required |
| Computation per state | $\mathcal{O}(\vert\mathcal{S}\vert^2 \vert\mathcal{A}\vert)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per visited state |
| Exploration needed | No | Yes (critical) |
| Variance of estimate | Zero (deterministic) | $\sigma^2 > 0$ (decreases as $1/N$) |
| Convergence rate | Geometric (contraction) | Sub-linear typically $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{N})$ |
| Scalability to large $\mathcal{S}$ | Poor | Good |
| Real-world applicability | Toy problems only | Industrial scale |

## 3.4 Derivation: UCB Action Selection under Sampled Feedback

**Step 1 — Define the gap for each action:**  
Let $\Delta_a = Q^{\star}(a^{\star}) - Q^{\star}(a)$, where $a^{\star} = \arg\max_a Q^{\star}(a)$.

**Step 2 — Hoeffding's inequality bound:**  
For action $a$ sampled $N_t(a)$ times, with reward in $[0,1]$:

$$\mathbb{P}\bigl(\vert \hat{Q}_t(a) - Q^{\star}(a)\vert > u\bigr) \le 2\exp(-2 N_t(a) u^2)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the confidence radius:**  
Setting $2\exp(-2 N_t(a) u^2) = t^{-4}$ gives:

$$u = \sqrt{\frac{2 \ln t}{N_t(a)}}$$

**Step 4 — Construct the UCB rule:**  
Pick the action that maximizes the optimistic estimate:

$$a_t = \arg\max_a \left[\hat{Q}_t(a) + c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}\right]$$

The constant $c$ is a tuning hyperparameter that trades off exploration vs exploitation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Sampled Feedback Learning Loop

The following block diagram depicts the canonical agent–environment interaction loop under sampled feedback, contrasting the regions that are *visited* (light-shaded) with those that remain *unvisited* (dark-shaded).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Agent with Policy pi"] --> B["Action Selection a_t"]
    B --> C["Environment Step"]
    C --> D["Sample Reward r_t+1"]
    C --> E["Sample Next State s_t+1"]
    D --> F["Tuple Storage"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Sample-Based Update V or Q"]
    G --> H["Updated Value Estimate"]
    H --> A
    I["Unvisited States S_unseen"] -.->|"No update signal"| H
    J["Exploration Strategy epsilon or UCB"] -.-> B
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Three Modes of Learning from Sampled Feedback

This schematic decomposes the architecture of a modern RL system that must learn from sampled feedback alone.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["INPUT LAYER - Environment Interaction"]
        A1["Sensor Stream"]
        A2["Reward Channel"]
        A3["Action Bus"]
    end

    subgraph B["SAMPLING LAYER - Experience Generation"]
        B1["Policy Network pi_theta"]
        B2["Exploration Module - epsilon-greedy or UCB or Thompson"]
        B3["Replay Buffer"]
    end

    subgraph C["FEEDBACK PROCESSING LAYER"]
        C1["Sample Extractor - selects mini-batch"]
        C2["TD Error or Monte Carlo Return Calculator"]
        C3["Advantage Estimator A_t"]
    end

    subgraph D["UPDATE LAYER - Parameter Adjustment"]
        D1["Gradient Step on Loss L_theta"]
        D2["Target Network Sync"]
        D3["Eligibility Trace Update"]
    end

    subgraph E["OUTPUT LAYER - Updated Knowledge"]
        E1["Updated Policy pi_theta_new"]
        E2["Updated Value Function V_theta_new"]
        E3["Optional Model P_hat and R_hat"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> A3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D1 --> E1
    D1 --> E2
    C3 --> E3
    E1 --> B1
    E2 --> B1
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: When to Update vs Explore

The agent must continuously decide whether to exploit current knowledge or explore unknown regions. The diagram below visualizes this decision branching.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Current State s_t"] --> Q{"Lookup Q-table or Network"}
    Q -->|Known state| G["Compute UCB or epsilon"]
    Q -->|Unvisited state| R["Initialize optimistic estimate"]
    G --> P{"UCB or epsilon says explore"}
    R --> P
    P -->|Yes| E["Pick random or UCB-maximizing action"]
    P -->|No| X["Pick greedy action a = argmax Q"]
    E --> T["Execute action and observe reward"]
    X --> T
    T --> U["Store transition in buffer"]
    U --> V["Sample mini-batch and compute loss"]
    V --> W["Gradient update on parameters theta"]
    W --> S
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition  
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**  
**Q: Define "sampled feedback" in reinforcement learning. How does it differ from exhaustive feedback?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**  
Sampled feedback is the learning paradigm in which an agent receives reward signals only for the specific state-action pairs it visits through interaction, rather than obtaining an exhaustive enumeration of rewards across the entire state-action space. In exhaustive (dynamic-programming style) feedback, the agent has access to the complete transition model $P(s' \mid s, a)$ and reward model $R(s, a)$ and can compute the Bellman backup over all states simultaneously. Under sampled feedback, in contrast, the agent must approximate the value function using Monte-Carlo realizations from a few trajectories. The key practical difference is that sampled feedback does not require a model of the environment, making it suitable for large-scale, real-world problems where exhaustive enumeration is computationally intractable. **(3 Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for contrast with exhaustive feedback, 1 for practical implication.)**

---

### Question 2: Practical Implication  
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**  
**Q: Why is exploration essential when learning from sampled feedback?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**  
Exploration is essential because the agent's value estimates $V(s)$ or $Q(s,a)$ can only be updated for states that are actually visited. Without systematic exploration (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy, UCB, or softmax/Boltzmann strategies), states that are never visited will retain their initial — typically incorrect — value estimates indefinitely. This means the agent will fail to discover actions that may yield higher long-term reward than the currently known greedy action, leading to suboptimal policies and permanent convergence to local optima. Mathematically, the Law of Large Numbers guarantees that $\hat{Q}(s,a) \to Q^{\star}(s,a)$ only as $N(s,a) \to \infty$, which requires ongoing exploration. **(3 Marks: 1 for identifying the visited-state bottleneck, 1 for consequence, 1 for mathematical justification.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model), Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the sampled Bellman backup update rule from the full Bellman expectation equation. Show clearly how the double expectation is reduced to a single stochastic sample, and state the Robbins-Monro conditions required for convergence of the resulting stochastic approximation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 3-armed bandit problem where the true reward means are $\mu_1 = 0.3, \mu_2 = 0.5, \mu_3 = 0.4$ with rewards drawn i.i.d. from Bernoulli distributions. Using the UCB action-selection rule with $c = \sqrt{2}$, perform the first 5 steps of action selection manually, assuming each chosen arm returns a reward of 1 on its first visit. State which arm is selected at each step and explain why. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

Starting from:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \bigl[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\bigr]$$

Recognize the inner sum as the expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}_{p}\bigl[R + \gamma V^{\pi}(S') \mid s, a\bigr]$$

Recognize the outer sum also as expectation:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\bigl[R + \gamma V^{\pi}(S') \mid s\bigr]$$

Replace expectation with one sample realization:

$$V^{\pi}(s) \approx r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')$$

Introduce learning rate $\alpha_t$:

$$V_{t+1}(s_t) = V_t(s_t) + \alpha_t \bigl[r_{t+1} + \gamma V_t(s_{t+1}) - V_t(s_t)\bigr]$$

Convergence requires Robbins-Monro conditions:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \alpha_t = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2 < \infty$$

Common choice: $\alpha_t = 1/(1 + N_t(s_t))$. **(7 Marks: 2 for derivation of single-sample form, 2 for introducing $\alpha_t$, 2 for Robbins-Monro conditions, 1 for example schedule.)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

Initial state: $N(a) = 0$, $\hat{Q}(a) = 0$ for all three arms.

**Step 1** — All $N(a) = 0$, so UCB values are infinite (or undefined). Tie-break by selecting arm 1. Pull arm 1, reward = 1. Update: $N(1) = 1$, $\hat{Q}(1) = 1$.

UCB values with $t = 1$:

$$\text{UCB}_a = \hat{Q}(a) + \sqrt{2}\,\sqrt{\frac{\ln 1}{N(a)}}$$

Since $\ln 1 = 0$, exploration bonus is zero for arm 1; arms 2 and 3 are still at $N=0$ → infinite UCB → must explore. Pull arm 2 (tie-break), reward = 1. $N(2) = 1$, $\hat{Q}(2) = 1$.

**Step 3** — Similarly arm 3 is forced-explored. $N(3) = 1$, $\hat{Q}(3) = 1$.

**Step 4** — Now all arms have $N = 1$, so we use the UCB formula with $t = 4$:

$$\text{UCB}_a = 1 + \sqrt{2}\,\sqrt{\frac{\ln 4}{1}} = 1 + \sqrt{2 \cdot 1.386} \approx 1 + 1.665 = 2.665$$

All three are still tied at $2.665$. Tie-break: pull arm 1, reward = 1. $N(1) = 2$, $\hat{Q}(1) = 1$.

**Step 5** — Now $t = 5$:

- $\text{UCB}_1 = 1 + \sqrt{2}\,\sqrt{\frac{\ln 5}{2}} = 1 + \sqrt{2 \cdot 0.805} \approx 1 + 1.269 = 2.269$
- $\text{UCB}_2 = 1 + \sqrt{2}\,\sqrt{\frac{\ln 5}{1}} = 1 + \sqrt{2 \cdot 1.609} \approx 1 + 1.795 = 2.795$
- $\text{UCB}_3 = 1 + \sqrt{2}\,\sqrt{\frac{\ln 5}{1}} = 2.795$ (same as arm 2)

Tie-break: pull arm 2 or 3; suppose arm 2. Reward = 1. $N(2) = 2$, $\hat{Q}(2) = 1$.

**Summary Table:**

| Step | Arm Pulled | N(1) | N(2) | N(3) | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | Forced exploration |
| 2 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 0 | Forced exploration |
| 3 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 | Forced exploration |
| 4 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 1 | Tie-break |
| 5 | 2 | 2 | 2 | 1 | Higher UCB bonus |

**(7 Marks: 2 for UCB formula application, 3 for correct numerical computation per step, 2 for explanation.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model), Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between on-policy and off-policy learning in the context of sampled feedback. Derive the importance-sampling ratio used to correct the distribution mismatch in off-policy Monte-Carlo updates. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An agent operates in a 4-state episodic MDP with transition probabilities as given below. The agent follows an equiprobable random policy $\pi(a \mid s) = 0.5$ for each of the two actions in every state. Discount factor $\gamma = 0.9$. Compute the first-visit Monte-Carlo estimate of $V(s_1)$ from the single observed episode:

$$s_1 \xrightarrow{a_2, r=+1} s_3 \xrightarrow{a_1, r=0} s_2 \xrightarrow{a_2, r=+1} s_4 \xrightarrow{a_1, r=+2} \text{terminal}$$

Show all calculations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**On-policy learning** updates the value of the policy *currently being executed*. Examples: SARSA, Monte-Carlo evaluation of the behavioral policy.

**Off-policy learning** evaluates or improves a target policy $\pi$ while data is generated by a different behavioral policy $b$. Examples: Q-learning, importance-sampled Monte-Carlo.

**Derivation of importance-sampling ratio:**

Probability of trajectory $\tau$ under policy $\pi$:

$$P^{\pi}(\tau) = p(s_0)\prod_{t=0}^{T-1} \pi(a_t \mid s_t) p(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$$

Under behavioral policy $b$:

$$P^{b}(\tau) = p(s_0)\prod_{t=0}^{T-1} b(a_t \mid s_t) p(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$$

Their ratio:

$$\rho(\tau) = \frac{P^{\pi}(\tau)}{P^{b}(\tau)} = \prod_{t=0}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(a_t \mid s_t)}{b(a_t \mid s_t)}$$

Truncating from time $t$ onward:

$$\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(a_k \mid s_k)}{b(a_k \mid s_k)}$$

The off-policy MC update becomes:

$$V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha\, \rho_{t:T-1}\bigl[G_t - V(s_t)\bigr]$$

**(7 Marks: 2 for conceptual distinction, 3 for trajectory-probability derivation, 2 for final update rule.)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

The episode is:

$$s_1 \to s_3 \to s_2 \to s_4 \to \text{terminal}$$

with rewards $r_1 = 1, r_2 = 0, r_3 = 1, r_4 = 2$.

**Step 1 — Compute the return from $s_1$:**

$$G_0 = r_1 + \gamma r_2 + \gamma^2 r_3 + \gamma^3 r_4$$

$$G_0 = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 0 + 0.81 \cdot 1 + 0.729 \cdot 2$$

$$G_0 = 1 + 0 + 0.81 + 1.458 = 3.268$$

**Step 2 — Check first-visit status of $s_1$:**  
$s_1$ appears at time $t = 0$ and never again → it is a first visit.

**Step 3 — Apply first-visit MC update with $\alpha = 1/N$ for incremental average:**

For the *first* visit, $N(s_1) = 0$, so incremental average gives:

$$V(s_1) = G_0 = 3.268$$

If we had multiple episodes, we would accumulate:

$$V(s_1) \leftarrow V(s_1) + \frac{1}{N(s_1)}\bigl[G_0^{(i)} - V(s_1)\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Verify against Bellman target (optional sanity check):**  
Computing the theoretical $V^{\pi}(s_1)$ requires the full transition matrix, which is not needed for the MC estimate; we accept $V(s_1) = 3.268$ as the unbiased sampled estimate.

**(7 Marks: 2 for return computation, 2 for first-visit recognition, 2 for correct application of update rule, 1 for final numerical answer.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing sampled backup with full backup:** Students often write $V(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s)\sum_{s'}[\dots]$ when asked for sampled feedback — this is a 3-mark penalty in derivations.
> 2. **Forgetting Robbins-Monro conditions:** Any derivation of an iterative update rule that lacks the conditions $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$ will be marked incomplete.
> 3. **UCB arithmetic errors:** Off-by-one in $\ln t$ vs $\ln(t+1)$ is a common 1-mark deduction; ensure the formula matches what your textbook module uses.
> 4. **Monte-Carlo first-visit vs every-visit:** Examiners explicitly test this distinction. The first-visit method averages returns only from the *first* occurrence of $s$ in an episode, while every-visit averages *all* occurrences.
> 5. **Importance sampling direction:** The ratio is $\pi / b$, not $b / \pi$. Reversing this silently corrupts the entire off-policy estimator.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sampled feedback** = learning value functions from trajectories rather than exhaustive backups.
- The agent receives rewards only for **visited states**; unvisited states are never updated without a model.
- **Bellman expectation (full backup):** $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[R + \gamma V^{\pi}(S') \mid s]$ — uses complete distribution.
- **Sampled backup:** $V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha[r + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)]$ — uses single sample.
- **Monte-Carlo return:** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$ — unbiased but high variance.
- **TD(0)** is the canonical sampled-feedback algorithm; combines bootstrapping with sampling.
- **Exploration strategies:** $\epsilon$-greedy, UCB, Boltzmann/softmax, Thompson Sampling.
- **UCB formula:** $a_t = \arg\max_a[\hat{Q}_t(a) + c\sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}]$.
- **Off-policy correction:** importance-sampling ratio $\rho = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1}\pi(a_k\mid s_k)/b(a_k\mid s_k)$.
- **Convergence of stochastic approximation:** requires Robbins-Monro conditions on $\alpha_t$.
- **Law of Large Numbers** guarantees $\hat{Q} \to Q^{\star}$ as $N \to \infty$ — hence exploration is non-negotiable.
- **Real-world systems** (robotics, autonomous driving, recommendation engines, game AI) all rely on sampled feedback because exhaustive enumeration of their state-action spaces is computationally impossible.
- **Examiner tip:** Always state the *form* of the estimator (sampled vs full), the *learning rate schedule*, and the *exploration policy* — these are the three pillars every KTU 14-mark question tests.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Function Approximation for Reinforcement Learning - High-Dimensional State and Action Spaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Function Approximation for Reinforcement Learning - High-Dimensional State and Action Spaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Function Approximation (FA) in Reinforcement Learning** is the class of computational techniques that estimate the value function $V_\pi(s)$ or action-value function $Q_\pi(s,a)$ — or even the policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ — using a parameterized function $\hat{f}(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ (e.g., linear basis functions, neural networks, decision trees) whose parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ are adjusted from sampled experience so that the approximator **generalizes** to states and actions that have never been explicitly visited.

When the number of distinct states or actions grows combinatorially or becomes continuous (e.g., pixel grids, joint angles, sensor vectors), an exact tabular representation $V(s)$ is infeasible. FA replaces the lookup table with a smooth, compact function and therefore constitutes the central engineering tool that enables RL to scale to **high-dimensional state and action spaces**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module explicitly couples *Function Approximation* with *High-Dimensional State and Action Spaces*. Students must distinguish between **(a)** approximation of the value function (used by value-based methods), **(b)** approximation of the policy (used by policy-based methods), and **(c)** approximation of the model (used by model-based methods).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cartographer vs. The Geologist

Imagine a student learning the altitude of every point on Earth.

- **Tabular RL** is like a **cartographer** who has to draw an altitude label at *every single coordinate* on the planet. For a 1000 × 1000 grid, that is one million labels; for an Atari screen of size $84 \times 84 \times 4$ pixels, the table would require $2^{84 \cdot 84 \cdot 4 \cdot 8}$ entries — astronomically more than the number of atoms in the universe.
- **Function Approximation** is like a **geologist** who learns a few rules of orography — *"altitude grows with latitude and is reduced by river erosion"* — and from those rules **infers** the altitude of any unseen location. The geologist stores only the *parameters* of the rule, not every data point.

The geologist (FA) is what makes RL tractable when the state space is image-like, the action space is continuous, or both.

## 1.3 The Curse of Dimensionality in Plain English

> [!NOTE]
> **Bellman (1957) — Curse of Dimensionality:** the number of samples required to uniformly cover a space grows **exponentially** with its dimensionality $d$.

Concretely, if a 1-D state interval is divided into $k$ bins, a tabular agent needs $O(k)$ samples; in 2-D it needs $O(k^2)$; in 10-D it needs $O(k^{10})$; and in 1,000,000-D (a small image) it needs more samples than the age of the universe could provide. FA defeats this explosion by leveraging the assumption that **nearby states have similar values** (the *smoothness* or *locality* prior), so a single shared parameter bank can serve millions of states.

## 1.4 Three High-Dimensional Regimes Addressed by FA

| Regime | Typical Input | Example Task | FA Component Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High-D State, Low-D Discrete Action** | $84 \times 84$ Atari pixels | Atari Pong | Deep Q-Network over $Q(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ |
| **Low-D State, High-D / Continuous Action** | Robot joint angles, torques | Robotic Arm Control | Policy network $\pi(a \mid s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ (Gaussian) |
| **High-D State AND High-D / Continuous Action** | Camera + joint torques | Autonomous Driving | Actor-Critic with both networks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Reported in KTU Board Exams:** *Mean Squared Value Error* $\overline{VE} = \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \mu(s)\big[V_\pi(s) - \hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta})\big]^2$, where $\mu(s)$ is the on-policy state-distribution. Bold figures typically asked: **MSE**, **RMSE**, and **Explained Variance**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Tabular Methods Fail — The Operational Logic

Tabular RL stores one value per state in a hash-map. This implicitly requires:

1. **Enumerability** — every state can be visited and indexed.
2. **Visit frequency** — each state is visited often enough for its estimate to converge.
3. **Finite action set** — $\arg\max_a$ is a discrete lookup.

When **any** of these three conditions is violated, FA becomes mandatory. The transition is therefore not optional but a *necessity* imposed by dimensionality.

## 2.2 The Three Families of Function Approximators

### A. Linear Function Approximation

$$\hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top} \mathbf{x}(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \theta_i x_i(s)$$

- $\mathbf{x}(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is a hand-crafted **feature vector** (e.g., polynomial, Fourier basis, tile coding, radial basis functions).
- Linear FA is the only family with **strong convergence guarantees** for control under on-policy sampling.
- In KTU 2024 scheme, Fourier basis $\phi_i(s) = \cos(\pi \, \mathbf{c}_i^{\top} s)$ is a *favourite exam topic*.

### B. Artificial Neural Networks (Deep RL)

- Multi-layer perceptron or convolutional backbone maps $s \mapsto \hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ or $(s, a) \mapsto \hat{Q}(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta})$.
- The convolutional encoder is *automatically* the feature extractor — no hand-crafted $\mathbf{x}(s)$ is needed.
- Examples: **DQN** (Mnih et al., 2015), **DDPG** (Lillicrap et al., 2015), **PPO** (Schulman et al., 2017), **SAC** (Haarnoja et al., 2018).

### C. Non-Parametric & Local Approximators

- $k$-Nearest Neighbours, Decision Trees, Locally Weighted Regression, *Coarse Coding*.
- Useful when the function is highly non-smooth (e.g., a grid-world with sharp cliffs).

## 2.3 Three Approaches to RL with Function Approximation

| Approach | What Is Approximated | Update Target | Representative Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Value-Based** | $V(s)$ or $Q(s, a)$ | Bootstrap from successor value | SARSA, Q-Learning, **DQN** |
| **Policy-Based** | $\pi(a \mid s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ directly | Monte-Carlo return $G_t$ | REINFORCE, **PPO**, TRPO |
| **Actor-Critic** | Both $V$ (Critic) and $\pi$ (Actor) | Bootstrapped one-step return | **A2C, A3C, DDPG, SAC** |
| **Model-Based** | Transition $P(s' \mid s, a)$ and $R(s, a)$ | Supervised regression on real transitions | World Models, MuZero |

## 2.4 The Deadly Triad

> [!WARNING]
> **The Deadly Triad** of modern RL — Sutton & Barto (2018): when **function approximation** is combined with **bootstrapping** (using one's own estimate as a target) and **off-policy learning** (learning about a policy different from the one generating data), the value function may **diverge**. This is the single most important pitfall students must internalize for KTU 14-mark questions.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top} \mathbf{x}(s)$ | Linear value approximation | $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| 2 | $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{x}(s)$ | Gradient of linear FA | Used in SGD update |
| 3 | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} + \alpha \big[v_\pi(S_t) - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)\big]\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$ | Gradient-descent MC update | $\alpha$ = step-size, dimensionless |
| 4 | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} + \alpha \big[R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(S_{t+1}; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t) - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)\big]\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$ | Semi-gradient TD(0) update | $0 \le \gamma < 1$ |
| 5 | $\mathcal{L}_i(\boldsymbol{\theta}_i) = \mathbb{E}_{s,a,r,s'}\big[\big(r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a'; \boldsymbol{\theta}_i^{-}) - Q(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta}_i)\big)^{2}\big]$ | DQN loss (Eq. widely used in 14-mark answers) | Sample mean over replay buffer |
| 6 | $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} Q(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \cdot \alpha \cdot \delta$ | Semi-gradient SARSA / Q-learning step | $\delta$ = TD error |
| 7 | $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta}\big[\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)\, Q_\pi(s, a)\big]$ | Policy Gradient Theorem (Sutton et al., 2000) | Continuous and discrete actions |
| 8 | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \alpha \gamma^{t} G_t \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$ | REINFORCE update | Monte-Carlo target $G_t$ |
| 9 | $\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\phi}, \mathcal{D}) = \mathbb{E}_{(s,a,r,s') \sim \mathcal{D}}\big[\big(r + \gamma V_{\boldsymbol{\phi}^{-}}(s') - V_{\boldsymbol{\phi}}(s)\big)^{2}\big]$ | Critic loss in actor-critic | Target network $\boldsymbol{\phi}^{-}$ |
| 10 | $\overline{VE}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{s} \mu(s)\big[V_\pi(s) - \hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta})\big]^{2}$ | Mean squared value error | $\mu(s)$ on-policy distribution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equations (3)–(5) underpin autonomous-driving perception stacks, robotic manipulation (e.g., OpenAI's Dactyl hand), recommender-system slate ranking, and dynamic-pricing engines. Equations (7)–(8) underpin continuous-control RL deployed in **quadrotor flight, dexterous in-hand manipulation, and language-model fine-tuning with PPO (RLHF)**.

## 2.6 Practical Considerations for High-D Spaces

1. **Normalization** — subtract running mean, divide by running standard deviation (Welford's online algorithm).
2. **Initialization** — orthogonal init for deep CNN backbones; zero init for the final value head.
3. **Optimiser** — RMSProp or Adam with $\beta_1 = 0.9$, $\beta_2 = 0.999$, $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$.
4. **Target Network** — periodic Polyak averaging $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{-} \leftarrow \tau \boldsymbol{\theta} + (1-\tau) \boldsymbol{\theta}^{-}$.
5. **Replay Buffer** — $\mathcal{D}$ of capacity $10^{5}$–$10^{6}$ transitions to decorrelate samples.
6. **Exploration** — $\epsilon$-greedy decays from **1.0 → 0.1** over the first $10^{6}$ steps; for continuous actions, add Gaussian noise with annealed variance.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Stochastic Gradient Descent for Linear Value FA

We start from the squared error for a single sample $S_t$ with target $v_\pi(S_t)$ (assumed known for the moment, as in Monte-Carlo RL).

$$\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\big[v_\pi(S_t) - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})\big]^{2}$$

Step 1 — *Differentiate the loss with respect to $\boldsymbol{\theta}$* using the chain rule.

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \big[v_\pi(S_t) - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})\big] \cdot \big[-\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})\big]$$

Step 2 — *Cancel the negative sign* by reversing the difference.

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; -\big[v_\pi(S_t) - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})\big]\,\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})$$

Step 3 — *Substitute the linear FA form* $\hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top} \mathbf{x}(s)$.

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \mathbf{x}(S_t) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; -\big[v_\pi(S_t) - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})\big]\,\mathbf{x}(S_t)$$

Step 4 — *Apply the SGD update rule* $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t - \alpha \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$.

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} + \alpha\,\big[v_\pi(S_t) - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)\big]\,\mathbf{x}(S_t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** This is the *exact* MC update used in **linear Monte-Carlo Policy Evaluation** (Sutton & Barto, Sec. 9.3). The factor $\alpha$ is dimensionless, the gradient has units of the feature, and the convergence to a **least-squares fixed point** is guaranteed when samples are drawn i.i.d. from the on-policy distribution.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Semi-Gradient TD(0) with Function Approximation

Tabular TD(0) updates $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \delta_t$ with $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$. To generalise to FA we **define** the update target using the *current* parameters and take the gradient *only* with respect to $\hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ — not the bootstrap target $V(S_{t+1}; \boldsymbol{\theta})$. This is why the method is called **semi-gradient**.

Step 1 — *Define the one-step TD target* $U_t$ using current parameters.

$$U_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma\,\hat{V}(S_{t+1}; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$$

Step 2 — *Form the squared error between the current prediction and the bootstrap target*.

$$\mathcal{L}_t(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\big[U_t - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})\big]^{2}$$

Step 3 — *Differentiate only with respect to $\hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})$* (ignore the dependence of $U_t$ on $\boldsymbol{\theta}$).

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} \;=\; -\big[U_t - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})\big]\,\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; -\delta_t \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})$$

where the **TD error** is

$$\delta_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(S_{t+1}; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t) - \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$$

Step 4 — *Write the semi-gradient ascent update* (negate the gradient of the loss).

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} + \alpha\,\delta_t\,\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$$

Step 5 — *For linear FA*, $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{V}(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{x}(S_t)$, giving the classical **LSTD/TD-Lambda** family.

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} + \alpha\,\delta_t\,\mathbf{x}(S_t)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Consequence:** Because the bootstrap target $U_t$ is treated as constant, semi-gradient methods are *not true gradient descent*. They converge for **on-policy linear FA** (TD(0) under the standard stochastic approximation conditions of Robbins-Monro) but may **diverge off-policy** — the famous *Baird's counter-example* illustrates this with **7 states**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — DQN Loss and Gradient

Deep Q-Network (Mnih et al., 2015) minimises the **squared TD error** with two stabilising tricks: a *target network* $\boldsymbol{\theta}_i^{-}$ and an *experience replay buffer* $\mathcal{D}$.

Step 1 — *Sample a mini-batch* $(s, a, r, s') \sim U(\mathcal{D})$ uniformly at random.

Step 2 — *Compute the Q-value for the chosen action* using the **online** network.

$$Q_{\text{online}} \;=\; Q(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta}_i)$$

Step 3 — *Compute the bootstrap target* using the **target** network; for a terminal state the bootstrap term is zero.

$$y_i \;=\; \begin{cases} r & \text{if } s' \text{ is terminal} \\ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a'; \boldsymbol{\theta}_i^{-}) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Step 4 — *Form the mean-squared loss over the mini-batch* of size $B$.

$$\mathcal{L}_i(\boldsymbol{\theta}_i) \;=\; \frac{1}{B}\sum_{j=1}^{B}\big(y_i^{(j)} - Q(s^{(j)}, a^{(j)}; \boldsymbol{\theta}_i)\big)^{2}$$

Step 5 — *Differentiate w.r.t. the online parameters* (the target network is held fixed).

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}_i} \mathcal{L}_i \;=\; \frac{2}{B}\sum_{j=1}^{B}\big(y_i^{(j)} - Q(s^{(j)}, a^{(j)}; \boldsymbol{\theta}_i)\big)\big[-\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}_i} Q(s^{(j)}, a^{(j)}; \boldsymbol{\theta}_i)\big]$$

Step 6 — *Perform one step of gradient descent* (e.g., RMSProp or Adam) on $\boldsymbol{\theta}_i$ and every $C$ steps replace $\boldsymbol{\theta}_i^{-}$ with $\boldsymbol{\theta}_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is DQN stable?** Three reasons: (i) **target network** removes the moving-target problem for $y_i$; (ii) **replay buffer** decorrelates successive samples; (iii) **reward clipping** to $\{-1, 0, +1\}$ bounds gradient magnitudes across diverse games.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Policy Gradient Theorem for High-D Action Spaces

For continuous action spaces $\arg\max_{a} Q(s, a)$ is intractable. Instead, we directly parametrise the policy $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ and maximise

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \sum_{s} d_\pi(s)\, V_\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{s} d_\pi(s)\sum_{a} \pi_\theta(a \mid s)\, Q_\pi(s, a)$$

Step 1 — *Differentiate the objective* w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ and use the **log-derivative trick** $\nabla \pi = \pi \nabla \log \pi$.

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \sum_{s} d_\pi(s)\sum_{a} Q_\pi(s, a)\,\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \;=\; \sum_{s} d_\pi(s)\sum_{a} \pi_\theta(a \mid s) Q_\pi(s, a)\,\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)$$

Step 2 — *Recognise the inner sum as an expectation under $\pi_\theta$*, yielding the **Policy Gradient Theorem**.

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta}\!\big[Q_\pi(s, a)\,\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)\big]$$

Step 3 — *In the REINFORCE algorithm* the expectation is replaced by a single-sample Monte-Carlo estimate using the episodic return $G_t$.

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;\approx\; G_t\,\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$$

Step 4 — *Update* $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ in the ascent direction with step-size $\alpha$.

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} + \alpha\,G_t\,\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$$

Step 5 — *Gaussian policy* for continuous control: $\pi_\theta(a \mid s) = \mathcal{N}\!\big(\mu_\theta(s),\, \sigma_\theta^{2}(s)\big)$.

$$\log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \;=\; -\tfrac{(a - \mu_\theta(s))^{2}}{2\sigma_\theta^{2}(s)} - \log \sigma_\theta(s) - \tfrac{1}{2}\log 2\pi$$

Step 6 — *Actor-Critic refinement* — replace $G_t$ with the **TD error** $\delta_t$ to obtain a low-variance, online estimate.

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} + \alpha\,\delta_t\,\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for KTU?** Policy-gradient methods do not require maximisation over $a$ and therefore scale linearly with action-space dimensionality — making them the *de facto* standard for high-dimensional continuous control (robotic arms with 7+ DOF, autonomous-vehicle steering/throttle/brake).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing FA by State/Action Space

| State Space | Action Space | Recommended FA | Algorithm | KTU Board-Exam Friendly? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Discrete, $\leq 10^{4}$ | Discrete, small | Tabular | Q-Learning | ✔ |
| Discrete, $\leq 10^{4}$ | Discrete, large | Linear FA | Approximate Q-Iteration | ✔ |
| Image $\mathbb{R}^{84\times84\times4}$ | Discrete, $\leq 18$ | CNN + FC head | **DQN**, Rainbow | ✔ (very common) |
| Continuous $\mathbb{R}^{d_s}$, small $d_s$ | Continuous $\mathbb{R}^{d_a}$ | Gaussian policy + value net | **DDPG, TD3, SAC** | ✔ |
| Continuous $\mathbb{R}^{d_s}$, $d_s$ large | Continuous $\mathbb{R}^{d_a}$ | CNN backbone + Gaussian head | SAC + encoder | ✔ |
| Discrete huge ($10^{9}$) | Discrete huge | Action embeddings | Action-Elimination DQN | ✗ (rare) |
| Continuous | Discrete huge | Policy over continuous "preference" | Preference-based RL | ✗ (rare) |
