# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]
**Define the curse of dimensionality in Reinforcement Learning and explain how function approximation overcomes it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The curse of dimensionality, formally stated by Richard Bellman in 1957, refers to the exponential growth in the number of samples required to uniformly populate a space as its dimensionality increases. In RL, the state space is the relevant space; for an Atari frame of size $84 \times 84 \times 4$ with 256 grey levels, the cardinality of the state space exceeds $10^{70000}$, far beyond the storage of any computer.
- **Why tabular RL fails (1 mark):** A tabular value function must allocate one parameter per state; storing a value for every pixel-frame state is physically impossible, and visiting every state often enough to learn it is even more impossible.
- **How FA overcomes it (1 mark):** Function approximation parametrises the value function as $\hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ or $\hat{Q}(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ with a small parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, where $d$ is far smaller than $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$. The approximator exploits the *smoothness prior* — neighbouring states typically have similar values — to **generalise** learning from a handful of visited states to the rest of the (continuous or high-dimensional) space. Linear FA, neural networks, and decision trees are the three standard families.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]
**State and briefly explain the three components of the "deadly triad" in deep reinforcement learning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — one mark per component):**
- **Component 1 — Function Approximation (1 mark):** A generalising function such as a neural network is used in place of a lookup table. The very act of sharing parameters across states creates a *coupling*: an update to $\hat{V}(s_1; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ inadvertently affects $\hat{V}(s_2; \boldsymbol{\theta})$.
- **Component 2 — Bootstrapping (1 mark):** The update target $U_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(S_{t+1}; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$ uses the agent's own current estimate of future value. Errors in the current estimate become self-reinforcing errors in subsequent updates.
- **Component 3 — Off-Policy Learning (1 mark):** The behaviour policy that gathers data differs from the target policy being improved (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy vs. greedy in Q-learning). The state-distribution shift amplifies the errors introduced by the previous two ingredients.
- **Why "deadly"?** When all three are present simultaneously, the value function can diverge — famously demonstrated by Baird's seven-state counter-example. Remedies include target networks, replay buffers, double-learning, and on-policy methods like PPO.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]

> **"Discuss function approximation methods in Reinforcement Learning with emphasis on handling high-dimensional state spaces such as raw image inputs. Sub-part (a) should cover linear and neural-network-based value approximation, while sub-part (b) should derive the DQN loss and explain the stabilising tricks used in deep Q-learning." (14 marks)**

#### Sub-part (a) — Value Approximation with Linear and Neural Models (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Linear value approximation (2 marks).** Define $\hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top} \mathbf{x}(s)$ with $\mathbf{x}(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ a hand-crafted feature vector — examples include tile coding, radial basis functions, and Fourier basis $\phi_i(s) = \cos(\pi \mathbf{c}_i^{\top} s)$. Mention that linear FA is the only family with rigorous convergence guarantees for on-policy TD control.

**Step 2 — Non-linear neural approximation (2 marks).** When the raw input is an image $s \in \mathbb{R}^{84 \times 84 \times 4}$, hand-crafted features are inadequate. A **convolutional neural network** learns the feature hierarchy automatically: lower layers learn edge and texture detectors, deeper layers learn object parts, and the final fully-connected head produces $\hat{Q}(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ for each discrete action. The same network shares computation across all actions, exploiting the locality of pixel statistics.

**Step 3 — The Deep Q-Network head (1 mark).** The output is a vector of $Q$-values, one per action: $\hat{Q}(s, \cdot; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$. The greedy action is then $\arg\max_a \hat{Q}(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta})$, computed in $O(\vert \mathcal{A} \vert)$ time, which is efficient when $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ is moderate (Atari: ≤ 18 actions).

**Step 4 — Loss surface and optimisation (2 marks).** The squared TD error $\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}\big[\delta_t^2\big]$ is non-convex in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ for neural networks. Stochastic gradient methods (SGD, RMSProp, Adam) are used with learning rate $\alpha \in [10^{-5}, 10^{-3}]$, batch size $B = 32$, and gradient clipping at unit norm to combat *exploding gradients* in recurrent value estimators.

#### Sub-part (b) — DQN Loss Derivation and Stabilising Tricks (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Bootstrap target (1 mark).** The TD target for transition $(s, a, r, s')$ is
$$y \;=\; r + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat{Q}(s', a'; \boldsymbol{\theta}^{-})$$
where $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{-}$ are the *target network* parameters.

**Step 2 — Loss function (1 mark).** Mean-squared error over a mini-batch sampled from the replay buffer $\mathcal{D}$:
$$\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{(s,a,r,s')\sim U(\mathcal{D})}\big[\big(y - \hat{Q}(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta})\big)^{2}\big]$$

**Step 3 — Gradient (1 mark).**
$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L} \;=\; 2\,\mathbb{E}\big[\big(y - \hat{Q}(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta})\big)\big(-\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{Q}(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta})\big)\big]$$

**Step 4 — Trick 1: Replay buffer (1 mark).** Transitions are stored in $\mathcal{D}$ of capacity $10^{5}$–$10^{6}$. Mini-batches are drawn uniformly at random, breaking the temporal correlation that would otherwise destabilise the online SGD update.

**Step 5 — Trick 2: Target network (1 mark).** $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{-}$ is held fixed for $C$ steps (typically $C = 10{,}000$) and then *replaced* by $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ (hard update) or *averaged* (soft/Poyak update $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{-} \leftarrow \tau \boldsymbol{\theta} + (1-\tau)\boldsymbol{\theta}^{-}$ with $\tau = 0.001$). This removes the *moving target* problem.

**Step 6 — Trick 3: Reward clipping (1 mark).** Rewards from different games are clipped to $\{-1, 0, +1\}$, bounding gradient magnitudes and letting a single hyper-parameter setting work across 49 Atari games.

**Step 7 — Trick 4: Double DQN (1 mark).** Decouple action *selection* (online network) and action *evaluation* (target network):
$$y^{\text{DoubleDQN}} \;=\; r + \gamma\,\hat{Q}\!\big(s',\, \arg\max_{a'} \hat{Q}(s', a'; \boldsymbol{\theta});\, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{-}\big)$$
This reduces the upward bias of $\max$ in noisy estimates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget that the gradient is taken **only** with respect to the online parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, *not* the target parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{-}$. Forgetting this "stop-gradient" is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. Additionally, ensure that you explicitly mention the role of the **replay buffer** — its absence is the most common reason given in model answers, but its presence in the actual DQN paper is what enables stable learning.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model) — Alternative Choice]

> **"Explain how Reinforcement Learning handles high-dimensional and continuous action spaces. Sub-part (a) should compare value-based and policy-based approaches, and sub-part (b) should derive the policy gradient theorem and discuss Gaussian policies for continuous control." (14 marks)**

#### Sub-part (a) — Value-Based vs. Policy-Based Approaches (7 marks)

**Step 1 — The challenge of high-D / continuous actions (1 mark).** When the action set $\mathcal{A}$ is a continuous subset of $\mathbb{R}^{d_a}$ (e.g., $d_a = 7$ torques for a robotic arm), the operation $\arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \hat{Q}(s, a; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ becomes a non-convex optimisation problem that is intractable at every timestep. Discretising the action space (as in the *NAF* and *Continuous Deep Q* papers) suffers from the curse of dimensionality: $k$ bins per dimension give $k^{d_a}$ action values.

**Step 2 — Value-based approach: workarounds (2 marks).**
- **Action elimination:** prune infeasible/dominated actions before the max.
- **Q-value parametrisation as quadratic in $a$ (NAF):** $\hat{Q}(s, a) = A(s, a) + V(s)$ with $A$ a learned negative-definite quadratic — gives a closed-form argmax.
- **Distributional perspectives:** implicitly avoid the max by modelling the full return distribution.

**Step 3 — Policy-based approach: direct parametrisation (2 marks).** Instead of learning $Q$ and deriving $\pi$, *directly* parametrise the policy $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$. For discrete actions, use a softmax $\pi_\theta(a \mid s) = \frac{\exp(f_\theta(s, a))}{\sum_{a'} \exp(f_\theta(s, a'))}$. For continuous actions, use a Gaussian $\pi_\theta(a \mid s) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_\theta(s), \Sigma_\theta(s))$. The agent samples an action in $O(d_a)$ and updates $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ by gradient *ascent* on expected return $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$.

**Step 4 — Comparison table (2 marks).**

| Property | Value-Based (DQN) | Policy-Based (REINFORCE / PPO) |
|---|---|---|
| Handles continuous actions? | Only with workarounds (NAF, discretisation) | Native, exact |
| Sample efficiency | High (replay + bootstrap) | Lower (on-policy, high variance) |
| Convergence stability | Good with target net & replay | Good with trust region (TRPO/PPO) |
| Stochastic policies? | Requires post-hoc softmax | Native |
| Exploration in continuous action | Hard | Native via $\sigma_\theta$ |

#### Sub-part (b) — Policy Gradient Theorem & Gaussian Policy (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Objective (1 mark).**
$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}\big[R(\tau)\big] \;=\; \sum_{s} d_{\pi_\theta}(s)\sum_{a} \pi_\theta(a \mid s)\, Q_{\pi_\theta}(s, a)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ (1 mark).**
$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \sum_{s} d_{\pi_\theta}(s)\sum_{a} Q_{\pi_\theta}(s, a)\,\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \pi_\theta(a \mid s)$$

**Step 3 — Log-derivative trick (1 mark).** Using $\nabla \pi = \pi \nabla \log \pi$:
$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta}\!\big[Q_{\pi_\theta}(s, a)\,\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)\big]$$

**Step 4 — REINFORCE update (1 mark).** Replace the expectation by a single-sample Monte-Carlo estimate using the episodic return $G_t$:
$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} + \alpha\,G_t\,\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$$

**Step 5 — Gaussian policy derivation (2 marks).** Let $\pi_\theta(a \mid s) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_\theta(s), \sigma_\theta^{2}(s))$. Then
$$\log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \;=\; -\frac{(a - \mu_\theta(s))^{2}}{2\sigma_\theta^{2}(s)} - \log \sigma_\theta(s) - \tfrac{1}{2}\log 2\pi$$
and
$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \;=\; \frac{(a - \mu_\theta(s))}{\sigma_\theta^{2}(s)}\,\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mu_\theta(s) \;+\; \left[\frac{(a - \mu_\theta(s))^{2}}{\sigma_\theta^{3}(s)} - \frac{1}{\sigma_\theta(s)}\right]\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \sigma_\theta(s)$$

**Step 6 — Variance reduction via baseline (1 mark).** Subtract a state-dependent baseline $b(s)$ from $G_t$:
$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} + \alpha\,(G_t - b(s_t))\,\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$$
Typical baselines: running average of returns, value-function estimate $V(s; \boldsymbol{\phi})$, or the *advantage* $A(s, a) = Q(s, a) - V(s)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Policy-Gradient Question):**
> 1. Students often forget the **on-policy distribution** $d_{\pi_\theta}(s)$ in the derivation — losing 1 mark.
> 2. The REINFORCE update is *ascent* on $J$, not descent on a loss — sign errors are a common 1-mark deduction.
> 3. For continuous actions, simply *mentioning* Gaussian is insufficient — you must derive at least the form of $\log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ to get full 2 marks in Step 5.
> 4. State the *reason* for the baseline: it reduces variance **without** introducing bias, because $\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi}[b(s)\nabla \log \pi(a \mid s)] = 0$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — Function Approximation for High-D State and Action Spaces**

- **Core idea:** Replace the lookup table $V(s)$ with a smooth parametrised function $\hat{V}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ so that experience at a few states generalises to the entire high-D space.
- **Three FA families:** Linear (interpretable, provably convergent on-policy), Neural (universal, automatic features, non-convex), Non-parametric (local, sample-efficient in low-D).
- **Three RL approaches with FA:** Value-based (DQN), Policy-based (REINFORCE/PPO), Actor-Critic (A2C, SAC), plus Model-based (PETS, MuZero).
- **Linear FA update:** $\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} + \alpha \delta_t \mathbf{x}(s_t)$, gradient is the feature vector.
- **DQN stabilisers (all must be mentioned):** target network, replay buffer, reward clipping, gradient clipping; extensions: double DQN, dueling DQN, prioritised replay, distributional C51.
- **Deadly triad:** FA + Bootstrapping + Off-policy ⇒ divergence risk; mitigations are target nets, replay, double learning, on-policy PPO, entropy bonus.
- **Continuous actions — the natural choice:** Gaussian policy $\mathcal{N}(\mu_\theta(s), \sigma_\theta^{2}(s))$; update via REINFORCE or PPO with baseline.
- **Policy gradient theorem:** $\nabla_\theta J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta}[Q_\pi(s, a) \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)]$; use log-derivative trick, baseline for variance reduction.
- **Actor-Critic:** critic $V_\phi(s)$ reduces variance; policy gradient becomes $\nabla_\theta J \approx \delta_t \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$ with TD error $\delta_t$.
- **High-D state pre-processing:** frame-skipping, frame stacking, grayscale conversion, image resizing, normalisation — these reduce input dimensionality and accelerate learning.
- **High-D continuous action regularisation:** squashing via $\tanh$, log-std parameterisation for numerical stability, action rescaling.
- **Evaluation metrics to remember:** $\overline{VE}$, episodic return, sample efficiency (steps to reach threshold performance), and final performance.
- **Convergence guarantees in KTU-typical answer:** Linear FA on-policy TD converges; non-linear FA off-policy bootstrap *may* diverge; PPO/SAC have empirical (not formal) guarantees.
- **Mnih et al. (2015) — DQN** for high-D *image* state spaces, **Lillicrap et al. (2015) — DDPG** and **Haarnoja et al. (2018) — SAC** for high-D *continuous* action spaces — these are the *must-cite* algorithms in any 14-mark answer.
- **Numerical defaults (KTU-favoured):** $\gamma = 0.99$, replay buffer $= 10^{6}$, batch size $= 32$, learning rate $= 10^{-4}$ (Adam), target-update period $C = 10^{4}$, target-network soft-update $\tau = 10^{-3}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Continuous State and Action Spaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4: Continuous State and Action Spaces

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous State and Action Spaces** refer to reinforcement learning (RL) problem settings in which the state space $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and/or the action space $\mathcal{A} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{m}$ are infinite, uncountable sets (typically continuous intervals, e.g., position, velocity, torque, angle). Since tabular methods cannot store a distinct value $Q(s,a)$ for every $(s,a)$ pair, the agent is forced to **generalize** by approximating the value function $V(s)$, action-value function $Q(s,a)$, or the policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ using a parameterized function $\hat{f}(s, a; \mathbf{w})$, where $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is a learnable parameter vector.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this module pivots the student's focus from exact, finite-Markov decision processes (MDPs) solved by dynamic programming (DP) or tabular Monte-Carlo (MC)/Temporal-Difference (TD) learning, to **approximate methods** capable of scaling to high-dimensional or real-valued environments such as robotics, autonomous driving, and game control.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Pin-Cushion vs. Net" Analogy**
> Imagine you are blindfolded in a vast, smooth hill. Your goal is to find the lowest valley. In the **discrete (tabular)** world, you have a map with a pin for every square inch of the hill — the map is exact, but you must remember the height of *every* pin. In the **continuous world**, you cannot place a pin at every point (there are infinitely many). So instead, you stretch a **flexible net** over the hill. The net is held up by a few pegs (the parameters $\mathbf{w}$). By adjusting the pegs, the net bends to match the hill's shape. This flexible net is your **function approximator** — it does not know the height of *every* point, but it *interpolates* a believable height for unseen points from the pegs it has already learned.

### 1.3 Why Continuous Spaces Break Tabular Methods

| Property | Tabular RL | Approximate / Continuous RL |
|---|---|---|
| State Space Size | Finite, $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert \le 10^{6}$ (practical cap) | Infinite or $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert \to \infty$ |
| Storage of $Q(s,a)$ | One entry per pair | Parameter vector $\mathbf{w}$ of fixed dim $d$ |
| Generalization | None (each state is unique) | Built-in (similar states $\to$ similar values) |
| Convergence Guarantees | Strong (e.g., Q-learning convergence) | Weaker (only local optima guaranteed) |
| Update Rule | Exact, point-wise | Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) over a loss |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU module specifically demands knowledge of **(a)** linear function approximation, **(b)** neural network approximation, **(c)** experience replay and target networks (famous from DQN), and **(d)** policy gradient methods (REINFORCE) for handling continuous action spaces.

### 1.4 The Two Grand Strategies

Continuous-space RL is handled by one of two paradigms:

1. **Value-Based Approximation** — Approximate $Q(s,a; \mathbf{w})$ and act greedily. Works for **discrete actions over continuous states** (e.g., Deep Q-Network). The action $a$ must be chosen from a finite set so that $\arg\max_{a} Q(s,a;\mathbf{w})$ is tractable.
2. **Policy-Based Approximation** — Directly parameterize the policy $\pi(a \vert s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ (typically as a Gaussian over a continuous action) and optimize its parameters by **gradient ascent on expected return** $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$. Works for **continuous actions** natively (e.g., REINFORCE, Actor-Critic).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General Approximation Framework

We replace the true value function $V_{\pi}(s)$ (or $Q_{\pi}(s,a)$) with an approximation $\hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w})$, where $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is a finite-dimensional weight vector. The goal is to find $\mathbf{w}^{*}$ that minimizes a **Mean Squared Value Error (MSVE)** objective:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[\left(V_{\pi}(S) - \hat{V}(S, \mathbf{w})\right)^{2}\right]$$

The minimization is done by **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)**. After observing a sample (or an MC/TD target) $U_{t}$, we update:

$$\mathbf{w}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}_{t} + \alpha \left[U_{t} - \hat{V}(S_{t}, \mathbf{w}_{t})\right] \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{V}(S_{t}, \mathbf{w}_{t})$$

where $\alpha$ is the step-size (learning rate) and $U_{t}$ is the bootstrapped target (e.g., $U_{t} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w})$ for semi-gradient TD(0)).

### 2.2 Linear Function Approximation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **linear function approximator** represents the value as a dot product of a feature vector and a weight vector: $\hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} w_{i} x_{i}(s)$, where $\mathbf{x}(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is a hand-crafted feature vector (e.g., polynomials, radial basis functions, tile coding).

**Feature construction techniques** (must know for KTU viva):
- **Polynomial features:** $\mathbf{x}(s) = (1, s, s^{2}, \ldots, s^{d})^{\top}$
- **Radial Basis Functions (RBFs):** $x_{i}(s) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Vert s - c_{i} \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_{i}^{2}}\right)$
- **Tile coding / Coarse coding:** Binary features over coarse grid tilings.
- **Fourier basis:** $x_{i}(s) = \cos(\pi \, \mathbf{c}_{i}^{\top} s)$

The gradient is trivially $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{x}(s)$, so the update is:

$$\mathbf{w}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}_{t} + \alpha \left[U_{t} - \hat{V}(S_{t}, \mathbf{w}_{t})\right] \mathbf{x}(S_{t})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why linear?** It is the *only* function class where convergence guarantees are well-understood for off-policy and bootstrapped targets. With non-linear approximators (e.g., neural networks), divergence is a real risk — hence the need for **target networks** and **experience replay**.

### 2.3 Non-Linear (Neural Network) Function Approximation

A neural network $\hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w})$ (or $\hat{Q}(s, a, \mathbf{w})$) is a *universal approximator* (per the Cybenko/Hornik theorems). The weights are updated by **backpropagation** of the TD error through the network. The network may be:
- A **shallow MLP** for low-dimensional state vectors.
- A **CNN** for image states (e.g., Atari frames in DQN).
- An **RNN/LSTM** for partially observable / time-series states.

The loss minimized is:

$$L(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbb{E}\left[\left(R + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat{Q}(S', a', \mathbf{w}^{-}) - \hat{Q}(S, A, \mathbf{w})\right)^{2}\right]$$

where $\mathbf{w}^{-}$ is the **target network's** weights and $\mathbf{w}$ is the **online network's** weights.

### 2.4 The Two Pillars of Stable Deep RL: Experience Replay & Target Networks

| Pillar | Problem it Solves | How it Works |
|---|---|---|
| **Experience Replay Buffer** $\mathcal{D}$ | Successive samples $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$ are highly **correlated**; SGD assumes i.i.d. samples and diverges otherwise. | Store transitions in a large buffer $\mathcal{D}$. Sample **mini-batches uniformly at random** for updates. Decorrelates samples and reuses data (improving sample efficiency). |
| **Target Network** $\mathbf{w}^{-}$ | The TD target depends on $\mathbf{w}$ (chases a moving target) → **non-stationarity** & divergence. | Maintain a **slowly updated copy** of the online network used only to compute the target. Updated either by (i) **periodic hard copy** every $C$ steps, or (ii) **Polyak averaging**: $\mathbf{w}^{-} \leftarrow \tau \mathbf{w} + (1-\tau)\mathbf{w}^{-}$. |

### 2.5 Policy Gradient Theorem & REINFORCE (for Continuous Actions)

When actions are continuous, $\arg\max_{a} \hat{Q}(s, a)$ is itself an optimization problem (often intractable). Solution: **directly parameterize the policy** and perform gradient *ascent* on the expected return.

**Policy parameterization:** For continuous actions, a common choice is a **Gaussian policy**:

$$a \sim \pi(a \vert s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathcal{N}\left(\mu(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}), \sigma^{2}(s; \boldsymbol{\theta})\right)$$

The **Policy Gradient Theorem** (Sutton et al., 2000) states that for any differentiable policy $\pi(a \vert s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ and any policy-compatible baseline $b(s)$:

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \propto \sum_{s} \mu_{\pi}(s) \sum_{a} Q_{\pi}(s,a) \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \pi(a \vert s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$$

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[Q_{\pi}(S_t, A_t) \, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \ln \pi(A_t \vert S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The term $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \ln \pi(A_t \vert S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ is called the **score function** or **eligibility vector**. Sampling $Q_{\pi}(S_t, A_t)$ by a Monte-Carlo return $G_t$ yields the celebrated **REINFORCE** algorithm.

**REINFORCE update** (per episode, per step):

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} + \alpha \, G_{t} \, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \ln \pi(A_t \vert S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$$

A **baseline** $b(s)$ is subtracted from $G_t$ to drastically **reduce variance** without changing the expected gradient. The state-value baseline $b(s) = \hat{V}(S_t, \mathbf{w})$ gives the **Advantage Function**:

$$A(S_t, A_t) = Q(S_t, A_t) - V(S_t)$$

### 2.6 Actor–Critic Architecture

Actor-Critic combines the strengths of value-based and policy-based methods:

- **Actor** — The policy $\pi(a \vert s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ that proposes actions.
- **Critic** — The value function $\hat{V}(s; \mathbf{w})$ that evaluates the actor's choices by computing the TD error $\delta_{t} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(S_{t+1}; \mathbf{w}) - \hat{V}(S_{t}; \mathbf{w})$, which serves as a low-variance estimator of the advantage.

Updates:

$$\delta_{t} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(S_{t+1}; \mathbf{w}) - \hat{V}(S_{t}; \mathbf{w})$$

$$\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} + \alpha_{c} \, \delta_{t} \, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{V}(S_t; \mathbf{w})$$

$$\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} + \alpha_{a} \, \delta_{t} \, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \ln \pi(A_t \vert S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta})$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note how $\delta_{t}$ (a **bootstrapped**, low-variance estimate) replaces $G_{t}$ (a high-variance MC return) in the actor's update — this is the critical Actor-Critic improvement over vanilla REINFORCE.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for the KTU Board Exam (Module 4)**

| # | Concept | Equation | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear value approximation | $\hat{V}(s,\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(s)$ | $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d},\ \mathbf{x}(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| 2 | MSVE objective | $J(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[\left(V_{\pi}(S) - \hat{V}(S, \mathbf{w})\right)^{2}\right]$ | Scalar, $\ge 0$ |
| 3 | SGD / semi-gradient update | $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} + \alpha\, [U_{t} - \hat{V}(S_t, \mathbf{w})]\, \mathbf{x}(S_t)$ | $\alpha$ = step-size (e.g., $10^{-3}$) |
| 4 | TD target with target net | $Y_{t} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'}\hat{Q}(S_{t+1}, a'; \mathbf{w}^{-})$ | $\gamma \in [0,1)$ |
| 5 | DQN loss | $L(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbb{E}\left[(Y_{t} - \hat{Q}(S, A; \mathbf{w}))^{2}\right]$ | Minimized per minibatch |
| 6 | Polyak (soft) target update | $\mathbf{w}^{-} \leftarrow \tau\, \mathbf{w} + (1-\tau)\, \mathbf{w}^{-}$ | $\tau \ll 1$ (e.g., $10^{-2}$) |
| 7 | Gaussian policy | $a \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu(s;\boldsymbol{\theta}),\, \sigma^{2}(s;\boldsymbol{\theta}))$ | Continuous $a \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 8 | Score function | $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \ln \pi(a\vert s;\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | Column vector in $\mathbb{R}^{\vert\boldsymbol{\theta}\vert}$ |
| 9 | Policy Gradient Theorem | $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[Q_{\pi}(S,A)\,\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\ln\pi(A\vert S;\boldsymbol{\theta})]$ | Foundation of policy methods |
| 10 | REINFORCE update | $\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} + \alpha\, G_{t}\, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\ln\pi(A_{t}\vert S_{t};\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | Uses MC return $G_t$ |
| 11 | Advantage | $A(S_t, A_t) = Q(S_t, A_t) - V(S_t)$ | Reduces variance in PG |
| 12 | TD error (= 1-step advantage estimate) | $\delta_{t} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(S_{t+1}) - \hat{V}(S_t)$ | Used in Actor-Critic |
| 13 | Actor update | $\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} + \alpha_{a}\, \delta_{t}\, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\ln\pi(A_{t}\vert S_t;\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | $\alpha_{a}$ = actor LR |
| 14 | Critic update | $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} + \alpha_{c}\, \delta_{t}\, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\hat{V}(S_t;\mathbf{w})$ | $\alpha_{c}$ = critic LR |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Continuous State/Action | Method Used |
|---|---|---|
| Robotic Arm Control (joint angles, torques) | $\mathcal{S} = \mathbb{R}^{n},\ \mathcal{A} = \mathbb{R}^{m}$ | Policy Gradient / DDPG |
| Autonomous Vehicle Steering (steering angle in $[-1,1]$) | $\mathcal{S}$ = image, $\mathcal{A} = \mathbb{R}$ | DDPG / PPO |
| HVAC / Energy Control in Smart Buildings | $\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}$ continuous | Actor-Critic |
| Inverted Pendulum / Cartpole (angle, velocity) | $\mathcal{S} = \mathbb{R}^{4}$ | DQN (discrete actions) or Policy Gradient |
| Portfolio Management (asset allocation weights) | $\mathcal{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ continuous | Policy Gradient |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From MSVE to Semi-Gradient TD(0) Update

**Goal:** Derive the linear TD(0) parameter update rule.

**Step 1.** Start with the MSVE objective under on-policy distribution $\mu_{\pi}$:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \mu_{\pi}(s) \left[ V_{\pi}(s) - \hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w}) \right]^{2}$$

**Step 2.** Take the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{w}$. Let $\hat{V}(s,\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(s)$:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{s} \mu_{\pi}(s) \cdot 2 \left[V_{\pi}(s) - \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(s)\right] \cdot (-\mathbf{x}(s))$$

**Step 3.** To make this a true gradient-descent update, the **Bellman optimality** substitutes $V_{\pi}(s) \approx \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w})]$ (the bootstrapped target). Because this target itself depends on $\mathbf{w}$, we **ignore its gradient** (the "semi-gradient" trick).

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(\mathbf{w}) \approx -2 \left[ U_{t} - \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(S_t) \right] \mathbf{x}(S_t)$$

**Step 4.** Move in the negative gradient direction with step-size $\alpha$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{w}_{t+1} &= \mathbf{w}_{t} - \frac{\alpha}{2} \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(\mathbf{w}_{t}) \\
&= \mathbf{w}_{t} + \alpha \left[ U_{t} - \mathbf{w}_{t}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(S_{t}) \right] \mathbf{x}(S_{t})
\end{aligned}$$

For TD(0), $U_{t} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, \mathbf{w}_{t}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(S_{t+1})$:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}_{t} + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \, \mathbf{w}_{t}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(S_{t+1}) - \mathbf{w}_{t}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(S_{t}) \right] \mathbf{x}(S_{t})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Linear TD(0) fixed point** solution. The fixed point $\mathbf{w}_{TD} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ is guaranteed to exist (under linear function approximation and on-policy data) where $\mathbf{A} = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}(S)(\mathbf{x}(S) - \gamma \mathbf{x}(S'))^{\top}]$ and $\mathbf{b} = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1}\mathbf{x}(S)]$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Policy Gradient Theorem (Sutton et al., 2000)

**Goal:** Show that $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[Q_{\pi}(S,A)\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\ln\pi(A \vert S;\boldsymbol{\theta})]$.

**Step 1.** Define the **objective** as the state-value from a designated start state $s_0$ (or average over $s_0 \sim d_0$):

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = V_{\pi_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}}(s_0) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s_0;\boldsymbol{\theta}) \, Q_{\pi_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}}(s_0, a)$$

**Step 2.** Differentiate w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. (The policy depends on $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ both directly *and* implicitly via the action distribution affecting future states — this is what makes the proof non-trivial.)

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) &= \sum_{a} \left[ \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\pi(a \vert s_0;\boldsymbol{\theta}) \, Q_{\pi}(s_0, a) + \pi(a \vert s_0;\boldsymbol{\theta}) \, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} Q_{\pi}(s_0, a) \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** Use the **log-derivative trick** $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\pi = \pi \cdot \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\ln\pi$:

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s_0;\boldsymbol{\theta}) \left[ \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\ln\pi(a \vert s_0;\boldsymbol{\theta}) \, Q_{\pi}(s_0, a) + \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} Q_{\pi}(s_0, a) \right]$$

**Step 4.** Recursively expand the gradient of $Q$ using the Bellman equation $Q_{\pi}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \vert s,a)[r + \gamma V_{\pi}(s')]$ and the chain rule. After repeated substitution and using the **visit distribution** $\mu_{\pi}(s)$ (steady-state probability of being in state $s$ under $\pi$), all the recursive terms collapse to give:

$$\boxed{\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \propto \sum_{s} \mu_{\pi}(s) \sum_{a} Q_{\pi}(s, a) \, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \pi(a \vert s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[Q_{\pi}(S, A) \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \ln \pi(A \vert S; \boldsymbol{\theta})\right]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The proof's key insight: even though $Q_{\pi}$ depends on $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ indirectly through the *future* trajectory distribution, the recursive expansion shows those terms cancel exactly, leaving only the **direct** dependence of $\pi$ on $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ scaled by the *current-step* $Q_{\pi}$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Score Function for a Gaussian Policy

**Goal:** Compute $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \ln \pi(a \vert s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ for a Gaussian policy with mean $\mu(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}_{\mu})$ and (state-independent) variance $\sigma^{2}$ (so $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\boldsymbol{\theta}_{\mu})$).

**Step 1.** Gaussian log-density:

$$\ln \pi(a \vert s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^{2}) - \frac{(a - \mu(s;\boldsymbol{\theta}))^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}$$

**Step 2.** Differentiate w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ (only $\mu$ depends on $\boldsymbol{\theta}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \ln \pi(a \vert s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) &= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \cdot 2(a - \mu(s;\boldsymbol{\theta})) \cdot (-1) \cdot \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mu(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) \\
&= \frac{(a - \mu(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}))}{\sigma^{2}} \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mu(s; \boldsymbol{\theta})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute into the REINFORCE update with MC return $G_t$:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} + \alpha \, G_{t} \cdot \frac{(A_{t} - \mu(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}))}{\sigma^{2}} \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mu(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $(A_t - \mu(S_t; \boldsymbol{\theta}))$ has a beautiful interpretation: if the action $A_t$ was "above average" (larger than the current mean), the policy is **reinforced** to push the mean in that direction (scaled by $G_t$).

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Baseline Subtraction Preserves Expected Gradient

**Claim:** Subtracting a state-only function $b(s)$ from $Q_{\pi}(s,a)$ in the policy gradient does not change $\mathbb{E}[\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J]$.

**Proof.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[b(S) \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \ln \pi(A \vert S; \boldsymbol{\theta})\right] &= \sum_{s} \mu_{\pi}(s) b(s) \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s;\boldsymbol{\theta}) \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \ln \pi(a \vert s;\boldsymbol{\theta}) \\
&= \sum_{s} \mu_{\pi}(s) b(s) \sum_{a} \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \pi(a \vert s;\boldsymbol{\theta}) \\
&= \sum_{s} \mu_{\pi}(s) b(s) \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \underbrace{\sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s;\boldsymbol{\theta})}_{= 1} \\
&= \sum_{s} \mu_{\pi}(s) b(s) \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} 1 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Hence the gradient with advantage $A(s,a) = Q_{\pi}(s,a) - b(s)$ equals the original gradient. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Why subtract? The variance of $\mathbb{E}[G_t \nabla \ln \pi]$ is reduced from $\text{Var}(G_t)$ to $\text{Var}(G_t - b(S_t))$, which is strictly smaller when $b(s)$ is correlated with $G_t$. The best baseline is $b(s) = \mathbb{E}[G_t \vert S_t = s] = V_{\pi}(s)$, yielding the advantage.

### 3.5 Worked Example (Analytical Matrix Style)

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A 1-D continuous-state MDP has states $s \in \mathbb{R}$, actions $a \in \{-1, +1\}$ (discrete). The feature vector is $\mathbf{x}(s) = (1, s)^{\top}$. True values: $V_{\pi}(s) = 2s$. An episode sample gives $S_t = 0.5$, $R_{t+1} = 1.0$, $S_{t+1} = 0.6$, $\gamma = 0.9$. Initial weights $\mathbf{w}_0 = (0, 0)^{\top}$, learning rate $\alpha = 0.1$. Compute one **linear TD(0)** update.

**Step 1 — Features:**
$\mathbf{x}(S_t) = (1, 0.5)^{\top}$, $\mathbf{x}(S_{t+1}) = (1, 0.6)^{\top}$.

**Step 2 — Predicted values:**
$\hat{V}(S_t, \mathbf{w}_0) = (0, 0) \cdot (1, 0.5)^{\top} = 0$. $\hat{V}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}_0) = 0$.

**Step 3 — TD target:**
$U_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}_0) = 1.0 + 0.9 \times 0 = 1.0$.

**Step 4 — TD error:**
$\delta_t = U_t - \hat{V}(S_t, \mathbf{w}_0) = 1.0 - 0 = 1.0$.

**Step 5 — Update:**
$\mathbf{w}_1 = \mathbf{w}_0 + \alpha \cdot \delta_t \cdot \mathbf{x}(S_t) = (0,0)^{\top} + 0.1 \cdot 1.0 \cdot (1, 0.5)^{\top} = (0.1,\ 0.05)^{\top}$.

**Step 6 — Verify new prediction:**
$\hat{V}(S_t, \mathbf{w}_1) = 0.1 \cdot 1 + 0.05 \cdot 0.5 = 0.125$. (Closer to true $V_{\pi}(0.5) = 1.0$; needs more steps.)

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture: Deep RL Agent in Continuous Spaces

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Env[Environment<br/>Continuous s in R^n, a in R^m]
    ActSel[Action Selector<br/>argmax or sample from pi]
    Actor[Actor Network<br/>pi(a mid s ; theta)]
    Critic[Critic Network<br/>hat V or hat Q s,a ; w]
    Replay[Experience Replay Buffer D]
    TargetNet[Target Network<br/>hat Q s,a ; w minus]
    Loss[Loss Computation<br/>TD Error delta]
    Upd[Gradient Update<br/>SGD on theta, w]
    PolicyOut[Continuous Action a]

    Env -- State s --> ActSel
    ActSel --> Actor
    Actor -- Policy params theta --> PolicyOut
    PolicyOut -- Action a --> Env
    Env -- Reward r, Next state s prime --> Replay
    Replay -- Mini-batch sampling --> Loss
    Critic -- Current value Q s,a;w --> Loss
    TargetNet -- Target value --> Loss
    Loss -- TD error delta --> Upd
    Upd -- Updates theta --> Actor
    Upd -- Updates w --> Critic
    Upd -- Periodic or Polyak copy --> TargetNet
```

### 4.2 Algorithmic Flow: REINFORCE with Baseline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Initialize theta, w, alpha])
    Init[Set step counter t = 0]
    Rollout[Generate episode using pi_a_mid_s ; theta]
    StepRecord[Record S_t, A_t, R_t+1 for t = 0 to T-1]
    CalcG[Compute return G_t for each step t]
    CalcV[Compute hat V S_t ; w as baseline]
    CalcAdv[Advantage A_t = G_t - hat V S_t ; w]
    ActorUpd[theta leftarrow theta + alpha_a A_t grad ln pi A_t mid S_t]
    CriticUpd[w leftarrow w + alpha_c A_t grad hat V S_t ; w]
    Conv{Converged?}
    EndN([End])
    DecT[Increment episode counter]

    Start --> Init --> Rollout --> StepRecord --> CalcG --> CalcV --> CalcAdv --> ActorUpd --> CriticUpd --> Conv
    Conv -- No --> DecT --> Rollout
    Conv -- Yes --> EndN
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: DQN with Replay & Target Net

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TrainingLoop[Training Loop]
        D1[Transition Sampling<br/>Uniform random from D]
        D2[Online Network Forward Pass<br/>hat Q S A ; w]
        D3[Target Network Forward Pass<br/>hat Q S prime a prime ; w minus]
        D4[TD Target Y = R + gamma max hat Q S prime a prime ; w minus]
        D5[Loss L = E half Y - hat Q S A ; w squared]
        D6[Backprop to update w]
    end
    subgraph SyncMechanism[Target Sync Mechanism]
        S1{Update Trigger}
        S2[Hard Copy: w minus leftarrow w every C steps]
        S3[Soft Polyak: w minus leftarrow tau w + 1 minus tau w minus]
    end
    D5 --> S1
    S1 -- Every C steps --> S2
    S1 -- Every step --> S3
    S2 --> D1
    S3 --> D1
```

### 4.4 Comparison Schematic: Value-Based vs Policy-Based vs Actor-Critic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VB[Value Based<br/>DQN Family]
    PB[Policy Based<br/>REINFORCE PG]
    AC[Actor Critic<br/>A2C DDPG PPO]

    VB -- Uses --> Argmax[argmax over discrete a<br/>Cannot handle continuous a]
    PB -- Uses --> Sample[Direct sampling from pi<br/>Handles continuous a natively]
    AC -- Combines --> Both[Critic evaluates Actor<br/>Continuous a with low variance]

    Argmax --> Lim1[Limitation: No continuous action]
    Sample --> Lim2[Limitation: High variance]
    Both --> Lim3[Limitation: Two networks to tune]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]** **Q1.** Differentiate between **on-policy** and **off-policy** learning in the context of function approximation. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**
In **on-policy** learning, the agent improves the same policy that is being followed during data collection (e.g., **SARSA with function approximation**, where the next action $A_{t+1}$ is sampled from the current $\pi$). In **off-policy** learning, the agent improves a *target* policy different from the *behaviour* policy used to gather experience (e.g., **DQN**, where the behaviour is $\epsilon$-greedy but the target is the greedy policy via $\max_{a'}\hat{Q}(S', a'; \mathbf{w}^{-})$). Off-policy methods are more powerful but face **stability issues** with bootstrapping under function approximation, often requiring experience replay and target networks.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** **Q2.** What is the **score function** in the policy gradient theorem, and why is the $\log$ of the policy used instead of the policy itself?

**Model Answer:**
The **score function** is $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \ln \pi(a \vert s; \boldsymbol{\theta})$. It is used because of the **log-derivative trick**: $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \pi = \pi \cdot \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \ln \pi$. This converts the gradient of a *probability* (which can be tiny and numerically unstable) into a gradient of a *log-probability* (well-behaved, additive across dimensions, and unbiased). Furthermore, in expectation, $\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\ln\pi] = 0$, so the score function acts as a centred, zero-mean "direction indicator" scaled by the advantage.

---

### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model — Module 4]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **experience replay** and **target network** mechanisms used in Deep Q-Networks (DQN). Why are they necessary when the function approximator is a non-linear neural network?
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Write the complete **loss function** and **parameter update** for DQN. Show how Polyak averaging of the target network differs from a hard copy.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Experience Replay and Target Networks:**

**Experience Replay:**
- A large buffer $\mathcal{D}$ of fixed capacity (e.g., $10^{6}$ transitions) stores tuples $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, \text{done})$.
- During training, **mini-batches** of size $B$ (e.g., $32$ or $64$) are sampled **uniformly at random** from $\mathcal{D}$.
- The Q-network is trained on this minibatch by stochastic gradient descent.
- **Why needed?** Sequential RL samples are **temporally correlated**, violating the i.i.d. assumption of SGD and causing divergence. Random sampling **decorrelates** the data. It also **reuses** each transition for multiple updates, improving **sample efficiency**.

**Target Network:**
- A *second* Q-network with parameters $\mathbf{w}^{-}$ is maintained. It is used **only** to compute the TD target $Y_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'}\hat{Q}(S_{t+1}, a'; \mathbf{w}^{-})$.
- The online network's parameters $\mathbf{w}$ are updated by backprop; the target network's parameters are held fixed (or slowly updated) for several steps.
- **Why needed?** Without it, the target $Y_t$ *chases* a moving quantity that itself depends on $\mathbf{w}$ — this is the **"moving target" problem** and is a primary cause of the **"deadly triad"** divergence (off-policy + bootstrapping + function approximation).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write "experience replay removes the correlation between samples." This is **imprecise**. The correct phrase is "*decorrelates*" or "*reduces correlation*." Correlation cannot be fully removed from a Markov chain, only mitigated. Marks deducted for over-claiming.

**(b) [7 Marks] DQN Loss and Polyak vs. Hard Copy:**

**Loss function (per sampled minibatch):**

$$L(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{i=1}^{B} \left( R_{i} + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat{Q}(S'_i, a'; \mathbf{w}^{-}) - \hat{Q}(S_i, A_i; \mathbf{w}) \right)^{2}$$

**Gradient update (online network):**

$$\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} - \alpha \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} L(\mathbf{w})$$

**Hard Copy** (periodic, every $C$ steps, e.g., $C = 10{,}000$):
$$\mathbf{w}^{-} \leftarrow \mathbf{w}$$

**Polyak / Soft Update** (every step, with small $\tau$, e.g., $\tau = 0.005$):
$$\mathbf{w}^{-} \leftarrow \tau \mathbf{w} + (1 - \tau) \mathbf{w}^{-}$$

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Hard Copy | Polyak Averaging |
|---|---|---|
| Update frequency | Every $C$ steps (rare) | Every step (continuous) |
| Target "freshness" | Stale for $C$ steps | Slowly tracks online |
| Stability vs. learning speed | More stable, slower learning | Less stable, faster learning |
| Hyperparameter to tune | $C$ | $\tau$ |
| Used in | Original DQN (Mnih et al., 2015) | DDPG, TD3, soft actor-critic |

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice — Alternative]:**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **Policy Gradient Theorem** starting from the objective $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = V_{\pi_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}}(s_0)$. Clearly state the role of the **log-derivative trick** and the **visit distribution** $\mu_{\pi}(s)$.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] For a 1-D continuous-action problem, suppose the policy is Gaussian with mean $\mu(s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \theta_{0} + \theta_{1} s$ and fixed $\sigma^{2} = 0.5$. The episode has a single transition: $S_0 = 1.0$, $A_0 = 1.2$, $R_1 = 2.0$. Initial $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{0} = (0.0, 0.0)^{\top}$, $\alpha = 0.1$. Apply **one REINFORCE update**.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Derivation of Policy Gradient Theorem:** (See full derivation in Section 3.2 above. Key steps to be written in the answer script:)

1. Start with $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = V_{\pi}(s_0) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s_0) Q_{\pi}(s_0, a)$.
2. Differentiate w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ using product rule.
3. Apply $\nabla \pi = \pi \nabla \ln \pi$ (log-derivative trick).
4. Expand $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} Q_{\pi}(s_0, a)$ using the Bellman equation; recursively substitute and use the steady-state visit distribution $\mu_{\pi}(s)$.
5. Conclude: $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \propto \sum_{s}\mu_{\pi}(s) \sum_{a} Q_{\pi}(s,a) \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\pi(a \vert s;\boldsymbol{\theta})$.

**Role of the log-derivative trick:** Converts gradient of a probability (tiny, possibly zero) into gradient of a log-probability (well-behaved, additive).

**Role of the visit distribution $\mu_{\pi}(s)$:** It is the long-run fraction of time the agent spends in state $s$ under $\pi$. It re-weights the gradient by *how often* each state is encountered, so the policy is updated proportionally to the relevance of each state.

**(b) [7 Marks] REINFORCE Update — Numerical Worked Example:**

**Step 1 — MC Return:** Episode has one step, so $G_0 = R_1 = 2.0$.

**Step 2 — Current mean:**
$\mu(S_0; \boldsymbol{\theta}_0) = 0.0 + 0.0 \cdot 1.0 = 0.0$.

**Step 3 — Score function for Gaussian policy:**
$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \ln \pi(a \vert s; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{(a - \mu(s;\boldsymbol{\theta}))}{\sigma^{2}} \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\mu(s;\boldsymbol{\theta})$.

Compute the components:
- $a - \mu = 1.2 - 0.0 = 1.2$
- $\frac{a-\mu}{\sigma^{2}} = \frac{1.2}{0.5} = 2.4$
- $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mu(s;\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} (\theta_0 + \theta_1 s) = (1,\ s)^{\top} = (1,\ 1.0)^{\top}$.

So $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \ln \pi = 2.4 \cdot (1, 1.0)^{\top} = (2.4,\ 2.4)^{\top}$.

**Step 4 — REINFORCE update:**
$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{0} + \alpha \cdot G_0 \cdot \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \ln \pi$
$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{1} = (0, 0)^{\top} + 0.1 \cdot 2.0 \cdot (2.4, 2.4)^{\top} = (0.48,\ 0.48)^{\top}$.

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** The positive return $G_0 = 2.0$ *reinforces* the action $A_0 = 1.2$ (which was already above the current mean $0.0$). The new mean shifts towards this "good" action. After update: $\mu(S_0; \boldsymbol{\theta}_1) = 0.48 + 0.48 \cdot 1.0 = 0.96$, closer to $1.2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is forgetting the factor $\frac{1}{\sigma^{2}}$ in the score function. Without it, the policy update is **scale-dependent on the variance**, leading to unstable learning when $\sigma$ changes. Always derive the score function carefully for the chosen policy distribution. Also, students confuse **REINFORCE** (MC, uses $G_t$) with **Actor-Critic** (bootstrapped, uses $\delta_t$); marks deducted if mixed up.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Why continuous spaces?** Real-world control problems (robotics, vehicles, finance) have infinite or high-dimensional real-valued state/action spaces — tabular methods are infeasible.
- ✅ **Function approximation** replaces the lookup table with a parameterized function $\hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w})$ or $\hat{Q}(s, a, \mathbf{w})$; the goal is to minimize **MSVE** via SGD.
- ✅ **Linear FA** ($\hat{V} = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(s)$) is the only class with strong theoretical convergence guarantees; uses features like polynomials, RBFs, or tile coding.
- ✅ **Neural FA** (Deep RL) is a universal approximator but risks **divergence** (the "deadly triad" of off-policy + bootstrapping + non-linear FA).
- ✅ **Experience Replay** buffer $\mathcal{D}$ stores transitions; minibatch SGD samples uniformly at random to break temporal correlation and improve sample efficiency.
- ✅ **Target Network** ($\mathbf{w}^{-}$) stabilizes the TD target; updated either by hard copy every $C$ steps or by Polyak averaging with rate $\tau$.
- ✅ **Policy Gradient Theorem:** $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J \propto \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[Q_{\pi}(S,A) \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \ln \pi(A \vert S; \boldsymbol{\theta})]$ — foundation of policy-based methods.
- ✅ **REINFORCE:** Monte-Carlo policy gradient using $G_t$ as the estimator of $Q_{\pi}(S_t, A_t)$. High variance, unbiased.
- ✅ **Baseline & Advantage:** Subtracting $b(s) = \hat{V}(s)$ (or $\hat{V}(S_t; \mathbf{w})$) preserves the expected gradient but reduces variance — the **Advantage function** $A(s,a) = Q(s,a) - V(s)$.
- ✅ **Actor-Critic:** Combines policy gradient (actor) with TD bootstrapping (critic). Critic provides low-variance TD error $\delta_t$ as the advantage estimate; actor updates $\boldsymbol{\theta}$; critic updates $\mathbf{w}$.
- ✅ **Gaussian Policy** is the standard parameterization for continuous actions: $a \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu(s;\boldsymbol{\theta}), \sigma^2)$; score function involves $\frac{(a-\mu)}{\sigma^{2}}$.
- ✅ **Convergence:** Linear TD(0) converges to a unique fixed point $\mathbf{w}_{TD}$; neural network RL has **no global convergence guarantee** — only empirical stability.
- ✅ **Engineering impact:** DQN (Atari), DDPG/PPO (robotics), A3C/AlphaGo (game AI) all rely on the ideas in this module.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Module 4 Specific):**
> 1. **Confusing value-based and policy-based methods** — DQN is value-based, REINFORCE/PPO is policy-based; mixing them up in viva loses easy marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the baseline argument** — always prove/cite that $\mathbb{E}[b(S) \nabla \ln \pi] = 0$ when justifying baseline subtraction.
> 3. **Miscounting the TD target** — DQN uses $\max_{a'} \hat{Q}(S', a'; \mathbf{w}^{-})$ inside a *target* net, not the *online* net. The double distinction is the heart of DQN's stability.
> 4. **Not stating the role of $\mu_{\pi}(s)$** in the Policy Gradient Theorem — examiners specifically test whether you understand *why* the theorem holds despite the recursive dependency of $Q_{\pi}$ on $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.
> 5. **Conflating "off-policy" with "experience replay"** — experience replay can be used in on-policy settings too (though less common); it primarily solves correlation, not off-policy distribution shift.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State-Value Function and Action-Value Function with and without Function Approximation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** State-Value Function and Action-Value Function with and without Function Approximation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The State-Value Function V(s) — Formal Definition

The **state-value function**, denoted $V_\pi(s)$, is the central predictive object in Reinforcement Learning. It quantifies *how good* it is for an agent to be in a given state $s$, assuming the agent continues to follow a particular policy $\pi$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard Formulation)**
>
> The state-value function for a policy $\pi$ is the expected return when starting in state $s$ and thereafter following policy $\pi$:
>
> $$V_\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,G_t \;\middle|\; S_t = s\,\right] \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k}\, R_{t+k+1} \;\middle|\; S_t = s\,\right]$$
>
> where $\gamma \in [0,1]$ is the **discount factor**, and the expectation is taken over the stochasticity of the policy and the environment transitions.

### Intuition: "The GPS Quality Score of a State"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Taxi Driver's Brain Map**
>
> Imagine a taxi driver who has driven in a city for years. Every intersection (state) has a "mental score" in the driver's head: *how much total tip money and passenger satisfaction can I expect from this point onwards if I follow my usual driving style?* That mental number, for each intersection, is exactly the state-value function $V_\pi(s)$. The policy $\pi$ is the driver's usual driving style (e.g., "prefer highways at night"). A high score means a great pickup location; a low score means a dead zone.
>
> The driver never computes a number in millions of states — they have a *compact mental model*. That is **function approximation** of the value function.

## 1.2 The Action-Value Function Q(s, a) — Formal Definition

The **action-value function**, also called the *Q-function*, evaluates not just states, but *state-action pairs*. It answers: *if I am in state $s$ and take action $a$, then follow policy $\pi$ afterwards, what return should I expect?*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard Formulation)**
>
> The action-value function for policy $\pi$ is:
>
> $$Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,G_t \;\middle|\; S_t = s,\; A_t = a\,\right] \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k}\, R_{t+k+1} \;\middle|\; S_t = s,\; A_t = a\,\right]$$
>
> The relationship to the state-value function is:
>
> $$V_\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s)\, Q_\pi(s, a)$$

### Intuition: "The Recipe Cookbook Entry"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Chef's Cookbook**
>
> If $V_\pi(s)$ is the taxi driver's intersection score, then $Q_\pi(s, a)$ is the chef's rating of *"I have these ingredients (state $s$). If I choose to bake a soufflé (action $a$) and follow my standard recipe book (policy $\pi$), how tasty will the meal be?"* The chef scores every (ingredients, dish) combination. The state-value $V_\pi(s)$ is the average of $Q_\pi(s, a)$ weighted by how often the chef picks each dish under their standard policy.

## 1.3 Why Function Approximation Is Needed

| Setting | Tabular | Function Approximation |
|---|---|---|
| State space size | Small, finite | Large or continuous |
| Storage | One value per state | Compact weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ |
| Generalization | None | Across similar states |
| Examples | Gridworld, Tic-Tac-Toe | Atari pixels, Go board, robotics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Curse of Dimensionality**
>
> In real-world RL (autonomous driving, AlphaGo, robotic locomotion), the state space is enormous or infinite. Storing a table $V(s)$ for every state is **physically impossible**. We therefore approximate the value function with a parameterized function $\hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w})$ or $\hat{Q}(s, a, \mathbf{w})$, where $\mathbf{w}$ is a learnable weight vector (linear weights, neural network parameters, decision tree splits, etc.).

## 1.4 The Two Key Distinctions (KTU Exam Focus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FOUR Canonical Objects You MUST Know:**
> 1. $V_\pi(s)$ — *state-value under policy* $\pi$
> 2. $Q_\pi(s, a)$ — *action-value under policy* $\pi$
> 3. $V_*(s) = \max_\pi V_\pi(s)$ — *optimal state-value*
> 4. $Q_*(s, a) = \max_\pi Q_\pi(s, a)$ — *optimal action-value*
>
> "Function approximation" can be applied to ANY of these four objects. The KTU exam routinely asks you to write the Bellman equation for each case, both with and without approximation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bellman Equation for $V_\pi(s)$ (Without Function Approximation)

The value function is *recursive*: the value of the current state equals the immediate reward plus the discounted value of the next state, averaged over the stochastic policy and transition dynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bellman Expectation Equation for $V_\pi$:**
>
> $$V_\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\,r + \gamma\, V_\pi(s')\,\right]$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

- Start in state $s$.
- The agent picks action $a$ with probability $\pi(a \mid s)$.
- The environment transitions to $s'$ and emits reward $r$ with probability $p(s', r \mid s, a)$.
- The agent receives $r$ immediately, then continues and accumulates $\gamma V_\pi(s')$.

## 2.2 The Bellman Equation for $Q_\pi(s, a)$ (Without Function Approximation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bellman Expectation Equation for $Q_\pi$:**
>
> $$Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\,r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q_\pi(s', a')\,\right]$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

- Take action $a$ in state $s$.
- Get reward $r$ and next state $s'$.
- The *next* action $a'$ is then chosen by following $\pi$ at $s'$, so we average $Q_\pi(s', a')$ over $a'$.

## 2.3 The Bellman Optimality Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bellman Optimality Equation for $V_*(s)$:**
>
> $$V_*(s) \;=\; \max_{a} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\,r + \gamma\, V_*(s')\,\right]$$
>
> **Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q_*(s, a)$:**
>
> $$Q_*(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\,r + \gamma\, \max_{a'} Q_*(s', a')\,\right]$$
>
> **Key Recovery Relation:**
>
> $$V_*(s) \;=\; \max_{a} Q_*(s, a)$$

## 2.4 Function Approximation — General Framework

Instead of a lookup table, we represent the value function as a parameterized function with weight vector $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Approximate State-Value Function:**
>
> $$\hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w}) \;\approx\; V_\pi(s)$$
>
> **Approximate Action-Value Function:**
>
> $$\hat{Q}(s, a, \mathbf{w}) \;\approx\; Q_\pi(s, a)$$

The weights $\mathbf{w}$ are tuned to minimize an error signal, typically the **Mean Squared Value Error**:

$$\overline{\text{VE}}(\mathbf{w}) \;=\; \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \mu(s)\!\left[\,V_\pi(s) - \hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w})\,\right]^2$$

where $\mu(s) \geq 0$ with $\sum_s \mu(s) = 1$ is the state distribution under the on-policy visitation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why on-policy distribution $\mu(s)$ matters:** It is the *fraction of time* the agent spends in state $s$ while following $\pi$. This is because we care most about accurately predicting values for states we actually visit often — not for unreachable states.

## 2.5 Linear Function Approximation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear State-Value Approximation:**
>
> $$\hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w}) \;=\; \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}(s) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{d} w_i\, x_i(s)$$
>
> where $\mathbf{x}(s) = (x_1(s), x_2(s), \ldots, x_d(s))^{\top}$ is the **feature vector** representing state $s$.

**Why linear?** Convex optimization landscape, guaranteed convergence for many algorithms (e.g., tabular TD(0) is the special case where $\mathbf{x}(s)$ is the one-hot indicator of $s$).

## 2.6 Non-Linear Function Approximation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deep Neural Network Approximation:**
>
> $$\hat{Q}(s, a, \mathbf{w}) \;=\; f_{\text{NN}}(s, a; \mathbf{w})$$
>
> where $\mathbf{w}$ is the concatenation of all network weights and biases, and $f_{\text{NN}}$ is a multi-layer perceptron, CNN, or transformer.

This is the basis of **Deep Q-Networks (DQN)**, where the loss is:

$$L(\mathbf{w}) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{(s,a,r,s') \sim \mathcal{D}}\!\left[\,\bigl(r + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat{Q}(s', a', \mathbf{w}^{-}) - \hat{Q}(s, a, \mathbf{w})\bigr)^2\,\right]$$

where $\mathbf{w}^{-}$ are the **target network weights** (periodically frozen copies of $\mathbf{w}$ for stability).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ | Definition of $V_\pi$ | Theory, Part A |
| 2 | $Q_\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | Definition of $Q_\pi$ | Theory, Part A |
| 3 | $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) Q_\pi(s, a)$ | Consistency relation | Theory |
| 4 | $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')]$ | Bellman expectation for $V_\pi$ | Part B derivations |
| 5 | $Q_\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q_\pi(s', a')]$ | Bellman expectation for $Q_\pi$ | Part B derivations |
| 6 | $V_*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma V_*(s')]$ | Bellman optimality for $V_*$ | Optimal control |
| 7 | $Q_*(s, a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_*(s', a')]$ | Bellman optimality for $Q_*$ | Q-learning derivations |
| 8 | $V_*(s) = \max_a Q_*(s, a)$ | Optimal V–Q link | Theory |
| 9 | $\hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}(s)$ | Linear FA for $V$ | Function approx Part B |
| 10 | $\hat{Q}(s, a, \mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}(s, a)$ | Linear FA for $Q$ | Function approx Part B |
| 11 | $\overline{\text{VE}}(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_s \mu(s)[V_\pi(s) - \hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w})]^2$ | Mean squared value error | Convergence theory |
| 12 | $\Delta \mathbf{w} = \alpha [r + \gamma \hat{V}(s', \mathbf{w}) - \hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w})]\, \mathbf{x}(s)$ | Linear TD(0) update | Algorithm derivations |
| 13 | $L(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbb{E}[(r + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat{Q}(s', a', \mathbf{w}^{-}) - \hat{Q}(s, a, \mathbf{w}))^2]$ | DQN loss | Deep RL |

## 2.8 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Applications of Value Function Approximation:**
> - **Game AI** (DQN on Atari, AlphaGo): $Q$-value approximated by deep CNN.
> - **Robotics** (continuous control): $V(s)$ approximated by neural net for actor-critic.
> - **Recommendation Systems**: $Q(\text{user, item})$ approximated by matrix factorization.
> - **Autonomous Driving**: $V(\text{lidar-image})$ approximated by CNN+MLP for policy evaluation.
> - **AlphaStar (StarCraft II)**: combined VFA with self-play imitation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bellman Equation for $V_\pi$ (Tabular / No Approximation)

**Step 1.** Start from the definition:

$$
V_\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,G_t \;\middle|\; S_t = s\,\right]
$$

**Step 2.** Expand the return $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$:

$$
V_\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \;\middle|\; S_t = s\,\right]
$$

**Step 3.** Use linearity of expectation:

$$
V_\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,R_{t+1} \;\middle|\; S_t = s\,\right] + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,G_{t+1} \;\middle|\; S_t = s\,\right]
$$

**Step 4.** Condition on the next action $A_t = a$ and next state $S_{t+1} = s'$ using the law of total expectation, weighted by $\pi(a \mid s)$ and the transition probability $p(s' \mid s, a)$:

$$
V_\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s, a) \left[\,\mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid s, a, s'] + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s']\,\right]
$$

**Step 5.** The expected reward given $(s, a, s')$ is $\bar{r}(s, a, s') = \sum_r r\, p(r \mid s, a, s')$, and the future-return expectation from $s'$ is exactly $V_\pi(s')$:

$$
V_\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\,r + \gamma\, V_\pi(s')\,\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Compact form:** This is the **Bellman expectation backup**. The right-hand side is a *contraction mapping* on $V_\pi$ with contraction factor $\gamma$, so iterative policy evaluation converges to a unique fixed point.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bellman Equation for $Q_\pi$ (Tabular / No Approximation)

**Step 1.** Start from the definition:

$$
Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,G_t \;\middle|\; S_t = s, A_t = a\,\right]
$$

**Step 2.** Expand $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$:

$$
Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,R_{t+1} \;\middle|\; s, a\,\right] + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,G_{t+1} \;\middle|\; s, a\,\right]
$$

**Step 3.** The first term is the expected immediate reward from $(s, a)$:

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,R_{t+1} \;\middle|\; s, a\,\right] \;=\; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\, r
$$

**Step 4.** The second term conditions on the next state $s'$ and the next action $a'$ chosen by $\pi$:

$$
\gamma \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s, a) \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q_\pi(s', a')
$$

**Step 5.** Combine:

$$
Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\,r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q_\pi(s', a')\,\right]
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q_*(s, a)$

**Step 1.** The optimal action-value function is the maximum over policies:

$$
Q_*(s, a) \;=\; \max_\pi Q_\pi(s, a)
$$

**Step 2.** An optimal policy is greedy w.r.t. $Q_*$:

$$
\pi_*(a' \mid s') \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } a' = \arg\max_{a''} Q_*(s', a'') \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3.** Therefore, in the Bellman expectation equation for $Q_\pi$, replace $\sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q_\pi(s', a')$ with $\max_{a'} Q_*(s', a')$:

$$
Q_*(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\,r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_*(s', a')\,\right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single equation is the heart of **Q-learning**. Once $Q_*(s, a)$ is known, the optimal action is $\arg\max_a Q_*(s, a)$ — no model required.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Linear TD(0) Update Rule for $\hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w})$

**Step 1.** Define the linear approximation:

$$
\hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w}) \;=\; \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}(s)
$$

**Step 2.** The TD(0) target is the one-step bootstrap:

$$
\text{Target} \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma\, \hat{V}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w})
$$

**Step 3.** The TD error (prediction error) is:

$$
\delta_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma\, \hat{V}(S_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}) - \hat{V}(S_t, \mathbf{w})
$$

**Step 4.** Minimize the squared TD error using **stochastic gradient descent**. The loss for sample $t$ is $\tfrac{1}{2} \delta_t^2$. Take the gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}} \!\left[\,\tfrac{1}{2} \delta_t^2\,\right] \;=\; -\delta_t\, \frac{\partial \hat{V}(S_t, \mathbf{w})}{\partial \mathbf{w}}
$$

**Step 5.** For the linear case, $\frac{\partial \hat{V}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{x}(S_t)$, so:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}} \!\left[\,\tfrac{1}{2} \delta_t^2\,\right] \;=\; -\delta_t\, \mathbf{x}(S_t)
$$

**Step 6.** Apply the gradient *descent* update $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} - \alpha \nabla$:

$$
\mathbf{w}_{t+1} \;=\; \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha\, \delta_t\, \mathbf{x}(S_t)
$$

**Step 7.** Substituting $\delta_t$:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{w}_{t+1} \;=\; \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha\!\left[\,R_{t+1} + \gamma\, \mathbf{w}_t^{\top} \mathbf{x}(S_{t+1}) - \mathbf{w}_t^{\top} \mathbf{x}(S_t)\,\right] \mathbf{x}(S_t)}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation of the DQN Loss (Non-Linear FA for $Q$)

**Step 1.** Approximate $Q(s, a)$ with a neural net $Q(s, a; \mathbf{w})$ having parameters $\mathbf{w}$.

**Step 2.** The Q-learning target uses a *target network* with parameters $\mathbf{w}^-$:

$$
y_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a'; \mathbf{w}^{-})
$$

**Step 3.** The loss is the squared TD error averaged over a minibatch sampled from replay buffer $\mathcal{D}$:

$$
L(\mathbf{w}) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{(s, a, r, s') \sim \mathcal{D}}\!\left[\,\bigl(y_t - Q(s, a; \mathbf{w})\bigr)^2\,\right]
$$

**Step 4.** Gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ via backpropagation:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} L(\mathbf{w}) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,2\,(Q(s, a; \mathbf{w}) - y_t)\, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} Q(s, a; \mathbf{w})\,\right]
$$

**Step 5.** Stochastic gradient step:

$$
\mathbf{w} \;\leftarrow\; \mathbf{w} - \alpha\, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} L(\mathbf{w})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why target network $\mathbf{w}^{-}$?** If we used $\mathbf{w}$ itself in the target, the loss would chase a moving target ("chasing your own tail"), leading to divergence. Freezing $\mathbf{w}^{-}$ for $C$ steps and periodically syncing $\mathbf{w}^{-} \leftarrow \mathbf{w}$ stabilizes training.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison: With vs. Without Function Approximation

| Aspect | Tabular (No FA) | Linear FA | Non-Linear FA (e.g., DNN) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Representation | One scalar $V(s)$ per state | $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(s)$ | $f_{\text{NN}}(s; \mathbf{w})$ |
| Parameter count | $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$ | $d$ (small) | millions |
| Generalization | None | Across features | Across raw observations |
| Convergence | Guaranteed, exact | Guaranteed to local optimum of $\overline{\text{VE}}$ | Not guaranteed; can diverge |
| Update rule | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta$ | $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} + \alpha \delta \mathbf{x}(s)$ | $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} + \alpha \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} L$ |
| Memory | $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert)$ | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathbf{w} \vert)$ |
| Best for | Toy MDPs, planning | Moderate-size feature MDPs | Atari, robotics, large observation spaces |
| Key challenge | Scalability | Feature engineering | Stability, sample efficiency |

## 3.7 Proof Sketch: Why $V_\pi$ is Unique (Contraction Mapping)

**Step 1.** Define the Bellman operator $T_\pi$ acting on any bounded function $V : \mathcal{S} \to \mathbb{R}$:

$$
(T_\pi V)(s) \;=\; \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma V(s')]
$$

**Step 2.** For any two functions $V_1, V_2$, take the max-norm $\Vert V \Vert_\infty = \max_s \vert V(s) \vert$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert (T_\pi V_1)(s) - (T_\pi V_2)(s) \vert &\;=\; \Bigl\vert \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s, a)\, \gamma\, [V_1(s') - V_2(s')] \Bigr\vert \\
&\;\leq\; \gamma \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s, a)\, \vert V_1(s') - V_2(s') \vert \\
&\;\leq\; \gamma\, \max_{s'} \vert V_1(s') - V_2(s') \vert \;=\; \gamma\, \Vert V_1 - V_2 \Vert_\infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Therefore $T_\pi$ is a **$\gamma$-contraction** in the max-norm. By the Banach fixed-point theorem, iterated application of $T_\pi$ converges to a unique fixed point, which is exactly $V_\pi$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This contraction property is **lost** when we switch to non-linear function approximation. That is precisely why DQN and policy gradient methods require extra tricks (target networks, experience replay, trust regions, PPO clipping, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow: Value Function Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Agent at State s"] --> B["Policy pi - chooses action a"]
    B --> C["Environment p(s',r | s,a)"]
    C --> D["Immediate reward r"]
    C --> E["Next state s'"]
    D --> F["Bellman Backup Operator"]
    E --> G["Value of s' : V_pi(s') or Q_pi(s',a')"]
    G --> F
    F --> H["V_pi(s) or Q_pi(s,a) fixed point"]
    H --> I{"Function Approximation?"}
    I -- "No - Tabular" --> J["Store exact value in table"]
    I -- "Yes - Linear" --> K["w-transpose x(s) - small w"]
    I -- "Yes - Non-linear" --> L["Deep neural net f_NN(s,a;w)"]
    J --> M["Used for policy improvement"]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of the Four Value Objects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Value Functions in RL"] --> P1["Prediction - given policy pi"]
    Start --> P2["Control - find optimal policy"]
    P1 --> V1["V_pi(s) - state-value"]
    P1 --> V2["Q_pi(s,a) - action-value"]
    P2 --> V3["V*(s) - optimal state-value"]
    P2 --> V4["Q*(s,a) - optimal action-value"]
    V1 --> R1["Bellman expectation equation"]
    V2 --> R2["Bellman expectation equation"]
    V3 --> R3["Bellman optimality equation - max over a"]
    V4 --> R4["Bellman optimality equation - max over a'"]
    R1 --> FA{"Function Approx?"}
    R2 --> FA
    R3 --> FA
    R4 --> FA
    FA -- "No" --> Tab["Tabular: V(s) lookup"]
    FA -- "Yes" --> App["w-transpose x(s) or NN"]
```

## 4.3 DQN Architecture with Function Approximation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["State s - e.g. game frame"] --> C["Conv Layers - feature extraction"]
    C --> F1["Flatten and FC layers"]
    F1 --> Q1["Q(s,a1;w)"]
    F1 --> Q2["Q(s,a2;w)"]
    F1 --> Q3["Q(s,a3;w)"]
    F1 --> Qn["Q(s,aN;w)"]
    Q1 --> MAX["max_a Q(s,a;w) or sample action"]
    Q2 --> MAX
    Q3 --> MAX
    Qn --> MAX
    MAX --> ACT["Selected action a"]
    ACT --> ENV["Environment step - r, s'"]
    ENV --> MEM["Replay Buffer D"]
    MEM --> MINIB["Sample minibatch"]
    MINIB --> TGT["Target Net Q(s',a';w-) frozen"]
    TGT --> LOSS["Loss L = E[(r + gamma max_a' Q(s',a';w-) - Q(s,a;w))^2]"]
    LOSS --> OPT["SGD update on w"]
    OPT -.periodically.-> TGT
```

## 4.4 Linear Function Approximation: Feature-Based State Encoding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["State s - raw observation"] --> PHI["Feature map phi - state to x(s)"]
    PHI --> X1["x1(s) - feature 1"]
    PHI --> X2["x2(s) - feature 2"]
    PHI --> XD["xd(s) - feature d"]
    X1 --> DOT["Dot product w-transpose x(s)"]
    X2 --> DOT
    XD --> DOT
    W1["w1"] --> DOT
    W2["w2"] --> DOT
    WD["wd"] --> DOT
    DOT --> V["Approximate value Vhat s,w"]
```

## 4.5 On-Policy State Distribution and Update Targets

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PI["Policy pi(a | s)"] --> A["Action a sampled"]
    A --> P["Transition p(s',r | s,a)"]
    P --> S1["Visit s1 with probability mu1"]
    P --> S2["Visit s2 with probability mu2"]
    P --> SN["Visit sN with probability muN"]
    S1 --> W1["Weight w1 by mu1 in VE"]
    S2 --> W2["Weight w2 by mu2 in VE"]
    SN --> WN["Weight wN by muN in VE"]
    W1 --> OBJ["Objective: minimize weighted MSE"]
    W2 --> OBJ
    WN --> OBJ
    OBJ --> OPT["Update w via gradient descent"]
    OPT --> PI
```

## 4.6 Summary Block Diagram — Value Function With vs. Without FA

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NOFA["Without Function Approximation - Tabular"]
        S1["State s"] --> T1["Look up table entry V(s)"]
        T1 --> OUT1["Exact V_pi(s) or Q_pi(s,a)"]
    end
    subgraph LINFA["With Linear Function Approximation"]
        S2["State s"] --> FE["Compute features x(s)"]
        FE --> LIN["Compute w-transpose x(s)"]
        LIN --> OUT2["Approximate Vhat s,w"]
    end
    subgraph NNFA["With Non-Linear Function Approximation"]
        S3["State s"] --> NN["Forward pass f_NN(s;w)"]
        NN --> OUT3["Approximate Vhat or Qhat from NN"]
    end
    NOFA --> USE["Used by policy iteration, value iteration, Q-learning"]
    LINFA --> USE
    NNFA --> USE
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Pattern Note:** Part A questions test direct recall of definitions, equations, and one-line derivations. Answer in 3–5 crisp lines.

### **Q1.** Define the state-value function $V_\pi(s)$ and the action-value function $Q_\pi(s, a)$ for a policy $\pi$. State the consistency relation between them. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The state-value function is the expected return when starting from state $s$ and following policy $\pi$:
$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$
- The action-value function is the expected return when starting from state $s$, taking action $a$, then following $\pi$:
$Q_\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$
- Consistency relation: $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) Q_\pi(s, a)$.

*(1 mark for each definition, 1 mark for the consistency relation.)*

---

### **Q2.** State the Bellman optimality equation for $Q_*(s, a)$ and explain why it is the cornerstone of model-free Q-learning. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Bellman optimality equation for $Q_*$ is:

$$
Q_*(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\,r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_*(s', a')\,\right]
$$

- **(1 mark)** It expresses the optimal Q-value recursively using only the optimal Q-values of the *next* state, picking the best next action.
- **(1 mark)** Crucially, the $\max$ is *inside* the expectation over $s'$ and $r$ — but the dynamics $p$ can be sampled by interacting with the environment, so no explicit model is needed.
- **(1 mark)** Hence Q-learning can update $Q(s, a)$ toward the sampled target $r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$, converging to $Q_*$ under standard stochastic approximation assumptions.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module-Internal Choice Pattern:** Two alternatives A and B. Pick ONE. Each alternative has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Sub-parts may have sub-divisions like (i) and (ii) for 3 and 4 marks.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Derive the Bellman expectation equation for $V_\pi(s)$ from its definition. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1.** Start with the definition:
$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$

**Step 2.** Expand $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \ldots = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$:
$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]$

**Step 3.** Use linearity:
$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s] + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]$

**Step 4.** Condition on the action $a$ chosen by $\pi$ and the next state $s'$:
$V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\, r \;+\; \gamma \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s, a)\, V_\pi(s')$

**Step 5.** Combine the two summations into a single expression:
