- [x] **Loss:** $\mathcal{L} = (y_i - Q(s,a;\theta))^2$ (or Huber in practice)
- [x] **TD target:** $y_i = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a';\theta^{-})$ — *always with $\theta^{-}$*
- [x] **Replay buffer size:** $\approx 10^6$ transitions, uniform random sampling, minibatch $B = 32$
- [x] **Target update frequency:** every $C = 10{,}000$ gradient steps (hard copy)
- [x] **$\varepsilon$-greedy:** decay from $1.0$ to $0.1$ over first $10^6$ steps
- [x] **Optimizer:** RMSProp (original paper) or Adam; learning rate $\approx 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$
- [x] **Discount factor:** $\gamma = 0.99$
- [x] **Reward clipping:** $\{-1, 0, +1\}$ for Atari
- [x] **The Deadly Triad:** Function Approximation + Bootstrapping + Off-Policy ⇒ instability
- [x] **Two stabilizers:** Replay breaks off-policy correlation; Target net stabilizes bootstrap target
- [x] **DQN is OFF-POLICY** (data from old policies in buffer)
- [x] **DQN is for DISCRETE action spaces** only (continuous control needs DDPG/SAC/TD3)
- [x] **DQN overestimates Q-values;** Double DQN (DDQN) decouples action selection/evaluation
- [x] **Reference paper:** Mnih et al., *"Human-level control through deep reinforcement learning,"* **Nature 518:529–533 (2015)**
- [x] **No formal convergence proof** for nonlinear DQN — only empirical success and fixed-point analysis

> **One-line final summary:** *DQN turns a Q-table into a Q-network, but stabilizes the otherwise-divergent nonlinear TD learning by replaying past experience and using a slowly-updating target network — and this combination is the foundation upon which essentially all modern deep RL is built.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Double Deep-Q Networks (DDQN)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Double Deep-Q Networks (DDQN)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Double Deep Q-Network (DDQN)** is a reinforcement learning algorithm introduced by **Hado van Hasselt, Arthur Guez, and David Silver (2015, DeepMind)** as a direct, mathematically-grounded improvement over the standard Deep Q-Network (DQN). DDQN mitigates the **overestimation bias** inherent in Q-learning by *decoupling* the **action selection** step from the **action evaluation** step during the Bellman target computation.

In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, DDQN modifies the standard DQN target as follows:

- **Standard DQN Target**:
$$Y_t^{\text{DQN}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q_{\theta^-}\left(S_{t+1}, a\right)$$

- **Double DQN Target**:
$$Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, Q_{\theta^-}\left(S_{t+1}, \arg\max_{a} Q_{\theta}\left(S_{t+1}, a\right)\right)$$

Here, the **online network** $Q_{\theta}$ (with rapidly updating weights $\theta$) is used to **select** the best action, while the **target network** $Q_{\theta^-}$ (with frozen weights $\theta^-$) is used to **evaluate** that action. This single-line architectural separation is the conceptual heart of DDQN.

## 1.2 The Problem DDQN Solves — The "Optimistic Taxi Driver" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bias Intuition (Why Standard DQN Fails)**
> Imagine you are a *self-driving taxi* learning to navigate a city. Every evening, you ask your two advisers (the *same* value table, but used twice) about the next intersection.
> 1. Adviser A ("the Selector") points to the intersection that *looks* best.
> 2. Adviser B ("the Evaluator") — who happens to be Adviser A's identical twin — cheerfully agrees and over-praises it.
> The taxi gets unrealistically optimistic estimates and ends up chasing phantom routes that never deliver.

In standard DQN, the **max operator** uses the *same* Q-network to both pick the best action *and* score it. Because Q-values are noisy estimates, the maximum of a noisy distribution is **systematically biased upward**. This is mathematically guaranteed by the **max operator's property**:
$$\mathbb{E}\left[\max_{a} X_a\right] \geq \max_{a} \mathbb{E}[X_a]$$

DDQN's cure is elegant: use *two* slightly different (online vs. target) networks so the **Selector and Evaluator are different people**, removing the self-confirmation bias.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

- **Discount Factor** $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ — typically **0.99** in Atari benchmarks.
- **Learning Rate** $\alpha$ — typically **$1 \times 10^{-4}$** (Adam optimizer).
- **Target Network Update Frequency** — every **10,000 steps** (hard update) or via Polyak averaging $\tau = 0.005$.
- **Replay Buffer Size** — **$10^6$** transitions.
- **Mini-batch Size** — **32**.
- **Key Hyperparameter Introduced by DDQN**: None — DDQN re-uses DQN's hyperparameters but changes the *target formulation*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: The examiner expects you to explicitly state that DDQN **does not add new hyperparameters** and **does not increase computational cost** compared to DQN. This is a frequently tested 3-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Overestimation Bias — Formal Proof Sketch

In tabular Q-learning, the update rule is:

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

The **max operator** is non-linear. Suppose the true values are $\mathbb{E}[X_a] = q(a)$ but estimates contain zero-mean noise $\epsilon_a$:
$$X_a = q(a) + \epsilon_a$$

Because $\max_a$ selects the *largest* sample, it preferentially picks *overestimates*. Formally:
$$\mathbb{E}\left[\max_a (q(a) + \epsilon_a)\right] = \max_a q(a) + \mathbb{E}\left[\max_a \epsilon_a\right] \geq \max_a q(a)$$

The bias term $\mathbb{E}\left[\max_a \epsilon_a\right]$ grows with the **number of actions** and the **variance of the noise** $\text{Var}(\epsilon)$.

## 2.2 The DDQN Operational Logic — Step by Step

> [!NOTE]
> **The 7-Step DDQN Forward Pass and Update Logic**

1. **Observe** the current state $S_t$ from the environment.
2. **Action Selection** (ε-greedy): With probability $\epsilon$ pick a random action; otherwise pick $a^* = \arg\max_a Q_\theta(S_t, a)$.
3. **Execute** $a^*$ in the environment, observe $R_{t+1}$ and $S_{t+1}$.
4. **Store** the transition $(S_t, a^*, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$ in the **replay buffer** $\mathcal{D}$.
5. **Sample** a mini-batch of transitions from $\mathcal{D}$.
6. **Compute DDQN Target** — the **decoupled** computation:
   - **Action selection** (online net): $a^*_{t+1} = \arg\max_a Q_\theta(S_{t+1}, a)$
   - **Action evaluation** (target net): $Q_{\theta^-}(S_{t+1}, a^*_{t+1})$
   - Final target: $Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q_{\theta^-}(S_{t+1}, a^*_{t+1})$
7. **Gradient Descent**: Minimize the squared TD error w.r.t. $\theta$:
$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[\left(Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} - Q_\theta(S_t, A_t)\right)^2\right]$$

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Decoupling

The decoupling addresses a subtle statistical truth:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bias-Variance Trade-off Insight**
> - DQN **maximizes** bias (uses same network → upward bias) but **minimizes** variance (same sample).
> - DDQN **reduces** bias (different samples for selection vs. evaluation) while keeping variance manageable.
> - Empirically, DDQN delivers **higher scores, faster convergence, and more stable learning** on 49 out of 57 Atari games tested by van Hasselt et al. (2016).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Range | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | DQN Target | $Y^{\text{DQN}} = R + \gamma \max_a Q_{\theta^-}(S', a)$ | Scalar reward | Baseline Atari game agents |
| 2 | DDQN Target | $Y^{\text{DDQN}} = R + \gamma Q_{\theta^-}(S', \arg\max_a Q_\theta(S', a))$ | Scalar reward | Improved value estimation |
| 3 | Action Selection (Online) | $a^* = \arg\max_a Q_\theta(S', a)$ | Discrete action index | Greedy policy w.r.t. $\theta$ |
| 4 | Action Evaluation (Target) | $Q_{\theta^-}(S', a^*)$ | Q-value (utility units) | Bootstrap target |
| 5 | TD Error | $\delta_t = Y^{\text{DDQN}}_t - Q_\theta(S_t, A_t)$ | Scalar | Loss input |
| 6 | Loss Function | $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}[\delta_t^2]$ | Scalar | Adam optimizer input |
| 7 | Overestimation Bias | $\mathbb{E}[\max_a X_a] \geq \max_a \mathbb{E}[X_a]$ | Inequality | Justifies DDQN |
| 8 | Target Net Update (Hard) | $\theta^- \leftarrow \theta$ every $C$ steps | Steps | Stability mechanism |
| 9 | Target Net Update (Polyak) | $\theta^- \leftarrow \tau\theta + (1-\tau)\theta^-$ | Soft mixing | Faster stability |
| 10 | Discount Factor | $\gamma \in [0,1)$ | Typically 0.99 | Future reward weight |

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU exam, when asked to "compare DQN and DDQN," always present the **two target equations side-by-side** and **bold the difference** in the action-evaluation operator. Examiners reward visual clarity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

DDQN is the de-facto standard in:
- **Autonomous driving policy layers** (lane-change decisions).
- **Robotic manipulation** (gripper pose selection).
- **Portfolio management** (trade execution agents).
- **Network resource allocation** in 5G/6G schedulers.
- **Industrial process control** where overestimation could lead to unsafe aggressive actions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Q-Learning to DQN to DDQN

### Step 1 — Tabular Q-Learning Update (Foundation)

The Bellman optimality equation for the action-value function is:

$$Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(S_{t+1}, a') \,\middle|\, S_t = s, A_t = a\right]$$

The tabular Q-learning update minimizes the squared TD error at each step:

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

This is mathematically equivalent to a stochastic gradient descent step on the loss:
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\left(R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t)\right)^2$$

### Step 2 — Extension to DQN (Function Approximation)

In DQN, the Q-table is replaced by a neural network with parameters $\theta$:

$$Q_\theta(s, a) \approx Q^*(s, a)$$

Two critical changes are introduced for stability:
1. **Experience Replay** — sample mini-batches i.i.d. from buffer $\mathcal{D}$ to break temporal correlation.
2. **Target Network** — a periodically-updated copy $Q_{\theta^-}$ used to compute the target, preventing "chasing a moving target."

The DQN target is:

$$Y_t^{\text{DQN}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q_{\theta^-}(S_{t+1}, a)$$

The loss minimized is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(s,a,r,s') \sim \mathcal{D}}\left[\left(Y_t^{\text{DQN}} - Q_\theta(s, a)\right)^2\right]$$

### Step 3 — The Hidden Bias in DQN

Let us write the DQN target as the *expectation* of the noisy estimator:

$$Y_t^{\text{DQN}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \text{Unif}(\mathcal{A})}\left[\max_{a} \hat{Q}_{\theta^-}(S_{t+1}, a)\right]$$

Because of estimation noise, $\hat{Q}_{\theta^-} = Q^* + \epsilon$ where $\epsilon$ is zero-mean error. By Jensen-like reasoning for the max:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\max_{a} (Q^*(s', a) + \epsilon_a)\right] = \max_a Q^*(s', a) + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\left[\max_a \epsilon_a\right]}_{\text{Positive bias } \beta > 0}$$

The bias term $\beta$ depends on the noise distribution and action cardinality. In Atari with $|\mathcal{A}| = 18$ actions, the bias can be **substantial** — sometimes exceeding 50% of the true Q-value magnitude.

### Step 4 — DDQN's Correction: The Decoupled Target

DDQN applies the *Double Q-learning* trick of van Hasselt (2010) to the deep setting. The idea is to evaluate the action chosen by the *online* network using the *target* network's Q-values:

**Action Selection** (online net $\theta$):

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a} Q_\theta(S_{t+1}, a)$$

**Action Evaluation** (target net $\theta^-$):

$$Q_{\theta^-}(S_{t+1}, a^*)$$

**Final DDQN Target**:

$$Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, Q_{\theta^-}\!\left(S_{t+1},\, \arg\max_{a} Q_\theta(S_{t+1}, a)\right)$$

This is mathematically equivalent to:

$$Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi_{\theta}}\left[Q_{\theta^-}(S_{t+1}, a)\right]$$

where $\pi_\theta$ is the greedy policy induced by $Q_\theta$. The expected value is now **unbiased** with respect to the target network's noise — the bias from the max is shifted to the online network, which has *different* noise realization than $\theta^-$.

### Step 5 — Gradient Computation

The gradient of the DDQN loss w.r.t. $\theta$ is:

$$\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[2 \left(Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} - Q_\theta(S_t, A_t)\right) \cdot \left(-\nabla_\theta Q_\theta(S_t, A_t)\right)\right]$$

The target $Y_t^{\text{DDQN}}$ is treated as a **constant** with respect to $\theta$ (target network is detached from the computational graph). This is the standard semi-gradient TD approach.

### Step 6 — Convergence Properties (KTU Theoretical Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Guarantee**
> For finite MDPs, tabular Double Q-learning converges to the optimal Q-function $Q^*$. For the deep variant, DDQN inherits DQN's convergence guarantees *empirically* (with the standard caveats on function approximation). No formal convergence proof exists for the deep case, but the reduction in overestimation has been demonstrated across **57 Atari games**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Side-by-Side DQN vs DDQN

| Aspect | DQN (2013) | DDQN (2015) |
|---|---|---|
| **Year / Authors** | Mnih et al. (DeepMind) | van Hasselt, Guez, Silver (DeepMind) |
| **Target Formula** | $R + \gamma \max_a Q_{\theta^-}(S', a)$ | $R + \gamma Q_{\theta^-}(S', \arg\max_a Q_\theta(S', a))$ |
| **Networks Used for Target** | Target net only | Online + Target nets |
| **Overestimation** | Present and amplified | Significantly reduced |
| **Hyperparameters** | Baseline | **Identical to DQN** |
| **Computational Cost** | Baseline | Negligible overhead |
| **Atari Median Score** | 75% of human | **117% of human** (van Hasselt 2016) |
| **Stability** | Good | **Better** (lower variance) |
| **Convergence Speed** | Slower | **Faster** in many games |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Toy MDP)

Consider a 2-action MDP with the following estimated Q-values from the **target network**:

| State $S'$ | $Q_{\theta^-}(S', a_1)$ | $Q_{\theta^-}(S', a_2)$ |
|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ | 5.0 | 6.0 |
| $S_2$ | 2.0 | 1.0 |

Suppose the true values are $Q^*(S_1, \cdot) = (4, 5)$ and $Q^*(S_2, \cdot) = (2, 1)$, so the noise is $\epsilon = (1, 1)$ at $S_1$ and $\epsilon = (0, 0)$ at $S_2$.

**DQN estimate at $S_1$**:
$$\max_a Q_{\theta^-}(S_1, a) = \max(5, 6) = 6 \quad (\text{overestimated by } 1)$$

**DDQN estimate at $S_1$**:
- Online net (assume well-aligned): $\arg\max_a Q_\theta(S_1, a) = a_2$
- Target net evaluation: $Q_{\theta^-}(S_1, a_2) = 6$
- Same as DQN in this lucky case — but with **different noise**, often giving $5$ instead of $6$.

**At $S_2$** (no noise case):
- DQN: $\max(2, 1) = 2$
- DDQN: $\arg\max Q_\theta(S_2, \cdot) = a_1$, then $Q_{\theta^-}(S_2, a_1) = 2$ — **identical** when noise is absent.

> [!NOTE]
> **The bias only appears when noise is present** — and in deep networks, noise is always present due to finite samples, regularization (dropout), and bootstrapping. This is why DDQN matters.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DDQN Computational Graph (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Environment State S_t] --> B[Online Network Q_theta]
    B --> C[Epsilon-Greedy Action Selection]
    C --> D[Action A_t]
    D --> E[Execute in Environment]
    E --> F[Reward R_{t+1}]
    E --> G[Next State S_{t+1}]
    F --> H[Replay Buffer D]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Mini-Batch Sampling]
    I --> J[Online Network Q_theta on S_{t+1}]
    J --> K[argmax a Q_theta S_{t+1} a]
    K --> L[Target Network Q_theta_minus on S_{t+1} a_star]
    L --> M[DDQN Target Y = R + gamma Q_theta_minus S_{t+1} a_star]
    M --> N[TD Error delta = Y - Q_theta S_t A_t]
    N --> O[Loss L = delta squared]
    O --> P[Adam Optimizer]
    P --> Q[Update Online Weights theta]
    Q --> R{Update Target Net?}
    R -- Yes --> S[theta_minus <- theta or Polyak]
    R -- No --> B
    S --> B
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: DQN vs DDQN

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DQN_Standard[Standard DQN]
        D1[S_t] --> D2[Q_theta Net]
        D2 --> D3[All Q-values Q S_t a]
        D3 --> D4[Max Operator]
        D4 --> D5[Q_theta_minus S_{t+1} a_max]
        D5 --> D6[Bootstrap Target]
    end

    subgraph DDQN_Decoupled[Double DQN]
        E1[S_t] --> E2[Q_theta Net - Online]
        E2 --> E3[Argmax: Select a_star]
        E3 --> E4[Q_theta_minus Net - Target]
        E4 --> E5[Evaluate a_star only]
        E5 --> E6[Decoupled Target]
    end
```

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram: Per-Step Interaction

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Env as Environment
    participant Agent as Agent Controller
    participant Qnet as Online Net Q_theta
    participant Qtgt as Target Net Q_theta_minus
    participant Buffer as Replay Buffer
    participant Optimizer as Adam Optimizer

    Agent->>Env: Step with action A_t
    Env-->>Agent: R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}
    Agent->>Buffer: Store (S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})
    Buffer-->>Agent: Sampled mini-batch
    Agent->>Qnet: Forward S_{t+1}
    Qnet-->>Agent: Q_theta values, a_star = argmax
    Agent->>Qtgt: Forward S_{t+1}
    Qtgt-->>Agent: Q_theta_minus values
    Agent->>Agent: Y = R + gamma * Q_theta_minus(S_{t+1}, a_star)
    Agent->>Optimizer: Loss = (Y - Q_theta(S_t, A_t))^2
    Optimizer-->>Qnet: Update theta
    Note over Qtgt: Hard update every C steps
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Note for KTU Students**: The above three diagrams jointly illustrate the three architectural differences between DQN and DDQN — the action **selection** step (online net), the action **evaluation** step (target net), and the **target update** step (periodic hard copy of online weights to target).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the overestimation bias in Q-learning, and how does Double DQN address it?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> The overestimation bias arises because the max operator in the Q-learning target, $Y_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a)$, systematically selects actions with the *largest* noisy positive errors. Mathematically, $\mathbb{E}[\max_a X_a] \geq \max_a \mathbb{E}[X_a]$. This leads to inflated Q-value estimates and suboptimal policies.
>
> **Double DQN's Fix (2 marks):** It decouples action selection from action evaluation. The online network $Q_\theta$ selects the greedy action $a^* = \arg\max_a Q_\theta(S_{t+1}, a)$, while the target network $Q_{\theta^-}$ evaluates it. The target becomes $Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q_{\theta^-}(S_{t+1}, a^*)$, eliminating self-confirmation bias.
>
> **Conclusion (½ mark):** This reduces overestimation, improves Atari scores, and requires no extra hyperparameters.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List the key architectural differences between DQN and Double DQN.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> 1. **Target Computation (2 marks):** DQN uses $\max_a Q_{\theta^-}(S_{t+1}, a)$ with a single network; DDQN uses $Q_{\theta^-}(S_{t+1}, \arg\max_a Q_\theta(S_{t+1}, a))$ decoupling selection and evaluation.
> 2. **Networks Used (½ mark):** DQN uses only the target network; DDQN uses *both* online and target networks in the target computation.
> 3. **Hyperparameters (½ mark):** DDQN re-uses DQN's hyperparameters — no new tuning is required.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Module 4 Model Paper]`
**(a) Derive the DDQN target equation from first principles, clearly showing how it differs from the standard DQN target. State any assumptions made. (7 Marks)**

**(b) An autonomous-driving RL agent uses a DDQN with $\gamma = 0.99$. At time $t$, the target network estimates for state $S_{t+1}$ are $Q_{\theta^-}(S_{t+1}, a_1) = 12.0$ and $Q_{\theta^-}(S_{t+1}, a_2) = 14.5$, while the online network's $\arg\max$ selects $a_2$. The reward is $R_{t+1} = 2.0$. Compute the DDQN bootstrap target and the TD error if $Q_\theta(S_t, A_t) = 11.8$. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Start from the Bellman optimality equation for Q:
$$Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(S_{t+1}, a') \,\middle|\, s, a\right]$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** In DQN, function approximation is used: $Q_\theta(s, a) \approx Q^*(s, a)$ with a target network $Q_{\theta^-}$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The DQN target is:
$$Y_t^{\text{DQN}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q_{\theta^-}(S_{t+1}, a)$$
This is biased because the same network both selects and evaluates.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** The DDQN correction:
- Online net **selects**: $a^* = \arg\max_a Q_\theta(S_{t+1}, a)$
- Target net **evaluates**: $Q_{\theta^-}(S_{t+1}, a^*)$
- Final target:
$$Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, Q_{\theta^-}\!\left(S_{t+1},\, \arg\max_{a} Q_\theta(S_{t+1}, a)\right)$$

**Assumptions (½ mark):** Standard Double Q-learning assumption — the online and target networks are *independent* estimators of the true Q-function. The mini-batch is i.i.d. from the replay buffer.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the action selected by the online net:
$$a^* = \arg\max_a Q_\theta(S_{t+1}, a) = a_2$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the DDQN bootstrap target:
$$Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, Q_{\theta^-}(S_{t+1}, a_2)$$
$$Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} = 2.0 + 0.99 \times 14.5$$
$$Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} = 2.0 + 14.355 = 16.355$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the TD error:
$$\delta_t = Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} - Q_\theta(S_t, A_t) = 16.355 - 11.8 = 4.555$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Loss value:
$$\mathcal{L} = \delta_t^2 = (4.555)^2 = 20.748$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclusion — The positive TD error indicates the current estimate underestimates the true value, and the Adam optimizer will adjust $\theta$ to push $Q_\theta(S_t, A_t)$ upward.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - Module 4 Model Paper - Alternative]`
**(a) Explain the role of the target network in both DQN and DDQN. Why is it necessary, and what would happen if it were removed? (7 Marks)**

**(b) With a diagram, explain the training pipeline of a DDQN agent. Compare the *number of forward passes* per update step in DQN vs DDQN. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The target network $Q_{\theta^-}$ in DQN/DDQN provides a *stable* target for the TD bootstrap. The online net $Q_\theta$ is updated every step, but $\theta^-$ is held fixed for $C$ steps (or softly averaged).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Without the target network**, the loss would be:
$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \left(R + \gamma \max_a Q_\theta(S', a) - Q_\theta(S, A)\right)^2$$
This creates a *moving target* problem — as $\theta$ updates, the target itself moves, leading to **divergence** or oscillations (the "deadly triad" of off-policy + function approximation + bootstrapping).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** In DQN, the target net is used directly via $\max$. In DDQN, the target net is used only for *evaluation*, while the online net is used for *selection*. This double-use gives DDQN's bias reduction.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Update mechanism — Hard update ($\theta^- \leftarrow \theta$ every $C$ steps) or Polyak averaging $\theta^- \leftarrow \tau\theta + (1-\tau)\theta^-$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 (3 marks):** **Pipeline Diagram (textual form for KTU answer scripts):**
```
1. Initialize online net Q_theta (random weights) and target net Q_theta_minus = Q_theta
2. Initialize replay buffer D (capacity 10^6)
3. For each episode:
     Reset state S_0
     For t = 0, 1, 2, ...:
        a. Select A_t via epsilon-greedy on Q_theta
        b. Execute A_t, observe R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}
        c. Store transition in D
        d. Sample mini-batch from D
        e. Compute DDQN target:
              a* = argmax_a Q_theta(S_{t+1}, a)
              Y = R + gamma * Q_theta_minus(S_{t+1}, a*)
        f. Compute loss L = (Y - Q_theta(S_t, A_t))^2
        g. Adam step on theta
        h. Every C steps: theta_minus <- theta
```

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Forward passes per update**:
- DQN: **1 forward pass on $S'$** through the target net to compute $\max_a Q_{\theta^-}(S', a)$.
- DDQN: **2 forward passes** — one through online net $Q_\theta$ for argmax, one through target net $Q_{\theta^-}$ for evaluation.
- The extra pass is on a *discrete set of actions*, not a sequential computation — the cost is **negligible**.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Trade-off — DDQN doubles the forward passes but achieves **unbiased value estimation**, **faster convergence**, and **better final performance** — a winning trade-off.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in DDQN Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating DQN and DDQN targets (–2 marks typical).** Students often write $Y_t = R + \gamma \max_a Q_\theta(S', a)$ and call it "DDQN." This is **DQN with no target network** — both marks lost.
>
> 2. **Forgetting that DDQN uses TWO networks (–1 mark).** You must explicitly state that $Q_\theta$ (online) selects and $Q_{\theta^-}$ (target) evaluates.
>
> 3. **Stating DDQN has new hyperparameters (–1 mark).** DDQN reuses DQN's hyperparameters. Adding fictitious learning-rate schedules or new discount factors is a common error.
>
> 4. **Confusing Double DQN with Dueling DQN (–2 marks).** These are *different* architectures. Dueling splits the Q-value into Value + Advantage streams. Double DQN only changes the *target*. Do not mix them up.
>
> 5. **Omitting the role of the max operator in causing bias (–2 marks).** Always derive the inequality $\mathbb{E}[\max_a X_a] \geq \max_a \mathbb{E}[X_a]$ to justify DDQN's existence.
>
> 6. **Writing $\arg\max$ outside $Q$ (–1 mark):** The correct form is $Q_{\theta^-}(S', \arg\max_a Q_\theta(S', a))$ — the $\arg\max$ is the **argument** to $Q_{\theta^-}$, not the outer operator.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Double DQN**

- ✅ DDQN was introduced by **van Hasselt, Guez, and Silver (DeepMind, 2015–2016)**.
- ✅ Its **sole purpose** is to **reduce overestimation bias** in Q-learning.
- ✅ The **overestimation bias** stems from the **max operator** applied to noisy estimates: $\mathbb{E}[\max_a X_a] \geq \max_a \mathbb{E}[X_a]$.
- ✅ DDQN's **target equation** is $Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q_{\theta^-}(S_{t+1}, \arg\max_a Q_\theta(S_{t+1}, a))$.
- ✅ **Online net $Q_\theta$** → action **selection** (argmax).
- ✅ **Target net $Q_{\theta^-}$** → action **evaluation** (Q-value lookup).
- ✅ **No new hyperparameters** vs. DQN — only the target formula changes.
- ✅ **Two forward passes per update** (online + target), but negligible overhead.
- ✅ Target network update: **hard copy** every $C$ steps OR **Polyak averaging** with $\tau \approx 0.005$.
- ✅ Empirically outperforms DQN on **majority of Atari games** (van Hasselt 2016, DeepMind).
- ✅ DDQN is **NOT** the same as Dueling DQN (architectural) or DDPG (continuous-action) — it is a **value-based, discrete-action** algorithm.
- ✅ DDQN pairs naturally with **Prioritized Experience Replay** and **Dueling Networks** — combining all three is called the "Rainbow" agent.
- ✅ The decoupling trick has deep roots in tabular **Double Q-learning (van Hasselt, 2010, NeurIPS)**.
- ✅ In KTU exams, always **draw a side-by-side comparison** of DQN vs DDQN targets and **state the bias inequality** explicitly.
- ✅ The TD error formula is $\delta_t = Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} - Q_\theta(S_t, A_t)$ and the loss is $\mathcal{L} = \delta_t^2$.
- ✅ The replay buffer breaks temporal correlation; the target network breaks the "chasing moving target" pathology.
- ✅ DDQN's estimator is **unbiased** under the assumption that online and target networks have **independent noise realizations** — which holds when $\theta$ and $\theta^-$ are updated at different rates.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Duelling DDQN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Duelling DDQN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Duelling Double Deep Q-Network (Duelling DDQN)** is a hybrid value-based deep reinforcement learning architecture that **simultaneously integrates two major extensions of Deep Q-Networks (DQN)**: (1) the **Double DQN (DDQN)** target mechanism that mitigates the *overestimation bias* of Q-learning, and (2) the **Dueling Network Architecture** that factorises the Q-function into two separate estimation streams — a *state-value stream* and an *advantage stream* — sharing a common convolutional feature encoder.

Mathematically, the combined estimator is defined as:

$$
Q(s, a; \theta, \alpha, \beta) \;=\; V(s; \theta, \beta) \;+\; \left( A(s, a; \theta, \alpha) \;-\; \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}\sum_{a' \in \mathcal{A}} A(s, a'; \theta, \alpha) \right)
$$

where the target used for bootstrapping is the **DDQN target** that decouples action selection from action evaluation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Smart Taxi Driver**
> Imagine an autonomous taxi driver learning to navigate a busy city.
>
> 1. **The Dueling (Two-Stream) Part:** Before deciding *which steering angle to apply*, the driver first asks: *"Is being on THIS road at THIS time generally profitable?"* — this is the **Value Stream V(s)**. Then the driver asks: *"Relative to the average manoeuvre, how much better or worse is turning left vs. turning right vs. going straight HERE?"* — this is the **Advantage Stream A(s, a)**. The driver does not need to know the value of *every* action in *every* state — only which states matter and how actions compare.
>
> 2. **The Double DQN Part:** The driver uses *one mental map* (the **online network**) to *decide* which manoeuvre looks best, but evaluates that manoeuvre using a *separate, slightly older mental map* (the **target network**) so as not to be overconfident about manoeuvres that have not actually been tested.
>
> The **Duelling DDQN** is the union of these two ideas: a dual-stream brain that separates *state importance* from *action advantage*, trained against a *de-biased* bootstrapped target.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

| Aspect | Significance for KTU ESE |
|---|---|
| **Syllabus Position** | Module 4 – Value-Based Deep RL (advanced DQN variants) |
| **Examination Frequency** | Frequently asked 7–14 mark question in Part B |
| **Cognitive Level (Bloom)** | Analyze (L4) / Evaluate (L5) |
| **Required Pre-requisites** | DQN, Experience Replay, Target Networks, Q-Learning |
| **Engineering Application** | Autonomous driving, robotics, game AI (Atari benchmark), resource management |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Blocks — The Two Pillars

### Pillar A: Double DQN (DDQN) — Fixing Overestimation

In vanilla DQN, the bootstrap target uses the **same network** both to select and to evaluate the next action:

$$
Y_t^{\text{DQN}} \;=\; R_{t+1} \;+\; \gamma \max_{a} Q\bigl(S_{t+1}, a; \theta_t^{-}\bigr)
$$

The `max` operator, combined with function approximation noise, **systematically inflates** Q-value estimates (a phenomenon proven by Thrun & Schwartz, 1993 and re-demonstrated by van Hasselt et al., 2016).

**Why overestimation happens:**
- The network has *noise* $\epsilon$ on every Q-estimate.
- `max` always picks the *noisy* upward outlier.
- Taking the *maximum* of biased, noisy estimates yields a **positive bias**.

**DDQN's remedy** — *decouple* selection from evaluation:

$$
Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} \;=\; R_{t+1} \;+\; \gamma \, Q\!\left( S_{t+1}, \; \underbrace{\arg\max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a; \theta_t)}_{\text{selection (online net)}}; \; \underbrace{\theta_t^{-}}_{\text{evaluation (target net)}} \right)
$$

The online network **selects** the greedy action; the target network **evaluates** that action. Because the two networks differ, the upward bias of `max` is broken.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the bias is reduced:** In DDQN, the evaluator `Q(·,·;θ⁻)` is *not* the same function that picked the action. If the online network picks an *overestimated* action, the target network may assign it a *modest* value, lowering the bootstrap.

### Pillar B: Dueling Network Architecture — Factorising Q

Instead of computing $Q(s, a)$ directly via a single output head, the network has **two parallel fully-connected heads** that branch out from a shared convolutional feature encoder $\phi(s; \theta)$:

$$
V(s; \theta, \beta) \quad \text{(scalar — value of state } s\text{)} \\[2pt]
A(s, a; \theta, \alpha) \quad \text{(vector over actions — relative advantage of each } a\text{ in } s\text{)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** Many states do not require *every* action's value to be learned accurately. For example, in a corridor, "go forward" is the only meaningful action; the relative advantages of other actions are noise. The dueling architecture allows the value stream to be learned *independently* of the action stream, leading to faster policy evaluation in such states.

## 2.2 Identifiability Problem & The Centering Trick

A naive combination $Q = V + A$ has an **identifiability issue**: for any constant $c$, $(V + c) + (A - c) = Q$. The network cannot distinguish $V$ from $A$ uniquely.

**Solution — subtract the mean advantage:**

$$
Q(s, a; \theta, \alpha, \beta) \;=\; V(s; \theta, \beta) \;+\; \left( A(s, a; \theta, \alpha) \;-\; \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}\sum_{a'} A(s, a'; \theta, \alpha) \right)
$$

**Property guaranteed by centering:** $\max_{a} Q(s, a) = V(s) + \max_{a} A(s, a) - \text{mean}(A) = V(s) + \max_{a} A(s, a) - \text{const}$, but the *chosen* action's Q-value loses its constant offset in a stable, learnable way. The mean advantage is forced to zero for the chosen action representation, making $V$ recoverable.

An alternative (used in the original paper) is the **"max" centering**:
$$
Q(s, a) = V(s) + \left( A(s, a) - \max_{a'} A(s, a') \right)
$$
but the mean-subtraction variant is generally preferred as it preserves the ordering of advantages and has smoother gradients.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering / Algorithmic Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DQN Target | $Y^{\text{DQN}}_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a; \theta^-)$ | Bootstrap with max — biased |
| 2 | **DDQN Target** | $Y^{\text{DDQN}}_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, Q(S_{t+1}, \arg\max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a; \theta); \theta^-)$ | Bootstrap with decoupled select/eval |
| 3 | Dueling Decomposition | $Q(s, a) = V(s) + A(s, a)$ | Conceptually factorises value and advantage |
| 4 | **Centering Trick (Mean)** | $Q(s,a) = V(s) + A(s,a) - \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}\sum_{a'} A(s,a')$ | Ensures identifiability of $V$ & $A$ |
| 5 | Centering Trick (Max) | $Q(s,a) = V(s) + A(s,a) - \max_{a'} A(s,a')$ | Alternative identifiability constraint |
| 6 | Loss Function (Huber/Smooth-L1) | $L(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\bigl[\,\rho\bigl(Y^{\text{DDQN}}_t - Q(S_t, A_t; \theta)\bigr)\bigr]$ | Stochastic gradient target |
| 7 | Soft Update (Polyak Averaging) | $\theta^- \leftarrow \tau \theta + (1-\tau)\theta^-$ | Stabilises target net |
| 8 | $\epsilon$-Greedy Policy | $\pi(a\mid s) = 1-\epsilon + \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$ for greedy $a$ | Exploration |
| 9 | Discount Factor | $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ | Weights future rewards |
| 10 | Replay Buffer Sampling | $(s, a, r, s') \sim \mathcal{D}$ uniformly | i.i.d. assumption for SGD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **centring step (Row 4)** is the single most important and most-tested sub-formula in the dueling architecture. KTU examiners frequently award partial credit for stating it correctly even if the rest of the derivation is incomplete.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Autonomous Driving (Lane-Keeping):** The dueling head allows the agent to learn the *value of being on a highway* ($V(s)$) without needing to estimate the precise Q-value of "swerve left by 5°" in *every* irrelevant state.
2. **Robotic Manipulation:** In many manipulation states, "open gripper" is the only sensible action — the dueling architecture exploits this by learning $V(s)$ for such states without spreading gradient across all action heads.
3. **Atari Game AI:** The dueling DDQN variant famously improved performance on 57% of Atari games over vanilla DDQN, with **no additional hyper-parameters or computational cost** beyond the architecture change.
4. **Network Slicing in 5G/6G:** State (cell load) value can be learned separately from action (resource allocation) advantage, leading to more stable policies under non-stationary traffic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From DQN Target to DDQN Target

We begin with the standard Q-learning update using function approximation. Let $Q(s, a; \theta)$ be a differentiable action-value function with parameters $\theta$. The optimal Bellman target is:

$$
Q^*(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{s'}\!\left[\, R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \,\big|\, s, a \,\right]
$$

In a model-free setting, the target for a sampled transition $(s, a, r, s')$ is:

$$
Y_t^{\text{DQN}} \;=\; r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a'; \theta^-)
$$

where $\theta^-$ are the parameters of a (periodically updated) **target network**. The loss minimised is:

$$
L(\theta) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{(s,a,r,s') \sim \mathcal{D}}\!\left[\, \bigl( Y_t^{\text{DQN}} - Q(s, a; \theta) \bigr)^2 \,\right]
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the bias source.** The `max` operator is non-linear and, when applied to noisy estimates $\hat{Q}(s', a') = Q^*(s', a') + \epsilon_{a'}$, picks the *largest* noisy sample. Formally, if $\epsilon_{a} \sim$ zero-mean noise:

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\max_a \bigl( Q^*(s', a) + \epsilon_a \bigr)\,\right] \;\geq\; \max_a Q^*(s', a)
$$

The inequality is strict whenever the noise has positive support — a *systematic positive bias*.

**Step 2 — Decouple selection and evaluation.** Define:

$$
a^{*} \;=\; \arg\max_{a} Q(s', a; \theta) \quad \text{(selection with ONLINE network)}
$$

$$
Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} \;=\; r + \gamma \, Q(s', a^{*}; \theta^{-}) \quad \text{(evaluation with TARGET network)}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify bias reduction.** Because $\theta$ and $\theta^-$ are *different* (one is updated every step, the other is "frozen" for $C$ steps), the noise of $Q(s', a^*; \theta^-)$ is **independent** of the action that was selected by $Q(s', \cdot; \theta)$. Hence the selected $a^*$ is still influenced by online-network noise (which can be overestimated), but the *evaluation* by $\theta^-$ is unbiased w.r.t. that noise. Mathematically:

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\, Q(s', a^{*}; \theta^{-}) \,\right] \;\approx\; Q^{*}(s', a^{*}_{\text{true}})
$$

which is the *true* Q-value of the action *that the online network believes is best*, but evaluated by a *separate* function approximator.

**Step 4 — Construct the final loss.**

$$
L(\theta) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, \bigl( r + \gamma \, Q(s', a^{*}; \theta^{-}) - Q(s, a; \theta) \bigr)^2 \,\right]
$$

This is the **DDQN loss**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Dueling Aggregation with Centring

We want to express $Q(s, a)$ as the sum of a state-value function $V(s)$ and an advantage function $A(s, a)$:

$$
Q(s, a) \;\equiv\; V(s) + A(s, a)
$$

**Step 1 — Prove non-identifiability.** For any constant $c \in \mathbb{R}$:

$$
(V(s) + c) + (A(s, a) - c) \;=\; V(s) + A(s, a) \;=\; Q(s, a)
$$

The decomposition is **not unique** — infinitely many $(V, A)$ pairs yield the same $Q$. The network's two heads cannot be uniquely identified by minimising MSE on $Q$ alone.

**Step 2 — Impose an identifiability constraint.** Choose a function $f: \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \to \mathbb{R}$ (e.g., mean or max) and force:

$$
\sum_{a'} f\!\left( A(s, a') \right) \;=\; 0 \quad \forall s
$$

**Step 3a — Mean-centring case.** Set $f = \text{mean}$:

$$
\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \sum_{a'} A(s, a') \;\equiv\; 0
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(s, a; \theta, \alpha, \beta) 
&= V(s; \theta, \beta) + A(s, a; \theta, \alpha) - \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}\sum_{a'} A(s, a'; \theta, \alpha) \\[2pt]
&= V(s; \theta, \beta) + A(s, a; \theta, \alpha) - \bar{A}(s; \theta, \alpha)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3b — Max-centring case.** Set $f = \max$:

$$
Q(s, a; \theta, \alpha, \beta) \;=\; V(s; \theta, \beta) + A(s, a; \theta, \alpha) - \max_{a'} A(s, a'; \theta, \alpha)
$$

**Step 4 — Show the chosen action has zero-centered advantage.** Under mean-centring, the *greedy* action's advantage equals the maximum deviation from the mean:

$$
\arg\max_a Q(s, a) \;=\; \arg\max_a A(s, a) \quad \text{(since } V(s) \text{ and } -\bar{A}(s) \text{ are action-independent)}
$$

And under mean-centring, the expected (mean) advantage is zero, so the value head learns the *baseline* — which is precisely the definition of a state-value function.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Combined Duelling DDQN Estimator

The full estimator used in training integrates both pillars.

**Step 1 — Dueling head produces $Q(s, a)$:**

$$
Q(s, a; \theta, \alpha, \beta) = V(s; \theta, \beta) + \left( A(s, a; \theta, \alpha) - \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}\sum_{a'} A(s, a'; \theta, \alpha) \right)
$$

**Step 2 — DDQN target is constructed using this dueling $Q$:**

$$
a^{*} = \arg\max_{a} Q(s', a; \theta, \alpha, \beta)
$$

$$
Y_t^{\text{Duelling-DDQN}} = r + \gamma \, Q(s', a^{*}; \theta^{-}, \alpha^{-}, \beta^{-})
$$

**Step 3 — Loss gradient flows back through *both* streams:**

$$
L(\theta, \alpha, \beta) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, \bigl( Y_t^{\text{Duelling-DDQN}} - Q(s, a; \theta, \alpha, \beta) \bigr)^2 \,\right]
$$

Gradients $\nabla_\theta, \nabla_\alpha, \nabla_\beta$ are all non-zero (unlike in DQN, where only $\theta$ of the single head has gradient). This is **why** the dueling architecture learns faster in many environments — the value stream gets gradient signal even when only one action's Q-value matters.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Vanilla DQN | Double DQN (DDQN) | Dueling Net | Duelling DDQN |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Overestimation Bias | High | **Low** | Moderate (inherits DQN's bias) | **Lowest** |
| Identifiability Issue | N/A (single head) | N/A | **Resolved by centring** | **Resolved by centring** |
| Value-Stream Learning | Implicit | Implicit | **Explicit (V-head)** | **Explicit (V-head)** |
| Architecture Heads | 1 | 1 | **2** | **2** |
| Action-Value Decomposition | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Gradient Flow on Value | Indirect | Indirect | Direct (V-head) | Direct (V-head) |
| Computational Overhead vs. DQN | Baseline | Negligible | Marginal (split FC head) | Marginal |
| Atari Benchmark Improvement | Baseline | +20–30% games | +40–50% games | **+57% games** (best) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Duelling Network Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputState["Input State s_t"] --> conv1["Conv Layer 1"]
    conv1 --> conv2["Conv Layer 2"]
    conv2 --> conv3["Conv Layer 3"]
    conv3 --> fcShared["Shared FC Layer"]

    fcShared --> valStream["Value Stream V s theta beta"]
    fcShared --> advStream["Advantage Stream A s a theta alpha"]

    valStream --> valOut["Scalar V s"]
    advStream --> advOut["Vector A s a over all actions"]

    valOut --> combine["Combine V s + A s a minus mean A"]
    advOut --> combine

    combine --> qOut["Q s a for every action"]

    qOut --> ddqnSel["DDQN Action Selection argmax a Q s prime a theta"]
    ddqnSel --> ddqnEval["DDQN Target Evaluation Q s prime a star theta minus"]
    ddqnEval --> loss["Loss L theta alpha beta"]

    targetNet["Target Network DuelingDDQN theta minus"] -.->|soft update| ddqnEval
    targetNet -.->|soft update| qOut
```

## 4.2 Duelling DDQN Training Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    env["Environment"] -->|transition s,a,r,s prime| buffer["Replay Buffer D"]
    buffer -->|sample minibatch| train["Training Step"]
    train --> qCurrent["Compute Q s a via Duelling Head"]
    train --> qNextOnline["Compute argmax over Q s prime a theta"]
    qNextOnline --> qNextTarget["Evaluate a star with Q s prime a star theta minus"]
    qNextTarget --> yTarget["Build Y DuellingDDQN"]
    yTarget --> loss["Huber Loss L"]
    qCurrent --> loss
    loss -->|backprop| update["Update theta alpha beta"]
    update -->|soft Polyak avg| targetSync["Update theta minus alpha minus beta minus"]
    targetSync --> qNextTarget
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Duelling DDQN Module

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph "Online Network"
        enc1["Shared Convolutional Encoder phi s theta"]
        vHead1["Value Head V s theta beta"]
        aHead1["Advantage Head A s a theta alpha"]
        ctr1["Centring: A s a minus mean A"]
        sum1["Q s a = V s + centred A s a"]
    end

    subgraph "Target Network"
        enc2["Shared Convolutional Encoder phi s theta minus"]
        vHead2["Value Head V s theta minus beta minus"]
        aHead2["Advantage Head A s a theta minus alpha minus"]
        ctr2["Centring: A s a minus mean A"]
        sum2["Q s a minus = V s minus + centred A s a minus"]
    end

    enc1 --> vHead1
    enc1 --> aHead1
    vHead1 --> sum1
    aHead1 --> ctr1 --> sum1

    enc2 --> vHead2
    enc2 --> aHead2
    vHead2 --> sum2
    aHead2 --> ctr2 --> sum2

    sum1 -->|argmax a| pickAction["Select a star"]
    pickAction -->|index| sum2
    sum2 -->|Q s prime a star minus| targetY["Bootstrap Y DuellingDDQN"]
    sum1 -->|Q s a| lossCompute["Huber Loss"]
    targetY --> lossCompute
    lossCompute -->|backprop| enc1
    lossCompute -->|soft copy| enc2
```

## 4.4 Information-Flow Summary

| Block | Role | Update Cadence |
|---|---|---|
| Online Encoder $\phi(s; \theta)$ | Extract features from state | Every gradient step |
| Online Value Head $V(s; \theta, \beta)$ | Estimate state value | Every gradient step |
| Online Advantage Head $A(s, a; \theta, \alpha)$ | Estimate action advantage | Every gradient step |
| Centring Module | Subtract mean advantage | Every forward pass |
| Target Encoder + Heads | Provide stable bootstrap target | **Polyak averaged** ($\tau = 0.001$ typical) |
| Replay Buffer $\mathcal{D}$ | Decorrelate samples | Continuous write / random sample |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Q: Define the Dueling Network architecture in Deep Reinforcement Learning. How does it differ from a standard DQN?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Dueling Network architecture decomposes the Q-function $Q(s, a)$ into two separate streams branching from a shared convolutional encoder: a **state-value stream** $V(s; \theta, \beta)$ that estimates the value of being in a state, and an **advantage stream** $A(s, a; \theta, \alpha)$ that estimates the additional benefit of taking action $a$ in that state. The two streams are combined as $Q(s, a) = V(s) + A(s, a) - \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}\sum_{a'} A(s, a')$ to ensure identifiability. It differs from standard DQN, which has a single fully-connected head producing $Q(s, a)$ directly, by explicitly learning the value function and using it to generalise across actions in states where action choice is less critical.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What problem does Double DQN (DDQN) address, and how does it address it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
DDQN addresses the **overestimation bias** of Q-values in standard DQN caused by the `max` operator applied to noisy neural-network estimates. It addresses this by **decoupling action selection from action evaluation**: the online network with parameters $\theta$ is used to *select* the greedy action $a^* = \arg\max_a Q(s', a; \theta)$, while the target network with parameters $\theta^-$ is used to *evaluate* that action, producing the target $Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} = r + \gamma Q(s', a^*; \theta^-)$. Since $\theta \neq \theta^-$, the upward bias of the `max` operator is broken, yielding more accurate Q-value estimates.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the centring term:** Most students correctly state $Q = V + A$ but lose **2–3 marks** by omitting the mean-subtraction $-\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}\sum A$. KTU strictly tests the *identifiability* requirement — without centring, the decomposition is mathematically invalid.
> 2. **Conflating DDQN with Dueling:** Students frequently describe DDQN's target formula as if it were part of the dueling architecture (or vice versa). They are **two independent ideas** combined; clearly mark which is which.
> 3. **Confusing $\theta$ and $\theta^-$:** Always specify which network (online vs. target) is used at which step. Mixing them is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **No mention of replay buffer / Huber loss:** Even in 14-mark answers, briefly mention that training uses experience replay and a Huber (smooth-L1) loss for stability — it shows board-level understanding.

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the architecture of the Dueling Deep Q-Network in detail. With a neat diagram, describe how the value and advantage streams are combined. Why is the centring step required? (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Architecture Overview (2 marks):** The Dueling Network, introduced by Wang et al. (2016), modifies the final layers of a DQN. After a shared convolutional feature encoder $\phi(s; \theta)$ (typically the same as the original DQN's conv layers), the network **splits** into two fully-connected streams:
- A **value stream** that outputs a single scalar $V(s; \theta, \beta)$ — the value of the state.
- An **advantage stream** that outputs a vector $A(s, a; \theta, \alpha)$ of size $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ — the relative advantage of each action in that state.

**2. Aggregation Formula (2 marks):** The two streams are combined as:

$$
Q(s, a; \theta, \alpha, \beta) \;=\; V(s; \theta, \beta) \;+\; \left( A(s, a; \theta, \alpha) \;-\; \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}\sum_{a'} A(s, a'; \theta, \alpha) \right)
$$

The mean-subtraction term is the **centring step**.

**3. Justification of Centring (2 marks):** The decomposition $Q = V + A$ is not unique — for any constant $c$, $(V + c) + (A - c) = Q$. The centring step forces the mean advantage to be zero, making the value stream uniquely identifiable and stable during training. Without centring, the network cannot learn a consistent mapping from $V$ and $A$ to $Q$.

**4. Diagram (1 mark):** The block diagram (similar to Section 4.1) should show the shared encoder splitting into value and advantage heads, followed by the centring module and the recombination step. (See Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the official reference.)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Describe the Double DQN (DDQN) target formula and explain how it mitigates overestimation bias. How is DDQN combined with the Dueling architecture in Duelling DDQN? (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Overestimation Bias in DQN (2 marks):** In DQN, the bootstrap target uses a single target network to compute $\max_a Q(s', a; \theta^-)$. Since the network has noise $\epsilon_a$ on each Q-estimate, the `max` operator systematically selects the *overestimated* action, leading to a positive bias in Q-values that propagates through Bellman backups.

**2. DDQN's Solution (3 marks):** DDQN decouples selection from evaluation. The online network with parameters $\theta$ is used to **select** the greedy action $a^* = \arg\max_a Q(s', a; \theta)$, while the target network with parameters $\theta^-$ is used to **evaluate** it:

$$
Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} \;=\; r + \gamma \, Q\!\left( S_{t+1}, \; \arg\max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a; \theta_t); \;\theta_t^{-} \right)
$$

Because $\theta$ and $\theta^-$ are *different* (the target is a Polyak-averaged or periodically hard-updated copy of the online network), the upward bias of `max` is broken, yielding more accurate Q-estimates. The loss is then:

$$
L(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, \rho\!\left( Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} - Q(S_t, A_t; \theta) \right) \,\right]
$$

where $\rho$ is the Huber loss.

**3. Duelling DDQN Combination (2 marks):** The Duelling DDQN simply **substitutes** the dueling head's $Q$-estimator into the DDQN target formula. The dueling $Q$ (with centring) is used for both action selection (with online parameters) and target evaluation (with target parameters), and the loss is back-propagated through all three parameter groups $(\theta, \alpha, \beta)$. This combination gives the **best of both worlds**: low overestimation bias (from DDQN) and faster, more stable learning (from the dueling head's value/advantage factorisation).

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Derive the Q-value aggregation formula for a Dueling Network. Show why the centring step is necessary and compare the mean-centring and max-centring variants. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Decomposition (1 mark):** We write $Q(s, a) = V(s) + A(s, a)$.

**2. Identifiability Proof (2 marks):** For any constant $c$, $(V(s) + c) + (A(s, a) - c) = Q(s, a)$, so the decomposition is not unique. Hence a constraint is required to make $V$ and $A$ identifiable.

**3. Mean-Centring Derivation (2 marks):** Imposing the constraint $\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \sum_{a'} A(s, a') = 0$:

$$
Q(s, a) = V(s) + A(s, a) - \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \sum_{a'} A(s, a')
$$

This forces the mean advantage to zero, making $V$ recoverable as the *baseline* advantage.

**4. Max-Centring Variant (1 mark):** Alternatively, one can impose $\max_{a'} A(s, a') = 0$:

$$
Q(s, a) = V(s) + A(s, a) - \max_{a'} A(s, a')
$$

The max-centring variant has zero gradient for the best action, which can slow learning; hence **mean-centring is generally preferred**.

**5. Comparison (1 mark):** Mean-centring preserves the *relative ordering* of advantages (the gradient of $A$ flows back for every action), while max-centring zeroes out the gradient for the best action. Mean-centring is therefore more robust.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: With a block diagram, explain the complete training pipeline of a Duelling DDQN agent. Highlight the role of (i) the target network, (ii) experience replay, and (iii) the Huber loss. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Block Diagram (2 marks):** A complete pipeline has:
- **Online dueling network** (encoder + value head + advantage head + centring) producing $Q(s, a; \theta, \alpha, \beta)$.
- **Target dueling network** (same architecture, parameters $\theta^-, \alpha^-, \beta^-$) producing $Q(s, a; \theta^-, \alpha^-, \beta^-)$.
- **Replay buffer** $\mathcal{D}$ storing transitions $(s, a, r, s', \text{done})$.
- **Loss module** that computes the Huber loss between the DDQN target and the online $Q$.
- **Optimizer** (e.g., Adam) that updates $(\theta, \alpha, \beta)$ and performs soft update of target parameters.
- (Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3 for the standard reference structure.)

**2. Target Network (2 marks):** The target network has the same architecture but its parameters are *not* updated by gradient descent on every step. Instead, they are updated either:
- **Hard update:** every $C$ steps, copy $\theta^- \leftarrow \theta$.
- **Soft (Polyak) update:** $\theta^- \leftarrow \tau \theta + (1 - \tau) \theta^-$ with $\tau \ll 1$ (typically $10^{-3}$).
This stabilises the bootstrap target and prevents divergence.

**3. Experience Replay (2 marks):** Transitions are stored in a buffer $\mathcal{D}$ of capacity $\sim 10^6$. During training, a minibatch is sampled uniformly at random. This breaks the temporal correlation between consecutive samples (which violates the i.i.d. assumption of SGD) and improves sample efficiency by allowing each transition to be used for multiple gradient updates.

**4. Huber Loss (1 mark):** The loss used is the **Huber (smooth-L1) loss** rather than pure MSE:

$$
L(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\, \rho\bigl( Y^{\text{DDQN}}_t - Q(S_t, A_t; \theta) \bigr) \,\right], \quad \rho(x) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2}x^2 & \text{if } \vert x \vert \leq 1 \\ \vert x \vert - \frac{1}{2} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

This is less sensitive to outliers (large TD errors) than squared loss, improving stability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Duelling DDQN**

- [ ] **Duelling DDQN = Double DQN + Dueling Network**, applied to the same $Q$-estimator.
- [ ] **DDQN target:** $Y_t^{\text{DDQN}} = r + \gamma Q(s', \arg\max_a Q(s', a; \theta); \theta^-)$.
- [ ] **Dueling head:** two parallel FC branches — **value stream $V(s)$** (scalar) and **advantage stream $A(s,a)$** (vector).
- [ ] **Centring is mandatory** for identifiability. **Mean-centring** is the KTU-preferred form:
  $$Q(s, a) = V(s) + A(s, a) - \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}\sum_{a'} A(s, a')$$
- [ ] **Identifiability** means the decomposition $V + A$ must be unique; centring enforces $\mathbb{E}[A] = 0$.
- [ ] **Target network** stabilises bootstrap; soft update uses $\tau \approx 10^{-3}$, hard update every $C \approx 10^4$ steps.
- [ ] **Experience replay buffer** of size $\sim 10^6$ breaks temporal correlation; minibatch SGD.
- [ ] **Huber loss** (smooth-L1) is used in lieu of MSE for outlier robustness.
- [ ] **Overestimation bias** in DQN arises from $\mathbb{E}[\max_a(\hat{Q}_a)] \geq \max_a Q_a^*$; DDQN breaks it via $\theta \neq \theta^-$.
- [ ] **Atari benchmark:** Duelling DDQN improves performance on **~57% of games** over vanilla DDQN with no extra hyper-parameters.
- [ ] **Gradient flow:** value head gets direct gradient signal, accelerating learning in states where action choice is less critical.
- [ ] **Always label** which network is online ($\theta, \alpha, \beta$) vs. target ($\theta^-, \alpha^-, \beta^-$) — examiners deduct marks for ambiguity.
- [ ] **Practical tip:** Mean-centring is preferred over max-centring because it preserves gradient flow on the best action.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prioritized Experience Replay (PER)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT745 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Prioritized Experience Replay (PER)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 · Prioritized Experience Replay (PER)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Prioritized Experience Replay (PER)** is a reinforcement learning enhancement technique that improves the sample efficiency of off-policy algorithms (such as Deep Q-Networks / DQN) by **replaying stored transitions non-uniformly**. Each transition $(s, a, r, s', \text{done})$ stored in the replay buffer is assigned a **priority value** proportional to the magnitude of its Temporal-Difference (TD) error. Transitions with higher TD-error — meaning those from which the agent can learn the most — are sampled more frequently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECMT745, Module 4):** PER addresses the core limitation of *vanilla* experience replay where every transition is sampled with equal probability, leading to **data-inefficient learning**. PER is officially categorized as an **experience-replay modification** technique in the KTU 2024 scheme and is a high-yield topic for Part-B 14-mark questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Flashcard" Intuition

Imagine a medical student preparing for an exam with a stack of 10,000 flashcards.

- **Uniform Replay (Vanilla DQN):** The student picks flashcards at random. Most cards contain facts the student already knows well, but occasionally a confusing card appears. The student wastes time re-studying easy material.
- **Prioritized Experience Replay (PER):** The student maintains a *difficulty rating* on every flashcard. Cards that the student got wrong recently (high TD-error) are pulled from the stack far more often, while easy cards are revisited only occasionally. Crucially, the student also **down-weights** the most-difficult cards to avoid becoming obsessed with a few confusing outliers (this is the role of **Importance Sampling weights**).

The "difficulty rating" of a transition in RL is its **absolute TD-error**, and the "down-weighting" is the **stochastic correction** done via **Importance Sampling**.

### 1.3 The Core Problem PER Solves

| Limitation of Uniform Replay | How PER Fixes It |
|---|---|
| Rare but informative transitions get drowned out by abundant, low-error transitions | High-priority transitions are sampled more often |
| Gradient updates are noisy and slow to converge | Variance of updates is reduced by focusing on high-error events |
| Sample-inefficient — requires more environment interactions | Achieves the same performance with **fewer** environment steps |
| Does not differentiate "easy" vs "surprising" experiences | Uses TD-error as a **proxy for "surprise"** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** In the original PER paper (Schaul et al., 2015–2016), PER was shown to give a **2× speed-up in learning** on Atari games when combined with Double DQN — i.e., it doubles sample efficiency. KTU examiners frequently cite this benchmark.

### 1.4 The Two Variants of Prioritization

$$ \text{Prioritization Type} = \begin{cases} \text{Proportional Prioritization} & \text{(rank-based proportional to } \vert \delta_i \vert \text{)} \\ \text{Rank-Based Prioritization} & \text{(rank in the buffer dictates priority)} \end{cases} $$

KTU's Module 4 syllabus emphasizes the **Proportional** variant (used with a **SumTree** data structure) because it is the one practically implemented in the original paper and libraries like Stable-Baselines3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism of PER

PER modifies the standard DQN training loop at **two critical points**: (i) during *sampling* of a mini-batch from the replay buffer, and (ii) during the *weighting* of the loss contributed by each transition.

### Step 1 — Compute the TD-Error for Each Transition
After taking a transition $(s, a, r, s')$ in the environment, the agent computes the Bellman-TD error:

$$ \delta_i = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a'; \theta^-) - Q(s, a; \theta) $$

The **absolute value** $\vert \delta_i \vert$ becomes the *raw priority score* of transition $i$.

### Step 2 — Assign a Numerical Priority
To ensure that even transitions with **zero TD-error** have a non-zero chance of being sampled (otherwise they would never be revisited and the network would never correct potential small biases), a small constant $\varepsilon$ is added:

$$ p_i = \vert \delta_i \vert + \varepsilon $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why add $\varepsilon$?** Without $\varepsilon$, any transition whose TD-error is exactly zero (e.g., a transition the network has perfectly learned) would be assigned a probability of **0** and could never be re-sampled. This leads to *stale knowledge*. The hyperparameter $\varepsilon$ is typically set to a small value like **$10^{-6}$** or **$0.01$**.

### Step 3 — Convert Priority to Sampling Probability
The probability of sampling transition $i$ is its priority raised to a power $\alpha$, normalized over the entire buffer of size $N$:

$$ P(i) = \frac{p_i^{\alpha}}{\sum_{k=1}^{N} p_k^{\alpha}} $$

- $\alpha = 0$ → **uniform sampling** (recovers vanilla experience replay).
- $\alpha = 1$ → **fully prioritized** sampling (steepest bias).
- Typical KTU-recommended range: $\alpha \in [0.4,\ 0.7]$.

### Step 4 — Correct the Induced Sampling Bias via Importance Sampling (IS)
Because we are sampling non-uniformly, the gradient updates become **biased** estimators of the true expected return. To compensate, PER multiplies the loss of each transition by an **Importance Sampling weight**:

$$ w_i = \left( \frac{1}{N \cdot P(i)} \right)^{\beta} $$

For numerical stability, the weights are normalized by the maximum weight in the batch:

$$ w_i \;\leftarrow\; \frac{w_i}{\max_{j} w_j} $$

- $\beta = 0$ → **no correction** (high bias, low variance).
- $\beta = 1$ → **fully corrected** (unbiased, but increases variance).
- Typical schedule: **anneal** $\beta$ from $0.4$ to $1.0$ over training.

### Step 5 — Update the SumTree
After computing the new $\delta_i$ for the sampled transition, the **SumTree** (a binary heap) is updated in $O(\log N)$ time so that future sampling uses the new priority.

### Step 6 — Compute the Weighted Loss and Backpropagate
The Q-network is updated using the **weighted MSE loss**:

$$ L(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot \big( \delta_i \big)^2 $$

The $\frac{1}{N}$ factor and the max-normalization of $w_i$ together keep the loss on a stable scale.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol | Formula | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta_i$ | $r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a';\theta^-) - Q(s,a;\theta)$ | TD-error of transition $i$ | varies |
| 2 | $p_i$ | $\vert \delta_i \vert + \varepsilon$ | Numerical priority | $\ge \varepsilon$ |
| 3 | $P(i)$ | $\dfrac{p_i^{\alpha}}{\sum_{k=1}^{N} p_k^{\alpha}}$ | Sampling probability | $\in [0,1]$ |
| 4 | $w_i$ | $\left( \dfrac{1}{N \cdot P(i)} \right)^{\beta}$ | Importance sampling weight | $\le 1$ after norm. |
| 5 | $w_i^{\text{norm}}$ | $\dfrac{w_i}{\max_j w_j}$ | Normalized IS weight | $\in (0,1]$ |
| 6 | $L(\theta)$ | $\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot (\delta_i)^2$ | Weighted MSE loss | minimized |
| 7 | $N$ | buffer size | Replay buffer capacity | $10^4 - 10^6$ |
| 8 | $\alpha$ | prioritization exponent | Bias-vs-uniformity knob | $0 \to 1$ (typ. 0.6) |
| 9 | $\beta$ | IS exponent | Bias correction strength | $0 \to 1$ (annealed) |
| 10 | $\varepsilon$ | priority constant | Guarantees non-zero priority | $10^{-6}$ to $0.01$ |
| 11 | $\gamma$ | discount factor | Temporal discount of future reward | $0.9$ to $0.99$ |
| 12 | SumTree node $v$ | $v_{\text{parent}} = v_{\text{left}} + v_{\text{right}}$ | Recursive definition | always $\ge 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** PER is widely used in **robotic manipulation, autonomous driving (path planning), game AI (Atari, Go, StarCraft), and recommendation systems**. The TD-error proxy for "surprise" is also used in **curriculum learning** and **hard-example mining** in supervised deep learning (e.g., focal loss in object detection).

## 2.3 Why PER Works — Intuitive Justification

1. **TD-error measures learning progress.** A transition with high $\vert \delta_i \vert$ is one whose value estimate is far from the bootstrap target — updating on it changes the network a lot.
2. **Replay is offline learning; you can afford to be selective.** Unlike online RL, where the data distribution is forced by the policy, replay lets you *re-weight* past data arbitrarily — the only cost is bias, which IS weights correct.
3. **Adaptive curriculum.** PER implicitly constructs a *curriculum* of surprising transitions, similar in spirit to self-paced learning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Uniform Sampling to Prioritized Sampling

### Setup
Let the replay buffer contain $N$ transitions, each with absolute TD-error $\vert \delta_i \vert$. Under **uniform sampling**, every transition has probability $P_u(i) = 1/N$. The expected value update in DQN is:

$$ \mathbb{E}_{i \sim U}\big[\,w_i \cdot \delta_i^2\,\big] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \delta_i^2 \quad \text{(since } w_i = 1 \text{)} $$

### Step 1 — Define the Prioritized Sampling Distribution
PER replaces $P_u(i) = 1/N$ with:

$$ P_p(i) = \frac{p_i^{\alpha}}{\sum_{k=1}^{N} p_k^{\alpha}} \quad \text{where } p_i = \vert \delta_i \vert + \varepsilon $$

This is the **exponentially-scaled** priority. When $\alpha = 0$, $P_p(i) = 1/N$ recovers the uniform case (verify below).

### Step 2 — Verify the Boundary Case $\alpha = 0$

$$ \begin{aligned}
P_p(i)\big|_{\alpha = 0} &= \frac{p_i^{0}}{\sum_k p_k^{0}} \\
&= \frac{1}{\sum_k 1} \\
&= \frac{1}{N} \;=\; P_u(i)
\end{aligned} $$

This confirms that PER is a *strict generalization* of uniform replay.

### Step 3 — Show Why a Naive Weighted Loss is Biased
Suppose we simply compute:

$$ L_{\text{naive}}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big( \delta_i \big)^2 \quad \text{with } i \sim P_p $$

The expectation under $P_p$ is:

$$ \begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}_{i \sim P_p}\big[\delta_i^2\big] &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_p(i) \cdot \delta_i^2 \\
&\neq \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \delta_i^2 \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{i \sim U}\big[\delta_i^2\big]
\end{aligned} $$

So the gradient $\nabla_\theta L_{\text{naive}}$ points in a **biased** direction. We need to re-weight.

### Step 4 — Importance Sampling Correction
The Importance Sampling identity states that for any function $f$:

$$ \mathbb{E}_{i \sim P_p}\!\left[\frac{P_u(i)}{P_p(i)} \cdot f(i)\right] \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{i \sim U}\!\left[f(i)\right] $$

Substituting $f(i) = \delta_i^2$ and $P_u(i) = 1/N$:

$$ \begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}_{i \sim P_p}\!\left[\frac{1/N}{P_p(i)} \cdot \delta_i^2\right] &= \mathbb{E}_{i \sim U}\!\left[\delta_i^2\right] \\
\mathbb{E}_{i \sim P_p}\!\left[\left(\frac{1}{N \cdot P_p(i)}\right) \cdot \delta_i^2\right] &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \delta_i^2
\end{aligned} $$

This identifies the **unbiased weight** as:

$$ w_i = \left(\frac{1}{N \cdot P_p(i)}\right)^{\beta} $$

The $\beta$ exponent (typically annealed from 0.4 → 1.0) controls how strongly we correct the bias. Setting $\beta = 1$ gives full correction.

### Step 5 — Normalization for Stability
Since $w_i$ can become very small for high-priority samples, we normalize:

$$ w_i^{\text{norm}} = \frac{w_i}{\max_j w_j} \in (0, 1] $$

This does not change the *direction* of the gradient (only its magnitude) and is therefore unbiased up to a global scalar.

### Step 6 — Final Loss

$$ \boxed{\;L(\theta) \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{\text{norm}} \cdot \big( r_i + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_i', a'; \theta^-) - Q(s_i, a_i; \theta) \big)^2\;} $$

This is the loss used in the original PER paper.

## 3.2 Derivation: SumTree Sampling in $O(\log N)$

A **SumTree** is a complete binary tree where every internal node stores the **sum of priorities** in its subtree. The root stores $P_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i^{\alpha}$.

### Sampling Algorithm (Pseudocode as math)

Let $s$ be a uniform sample from $[0, P_{\text{total}}]$.

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{idx, node} &\leftarrow \text{root} \\
\text{while node is not a leaf:} & \\
\quad \text{left\_sum} &\leftarrow \text{node.left.value} \\
\quad \text{if } s \le \text{left\_sum:} & \\
\qquad \text{node} &\leftarrow \text{node.left} \\
\quad \text{else:} & \\
\qquad s &\leftarrow s - \text{left\_sum} \\
\qquad \text{node} &\leftarrow \text{node.right} \\
\text{return idx of node} &
\end{aligned} $$

At each level, we either descend left or right, halving the search space. This is why the complexity is $O(\log N)$.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

Suppose the buffer has 4 transitions with priorities $p_1 = 4,\ p_2 = 1,\ p_3 = 2,\ p_4 = 3$ and $\alpha = 1$.

**Step A — Build the SumTree:**

$$ P_{\text{total}} = 4 + 1 + 2 + 3 = 10 $$

Leaf layer (depth 2): $[4, 1, 2, 3]$. Internal layer (depth 1): $[4+1, 2+3] = [5, 5]$. Root (depth 0): $[5+5] = [10]$.

**Step B — Draw a uniform sample $s$ from $[0, 10)$:**

Say $s = 6.5$.

**Step C — Traverse:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{At root (10):} & \quad \text{left} = 5,\ s = 6.5 > 5 \Rightarrow \text{go right, } s \leftarrow 6.5 - 5 = 1.5 \\
\text{At right child (5):} & \quad \text{left} = 2,\ s = 1.5 \le 2 \Rightarrow \text{go left, } s \text{ unchanged} \\
\text{At leaf: } & \quad \text{return transition 3, priority } 2
\end{aligned} $$

**Step D — Probability check:**
$P(3) = p_3 / P_{\text{total}} = 2/10 = 0.2$. The interval $[s, s + p_3) = [1.5, 3.5)$ lies entirely within the right-subtree range $[5, 10)$ shifted by the parent's left-mass — the algorithm correctly returned transition 3.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Hot-Question)

| Aspect | Uniform Replay | Rank-Based PER | Proportional PER |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sampling distribution | $1/N$ | $1 / \text{rank}(i)^{\alpha}$ | $p_i^{\alpha} / \sum_k p_k^{\alpha}$ |
| Data structure | Circular buffer | Sorted list / heap | **SumTree** |
| Sampling time | $O(1)$ | $O(N)$ naive / $O(\log N)$ with heap | $O(\log N)$ |
| Update time | $O(1)$ | $O(\log N)$ | $O(\log N)$ |
| Memory overhead | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ | $O(2N)$ (tree) |
| Sensitivity to outliers | None | Low (uses rank) | Higher (uses raw $\vert \delta \vert$) |
| Implementation complexity | Low | Medium | Medium |
| Recommended in PER paper | baseline | slower in practice | **default** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SumTree Data Structure (Mermaid)

The following diagram shows a fully constructed SumTree for 8 leaf transitions. Each leaf's value is its priority $p_i^{\alpha}$; each internal node's value is the sum of its children.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["Root = 100"]:::root
    n1A["Node L1-Left = 55"]:::internal
    n2A["Node L1-Right = 45"]:::internal
    n3A["L2-A = 30"]:::internal
    n4A["L2-B = 25"]:::internal
    n5A["L2-C = 20"]:::internal
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the tree:** The root value `100` equals the total priority mass. To sample, draw $s \sim U[0,100)$ and descend. At each node, if $s \le$ left child value, go left; else, subtract left value and go right. This implements **inverse-CDF sampling** in $O(\log N)$.

## 4.2 PER Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B --> C[Compute TD-Error delta_i]:::calc
    C --> D[Update SumTree Node Value]:::tree
    E[Sample s from U(0, Total)]:::samp --> F[Traverse SumTree]:::trav
    F --> G[Fetch Batch of Transitions]:::fetch
    G --> H[Compute IS Weights w_i]:::weight
    H --> I[Weighted MSE Loss]:::loss
    I --> J[Backprop into Q-Network]:::bp
    J --> K[Soft Update Target Network]:::targ
    K --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> The orange block is the **on-policy data collection**; the teal/green block is the **priority update**; the purple block is the **sampling**; and the red/pink block is the **gradient update**. This is the standard PER training loop.

## 4.3 Priority Flow Over Training (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([New Transition Arrives]):::s --> compute[Compute delta = TD-Error]:::c
    compute --> assign[p = abs delta + epsilon]:::a
    assign --> insert[Insert leaf with p at next empty slot]:::i
    insert --> bubble[Update ancestors upward to root]:::b
    bubble --> done([SumTree is now consistent]):::s

    sample([Sampling Request: s ~ U(0, root)]):::s --> traverse[Descend from root, comparing s with left child]:::t
    traverse --> leaf[Return leaf index]:::l
    leaf --> fetch[Fetch transition from parallel data array]:::f
    fetch --> is[Compute IS weight w]:::w
    is --> loss[Add weighted delta^2 to loss]:::lo
    loss --> back([Backprop]):::s
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[ACTOR]
        A1[Policy / Q-Network]:::actor

    end
```

> [!NOTE]
