# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]
**What is Prioritized Experience Replay (PER) in Deep Reinforcement Learning? Why is it preferred over uniform sampling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Prioritized Experience Replay is a replay-buffer modification in which transitions are sampled **non-uniformly** according to a priority score, typically the **absolute TD-error** $\vert \delta_i \vert + \varepsilon$. Transitions with higher TD-error — i.e., those where the agent's value estimate is most "surprised" — are sampled more frequently. This is preferred over uniform sampling because it **(1)** improves sample efficiency, **(2)** accelerates convergence on hard examples, and **(3)** implicitly forms an adaptive curriculum, focusing gradient updates on the most informative experiences. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for priority, 1 mark for advantage.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]
**What is the role of the Importance Sampling (IS) weight $w_i$ in PER?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Importance Sampling weight $w_i = \left(\frac{1}{N \cdot P(i)}\right)^{\beta}$ is introduced to **remove the bias** introduced by non-uniform sampling. Without IS weights, the loss estimator would be biased because the sampling distribution $P(i)$ no longer matches the uniform data distribution. The IS weight re-weights each transition's contribution so that the expected gradient under $P(i)$ equals the expected gradient under uniform sampling. The exponent $\beta$ is annealed from 0 to 1 during training, trading off bias vs. variance. *(1 mark for bias issue, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for $\beta$ annealing.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT forget to normalize the IS weights** by $\max_j w_j$ — students lose up to 1 mark for omitting this stability step.
> 2. **Always mention the $\varepsilon$ small constant** in the priority formula $p_i = \vert \delta_i \vert + \varepsilon$ — without it, the buffer can become "locked out" and only high-priority items get sampled, causing divergence.
> 3. **Sketch the SumTree** if asked about the data structure; verbal description alone gets only partial credit.
> 4. **State the value of typical hyperparameters** (e.g., $\alpha = 0.6$, $\beta = 0.4 \to 1.0$, $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$) — examiners award 1 mark for "implementation awareness."

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**

**a)** Explain the limitations of uniform Experience Replay in Deep Q-Networks. With a neat diagram of a SumTree, derive the priority-based sampling probability $P(i)$ used in Prioritized Experience Replay. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Derive the Importance Sampling correction used in PER and show that when $\alpha = 0$, the prioritized scheme reduces to uniform sampling. Discuss the role of annealing the hyperparameter $\beta$ during training. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Limitations of Uniform Replay (2 marks):**

1. **Equal sampling probability** $P(i) = 1/N$ ignores the fact that some transitions are far more *informative* than others. Rare, high-reward, or "surprising" transitions are drowned out by abundant low-error ones.
2. **Sample-inefficient** — the agent must collect many more environment steps to learn the same policy, increasing wall-clock training time and compute cost.
3. **No adaptive curriculum** — uniform sampling does not exploit the structure of what the network *doesn't yet know*.

**Derivation of $P(i)$ (3 marks):**

The priority of transition $i$ is its absolute TD-error plus a small constant:

$$ p_i = \vert \delta_i \vert + \varepsilon $$

Exponentiate by $\alpha$ to control the steepness of the priority distribution:

$$ p_i^{\alpha} $$

Normalize over all $N$ transitions in the buffer:

$$ P(i) = \frac{p_i^{\alpha}}{\sum_{k=1}^{N} p_k^{\alpha}} $$

This is the **proportional prioritization** scheme, originally proposed by Schaul et al. (2015).

**SumTree Diagram (2 marks):**

> The SumTree is a complete binary tree where every leaf stores $p_i^{\alpha}$ and every internal node stores the sum of its children. The root equals the **total priority mass** $P_{\text{total}} = \sum_k p_k^{\alpha}$. Sampling proceeds by drawing $s \sim U[0, P_{\text{total}})$ and descending: at each node, if $s \le$ left-child value, go left; else subtract the left value and go right. The leaf reached is the sampled index. This is an $O(\log N)$ **inverse-CDF sampler**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Derivation of IS Correction (4 marks):**

Starting from the importance-sampling identity:

$$ \mathbb{E}_{i \sim P_p}\!\left[\frac{P_u(i)}{P_p(i)} f(i)\right] = \mathbb{E}_{i \sim P_u}\!\left[f(i)\right] $$

With $P_u(i) = 1/N$ and $f(i) = (\delta_i)^2$:

$$ \begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}_{i \sim P_p}\!\left[\frac{1/N}{P_p(i)} \cdot \delta_i^2\right] &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \delta_i^2 \\
\mathbb{E}_{i \sim P_p}\!\left[\left(\frac{1}{N \cdot P_p(i)}\right)^{\beta} \cdot \delta_i^2\right] &\approx \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \delta_i^2 \quad \text{when } \beta = 1
\end{aligned} $$

Hence the **Importance Sampling weight**:

$$ w_i = \left(\frac{1}{N \cdot P_p(i)}\right)^{\beta} $$

For numerical stability, normalize by the batch-maximum:

$$ w_i^{\text{norm}} = \frac{w_i}{\max_j w_j} $$

The final weighted loss is:

$$ L(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{\text{norm}} \cdot \delta_i^2 $$

**Reduction to Uniform Sampling when $\alpha = 0$ (2 marks):**

$$ \begin{aligned}
P_p(i)\big|_{\alpha = 0} &= \frac{p_i^{0}}{\sum_k p_k^{0}} = \frac{1}{\sum_k 1} = \frac{1}{N} = P_u(i)
\end{aligned} $$

Substituting back:

$$ w_i = \left(\frac{1}{N \cdot (1/N)}\right)^{\beta} = 1^{\beta} = 1 $$

The loss becomes $\frac{1}{N}\sum \delta_i^2$, identical to vanilla DQN with uniform replay. ✓

**Role of Annealing $\beta$ (1 mark):**

- At the start of training, the value estimates are very inaccurate; full bias correction ($\beta = 1$) is unstable. So $\beta$ is set to a low value (e.g., $0.4$), giving a *biased but low-variance* estimator that allows fast early learning.
- As training proceeds, $\beta$ is **annealed** linearly toward $1.0$, gradually removing the bias.
- This annealing schedule is empirically critical: skipping it (using $\beta = 1$ from the start) often *decreases* performance because the variance of the gradient is too high early on.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice**

**a)** With a suitable block diagram, describe the complete Prioritized Experience Replay (PER) training pipeline. Explain the role of the **SumTree** data structure and state its time complexity for sampling and priority update. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A replay buffer of size $N = 4$ contains transitions with TD-errors $\delta_1 = 4,\ \delta_2 = 1,\ \delta_3 = 2,\ \delta_4 = 3$. With $\alpha = 1$ and $\varepsilon = 0$, construct the SumTree, draw a uniform sample $s = 6.5$, and trace the descent to identify the sampled transition. Verify your answer by computing $P(i)$ for each transition. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Pipeline Description (4 marks):**

The PER training loop consists of the following stages, executed repeatedly:

1. **Interact:** Use the Q-network to select action $a$ in state $s$ via $\varepsilon$-greedy; observe $r$ and next state $s'$.
2. **Store:** Append $(s, a, r, s', \text{done})$ to the replay buffer and assign it a priority $p_i = \vert \delta_i \vert + \varepsilon$ in the SumTree.
3. **Sample:** Draw $s \sim U[0, P_{\text{total}})$ and traverse the SumTree to retrieve a **mini-batch** of indices in $O(\log N)$ per sample.
4. **Compute targets:** For each transition in the batch, compute $y_i = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a'; \theta^-)$.
5. **Compute weights:** Calculate $w_i = \left(\frac{1}{N \cdot P(i)}\right)^{\beta}$ and normalize by $\max_j w_j$.
6. **Loss & Update:** Backprop the weighted loss $L(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum w_i \cdot (y_i - Q(s, a; \theta))^2$ into the Q-network.
7. **Refresh priorities:** Recompute $\delta_i$ for the sampled transitions and update their SumTree leaves.

**Role & Complexity of SumTree (3 marks):**

- The SumTree is needed because naively sampling from $P(i) \propto p_i^{\alpha}$ requires $O(N)$ time per draw (you'd have to compute all probabilities and do inverse-CDF lookup in a flat array). For Atari training with $N = 10^6$ and millions of updates, this is infeasible.
- The SumTree stores the **cumulative sum of priorities** in a binary tree so that an inverse-CDF sample can be located in $O(\log N)$ time.
- **Sampling complexity:** $O(\log N)$ per transition, since we descend at most $\log_2 N$ levels.
- **Priority update complexity:** $O(\log N)$ per updated transition, since we only need to recompute the $\log_2 N$ ancestors of the modified leaf.
- **Memory:** $O(2N)$ to store the tree's internal nodes plus leaves.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute priorities (1 mark):**

With $\varepsilon = 0$ and $\alpha = 1$:

$$ p_1 = 4,\ p_2 = 1,\ p_3 = 2,\ p_4 = 3 $$

**Step 2 — Construct the SumTree (2 marks):**

Leaves (depth 2): $[4, 1, 2, 3]$.

Internal nodes (depth 1): $[4+1, 2+3] = [5, 5]$.

Root (depth 0): $[5+5] = [10]$.

**Tree visualization:**

```
            Root(10)
           /       \
        N1(5)     N2(5)
        /  \       /  \
      L1(4) L2(1) L3(2) L4(3)
```

**Step 3 — Trace the descent for $s = 6.5$ (2 marks):**

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{At root (10): left} = 5,\ & 6.5 > 5 \Rightarrow \text{go right, } s \leftarrow 6.5 - 5 = 1.5 \\
\text{At right child N2 (5): left} = 2,\ & 1.5 \le 2 \Rightarrow \text{go left, } s \text{ unchanged} \\
\text{At leaf: return transition 3 (priority 2)}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 4 — Verification (2 marks):**

$$ P(1) = 4/10 = 0.40, \quad P(2) = 1/10 = 0.10, \quad P(3) = 2/10 = 0.20, \quad P(4) = 3/10 = 0.30 $$

Transition 3 occupies the cumulative interval $[0.4 + 0.1, 0.4 + 0.1 + 0.2) = [0.5, 0.7)$ of the total probability mass. In SumTree terms, transition 3 lives in the right subtree of the root, occupying offset range $[5, 7)$ within $[0, 10)$. The sample $s = 6.5$ falls inside this range, **confirming the descent returned transition 3**. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Recap — "Important Things to Remember" (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The PER Survival Kit — High-Yield Points for Last-Minute Revision**

- ☐ **PER = prioritized replay of transitions based on TD-error magnitude**, not uniform sampling.
- ☐ **Priority formula:** $p_i = \vert \delta_i \vert + \varepsilon$ — the **$\varepsilon$** guarantees no transition has zero sampling chance.
- ☐ **Sampling probability:** $P(i) = p_i^{\alpha} / \sum_k p_k^{\alpha}$; $\alpha = 0$ reduces to uniform; typical $\alpha = 0.6$.
- ☐ **Importance Sampling weight:** $w_i = (1/(N \cdot P(i)))^{\beta}$, then **normalize** by $\max_j w_j$.
- ☐ **Anneal $\beta$ from 0.4 → 1.0** during training for stability.
- ☐ **Final loss:** $L(\theta) = (1/N) \sum_i w_i^{\text{norm}} \cdot \delta_i^2$.
- ☐ **SumTree** gives $O(\log N)$ sampling and update; root stores $\sum p_i^{\alpha}$.
- ☐ **Two data structures** are kept in sync: a transition array + a SumTree of priorities.
- ☐ PER was introduced by **Schaul et al. (2015–2016)** to improve DQN/Double-DQN sample efficiency by ~2× on Atari.
- ☐ PER is **off-policy only** — incompatible with on-policy algorithms like vanilla Policy Gradient / PPO.
- ☐ PER reduces **variance** of the gradient at the cost of (corrected) **bias**; IS weights restore unbiasedness.
- ☐ **Common pitfalls:** forgetting $\varepsilon$, forgetting max-normalization, using $\beta = 1$ too early, forgetting to update SumTree after each training step.
- ☐ Engineering applications: **Atari game AI, robotic manipulation (OpenAI Rubik's cube hand), autonomous driving planners, recommender systems** that maintain a prioritized feedback buffer.
